The respondent subsequently filed a petition of appeal. The appellant then filed an application for the execution of the decree pending appeal pursuant to Section 763 of the Civil Procedure Code. The respondent resisted this application, arguing that there were substantial questions of law regarding the doctrine of ultra vires to be adjudicated in the final appeal. The Commercial High Court dismissed the appellant's application, concluding that the ultra vires defense constituted a substantial question of law. The appellant appealed this dismissal to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether English Law is applicable to the matters in issue in terms of Section 3 of the Civil Law Ordinance. Whether the Commercial High Court erred in determining that the doctrine of ultra vires raised a substantial question of law warranting the dismissal of the application for execution of decree pending appeal. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Law Ordinance Companies Act No. 17 Civil Procedure Code Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka Cases Referred to: Colletts v Bank of Ceylon - [1982/v2-sri-lr-514] Boyd v Staples - [1820-33-ramanathan-21] Sivapooniam - [1820-33-ramanathan-78] Gerard and Brown v Fulton - [1843-55-ramanathan-124] The Mahakande Housing Company Ltd. v Duhilamomal - [1981/v2-sri-lr-232] Wright v Peoples Bank - [1985/v2-sri-lr-292] Usman v Rahim - [32-nlr-259] In re Amand - [1942-1-kb-445] The Shantha Rohan - [1994/v3-sri-lr-54] Attorney-General v Howard Reformed Church, Bedford - [1975/v2-all-er-337] Chunilal Mehta v Century Shipping and Manufacturing Co. Ltd. - [1962-air-sc-1314] Subbarao v Veeraju - [1951-air-madras-969] Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of English Law Rule applied: Section 3 of the Civil Law Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the Companies Act No. 17 contained a lacuna regarding ultra vires transactions. Under Section 3 of the Civil Law Ordinance, in the absence of local statutory provisions, the law to be administered in commercial matters, including banking and corporations, is the law that would be administered in England at the corresponding period. The court rejected the respondent's argument that the post-1972 sovereignty of Sri Lanka precluded the application of English law, noting that the incorporation of English law by reference is a limited process subject to local legislative override. Finding: English Law is applicable to the matters in issue. Issue 2: Substantial Question of Law Rule applied: The test for a "substantial question of law" as established in Collettes Ltd. v Bank of Ceylon. Court's reasoning: The court applied the test from Collettes Ltd. v Bank of Ceylon, which states that if general principles are well-settled, the matter does not constitute a substantial question of law. Since English law (specifically Sections 35A and 35B of the English Companies Act) clearly addresses the validity of acts done by a company and precludes the ultra vires defense in these circumstances, the legal position is settled. The High Court judge had acknowledged this clarity but erroneously concluded it remained a substantial question. Finding: The High Court erred in its determination; there was no substantial question of law. Interaction of Issues The resolution of the first issue—that English Law applies to fill the lacuna in the local Companies Act—directly informs the second issue. By establishing that the applicable English law is clear and settled, the court demonstrated that the respondent's defense of ultra vires was legally unsustainable, thereby negating the existence of a "substantial question of law" that would justify staying the execution of the decree. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, and the order of the Commercial High Court dated 16.02.2006 is set aside. The Commercial High Court is directed to order the execution of the decree pending appeal and issue the writ accordingly. Keywords Appeal from Commercial High Court order — Execution of Decree pending appeal — Ultra vires transactions — Applicability of English Law — Civil Law Ordinance Section 3 — Companies Act No. 17 — Banking transactions — Doctrine of ultra vires — Substantial question of law

Parties: Peoples Bank, Colombo 02 v. Yashodha Holdings (PVT) Ltd.
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N. G. Amaratunga, J; Saleem Marsoof, J.
Keywords: Ultra vires — Section 35(1) of the English Companies Act — Section 3 of the Civil Law Ordinance — Estoppel by representation — Doctrine of unjust enrichment — Companies Act No. 17 — Primary objects of a company
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Petitioner-Appellant, Peoples Bank, instituted actions in the Commercial High Court in July 1999 to recover funds advanced as short-term loans to the Defendant-Respondent-Respondent, Yashodha Holdings (Pvt.) Ltd. The loans were intended to finance the import of cement and sugar for trading purposes. The Memorandum of Association of the Respondent company indicated that its primary object was to function as a holding company, and it did not include trading in cement or sugar. The Respondent failed to repay the loans and raised the defense of ultra vires, asserting that the loan transactions were outside the scope of its objects as defined in its Memorandum of Association. The Commercial High Court ruled in favor of the Respondent, and the Appellant appealed against the order refusing the execution of the decree pending appeal. Issues Whether Section 35(1) of the English Companies Act is applicable in Sri Lanka via Section 3 of the Civil Law Ordinance to validate acts of a company that are otherwise ultra vires. Whether the Respondent is estopped from asserting the defense of ultra vires regarding the loan transactions. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Law Ordinance No. 5 (Cap. 79, Legislative Enactments of Ceylon, 1956) Companies Act No. 17 of 1982 Companies Act No. 7 of 2007 Companies Ordinance No. 51 (Cap. 145, Legislative Enactments of Ceylon, 1956) Joint Stock Companies Ordinance No. 4 Joint Stock Companies Ordinance No. 6 Joint Stock Companies Ordinance No. 3 Joint Stock Companies Ordinance No. 2 Bills of Exchange Ordinance Auctioneers and Brokers Ordinance (Cap. 109, Legislative Enactments of Ceylon, 1956) Cases Referred to: Wright and Three Others v Peoples Bank - [1985/v2-sri-lr-292] Mrs. Lily M. de Costa v Bank of Ceylon - [72-nlr-457] Duhilanomal and Others v Mahakande Housing Co. Ltd. - [1982/v2-sri-lr-504] Amarasekere v Mitsui and Company Ltd. and Others - [1993/v1-sri-lr-22] Foss v Harbottle - [1843/2-hare-461] Wallersteiner v Moir (No.2) - [original citation as in decision_text] Sunila Abeysekera v Ariya Rubasinghe, Competent Authority and Others - [2000/v1-sri-lr-314] Durayappah v Fernando and Others - [69-nlr-265] The Ceylonese Union Co. v Vyramuttan - [19-nlr-250] Ashbury Railway Carriage and Iron Company Ltd. v. Riche - [1875/lr-7-hl-653] Jupiter Cigarette & Tobacco Co. Ltd. v. Soysa - [72-nlr-12] Royal British Bank v Turquand - [1856/6-e&b-327] Patrick Lowe & Sons and Others v Commercial Bank of Ceylon Ltd - [2001/v1-sri-lr-280] Attorney General v The Great Eastern Railway - [original citation as in decision_text] Sinclair v Brougham - [1914/ac-398] Moses v Macferlan - [1760/2-burr-1055] Re Coltman; Coltman v. Coltman - [1881/19-ch-d-64] In re K.L. Tractors Ltd. - [1961/106-c-l-r-318] Breckenridge Speedway Ltd. et al. v. R. - [1970/s-c-r-175] Dodwell & Co v John - [20-nlr-206] Freeman and Lockyer v. Buckhurst Park Properties (Mangal) Limited - [1964/1-all-e-r-630] Whitney Arms Co. v Barlow - [63-new-york-62] Eastern Building & Loan Association of Syracuse v Williamson - [189-us-122] Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of English Companies Act Rule applied: Section 3 of the Civil Law Ordinance No. 5. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that while Section 3 of the Civil Law Ordinance allows for the application of English law in specific areas, this is subject to the qualification that such law does not apply if local enactments provide otherwise. The Companies Act No. 17 of 1982 was a comprehensive, exhaustive enactment that specifically addressed company objects and powers. Therefore, the court held that the doctrine of ultra vires as defined in the local Act precluded the importation of Section 35(1) of the English Companies Act. Finding: Section 35(1) of the English Companies Act is not applicable in Sri Lanka. Issue 2: Estoppel against the defense of ultra vires Rule applied: Principles of estoppel by representation. Court's reasoning: The court examined the loan application (X18/X16) and found that the Respondent had made explicit representations regarding its competence to enter into the loan agreement and its liability to repay. The Appellant acted in good faith on these representations to its detriment. The court noted that while English courts have been cautious, American jurisprudence (e.g., Whitney Arms Co. v Barlow) supports the use of estoppel to prevent a corporation from repudiating a contract after receiving the full benefit of performance. Finding: The Respondent is estopped from asserting the defense of ultra vires. Interaction of Issues The court's finding that the English statutory provision did not apply necessitated an alternative legal basis to prevent the Respondent from benefiting from its own ultra vires act. The application of estoppel effectively bridged this gap, ensuring that the Respondent could not avoid its contractual obligations after having induced the Appellant to advance funds based on the Respondent's own representations of authority. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The order of the Commercial High Court dated 16th February 2006 is set aside, and the High Court is directed to order the execution of the decree pending appeal. Keywords Ultra vires — Section 35(1) of the English Companies Act — Section 3 of the Civil Law Ordinance — Estoppel by representation — Doctrine of unjust enrichment — Companies Act No. 17 — Primary objects of a company

Parties: People’s Bank v. Yashodha Holdings (Pvt.) Ltd.
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N.G. Amaratunga, J.; Saleem Marsoof, J.
Keywords: ultra vires — English Law — Companies Act No. 17 of 1982 — Civil Law Ordinance — banking transactions — substantial question of law — execution of decree — appeal process — legal authority — corporate capacity
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal by the People’s Bank (the appellant) against Yashodha Holdings (Pvt.) Ltd. (the respondent) concerning a loan facility of Rs. 210,000,000/- that the respondent had borrowed from the appellant. The appellant filed a civil action on 27 July 1999, seeking a decree for Rs. 328,474,520.55, which included interest at 26% per annum from 1 January 1998 until the date of the decree. The respondent contested the claim, asserting that the borrowing was ultra vires its memorandum and articles of association, and thus, it was not authorized to receive the loan. The Commercial High Court dismissed the appellant's application for execution of the decree pending appeal on 16 February 2006, leading to this appeal. The appellant contended that the respondent was estopped from denying liability due to the acceptance and utilization of the loan. The core issues revolved around the applicability of English law to the banking transactions in question, the interpretation of ultra vires transactions under the Companies Act, and whether substantial questions of law warranted the dismissal of the execution application. Issues The primary issues framed for determination were: 1. Whether English Law would be applicable to the matters in issue in this appeal, as set out in the Plaint, in terms of Section 3 of the Civil Law Ordinance. 2. Whether the learned Judge of the Commercial High Court erred in concluding that there was a substantial question of law based on the doctrine of ultra vires to be determined in the final appeal. Applicable Law Statutes: Companies Act No. 17 of 1982; Civil Law Ordinance; Companies Act of 1985 (UK). Cases Referred to: Colletts v. Bank of Ceylon - [1982] 2 Sri L.R. 514; The Mahakande Housing Company Ltd. v. Duhilamomal and others - [1981] 2 Sri L.R. 232; Wright and Three others v. People’s Bank - [1985] 2 Sri L.R. 292; Usman v. Rahim - (1930) 32 N.L.R. 259; The Shantha Rohan - [1994] 3 Sri L.R. 54. Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of English Law Rule applied: Section 3 of the Civil Law Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The court examined the applicability of English law in the context of the Companies Act No. 17 of 1982, which did not contain provisions regarding ultra vires transactions. The court noted that the Civil Law Ordinance provides for the application of English law in commercial matters, including banking transactions. The appellant argued that the absence of specific provisions in the Companies Act regarding ultra vires transactions necessitated the application of English law. The court referenced historical precedents and legal principles indicating that English law had been incorporated into Sri Lankan law through various means, including legislative enactments and judicial interpretations. The court concluded that English law, particularly the provisions of the Companies Act of 1985, which addressed ultra vires transactions, would apply to the case at hand. Finding: The court found that English law was applicable to the matters in issue, as the Companies Act did not provide for ultra vires transactions. Issue 2: Substantial Question of Law Rule applied: Definition of substantial question of law. Court's reasoning: The court analyzed whether the learned Judge of the Commercial High Court had erred in determining that there was a substantial question of law regarding the ultra vires defense raised by the respondent. The court referred to the definition of a substantial question of law, emphasizing that it arises when there is room for difference of opinion or when the courts below have dealt with the question at length. The court noted that the principles governing ultra vires transactions were well settled in English law and that the Companies Act No. 17 of 1982 did not provide for such transactions. The court concluded that the learned Judge had erred in finding a substantial question of law, as the applicable legal principles were clear and established. Finding: The court determined that there was no substantial question of law that warranted the dismissal of the execution application. Interaction of Issues The issues were interrelated, as the determination of the applicability of English law directly influenced the court's assessment of whether a substantial question of law existed. The court's finding that English law applied to the case undermined the respondent's ultra vires defense, thereby negating the basis for the learned Judge's conclusion regarding the substantial question of law. Conclusion The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, set aside the order of the Commercial High Court, and directed the execution of the decree pending appeal. The court emphasized that the learned Judge had erred in concluding that there was a substantial question of law based on the doctrine of ultra vires. The court made no order as to costs.
Counsel: S. A. Parathalingam, PC, with Kushan de Alwis, Hiran Jayasuriya and Nishkan Parathalingam for Plaintiff-Petitioner-Appellant. Manohara de Silva, PC, with Yasa Jayasekera and Nimal Hippola for Defendant-Respondent-Respondent.

Parties: Bank of Ceylon v. Somaratne
Bench: R.A.N. Gamini Amaratunga, J; K. Sripavan, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Appeal from Labour Tribunal — Industrial Disputes Act — Certificate by Attorney-at-Law — Code of Criminal Procedure Act — Section 322(2) — Section 31D(9) — Petition of appeal — Form of petition — Legislative intent — Construction of statutes
Summary: Facts The workman, H.S. Somaratne, was employed by the Bank of Ceylon as a junior clerk cum assistant cashier starting on 16th May 1997. Following a disciplinary inquiry into alleged misconduct at the Anuradhapura Bazaar Branch, the Bank terminated the workman's services effective 19th December 1997. The workman subsequently initiated proceedings at the Labour Tribunal in Anuradhapura to challenge the termination. The Labour Tribunal concluded the inquiry and ordered the Bank to pay the workman compensation of Rs. 304,200. The Bank appealed this decision to the Provincial High Court of the North Central Province, which was later transferred to the Civil High Court of Appeal. During the appeal proceedings, the workman raised a preliminary objection, arguing that the petition of appeal failed to include a certificate by an Attorney-at-Law stating that the questions of law were fit for adjudication by the court. The High Court upheld this objection and dismissed the appeal, citing a requirement for such a certificate under the Code of Criminal Procedure Act. The Bank then sought and was granted leave to appeal to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court erred in law by finding that compliance with Section 322(2) of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979 is mandatory for appeals filed under Section 31D(9) of the Industrial Disputes Act. Whether the High Court erred in law by determining that a petition of appeal must contain a certificate by an Attorney-at-Law confirming that the questions of law are fit for adjudication. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 154P High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 19 of 1990 Industrial Disputes Act No. 43 of 1950 Industrial Disputes (Amendment) Act No. 32 of 1990 Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979 Cases Referred to: Thavarayan v. Balakrishnan - [1984/v1-sri-lr-189] Thomas v. Ceylon Wharfage Co. Ltd. - [49-nlr-397] Saranelis v. Civilian Labour Administrative Officer - [56-nlr-366] Liyanage v. Weeraman - [sc-235/72] Walker Sons & Co. Ltd. v. Fry - [68-nlr-73] Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act Rule applied: Section 31D(9) of the Industrial Disputes Act and Section 322(2) of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979. Court's reasoning: The court examined whether the procedural requirements for the "hearing and disposal" of an appeal under the Industrial Disputes Act incorporate the formal requirements for drafting a petition of appeal found in the Code of Criminal Procedure. The court noted that previous jurisprudence, specifically Thavarayan v. Balakrishnan, had erroneously relied on Thomas v. Ceylon Wharfage Co. Ltd., which had been overruled by Saranelis v. Civilian Labour Administrative Officer. The court reasoned that the phrase "hearing and disposal" does not encompass the formal requirements or the style of drafting a petition of appeal. Finding: The High Court erred in finding that Section 322(2) of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act is mandatory for such appeals. Issue 2: Requirement for an Attorney-at-Law Certificate Rule applied: Section 31D(6) of the Industrial Disputes Act (as amended). Court's reasoning: The court analyzed the specific requirements for a petition of appeal under Section 31D(6), which mandates only the inclusion of uncancelled stamps and, where applicable, a security certificate from the Labour Tribunal. The court followed the principle established in Walker Sons & Co. Ltd. v. Fry, which held that the statute does not require the petition to state questions of law, nor does the manner of drafting fall under the scope of "hearing and disposal." The court emphasized that it must adhere to the literal meaning of the statute and avoid expanding requirements not explicitly stated by the legislature. Finding: The petition of appeal does not require a certificate by an Attorney-at-Law. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are mutually reinforcing. By determining that the "hearing and disposal" provisions of the Industrial Disputes Act do not incorporate the formal drafting requirements of the Code of Criminal Procedure, the court effectively negated the necessity for the Attorney-at-Law certificate. The resolution of the first issue regarding the scope of the Industrial Disputes Act rendered the second issue regarding the certificate a logical consequence of the same statutory interpretation. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, and the Order of the Civil High Court of Appeal dated 13th November 2008 is set aside. The Civil High Court of Appeal is directed to hear and determine the appeal on its merits as expeditiously as possible. Keywords Appeal from Labour Tribunal — Industrial Disputes Act — Certificate by Attorney-at-Law — Code of Criminal Procedure Act — Section 322(2) — Section 31D(9) — Petition of appeal — Form of petition — Legislative intent — Construction of statutes

Parties: Kalinga Padmatillake v. The Director General, Commission to Investigate Allegations of Bribery or Corruption
Bench: Dr. Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; D.J.DE S. Balapatabend, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Bribery Act — Solicitation — Acceptance of bribe — Evidence evaluation — Credibility of witnesses — Standard of proof — Reasonable doubt — Hearsay evidence — Acquittal
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal by Kalinga Padmatillake, who was convicted by the Magistrate's Court for bribery under the Bribery Act. The appellant was accused of soliciting and accepting bribes from a complainant, M. Sumathipala, who testified that the appellant, a police sergeant, demanded money in exchange for not prosecuting him regarding a traffic violation. The prosecution's case was built on the testimonies of several witnesses, including Sumathipala and his family members. The appellant was sentenced to six months of imprisonment and fined for each of the four counts against him. The High Court dismissed the appellant's appeal against the conviction, leading to the present appeal to the Supreme Court. The Supreme Court granted special leave to appeal on specific legal questions, particularly regarding the admissibility of evidence related to subsequent incidents that were not part of the charges. Issues The primary issues before the court were: Whether the learned High Court Judge erred in fact and in law by not addressing the allegations regarding the consideration of evidence pertaining to subsequent incidents as corroborative material for the charges. Whether the evidence presented by the prosecution was sufficient to establish the guilt of the appellant beyond a reasonable doubt. Applicable Law Statutes: Bribery Act Cases Referred to: R.Ananda vs. The Commissioner General to Investigate Allegation of Bribery or Corruption - [2008 1 BLR- Part II 136]; Gurcharan Singh, Appellant V. State of Haryana, Respondent - [Criminal Law Journal 1994 (2) 1710] Analysis Issue 1: Whether the learned High Court Judge erred in fact and in law by not addressing the allegations regarding the consideration of evidence pertaining to subsequent incidents as corroborative material for the charges. Rule applied: The principle of admissibility of evidence and the requirement for corroboration in bribery cases. Court's reasoning: The court examined the evidence presented during the trial, particularly focusing on the testimonies of the prosecution witnesses. It noted that the credibility of the primary witness, Sumathipala, was significantly undermined due to inconsistencies in his statements. The court highlighted that the learned Magistrate had relied heavily on Sumathipala's testimony, which was riddled with contradictions regarding the dates and circumstances of the alleged solicitation. The court emphasized that the learned High Court Judge failed to adequately address the implications of these inconsistencies and did not provide a clear rationale for accepting the evidence of subsequent incidents as corroborative material. The court reiterated that the standard of proof in criminal cases, particularly in bribery, is 'beyond reasonable doubt,' and any reasonable doubt must lead to an acquittal. Finding: The court concluded that the learned High Court Judge erred in not addressing the critical issues surrounding the reliability of the evidence and the implications of the inconsistencies in the testimonies. The court found that the prosecution had failed to establish the guilt of the appellant beyond a reasonable doubt. Interaction of Issues The issues were interrelated, as the credibility of the witnesses directly impacted the court's ability to ascertain whether the prosecution had met its burden of proof. The court's analysis of the admissibility of evidence and the reliance on subsequent incidents as corroborative material was crucial in determining the overall reliability of the prosecution's case. Conclusion The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, set aside the convictions and sentences imposed by the lower courts, and acquitted the appellant of all charges. The court directed the Registrar to forward copies of the judgment to the respective High Court and Magistrate's Court. The judgment underscored the importance of evaluating evidence critically and adhering to the standard of proof required in criminal cases, particularly those involving allegations of bribery. Keywords Bribery Act — Solicitation — Acceptance of bribe — Evidence evaluation — Credibility of witnesses — Standard of proof — Reasonable doubt — Hearsay evidence — Acquittal
Counsel: Rienz Arasacularatne P.C. with Wasantha Batagoda and Dharshana Kuruppu for the Accused-Appellant-Petitioner. Dilan Ratnayake SSC with Ms. Fernando for the Complainant-Respondent-Respondent.

Parties: Kushan Indika v. Wijesekera and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N.G. Amaratunga, J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Article 11 of the Constitution — Article 13(1) of the Constitution — Arbitrary arrest — Freedom from torture — Cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment — Police conduct — Reasonable suspicion — Procedure established by law — Violation of fundamental rights
Summary: Facts The petitioner, a driver for the Sri Lanka State Plantation Corporation, was waiting for a bus at the Gallinda Junction on 02.04.2007 to travel to Colombo for work. The 1st respondent, an Officer-in-Charge of the Pitigala Police Station, arrived at the location in a police jeep accompanied by other officers and a person in custody. The 1st respondent approached the petitioner and demanded identification. Despite the petitioner producing his driving license, the 1st respondent assaulted him and accused him of loitering. The 1st respondent subsequently forced the petitioner to load a motorbike into a truck and then into the police jeep, assaulting him with a club when he requested assistance. The petitioner was taken to the Pitigala Police Station, detained in a cell, and released approximately one hour later without any statement being recorded or charges being filed. The petitioner later sought medical examination and filed a complaint with the 2nd respondent. The 1st respondent denied the allegations, claiming the arrest was based on suspicion of loitering in an area known for criminal activity and that the petitioner failed to provide identification. Issues Whether the arrest of the petitioner by the 1st respondent violated Article 13(1) of the Constitution regarding freedom from arbitrary arrest. Whether the actions of the 1st respondent, including the physical assault and detention of the petitioner, violated Article 11 of the Constitution regarding freedom from torture or cruel, inhuman, or degrading treatment. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 11 and 13(1) Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979, Section 32(1)(b) Registration of Persons Act No. 32 of 1968, Section 15(1) and 16(1) Cases Referred to: Piyasena v. OIC Wadduwa - [sc-application-433/93] Gamlath v. Neville Silva - [1991/v2-sri-lr-267] Muttusamy v. Kannangara - [52-nlr-324] Veeradas v. Controller of Immigration and Emigration - [1989/v2-sri-lr-205] W.D.K. de Silva v. Ceylon Fertilizer Corporation - [1989/v2-sri-lr-393] Christie v. Leachinsky - [1933-30-cox-gc-67] Analysis Issue 1: Arbitrary Arrest Rule applied: Article 13(1) of the Constitution and Section 32(1)(b) of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979. Court's reasoning: The court held that an arrest without a warrant under Section 32(1)(b) requires a "reasonable suspicion." The 1st respondent failed to demonstrate that his suspicion was founded on objective facts. The petitionerâ€™s possession of a driving license constituted valid identification, as the Registration of Persons Act does not mandate the National Identity Card as the exclusive form of identification. Furthermore, the 1st respondent failed to inform the petitioner of the true grounds for his arrest, a mandatory requirement under Article 13(1). Finding: The arrest was arbitrary and violated Article 13(1) of the Constitution. Issue 2: Torture and Degrading Treatment Rule applied: Article 11 of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The court noted that Article 11 protects against both physical and mental suffering. Although the petitioner's injuries were non-grievous, the combination of physical assault, the forced labor of moving a motorbike, the arbitrary detention, and the subsequent release without a recorded statement constituted a high degree of humiliation. The court found that these actions, performed under color of office without lawful sanction, amounted to degrading treatment. Finding: The 1st respondent violated the petitioner's fundamental rights under Article 11 of the Constitution. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected as the lack of a lawful basis for the arrest (Article 13(1)) directly facilitated the subsequent physical assault and degrading treatment (Article 11). The absence of procedural compliance, such as recording a statement, reinforced the court's conclusion that the entire encounter was an arbitrary exercise of power rather than a legitimate law enforcement action. Conclusion The court held that the 1st respondent violated the petitioner's fundamental rights under Articles 11 and 13(1) of the Constitution. The 1st respondent is directed to pay the petitioner Rs. 50,000 as compensation and costs within three months. Keywords Fundamental rights — Article 11 of the Constitution — Article 13(1) of the Constitution — Arbitrary arrest — Freedom from torture — Cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment — Police conduct — Reasonable suspicion — Procedure established by law — Violation of fundamental rights

Parties: Bank of Ceylon v. H.S. Somaratne
Bench: R.A.N. Gamini Amaratunga, J.; K. Sripavan, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Industrial Disputes Act — Labour Tribunal — Appeal Procedure — Certificate of Attorney-at-Law — Compliance with Statutory Requirements — Judicial Interpretation — Hearing and Disposal of Appeals — Substantial Compliance — Legal Precedents — Supreme Court Jurisdiction — Employment Law
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for special leave to appeal concerning the termination of employment of H.S. Somaratne (the workman) by the Bank of Ceylon (the employer). The workman commenced his employment with the Bank on 16th May 1997 as a junior clerk cum assistant cashier. His services were terminated on 19th December 1997 following a disciplinary inquiry that found him guilty of misconduct. Subsequently, the workman sought relief from the Labour Tribunal in Anuradhapura, which ruled in his favor on 30th June 2006, awarding him compensation of Rs. 304,200. The employer contested this decision, leading to an appeal in the Provincial High Court of the North Central Province, which was later transferred to the Civil High Court of Appeal. In the High Court, the workman raised a preliminary objection regarding the appeal's compliance with statutory requirements, specifically the absence of a certificate from an Attorney-at-Law certifying that the matter was fit for adjudication. The High Court upheld this objection on 13th November 2008, resulting in the dismissal of the employer's appeal. The employer then sought leave to appeal to the Supreme Court, which granted it on several questions of law regarding the necessity of the Attorney-at-Law's certificate and compliance with the Industrial Disputes Act. Issues The Supreme Court was tasked with addressing several critical legal questions: Whether the learned High Court Judge erred in law by ruling that compliance with Section 322(2) of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act was mandatory for appeals from Labour Tribunal orders under the Industrial Disputes Act. Whether the learned High Court Judge erred in requiring a certificate from an Attorney-at-Law for the appeal to be valid. Whether the learned High Court Judge was correct in finding that the employer's petition did not meet the requirements of the Industrial Disputes Act and the Code of Criminal Procedure. Whether the learned High Court Judge failed to consider substantial compliance with the statutory requirements despite the lack of literal adherence. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act No. 43 of 1950 as amended by Act No. 32 of 1970; Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979. Cases Referred to: Thavarayan and two Others v. Balakrishnan (1984) 1 S.L.R. 189; Liyanage v. Weeraman, S.C. 235/72; Walker Sons & Co, Ltd. v. Fry, 68 N.L.R 73; Saranelis v. Civilian Labour Administrative Officer, 56 N.L.R. 366. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the learned High Court Judge erred in law regarding the necessity of compliance with Section 322(2) of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act. Rule applied: The interpretation of statutory requirements for appeals under the Industrial Disputes Act. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the statutory framework surrounding appeals from Labour Tribunal decisions, particularly focusing on Section 31(D)(9) of the Industrial Disputes Act and its relationship with the Code of Criminal Procedure. The Court noted that the High Court's interpretation mandated strict adherence to procedural requirements, which may not align with the legislative intent. The Court emphasized that the Industrial Disputes Act does not explicitly require the petition of appeal to conform to the formalities outlined in the Code of Criminal Procedure. The judgment referenced the precedent set in Liyanage v. Weeraman, where it was determined that the absence of a certificate from an Attorney-at-Law did not invalidate the appeal. The Court highlighted that the primary objective of the legislation is to ensure fair hearing and resolution of disputes rather than to impose rigid procedural barriers. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the High Court erred in its interpretation, affirming that the petition of appeal need not contain the Attorney-at-Law's certificate. Issue 2: Whether the learned High Court Judge erred in requiring a certificate from an Attorney-at-Law. Rule applied: The necessity of a certificate for appeals under the Industrial Disputes Act. Court's reasoning: The Court reiterated that the requirement for a certificate was not explicitly stated in the Industrial Disputes Act. It analyzed the legislative history and intent behind the provisions, noting that the Act aimed to facilitate access to justice for workers. The Court criticized the High Court's reliance on Thavarayan's case, which had not considered subsequent legal developments that altered the landscape of appeal procedures. The Court emphasized that the absence of a certificate should not preclude the adjudication of legitimate disputes, particularly in labor matters where the stakes for employees are significant. Finding: The Supreme Court found that the High Court's insistence on the certificate was misplaced and contrary to the principles of justice and fair play. Issue 3: Whether the learned High Court Judge was correct in finding that the employer's petition did not meet the requirements of the Industrial Disputes Act. Rule applied: Compliance with statutory requirements for appeals. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the specific provisions of the Industrial Disputes Act and the Code of Criminal Procedure, determining that the High Court's interpretation imposed undue restrictions on the employer's right to appeal. The Court noted that the statutory language did not support the High Court's conclusions regarding the necessity of strict compliance with procedural formalities. The Court emphasized that the focus should be on the substantive issues at hand rather than procedural technicalities that could obstruct justice. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the High Court's findings were erroneous and that the employer's petition was valid despite the lack of a certificate. Interaction of Issues The issues presented were interrelated, as they all revolved around the interpretation of statutory requirements for appeals from Labour Tribunal decisions. The Supreme Court's analysis highlighted the need for a balanced approach that prioritizes access to justice while ensuring that procedural safeguards are respected. The Court's findings collectively underscored the importance of judicial discretion in interpreting legislative intent and the necessity of allowing appeals to proceed on their merits rather than being dismissed on technical grounds. Conclusion The Supreme Court set aside the order of the Civil High Court of Appeal dated 13th November 2008, directing that the appeal be heard and determined on its merits. The Court emphasized the need for expeditious resolution of labor disputes, reinforcing the principle that procedural requirements should not hinder the pursuit of justice. The judgment reaffirmed the importance of judicial interpretation in ensuring that the rights of workers are protected while maintaining the integrity of the legal process. Keywords Industrial Disputes Act — Labour Tribunal — Appeal Procedure — Certificate of Attorney-at-Law — Compliance with Statutory Requirements — Judicial Interpretation — Hearing and Disposal of Appeals — Substantial Compliance — Legal Precedents — Supreme Court Jurisdiction — Employment Law
Counsel: Sanjaya Rajaratnam, D.S.G. for Respondent-Appellant-Appellant. Nimal Hapuarachchi for the Applicant-Respondent-Respondent.

Parties: Thilakaratne v. University Grants Commission
Bench: J.A.N. De Silva, J.; K. Sripavan, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — University admissions — Inter-university transfer — Administrative action — Appeals Committee — Universities Act No. 16 — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Article 126(4) of the Constitution
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Liyanaarachchige Samurddhi Chakra Tillekeratne, was admitted to the M.B.B.S. degree programme at the Faculty of Medicine of the University of Sri Jayawardenapura for the 2008/2009 academic year. The 4th Respondent, Thirani Gimhani Pathirana, was admitted to the M.B.B.S. degree programme at the Faculty of Medicine of the University of Colombo for the same academic year. The Petitioner sought an inter-university transfer to the University of Colombo, while the 4th Respondent sought a transfer to the University of Sri Jayawardenapura. The University Grants Commission, the 1st Respondent, acknowledged the Petitioner's appeal but indicated that transfers were subject to the availability of vacancies and the Z-score ranking. The Petitioner and the 4th Respondent subsequently sought to swap their respective university placements. The 1st Respondent failed to facilitate these transfers, leading the Petitioner to file the instant application alleging an infringement of Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The 1st Respondent argued that any vacancy at the 3rd Respondent University should be filled by a student with a higher Z-score than the Petitioner. Issues Whether the failure of the 1st Respondent to effect the inter-university transfers of the Petitioner and the 4th Respondent constituted a violation of the Petitioner's fundamental right to equality under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the creation of an Appeals Committee by the 1st Respondent was intra vires the powers granted under Section 15 of the Universities Act No. 16 of 1978. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 12(1), 17, 126, 126(4) Universities Act No. 16 of 1978, Section 15 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution, which guarantees the right to equality before the law and equal protection of the law. Court's reasoning: The Court evaluated the requests for transfer made by the Petitioner and the 4th Respondent. It found that the requests were fair and reasonable and that granting them would not cause hardship or prejudice to other students already selected for the medical programmes. The Court rejected the 1st Respondent's argument regarding the availability of vacancies, noting that the proposed swap between the two students did not require additional vacancies. Furthermore, the Court noted that the 1st Respondent's previous assignment of another student to a different university via the Appeals Committee lacked a clear, justifiable basis. Finding: The Court held that the 1st Respondentâ€™s failure to effect the transfers violated the Petitioner's fundamental rights under Article 12(1). Issue 2: Authority of the Appeals Committee Rule applied: Section 15 of the Universities Act No. 16 of 1978. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that the 1st Respondent was unable to point to any specific statutory provision within the Universities Act that empowered the Commission to establish an Appeals Committee to handle student transfers. Finding: The Court questioned the intra vires nature of the Appeals Committee's creation, implying that the committee lacked the necessary statutory foundation to override the reasonable requests of the students. Interaction of Issues The findings are closely linked; the Court's determination that the Appeals Committee lacked clear statutory authority (Issue 2) undermined the 1st Respondent's reliance on the committee's procedures to deny the Petitioner's transfer. Consequently, the arbitrary nature of the 1st Respondent's refusal to grant the transfer, despite the lack of prejudice to other students, directly supported the finding of a violation of Article 12(1) (Issue 1). Conclusion The Court declared that the Petitioner's fundamental rights under Article 12(1) were violated. The 1st Respondent was directed to forthwith transfer the 4th Respondent to the Faculty of Medicine of the 2nd Respondent University and the Petitioner to the Faculty of Medicine of the 3rd Respondent University. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — University admissions — Inter-university transfer — Administrative action — Appeals Committee — Universities Act No. 16 — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Article 126(4) of the Constitution

Parties: Samarasekera v. Alwis and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Article 11 (freedom from torture) — Article 13(1) (freedom from arbitrary arrest) — Article 13(2) (right to be brought before a judge) — Police brutality — Unlawful arrest — Detention without production before a magistrate — Reasonable suspicion — Treasure hunting — Code of Criminal Procedure Act
Summary: Facts The petitioner, Bandula Samarasekera, was involved in an incident on 28.02.2007 while traveling in a jeep with an exorcist and a friend. After performing rituals at his son's residence, the petitioner encountered a vehicle driven by the 1st respondent, Vijitha Alwis, on a narrow road. A dispute arose regarding the right of way, during which the 1st respondent allegedly snatched the petitioner's ignition key and assaulted the petitioner and his companions. The 1st respondent then drove the petitioner to the Kandy Police Station, claiming the petitioner was a treasure hunter. The petitioner was detained at the Kandy Police Station and was not produced before a Magistrate until approximately 4:00 p.m. on 01.03.2007, at which point he was released on bail. The petitioner sustained various injuries, including a fractured nasal bone and a perforated ear drum, as documented in a Medico-Legal Report. The 1st respondent contended that the petitioner was apprehended by a mob for treasure hunting and that he merely intervened to protect the petitioner. However, investigations by the Archaeological Department confirmed the site had no archaeological value. The petitioner filed this application alleging violations of his fundamental rights under Articles 11, 13(1), and 13(2) of the Constitution. Issues Whether the arrest of the petitioner by the 1st respondent violated Article 13(1) of the Constitution. Whether the detention of the petitioner at the Kandy Police Station violated Article 13(2) of the Constitution. Whether the assault on the petitioner by the 1st respondent violated Article 11 of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 11, 12(1), 13(1), 13(2) Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979, Sections 32, 35, 37 Penal Code, Section 311 Cases Referred to: Kushan Indika v Ranjan Wijesekera - [sc-appeal-129/2007] Pelawattage (A.A.L.) for Piyasena v O.I.C. Wadduwa - [sc-application-433/93] Gamlath v Neville Silva - [1991/v2-sri-lr-267] Muttusamy v Kannangara - [52-nlr-324] Veeradas v Controller of Immigration and Emigration - [1989/v2-sri-lr-205] Holgate-Mohammed v Duke - [1984/v1-all-e-r-1056] Ansalin Fernando v Sarath Perera - [1992/v1-sri-lr-411] Namasivayam v Gunawardena - [1989/v1-sri-lr-394] Amal Sudath Silva v Kodituwakku - [1987/v2-sri-lr-119] Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 13(1) Rule applied: Article 13(1) of the Constitution and Section 32(1)(b) of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979. Court's reasoning: The court found that an arrest without a warrant requires a reasonable suspicion that the person committed a cognizable offense. The 1st respondentâ€™s claim that the petitioner was a treasure hunter was contradicted by the Archaeological Department, which confirmed the site had no archaeological value. Furthermore, the 1st respondent provided inconsistent accounts, initially claiming he arrested the petitioner for treasure hunting and later stating it was for the petitioner's own safety. Finding: The arrest was unlawful, violating Article 13(1). Issue 2: Violation of Article 13(2) Rule applied: Article 13(2) of the Constitution and Section 37 of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979. Court's reasoning: The petitioner was held in custody for over 18 hours without being produced before a Magistrate. The court held that even if a person is taken into custody for their own protection, they must be produced before a Magistrate as early as possible without unnecessary delay. The delay in this case was unjustified. Finding: The detention violated Article 13(2). Issue 3: Violation of Article 11 Rule applied: Article 11 of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The court relied on the Medico-Legal Report, which confirmed the petitioner suffered grievous and non-grievous injuries consistent with blunt force trauma. Given the lack of evidence supporting the 1st respondent's claim that a mob assaulted the petitioner, the court accepted the petitioner's version that the 1st respondent assaulted him due to a road dispute. Finding: The 1st respondent violated Article 11. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected; the unlawful arrest (Issue 1) set the stage for the subsequent unlawful detention (Issue 2). The 1st respondent's abuse of power, evidenced by the assault (Issue 3), confirms the lack of a legitimate basis for the initial arrest, thereby reinforcing the conclusion that the entire process was a violation of the petitioner's fundamental rights. Conclusion The court held that the petitioner's fundamental rights under Articles 11, 13(1), and 13(2) were violated. The 1st respondent is ordered to pay Rs. 50,000 as compensation and costs, and the State is ordered to pay Rs. 15,000 as compensation and costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Article 11 (freedom from torture) — Article 13(1) (freedom from arbitrary arrest) — Article 13(2) (right to be brought before a judge) — Police brutality — Unlawful arrest — Detention without production before a magistrate — Reasonable suspicion — Treasure hunting — Code of Criminal Procedure Act

Parties: Vasudeva Nanayakkara v. K.N. Choksy and Others
Bench: J.A.N. de Silva, CJ.; Dr. Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; Shiranee Tilakawardane, J.; S. Marsoof, PC, J.; Jagath Balapatabendi, J.; K. Sripavan, J.; P.A. Ratnayake, PC, J.
Keywords: fundamental rights — Article 14(1)(g) — right to work — administrative functions — public office — constitutional provisions — judicial review — executive authority — appointment powers — legal standing — affidavit requirements — national interest — public service — due process — restrictions on rights — separation of powers
Summary: Facts The case revolves around the application filed by the 8th respondent-petitioner, Dr. P.B. Jayasundera, who sought relief from a previous court order that restricted him from holding any public office or exercising executive or administrative functions. The order in question was made on 08 October 2008, following a judgment in SC (Application) No. 209/2007, which had significant implications for the 8th respondent-petitioner’s career as a high-ranking government official. The petitioner argued that the order was made under duress and that he had been compelled to submit an affidavit stating he would not hold any public office. The context of the case is critical; the 8th respondent-petitioner had been a prominent figure in the Sri Lankan government, serving as Secretary to the Ministry of Finance and Planning, and was involved in various high-level governmental functions. The petitioner contended that the order impeded his fundamental rights, particularly his right to work as guaranteed under Article 14(1)(g) of the Constitution. The Supreme Court had to consider whether the restrictions imposed by the previous order were justifiable and whether the petitioner’s rights had been violated. Issues The primary issues before the court were: Whether the order dated 08 October 2008, which restricted the 8th respondent-petitioner from holding public office, was valid and enforceable. Whether the 8th respondent-petitioner’s fundamental rights, particularly his right to work, were violated by the court’s previous order. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of Sri Lanka, Article 14(1)(g) — Right to engage in lawful occupation; Article 15(5) — Restrictions on fundamental rights; Article 52(1) — Appointment powers of the President. Cases Referred to: State of West Bengal V. Subodh Gopal - [AIR (1954) SC 92]; Gopalan V. State of Madras - [AIR (1950) SC 27]; Brigadier Rohan Liyanage V. Chandrananda de Silva - [SC (Application) No.506/99 SCM of 18.07.2000]; Nagle V Feilden - [1966] 1 All E.R. 689. Analysis Issue 1: Validity of the Order Rule applied: The court examined the validity of the order made on 08 October 2008, which required the 8th respondent-petitioner to submit an affidavit stating he would not hold any public office. The court noted that the order was made under the premise that the 8th respondent-petitioner had acted in violation of certain legal principles. The court emphasized that the right to work is a fundamental right protected by the Constitution, and any restrictions on this right must be justified under Article 15(5). The court highlighted that the affidavit was not submitted voluntarily but was a result of the court's directive, raising questions about its enforceability. The court further stated that the previous bench that made the order did not have the authority to impose such restrictions without a clear legal basis. The court referenced the importance of due process and the need for any restrictions on fundamental rights to be proportionate and necessary. The court concluded that the order was not valid as it imposed undue restrictions on the 8th respondent-petitioner’s rights without sufficient justification. Finding: The court found that the order dated 08 October 2008 was invalid and unenforceable, as it violated the fundamental rights of the 8th respondent-petitioner. Issue 2: Violation of Fundamental Rights Rule applied: The court analyzed whether the previous order constituted a violation of the 8th respondent-petitioner’s fundamental rights. The court reiterated that Article 14(1)(g) guarantees the right to engage in lawful occupation, which is a cornerstone of individual freedom in a democratic society. The court emphasized that any restrictions on this right must be clearly defined and justified by law. The court noted that the 8th respondent-petitioner had been a high-ranking official with significant responsibilities, and the order effectively barred him from resuming his duties in the national interest. The court referenced previous judgments that underscored the importance of protecting citizens' rights to work and the role of the judiciary in safeguarding these rights. The court concluded that the order not only restricted the 8th respondent-petitioner’s ability to work but also undermined the principles of justice and fairness that the Constitution seeks to uphold. Finding: The court determined that the previous order constituted a violation of the 8th respondent-petitioner’s fundamental rights, particularly his right to work. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as the validity of the order directly impacts the assessment of whether the fundamental rights of the 8th respondent-petitioner were violated. If the order is deemed invalid, it follows that the restrictions imposed on the petitioner’s rights are also invalid, thereby reinforcing the court’s obligation to protect fundamental rights. The court’s analysis highlights the delicate balance between the exercise of judicial authority and the protection of individual freedoms. Conclusion The Supreme Court ultimately ruled in favor of the 8th respondent-petitioner, declaring the order dated 08 October 2008 invalid and affirming the petitioner’s fundamental rights. The court emphasized the importance of upholding constitutional protections and ensuring that any restrictions on rights are justified and proportionate. The ruling serves as a significant affirmation of the right to work and the role of the judiciary in safeguarding individual freedoms against arbitrary restrictions. Keywords fundamental rights — Article 14(1)(g) — right to work — administrative functions — public office — constitutional provisions — judicial review — executive authority — appointment powers — legal standing — affidavit requirements — national interest — public service — due process — restrictions on rights — separation of powers
Counsel: M.A. Sumanthiran with Viran Corea for the Petitioner; Faiz Musthapha, PC., with Anura Meddegoda and Lakdini Perera for the 8th Respondent-Petitioner; Mohan Pieris, PC AG., with Y.J. Wijayatilake, PC, ASG., Sanjaya Rajaratnam, DSG, and Nerin Pulle, SSC., as amicus; Nihal Sri Amarasekera for 22nd Respondent appears in person.

Parties: Vasudeva Nanayakkara v. K. N. Choksy and Others
Bench: J. A. N. de Silva, C.J.; Dr. S. A. Bandaranayake, J.; S. Tilakawardane, J.; S. Marsoof, P.C., J.; D. J. De S. Balapatabendi, J.; K. Sripavan, J.; P. A. Ratnayake, P.C., J.
Keywords: fundamental rights — public office — jurisdiction — coercion — affidavit — natural justice — inherent powers — constitutional law — administrative law — due process — judicial review — legal representation — disciplinary authority — proportionality — fit person
Summary: Facts The case revolves around the Petitioner, Vasudeva Nanayakkara, who sought to vacate an order from the Supreme Court that required him to file an affidavit stating he would not hold any office in any governmental institution. This order stemmed from a previous judgment that found him responsible for violations of fundamental rights, leading to a compensation order against him. The Petitioner argued that the order was made without proper jurisdiction and that he was coerced into making the affidavit. The Respondents included prominent political figures and the Attorney General, who opposed the Petitioner’s request. The case highlights the tension between the judiciary's authority to enforce fundamental rights and the rights of individuals to hold public office. Issues Whether the Supreme Court has the jurisdiction to vacate its previous orders regarding the Petitioner’s fitness to hold public office. Whether the affidavit filed by the Petitioner was made under coercion and thus should be considered invalid. Whether the Petitioner can withdraw his undertaking not to hold public office. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of Sri Lanka, Article 14(1)(g); Article 126; Article 52. Cases Referred to: Jeyaraj Fernandopulle v. Premachandra de Silva and Others [1996] 1 Sri LR 70; Kumarasinghe v. Ratnakumara and Others [1983] 2 Sri LR 393; Basheshar Nath v. The Commissioner of Income Tax, Delhi and Rajasthan & Another (1959) Vol. 46 AIR (SC) 149; Herath Banda v. Sub Inspector of Police, Wasgiyawatta Police Station, and Others [1993] 2 Sri LR 324. Analysis Issue 1: Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court Rule applied: Article 126 of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court's jurisdiction under Article 126 is unique, allowing it to address infringements of fundamental rights without the possibility of appeal or review. The Court emphasized the need for caution in exercising this power, as it could lead to an “unruly horse” if not properly managed. The Court referenced the case of Jeyaraj Fernandopulle, which established that the Supreme Court lacks the authority to revise its own orders in fundamental rights applications unless under its inherent power to remedy a serious miscarriage of justice. The Petitioner’s argument that the previous judgment did not declare him unfit for public office was significant. The Court noted that the previous judgment did not impose a lifetime ban on the Petitioner holding public office, which was a crucial point in determining the validity of the subsequent order. Finding: The Court concluded that it could not lawfully vary its previous orders without proper jurisdiction. Issue 2: Coercion in Affidavit Rule applied: Voluntary nature of affidavits. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the nature of affidavits, emphasizing that they must be voluntary declarations. The Petitioner’s claim of coercion was critical, as it questioned the validity of the affidavit he filed. The Court acknowledged that if an affidavit is extracted under duress, it loses its voluntary character. However, the Court also noted that the Petitioner did not sufficiently prove the coercion claim. The Court highlighted that the affidavit compromised the Petitioner’s fundamental rights, particularly his right to hold public office as guaranteed by Article 14(1)(g) of the Constitution. The Court stressed that the imposition of a lifetime bar on holding public office without due process would violate the Petitioner’s rights. Finding: The affidavit was deemed to have been filed under circumstances that compromised the Petitioner’s rights, leading to its classification as a nullity. Issue 3: Withdrawal of Undertaking Rule applied: Inherent powers of the Court. Court's reasoning: The Court recognized its inherent power to rectify injustices and prevent abuse of its process. It noted that while the Petitioner had complied with the order to file the affidavit, the circumstances surrounding its filing were tainted with illegality. The Court emphasized that the President, as the appointing authority, had the ultimate discretion regarding the Petitioner’s fitness for public office. The Court concluded that the Petitioner could not be bound by the undertaking given in the affidavit due to the violation of his fundamental rights. Finding: The Court held that the Petitioner should not be prevented from holding public office, and the undertaking was rendered void. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as they all stem from the fundamental question of the Petitioner’s right to hold public office in light of the previous judgments and the subsequent orders made by the Court. The jurisdictional concerns raised by the Petitioner directly impacted the validity of the affidavit and the undertaking, which were central to the Court's analysis. The Court's recognition of its inherent powers also played a crucial role in addressing the Petitioner’s claims. Conclusion The Supreme Court ultimately ruled in favor of the Petitioner, allowing him to withdraw the affidavit and relieving him of the undertaking not to hold public office. The Court emphasized the importance of protecting fundamental rights and ensuring that individuals are not unjustly barred from public office without due process. The judgment underscored the delicate balance between judicial authority and individual rights within the framework of constitutional law.
Counsel: Faiz Musthapha, P.C., with Anura Meddegoda and Lakdini Perera for the 8th Respondent–Petitioner. M. A. Sumanthiran with Viran Corea for the Petitioner-Respondent. Nihal Sri Amarasekera for 22nd Respondent-Respondent appearing in person. Mohan Pieris, P.C. for the Attorney General with J. Wijetilleke, A.S.G., Sanjaya Rajaratnam, D.S.G., and Nerin Pulle, S.S.C. as amicus.

Parties: Target Environmental Services and Another v. Minister of Healthcare & Nutrition
Bench: J.A.N. De Silva, J.; D.J. De S. Balapatabendi, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Janitorial services — Procurement guidelines — Sealed bids — Technical evaluation committee — Bid examination — Major deviations — Tax returns — Financial statements — Ambiguity in tender requirements — Discount offers — Unfair advantage
Summary: Facts The Petitioners, Target Environmental Services Company (private) Limited and its Chairman, R.M. Anura Jayantha Ratnayake, challenged a tender process initiated by the Ministry of Healthcare and Nutrition for the supply of janitorial services to eighteen healthcare institutes. The Petitioners, along with the 13th, 14th, and 15th Respondents, submitted sealed bids in response to an advertisement. A Technical Evaluation Committee (TEC) was initially constituted to evaluate these bids. The Petitioners alleged irregularities in the tender process, specifically regarding the disqualification of other bidders and the subsequent reconstitution of a second TEC. The initial TEC had recommended the Petitioners for thirteen institutes, primarily because other bidders were disqualified for failing to submit tax and financial documents. However, an ambiguity regarding the relevant financial period for these documents was identified. A letter was subsequently issued to clarify the required timeframe, and a second TEC was constituted to review the bids. The inquiry into the process suggested that the 13th and 14th Respondents had likely submitted the required documents, but these were not properly accounted for initially. Furthermore, it was discovered that the Petitioners had submitted separate documents offering discounts on their bids, which were not part of the original sealed bids. Issues Whether the reconstitution of the Technical Evaluation Committee (TEC) by the Procurement Committee was legally valid under the Procurement Guidelines-2006. Whether the Petitioners' submission of separate discount offers constituted an attempt to gain an unfair advantage in violation of the tender process and the Procurement Guidelines-2006. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka (Articles 17 and 126) Procurement Guidelines-2006 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Validity of the reconstituted TEC Rule applied: Paragraph 8.1 and Paragraph 7.9.7 of the Procurement Guidelines-2006. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the Procurement Committee possesses the authority under paragraph 8.1 to request a TEC to resubmit a report. Given that allegations were leveled against members of the first TEC and an ambiguity existed regarding the required financial documentation, the decision to reconstitute the committee was within the administrative powers of the Procurement Committee. Furthermore, under paragraph 7.9.7, a TEC is authorized to seek clarifications from a bidder provided the bid price remains unchanged. The court found that the clarification process ensured a level playing field for all bidders. Finding: The reconstitution of the TEC and the subsequent clarification process were procedurally regular and legally valid. Issue 2: Validity of the Petitioners' discount offers Rule applied: Paragraph 3 of the bid requirements (document R23) and Paragraph 7.8(a) of the Procurement Guidelines-2006. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the Petitioners' submission of a separate document offering discounts constituted an attempt to submit multiple bids, which is prohibited by the tender requirements. By offering discounts outside the sealed bid, the Petitioners sought to alter their bid price based on the knowledge of competitors' offers, thereby undermining the fairness of the competitive bidding process. Additionally, the court noted that applying a discount to the total bid price would improperly reduce the statutory labor costs, which constitutes a major deviation under the Procurement Guidelines. Finding: The Petitioners' conduct was objectionable and violated the principles of fair play and the specific prohibitions against multiple bidding. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are mutually reinforcing. The court's determination that the tender process was conducted fairly through the valid reconstitution of the TEC and the clarification of ambiguities highlights the Petitioners' attempt to circumvent these fair procedures by submitting unauthorized discount offers. The procedural integrity upheld in the first issue underscores the impropriety of the Petitioners' actions addressed in the second issue. Conclusion The Petitioners failed to establish an infringement of their fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The application is dismissed with costs fixed at Rs 25,000. Keywords Janitorial services — Procurement guidelines — Sealed bids — Technical evaluation committee — Bid examination — Major deviations — Tax returns — Financial statements — Ambiguity in tender requirements — Discount offers — Unfair advantage

Parties: Stassen Exports Ltd v. Lipton Ltd and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; Saleem Marsoof, J.; D.J. De S. Balapatabendi, J.
Keywords: Trade mark registration — Director-General of Intellectual Property — District Court judgment — Court of Appeal judgment — Res judicata — Doctrine of stare decisis — Binding judgments — Unfair competition
Summary: Facts The Appellant, Stassen Exports Ltd., is a Sri Lankan company engaged in the tea export business. The 1st Respondent, Lipton Ltd., is a United Kingdom-based company also involved in the tea trade. In 1985, the Appellant applied to the 2nd Respondent, the Director-General of Intellectual Property, for the registration of its trade mark "SEL STASSEN PURE CEYLON TEA". The 1st Respondent filed a notice of opposition, alleging similarity to its own registered and pending trade marks. While the registration proceedings were pending, the 1st Respondent instituted an action in the District Court of Colombo seeking injunctive relief against the Appellant for alleged trade mark infringement and unfair competition. The District Court dismissed the 1st Respondent's action in 1992, finding no likelihood of confusion. The 1st Respondent appealed this decision to the Court of Appeal, which affirmed the District Court's judgment in 1996. The Appellant subsequently filed these judgments before the 2nd Respondent during the registration inquiry. However, the 2nd Respondent refused the Appellant's application for registration in 2001, effectively disregarding the prior judicial findings. The Appellant appealed this order to the High Court, which affirmed the 2nd Respondent's decision. The Appellant then sought and was granted leave to appeal to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Director-General of Intellectual Property was required to consider the judgments of the District Court and the Court of Appeal in determining the registration of the propounded mark. Whether the judgments of the District Court and the Court of Appeal were binding on the Director-General. Whether the failure to consider these judgments vitiates the order of the Director-General. Whether the Director-General was entitled to independently determine the registration application without reference to the prior judicial findings. Applicable Law Statutes: Code of Intellectual Property Act Industrial Disputes Act Cases Referred to: Duchess of Kingstons case - [1776] 2 Smith L.C. 13th edition, 644 Henderson v. Henderson - [1843] 3 Hare, 114 Hoystead v. Commissioner of Taxation - [1926/ac-155] Carl Zeiss (No. 2) - [1967/v1-ac-853] Badar Bee v. Habib Mericen Noordin - [1909/ac-615] R v. Middlesex Justices, ex. P. Bond - [1933/v2-kb-1] Mendis v. Himmappooa - [Ramanathan Reports (1843-1855) 88] Cassim v. Maricar - [12-nlr-184] Palaniappa Chetty v. Gomes - [4 Balasingham Reports, 21] Herath v. The Attorney-General - [60-nlr-193] K.R. Chinnakrishna Setty v. Sri Ambal and Co. - [A.I.R. 1973 Mysore 74] Massachusetts Saw Works - [1918/v35-rpc-137] Raj Lakshmi Dasi v. Banamali Sen - [A.I.R. 1953 S.C. 33] Municipal Council of Colombo v. Munasinghe - [71-nlr-223] New Brunswick Railway Co. v. British and French Trust Corporation Ltd. - [1939/ac-1] The Sennar - [1985/v2-all-er-104] Analysis Issue 1: Consideration of prior judgments Rule applied: Doctrine of res judicata and the principle of finality in litigation. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that the dispute regarding the similarity of the marks had already been adjudicated by competent courts. The 2nd Respondent, as a statutory authority, cannot ignore final judicial determinations on the identical issue between the same parties. Finding: Yes, the Director-General was required to consider the prior judgments. Issue 2: Binding nature of judgments Rule applied: Doctrine of res judicata and judicial precedent. Court's reasoning: The court held that while the 2nd Respondent is not a court, the doctrine of res judicata applies to quasi-judicial tribunals to prevent the re-litigation of settled matters. The 2nd Respondent is not free to ignore findings of superior courts. Finding: Yes, the judgments were binding on the Director-General. Issue 3: Vitiation of the order Rule applied: Principles of administrative law and finality of judicial decisions. Court's reasoning: By ignoring the final judgments of the District Court and the Court of Appeal, the 2nd Respondent acted in a manner that undermined the finality of litigation. This failure to account for binding judicial findings renders the order legally flawed. Finding: Yes, the failure to consider the judgments vitiates the order. Issue 4: Independent determination Rule applied: Code of Intellectual Property Act. Court's reasoning: The court rejected the argument that the Director-General has the discretion to ignore prior judicial findings. The Director-General cannot act like a "wild horse" and must respect the finality of judicial decisions regarding the same parties and subject matter. Finding: No, the Director-General was not entitled to arrive at an independent conclusion contrary to the prior judicial findings. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected as they all stem from the application of the doctrine of res judicata. Because the prior judgments were final and between the same parties, they created an estoppel that bound the 2nd Respondent. Consequently, the Director-General's failure to recognize this estoppel rendered the subsequent order invalid, as the authority lacked the legal power to re-litigate a matter already settled by superior courts. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, and the orders of the High Court and the 2nd Respondent are set aside. The 2nd Respondent is directed to reconsider the Appellant's application for registration of the trade mark in accordance with the law, while giving due effect to the plea of res judicata based on the prior judgments of the District Court and the Court of Appeal. No costs are ordered. Keywords Trade mark registration — Director-General of Intellectual Property — District Court judgment — Court of Appeal judgment — Res judicata — Doctrine of stare decisis — Binding judgments — Unfair competition

Parties: Rodrigo v. Central Engineering Consultancy Bureau
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N. G. Amaratunga, J; Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J
Keywords: Industrial Disputes Act — Labour Tribunal jurisdiction — Maintainability of application — Termination of services — Fundamental rights application — Back wages — Reinstatement — Just and equitable relief — Disciplinary proceedings — Evidence
Summary: Facts The Appellant, W. K. P. Indrajith Rodrigo, was employed by the Respondent, Central Engineering Consultancy Bureau. Following disciplinary proceedings, the Appellant was dismissed via a letter dated 14 November 1996. The Appellant challenged this dismissal in the Labour Tribunal and filed a fundamental rights application in the Supreme Court. The Supreme Court set aside the dismissal on the basis that the Chairman lacked the authority to dismiss the Appellant. The Respondent subsequently issued a second dismissal letter dated 6 July 1998, following a decision by the Board of Directors. The Appellant challenged this second dismissal in a further fundamental rights application, which was settled by an order granting the Appellant wages and dues for the period between the two dismissal letters. The Labour Tribunal subsequently resumed the Appellant's original application. The Respondent argued that the application was not maintainable because the initial dismissal had been withdrawn and the Appellant had not formally amended his pleadings to challenge the second dismissal. The Labour Tribunal rejected this objection, finding the termination unjust and ordering reinstatement and compensation. The High Court overturned this decision, ruling that the failure to amend the pleadings rendered the application fatally defective. The Appellant appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Labour Tribunal application was maintainable despite the Appellant's failure to amend his pleadings to reflect the second termination letter. Whether the Respondent failed to provide sufficient evidence to justify the termination of the Appellant's services. Whether the Labour Tribunal erred in the computation of back wages regarding the terminal salary and the relevant period. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act No. 43 State Industrial Corporations Act No. 49 Evidence Ordinance Cases Referred to: AG v. Windsor - [24-beav-679] Manager, Ury Group, Passara v. the Democratic Workers Congress - [71-nlr-47] Up Country Distributors (Pvt.) Ltd v. Subasinghe - [1996/v2-sri-lr-330] Associated Cables Ltd. v. Kulatunga - [1999/v2-sri-lr-314] Millars Ltd. v. Ceylon Mercantile Industrial and General Workers Union - [1993/v1-sri-lr-179] Amarajeewa v. University of Colombo - [1993/v2-sri-lr-327] The Caledonian (Ceylon) Tea and Rubber Estates Ltd. v. J. S. Hillman - [79-nlr-421] Sithamparanathan v. Peoples Bank - [1989/v1-sri-lr-124] Jayasuriya v. Sri Lanka State Plantations Corporation - [1995/v2-sri-lr-379] Hatton National Bank v. Perera - [1996/v2-sri-lr-231] Saleem v. Hatton National Bank - [1994/v3-sri-lr-409] Vasudeva Nanayakkara v. K. N. Choksy and Others - [sc-application-209/07] Analysis Issue 1: Maintainability of the Application Rule applied: Section 31C(1) of the Industrial Disputes Act. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that the two termination letters were part of a single continuous process based on the same disciplinary report. The Labour Tribunal is not bound by strict procedural technicalities and must prioritize just and equitable outcomes. The Respondent had admitted the termination in its pleadings, and the subsequent procedural history did not negate the reality of the Appellant's loss of employment. Finding: The application was maintainable; the High Court erred in adopting an overly technical approach. Issue 2: Sufficiency of Evidence Rule applied: Section 101 of the Evidence Ordinance. Court's reasoning: Once the termination of service is admitted, the burden of proof shifts to the employer to justify the dismissal. The Respondent failed to produce the disciplinary inquiry report or lead evidence to substantiate the alleged misconduct. Finding: The termination was unjust and unreasonable due to a total lack of evidentiary justification. Issue 3: Computation of Relief Rule applied: Discretionary powers of the Labour Tribunal under the Industrial Disputes Act. Court's reasoning: The court determined that back wages should be based on the salary at the time of the initial termination (14 November 1996) because that was the date the Appellant was effectively removed from service. However, the period for back wages should extend to the date of reinstatement to ensure a just and equitable remedy. Finding: The Labour Tribunal did not err in the salary basis, but the period for back wages was extended to the date of reinstatement (1 January 2010). Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the determination that the termination was a single process (Issue 1) allowed the court to bypass technical pleading defects and address the merits of the dismissal (Issue 2). The lack of evidence justifying the dismissal (Issue 2) necessitated the exercise of the court's equitable discretion to award reinstatement and back wages (Issue 3). Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the High Court order is vacated. The Labour Tribunal's decision is affirmed with the variation that the Appellant is reinstated as an Engineer (Grade III) effective 1 January 2010, with back wages calculated from 15 November 1996 to 31 December 2009. Keywords Industrial Disputes Act — Labour Tribunal jurisdiction — Maintainability of application — Termination of services — Fundamental rights application — Back wages — Reinstatement — Just and equitable relief — Disciplinary proceedings — Evidence

Parties: W. K. P. Indrajith Rodrigo v. Central Engineering Consultancy Bureau
Bench: Dr. Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N. G. Amaratunga, J.; Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J.
Keywords: Industrial Disputes Act — termination of service — Labour Tribunal — reinstatement — back wages — procedural propriety — just and equitable relief — burden of proof — disciplinary proceedings
Summary: Facts The case revolves around W. K. P. Indrajith Rodrigo, who was employed as an Engineer (Grade III) by the Central Engineering Consultancy Bureau. He initially joined the Bureau on a contract basis in 1986 and was later absorbed into the permanent cadre. In June 1995, he was served with a charge sheet alleging failure to comply with directions. Following a disciplinary inquiry, he was dismissed in November 1996. Rodrigo invoked the jurisdiction of the Labour Tribunal against his dismissal, seeking reinstatement and compensation. The Tribunal ruled in his favor, reinstating him and awarding back wages.
