Parties: Kulanthan Palaniyandy v. G. Premjee Limited
Bench: Shiranee Tilakawardane, J.; K. Sripavan, J.; Ratnayake, J.
Keywords: Civil Procedure Code — substituted service — ex parte judgment — due diligence — presumption of regularity — evasive tactics — burden of proof — High Court — appeal — service of summons
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal made by Kulanthan Palaniyandy, who operates under the name of Paramount Exporters, against G. Premjee Limited. The appeal was directed against an order made by the Commercial High Court of the Western Province on 5 October 2001, which refused to vacate an ex parte judgment and decree due to the Appellant's failure to appear in court. The initial plaint was filed on 19 July 1999, and summons were issued for service through a fiscal officer named Hemachandra. The fiscal officer reported on 15 September 1999 that he was unable to serve the summons personally as the Appellant was allegedly avoiding service. The summons was re-issued on three occasions, but the fiscal officer maintained that the Appellant was deliberately evading service. The Appellant's counsel contested the evidence presented during the inquiry, but the court noted that the Appellant failed to raise pertinent issues during cross-examination. The court emphasized that there is a presumption under Section 114 of the Evidence Ordinance that official acts of the court are performed regularly, and the burden to rebut this presumption lies with the Appellant. The court found that the Appellant had not provided sufficient evidence to demonstrate that he had a valid reason for not being served originally. The fiscal officer's evidence indicated that reasonable diligence was exercised in attempting to serve the summons, which ultimately led to an ex parte trial and decree against the Appellant. Issues Whether the service of summons on the Appellant by way of substituted service was duly executed under Section 60 (2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Whether the order of the learned High Court Judge refusing to vacate the ex parte judgment and decree was erroneous. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Sections 5 and 6 of the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996. Cases Referred to: None explicitly mentioned in the judgment. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the service of summons on the Appellant by way of substituted service was duly executed under Section 60 (2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Rule applied: Section 60 (2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court examined the circumstances surrounding the service of summons and the Appellant's conduct in evading service. The fiscal officer's reports indicated that the Appellant was aware of the summons but chose not to accept them. The court highlighted that the fiscal officer had made multiple attempts to serve the summons, and on each occasion, the Appellant was not available, despite the business premises being open. The court noted that the fiscal officer's opinion that the Appellant was deliberately evading service was credible and supported by the evidence presented. The court also referenced the presumption of regularity in official acts, stating that the Appellant bore the burden of proving otherwise. The court found that the Appellant failed to provide adequate evidence to rebut this presumption or to justify his absence during the service attempts. The court concluded that the service of summons was valid and complied with the requirements of the Civil Procedure Code. Finding: The court upheld the validity of the service of summons and found no error in the High Court's refusal to vacate the ex parte judgment. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as the validity of the service of summons directly impacts the legitimacy of the ex parte judgment. If the summons were not properly served, the Appellant could argue that the judgment was unjust. However, the court's findings regarding the Appellant's evasive behavior and the presumption of regularity in official acts reinforced the decision to uphold the ex parte judgment. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal, affirming the order of the High Court. The court found that the Appellant had not demonstrated any valid grounds to vacate the ex parte judgment, and the service of summons was deemed valid. The Registrar was directed to send the original case record and judgment to the original court for the expeditious conclusion of the case.
Counsel: Kushan D’ Alwis with Chamila Wickramanayake instructed by Sinnadurai Sundaralingam and Balendra for Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant. Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent is absent and unrepresented.

Parties: Fernando v. Tennakoon and Others
Bench: Shiranee Tilakawardane, J; R. A. N. G. Amaratunga, J; Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J
Keywords: Partnership agreement — Business Names Ordinance — Intervention in action — Civil Procedure Code — Prescription Ordinance — Delay and laches — Power of Attorney — Statement of Change — Fraud — Fiduciary relationship
Summary: Facts The Plaintiffs, E.V.T. de Silva and Geetha Amarasinghe, instituted an action in the District Court of Mount Lavinia against the Defendant, Sena Ranjith Fernando, seeking the dissolution and winding up of a partnership known as General Trade Agency. The business was originally registered in 1983 by Tennakoon Mudiyanselage Ranjith Tennakoon and Udaya Silva. Tennakoon migrated to Australia in 1988, having executed a Power of Attorney in favor of E.V.T. de Silva. In 1989, Udaya Silva unilaterally filed a statement of change with the Registrar of Business Names, claiming Tennakoon had ceased to be a partner and replacing him with E.V.T. de Silva. Tennakoon subsequently sought to intervene in the District Court action under Section 18 of the Civil Procedure Code, asserting that he remained a partner and that the partnership agreement required unanimous consent for the admission of new partners. He alleged that the purported change in the partnership register was fraudulent and that he was entitled to the assets of the partnership following the death of Udaya Silva in 2005. The District Court permitted the intervention, and the High Court of Civil Appeal affirmed this order, refusing leave to appeal. The Defendant appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court of Civil Appeal erred in holding that the Intervenient Petitioner (Tennakoon) is a necessary party to the action under Section 18 of the Civil Procedure Code. Whether the Intervenient Petitionerâ€™s claim is barred by laches, inordinate delay, or the Prescription Ordinance. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code No. 2 of 1889 (as amended) Business Names Ordinance No. 6 of 1918 (as amended) Prevention of Frauds Ordinance No. 7 of 1840 (as amended) Prescription Ordinance Cases Referred to: Norris v. Beazley - [1877] 2 CPD 80 Byrne v. Browne and Diplock - [1889] 22 QBD 657 Arumugam Coomaraswamy v. Andiris Appuhamy - [1985/v2-sri-lr-219] Hilda Enid Perera v. Somawathie Lokuge - [2000/v3-sri-lr-200] Analysis Issue 1: Necessary Party Status Rule applied: Section 18 of the Civil Procedure Code No. 2 of 1889. Court's reasoning: The court adopted the "wider construction" of Section 18, as established in Byrne v. Browne and Diplock and affirmed in Arumugam Coomaraswamy v. Andiris Appuhamy. The court reasoned that the objective of the provision is to avoid a multiplicity of actions and to determine the rights of all parties in a single proceeding. Since Tennakoonâ€™s claim involves the same partnership assets and questions of fraud regarding the partnership's composition, his presence is necessary for the court to effectually and completely adjudicate the matter. Finding: The High Court did not err; Tennakoon is a necessary party. Issue 2: Laches and Prescription Rule applied: Prescription Ordinance and the principles of equity regarding laches. Court's reasoning: The court rejected the argument that the claim was prescribed or barred by laches at this stage. It noted that the partnership agreement (A3) required specific procedures for resignation, which were not followed. Furthermore, the court held that allegations of fraud by an agent or partner in a fiduciary relationship cannot be defeated by a plea of laches at a preliminary stage. The court emphasized that these are factual matters to be determined after trial. Finding: The intervention should not be denied on the basis of pre-judgment regarding delay or prescription. Interaction of Issues The findings are reinforcing: the courtâ€™s determination that Tennakoon is a necessary party (Issue 1) necessitates a full trial to resolve the disputed facts regarding his alleged resignation and the validity of the partnership changes (Issue 2). By allowing the intervention, the court ensures that all claims of fraud and entitlement to assets are heard together, preventing inconsistent findings in separate future litigation. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed, and the order of the High Court of Civil Appeal dated 3rd December 2007 is affirmed. The Intervenient Petitioner is to be added as a party defendant in the District Court action. Keywords Partnership agreement — Business Names Ordinance — Intervention in action — Civil Procedure Code — Prescription Ordinance — Delay and laches — Power of Attorney — Statement of Change — Fraud — Fiduciary relationship

Parties: Nona and Another v. Muthubanda and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Sripavan, J; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Unlawful arrest — Detention — Right to personal liberty — Police powers — Breach of peace — Reasonable grounds for arrest — Epilepsy — Alcohol withdrawal — Postmortem Examination Report — Sub-dural haemorrhage — Blunt trauma — Cause of death
Summary: Facts The Petitioners, the mother and sister of the deceased Thisera Sunil Hemachandra, alleged that the deceased was subjected to torture and illegal arrest by the 1st to 3rd Respondents, who were police officers at the Moragahahena Police Station. The Petitioners claimed that the deceased won a lottery and was subsequently harassed by the police and a lottery agent named Ranasinghe Lionel. They alleged that on 21.07.2003, police officers entered the home of the deceased's aunt, Piyawathi, assaulted the deceased, and took him into custody along with one Chanaka. The Petitioners further alleged that the deceased was assaulted while in police custody, leading to his death on 26.07.2003 after surgery at the National Hospital. The Respondents provided a contradictory account, stating that they were acting on a complaint made by Ranasinghe Lionel regarding an imminent assault by his son, Chanaka. The Respondents asserted that when they arrived at the house to arrest Chanaka, the deceased, who was intoxicated, obstructed the police, attempted to assault an officer, and threatened suicide if Chanaka were arrested. The Respondents maintained that the deceased was taken into custody for his own safety and to prevent obstruction of justice. They further alleged that the deceased suffered a fall in the police cell due to an epileptic attack, which resulted in his injuries and subsequent death. Issues Whether the arrest and detention of the deceased were in violation of Article 13(1) and 13(2) of the Constitution. Whether the death of the deceased was caused by police assault in violation of Article 11 of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 11, 13(1), 13(2), 13(4), 17, 126(2). Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka Act No. 21 of 1996. Cases Referred to: R v. Turner - [1971] 2 WLR 56 R v. Howell - [1981] 3 All ER 383 Analysis Issue 1: Legality of arrest and detention Rule applied: Article 13(1) and 13(2) of the Constitution; common law power to arrest for breach of peace as discussed in R v. Howell. Court's reasoning: The court examined contemporaneous police records and the testimony of the officers involved. The records indicated that the police were responding to a legitimate complaint of an imminent breach of peace by Chanaka. The court found that the deceased actively obstructed the police in the discharge of their duties and threatened self-harm. Under the principles established in R v. Howell, police possess the power to arrest to prevent a breach of the peace when there are reasonable grounds for such a belief. The court concluded that the police had reasonable grounds to arrest the deceased for obstruction and for his own protection. Finding: The arrest and detention were lawful and did not violate the Constitution. Issue 2: Cause of death and alleged torture Rule applied: Article 11 of the Constitution; expert opinion evidence standards as discussed in R v. Turner. Court's reasoning: The court noted significant contradictions in the affidavits and statements provided by the Petitioners' witnesses, Piyawathi and Chanaka, regarding the alleged assault. Conversely, the court relied on the Postmortem Examination Report by the Consultant Judicial Medical Officer, which identified the cause of death as acute sub-dural haemorrhage following a head injury from a fall. The report indicated the injury pattern was consistent with a fall, likely caused by a fit resulting from alcohol withdrawal, given the deceased's history of alcohol consumption and liver condition. The court found no medical evidence of a definite assault that would have caused the fatal injury. Finding: The death was not caused by police assault; therefore, no violation of Article 11 occurred. Interaction of Issues The findings on the legality of the arrest and the cause of death are mutually reinforcing. Because the court found the arrest was a lawful response to an imminent breach of peace, the Petitioners' narrative of a targeted, malicious assault by the police lost credibility. Furthermore, the medical evidence regarding the cause of death as an accidental fall, rather than trauma from an assault, rendered the allegations of torture under Article 11 unsubstantiated, thereby confirming the overall legality of the police conduct. Conclusion The court found that the arrest was lawful and that the death of the deceased was not the result of police assault. The application is dismissed. No costs are ordered. Keywords Fundamental rights — Unlawful arrest — Detention — Right to personal liberty — Police powers — Breach of peace — Reasonable grounds for arrest — Epilepsy — Alcohol withdrawal — Postmortem Examination Report — Sub-dural haemorrhage — Blunt trauma — Cause of death

Parties: Mangalasiri v. Seneviratne and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Sripavan, J; Ratnayake, J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Article 11 of the Constitution — Torture — Cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment — Police assault — Medical evidence — Burden of proof — Balance of probability — Time bar — Remand prisoner — Access to legal redress
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, an anesthetist and political organizer, alleged that he was assaulted by the 1st Respondent, an Inspector of Police, at the Kurunegala Police Station on 21.06.2006. The Petitioner had been summoned to the station to provide a statement regarding a money order dispute involving his wife. After recording his statement, the Petitioner claimed the 1st Respondent led him toward the rear of the station, where he was kicked and assaulted, causing him to fall and sustain injuries. Following the incident, the Petitioner was produced before the Magistrate and remanded. The Petitioner reported the assault to the Chief Jailor of the Kegalle Remand Prison and was subsequently admitted to the Kegalle Teaching Hospital on 22.06.2006. Medical records indicated contusions on his back and chest and the presence of red blood cells in his urine. The 1st Respondent denied the allegations, asserting that the Petitioner was taken to the Magistrate's Court in a vehicle driven by his own father and that no assault occurred. The Petitioner filed the present application alleging a violation of Article 11 of the Constitution. The Respondents raised a preliminary objection that the application was time-barred under Article 126(2) of the Constitution. Issues Whether the Petitioner has proven, on a balance of probabilities, that he was subjected to torture or cruel, inhuman, or degrading treatment by the 1st Respondent in violation of Article 11 of the Constitution. Whether the application is time-barred under Article 126(2) of the Constitution, given the Petitioner's period of remand and hospitalization. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 11, 126 Cases Referred to: Silva v. Chairman, Fertilizer Corporation - [1989/v2-sri-lr-393] Velmurugu v. Attorney General - [1981/v1-sri-lr-406] Liyanage v. Upasena - [sc-fr-13/97] Malinda Channa Peiris v. Attorney General - [1994/v1-sri-lr-1] Jayasinghe v. Appuhamy - [sc-fr-15/95] Sudath Silva v. Kodithuwakku - [1987/v2-sri-lr-126] Gerald Perera v. Suraweera - [sc-fr] Namasivayam v. Gunawardene - [1989/v1-sri-lr-394] Saman v. Leeladasa - [1989/v1-sri-lr] Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 11 Rule applied: Article 11 of the Constitution, which prohibits torture or cruel, inhuman, or degrading treatment. Court's reasoning: The court applied the civil standard of proof (balance of probabilities). While the 1st Respondent produced affidavits from witnesses, the court questioned their independence. The court found the medical evidence (Diagnosis Card P7) corroborated the Petitioner's account of injuries, including contusions and hematuria. Relying on Sudath Silva v. Kodithuwakku, the court held that the Petitioner's failure to complain to the Magistrate immediately was not fatal to his case, as he was in custody and lacked legal representation. The court emphasized the inherent difficulty in proving police torture and the state's duty to protect fundamental rights. Finding: The Petitioner successfully proved that his rights under Article 11 were violated by the 1st Respondent. Issue 2: Time-bar under Article 126(2) Rule applied: Article 126(2) of the Constitution, requiring applications to be filed within one month of the alleged infringement. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the Petitioner remained in remand until 04.07.2006 and was hospitalized until that date. Citing Saman v. Leeladasa, the court applied the principle of lex non cogit ad impossibilia, noting that the Petitioner was under restraint and lacked access to legal counsel during the period of detention and hospitalization. Finding: The application was not time-barred as the computation of time began only when the Petitioner was no longer under restraint. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. The court's determination that the Petitioner was in a vulnerable state of detention (relevant to the time-bar issue) directly supported the court's decision to excuse the Petitioner's failure to report the assault to the Magistrate immediately (relevant to the Article 11 violation). Conclusion The court declared that the Petitioner's fundamental rights under Article 11 of the Constitution were violated by the 1st Respondent. The 1st Respondent was ordered to pay the Petitioner Rs. 50,000 as compensation in his personal capacity within one month. Keywords Fundamental rights — Article 11 of the Constitution — Torture — Cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment — Police assault — Medical evidence — Burden of proof — Balance of probability — Time bar — Remand prisoner — Access to legal redress

Parties: The Finance Company PLC v. Agampodi Mahapedige Priyantha Chandana and Others
Bench: Dr. Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N.G. Amaratunga, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: confiscation — Forest Ordinance — hire purchase agreement — absolute owner — knowledge of offence — legal representation — inquiry — burden of proof — judicial discretion — appeal — Magistrate's Court — High Court — special leave to appeal — legal principles — constitutional rights
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal by The Finance Company PLC (the appellant), which is a registered finance company involved in leasing motor vehicles. The appellant was the absolute owner of a vehicle bearing registration No. 227-8130, which was leased to the first respondent, Agampodi Mahapedige Priyantha Chandana. On 20 August 2000, the Beliatta Police arrested the third, fourth, and fifth respondents for transporting timber without a lawful permit, using the appellant's vehicle. The police seized the vehicle, leading to a confiscation inquiry in the Magistrate's Court. The first respondent applied for the vehicle's release, which was granted based on an undertaking to pay outstanding rentals. However, the first respondent failed to pay, prompting the appellant to seek the vehicle's return through the Magistrate's Court. The learned Magistrate ordered the vehicle's confiscation on 25 August 2005, which the High Court affirmed on 30 June 2008. The appellant appealed to the Supreme Court, questioning whether the High Court misdirected itself in law and fact regarding the appellant's ownership and knowledge of the vehicle's use in the offence. Issues Whether the learned High Court Judge misdirected himself in fact and in law in failing to appreciate that the appellant was the absolute owner of the vehicle and that the inquiry should have been limited to whether the appellant was aware of the vehicle's use in the commission of the offence. Applicable Law Statutes: Forest Ordinance, No. 16 of 1907; Code of Criminal Procedure Act, as amended. Cases Referred to: Manawadu v Attorney-General - [1987] 2 Sri L.R. 30; Mercantile Investments Ltd. v Mohamed Mauloom and others - [1998] 3 Sri L.R. 32; Inspector Fernando v Marther - (1932) 1 CLW 249; Mudankotuwa v Attorney-General - [1996] 2 Sri L.R. 77; Nizer v I.P. Wattegama - (1978-79) 2 Sri L.R. 304; Faris v OIC, Police Station, Galenbindunuwewa - [1992] 1 Sri L.R. 167; Rasiah v Thambiraj - (1951) 53 NLR 574. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the learned High Court Judge misdirected himself in fact and in law. Rule applied: The principle that an absolute owner must show that they took all precautions to prevent the vehicle's use in the commission of an offence. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the legal framework surrounding the confiscation of vehicles under the Forest Ordinance, particularly section 40, which allows for confiscation if the vehicle was used in the commission of an offence. The Court emphasized that the absolute owner must demonstrate that they had no knowledge of the offence and had taken all reasonable steps to prevent the vehicle's misuse. The appellant's representative failed to present the hire purchase agreement during the inquiry, which was crucial in establishing the appellant's lack of knowledge and participation in the offence. The Court referenced previous cases, including Manawadu v Attorney-General, which established that an owner must be given an opportunity to be heard regarding confiscation. The Court noted that the learned Magistrate had provided such an opportunity but found that the appellant did not satisfy the burden of proof required to prevent confiscation. The Court highlighted that the appellant's failure to provide evidence of the steps taken to prevent the vehicle's misuse was significant. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the learned High Court Judge did not misdirect himself and affirmed the lower court's decision. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as they hinge on the appellant's status as the absolute owner and the legal obligations that come with that status. The requirement for the appellant to demonstrate a lack of knowledge and preventative measures is central to the determination of whether the confiscation was justified. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal, affirming the High Court's order and the Magistrate's decision to confiscate the vehicle. The Court made no order as to costs, emphasizing the importance of the legal principles surrounding ownership and the responsibilities that accompany it. The judgment reinforces the necessity for absolute owners to actively protect their property from misuse to avoid forfeiture under the law. Keywords confiscation — Forest Ordinance — hire purchase agreement — absolute owner — knowledge of offence — legal representation — inquiry — burden of proof — judicial discretion — appeal — Magistrate's Court — High Court — special leave to appeal — legal principles — constitutional rights
Counsel: I. S. de Silva with Suren de Silva for Claimant-Appellant-Appellant; Riyaz Hamza, SSC, for 6th Respondent

Parties: Jayasekera and Others v. Rasika Lakmini and Others
Bench: J.A.N. De Silva, J.; Saleem Marsoof P.C., J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Special leave to appeal — High Court order — Notice of appeal — Civil Procedure Code — Section 755 — Security for costs — Material prejudice — Section 770 — Partition action
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Petitioner instituted a partition action in the District Court of Panadura against the 1st to 4th Defendants. Following a trial, the District Judge entered judgment allocating shares to the Plaintiff, the 1st Defendant, and the 2nd Defendant. The 3rd Defendant, who had reserved a life interest, passed away prior to the judgment. The 4th Defendant-Appellant-Respondent subsequently filed an appeal against this judgment in the High Court of Civil Appeal. In the notice of appeal and the petition of appeal filed in the District Court, the 4th Defendant failed to name the 1st and 2nd Defendants as respondents. Furthermore, the security bond furnished by the 4th Defendant covered only the costs of the Plaintiff and did not include the 1st and 2nd Defendants. The Plaintiff raised a preliminary objection in the High Court, contending that the appeal was not properly constituted due to the failure to name all necessary parties and the failure to comply with the mandatory provisions of the Civil Procedure Code regarding notice and security. The High Court overruled the preliminary objection, holding that all necessary parties had been noticed. The Plaintiff then sought special leave to appeal to the Supreme Court against the High Court's order. Issues Whether the failure of the 4th Defendant to name the 1st and 2nd Defendants as respondents and to provide security for their costs constitutes a fatal non-compliance with Sections 755 and 758 of the Civil Procedure Code. Whether the Court has the discretion under Section 759(2) of the Civil Procedure Code to grant relief for defects in the notice of appeal where no material prejudice is caused to the respondents. Whether the High Court has the power under Section 770 of the Civil Procedure Code to direct that parties omitted from the appeal be added as respondents. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 128(2), Article 136, Article 154P High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) (Amendment) Act No. 54 of 2006 Partition Act No. 21 of 1977 Civil Procedure Code, Section 755(1), Section 755(2)(a), Section 755(2)(b), Section 758(1), Section 759(2), Section 770 Cases Referred to: Kiri Mudiyanse v. Bandara Menika - [76-nlr-371] Nanayakkara v. Warnakulasuriya - [1993/v2-sri-lr-289] Keerthisiri v. Weerasena - [1997/v1-sri-lr-70] Dias v. Arnolis - [17-nlr-200] Ibrahim v. Beebee - [19-nlr-289] Evans v. Bartlam - [1937/2-a.e.r-646] Gardner v. Jay - [49-ch-d-538] Hope v. Great Western Railway Company - [1910/2-k-b-464] Jenkins v. Bushby - [1891/1-ch-484] Analysis Issue 1: Compliance with Civil Procedure Code Rule applied: Sections 755(1), 755(2)(a), 755(2)(b), and 758(1) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that the 4th Defendant failed to name the 1st and 2nd Defendants as respondents in the notice of appeal and the petition of appeal. Additionally, the security bond provided did not cover the costs of these parties. The Court held that these omissions constituted a failure to comply with the mandatory procedural requirements of the Civil Procedure Code. Finding: The questions of law were answered in the affirmative, confirming that the 4th Defendant failed to comply with the mandatory provisions of the Civil Procedure Code. Issue 2: Discretion to grant relief Rule applied: Section 759(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Court held that Section 759(2) grants the Court wide and discretionary power to provide relief for mistakes, omissions, or defects, provided the respondent has not been "materially prejudiced." Citing Nanayakkara v. Warnakulasuriya and Keerthisiri v. Weerasena, the Court determined that negligence of an attorney does not fetter the Court's discretion to grant relief if it is just and fair. Finding: The Court found that the Plaintiff was not materially prejudiced and that it was appropriate to exercise its discretion to grant relief. Issue 3: Power to add parties Rule applied: Section 770 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Court determined that Section 770 provides an unfettered, judicial discretion to issue notice to parties who were involved in the original action but omitted from the appeal. Given that the rights of the 1st and 2nd Defendants could be affected by the outcome of the partition appeal, it was necessary to include them to safeguard their interests. Finding: The Court directed the High Court to exercise its power under Section 770 to add the 1st and 2nd Defendants as respondents and issue the requisite notices. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected as the Court first established the procedural default (Issue 1), then utilized its discretionary power to cure that default (Issue 2) by invoking the specific mechanism of adding parties (Issue 3) to ensure the appeal is properly constituted without causing material prejudice to the parties involved. Conclusion The Supreme Court set aside the impugned judgment of the High Court. The High Court is directed to add the 1st and 2nd Defendants as respondents to the appeal and issue the necessary notices, thereafter proceeding to conclude the appeal expeditiously. The Plaintiff-Respondent-Appellant is awarded costs of Rs. 15,000 payable by the 4th Defendant-Appellant-Respondent. Keywords Special leave to appeal — High Court order — Notice of appeal — Civil Procedure Code — Section 755 — Security for costs — Material prejudice — Section 770 — Partition action

Parties: Singer Industries (Ceylon) Ltd v. Ceylon Mercantile Industrial & General Workers Union and Others
Bench: J A N de Silva, C.J.; Shiranee Thilakawardena, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: gratuity — collective bargaining — industrial relations — arbitration — Payment of Gratuity Act No. 12 of 1983 — offer and acceptance — judicial approach — industrial disputes — just and equitable — counter offer — expectations of employees — collective agreement — evidence — legal principles — rights of parties
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for special leave to appeal from the judgment of the Court of Appeal regarding an arbitral award concerning gratuity payments to employees of Singer Industries (Ceylon) Ltd. The appellant, Singer Industries, contested an arbitral award that mandated the payment of 3/4ths of a month’s salary as gratuity for each year of service to employees who had served for more than 20 years. The dispute originated from negotiations between the appellant and the Ceylon Mercantile Industrial & General Workers Union (CMU) regarding enhanced gratuity payments beyond what was stipulated in the Payment of Gratuity Act No. 12 of 1983. The appellant argued that no final agreement was reached on enhanced gratuity payments, while the CMU contended that the appellant had shown willingness to pay more than the statutory requirement. The Court of Appeal upheld the arbitrator's decision, leading to the appellant's appeal to the Supreme Court. Issues The primary issues framed for consideration were: Whether the Court of Appeal erred by failing to appreciate that no agreement had ever been finally reached between the CMU and the Petitioner in respect of any enhanced gratuity payments in excess of that mandated by the Gratuity Act No. 12 of 1983? Did the Court of Appeal err by failing to appreciate that the learned arbitrator had erred in law by holding that the petitioner company could be compelled to make gratuity payments to its employees in excess of that mandated by the Payment of Gratuity Act, No. 12 of 1983? Did the Court of Appeal err by failing to appreciate that the arbitrator had erred by holding that the petitioner had made a binding and enforceable offer to make enhanced gratuity payments to its employees in excess of that mandated by the Payment of Gratuity Act No. 12 of 1983? Applicable Law Statutes: Payment of Gratuity Act No. 12 of 1983; Industrial Disputes Act. Cases Referred to: Muthukuda vs Sumanawathie - [1964] 65 NLR 205; Municipal Council of Colombo vs Munasinghe - [71 NLR 223]; Heath & Co. (Ceylon) Ltd. vs Kariyawasam - [71 NLR 382]; All Ceylon Commercial and Industrial Workers’ Union vs Nestle Lanka Ltd. - [1999] 1 SLR 343. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Court of Appeal erred by failing to appreciate that no agreement had ever been finally reached between the CMU and the Petitioner in respect of any enhanced gratuity payments in excess of that mandated by the Gratuity Act No. 12 of 1983? Rule applied: The principles of offer and acceptance in contract law. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the evidence presented during the arbitration proceedings, particularly focusing on the negotiations that took place between the appellant and the CMU. The Court noted that the appellant had made an offer to pay 3/4ths of a month’s salary as gratuity for employees with more than 20 years of service, but this offer was rejected by the CMU, which proposed a higher gratuity payment. The Court emphasized that for a binding agreement to exist, there must be a clear offer and acceptance, which was absent in this case. The arbitrator had incorrectly concluded that the appellant's willingness to consider enhanced gratuity payments constituted a binding agreement. The Supreme Court highlighted that the evidence indicated that the negotiations did not culminate in a final agreement, and the expectations of the employees could not create a legal obligation on the part of the appellant. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that there was no final agreement reached regarding enhanced gratuity payments, and thus the arbitrator's award was flawed. Interaction of Issues The issues were interrelated as they all revolved around the existence of a binding agreement regarding gratuity payments. The failure to establish a final agreement directly impacted the validity of the arbitrator's award and the subsequent judgment of the Court of Appeal. Conclusion The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, set aside the judgment of the Court of Appeal, and quashed the arbitral award. The Court directed that a writ of certiorari be issued to annul the award and the related gazette notification. The appellant was awarded costs of the appeal. Keywords gratuity — collective bargaining — industrial relations — arbitration — Payment of Gratuity Act No. 12 of 1983 — offer and acceptance — judicial approach — industrial disputes — just and equitable — counter offer — expectations of employees — collective agreement — evidence — legal principles — rights of parties
Counsel: Sanjeewa Jayawardena with Ms. Senani Dayaratne for the Petitioner-Appellant. Shirley M Fernando PC with D V Dias and Palitha Perera for the 1st Respondent-Respondent. Mrs. M N B. Fernando, DSG for the 2nd and 3rd Respondent-Respondents.

Parties: Arsakulasooriya v. Tissera
Bench: R. A. N. G. Amaratunga, J; Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J; P. A. Ratnayake, P.C., J
Keywords: Motor vehicle possession — Entitlement to possession — Bona fide purchaser
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent (Respondent) instituted an action for the ejectment of the Defendant-Appellant-Appellant (Appellant) from premises in Kandy, citing the reasonable requirement of the premises for her own residence. The Appellant had been a tenant of the premises since approximately 1969 or 1979. During the proceedings, it was established that the Appellant had entered into a separate lease agreement with the Basnayaka Nilame of the Sri Naatha Devalaya regarding the same premises and had initiated a separate legal action challenging the Respondent's title as landlord. The District Court of Kandy ruled in favor of the Respondent, ordering the ejectment of the Appellant and awarding damages. The court held that the Appellantâ€™s conduct in challenging the Respondent's title and entering into a competing lease agreement constituted a repudiation of the tenancy, thereby forfeiting the protection afforded by the Rent Act. The Civil Appellate High Court of the Central Province affirmed this decision. The Appellant subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Civil Appellate High Court misdirected itself by failing to consider that the plaint did not specify the exact date of the commencement of the tenancy, which is necessary to determine the applicability of Section 22(1)(b) or 22(1)(bb) of the Rent Act. Whether the Civil Appellate High Court misdirected itself by failing to consider the Respondent's failure to prove that notice was served on the Commissioner for National Housing, as required by Section 22(1A) of the Rent Act. Applicable Law Statutes: Rent Act No. 7 of 1972 Civil Procedure Code Cases Referred to: Mudali Appuhamy v. Tikarala - [2-clw-35] Actalina Fonseka v. Dharshani Fonseka - [1989/v2-sri-lr-95] Hanaffi v. Nallamma - [1998/v1-sri-lr-73] Miriam Lawrence v. Arnolda - [1981/v1-sri-lr-232] Wiesinghe v. Nadarajah Eswaran - [1984/v1-sri-lr-33] Muthu Natchia v. Pathuma Natchia - [1-nlr-21] Sundrammal v. Jusey Appu - [36-nlr-40] Pedrick v. Mendis - [62-nlr-47] Hassun v. Nagaria - [75-nlr-335] Edirisinghe v. Patel - [79/v1-nlr-217] Kanthasamy v. Gnanaekeram - [1983/v2-sri-lr-1] Ranasinghe v. Premadharma - [1985/v1-sri-lr-63] Imbuldeniya v. D. de Silva - [1987/v1-sri-lr-367] Analysis Issue 1: Adequacy of pleadings regarding tenancy commencement Rule applied: Section 40(d) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the Appellant did not move to have the plaint returned for amendment under Section 46(2) of the Civil Procedure Code for lack of particulars. Furthermore, once issues are settled at trial, pleadings recede to the background. The exact date of commencement was not a material fact because neither party alleged the tenancy began in 1972, the year that would trigger the specific legal distinction between the relevant sections of the Rent Act. Finding: The court held that the High Court did not misdirect itself; the failure to plead the exact date was not fatal to the action. Issue 2: Failure to serve notice under Section 22(1A) Rule applied: Section 22(1A) of the Rent Act No. 7 of 1972. Court's reasoning: The court applied the principle that a tenant who repudiates the contract of tenancy by challenging the landlord's title and asserting an independent right to possession (as seen in the Appellant's lease with the Devalaya) forfeits the protection of the Rent Act. The court cited Ranasinghe v. Premadharma, which established that a defendant cannot repudiate a contract and simultaneously rely on statutory defenses arising from that same contract. Finding: The court held that the Appellantâ€™s conduct constituted a repudiation of the tenancy, rendering the protective provisions of Section 22(1A) inapplicable. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. Because the Appellantâ€™s conduct (challenging title and asserting a different lease) constituted a repudiation of the tenancy, the technical requirements of the Rent Act—such as the specific date of commencement and the mandatory notice to the Commissioner for National Housing—became moot. The Appellant could not rely on the protections of the Rent Act while simultaneously denying the existence of the landlord-tenant relationship. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed and the decisions of the lower courts are affirmed. The Appellant is ordered to pay costs in the sum of Rs. 25,000/- to the Respondent. Keywords Motor vehicle possession — Entitlement to possession — Bona fide purchaser

Parties: Gunadasa v. Marywathy
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N.G. Amaratunga, J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Land Development Ordinance — Section 60 — Succession to land — Nomination of successor — Registration of nomination — Date of death of permit-holder — Validity of nomination
Summary: Facts The appellant and the respondent are siblings and the children of the late Palate Gedera Jamis, who was the original permit holder of a land alienated under the Land Development Ordinance. The appellant claimed that his father had nominated both the appellant and the respondent as successors to the land. The respondent, however, claimed that their father had subsequently changed this disposition to name only the respondent as the successor. The respondent relied on a letter dated 05.04.1994, which led to an entry in the Register of Permits/Grants on 22.11.1994. The original permit holder, Palate Gedera Jamis, died on 25.05.1994. The appellant filed an action in the District Court of Polonnaruwa seeking a declaration that he was a lawful successor and requesting the eviction of the respondent. The District Court ruled in favor of the appellant, finding that the registration of the respondent as the sole successor occurred after the death of the permit holder and thus failed to comply with the requirements of the Land Development Ordinance. The respondent appealed to the High Court of Civil Appeal, which set aside the District Court judgment. The appellant then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the learned Judges of the High Court erred by failing to consider and apply Section 60 of the Land Development Ordinance regarding the validity of the nomination of a successor. Applicable Law Statutes: Land Development Ordinance Cases Referred to: Madurasinghe v. Madurasinghe - [1988/v2-sri-lr-142] Analysis Issue 1: Application of Section 60 of the Land Development Ordinance Rule applied: Section 60 of the Land Development Ordinance, which mandates that no nomination or cancellation of a nomination of a successor shall be valid unless the document effecting such nomination or cancellation is duly registered before the date of the death of the owner or permit holder. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the original permit holder, Palate Gedera Jamis, died on 25.05.1994. While the respondent relied on a registration entry (P5) dated 22.11.1994 to establish her status as the sole successor, this date was several months after the death of the permit holder. The court held that pursuant to Section 60, any nomination or cancellation must be registered prior to the death of the permit holder to be legally effective. Consequently, the registration of the respondent as the sole successor was invalid under the Ordinance. Finding: The High Court erred in failing to apply Section 60 of the Land Development Ordinance. The nomination of the respondent was invalid because it was not registered before the death of the permit holder. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The order of the High Court dated 07.05.2008 is set aside, and the judgment of the District Court of Polonnaruwa dated 24.10.2001 is affirmed. Keywords Land Development Ordinance — Section 60 — Succession to land — Nomination of successor — Registration of nomination — Date of death of permit-holder — Validity of nomination

Parties: Andiapillai Karuppannapillai and Others v. Raja Rajeswari Visvanathan and Others
Bench: Dr. Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N.G. Amaratunga, J.; K. Sripavan, J.
Keywords: Ceiling on Housing Property Law — legitimate expectation — natural justice — statutory right of appeal — divesting order — tenant rights — administrative duty — communication of decision — inquiry process — surplus house — application timeline — Board of Review — appeal process — judicial review — procedural fairness.
Summary: Facts The case revolves around the appeal filed by the Trustees of Sammangodu Sri Kathirvelayutha Swami Temple, who are the appellants in this matter. They owned a property located at No. 27, Lorensz Road, Colombo 04, which was vested as a surplus house under the Ceiling on Housing Property Law (CHP Law). The appellants had made a declaration to the Commissioner of National Housing, leading to the property being vested as surplus. The original tenant, Kandiah Visvanathan, had a history of tenancy that extended back to his father, who was the original tenant until his death in 1952. Following the death of the original tenant's widow, Kandiah Visvanathan became involved in a series of legal proceedings regarding the property, culminating in an appeal to the Board of Review, which ruled in favor of the respondent, affirming his status as the tenant. The appellants, after years of seeking divestiture of the property, applied to the Commissioner for divesting the ownership of the property to them, which was approved by the Minister on 19 February 2003 and published in the Gazette on 25 February 2003. However, the respondent, who had not been notified of the divesting decision prior to its publication, filed for a writ of certiorari to quash the Minister's approval and the divesting order, claiming a legitimate expectation to purchase the property based on prior communications from the Commissioner. The Court of Appeal ruled in favor of the respondent, leading to the current appeal. Issues The primary issue in this appeal is whether the original respondent, Kandiah Visvanathan, was entitled to a communication of the decision of the Commissioner of National Housing prior to its publication. This raises several sub-issues: Whether the respondent had a legitimate expectation of purchasing the property. Whether the failure to notify the respondent constituted a violation of natural justice. Whether the respondent had a statutory right of appeal to the Board of Review under the CHP Law. Applicable Law Statutes: Ceiling on Housing Property Law (CHP Law) Cases Referred to: Goonewardene and Wijesooriya v Minister of Local Government, Housing and Construction - [1999] 2 Sri L.R. 263; Desmond Perera and Others v Karunaratne, Commissioner of National Housing and Others - [1994] 3 Sri L.R. 316; Desmond De Perera and Others v Karunaratne, Commissioner for National Housing - [1997] 1 Sri L.R. 148. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the respondent was entitled to a communication of the decision of the Commissioner of National Housing prior to its publication. Rule applied: The principle of natural justice and the statutory provisions of the CHP Law. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the procedural history and the legal framework surrounding the CHP Law, particularly focusing on the provisions that govern the rights of tenants and the obligations of the Commissioner. The Court noted that the respondent had not made a timely application to purchase the property under section 9 of the CHP Law, which required such applications to be made within four months of the law's commencement. The respondent's application was made six years late, which the Court found to be a significant factor undermining his claim. The Court emphasized that the CHP Law is clear and unambiguous regarding the timelines for applications, and failure to comply with these timelines negated any entitlement to a communication regarding the divesting decision. The Court also referenced previous cases, including Desmond Perera, where similar issues were adjudicated, reinforcing the notion that tenants must act within the prescribed timeframes to assert their rights. The Court further analyzed the concept of legitimate expectation, concluding that it cannot exist in isolation from the statutory framework. The Court found that while the Commissioner had previously communicated with the respondent regarding the assessed value of the property, this did not confer a legal right to purchase the property, especially given the lack of a valid application. Finding: The Court concluded that the respondent was not entitled to a communication of the decision of the Commissioner prior to its publication, as he had failed to comply with the necessary procedural requirements. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as the determination of the respondent's entitlement to communication hinges on the validity of his application under the CHP Law. The failure to apply within the stipulated timeframe directly impacts the legitimacy of his expectation and his claims of natural justice violations. The Court's analysis of these interconnected issues illustrates the importance of adhering to statutory procedures in asserting rights. Conclusion The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, setting aside the judgment of the Court of Appeal dated 21 August 2006. The Court found that the respondent was not entitled to the relief sought, as he had not complied with the procedural requirements of the CHP Law. The Court made no order as to costs, reflecting the complexities of the case and the legal principles involved. Keywords Ceiling on Housing Property Law — legitimate expectation — natural justice — statutory right of appeal — divesting order — tenant rights — administrative duty — communication of decision — inquiry process — surplus house — application timeline — Board of Review — appeal process — judicial review — procedural fairness.
Counsel: Wijayadasa Rajapakse, PC, with Nilantha Kumarage for 2nd Respondent-Appellants. A. Gnanathasan, ASG, PC, with N. Wigneswaran, SC, for 3rd, 4th and 5th Respondents-Respondents. Dr. Sunil Coorey for Substituted Respondents-Petitioners-Respondents.

Parties: Elgitread Lanka (Private) Limited v. Bino Tyres (Private) Limited
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J; P. A. Ratnayake, P.C., J; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Arbitration agreement — Jurisdiction of the Commercial High Court — Arbitration Act No. 11 — Section 5 of the Arbitration Act — Section 4 of the Arbitration Act — Appointment of arbitrators — Frustration of contract — Public policy — Arbitrability
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent, Bino Tyres (Private) Limited, instituted an action in the Commercial High Court of Colombo against the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant, Elgitread Lanka (Private) Limited, seeking damages for an alleged breach of a Franchise Agreement dated 2nd June 2005. The agreement included Clause 14, which stipulated that any dispute arising from the agreement should be referred to the Sri Lanka Chamber of Commerce and Industry, Colombo, for binding arbitration. The Appellant filed an answer objecting to the jurisdiction of the Commercial High Court, citing Section 5 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 and the existence of the arbitration clause. The Respondent contended that the arbitration clause was void or frustrated because the entity named in the clause, the Sri Lanka Chamber of Commerce and Industry, Colombo, did not exist. The Commercial High Court ruled in favor of the Respondent, holding that the arbitration agreement was void ab initio due to the non-existence of the named institution and that the court retained jurisdiction to hear the matter. The Appellant appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether Clause 14 of the Franchise Agreement constitutes a valid arbitration agreement under Section 3 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995, thereby triggering the court's lack of jurisdiction under Section 5 of the Act. Whether the Commercial High Court erred in failing to apply Section 4 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 regarding the mandatory nature of arbitration for disputes covered by an arbitration agreement. Whether the Commercial High Court erred in failing to apply Section 7 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995, which provides mechanisms for the appointment of arbitrators in the absence of an agreed procedure. Applicable Law Statutes: Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 Civil Procedure Code (Chapter 101) High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996 Cases Referred to: Astro Vencedor Compania Naviera S.A. v. Mabanaft G. M.B.H. - [1970/v2-lloyds-reports-267] Taylor v. Caldwell - [1863/3-b-&-s-826] Scott v. Avery - [1836/5-hl-cas-811] Thompson v. Charmock - [1799/8-term-rep-139] Hotel Galaxy (Pvt) Ltd. v. Mercantile Hotels - [1987/v1-sri-lr-5] Mangistaumunaigaz Oil Production Association v. United World Trade Inc. - [1995/v1-lloyds-reports-617] Onex Corp. v. Ball Corp. - [1994/v12-b.l.r.-2nd-151] Star Shipping AS v. China National Foreign Trade Transportation Corporation - [1993/v2-lloyds-reports-445] SONATRACH v. Distrigas - [80-br-606] Mitsubishi Motors Corp. v. Soler Chrysler Plymouth Inc. - [1985/473-u.s.-614] Eco Swiss China Time Ltd v. Benetton International N.V. - [1999/e.c.r.-i-3055] ET Plus S.A. v. Jean-Paul Welter & The Channel Tunnel Group Ltd. - [2005/ewhc-2115-comm] Rodriguez de Quijas v. Shearson/American Express - [490-us-477] Philip Alexander Securities v. Bamberger - [1997/eulr-63] Light Weight Body Armour Ltd. v. Sri Lanka Army - [2007/balr-10] Nallaratnam Singarasa v. Attorney General - [sc-spl-la-182/99] Sunila Abeysekera v. Ariya Rubasinghe - [2000/v1-sri-lr-314] P. Beatrice Perera v. Commissioner of National Housing - [77-nlr-361] Analysis Issue 1: Validity of the Arbitration Agreement Rule applied: Section 3 and Section 50 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. Court's reasoning: The court held that Clause 14 clearly manifested the parties' intent to arbitrate. The non-existence of the specific institution named does not invalidate the agreement, as the essence of an arbitration agreement is the consent to arbitrate, not the naming of a specific forum. The court leaned in favor of giving effect to the parties' intention. Finding: Clause 14 is a valid arbitration agreement. Issue 2: Mandatory Nature of Arbitration Rule applied: Section 4 and Section 5 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. Court's reasoning: Section 4, read with Section 5, mandates that where parties have agreed to arbitrate and one party objects to the court's jurisdiction, the court must give effect to that agreement unless the matter is contrary to public policy or not arbitrable. No such exceptions were present here. Finding: The High Court erred by not giving effect to the mandatory nature of the arbitration agreement. Issue 3: Appointment of Arbitrators Rule applied: Section 7 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. Court's reasoning: Section 7 provides a comprehensive default mechanism for the appointment of arbitrators when the parties' agreed procedure fails or is absent. The court noted that the High Court failed to consider these provisions, which are designed to prevent the frustration of arbitration proceedings. Finding: The High Court misdirected itself by failing to apply the statutory appointment procedures. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected: the validity of the arbitration clause (Issue 1) triggers the mandatory requirement to arbitrate (Issue 2), which is supported by the statutory procedural mechanisms (Issue 3) that cure any perceived frustration of the agreement. Together, these findings confirm that the High Court lacked jurisdiction. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the order of the Commercial High Court dated 7th October 2008 is set aside. The action filed by the Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent is dismissed for lack of jurisdiction. No costs are ordered. Keywords Arbitration agreement — Jurisdiction of the Commercial High Court — Arbitration Act No. 11 — Section 5 of the Arbitration Act — Section 4 of the Arbitration Act — Appointment of arbitrators — Frustration of contract — Public policy — Arbitrability

Parties: Jamaldeen Abdul Latheef and Koya Mohideen Nizardeen v. Abdul Majeed Mohamed Mansoor and Abdul Majeed Mohamed Nizar
Bench: Hon. J.A.N. de Silva, C.J.; Hon. Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J.; Hon. P. A. Ratnayake, P.C., J.
Keywords: declaration of title — Power of Attorney — rei vindicatio — notarial attestation — Evidence Ordinance — possession — ownership — prescription — land disputes — Sri Lankan law — legal representation — court procedure — surveyor's report — burden of proof — authenticity of documents
Summary: Facts This case arises from an action for declaration of title initiated by the Petitioner-Respondent-Respondents (hereinafter referred to as the Respondents) in the District Court of Anuradhapura in December 1989. The Respondents claimed ownership of a four-acre land known as 'Palugahakumbura' located in Mahawela, Pandiyankulama village, Anuradhapura District, based on Deed No. 6165 dated 9th February 1987 (P1). This deed was executed by Ibrahim Lebbe Noor Lebbai, who purported to act as the Attorney for Meydeen Sadakku Mohideen Abdul Cader, under a Power of Attorney (P7) dated 30th October 1981. The Respondents alleged they purchased the land for Rs. 20,000 and sought a declaration of title and a permanent injunction against the Defendant-Appellant-Appellants (hereinafter referred to as the Appellants), who disputed their title and attempted to prevent the Respondents from cultivating the land. The Appellants denied any disturbance and claimed ownership of a different land named 'Nilaththu Patti Wayal.' The trial involved testimonies from various witnesses, including surveyors, and culminated in a judgment by the District Court favoring the Respondents, leading to an appeal by the Appellants. The Court of Appeal upheld the District Court's decision, prompting the Appellants to seek special leave to appeal to the Supreme Court. Issues The Supreme Court was tasked with addressing several key issues: Whether the Power of Attorney (P7) was duly proved. Whether Deed No. 6165 (P1) effectively conveyed title to the Respondents. Whether the Court of Appeal erred in affirming the District Judge's finding that the Respondents established title to the land. Whether the Court of Appeal failed to consider the evaluation of evidence regarding prescription. Applicable Law Statutes: Evidence Ordinance No. 14 of 1895; Prevention of Frauds Ordinance No. 7 of 1840; Notaries Ordinance No. 1 of 1907; Powers of Attorney Ordinance No. 4 of 1902. Cases Referred to: Wanigaratne v. Juwanis Appuhamy (1962) 65 NLR 167; Dharmadasa v. Jayasena [1997] 3 Sri LR 327; Mudalihamy v. Appuhamy (1891) CLRep 67; Allis Appu v. Edris Hamy (1894) 3 SCR 87. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Power of Attorney (P7) was duly proved. Rule applied: The requirement for proving documents under the Evidence Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court emphasized the necessity of proving the authenticity of the Power of Attorney marked P7, which was executed in India. The Respondents failed to call any attesting witnesses to testify regarding the execution of P7, nor was there any evidence to establish the identity of the executant. The Court noted that the notary public who attested P7 did not know the executant personally and relied on information from witnesses who were not called to testify. The Court highlighted that the failure to produce the original document and the lack of proper authentication rendered P7 inadmissible as evidence. The Court concluded that the Power of Attorney had not been duly proved, thus impacting the validity of Deed P1. Finding: The Power of Attorney marked P7 was not proved as required by law. Issue 2: Whether Deed No. 6165 (P1) effectively conveyed title to the Respondents. Rule applied: The legal requirements for the conveyance of property under Sri Lankan law. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the language of Deed P1 and noted that it did not clearly indicate that Noor Lebbai was acting as an attorney on behalf of Abdul Cader. The Court found that the deed appeared to convey ownership directly from Noor Lebbai, who did not possess the title to the land. Furthermore, the Court pointed out that there was insufficient evidence to establish that Abdul Cader had title to the land, as the Respondents failed to provide documentation proving the devolution of title from the original owner, Sadakku. The Court concluded that without a valid Power of Attorney, Noor Lebbai could not convey any title to the Respondents through Deed P1. Finding: Deed P1 did not operate to convey title to the Respondents. Issue 3: Whether the Court of Appeal erred in affirming the District Judge's finding. Rule applied: The standard of proof required in a rei vindicatio action. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court reiterated that the identity of the property is crucial in a vindicatory action. The Court noted discrepancies in the descriptions of the land claimed by both parties and emphasized the importance of clear identification of the property. The Court found that the Respondents had not established the identity of the land they claimed, which was essential for their action. The Court concluded that the Court of Appeal erred in affirming the District Judge's finding without addressing the issues of title and identity of the property adequately. Finding: The Court of Appeal's affirmation was erroneous. Interaction of Issues The issues were interrelated, as the validity of the Power of Attorney directly affected the conveyance of title through Deed P1. Additionally, the failure to establish the identity of the property undermined the Respondents' claim in a rei vindicatio action. Conclusion The Supreme Court ruled in favor of the Appellants, concluding that the Power of Attorney was not duly proved, and consequently, Deed P1 did not convey title to the Respondents. The Court of Appeal's decision was overturned, and the matter was remitted for further proceedings consistent with the Supreme Court's findings. Keywords: declaration of title — Power of Attorney — rei vindicatio — notarial attestation — Evidence Ordinance — possession — ownership — prescription — land disputes — Sri Lankan law — legal representation — court procedure — surveyor's report — burden of proof — authenticity of documents
Counsel: Faisz Musthapha, P.C., with N. M. Shaheed for the Defendant-Appellant-Appellants. W. Dayaratne, P.C., with R. Jayawardane for the Petitioner-Respondent-Respondents.

Parties: Sarath Fonseka v. Mahinda Rajapakse and Others
Bench: J.A.N. de Silva, C.J.; Dr. Bandaranayake, J.; Sripavan, J.; Ratnayake, J.; Imam, J.
Keywords: Presidential Elections Act — Election Petition — Jurisdiction — Corrupt Practices — Preliminary Objections — Non-joinder of Parties — Material Facts — Affidavit Requirements — Election Law — Statutory Interpretation — Judicial Review — Election Integrity — Voting Rights — Legal Standing — Election Results
Summary: Facts The case revolves around the presidential election held on January 26, 2010, where Sarath Fonseka, the petitioner, contested against Mahinda Rajapakse, the first respondent, among others. Fonseka alleged various corrupt practices and irregularities that purportedly affected the election's integrity. He claimed that the election was marred by general intimidation, bribery, and false statements broadcasted by state-controlled media, which he argued prevented voters from exercising their franchise freely. The petitioner filed an election petition seeking to declare the election void, assert that the return of the first respondent was undue, and request a scrutiny of the ballots. However, the petition faced preliminary objections regarding its maintainability, particularly concerning the failure to join necessary parties and the lack of material facts as required by the Presidential Elections Act. Issues Whether the reliefs sought in the petition are misconceived in law and cannot be granted by the Court. Whether the petitioner has failed to join necessary parties as Respondents. Whether the petitioner has failed to furnish material facts in accordance with Section 96(c) of the Presidential Elections Act. Whether the petition conforms to the requirements of Section 96(d) of the Act regarding particulars of corrupt practices alleged. Applicable Law Statutes: Presidential Elections Act No. 15 of 1981. Cases Referred to: Nanayakkara vs. Kiriella - [1985] 2 SLR 391; Gamini Athukorale vs. Chandrika Bandaranaike Cumaratunga - [2001] 1 SLR 60; Kobbekaduwa vs. Jayewardene - [1983] 1 SLR 416; Bandaranaike vs. Premadasa - [1989] 1 SLR 240; Dhartipakar Madanlal Agarwal vs. Shri Rajiv Ghandi - [1987] 3 SCR 369; Udhav Singh vs. Madhav Rao Scindia - [1977] 1 Supreme Court case. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the reliefs sought in the petition are misconceived in law and cannot be granted by the Court. Rule applied: Section 94 of the Presidential Elections Act. Court's reasoning: The Court emphasized that the reliefs available under the Presidential Elections Act are strictly defined. The petitioner failed to seek a declaration that the election was void as required by Section 94(a). The Court noted that the petitioner’s request for a declaration of the election being void was not included in the prayer of the petition, which is a fundamental flaw. The Court reiterated that election petitions are statutory proceedings, and the statutory requirements must be strictly adhered to. The absence of a specific request for the relief under Section 94(a) precludes the Court from granting any other reliefs. The Court further stated that the language of the statute must be interpreted in its plain meaning, and the petitioner cannot seek relief beyond what is specified in the Act. Finding: The Court concluded that the reliefs sought by the petitioner were misconceived in law and could not be granted. Issue 2: Whether the petitioner has failed to join necessary parties as Respondents. Rule applied: Section 95(1)(b) of the Presidential Elections Act. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the petitioner failed to join several necessary parties, including media institutions alleged to have committed corrupt practices. The Court highlighted that the failure to join necessary parties is a fatal irregularity that renders the entire petition invalid. The Court referred to previous judgments that established the importance of joining all parties against whom allegations of corrupt practices are made. The Court emphasized that the provisions of Section 95 are mandatory, and non-compliance with these provisions results in the petition being dismissed. Finding: The Court held that the failure to join necessary parties was a fundamental flaw, leading to the dismissal of the petition. Issue 3: Whether the petitioner has failed to furnish material facts in accordance with Section 96(c) of the Presidential Elections Act. Rule applied: Section 96(c) of the Presidential Elections Act. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the petition and found that the petitioner did not provide a concise statement of material facts supporting the allegations of corrupt practices. The Court noted that the allegations were vague and lacked specificity, which is essential for the respondent to understand the case against them. The Court referred to the strict standards applied in similar cases, emphasizing that the failure to plead material facts is fatal to the election petition. The Court concluded that the petitioner’s pleadings did not meet the statutory requirements, rendering the petition defective. Finding: The Court determined that the petition did not comply with the requirements of Section 96(c), leading to its dismissal. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as the failure to seek proper reliefs, join necessary parties, and provide material facts collectively undermine the validity of the petition. Each issue highlights a different aspect of the statutory requirements that must be met for an election petition to be maintainable. The cumulative effect of these failures led the Court to dismiss the petition in limine. Conclusion The Supreme Court upheld the preliminary objections raised by the respondents and dismissed the petition in limine, ordering no costs. The Court emphasized the importance of adhering to the statutory requirements set forth in the Presidential Elections Act, reiterating that election petitions must be strictly compliant with the law. The judgment serves as a reminder of the rigorous standards that govern election petitions in Sri Lanka, ensuring the integrity of the electoral process. Keywords Presidential Elections Act — Election Petition — Jurisdiction — Corrupt Practices — Preliminary Objections — Non-joinder of Parties — Material Facts — Affidavit Requirements — Election Law — Statutory Interpretation — Judicial Review — Election Integrity — Voting Rights — Legal Standing — Election Results

Parties: Akarawita and Others v. Wickramasinghe and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N.G. Amaratunga, J; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Salary scales — Librarians Service — Sri Lanka Administrative Service — Public Administration Circulars — National Salaries and Cadre Commission — Administrative action — Salary anomaly
Summary: Facts The petitioners, who were the only three officers serving in the Supra Grade of the Sri Lanka Librarians Service, challenged the decision of the 5th to 19th respondents to place them in the MN-7 salary scale under Public Administration Circular No. 06/2006. The petitioners contended that this placement constituted a demotion and a violation of their fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. They argued that their duties were comparable to those of Assistant Commissioners, Assistant Directors, and officers in Class II Grade II of the Sri Lanka Administrative Service, and that they had historically received a higher salary than the latter. The petitioners sought a directive to be placed in a salary scale comparable to that of Class II Grade II of the Sri Lanka Administrative Service. They further alleged that the new salary structure deprived them of privileges, such as duty-free motor vehicle permits, which they had previously enjoyed. The respondents maintained that the salary restructuring was a policy decision based on the 2006 Budget proposals, which consolidated numerous salary scales into a smaller number of categories. The respondents further asserted that the petitioners were not comparable to the Sri Lanka Administrative Service and that, following a subsequent adjustment, the petitioners' salary remained higher than that of the entry-level officers of the Sri Lanka Administrative Service. Issues Whether the placement of Supra Grade Librarians in the MN-7 salary scale under Public Administration Circular No. 06/2006 violated the petitioners' fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the Supra Grade Librarians and the officers of the Sri Lanka Administrative Service constitute a single class of equals for the purpose of salary parity. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Cases Referred to: Ram Krishna Dalmia v. Justice Tendolkar - [AIR 1958 S.C. 538] Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution, which guarantees equality before the law and equal protection of the law, prohibiting arbitrary discrimination between equals. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the salary restructuring under Public Administration Circular No. 06/2006 was a broad policy initiative aimed at consolidating 126 salary scales into 37. The court noted that the 19th respondent had subsequently adjusted the placement of Supra Grade Librarians to the 16th step of the MN-7 scale, resulting in a salary of Rs. 24,630/-, which exceeded the Rs. 22,935/- salary of Class II Grade II officers of the Sri Lanka Administrative Service. Consequently, the court found no evidence of arbitrary treatment or discrimination. Finding: The petitioners failed to establish that their fundamental rights under Article 12(1) were violated. Issue 2: Comparability of Services Rule applied: The principle that classifications are permissible if they are founded upon intelligible differentia and are not arbitrary. Court's reasoning: The court distinguished the Sri Lanka Librarians Service from the Sri Lanka Administrative Service, noting that they are governed by different administrative minutes and fall under different regulatory controls. The court rejected the petitioners' claim of parity, finding that the two services represent distinct categories of public employment. Furthermore, the court clarified that the loss of vehicle permit privileges was a separate policy decision by the government and not a consequence of the salary scale placement. Finding: The Supra Grade Librarians and the Sri Lanka Administrative Service officers are not equals for the purpose of salary parity. Interaction of Issues The court's finding that the two services are not comparable (Issue 2) directly informs the conclusion that there was no violation of the right to equality (Issue 1). Because the groups are not equals, the state is not constitutionally required to provide identical salary scales. Furthermore, the factual evidence that the petitioners' salary remained higher than the comparison group rendered the claim of discriminatory demotion moot. Conclusion The application is dismissed. There is no order as to costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Salary scales — Librarians Service — Sri Lanka Administrative Service — Public Administration Circulars — National Salaries and Cadre Commission — Administrative action — Salary anomaly

Parties: Somapala and Karapincha v. Wanasundara
Bench: R.A.N.G. Amaratunge, J.; Saleem Marsoof, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Appeal — Leave to appeal — District Court order — Civil Procedure Code Section 328 — Dispossession — Ejectment — Commission to survey land — Maintainability of appeal — Academic exercise
Summary: Facts The original plaintiff in District Court Ratnapura case number 2129/L sought a declaration of title to an undivided 1/3 share of a land and an order for ejectment. The Supreme Court eventually declared the substituted plaintiff entitled to the share, and the District Court issued a writ of possession. During the execution of the writ, a licensed surveyor demarcated the land, and the defendants vacated the premises. The petitioner-appellant, who was not a party to the original action, claimed that the surveyor had included a portion of his own land in the delivery of possession and complained to the Fiscal. The petitioner-appellant subsequently filed an application in the District Court under Section 328 of the Civil Procedure Code, alleging he was dispossessed of his own land during the execution of the writ. The District Judge allowed the petitioner-appellant's request for a commission to survey the land. The substituted plaintiff appealed this order to the Civil Appellate High Court, which set aside the District Judge's order, concluding that the petitioner-appellant had failed to establish dispossession from the land described in the writ and that his remedy lay in a separate action. Following this, the District Court terminated the Section 328 proceedings on August 1, 2008. The petitioner-appellant filed an appeal in the Supreme Court against the Civil Appellate High Court's decision but failed to challenge the District Court's termination order through appropriate appellate or revisionary procedures. Issues Whether the Civil Appellate High Court erred in its interpretation of Section 328 of the Civil Procedure Code and the issuance of a commission for local investigation. Whether the appeal is maintainable given that the underlying Section 328 application in the District Court has been terminated and no longer exists as a live proceeding. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 328 Civil Procedure Code, Section 839 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Interpretation of Section 328 and Commission Rule applied: Section 328 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the Civil Appellate High Court had determined that the petitioner-appellant failed to prove he was dispossessed from the land subject to the decree and that Section 328 does not cover dispossession from land outside the scope of the writ. However, the court found that because the underlying application had been terminated by the District Court, the substantive legal questions regarding the scope of Section 328 and the propriety of the commission had become moot. Finding: The court declined to rule on these questions as they no longer held practical significance. Issue 2: Maintainability of the Appeal Rule applied: General principles of appellate jurisdiction regarding live legal proceedings. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the District Court terminated the Section 328 proceedings on August 1, 2008, following the High Court's order. The petitioner-appellant failed to challenge this termination order through a separate appeal or revision. Consequently, there is no pending application in the District Court. The court held that it will not pronounce on questions of law that do not have practical significance to a pending or live legal proceeding. Finding: The appeal is not maintainable as it constitutes an academic exercise. Interaction of Issues The finding on the maintainability of the appeal (Issue 2) rendered the resolution of the substantive legal questions regarding Section 328 (Issue 1) unnecessary. Because the underlying proceeding was terminated and the termination order remained unchallenged, any decision on the merits of the Section 328 application would have no practical effect, thereby precluding the court from exercising its appellate function. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed without costs. The court held that the petitioner-appellant's failure to challenge the District Court's termination order rendered the appeal an academic exercise, making it unnecessary to address the remaining questions of law. Keywords Appeal — Leave to appeal — District Court order — Civil Procedure Code Section 328 — Dispossession — Ejectment — Commission to survey land — Maintainability of appeal — Academic exercise

Parties: Car Plan Ltd. and Others v. K.L.G.T. Perera and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J.; Ratnayake, J.; Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Customs Ordinance — false declaration — CIF value — forfeiture — right hand drive — KIA Sportage — evidence — mitigation of forfeiture — Writ of Mandamus — legal principles — statutory interpretation — appeal dismissal — Supreme Court jurisdiction
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for special leave to appeal against a judgment of the Court of Appeal concerning the importation of vehicles by Car Plan Ltd., a company involved in the business of importing and selling motor vehicles. The petitioners, including the Managing Director and a Director of Car Plan Ltd., contested the decision made by the Customs authorities regarding the importation of 15 KIA Grand Sportage vehicles. The core issue was whether the petitioners had made a false declaration to the Sri Lanka Customs by failing to include the conversion cost of US $1500 for each vehicle imported. The petitioners had imported these vehicles under the premise that they were Right Hand Drive models, which required conversion from the Left Hand Drive models manufactured by KIA.
