The CIF price of the Left Hand Drive model was US $10,920, while the total CIF value for the Right Hand Drive model, including the conversion cost, was claimed to be US $12,420. The petitioners argued that they had complied with all legal requirements and that the conversion cost should not have been included in the CIF value declared to Customs. However, the evidence presented during the customs inquiry indicated that the petitioners had issued two pro forma invoices for each vehicle, one for the CIF value of the Left Hand Drive model and another for the conversion cost, which raised suspicions of undervaluation. The inquiry revealed that the petitioners had been aware of the need to declare the full CIF value, including the conversion cost, and had attempted to mislead the Customs authorities. Issues The primary issues before the Supreme Court were: Whether the petitioners made a false declaration to the Sri Lanka Customs regarding the CIF value of the vehicles imported. Whether the vehicles were liable to forfeiture under the Customs Ordinance due to the false declaration. Whether the Court of Appeal's judgment should be upheld or overturned. Applicable Law Statutes: Customs Ordinance, Sections 52, 119, 163, 164, 165. Cases Referred to: Culasubadhra vs. University of Colombo - [1985 1 SLR 244]. Analysis Issue 1: False Declaration Rule applied: Customs Ordinance, Sections 52 and 119. Court's reasoning: The Court meticulously examined the evidence presented during the customs inquiry, highlighting the discrepancies in the petitioners' declarations. The judgment emphasized that the petitioners had issued two pro forma invoices, one for the Left Hand Drive model and another for the conversion cost, which was a clear indication of their intent to mislead the Customs authorities. The Court noted that the petitioners had been aware of the legal requirement to declare the full CIF value, including the conversion cost, and had failed to do so. The evidence from the inquiry indicated that the CIF value of the Right Hand Drive KIA Sportage was indeed US $12,420, which included the conversion cost. The Court referenced the letter from KIA Motor Corporation, which explicitly stated that the conversion cost should be included in the CIF value. The petitioners' argument that the conversion cost should not be included was dismissed as unfounded. The Court further noted that the petitioners had attempted to downplay the significance of the conversion cost, despite it being a crucial aspect of the vehicle's value. The judgment underscored the importance of accurate declarations to the Customs authorities, stating that any attempt to undervalue goods imported into the country undermines the integrity of the customs system. The Court concluded that the petitioners had indeed made a false declaration, which warranted the forfeiture of the vehicles under the relevant sections of the Customs Ordinance. Finding: The Court found that the petitioners had made a false declaration regarding the CIF value of the vehicles, leading to their liability for forfeiture. Interaction of Issues The issues were interrelated, as the determination of whether a false declaration was made directly impacted the question of forfeiture. The Court's analysis of the evidence and the legal requirements for declaring the CIF value were crucial in establishing the basis for the forfeiture of the vehicles. The Court's findings on the false declaration also informed its decision to uphold the Court of Appeal's judgment, as the legal implications of the false declaration were clear and supported by the evidence presented. Conclusion The Supreme Court affirmed the judgment of the Court of Appeal, concluding that the petitioners had made a false declaration regarding the CIF value of the vehicles imported. The appeal was dismissed, and the Court found no grounds to interfere with the lower court's decision. The judgment reinforced the importance of accurate declarations to the Customs authorities and the legal consequences of failing to comply with statutory requirements. The Court also noted that while the Director General of Customs had the authority to mitigate forfeiture under certain circumstances, the specific case did not warrant such action. The outcome underscored the need for compliance with customs regulations and the consequences of misrepresentation in import declarations. Keywords Customs Ordinance — false declaration — CIF value — forfeiture — right hand drive — KIA Sportage — evidence — mitigation of forfeiture — Writ of Mandamus — legal principles — statutory interpretation — appeal dismissal — Supreme Court jurisdiction
Counsel: Shibly Aziz, P.C., with Nigel Hatch, P.C., and Aneeta Perera instructed by Julius & Creasy for the Petitioner-Petitioner. Deputy Solicitor General U Y Wijetilleke with Rajitha Perera, S.C., for the Respondent-Respondents

Parties: Sivalingam v. Jayasekera and Others
Bench: Shiranee Tilakawardane, J; Saleem Marsoof, J.; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Article 11 — Article 12(1) — Article 13(1) — Article 13(2) — Arrest — Detention — Torture — Confession — Emergency Regulations — Judicial Medical Officer — LTTE involvement
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Edward Sivalingam, was a resident of Killinochchi who alleged that he was arrested by the Vavuniya Police on August 4, 2006, while staying at the YMHA in Vavuniya. He was subsequently handed over to the Criminal Investigations Department (CID) and transferred to Colombo. The Petitioner alleged that he was subjected to brutal assault and torture by CID officers during his interrogation, resulting in injuries to his right arm, head, and back. He further claimed that he was forced to sign a confession written in Sinhala, a language he purportedly did not understand. The Petitioner was detained at the CID headquarters for eight months and subsequently at the Boossa Detention Camp for four months before being produced before the Magistrate of Balapitiya. Throughout his detention, the Petitioner claimed he was denied proper medical care and that his requests for assistance were ignored. The Respondents, including the 1st Respondent (a Sub Inspector of the CID), denied all allegations of torture and maintained that the Petitioner was an active member of the LTTE. The Respondents produced evidence, including identity cards recovered from the Petitioner at the time of arrest and contemporaneous police records, to justify the arrest and detention. The Petitioner's detention was authorized under various Detention Orders issued pursuant to the Emergency (Miscellaneous Provisions and Powers) Regulations No. 1. Issues Whether the arrest and detention of the Petitioner violated his fundamental rights under Article 12(1) and Article 13(1) of the Constitution. Whether the Petitioner was subjected to torture in violation of Article 11 of the Constitution. Whether the Petitionerâ€™s detention violated Article 13(2) of the Constitution regarding the legality of the place and duration of custody. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 11, 12(1), 13(1), 13(2), 126 Evidence Ordinance, Section 114 Emergency (Miscellaneous Provisions and Powers) Regulations No. 1 Cases Referred to: Fox, Campbell and Hartley v. U.K. - [1990] McKee v. Chief Constable for Northern Ireland - [1984] Abhinandan Jha v. Dinesh Mishra - [AIR 1968 SC 117] Director, C.B.I v. Niyamavedi - [1995 AIR SCW 2212] Analysis Issue 1: Legality of Arrest and Detention Rule applied: The court applied the standard of "reasonable suspicion" for arrests and considered the totality of evidence, including contemporaneous police records and recovered items. Court's reasoning: The court found that the Petitioner was in possession of an LTTE-issued travel permit and other identity cards at the time of his arrest. This, combined with information linking the Petitioner to a lorry containing a large cache of arms and explosives, provided a reasonable basis for the police to suspect the Petitioner of involvement in terrorist activities. The court held that the police acted within their mandate to investigate. Finding: There was no violation of Article 12(1) or Article 13(1) of the Constitution. Issue 2: Allegations of Torture Rule applied: The court applied the balance of probabilities test, emphasizing that the burden of proof lies with the Petitioner and requires cogent, consistent evidence to rebut the presumption of regularity of official acts under Section 114 of the Evidence Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The court noted that multiple Medico-Legal Examination Reports from different doctors found no injuries consistent with the Petitioner's claims of torture. Furthermore, contemporaneous records of ICRC visits and journal entries from the Magistrateâ€™s Court showed no complaints of ill-treatment by the Petitioner, despite multiple opportunities to report such incidents. The court also found the Petitioner's claim regarding his inability to understand Sinhala to be a falsehood, as evidence showed he was fluent in the language. Finding: The Petitioner failed to prove his allegations; there was no violation of Article 11. Issue 3: Legality of Detention under Emergency Regulations Rule applied: Emergency (Miscellaneous Provisions and Powers) Regulations No. 1, specifically Regulations 19 and 21. Court's reasoning: The court acknowledged that while subsequent jurisprudence (SC Application 173/08) clarified that detainees should be transferred to fiscal custody after 90 days, this interpretation was prospective. The Petitionerâ€™s detention was authorized by validly issued Detention Orders signed by the relevant authority, and the authorities complied with the requirement to produce the Petitioner before a Magistrate. Finding: There was no violation of Article 13(2) of the Constitution. Interaction of Issues The court's finding that the Petitioner lacked testimonial creditworthiness—due to his false claims regarding his language proficiency and the lack of medical evidence to support his torture allegations—undermined his entire petition. This lack of credibility reinforced the court's reliance on the official, contemporaneous records produced by the Respondents, which ultimately led to the dismissal of all alleged fundamental rights violations. Conclusion The Application is dismissed. The court found no violation of the Petitioner's fundamental rights, noting that the allegations were not supported by the evidence and that the Petitioner's testimony lacked credibility. No costs are ordered. Keywords Fundamental rights — Article 11 — Article 12(1) — Article 13(1) — Article 13(2) — Arrest — Detention — Torture — Confession — Emergency Regulations — Judicial Medical Officer — LTTE involvement

Parties: Wimalsundera and Others v. Vocational Training Authority of Sri Lanka and Others
Bench: J.A.N. De Silva, J.; S.I. Imam, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Promotions — Political victimization — Cabinet approval — Necessary parties
Summary: Facts The Petitioners were employees of the Vocational Training Authority of Sri Lanka who sought to challenge the promotions of the 5th, 6th, and 7th Respondents to the post of Deputy Director. The Petitioners alleged that these promotions were the result of a report by a Political Victimization Committee and were violative of their fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The Petitioners claimed they were unaware of the promotions until October 2005 and subsequently filed their application in November 2005. The Respondents contended that the application was filed out of time, as the Petitioners were aware of the promotion process and the relevant committee report as early as September 2005. Furthermore, the Respondents argued that the application was fundamentally defective because the Petitioners failed to name the Political Victimization Committee and the Cabinet of Ministers as parties, despite the fact that the impugned promotions were based on the Committee's recommendations and subsequent Cabinet approval. During the proceedings, it was noted that the 1st Petitioner had retired, the 2nd Petitioner had been promoted, and the 3rd Petitioner had received service extensions. Issues Whether the Petitioners' application was filed within the time limit prescribed for fundamental rights applications. Whether the failure to name the Cabinet of Ministers and the Political Victimization Committee as respondents constitutes a fatal defect in the application. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 126 Cases Referred to: Dr. K.D.G. Wimalaratne v. The Secretary to the Ministry of Public Administration - [sc-application-654/95] H.A.S. Hettiarachchi v. Secretary of Public Administration and Home Affairs - [sc-application-780/1999] Analysis Issue 1: Timeliness of the application Rule applied: Article 126 of the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka. Court's reasoning: The court examined the evidence regarding when the Petitioners became aware of the impugned promotions. Although the Petitioners claimed they only learned of the appointments on October 19, 2005, the court noted that the Petitioners had access to the Political Victimization Committee report in September 2005 and were aware of the promotion process. The court concluded that the Petitioners were aware of the promotions by October 3, 2005, at the latest. Finding: The application filed on November 9, 2005, was out of time. Issue 2: Failure to join necessary parties Rule applied: The principle of cursus curiae regarding the necessity of joining the Cabinet of Ministers in challenges involving Cabinet decisions. Court's reasoning: The court held that because the promotions were based on the recommendations of the Political Victimization Committee and approved by the Cabinet of Ministers, these entities were necessary parties. The Petitioners' attempt to challenge Cabinet decisions while failing to name the Cabinet as a respondent is a fatal procedural defect. The court relied on established precedents, specifically Dr. K.D.G. Wimalaratne v. The Secretary to the Ministry of Public Administration and H.A.S. Hettiarachchi v. Secretary of Public Administration and Home Affairs, which confirm that failure to cite the Cabinet of Ministers is fatal to such applications. Finding: The failure to join the Cabinet of Ministers and the Political Victimization Committee is a fatal defect that renders the application unmaintainable. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues reinforce the dismissal of the application. The procedural failure to join necessary parties (Issue 2) is a substantive bar to the action, while the delay in filing (Issue 1) provides an additional ground for rejection. The court noted that the Petitioners' argument—that the Cabinet had no authority—actually necessitated the inclusion of the Cabinet as a party to contest that very authority, thereby highlighting the inconsistency in the Petitioners' procedural approach. Conclusion The application is dismissed due to the failure to join necessary parties and the filing of the application out of time. No costs are ordered. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Promotions — Political victimization — Cabinet approval — Necessary parties

Parties: Senaweera and Others v. Vocational Training Authority of Sri Lanka
Bench: J.A.N. De Silva, J.; S.I. Imam, J.; R.K.S.Suresh Chandra, J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Promotions in public service — Political victimization — Cabinet approval — Necessary parties to a suit
Summary: Facts The Petitioners were employed as Training Managers at the Vocational Training Authority of Sri Lanka. They alleged that their fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution were violated by the promotion of the 4th through 12th Respondents to the positions of Assistant Directors. The Petitioners contended that these promotions were made without following due procedure and were based on a report regarding political victimization, the existence of which they disputed. The Respondents maintained that the promotions of the 4th, 5th, 6th, 7th, and 8th Respondents were based on recommendations by a Political Victimization Committee, which received adequate publicity and subsequent Cabinet approval. The 9th, 10th, 11th, and 12th Respondents were appointed via a Board decision of the Authority. The Respondents raised preliminary objections regarding the maintainability of the application, specifically noting that the application was filed out of time regarding certain Respondents and that the Petitioners failed to join necessary parties, namely the Political Victimization Committee and the Cabinet of Ministers. Issues Whether the failure to join the Cabinet of Ministers and the Political Victimization Committee as respondents constitutes a fatal defect in the application. Whether the application was filed within the time limits prescribed for fundamental rights applications under Article 126 of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 126 Cases Referred to: Jayanetti v. Land Reform Commission - [1984/v2-sri-lr-172] Dr. K. D. G. Wimalaratne v. The Secretary to the Ministry of Public Administration - [sc-application-654/95] H. A. S. Hettiarachchi v. Secretary of Public Administration and Home Affairs - [sc-application-780/1999] Analysis Issue 1: Failure to join necessary parties Rule applied: The principle that the Cabinet of Ministers is a necessary party in challenges to decisions approved by the Cabinet, and failure to cite them is a fatal defect. Court's reasoning: The Petitioners sought to quash decisions that were explicitly approved by the Cabinet of Ministers. The court held that a petitioner must identify and join all necessary parties at the time of filing. The court rejected the Petitioners' attempt to reserve the right to add parties later, noting that the failure to include the Cabinet of Ministers and the Political Victimization Committee at the outset is a fatal procedural defect. The court followed established precedents, including Dr. K. D. G. Wimalaratne v. The Secretary to the Ministry of Public Administration and H. A. S. Hettiarachchi v. Secretary of Public Administration and Home Affairs, which mandate the dismissal of applications where the Cabinet is not cited. Finding: The failure to join the Cabinet of Ministers and the Political Victimization Committee is a fatal defect that renders the application unmaintainable. Issue 2: Timeliness of the application Rule applied: Article 126 of the Constitution regarding the time limit for filing fundamental rights applications. Court's reasoning: While the court gave the Petitioners the benefit of the doubt regarding the initial filing date of 13th October 2005, it noted that the amended petition, which sought to include the 10th, 11th, and 12th Respondents, was filed on 9th November 2005. Since the Petitioners were aware of these specific appointments by 3rd October 2005, the application against these specific parties was filed out of time. Finding: The application against the 10th, 11th, and 12th Respondents was filed out of time. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues reinforce the dismissal of the application. The procedural failure to join necessary parties (the Cabinet) serves as a fundamental bar to the action, while the delay in filing against specific respondents provides an additional ground for the rejection of those specific claims. Conclusion The application is dismissed due to the failure to join necessary parties and the filing of the application out of time against certain respondents. There is no order as to costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Promotions in public service — Political victimization — Cabinet approval — Necessary parties to a suit

Parties: Hewa Alankarage Rosalin Hami v. E. Hewage Hami and Others
Bench: Ms. S. Tilakawardane, J.; Amarathunga, J; Imam, J.
Keywords: possession — eviction — title — co-ownership — prescription — evidence — fiscal report — judicial acts — uninterrupted possession — adverse possession — legal principles — court orders — statutory presumption — deeds — land disputes
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a land dispute involving Hewa Alankarage Rosalin Hami (the Appellant) and E. Hewage Hami and others (the Respondents). The Appellant, who was the spouse of the 1st Defendant in a previous case, was not a party to the earlier District Court action (Case No. L/882) initiated by the Respondents to obtain a declaration of title and possession of the disputed land. The Respondents claimed to have purchased the land in 1954 and sought eviction of the 1st Defendant, who was occupying the land at the time. The Appellant did not intervene in the earlier proceedings and later contested the execution of the writ of possession, asserting that possession had never been handed over to the Respondents. The court had to determine whether the Fiscal had indeed delivered possession to the Respondents and whether the Appellant could prove exclusive and uninterrupted possession of the land. The Respondents produced evidence, including deeds and a survey plan, to establish their claim to the land. The Appellant countered with her own deed, claiming a portion of the land, but the court found inconsistencies in her evidence. Issues The primary issues for determination were: Whether possession had been handed over to the Plaintiff by the Fiscal in District Court Tangalle Case No. L/882? Whether there is evidence to prove exclusive and uninterrupted possession of the disputed corpus by the 2nd Defendant-Appellant-Petitioner? Applicable Law Statutes: Evidence Ordinance, Section 114(d) Cases Referred to: Hariette Vs. Pathmasiri - [1996] 1 SRI L R 358; Sura Vs. Fernando - [1 ACR 95]; Siman Appu Vs. Christian Appu - [1896] 1 NLR 288; Ettana Vs. Naide - [1878] 1 S.C.C.11. Analysis Issue 1: Whether possession had been handed over to the Plaintiff by the Fiscal in District Court Tangalle Case No. L/882? Rule applied: Section 114(d) of the Evidence Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The court examined the evidence presented, particularly the Fiscal's report and affidavit dated 17.09.1962, which indicated that possession of the land had been delivered to the Respondent. The court noted that the Appellant did not challenge this evidence or call the Fiscal Officer to testify, which led to a statutory presumption in favor of the execution of the writ. The court emphasized that the Appellant's claim that possession was never handed over was not substantiated by independent evidence. The court found that the Appellant's witness, Wijemuni Arachchige Peiris, lacked credibility as he was not present during the execution of the writ. The court concluded that the possession had indeed been handed over to the Respondent on 17.09.1962, and there was no legal error in the Court of Appeal's judgment affirming this finding. Finding: The court found that possession had been duly handed over to the Respondent by the Fiscal. Issue 2: Whether there is evidence to prove exclusive and uninterrupted possession of the corpus by the Appellant? Rule applied: Legal principles regarding co-ownership and adverse possession. Court's reasoning: The Appellant's claim to exclusive possession was undermined by her own deed, which indicated ownership of only an undivided portion of the land. The court referenced the case of Hariette Vs. Pathmasiri, which established that a party claiming exclusive possession must provide evidence of uninterrupted possession and ouster of co-owners. The court noted that the Appellant had not proven that she was in undisturbed possession for the requisite period, as litigation regarding the land was ongoing. The court also highlighted that the Appellant's actions did not demonstrate exclusive possession, as she had not successfully ousted the Respondent from the land. The court concluded that the Appellant failed to establish her claim of adverse possession. Finding: The court found that the Appellant had not proven her claim of exclusive and uninterrupted possession. Interaction of Issues The issues were interrelated, as the determination of whether possession had been handed over directly impacted the Appellant's ability to claim exclusive possession. The court's findings on the execution of the writ and the Appellant's failure to establish her possession were critical in resolving the dispute. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the Appellant's appeal, affirming the lower court's findings regarding possession and the lack of evidence for the Appellant's claims. The court ruled that the Appellant's assertions were untenable and not supported by the facts of the case. No costs were awarded. Keywords possession — eviction — title — co-ownership — prescription — evidence — fiscal report — judicial acts — uninterrupted possession — adverse possession — legal principles — court orders — statutory presumption — deeds — land disputes
Counsel: Faiz Musthapha, P.C., with Amarasiri Panditharatne for the 2nd Defendant-Appellant-Petitioner. D.M.G. Dissanayake for the Substituted Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondents.

Parties: Kesara Dahamsonda Senanayake v. Hon. The Attorney-General and Commission to Investigate Allegations of Bribery & Corruption
Bench: Dr. Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; K. Sripavan, J.; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Bribery Act — Criminal Procedure — Appeal — Necessary Parties — Supreme Court Rules — Complainant — Director-General — Commission to Investigate Allegations of Bribery and Corruption — Mens Rea — Conviction — Preliminary Objection
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal by Kesara Dahamsonda Senanayake, the former Mayor of Kandy, against a conviction and sentence imposed by the Magistrate's Court for alleged bribery under the Bribery Act, No. 20 of 1994. The appellant was charged with two counts: the first count alleged that he had obtained funds for attending a workshop in Taiwan but instead traveled to Singapore, causing a loss to the government. The second count was for obtaining an illegal benefit of the same amount. The appellant contended that he could not attend the workshop due to visa issues and claimed he had no mens rea to commit the alleged offences. After a trial, he was convicted and sentenced to one year’s imprisonment suspended for five years and fined Rs. 100,000 for each count. The High Court affirmed this conviction, leading to the current appeal. Issues The primary issues raised in this appeal are: Whether the appeal is maintainable given the failure to name the Director-General of the Bribery Commission as a party respondent? Whether the appellant had the requisite mens rea to commit the alleged offences? Applicable Law Statutes: Bribery Act, No. 20 of 1994; Code of Criminal Procedure Act; Commission to Investigate Allegations of Bribery and Corruption Act, No. 19 of 1994. Cases Referred to: The Attorney-General v. Herath Singho - [1948] 49 NLR 108; Ibrahim v. Nadarajah - [1991] 1 Sri L.R. 131. Analysis Issue 1: Maintainability of the Appeal Rule applied: Supreme Court Rules, 1990, specifically Rules 4 and 28. Court's reasoning: The court first addressed the preliminary objection raised by the respondents regarding the maintainability of the appeal due to the appellant's failure to name the Director-General of the Bribery Commission as a party respondent. The court emphasized the importance of naming all necessary parties in an appeal, as outlined in the Supreme Court Rules. The learned Senior State Counsel argued that the Director-General, being the complainant, was a necessary party whose absence rendered the appeal non-compliant with the rules. The court referenced the provisions of the Commission to Investigate Allegations of Bribery and Corruption Act, which delineate the role of the Director-General in instituting proceedings. The court noted that the Director-General acts on the directions of the Commission and is responsible for the prosecution of offences under the Bribery Act. The court cited previous case law, particularly the decision in Ibrahim v. Nadarajah, which established that all parties who may be adversely affected by the outcome of the appeal must be included. The court concluded that the absence of the Director-General as a party respondent was a fatal flaw in the appeal, as it violated the Supreme Court Rules. Finding: The court upheld the preliminary objection and dismissed the appeal for non-compliance with the Supreme Court Rules. Issue 2: Mens Rea The court did not delve into this issue due to the dismissal of the appeal on procedural grounds. Interaction of Issues The issues of maintainability and mens rea are interconnected, as the determination of whether the appeal could proceed hinged on the proper identification of parties. The court's ruling on the preliminary objection effectively precluded any examination of the substantive issues related to the appellant's mens rea. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal on the grounds of non-compliance with the Supreme Court Rules, specifically the failure to name the necessary party, the Director-General of the Bribery Commission. The court made no order as to costs, emphasizing the procedural requirements that must be adhered to in appeals. Keywords Bribery Act — Criminal Procedure — Appeal — Necessary Parties — Supreme Court Rules — Complainant — Director-General — Commission to Investigate Allegations of Bribery and Corruption — Mens Rea — Conviction — Preliminary Objection
Counsel: C.R. de Silva, PC, with R.J. de Silva and Dulan Weerawardena for Accused-Appellant-Appellant; Gihan Kulathunga, SSC, with Asitha Anthony for Respondents-Respondents

Parties: J. S. Dominic v. Hon. Jeevan Kumarathunga and Others
Bench: N. G. Amaratunga, J.; Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J.; C. Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Land Acquisition Act — Writ of Certiorari — Writ of Mandamus — Preliminary Objections — Necessary Parties — Judicial Review — Administrative Law — Equity — Procedural Fairness — Natural Justice — Appeal Process — Court of Appeal — Supreme Court
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for special leave to appeal concerning a writ application filed by the Petitioner-Appellant, J. S. Dominic, against several Respondents, including the Minister of Lands and the Urban Development Authority. The Appellant claimed ownership of a condominium unit in Kollupitiya, Colombo, which was acquired by the State under the Land Acquisition Act in 1987. Following the acquisition, the Appellant was provided alternative accommodation by the Urban Development Authority. However, he alleged that he had not received compensation for his property and that a divesting order made in 1991 was improperly varied by a subsequent order in 2004, which sought to release the property from the divesting order. The Appellant filed a writ application in the Court of Appeal seeking to quash the 2004 order and compel the Respondents to transfer the title of the alternative accommodation to him. The Respondents raised preliminary objections, claiming that the Appellant had failed to join necessary parties to the writ application, which led to the dismissal of his application. Issues The primary issue is whether the Court of Appeal erred in upholding the preliminary objections raised by the Urban Development Authority and Finco Limited, leading to the dismissal of the Appellant's writ application. Specifically, the issues can be framed as follows: 1. Whether the Court of Appeal erred in rejecting the Appellant's amended petition to add necessary parties to the writ application? 2. Whether the failure to join necessary parties was fatal to the maintainability of the writ application? 3. Whether the Appellant was denied procedural fairness in the proceedings before the Court of Appeal? Applicable Law Statutes: Land Acquisition Act No. 9 of 1950, Ceiling on Housing Property Law No. 1 of 1973. Cases Referred to: Perera v. National Housing Development Authority [2001] 3 Sri LR 50; Fernando v. Fernando (1919) 6 Ceylon Weekly Reporter 262; Vinnasithamby v. Joseph (1961) 65 NLR 359; Goonetilleke v. Government Agent, Galle (1946) 47 NLR 549; Jamila Umma v. Mohamed (1948) 50 NLR 15; Dharmaratne v. Commissioner of Elections (1950) 52 NLR 429; Francis Samarawickrema v. Dona Enatto Hilda Jayasinghe and another [2000] BALJR 000; V. Ramasamy v. Ceylon State Mortgage Bank (1976) 78 NLR 510. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Court of Appeal erred in rejecting the Appellant's amended petition to add necessary parties to the writ application? Rule applied: The principle that the failure to make a necessary party a respondent is fatal to the maintainability of a writ application. Court's reasoning: The Court of Appeal initially dismissed the Appellant's motion to amend his writ petition to include necessary parties, citing that the objections raised by the Urban Development Authority and Finco Limited were valid. The Appellant argued that he was unaware of the interests of ICC Housing (Pvt.) Ltd., Ocean View Development Company (Pvt.) Ltd., and the National Housing Development Authority at the time of filing his writ petition. The Supreme Court found that the Appellant acted with reasonable expedition in seeking to add these parties once he became aware of their interests. The Court emphasized that the Appellant's failure to join these parties was not due to negligence but rather a lack of knowledge, which should not prejudice his application. The Supreme Court also noted that the Court of Appeal had erred in treating the Appellant's application to add necessary parties as belated when it was made within a reasonable time after he became aware of their interests. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the Court of Appeal erred in rejecting the Appellant's amended petition and upheld the Appellant's right to include necessary parties in the writ application. Issue 2: Whether the failure to join necessary parties was fatal to the maintainability of the writ application? Rule applied: The principle that the failure to join necessary parties is fatal to the maintainability of a writ application. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court analyzed the implications of the failure to join necessary parties and noted that while it is generally fatal to a writ application, the circumstances of this case were unique. The Appellant had made efforts to seek administrative relief and was not aware of the necessary parties' interests until the objections were filed. The Court highlighted that the Appellant's claims were distinct and that the relief sought in the writ application was not solely dependent on the inclusion of the necessary parties. The Court emphasized that procedural fairness must be upheld, and the Appellant should not be penalized for the lack of knowledge regarding the necessary parties. Finding: The Supreme Court found that the failure to join necessary parties was not fatal to the maintainability of the writ application in this instance. Issue 3: Whether the Appellant was denied procedural fairness in the proceedings before the Court of Appeal? Rule applied: The principle of procedural fairness in judicial proceedings. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the procedural history of the case and noted that the Appellant was denied the opportunity to present his case fully due to the Court of Appeal's refusal to allow the addition of necessary parties. The Court emphasized that the Appellant had made reasonable efforts to seek relief and that the procedural decisions made by the Court of Appeal were inconsistent and contradictory. The Court highlighted that the Appellant's right to a fair hearing was compromised by the Court of Appeal's decisions. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the Appellant was indeed denied procedural fairness in the proceedings before the Court of Appeal. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as the failure to join necessary parties directly impacted the maintainability of the writ application and the Appellant's right to a fair hearing. The Court of Appeal's decisions on these matters were found to be flawed, leading to a denial of justice for the Appellant. Conclusion The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, set aside the judgments of the Court of Appeal, and directed that the Appellant's amended petition be accepted. The Court ordered that the necessary parties be added as respondents and that the case be expedited for hearing in the Court of Appeal. The Supreme Court emphasized the importance of procedural fairness and the need to ensure that all parties with a legitimate interest in the matter are given an opportunity to be heard. Keywords Land Acquisition Act — Writ of Certiorari — Writ of Mandamus — Preliminary Objections — Necessary Parties — Judicial Review — Administrative Law — Equity — Procedural Fairness — Natural Justice — Appeal Process — Court of Appeal — Supreme Court
Counsel: Mr. J. C. Weliamuna with Pulasthi Hewamanne for the Petitioner-Appellant. Mrs. Ganga Wakishtarachchi, S.C., for the 1st to 3rd and 6th Respondent-Respondents. Mr. S. L. Gunasekara, Mr. Ananda Dharmaratne with Ms. R. Senaratne for the 5th Respondent-Respondent.

Parties: Woodman Exports (Pvt.) Ltd. v. Commissioner-General of Labour and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N.G. Amaratunga, J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Special Leave to Appeal — Supreme Court Rules 1990 — Tender of notices — Service on respondents — Preliminary objections — Non-compliance with rules — Mandatory requirements
Summary: Facts The petitioner, Woodman Exports (Pvt.) Ltd., filed an application for Special Leave to Appeal in the Supreme Court on 23.12.2008, seeking to challenge a judgment of the Court of Appeal dated 21.11.2008 which had dismissed the petitioner's application for a writ of certiorari and/or mandamus. Upon the filing of the application, the petitioner failed to tender the required notices for service on the respondents. On 03.02.2009, the Registrar noted that no notices had been tendered. When the matter was listed for support on 20.03.2009, the Court directed the petitioner to support the application with notice to the parties. The petitioner subsequently dispatched notices to the first through sixth respondents on 14.05.2009. Following this, it was discovered that notices sent to the fourth and sixth respondents were returned undelivered. The petitioner filed a motion on 05.06.2009 to provide a corrected address for the fourth respondent, and further notices were dispatched on 09.06.2009. The respondents raised preliminary objections regarding the petitioner's failure to comply with the procedural requirements of the Supreme Court Rules 1990. Issues Whether the petitioner failed to comply with the mandatory requirements of Rules 8(3) and 8(5) of the Supreme Court Rules 1990 regarding the tendering of notices for service on the respondents. Whether the failure to comply with the aforementioned rules warrants the dismissal of the application for Special Leave to Appeal. Applicable Law Statutes: Supreme Court Rules 1990 Cases Referred to: Nanayakkara v Kyoko Kyuma - [sc-appeal-115/2008] Samantha Niroshana v A.M.S.S. Abeyruwan nee Gunasekera - [sc-appeal-145/2006] A.H.M. Fowzie v Vehicles Lanka (Pvt.) Ltd. - [sc-appeal-286/2007] Wikramatillake v Marikar - [2-nlr-9] K. Reaindran v K. Velusomasunderam - [sc-appeal-298/99] N.A. Premadasa v The Peoples Bank - [sc-appeal-212/99] Hameed v Majbdeen - [sc-appeal-38/2001] K.M. Samarasinghe v R.M.D. Ratnayake - [sc-appeal-51/2001] Soong Che Foo v Harosha K. De Silva - [sc-appeal-184/2003] C.A. Haroon v S.K. Muzoor - [sc-appeal-158/2006] Analysis Issue 1: Compliance with Rules 8(3) and 8(5) Rule applied: Rules 8(3), 8(5), and 40 of the Supreme Court Rules 1990. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that Rule 8(3) mandates that a petitioner must tender the required number of notices for service on respondents at the time of filing the application for Special Leave to Appeal. The petitioner filed the application on 23.12.2008 but did not dispatch notices until 14.05.2009, nearly five months later. Furthermore, the petitioner failed to utilize the procedure under Rule 40 to seek a variation or extension of time for compliance. The Court rejected the petitioner's reliance on the principle that rules should not be "juggernaut cars," noting that these rules are mandatory to ensure all parties are properly notified. Finding: The petitioner failed to comply with the mandatory requirements of Rules 8(3) and 8(5). Issue 2: Consequences of non-compliance Rule applied: Supreme Court Rules 1990. Court's reasoning: The Court held that while technicalities should not hinder the administration of justice, the requirements of Rule 8 are not mere technicalities but essential procedures to ensure due process and notice to all parties. Citing a long line of precedents, the Court determined that non-compliance with Rule 8(3) is fatal to an application. Finding: The failure to comply with the mandatory rules necessitates the dismissal of the application. Interaction of Issues The findings are intrinsically linked; the determination that the rules are mandatory (Issue 1) directly dictates the consequence of dismissal (Issue 2). The Court's reasoning emphasizes that because the rules serve the fundamental purpose of ensuring notice, their breach cannot be excused as a minor procedural error, thereby reinforcing the necessity of the dismissal. Conclusion The preliminary objections raised by the respondents are upheld. The petitioner's application for Special Leave to Appeal is dismissed for non-compliance with the Supreme Court Rules 1990. No order is made as to costs. Keywords Special Leave to Appeal — Supreme Court Rules 1990 — Tender of notices — Service on respondents — Preliminary objections — Non-compliance with rules — Mandatory requirements

Parties: Ukwatta v. Marasinghe and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J; P. A. Ratnayake, P.C., J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Arrest — Detention — Procedure established by law — Torture — Cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment — Human Rights Commission Act — Time limit for application — Exceptional circumstances — Mandamus
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Roshan Mahesh Ukwatta, alleged that he was arrested on 22 February 2006 by officers of the Welikada Police Station and subjected to illegal detention and torture. The Petitionerâ€™s brother filed a complaint with the Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka on 24 February 2006 regarding the arrest and the assault. The Petitioner was eventually produced before a Magistrate on 28 February 2006, at which point the Magistrate observed his injuries and ordered medical treatment. The 1st Respondent, the Officer in Charge of the Crime Division, claimed the Petitioner was arrested on 27 February 2006 and sustained injuries by falling off a wall while attempting to escape. The Petitioner sought relief for the infringement of his fundamental rights under Articles 11 and 13(2) of the Constitution. The Court examined medical reports from the Assistant Judicial Medical Officer and other specialists, which confirmed multiple injuries, including a fractured mandible and a perforated eardrum, consistent with the Petitioner's account of torture. The Court also scrutinized the police Grave Crime Information Book and other production receipts, finding that the 1st Respondent had falsified official records to conceal the true date of arrest and the nature of the detention. Issues Whether the Petitionerâ€™s application was time-barred under Article 126(2) of the Constitution. Whether the detention of the Petitioner violated Article 13(2) of the Constitution. Whether the Petitioner was subjected to torture in violation of Article 11 of the Constitution. Whether the 2nd Respondent, as the Officer in Charge of the police station, is liable for the infringement of the Petitioner's fundamental rights. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 11, 12, 13(1), 13(2), 126 Human Rights Commission Act No. 21 of 1996, Sections 13, 14 Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979, Section 37 Police Ordinance, Section 66 Cases Referred to: Gamaethige v. Siriwardane - [1988/v1-sri-lr-384] Saman v. Leeladasa - [1989/v1-sri-lr-1] Namasivayam v. Gunawardena - [1989/v1-sri-lr-394] Channa Peiris v. Attorney General - [1994/v1-sri-lr-1] Queen v. Jinadasa - [59-clw-97] Sudath Silva v. Kodituwakku - [1987/v2-sri-lr-119] Balasekaran v. OIC JOOSSP Army Camp - [sc-fr-547/98] Sriyani Silva v. Iddamalgoda - [2003/v2-sri-lr-63] Rani Fernando - [sc-fr-700/2002] Analysis Issue 1: Time-bar Rule applied: Article 126(2) of the Constitution and the principle of lex non cogit ad impossibilia. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the one-month time limit is mandatory but subject to exceptions where the delay is not the fault of the petitioner. Given the Petitioner was in custody and under restraint, the Court applied the principle that time should be calculated from when the petitioner is no longer under restraint. Furthermore, the period spent pursuing a complaint before the Human Rights Commission is excluded from the computation. Finding: The preliminary objection was overruled; the application was timely. Issue 2: Illegal Detention Rule applied: Article 13(2) of the Constitution and Section 37 of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979. Court's reasoning: The evidence, including the complaint to the Human Rights Commission, established that the Petitioner was arrested on 22 February 2006 but not produced before a Magistrate until 28 February 2006. This exceeded the 24-hour limit prescribed by law. The Court rejected the 1st Respondent's version of events as a fabrication. Finding: The Petitioner's fundamental right under Article 13(2) was violated. Issue 3: Torture Rule applied: Article 11 of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Court relied on medical evidence, including the report of the Assistant Judicial Medical Officer, which identified 19 injuries consistent with the Petitioner's account of being beaten with batons and other blunt objects. The Court found the 1st Respondent's explanation of a "fall from a wall" to be medically impossible and a fabrication. Finding: The Petitioner was subjected to torture in violation of Article 11. Issue 4: Liability of the 2nd Respondent Rule applied: Police Ordinance regulations and the principle of command responsibility. Court's reasoning: As the Officer in Charge, the 2nd Respondent had a mandatory duty to inspect the station and monitor the activities of subordinates. His failure to prevent the illegal detention and torture, and his complicity in the falsification of records, rendered him liable. Finding: The 2nd Respondent is liable for the infringement of the Petitioner's rights. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected: the illegal detention (Issue 2) provided the environment for the torture (Issue 3) to occur. The falsification of records by the 1st Respondent to cover up the illegal detention undermined his credibility regarding the torture allegations. The 2nd Respondentâ€™s failure to perform his administrative duties (Issue 4) facilitated both the illegal detention and the torture. Conclusion The Court declares that the fundamental rights of the Petitioner under Articles 11 and 13(2) were infringed by the 1st and 2nd Respondents. The State is directed to pay the Petitioner Rs. 80,000 as compensation and Rs. 20,000 as costs within three months. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Arrest — Detention — Procedure established by law — Torture — Cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment — Human Rights Commission Act — Time limit for application — Exceptional circumstances — Mandamus

Parties: Kotagala Plantations Ltd and Lankem Tea & Rubber Plantations (Pvt) Ltd v. Ceylon Planters Society
Bench: J.A.N. De Silva, J.; Sripavan, J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Termination of employment — Misconduct — Assault — Evidence — Perverse decision — Labour Tribunal — High Court — Industrial Disputes Act — Just and equitable order — Efficiency — Past record of service
Summary: Facts The Applicant-Appellant-Respondent, L.P.D. Seneviratne, was employed as a Planter. His services were terminated by the 1st Respondent-Respondent-Appellant, Kotagala Plantations Ltd., following a domestic inquiry where he was found guilty of misconduct, specifically the assault of a Field Officer named Jayakody. The 2nd Respondent-Respondent-Appellant, Lankem Tea & Rubber Plantations (Pvt) Ltd., acted as the Managing Agent and denied the existence of an employment contract with the applicant. The applicant filed an application with the Labour Tribunal of Matugama seeking reinstatement or compensation. The Labour Tribunal concluded that the termination was justified based on the evidence of misconduct and the applicant's past service record, and subsequently dismissed the application. The applicant appealed this decision to the Provincial High Court of Kalutara. The High Court allowed the appeal and awarded the applicant compensation in the sum of Rs. 840,000/-. The Respondents then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court erred in law by failing to properly evaluate the evidence and the award of the Labour Tribunal, specifically regarding the finding of perversity. Whether the High Court erred in law by disregarding the relevance of the employee's past service record and conduct in determining the justifiability of the termination. Whether the High Court's decision to award compensation was contrary to law and the principles of justice and equity under the Industrial Disputes Act. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act (as amended) Cases Referred to: Caledonian Estates Ltd. v. Hillman - [79-nlr-421] Analysis Issue 1: Evaluation of Evidence and Perversity Rule applied: The principle that an appellate court should not disturb a Labour Tribunal's finding of fact unless the decision is perverse, meaning it is inconsistent with or unsupported by the evidence. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court observed that the Labour Tribunal President was in the best position to assess the credibility of witnesses regarding the assault incident. The High Court misdirected itself by failing to recognize that the primary fact-finding role belongs to the Tribunal. The High Court failed to state why the evidence of the two Assistant Field Officers, which corroborated the assault, was insufficient or unacceptable. Finding: The High Court erred in law by failing to identify any perversity in the Tribunal's findings, effectively substituting its own view of the facts without a legal basis. Issue 2: Relevance of Past Service Record Rule applied: The principle that evidence of past conduct, including inefficiency and disciplinary issues, is relevant to the employer-workman relationship and the determination of whether an employee is a fit and proper person for continued employment. Court's reasoning: The High Court incorrectly dismissed the relevance of the applicant's past record, labeling it as extraneous. The Supreme Court held that past conduct, including unauthorized work and disciplinary shortcomings, is a valid factor for an employer to consider. Such evidence is not rendered inadmissible simply because immediate disciplinary action was not taken at the time of each specific incident. Finding: The High Court committed a clear misdirection by excluding relevant evidence of the applicant's past conduct from the assessment of the termination's justifiability. Issue 3: Award of Compensation Rule applied: The requirement that judicial discretion under the Industrial Disputes Act must be exercised fairly and reasonably, balancing the interests of both the employer and the workman. Court's reasoning: The High Court's observation that the employer failed to provide a safe workplace was contradicted by evidence showing the employer had also terminated the employees who attacked the applicant. The Supreme Court emphasized that an employee who engages in misconduct, such as assault, cannot in justice and equity claim compensation for loss of career. Finding: The High Court's award of compensation lacked a sound legal basis and failed to reflect the principles of social justice intended by the Industrial Disputes Act. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected as the High Court's failure to respect the Tribunal's factual findings (Issue 1) led to an erroneous exclusion of relevant evidence (Issue 2), which ultimately resulted in an unjustified and legally flawed award of compensation (Issue 3). Conclusion The Supreme Court set aside the Order of the High Court and affirmed the Order of the Labour Tribunal. The appeal is allowed without costs. Keywords Termination of employment — Misconduct — Assault — Evidence — Perverse decision — Labour Tribunal — High Court — Industrial Disputes Act — Just and equitable order — Efficiency — Past record of service

Parties: R.P. Annama and Senarath Pathiranalage Gunathilake v. S.P. Sunil Ekanayake
Bench: Hon. J.A.N. de Silva CJ; Hon. Sripavan J; Hon. Ekanayake J
Keywords: Civil Procedure Code — Proxy — Substitution of parties — Legal representation — Appeal — Jurisdiction — Ratification — Nullity — Agency — Authority — Acquiescence
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a dispute concerning the title and eviction of a property involving R.P. Annama (the Plaintiff) and S.P. Sunil Ekanayake (the Defendant-Appellant). The Plaintiff initiated legal proceedings in the District Court of Polonnaruwa seeking a declaration of title and eviction of the Defendant from the property known as 'Shanthi Rice Mill'. During the trial, both the Plaintiff and a land officer provided evidence, and the Defendant also testified. However, on 28 February 2001, the Court was notified of the Plaintiff's death, prompting the need for substitution. The Attorney for the deceased Plaintiff, Mr. Iddawela, filed a petition to substitute the current Appellant as the substituted Plaintiff. The Defendant objected to this substitution, but the Court allowed it. Following this, further evidence was presented, and the District Court ruled in favor of the substituted Plaintiff. The Defendant subsequently appealed this decision, leading to a series of legal maneuvers including an application for a writ pending appeal, which was granted despite objections from the Defendant. The Defendant's appeal to the Court of Appeal was dismissed, and a special leave to appeal to the Supreme Court was sought, which was granted on several questions regarding the validity of the substitution and the proxy filed. Issues The Supreme Court was tasked with addressing several pivotal issues: (a) Whether the Civil Appellate High Court erred in law by allowing the appeal based on the claim that the petitioner was not properly substituted in the District Court. (b) Whether the Civil Appellate High Court was correct in asserting that the petitioner was not properly substituted. (c) Whether the absence of a valid proxy rendered the proceedings illegal and void ab initio. (d) Whether the Respondent had acquiesced to the substitution and was therefore estopped from raising objections regarding the proxy. (e) Whether the Civil Appellate High Court erred in determining that there was a valid final appeal for the exercise of appellate jurisdiction. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 27 Cases Referred to: Paul Coir v. Waas - [2002 (1) SLR 13]; AG v. Silva - [61 NLR 500]; Tillekeratne v. Wijesinhe - [11 NLR 270]; Nelson De Silva v. Casinathan - [55 NLR 121]; Udeshi v. Mather - [1998 (2) SLR 12]; Kadirgamadas v. Suppiah - [56 NLR 172]; Jalaldeen v. Rajaratnam - [1986 (2) SLR 201]. Analysis Issue 1: Validity of Proxy Rule applied: Section 27 of the Civil Procedure Code Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court began its analysis by emphasizing the importance of the proxy in legal proceedings, particularly in the context of representation by an attorney. The Court noted that Section 27(1) mandates that a registered attorney must be appointed in writing and that this appointment must be filed in Court. The Court highlighted that the absence of a valid proxy could potentially render the proceedings a nullity. However, the Court also recognized that the relationship between a client and their attorney is distinct from the proxy itself. Citing previous judgments, the Court underscored that the proxy serves as an authority granted by the client to the attorney, and the failure to file a proxy does not necessarily invalidate the proceedings if the attorney had the authority to act on behalf of the client. The Court examined the facts surrounding the substitution of the Plaintiff, noting that Mr. Iddawela had consistently represented the Plaintiff in Court without objection from the Defendant. The Court found that the Plaintiff's actions indicated a clear intention to authorize Mr. Iddawela to act on their behalf, despite the initial failure to file a proxy. The Court further referenced the case of Paul Coir v. Waas, which established that a defective proxy could be rectified by subsequent actions that demonstrate the client's intent to authorize their attorney. The Court concluded that the belated filing of the proxy by the substituted Plaintiff effectively ratified all prior actions taken by Mr. Iddawela, thereby validating the proceedings. Finding: The Supreme Court held that the absence of a valid proxy did not render the proceedings null and void, as the substituted Plaintiff had ratified the actions of their attorney. The appeal was allowed, and the judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court was set aside, restoring the District Court's ruling. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in this case are intricately linked, particularly the question of the validity of the proxy and the implications of acquiescence by the Respondent. The Court's analysis of the proxy's validity directly influenced its findings on the other issues, as the determination that the proxy was ultimately valid allowed the Court to dismiss the objections raised by the Respondent. The Court's reasoning emphasized the need for procedural fairness and the avoidance of unjust outcomes based on technicalities. Conclusion The Supreme Court ultimately ruled in favor of the substituted Plaintiff, allowing the appeal and restoring the District Court's judgment. The Court's decision underscored the importance of recognizing the authority of attorneys in legal proceedings and the necessity of ensuring that procedural requirements do not unjustly impede the pursuit of justice. The Court ordered no costs, reflecting its recognition of the complexities involved in the case. Keywords Civil Procedure Code — Proxy — Substitution of parties — Legal representation — Appeal — Jurisdiction — Ratification — Nullity — Agency — Authority — Acquiescence
Counsel: Mr. Ananda Kasturiarachchi with Theja Malawarachchi for the Plaintiff-Respondent-Appellant; Mr. W Dayaratne PC with Ms. R Jayawardene for the Defendant-Appellant-Respondent

Parties: Gardihewa Sarath C. Fonseka v. Mr. Dhammika Kithulegoda and Others
Bench: J.A.N. De Silva, CJ; Dr. Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J; R.A.N. Gamini Amaratunga, J; Saleem Marsoof, J; K. Sripavan, J
Keywords: Article 89(d) — Courts Martial — Judicial Power — Constitution of Sri Lanka — Separation of Powers — Competent Court — Army Act — Fair Trial — Disqualification from Parliament — Judicial Service Commission — Evidence Ordinance — Conviction and Sentence — Legislative Intent — Administrative Tribunal — Fundamental Rights
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a reference made by the Court of Appeal to the Supreme Court concerning the interpretation of the term 'any court' as mentioned in Article 89(d) of the Constitution of Sri Lanka. The petitioner, Gardihewa Sarath C. Fonseka, challenged the validity of a conviction and sentence imposed by a Court Martial, arguing that such a tribunal should not be classified as a 'court' under the Constitution. The respondents included various officials, including the Secretary General of Parliament and the Commissioner of Elections, among others. The petitioner contended that the disqualification process initiated by a conviction from a Court Martial should not apply, as it does not constitute a competent court in the sense defined by the Constitution. The Attorney General, representing the respondents, argued that the Court Martial is indeed a competent court and that the disqualification under Article 89(d) should apply to convictions from such tribunals. The Supreme Court was tasked with determining whether the term 'any court' includes Courts Martial or is limited to civil courts and other judicial bodies. Issues The primary issues framed for consideration were: Whether the words 'any court' in Article 89(d) of the Constitution include Courts Martial. Whether a conviction and sentence from a Court Martial should disqualify a member of Parliament from holding office. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 89(d), Article 4(c), Article 105, Article 13(4), Army Act No. 17 of 1949, Evidence Ordinance. Cases Referred to: Bribery Commissioner v. Ranasinghe - [1964] 66 NLR 73; Walker Sons & Co. Ltd. and Others v. Fry - [1965] 68 NLR 73; Liyanage and Others v. The Queen - [1965] 68 NLR 265; Panagoda v. Budinis Singho - [1966] 68 NLR 490; Gunaseela v. Udugama (Major General and Commander of the Army) - [1966] 69 NLR 193. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the words 'any court' in Article 89(d) include Courts Martial. Rule applied: The interpretation of constitutional provisions must be consistent with the overall framework of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the context of Article 89(d) and its relationship with other constitutional provisions. The petitioner argued that Courts Martial lack the characteristics of a judicial body, being convened by the Executive and not adhering to the same procedural standards as civil courts. The petitioner emphasized that Courts Martial do not follow the Evidence Ordinance and are not composed of judicial officers as defined in Article 170 of the Constitution. The Attorney General countered that the Court Martial is recognized as a competent court under Article 13(4) of the Constitution, which states that only a competent court can impose sentences of death or imprisonment. The Court noted that the Army Act empowers Courts Martial to impose such sentences, thus affirming their status as competent courts. The Court also highlighted that the Constitution preserves the validity of existing laws, including the Army Act, which governs the operation of Courts Martial. The Court emphasized that the interpretation of 'any court' must align with the legislative intent to prevent individuals convicted by a Court Martial from holding parliamentary office, thereby maintaining the integrity of the legislative body. Finding: The Court concluded that the term 'any court' in Article 89(d) does include Courts Martial, affirming the Attorney General's position. Issue 2: Whether a conviction and sentence from a Court Martial should disqualify a member of Parliament from holding office. Rule applied: The principle of legislative intent and the need to uphold the integrity of Parliament. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the implications of allowing individuals convicted by Courts Martial to retain their parliamentary seats. The Attorney General argued that permitting such individuals to serve would create an absurd situation where those serving prison sentences could participate in the legislative process. The Court recognized the importance of maintaining the integrity of Parliament and the necessity of disqualifying individuals who have been convicted of serious offenses, regardless of the tribunal that imposed the sentence. The Court reiterated that the Constitution's provisions aim to safeguard the legislative body from individuals who have been found guilty of serious misconduct. Finding: The Court upheld the disqualification of individuals convicted by Courts Martial from holding parliamentary office, reinforcing the legislative intent behind Article 89(d). Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated, as the determination of whether Courts Martial are included in the definition of 'any court' directly impacts the applicability of disqualification under Article 89(d). The Court's interpretation of the term influences the broader implications for the integrity of the parliamentary system and the standards of conduct expected from its members. Conclusion The Supreme Court ruled that the term 'any court' in Article 89(d) encompasses Courts Martial, thereby affirming the validity of convictions and sentences imposed by such tribunals. Consequently, individuals convicted by Courts Martial are disqualified from holding parliamentary office, aligning with the constitutional mandate to uphold the integrity of the legislative body. The Court's decision reinforces the principle that all judicial bodies, including military tribunals, must adhere to the standards set forth in the Constitution, ensuring that the rule of law prevails in all aspects of governance. Keywords Article 89(d) — Courts Martial — Judicial Power — Constitution of Sri Lanka — Separation of Powers — Competent Court — Army Act — Fair Trial — Disqualification from Parliament — Judicial Service Commission — Evidence Ordinance — Conviction and Sentence — Legislative Intent — Administrative Tribunal — Fundamental Rights
Counsel: Mr. Romesh De Silva PC, Mr. Saliya Peries for the Petitioner. Mr. Shibly Aziz PC with Mr. C. Warnasuriya for the 7th Respondent. Attorney General Mr. Mohan Pieries PC with Deputy Solicitor Generals Mr. S. Rajaratnam and Mrs. S.W.F. Jameel, and Senior State Counsel Mr. A.N.R. Pulle for the Respondents.

Parties: Dulani v. Bandara and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N.G. Amaratunga, J; K. Sripavan, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Teacher Educator appointment — Gazette notification — Qualifications for appointment — Degree requirements — Computer Science — Library and Information Technology — Administrative action — Public Service Commission
Summary: Facts The petitioner, Walawe Durage Dulani, was a teacher in the Sri Lanka Teachers Service who served on secondment as a lecturer at the Ruhunu National College of Education. In 2005, the Ministry of Education issued a Gazette notification calling for applications for the post of Teacher Educator in the Sri Lanka Teacher Education Service. The petitioner applied for the position in the subject of Information Technology. She attended interviews and was placed in a pool of candidates. The petitioner alleged that her fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution were violated when she was not selected for a permanent appointment. She contended that the respondents misinterpreted the qualification criteria set out in the Gazette notification, specifically regarding the requirements for candidates under Clause 6.4.6. The respondents maintained that the selection process followed the priority criteria established in the Gazette, which prioritized candidates under Clause 6.4.6(i) over those under Clause 6.4.6(ii). The petitioner was assessed under the latter category, and because other candidates scored higher marks, she was not selected for the limited number of vacancies available. Issues Whether the interpretation of the qualification criteria in Clause 6.4.6(i) of the Gazette notification dated 09.09.2005 by the respondents was erroneous and violated the petitioner's right to equality under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the non-selection of the petitioner for the post of Teacher Educator constituted arbitrary or discriminatory treatment in violation of Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 12(1), 14(1)g Universities Act, No. 16 of 1978 Cases Referred to: Ram Krishna Dalmia v. Justice Tendolkar - [A.I.R. 1958 S.C. 538] Analysis Issue 1: Interpretation of qualification criteria Rule applied: Clause 6.4.6(i) and (ii) of the Gazette notification dated 09.09.2005. Court's reasoning: The petitioner argued that the respondents wrongly interpreted Clause 6.4.6(i) by allowing candidates with degrees in subjects like Mathematics to qualify as having "Computer Science, Library and Information Technology" as subjects. The Court examined the wording of the clause and concluded that it did not require a specific degree title in those subjects, but rather a degree that included those subjects. The Court noted that the University Grants Commission confirmed no degree existed in Sri Lanka at the time with the specific title of "Computer Science, Library and Information Technology." Therefore, the respondents' interpretation was consistent with the practical availability of qualifications and the text of the Gazette. Finding: The respondents' interpretation of the qualification criteria was not erroneous. Issue 2: Alleged violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution; the principle of reasonable classification as established in Ram Krishna Dalmia v. Justice Tendolkar. Court's reasoning: The Court applied the two-pronged test from Ram Krishna Dalmia v. Justice Tendolkar, requiring an intelligible differentia and a rational relation to the objectives. The Gazette notification established a clear hierarchy of qualifications to fill vacancies. The respondents demonstrated that 34 vacancies were filled by candidates under Clause 6.4.6(i), leaving only one vacancy for the category under which the petitioner applied (Clause 6.4.6(ii)). The petitioner was ranked 4th in her category and was outperformed by other candidates. The Court found that the selection process was based on merit and established criteria, not arbitrary or discriminatory factors. Finding: The petitioner failed to establish a violation of Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the determination that the qualification criteria were interpreted correctly (Issue 1) directly supported the conclusion that the selection process was based on a rational and non-arbitrary classification (Issue 2). Because the classification was valid and the petitioner was assessed fairly within that framework, no violation of equality occurred. Conclusion The Court held that the petitioner failed to establish an infringement of her fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The application is dismissed with no order as to costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Teacher Educator appointment — Gazette notification — Qualifications for appointment — Degree requirements — Computer Science — Library and Information Technology — Administrative action — Public Service Commission

Parties: Perumpulli Hewage Piyasena v. Ilankai Tamil Arasu Kadchi and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J.; K. Sripavan, J.; R. K. S. Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: expulsion — political party — disciplinary proceedings — Article 99(13)(a) — natural justice — membership — clean hands doctrine — procedural impropriety — validity of expulsion — ITAK Constitution — Supreme Court jurisdiction — good faith — burden of proof — political rights — parliamentary membership
Summary: Facts The case revolves around the Petitioner, Perumpulli Hewage Piyasena, who challenged his expulsion from the Ilankai Tamil Arasu Kadchi (ITAK), a recognized political party, following his election as a Member of Parliament for the Digamadulla District in the April 2010 General Election. The expulsion was communicated to him via a letter dated 28th November 2010, which stated that the Disciplinary Committee of ITAK had unanimously recommended his expulsion for acting against party discipline. The Petitioner filed a petition on 10th December 2010, seeking a declaration that the expulsion was invalid and that he remained a Member of Parliament. The case was argued over several dates in January 2011, with the judgment delivered on 8th February 2011. The Petitioner contended that he had not ceased to be a member of ITAK and that the expulsion was not conducted in accordance with the party's constitution. The Respondents, including the General Secretary of ITAK and the Commissioner of Elections, raised preliminary objections regarding the maintainability of the application, alleging that the Petitioner had suppressed material facts. Issues The primary issues for determination were: Whether the Petitioner was a member of ITAK and thus amenable to its disciplinary control? Whether the expulsion was valid under the provisions of the ITAK Constitution? Whether the Petitioner had suppressed or misrepresented material facts in his petition? Applicable Law Statutes: Article 99(13)(a) of the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, 1978; Provincial Councils Act No. 2 of 1988. Cases Referred to: W. S. Alphonso Appuhamy v. L. Hettiarachchi - [1973] 77 NLR 131; Gamini Dissanayake v. Kaleel and Others - [1993] 2 Sri LR 135; Tilak Karunaratne v. Sirimavo Bandaranaike - [1993] 2 Sri LR 90; Basheer Segu Dawood v. Ferial Ashraff and Others - [2002] 1 Sri LR 26. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Petitioner was a member of ITAK and thus amenable to its disciplinary control? Rule applied: The determination of membership in a political party and the applicability of disciplinary measures as per the party's constitution. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the Petitioner’s claims regarding his membership status, noting inconsistencies in his assertions. Initially, he claimed to be a member of ITAK, but later suggested he was only a de facto member. The Court emphasized that only a recognized member could be subjected to disciplinary actions. The Petitioner’s name appeared on the nomination paper for the election, which indicated a level of affiliation with ITAK. The Court referenced previous judgments that established that a member of Parliament could invoke the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court under Article 99(13)(a) regardless of formal membership status. The Court concluded that the Petitioner could not change his stance regarding membership to suit his argument. Finding: The Court determined that the Petitioner was indeed a member of ITAK at the time of his expulsion. Issue 2: Whether the expulsion was valid under the provisions of the ITAK Constitution? Rule applied: The procedural requirements for expulsion as outlined in the ITAK Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Court scrutinized the disciplinary process that led to the Petitioner’s expulsion. It was noted that the expulsion was purportedly based on a recommendation from the Disciplinary Committee, which was not the authorized body to expel members according to the ITAK Constitution. The Constitution specified that only the Central Committee had the authority to expel members. The Court highlighted that the expulsion letter did not reflect a decision made by the Central Committee, thus rendering the expulsion invalid. The Court also pointed out that the disciplinary proceedings lacked proper adherence to the procedural requirements set forth in the ITAK Constitution. Finding: The Court concluded that the expulsion was ex-facie illegal as it was not executed by the appropriate disciplinary authority. Issue 3: Whether the Petitioner had suppressed or misrepresented material facts in his petition? Rule applied: The clean hands doctrine and the requirement for full disclosure in seeking judicial relief. Court's reasoning: The Respondents alleged that the Petitioner had suppressed material facts regarding amendments to the ITAK Constitution and his conduct during the disciplinary proceedings. The Court noted that while the Petitioner had a duty to disclose relevant facts, the jurisdiction conferred by Article 99(13)(a) did not allow for dismissal based solely on allegations of suppression or misrepresentation. The Court emphasized that the validity of the expulsion was the primary concern, and the conduct of the Petitioner post-expulsion was irrelevant to the determination of the case. Finding: The Court overruled the preliminary objection regarding suppression of facts, affirming that the focus should remain on the validity of the expulsion itself. Interaction of Issues The issues were interrelated, as the determination of the Petitioner’s membership status directly impacted the validity of the expulsion. The procedural integrity of the disciplinary process was also crucial in assessing the legality of the expulsion. The Court's analysis highlighted that even if the Petitioner had acted against party discipline, the expulsion process itself was flawed, rendering any disciplinary action ineffective. Conclusion The Supreme Court determined that the purported expulsion of Perumpulli Hewage Piyasena from ITAK was invalid. The Court emphasized the importance of adhering to the constitutional provisions governing political party membership and disciplinary actions. The judgment underscored the necessity for political parties to follow their established procedures when expelling members, ensuring that such actions are lawful and just. In light of these findings, the Court made no order as to costs.
Counsel: D.S. Wijesinghe, P.C with N.M. Saheed, Shantha Jayawardena, Kaushalya Molligoda, Chamila Talagala and Isuru Somadasa for the Petitioner; K. Kanag-Isvaran, P.C with Viran Corea, Lakshmanan Jayakumar, Niran Anketell and Juanita Arulanantham for the 3rd Respondent; Shavindra Fernando, Deputy Solicitor General, with Nerin Pulle, State Counsel for the 4th and the 5th Respondents.

Parties: Indian Bank v. Acuity Stock Brokers (Pvt) Limited
Judgment by: R. K. S. Suresh Chandra, J.
Bench: S. Tilakawardane, J.; N. G. Amaratunge, J.; R. K. S. Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: contract law — breach of contract — Roman Dutch Law — consideration — enforceability — banking transactions — tripartite agreement — legal interest — damages — liability
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal by the Indian Bank (the Plaintiff-Appellant) against Acuity Stock Brokers (Pvt) Limited (the Defendant-Respondent) concerning a breach of contract related to an overdraft facility. The Plaintiff-Appellant had lent money to one M. Sivasubramaniam, who was a customer of both the bank and the stockbroker. The Plaintiff-Appellant alleged that the Defendant-Respondent had assured them that the sale proceeds of shares held on behalf of Sivasubramaniam would be credited to his account at the bank. However, the Defendant-Respondent later countermanded these instructions without informing the bank, leading to a significant financial loss for the Plaintiff-Appellant. The Plaintiff-Appellant sought to recover Rs. 5,558,841, which represented the market value of the shares that were not credited to Sivasubramaniam's account. The Commercial High Court dismissed the Plaintiff-Appellant's action, leading to this appeal.
