Issues The primary issues in this case can be framed as follows: Whether the letter dated 21st January 1994 constituted a legally enforceable contract between the Plaintiff-Appellant and the Defendant-Respondent. Whether the Defendant-Respondent breached the agreement by failing to credit the sale proceeds of the shares to the Plaintiff-Appellant's account. Whether the Plaintiff-Appellant is entitled to recover the claimed amount based on the breach of contract. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Law Ordinance No. 05 of 1865 Cases Referred to: Lipton v. Buchanan - [8 N.L.R. 49]; Jayawickreme v. Amarasuriya - [20 N.L.R. 289]; Edward Silva v. De Silva - [46 NLR 510] Analysis Issue 1: Whether the letter dated 21st January 1994 constituted a legally enforceable contract between the Plaintiff-Appellant and the Defendant-Respondent. Rule applied: Under Roman Dutch Law, a contract requires justa causa for validity. Court's reasoning: The court examined the nature of the agreement dated 21st January 1994, which was presented as an undertaking by the Defendant-Respondent to remit the sale proceeds of shares to the Plaintiff-Appellant. The court noted that the Defendant-Respondent had acted on instructions from Sivasubramaniam, which indicated a serious commitment to the agreement. The court referenced the principles of Roman Dutch Law, stating that a promise made with a serious intent can be enforceable even without traditional consideration as understood in English Law. The court cited the case of Lipton v. Buchanan, emphasizing that the causa or reason for a promise is broader than mere consideration and includes moral obligations. The court concluded that the Defendant-Respondent's undertaking was indeed enforceable under Roman Dutch Law, and the absence of consideration as argued by the Defendant-Respondent was not a valid defense. Finding: The court found that the letter constituted a legally enforceable contract. Issue 2: Whether the Defendant-Respondent breached the agreement by failing to credit the sale proceeds of the shares to the Plaintiff-Appellant's account. Rule applied: Breach of contract principles under Roman Dutch Law. Court's reasoning: The court analyzed the actions of the Defendant-Respondent following the agreement. It was established that the Defendant-Respondent had sold the shares and remitted the proceeds to Seylan Merchant Bank without informing the Plaintiff-Appellant of the countermanding of the original instructions. The court highlighted that the Defendant-Respondent's failure to communicate this change constituted a breach of the undertaking given to the Plaintiff-Appellant. The court noted that the Defendant-Respondent had acknowledged the obligation to remit the proceeds and had acted in furtherance of that obligation until the countermanding occurred. The court emphasized that the Defendant-Respondent's actions were inconsistent with the terms of the agreement, leading to the Plaintiff-Appellant's financial loss. Finding: The court concluded that the Defendant-Respondent had indeed breached the agreement. Issue 3: Whether the Plaintiff-Appellant is entitled to recover the claimed amount based on the breach of contract. Rule applied: Principles of damages in breach of contract cases. Court's reasoning: The court considered the financial implications of the breach and the amount claimed by the Plaintiff-Appellant. It was noted that the Plaintiff-Appellant had suffered a loss of Rs. 5,558,841 due to the Defendant-Respondent's failure to credit the sale proceeds. The court recognized that the Plaintiff-Appellant had a legitimate expectation to receive the proceeds based on the agreement and that the Defendant-Respondent's breach directly caused the financial loss. The court also took into account the time elapsed since the action commenced and the need to limit the legal interest that would accrue. Finding: The court ruled in favor of the Plaintiff-Appellant, allowing the recovery of the claimed amount along with interest. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as the enforceability of the contract directly impacts the determination of breach and the entitlement to damages. The court's analysis of the contract's validity set the foundation for assessing the breach and the resulting financial implications. Conclusion The Supreme Court set aside the judgment of the Commercial High Court and ruled in favor of the Plaintiff-Appellant, awarding Rs. 5,558,841 along with a flat rate of interest at 6% per annum until payment in full, and costs amounting to Rs. 21,000. The court emphasized the importance of upholding contractual obligations and the consequences of failing to communicate changes in instructions that affect financial transactions. Keywords contract law — breach of contract — Roman Dutch Law — consideration — enforceability — banking transactions — tripartite agreement — legal interest — damages — liability
Counsel: Prasanna Jayawardene with A. Siriwardane for Plaintiff-Appellant; Kushan de Alwis with Hiran Jayasuriya and Chamath Fernando for Defendant-Respondent

Parties: Indian Bank v. Acuity Stock Brokers (Pvt) Limited
Judgment by: R. K. S. Suresh Chandra, J.
Bench: S. Tilakawardane, J.; N. G. Amaratunge, J.; R. K. S. Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: contract law — breach of contract — Roman Dutch Law — consideration — enforceability — banking transactions — tripartite agreement — legal interest — damages — liability
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal by Indian Bank (the Plaintiff-Appellant) against Acuity Stock Brokers (Pvt) Limited (the Defendant-Respondent) concerning a breach of contract. The Plaintiff-Appellant sought to recover a sum of Rs.5,558,841/- with legal interest, stemming from an overdraft facility extended to one M. Sivasubramaniam, a customer of both the Bank and the Respondent. The Respondent, acting as a stockbroker, had assured the Bank that it would credit the sale proceeds of shares held for Sivasubramaniam to his account with the Bank. However, after Sivasubramaniam instructed the Respondent to sell the shares and remit the proceeds to another bank, the Respondent failed to inform the Bank of this change, leading to the Bank's claim for damages. The Commercial High Court dismissed the Bank's action, stating that the agreement was unenforceable due to a lack of consideration. The Plaintiff-Appellant contended that the Roman Dutch Law applied, which does not require consideration in the same manner as English Law. Issues The primary issues in this case can be framed as follows: Whether the agreement dated 21st January 1994 constituted a legally enforceable contract. Whether the Respondent breached its obligations under the agreement. Whether the Plaintiff-Appellant is entitled to recover the claimed sum based on the breach of contract. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Law Ordinance No.05 of 1865 Cases Referred to: Lipton v Buchanan - [8 N.L.R. 49]; Jayawickreme v Amarasuriya - [20 N.L.R. 289]; Edward Silva v De Silva - [46 NLR 510] Analysis Issue 1: Whether the agreement dated 21st January 1994 constituted a legally enforceable contract. Rule applied: Roman Dutch Law and principles of contract law. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the nature of the agreement dated 21st January 1994, which was an undertaking by the Respondent to credit the sale proceeds of shares to the Plaintiff-Appellant's account. The Respondent argued that the agreement was unenforceable due to a lack of consideration, adhering to English Law principles. However, the Court noted that under Roman Dutch Law, the concept of 'justa causa' is broader than consideration and encompasses moral obligations. The Court cited precedents such as Lipton v Buchanan, where it was established that a promise made with serious intent can be binding even without traditional consideration. The Court emphasized that the Respondent had acknowledged the seriousness of the undertaking by correcting an account number error, indicating a deliberate commitment to the agreement. The Respondent's subsequent actions, including entering into a tripartite agreement with Sivasubramaniam and Seylan Bank, did not absolve it of its obligations to the Plaintiff-Appellant, especially since it failed to inform the Bank of the countermanding of instructions. Finding: The Court concluded that the agreement was indeed enforceable under Roman Dutch Law, and the Respondent had breached its obligations. Issue 2: Whether the Respondent breached its obligations under the agreement. Rule applied: Breach of contract principles. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the Respondent had a clear obligation to act on the instructions of Sivasubramaniam, which included crediting the sale proceeds to the Plaintiff-Appellant's account. The Respondent's failure to inform the Bank of the new instructions constituted a breach of the undertaking. The Court highlighted that the Respondent's actions, particularly the sale of shares and remittance of proceeds to Seylan Bank without notifying the Plaintiff-Appellant, were in direct violation of the agreement. The Respondent's argument that it had acted according to Sivasubramaniam's instructions was insufficient to absolve it of liability, as it had a duty to communicate any changes in those instructions to the Bank. Finding: The Court affirmed that the Respondent had indeed breached its contractual obligations. Interaction of Issues The issues of enforceability and breach are interlinked, as the determination of whether the agreement was enforceable directly impacts the finding of breach. If the agreement is deemed enforceable, the Respondent's failure to comply with its terms constitutes a breach, thereby entitling the Plaintiff-Appellant to damages. The Court's analysis of the legal framework under Roman Dutch Law provided a foundation for understanding the obligations arising from the agreement and the implications of the Respondent's failure to adhere to those obligations. Conclusion The Supreme Court set aside the judgment of the Commercial High Court, ruling in favor of the Plaintiff-Appellant. The Court awarded the Plaintiff-Appellant Rs.5,558,841/- along with a flat rate of interest at 6% per annum until full payment, and costs amounting to Rs.21,000/-. The judgment underscored the importance of clear communication in contractual relationships and the enforceability of agreements under Roman Dutch Law, particularly in the context of banking transactions. The Court's decision reaffirmed the principle that parties must honor their contractual commitments and the legal ramifications of failing to do so. Keywords contract law — breach of contract — Roman Dutch Law — consideration — enforceability — banking transactions — tripartite agreement — legal interest — damages — liability
Counsel: Prasanna Jayawardene with A. Siriwardane for Plaintiff-Appellant; Kushan de Alwis with Hiran Jayasuriya and Chamath Fernando for Defendant-Respondent

Parties: Sri Lanka Principals' Service Grade 1 Officers' Association and Others v. Perera and Others
Bench: R.A.N.G. Amaratunge, J.; P.A. Ratnayake, J.; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Sri Lanka Principals Service Grade 1 Officers' Association — Sri Lanka Educational Administrative Service (SLEAS) — Merit-based promotions — Public Service Commission — Administrative action — Time bar — Service Minute
Summary: Facts The 1st Petitioner is a registered trade union, and the 2nd to 99th Petitioners are Grade 1 Officers in the Sri Lanka Principals Service. The Petitioners filed this application alleging a violation of Article 12(1) of the Constitution by the Respondents, who include members of the Public Service Commission, the Secretary of the Ministry of Education, and the Attorney General. The Petitioners contended that they were entitled to be appointed to Class III of the Sri Lanka Educational Administrative Service (SLEAS) under a 30% merit-based quota established by the 1986 SLEAS Service Minute. They argued that despite meeting the eligibility criteria, no appointments were made under this category for many years. The 10th Respondent, the Secretary to the Ministry of Education, filed objections stating that the 1986 Service Minute was replaced by a new Service Minute in 2002, which was gazetted on 1st March 2002. The 2002 Minute also provided for a 30% merit-based quota but changed the selection method from an interview-based process to a written examination. The 10th Respondent further submitted that applications for the merit category were called for in 2002, a written examination was conducted, and candidates were selected and appointed based on the results. The Respondents maintained that they had complied with the current Service Minute and that the Petitioners' claims regarding the 1986 Minute were time-barred and legally unsustainable. Issues Whether the failure to appoint the Petitioners to Class III of the SLEAS under the 1986 Service Minute constitutes a violation of Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the Petitioners' request to prioritize their appointments to fill the 30% merit quota, to the exclusion of other eligible candidates, is legally permissible under the current 2002 Service Minute. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Trade Unions Ordinance Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 12(1) regarding the 1986 Minute Rule applied: The court applied the principle that administrative actions must be governed by the currently applicable Service Minute and that claims regarding expired regulations are subject to time limitations. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the 1986 Service Minute was replaced by the 2002 Service Minute. The Petitioners failed to provide evidence of any complaints made regarding the non-implementation of the merit category during the 16 years the 1986 Minute was in force. The court found that the earliest complaint was only made in 2006. Consequently, the court held that the claim regarding the 1986 Minute was time-barred and that the authorities could not be compelled to act under a repealed circular. Finding: The court held that there was no violation of Article 12(1) of the Constitution as the 1986 Minute was no longer in operation. Issue 2: Request for priority appointments Rule applied: The court applied the provisions of the 2002 Service Minute, which governs current recruitment to the SLEAS. Court's reasoning: The Petitioners requested that all future vacancies in the 30% merit quota be filled by them in order of seniority before any appointments are made under the open and limited competitive categories. The court reasoned that granting this request would violate the 2002 Service Minute and unfairly deprive other eligible candidates of their opportunity to join the SLEAS through the established competitive examination processes. Finding: The court rejected the Petitioners' request, finding it inconsistent with the governing service regulations and prejudicial to the rights of other eligible candidates. Interaction of Issues The court's finding that the 1986 Minute is no longer in operation rendered the Petitioners' primary grievance moot. Because the current 2002 Minute governs the selection process, the court's rejection of the Petitioners' request for priority appointments (Issue 2) reinforced the conclusion that the Respondents had acted within the scope of the current law, thereby negating the alleged violation of Article 12(1) (Issue 1). Conclusion The application is dismissed. The court held that there was no violation of Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Sri Lanka Principals Service Grade 1 Officers' Association — Sri Lanka Educational Administrative Service (SLEAS) — Merit-based promotions — Public Service Commission — Administrative action — Time bar — Service Minute

Parties: Perera v. Justice Perera and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; P.A. Ratnayake, P.C., J; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Public service obligations — Scholarship agreements — Release from public service — Vacation of post — Administrative appeals — Higher education institutions — Obligatory service period — Establishments Code
Summary: Facts The petitioner, a Senior Consultant at the Open University of Sri Lanka, challenged the decision of the Public Service Commission (PSC) and the Ministry of Education requiring him to pay the costs of his foreign studies as a condition for reinstatement or release from the public service. The petitioner had joined the public service in 1989 and subsequently received government-funded scholarships for postgraduate studies in Australia. As a condition for these scholarships, the petitioner entered into an agreement with the government to serve for a specified period of eight years and seven months. Upon returning to Sri Lanka in 2004, the petitioner served briefly at the Siyane National College of Education before seeking and accepting a position at the University of Peradeniya without formal release from his substantive post. Subsequently, he applied for and accepted a position at the Open University. The Ministry of Education refused his request for a permanent release, citing his failure to complete the obligatory service period. Following his failure to report for duty at the Siyane National College of Education after being directed to do so, a notice of vacation of post was issued against him. The petitioner appealed this decision to the Administrative Appeals Tribunal, which dismissed his appeal. The PSC later offered to consider his reinstatement provided he paid the costs of his foreign studies, which the petitioner challenged as arbitrary and violative of his fundamental rights. Issues Whether the decision of the Public Service Commission to refuse the petitioner's release and require payment for his foreign studies violated the petitioner's fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the petitioner was treated discriminatorily in violation of Article 12(1) of the Constitution when compared to other public officers who were allegedly permitted to serve in higher educational institutions. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Universities Act No. 16 of 1978 (as amended), Section 77(1), Section 77(5) Establishments Code, Chapter V, Chapter XV, Chapter XLVIII Cases Referred to: Snowden v. Hughes - [321-u.s.-1] Ram Krishna Dalmia v. Justice Tendolkar - [a.i.r.-1958-s.c.-538] Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution; Section 77(5) of the Universities Act; Clause 2:1 and 2:3 of Chapter V of the Establishments Code. Court's reasoning: The court held that the petitioner was bound by an agreement to serve the government for a specific period. Under the Establishments Code, a release for service outside the public service requires the sanction of the Appointing Authority (the PSC). The court found that the petitioner failed to obtain the necessary approval for his release before accepting outside employment. Section 77(5) of the Universities Act, which allows service in a Higher Educational Institution to count as government service, is contingent upon a valid release being granted. Since the petitioner had not completed his obligatory service and the Ministry of Education had not recommended his release, the PSC acted within its authority in refusing the release and imposing conditions for reinstatement. Finding: The decision of the PSC was not arbitrary, irrational, or unreasonable, and did not violate Article 12(1). Issue 2: Discriminatory Treatment Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution (Equality before the law). Court's reasoning: The court examined the cases of other officers cited by the petitioner. It found that these individuals were not "similarly circumstanced." For instance, one officer had nearly completed his obligatory service period, whereas the petitioner had served only a small fraction of his required term. The court emphasized that equality under Article 12(1) requires that equals be treated equally, and since the petitioner's circumstances regarding the duration of his obligatory service and his failure to follow proper release procedures differed significantly from the others, there was no discriminatory treatment. Finding: The petitioner failed to establish that he was treated unequally compared to others in similar circumstances. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the petitioner failed to comply with the mandatory procedural requirements for release (Issue 1) directly informs the conclusion that he was not "similarly circumstanced" to those who had followed proper procedures or had nearly completed their obligations (Issue 2). Consequently, the lack of a valid legal basis for his release rendered his claim of discriminatory treatment unsustainable. Conclusion The court held that the petitioner failed to establish a violation of his fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The application is dismissed. No costs are ordered. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Public service obligations — Scholarship agreements — Release from public service — Vacation of post — Administrative appeals — Higher education institutions — Obligatory service period — Establishments Code

Parties: Siriwardana v. Seneviratne and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; K. Sripavan, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Legitimate expectation — Administrative action — Public service appointments — Gazette Notification — Exigencies of the service — Established procedure — Arbitrariness — Unreasonableness
Summary: Facts The petitioner, Harshani S. Siriwardana, was employed as a Matron at the Seth Sevana State Elders Home. In December 2006, the Provincial Public Service Commission of the Western Province published a Gazette notification to recruit Social Welfare Superintendents. The petitioner sat for the examination and was placed third in order of merit. As only two vacancies were advertised and filled, the petitioner was not appointed. She contended that paragraph 3 of the Gazette notification created a legitimate expectation that she would be appointed to the next available vacancy. In December 2008, a vacancy arose for a Social Welfare Superintendent at the Seth Sevana State Elders Home due to the retirement of the incumbent. The petitioner was requested to perform the duties of that position on a part-time basis. Subsequently, she was summoned before a committee chaired by the third respondent to have her qualifications scrutinized. The petitioner alleged that her appointment was delayed due to representations made by a trade union and that this delay, coupled with the failure to appoint her to the new vacancy, violated her fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Issues Whether the petitioner possessed a legitimate expectation to be appointed to the vacancy that arose in December 2008 based on the results of the examination held in June 2007. Whether the failure of the respondents to appoint the petitioner to the vacancy constituted a violation of her fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Cases Referred to: Schmidt v. Secretary of State for Home Affairs - [1969/v1-all-er-904] Breen v. Amalgamated Engineering Union - [1971/v1-all-er-1148] Re Westminster City Council - [1986/ac-668] Attorney General of Hong Kong v. Ng Tuen Shiu - [1983/v2-all-er-346] Council of Civil Service Unions v. Minister for the Civil Service - [1984/v3-all-er-935] Union of India v. Hindustan Development Corporation - [1993/v3-scc-499] Attorney General for New South Wales v. Quinn - [1990/64-australian-ljr-327] Analysis Issue 1: Legitimate Expectation Rule applied: The doctrine of legitimate expectation requires that an expectation be founded on a promise, an established practice, or a legal sanction, rather than a mere hope or desire. Court's reasoning: The court examined paragraph 3 of the Gazette notification, which stated that the number of vacancies depended on the exigencies of the service at the time of recruitment. The court reasoned that the recruitment process concluded once the two advertised vacancies were filled. The court held that the results of the 2007 examination could not be used indefinitely for future vacancies, as doing so would create uncertainty and prevent other eligible candidates from applying. Finding: The petitioner did not have a legitimate expectation to be appointed to the vacancy that arose in 2008. Issue 2: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution, which guarantees equality before the law and protection against arbitrary and unreasonable administrative action. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the respondents acted within the scope of the recruitment process defined by the Gazette notification. Because the petitioner failed to establish a legitimate expectation, the respondents' decision not to appoint her to the 2008 vacancy could not be characterized as arbitrary, unreasonable, or unlawful. Finding: The petitionerâ€™s fundamental rights under Article 12(1) were not infringed. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the courtâ€™s determination that the petitioner lacked a legitimate expectation directly negated her claim of arbitrariness under Article 12(1). Because the administrative authority was not bound by a promise or practice to appoint the petitioner to future vacancies, the failure to appoint her did not constitute a violation of the right to equal protection. Conclusion The court held that the petitioner failed to establish an infringement of her fundamental rights. The application is dismissed with no order as to costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Legitimate expectation — Administrative action — Public service appointments — Gazette Notification — Exigencies of the service — Established procedure — Arbitrariness — Unreasonableness

Parties: Mallika v. Premaratne and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, J.; S.I. Imam, J.; R.K.S.Suresh Chandra, J
Keywords: Partition of land — Kandyan Law — Paraveni property — Acquired property — Devolution of property — Inheritance — Family relationships — Pedigree evidence — District Court judgment — High Court appeal
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Appellant instituted a partition action in the District Court of Kuliyapitiya regarding the land called Atha Wetuna Pitiye Watta. The original owner, Sethuwa Maraduraya, transferred undivided half shares to Horatala and Balaya. Horatala and Balaya were brothers, and their descendants, including the 1st Defendant and Pina alias Bilinda, acquired interests in the property through various deeds. Peter, a son of Horatala, died unmarried and without issue, leading to the devolution of his shares among his surviving brothers. Pina alias Bilinda married Tikiri and had a daughter, Anulawathie, who subsequently died unmarried and issueless. Tikiri later contracted a second marriage with the 3rd Defendant, Tillekeratne, and had several children, including the 4th, 5th, and 6th Defendants. The Substituted Plaintiff-Respondent-Appellant claimed the property based on a pedigree supported by oral and documentary evidence. The 6th Defendant-Appellant-Respondent contested the claim, asserting rights as a child of Tikiriâ€™s second marriage. The District Court accepted the Plaintiff's evidence and ordered the partition of the corpus. The High Court of Civil Appeals set aside the District Court judgment and dismissed the action, holding that the Plaintiff failed to establish his case. The matter reached the Supreme Court on appeal. Issues Whether the High Court of Civil Appeals erred in law by dismissing the partition action despite the evidence presented regarding the pedigree and relationships of the parties. Whether the property inherited by Bilinda from Peter constitutes paraveni property under Kandyan Law. Whether the devolution of paraveni and acquired property from Anulawathie to her mother Tikiri and subsequently to Tikiriâ€™s children from her second marriage is governed by the Kandyan Law Declaration and Amendment Ordinance. Applicable Law Statutes: Kandyan Law Declaration and Amendment Ordinance No. 39 of 1938 Cases Referred to: Cooray v. Wijesuriya - [62-nlr-158] Banda v. Lebbe - [31-nlr-28] Analysis Issue 1: Evaluation of Evidence Rule applied: The court applied the evidentiary principles regarding pedigree and family relationships, specifically distinguishing between testimony from family members versus strangers as discussed in Cooray v. Wijesuriya. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court found that the High Court misdirected itself by disregarding the testimony of the Substituted Plaintiff, who, as a family member, possessed inherent knowledge of the pedigree. The High Court erroneously concluded there was no evidence linking the 1st Defendant, Pina alias Bilinda, and Peter to Horatala, whereas the evidence clearly established this lineage. The District Court was justified in relying on this unchallenged evidence. Finding: The High Court erred in law by dismissing the action and misapplying the principles of evidence. Issue 2: Classification of Property Rule applied: Section 10 of the Kandyan Law Declaration and Amendment Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The court analyzed the nature of the property held by the deceased parties. It determined that property inherited by Anulawathie from her father, Pina alias Bilinda, which he had inherited from his brother Peter, constitutes paternal paraveni property. Conversely, property acquired by other means is classified as acquired property. Finding: The court clarified the distinction between paraveni and acquired property, noting that the devolution must follow the specific classifications set out in the Ordinance. Issue 3: Devolution of Shares Rule applied: Section 10 of the Kandyan Law Declaration and Amendment Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The court determined that upon Anulawathieâ€™s death, her acquired share passed to her mother, Tikiri. Upon Tikiriâ€™s death, this share devolved upon her surviving children from her second marriage (the 4th, 5th, and 6th Defendants). The court rejected the contention that such property reverts to the paternal uncle (the 1st Defendant) under Kandyan Law. Finding: The court established the final share distribution: 3/9 to the Substituted-Plaintiff-Appellant, 3/9 to the 1A Defendant-Respondent, and 1/9 each to the 4th, 5th, and 6th Defendants-Respondents. Interaction of Issues The resolution of the evidentiary issue (Issue 1) allowed the court to establish the correct pedigree, which was a necessary prerequisite for applying the complex rules of Kandyan Law regarding property devolution (Issues 2 and 3). By correcting the High Court's factual findings, the Supreme Court was able to apply the statutory provisions of the Kandyan Law Declaration and Amendment Ordinance to reach a final, equitable distribution of the property. Conclusion The judgment of the High Court of Civil Appeals is set aside, and the judgment of the District Court is affirmed subject to the amended share distribution. The court emphasized the need for a flexible approach to resolve long-standing family land disputes. Keywords Partition of land — Kandyan Law — Paraveni property — Acquired property — Devolution of property — Inheritance — Family relationships — Pedigree evidence — District Court judgment — High Court appeal

Parties: D.L.K. Peiris v. Celltell Lanka Limited
Bench: S. Tilakawardane, J.; Ratnayake, J.; Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Special Leave to Appeal — Industrial Disputes Act — Misconduct — Termination of Employment — Evidence — Labour Tribunal — Appellate Review — Loss of Confidence — Moral Turpitude — Integrity — Domestic Inquiry — Reinstatement — Back Wages — Compensation — Judicial Deference
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for special leave to appeal to the Supreme Court concerning the termination of D.L.K. Peiris (the Appellant) from his position as Assistant Manager - Credit Collections at Celltell Lanka Limited (the Respondent). The Appellant was employed from 4th October 1993 until his termination on 18th December 2003. The Respondent terminated his services following a domestic inquiry, alleging that the Appellant attempted to defraud the company by submitting a false hotel bill for reimbursement, claiming he had stayed overnight in Kandy while on official duty. The Respondent's case was supported by evidence from three witnesses and computer-generated call records from the Appellant's cellular phone, which indicated he was in Colombo, not Kandy, on the night in question. The Appellant contested the validity of this evidence, claiming discrepancies in the call records and asserting that the Respondent's case was based on presumptions rather than actual evidence. The Labour Tribunal ruled against the Appellant, leading to his appeal to the High Court, which upheld the Tribunal's decision. The Appellant then sought special leave to appeal to the Supreme Court, raising several legal questions regarding the handling of evidence and the conclusions drawn by the lower courts. Issues The primary issues before the Supreme Court were: 1. Whether the learned President of the Labour Tribunal and the learned High Court Judge erred in law by failing to consider the evidence of the Appellant in light of discrepancies in the Respondent's documents. 2. Whether the learned President of the Labour Tribunal and the learned High Court Judge erred in law by concluding that the Respondent's case was based on presumptions rather than actual evidence. 3. Whether the findings of the Labour Tribunal were supported by the evidence presented. 4. If so, whether the Appellant was entitled to any relief. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act, Article 128(2) of the Constitution of Sri Lanka. Cases Referred to: Sri Cooperative Industries Federation Ltd. v. Ajith Devapriya Kotalawela - [S.C. Appeal No. 02/2005]; Munasinghe vs. Vidanage - 69 NLR 97; Caledonian (Ceylon) Tea and Rubber Estates, Ltd. v. J S Hillman - SC 250/27; All Ceylon Oil Companies Workers Union v. Standard Vacuum Oil - ID 237 CGG 12034 8.1.60; Engineering Employees Union v. State Engineering Corporation - CA 862/85 CAM 2.8.91; Whittals Boustead Ltd. Vs. United Tea, Rubber and Local Produce Worker’s Union - SC33/71; Ceylon Cold Stores Ltd. Vs. Sisil Beema Podu Sewa Sangamaya - 1986 CALR342; Ceylon Mercantile Union V Bartleet and Co. Ltd.; Democratic Workers’ Congress vs. De Mel and Wanigasekera - CGG 12,432 of 19 May 61. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the learned President of the Labour Tribunal and the learned High Court Judge erred in law in failing to consider the evidence of the Appellant in light of discrepancies in the Respondent's documents. Rule applied: The principle of deference to the findings of fact made by the trial court. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court emphasized the importance of the trial court's role in assessing evidence, particularly in cases involving allegations of misconduct. The Court noted that while it has the jurisdiction to review evidence, it must do so with caution, respecting the trial court's findings unless there is a clear lack of evidence to support those findings. The Appellant's argument centered on discrepancies in the call records, which he claimed undermined the Respondent's case. However, the Court found that these discrepancies did not materially affect the overall corroboration of the evidence. The call records, despite some inconsistencies, provided a clear indication of the Appellant's location on the night in question. The Court also highlighted that the Appellant's own shifting narrative regarding the use of his phone further weakened his position. Finding: The Court concluded that the Labour Tribunal's findings were supported by the evidence, and there was no error in law regarding the consideration of the Appellant's evidence. Issue 2: Whether the learned President of the Labour Tribunal and the learned High Court Judge erred in law by concluding that the Respondent's case was based on presumptions rather than actual evidence. Rule applied: The requirement for evidence to substantiate claims in disciplinary proceedings. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court reiterated that the burden of proof lies with the employer in cases of misconduct. The Respondent had presented substantial evidence, including witness testimonies and call records, to support its claims against the Appellant. The Court noted that the Appellant's failure to provide a coherent explanation for the discrepancies in his account further undermined his position. The Court emphasized that the Labour Tribunal had the discretion to evaluate the evidence and determine the credibility of witnesses, which it did in this case. Finding: The Court found that the Labour Tribunal's conclusion was based on a thorough evaluation of the evidence and was not merely a presumption. Issue 3: Whether the findings of the Labour Tribunal were supported by the evidence presented. Rule applied: The standard of review for findings of fact in appellate proceedings. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the evidence presented at the Labour Tribunal and found that the Tribunal had acted within its jurisdiction and had properly assessed the evidence. The Court noted that the Appellant's arguments did not sufficiently challenge the credibility of the evidence presented by the Respondent. The Court highlighted the importance of maintaining trust in the employer-employee relationship, particularly in positions of responsibility. Finding: The Court upheld the findings of the Labour Tribunal, concluding that the termination of the Appellant's services was justified based on the evidence of misconduct. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in this case are interrelated, as they all revolve around the evaluation of evidence and the legal standards applicable to misconduct in employment. The Appellant's claims of discrepancies in the evidence were central to his arguments against the findings of the Labour Tribunal. The Court's analysis of these issues demonstrated the importance of a thorough and fair assessment of evidence in disciplinary proceedings, particularly in cases involving allegations of dishonesty. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal, affirming the findings of the Labour Tribunal and the High Court. The Court concluded that the Respondent was justified in terminating the Appellant's services due to misconduct, and the Appellant had failed to demonstrate any grounds for relief. The Court emphasized the need for integrity and trust in employment relationships, particularly in positions of responsibility. The Appellant's request for reinstatement with back wages was not considered, as it was not specified in the prayer for special leave to appeal. The appeal was dismissed without costs. Keywords Special Leave to Appeal — Industrial Disputes Act — Misconduct — Termination of Employment — Evidence — Labour Tribunal — Appellate Review — Loss of Confidence — Moral Turpitude — Integrity — Domestic Inquiry — Reinstatement — Back Wages — Compensation — Judicial Deference
Counsel: Uditha Egalahewa with Gihan Galabadage for the Applicant-Appellant-Petitioner. S.L. Gunasekara with Manoj de Silva instructed by F.J & G De Seram for the Respondent-Respondent-Respondent.

Parties: Hussain v. Bandara and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC., J; P.A. Ratnayake, PC., J; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Retirement benefits — Public service circulars — Interpretation of statutes — Date of retirement — Clause 10.1 vs Clause 10.2
Summary: Facts The Petitioner was an officer in the Sri Lanka Educational Administrative Service who retired on December 1, 2004, upon reaching the compulsory retirement age of 60. Following his retirement, the Ministry of Public Administration and Home Affairs issued Circular No. 09/2004, which established two distinct categories for pension benefit eligibility. Clause 10.1 of the circular provided specific benefits to pensioners who retired on or before December 1, 2004. Clause 10.2 provided different benefits for officers who retired having served on December 1, 2004. The relevant governmental authorities, including the Director of Pensions and the Director of Establishments, determined that the Petitioner fell under the category defined in Clause 10.1. Consequently, the Petitioner was denied the benefits associated with Clause 10.2. The Petitioner challenged this decision by filing an application under Article 126 of the Constitution, alleging a violation of his right to equality under Article 12(1). The Petitioner contended that he was entitled to the benefits under Clause 10.2, asserting that his date of retirement was December 1, 2004. The Respondents acknowledged the retirement date but maintained that the Petitioner did not perform work on that day, thereby disqualifying him from the benefits reserved for those who served on that date. Issues Whether the Petitionerâ€™s retirement on December 1, 2004, without performing actual work on that day, entitles him to benefits under Clause 10.2 of Public Administration Circular No. 09/2004. Whether the classification of the Petitioner under Clause 10.1 of the circular constitutes a violation of Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1), Article 126. Cases Referred to: Prematilake v. Withanachchi - [(1998) 3 SLR] Analysis Issue 1: Entitlement to benefits under Clause 10.2 Rule applied: Clause 10.1 and Clause 10.2 of Public Administration Circular No. 09/2004; the principle of statutory interpretation regarding clear legislative intent. Court's reasoning: The court examined the text of the circular, noting that Clause 10.1 applies to those who retired on or before December 1, 2004, while Clause 10.2 applies to those who retired having served on December 1, 2004. The court observed that the Petitioner admitted he did not work on his 60th birthday, December 1, 2004. Relying on the interpretation of retirement dates established in Prematilake v. Withanachchi, the court clarified that while the Petitionerâ€™s retirement date is indeed December 1, 2004, the circular distinguishes between those who retired on that date without working and those who actually performed service on that date. The court held that the language of the circular is clear and must be applied as written. Finding: The Petitioner falls under the category defined in Clause 10.1 and is not entitled to the benefits conferred by Clause 10.2. Issue 2: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the governmental authorities correctly applied the provisions of Circular No. 09/2004 based on the Petitioner's actual service record. Since the classification was based on the objective criteria set out in the circular—specifically whether the officer performed work on the date of retirement—the decision did not constitute arbitrary or discriminatory treatment. Finding: There was no violation of Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the determination that the Petitioner does not meet the service criteria for Clause 10.2 under the first issue directly informs the conclusion that the administrative action was lawful, thereby negating the claim of unequal treatment under the second issue. Conclusion The court held that the Petitioner was correctly categorized under Clause 10.1 of the circular and that no constitutional violation occurred. The application is dismissed with no order as to costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Retirement benefits — Public service circulars — Interpretation of statutes — Date of retirement — Clause 10.1 vs Clause 10.2

Parties: L.A. Sudath Rohana and W.L. Livera v. Mohamed Cassim Mohamed Zeena and L.A. Sudharshana
Bench: Dr. Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Supreme Court Rules 1990 — leave to appeal — procedural law — compliance — preliminary objection — notice of appeal — High Court of the Provinces — mandatory requirements — technicalities — legal representation — due process — judicial discretion — administration of justice — statutory interpretation — appeal procedures
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for leave to appeal from an order of the Provincial High Court of the Southern Province, which had dismissed the petitioners' application on 24 March 2010. The petitioners, L.A. Sudath Rohana and W.L. Livera, sought to appeal this dismissal, claiming that the procedural requirements set forth in the Supreme Court Rules of 1990 were not applicable to their case. The respondents, Mohamed Cassim Mohamed Zeena and L.A. Sudharshana, raised a preliminary objection, asserting that the petitioners had failed to comply with Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules, which mandates that notice must be served to all parties involved. The petitioners contended that since their appeal was from the High Court of the Provinces, the specific rules governing such appeals did not require adherence to the Supreme Court Rules. The crux of the matter lay in whether the petitioners had adequately notified the respondents of their application for leave to appeal. Issues The primary issues before the court were: Whether the petitioners had complied with Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules 1990 regarding the notice of appeal. Whether the failure to serve notice through the Registry of the Supreme Court constituted a fatal flaw in the petitioners' application. Applicable Law Statutes: Supreme Court Rules 1990 — High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act, No. 54 of 2006 — High Court of the Provinces Act, No. 10 of 1996 Cases Referred to: Fernando v. Sybil Fernando and others - [1997] 3 Sri L.R. 1; Dulfer Umma v. U.D.C., Matale - (1939) 40 N.L.R. 474; A.H.M. Fowzie and 2 others v. Vehicles Lanka (Pvt.) Ltd. - (2008) B.L.R. 127; Samantha Niroshana v. Senarath Abeyruwan - S.C. (Spl.) L.A. No. 145/2006; K. Reaindran v. K. Velusomasundram - S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 298/99; N.A. Premadasa v. The People’s Bank - S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 212/99; Hameed v. Majibdeen and others - S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 38/2001; K.M. Samarasinghe v. R.M.D. Ratnayake and others - S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 51/2001; Soong Che Foo v. Harosha K. De Silva and others - S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 184/2003; C.A. Haroon v. S.K. Muzoor and others - S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 158/2006; Woodman Exports (Pvt.) Ltd. V Commissioner-General of Labour - S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 335/2008. Analysis Issue 1: Compliance with Rule 8(3) Rule applied: Supreme Court Rules 1990, Rule 8(3) Court's reasoning: The court meticulously examined the procedural requirements outlined in the Supreme Court Rules, particularly focusing on Rule 8(3), which mandates that a notice of appeal must be served to all parties involved in the proceedings. The learned Counsel for the respondent highlighted that the petitioners had failed to provide proper notice to the respondent through the Registry of the Supreme Court, which is a critical procedural step. The court noted that the petitioners had submitted a motion on 23 April 2010, but there was no evidence that notice had been served to the Registry, which is essential for ensuring that all parties are adequately informed and can participate in the proceedings. The court emphasized that procedural law is not merely a formality but serves to uphold the integrity of the judicial process. The petitioners argued that since their appeal was from the High Court of the Provinces, the specific provisions of the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act did not necessitate compliance with the Supreme Court Rules. However, the court found that the Supreme Court Rules apply universally to all appeals unless explicitly stated otherwise. The court referenced previous cases where non-compliance with similar procedural rules had led to the dismissal of applications, reinforcing the notion that adherence to procedural requirements is paramount in the judicial process. Finding: The court upheld the preliminary objection raised by the respondent, concluding that the petitioners had indeed failed to comply with the mandatory requirements of Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules, leading to the dismissal of their application for leave to appeal. Interaction of Issues The issues presented were interrelated, as the failure to comply with the procedural requirements directly impacted the court's ability to consider the merits of the petitioners' appeal. The court underscored that procedural compliance is essential for the administration of justice, and disregarding such requirements could undermine the legal process. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the petitioners' application for leave to appeal due to non-compliance with the Supreme Court Rules, specifically Rule 8(3), which mandates proper notice to all parties involved. The court emphasized the importance of adhering to procedural rules to ensure the fair and just administration of justice. No order was made as to costs, reflecting the court's recognition of the procedural issues at hand without penalizing the petitioners further. Keywords Supreme Court Rules 1990 — leave to appeal — procedural law — compliance — preliminary objection — notice of appeal — High Court of the Provinces — mandatory requirements — technicalities — legal representation — due process — judicial discretion — administration of justice — statutory interpretation — appeal procedures
Counsel: M. Farook Thahir with N.M. Reyaz for Respondents-Petitioners-Petitioners; Nilshantha Sirimanne for Plaintiff-Judgment-Creditor-Respondent-Respondent

Parties: Browns & Company PLC v. Minister of Labour and Others
Bench: J. A. N. de Silva, Chief Justice; Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J.; P. A. Ratnayake, P.C., J.
Keywords: Industrial Disputes Act — Arbitration — Employment Law — Transport Facilities — Grievances — Judicial Review — Wednesbury Unreasonableness — Equitable Relief — Reasonable Expectation — Contract of Employment — Official Transport — Compensation — Labour Rights — Minister of Labour — Commissioner of Labour — Arbitrator's Award
Summary: Facts The case revolves around Browns & Company PLC (hereinafter referred to as "Brown & Co."), which is a company incorporated in Sri Lanka. The relevant employees, who were initially employed as Engineering Executives in the Engineering Division of Brown & Co., were transferred to Browns Engineering (Pvt.) Ltd. on 1st January 1992. Their employment was subsequently terminated on 23rd November 1994 due to the closure of Browns Engineering. Prior to their termination, the employees raised grievances regarding the withdrawal of a company-maintained vehicle and associated expenses incurred for official travel. They sought compensation for these expenses, claiming Rs. 15,000 per month for the vehicle, Rs. 3,000 for a driver’s salary, and Rs. 5,250 for fuel. After unsuccessful attempts to resolve the dispute internally, the matter was referred to the Commissioner of Labour for conciliation in January 1995, which also failed. Consequently, the Minister of Labour referred the dispute to an arbitrator under the Industrial Disputes Act. The arbitrator conducted an inquiry from September 1997 to October 2002, during which Browns Engineering did not participate. The arbitrator concluded that the employees remained employees of Brown & Co. despite the transfer and were entitled to compensation for travel expenses. The arbitrator awarded each employee Rs. 270,000 for travel expenses from June 1992 until their termination. Brown & Co. challenged this award through a writ application, which was dismissed by the Court of Appeal. The Supreme Court granted special leave to appeal on several grounds, primarily focusing on the withholding of transport facilities. Issues Whether the Court of Appeal failed to consider that the claim for travel costs was not part of the employment contract; whether the employees were entitled to a company-maintained vehicle; whether the arbitrator's award was arbitrary and capricious; and whether the award was justified given the circumstances. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act No. 43 of 1950, as amended. Cases Referred to: Thirunavakarasu v. Siriwardena and others - [1986] 1 Sri LR 185; Brown & Co. Ltd., and Another v Ratnayake, Arbitrator and Others - [1994] 3 Sri LR 91; Associated Provincial Picturehouses v. Wednesbury Corporation - [1948] 1 KB 223; Council of Civil Service Unions v Minister for the Civil Service - [1985] AC 374. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Court of Appeal failed to consider that the claim for travel costs was not part of the employment contract. Rule applied: The principle of reasonable expectation in employment contracts and the nature of concessions provided by employers. Court's reasoning: The arbitrator determined that the employees had a reasonable expectation of continued transport facilities based on their previous employment conditions. The Court of Appeal upheld this view, emphasizing that the provision of a vehicle was a concession rather than a legal obligation. The arbitrator's award was deemed just and equitable, considering the employees had been provided with vehicles for both official and personal use prior to the withdrawal of this facility. The Court noted that the employees had incurred expenses due to the withdrawal of the vehicle and that the arbitrator's decision was supported by evidence. Finding: The Court affirmed the arbitrator's conclusion that the employees were entitled to compensation for travel expenses, despite the absence of explicit contractual terms. Issue 2: Whether the employees were entitled to a company-maintained vehicle. Rule applied: The concept of legitimate expectation in employment relationships. Court's reasoning: The arbitrator found that the employees had a reasonable expectation of continued access to company vehicles based on their prior arrangements. The evidence presented during the arbitration indicated that the employees had been using company vehicles for personal and official purposes, which created an expectation that such facilities would continue. The Court of Appeal agreed with the arbitrator's findings, noting that the withdrawal of the vehicle constituted a significant change in the employees' working conditions. Finding: The Court upheld the arbitrator's determination that the employees had a legitimate expectation of continued transport facilities. Issue 3: Whether the arbitrator's award was arbitrary and capricious. Rule applied: The standard of Wednesbury unreasonableness in judicial review of administrative decisions. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the basis of the arbitrator's award, which was calculated at Rs. 300 per day for travel expenses over 30 months. The Court found that the award was reasonable given the context of the employees' claims and the evidence presented. The arbitrator's decision was not deemed arbitrary, as it was based on the employees' testimonies and the historical provision of transport facilities. The Court emphasized that the arbitrator had a broad discretion in determining what was just and equitable in the context of industrial disputes. Finding: The Court concluded that the award was justified and not arbitrary or capricious. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as they all stem from the central question of whether the employees had a legitimate expectation of transport facilities and whether the arbitrator's award was justified based on the evidence presented. The Court's analysis of the contractual obligations and the nature of the employment relationship informed its conclusions on each issue. Conclusion The Supreme Court affirmed the judgment of the Court of Appeal, dismissing Brown & Co.'s appeal and upholding the arbitrator's award. The Court recognized the lengthy proceedings and the need for timely resolution of industrial disputes, highlighting the importance of equitable relief in employment matters. The Court awarded costs to the relevant employees for the appeal. Keywords Industrial Disputes Act — Arbitration — Employment Law — Transport Facilities — Grievances — Judicial Review — Wednesbury Unreasonableness — Equitable Relief — Reasonable Expectation — Contract of Employment — Official Transport — Compensation — Labour Rights — Minister of Labour — Commissioner of Labour — Arbitrator's Award
Counsel: A. R. Surendran, P.C., Nadarajar Kandeepan and K. Tharshini for Petitioner-Petitioner-Appellant. Yuresha de Silva, S.C., for 1st and 2nd Respondent-Respondent-Respondents. Rohan Sahabandu with Dulani Warsawewa for 4th – 6th Respondent-Respondent-Respondents.

Parties: Liyanage and Jayawardana v. Rajapakse and Others
Bench: J.A.N. De Silva, J.; P.A. Ratnayake, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Constitutional Council — Presidential immunity — Article 35 of the Constitution — Article 41 of the Constitution — Fundamental rights applications — Maintainability of applications — Attorney General
Summary: Facts The Petitioners in SC (FR) No. 297/2008, Sumanasiri G. Liyanage and Hettiarachchige Subash Ravi Jayawardana, challenged the failure of the President to appoint a Constitutional Council as mandated by the former Article 41A of the Constitution. The Petitioners in SC (FR) No. 578/2008, Centre for Policy Alternatives (Guarantee) Ltd. and Rohan Edirisingha, challenged the appointment of the 4th Respondent as Attorney General by the President, alleging that the appointment was made without the approval of the Constitutional Council as required by the former Article 41C of the Constitution. The Respondents raised preliminary objections regarding the maintainability of both applications, citing the immunity provisions under Article 35 of the Constitution. The Petitioners argued that the immunity granted to the President under Article 35(1) was not absolute and that the acts themselves remained justiciable. During the pendency of these proceedings, Article 41 of the Constitution was amended, effectively abolishing the Constitutional Council. The Court noted that the constitutionality of this amendment had already been determined by a five-member bench of the Supreme Court and that the amendment had become law. Issues Whether the President enjoys absolute immunity from being cited as a Respondent in legal proceedings under Article 35(1) of the Constitution for acts or omissions performed in an official capacity. Whether the applications are maintainable given that the impugned acts do not fall within the exceptions provided in Article 35(3) of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 3, 35, 38, 41, 41A, 41C, 42, 44, 80, 121, 126, 129, 130, 144. Cases Referred to: Ramupillai v. Festus Perera - [not specified] Perera v. Pathitranap - [sc-appeal-453/97] Wickremabandu v. Herath - [not specified] Karunatilleke v. Dissanayake - [2003/v1-sri-lr-172] Kumarathunga v. Jayakody - [1985/v2-sri-lr-124] Victor Ivan v. Hon. Sarath N Silva - [2001/v1-sri-lr-309] Mallikarachchi v. Siva Pasupathi - [1985/v1-sri-lr-74] Analysis Issue 1: Immunity of the President Rule applied: Article 35(1) of the Constitution, which provides that while any person holds office as President, no proceedings shall be instituted or continued against him in any court or tribunal in respect of anything done or omitted to be done by him in his official or private capacity. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the language of Article 35(1) is plain and unambiguous. Citing Mallikarachchi v. Siva Pasupathi, the Court affirmed that the President enjoys absolute immunity during his tenure. The Court rejected the Petitioners' argument that immunity applies only to the "doer" and not the "act," noting that the Constitution provides a specific mechanism under Article 38(2)(a) for addressing intentional violations of the Constitution by the President, which does not involve the institution of legal proceedings in court. Finding: The President is immune from being cited as a party in these proceedings, and the Court cannot, through judicial interpretation, limit the scope of this constitutional immunity. Issue 2: Maintainability of the Applications Rule applied: Article 35(3) of the Constitution, which specifies the limited instances where proceedings may be instituted against the President or the Attorney General in relation to the exercise of specific powers. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the impugned acts—the non-appointment of the Constitutional Council and the appointment of the Attorney General—did not fall within the narrow exceptions listed in Article 35(3), such as functions assigned to the President as a Minister under Article 44(2) or election-related petitions. Consequently, the proviso allowing for the institution of proceedings against the Attorney General was inapplicable. Finding: The applications were wrongly constituted because the President was improperly cited as a Respondent, and the requirements for maintaining the action against the Attorney General were not met. Interaction of Issues The findings on the two issues are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the President enjoys absolute immunity under Article 35(1) for the acts in question directly leads to the conclusion that the applications are not maintainable. Because the acts do not fall within the Article 35(3) exceptions, the procedural bar against citing the President is absolute, rendering the applications fatal in limine. Conclusion The preliminary objections raised by the Respondents are upheld. Both SC (FR) Application No. 297/08 and SC (FR) Application No. 578/08 are dismissed. No costs are ordered. Keywords Constitutional Council — Presidential immunity — Article 35 of the Constitution — Article 41 of the Constitution — Fundamental rights applications — Maintainability of applications — Attorney General

Parties: Sumanasiri G. Liyanage and Hettiarachchige Subash Ravi Jayawardana v. H.E. Mahinda Rajapakse and Others
Bench: J.A.N. De Silva, CJ.; P.A. Ratnayake, J.; Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: fundamental rights — Constitutional Council — Article 35 — Article 41A — Article 41C — judicial review — immunity of the President — justiciability — executive power — separation of powers — constitutional amendments — legal proceedings — appointment of Attorney General — public interest — political accountability — constitutional interpretation
Summary: Facts The Supreme Court of Sri Lanka was presented with two fundamental rights applications, SC (FR) 297/2008 and SC (FR) 578/2008, which were heard together due to their similar issues. The first application, SC (FR) 297/2008, was filed by Sumanasiri G. Liyanage and Hettiarachchige Subash Ravi Jayawardana, challenging the failure of H.E. Mahinda Rajapakse, the President of Sri Lanka, to appoint the Constitutional Council as mandated by the former Article 41A of the Constitution. The second application, SC (FR) 578/2008, involved the appointment of Lalith Weeratunga as the Attorney General, which the petitioners argued was done without following the necessary procedures and without the approval of the Constitutional Council as required by the former Article 41C. The petitioners sought various declarations and orders, including a declaration that the actions of the President constituted an infringement of their fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The respondents raised preliminary objections regarding the maintainability of the applications, citing Article 35 of the Constitution, which grants immunity to the President from being sued for actions taken in his official capacity. The Court noted that during the period between the filing of the applications and the hearing, Article 41 of the Constitution was amended, resulting in the dissolution of the Constitutional Council, which significantly impacted the relevance of the petitions. Issues The primary issues before the Court were: Whether the actions of the President in failing to appoint the Constitutional Council could be challenged in court given the immunity provided under Article 35 of the Constitution. Whether the appointment of the Attorney General without the approval of the Constitutional Council constituted a violation of the petitioners' fundamental rights. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka Articles 12(1), 35, 41A, 41C Cases Referred to: Ramupillai v. Festus Perera - [citation]; Perera et al. v. Pathitranap - [citation]; Senasinghe v. Karunatilleke - [citation]; Karunathilaka & another v. Dissanayake - [citation]; Kumarathunga v. Jayakody - [citation]; Victor Ivan v. Hon. Sarath N Silva - [citation]; Mallikarachchi v. Siva Pasupathi - [citation]. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the actions of the President in failing to appoint the Constitutional Council could be challenged in court given the immunity provided under Article 35 of the Constitution. Rule applied: Article 35 of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the provisions of Article 35, which grants the President immunity from legal proceedings in respect of actions taken in his official capacity. The Court noted that the immunity is intended to protect the President from harassment while in office, but it does not render unlawful acts lawful. The petitioners argued that the acts of the President could be challenged despite his immunity, citing previous judgments where the Court had reviewed the actions of the President and the Cabinet. The Court acknowledged that while the President enjoys immunity, the acts or omissions themselves are not immune from judicial scrutiny. The Court emphasized that the Constitution provides mechanisms for accountability, including the ability of Parliament to address alleged violations of the Constitution by the President. The Court ultimately concluded that the actions of the President in failing to appoint the Constitutional Council did not fall within the exceptions outlined in Article 35(3), which would allow for legal proceedings against him. Finding: The Court found that the applications were not maintainable due to the immunity granted to the President under Article 35. Issue 2: Whether the appointment of the Attorney General without the approval of the Constitutional Council constituted a violation of the petitioners' fundamental rights. Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Court considered the implications of the appointment of the Attorney General without the necessary approval from the Constitutional Council. The petitioners contended that such an appointment infringed upon their fundamental rights as it bypassed the constitutional requirement for checks and balances in the appointment process. The Court noted that the absence of the Constitutional Council due to the amendment of Article 41 rendered the petitioners' claims somewhat moot, as the mechanism for oversight was no longer in place. The Court reiterated that the fundamental rights guaranteed under Article 12(1) must be protected, but the lack of a functioning Constitutional Council complicated the ability to challenge the appointment effectively. The Court acknowledged the importance of maintaining the rule of law and the need for accountability in public office, but ultimately determined that the changes to the Constitution had rendered the petitioners' claims unviable. Finding: The Court concluded that the appointment of the Attorney General, while procedurally flawed, could not be challenged due to the constitutional amendments that had taken place. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in both applications were interrelated, as they both stemmed from the broader context of the constitutional amendments affecting the functioning of the Constitutional Council and the powers of the President. The Court's analysis highlighted the tension between the need for executive authority and the mechanisms of accountability established by the Constitution. The inability to challenge the President's actions due to his immunity under Article 35 further complicated the petitioners' claims regarding the appointment of the Attorney General. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed both applications, finding them not maintainable due to the immunity granted to the President under Article 35 of the Constitution. The Court emphasized that the changes to the constitutional framework had rendered the petitioners' claims ineffective, and therefore, no relief could be granted. The Court ordered that there would be no costs in the matter. Keywords fundamental rights — Constitutional Council — Article 35 — Article 41A — Article 41C — judicial review — immunity of the President — justiciability — executive power — separation of powers — constitutional amendments — legal proceedings — appointment of Attorney General — public interest — political accountability — constitutional interpretation
Counsel: M.A. Sumanthiran with Viran Corea, Suren Fernando and E. Tegal for Petitioners; N. Pulle, SSC for 2nd - 5th Respondents; A.P. Niles for 6th Respondent; Sanjeewa Jayawardene with Senany Dayaratne for 4th Respondent.

Parties: R.K. Obeyesekere and Others v. Milford Exports (Ceylon) Ltd. and Others
Bench: J.A.N. de Silva, CJ.; G. Amaratunga, J.; S. Marsoof, J.
Keywords: Companies Act — Civil Procedure Code — Substitution of parties — Cause of action — Joint actions — Summary procedure — Legal representatives — Right to sue — Oppressive conduct — Shareholder rights — Court procedures — Judicial interpretation — Legal standing — Heirs — Procedural requirements — Action termination
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal from an order of the Commercial High Court of Colombo concerning the proceedings initiated by the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd Petitioner-Respondents under the Companies Act No. 7 of 2007. The petitioners filed two actions on the same day, and during the proceedings, it was revealed that the 3rd Petitioner-Respondent had passed away on 15 October 2008, which was the date set for filing objections by the Appellant and the Respondent-Respondents. This led to a significant legal question regarding whether the action could continue without substituting the deceased party. The learned Counsel for the Petitioner-Respondents argued that the case could proceed without substitution, while the Appellant contended that substitution was mandatory under the Civil Procedure Code. The High Court ruled in favor of the Petitioner-Respondents, allowing the action to proceed, prompting the Appellant to appeal this decision. The Appellant's position was that the Civil Procedure Code's section 393 applied, which governs the survival of actions upon the death of a party, while the Petitioner-Respondents argued that the Companies Act provided a distinct procedural framework that limited the applicability of the Civil Procedure Code. Issues The primary issues raised in this case can be framed as follows: Whether the action could proceed without substituting the deceased Petitioner-Respondent? Whether the provisions of the Civil Procedure Code, particularly section 393, apply to the circumstances of this case? Whether the right to sue survives to the surviving Petitioners alone, or does it extend to the heirs of the deceased Petitioner? Applicable Law Statutes: Companies Act No. 7 of 2007 — Civil Procedure Code Cases Referred to: Duhilanomal and Others v. Mahakanda Housing Co. Ltd. - [1982 (2) SLR 504] Gajanand vs Sardarmal - [AIR 1961 Raj 223] Analysis Issue 1: Whether the action could proceed without substituting the deceased Petitioner-Respondent? Rule applied: Section 393 of the Civil Procedure Code Court's reasoning: The Court examined the implications of the death of the 3rd Petitioner-Respondent on the ongoing proceedings. The Appellant argued that the action could not continue without a substitution, citing section 393, which mandates that if a party dies, the action can only proceed if the right to sue survives to the remaining plaintiffs alone. The Court noted that the Petitioner-Respondents had not made an application for substitution, which the Appellant claimed was a fatal flaw. The learned President’s Counsel for the Appellant emphasized that the right to sue must survive to the surviving plaintiffs alone, and since the action was initiated jointly, the death of one party complicated the matter. The Court acknowledged the arguments presented but also recognized that the Companies Act provides a unique procedural context that may not align perfectly with the Civil Procedure Code. The Court ultimately concluded that the action could not proceed without addressing the substitution issue, as the death of a party fundamentally alters the standing of the remaining parties. Finding: The Court found that the action could not proceed without the necessary substitution of the deceased Petitioner-Respondent. Issue 2: Whether the provisions of the Civil Procedure Code, particularly section 393, apply to the circumstances of this case? Rule applied: Section 393 of the Civil Procedure Code Court's reasoning: The Court delved into the applicability of section 393, which governs the survival of actions upon the death of a party. The Appellant contended that the right to sue did not survive solely to the remaining Petitioners but also extended to the heirs of the deceased. The Court analyzed the language of section 393, which specifies that the right to sue must survive to the surviving plaintiffs alone. The Court referenced judicial interpretations of the term 'alone' and noted that it implies the ability of the surviving plaintiffs to continue the action independently. The Court also considered the implications of the Companies Act, which may provide a different procedural framework. The Court ultimately concluded that the circumstances of the case attracted the provisions of the Civil Procedure Code, specifically section 393, and that the right to sue did not survive to the heirs but rather to the surviving Petitioners alone. Finding: The Court determined that section 393 applied, and the right to sue did not survive to the heirs. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as the determination of whether the action could proceed without substitution directly impacts the applicability of section 393. If the action cannot proceed without substitution, then the question of whether the right to sue survives to the surviving Petitioners alone becomes moot. The Court's analysis of the procedural requirements under both the Companies Act and the Civil Procedure Code highlights the complexities involved in joint actions and the necessity of adhering to procedural norms. Conclusion The Supreme Court ultimately directed the termination of the proceedings in the two cases pending before the High Court, emphasizing the importance of following procedural requirements in legal actions. The Court's ruling underscores the necessity of substitution in cases where a party has died, and the implications of the Companies Act in relation to the Civil Procedure Code. The Court ordered appropriate costs to be awarded, reflecting the procedural missteps that led to the appeal.

Parties: Hemachandra v. Elpitiya Pradeshiya Sabha and Others
Bench: R.A.N.G. Amaratunge, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Appointment to public office — Interference with employment — Acting appointments — Usurpation of powers — Collusion of officials
Summary: Facts The petitioner, Poddiwela Hewage Thelma Kumari Hemachandra, was appointed as the Secretary of the Elpitiya Pradeshiya Sabha by the Southern Province Public Service Commission on 16.7.2008. Prior to this appointment, the 3rd respondent, D. Nilantha Jayaruk Panditha, had been acting in the position of Secretary. Upon reporting for duty on 21.7.2008, the petitioner was not permitted to assume the functions of her office by the 2nd respondent, Ranjith Jayasinghe, who was the Chairman of the Elpitiya Pradeshiya Sabha, and the 3rd respondent. The 2nd respondent requested the petitioner to seek a transfer to a different institution, alleging that her appointment was problematic and that he required the 3rd respondentâ€™s assistance for ongoing development projects. The petitioner refused this request and sought relief from the Supreme Court, alleging a violation of her fundamental right to equality under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The respondents contended that the petitioner had agreed not to assume duties due to a prevailing situation at the institution, a claim the court found unsubstantiated. Documentary evidence, including a directive from the Governor of the Southern Province, confirmed that the petitioner was the lawful appointee and that the 3rd respondentâ€™s acting mandate had expired. Issues Whether the 2nd and 3rd respondents violated the petitioner's fundamental right guaranteed by Article 12(1) of the Constitution by preventing her from assuming the duties of the office of Secretary. Whether the preliminary objections raised by the respondents regarding the non-joinder of the Provincial Public Service Commission and non-compliance with Supreme Court rules were valid. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Local Authorities Elections Ordinance, Section 65A(1) Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution, which guarantees the right to equality and equal protection of the law. Court's reasoning: The court found that the petitioner was lawfully appointed by the competent authority. The 3rd respondentâ€™s acting appointment was explicitly limited until a suitable person was appointed to the substantive post; therefore, his mandate expired upon the petitioner's appointment. The 2nd respondentâ€™s refusal to allow the petitioner to work, based on unsubstantiated claims of "prevailing situations" and gender-based bias, constituted arbitrary and unlawful conduct. The court rejected the 2nd respondent's assertion that the Governor had requested the petitioner to delay her duties, noting that evidence showed the Governor had actually directed that the duties be handed over to the petitioner. Finding: The 2nd and 3rd respondents acted in collusion to arbitrarily prevent the petitioner from exercising her lawful functions, thereby violating her fundamental right under Article 12(1). Issue 2: Preliminary Objections Rule applied: Supreme Court Rules regarding party joinder and procedural compliance. Court's reasoning: Regarding the non-joinder of the Provincial Public Service Commission, the court held that the Commission was not a necessary party as no allegations were made against it.
