Regarding the alleged failure to adhere to Rule 45 and the sufficiency of the affidavit, the court found that the respondents failed to provide any specific reasoning or evidence to demonstrate how the petitioner had failed to comply with these requirements. Finding: The preliminary objections were devoid of merit and were dismissed. Interaction of Issues The dismissal of the preliminary objections allowed the court to reach the merits of the fundamental rights claim. The finding that the 2nd and 3rd respondents acted arbitrarily and without legal basis (Issue 1) was reinforced by the fact that they could not provide a valid legal justification for their conduct, nor could they substantiate their procedural objections (Issue 2). Conclusion The court declared that the 2nd and 3rd respondents violated the petitioner's fundamental right under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The respondents were directed to immediately hand over the duties of the Secretary to the petitioner and were ordered to pay personal compensation and costs to the petitioner. Keywords Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Appointment to public office — Interference with employment — Acting appointments — Usurpation of powers — Collusion of officials

Parties: Upali Indrathilake Amadoru v. Officer-in-Charge, Special Criminal Investigation Unit and Others
Bench: Ms. S. Tilakawardane, J.; Ratnayake, J; Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: Criminal Procedure Code — Acquittal — Discharge — Section 186 — Section 314 — Cheating — Criminal Misappropriation — Judicial Discretion — Evidentiary Proceedings — Finality of Proceedings — Rights of the Accused — Summary Trial — Framing of Charges — Legal Interpretation — Case Law
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for special leave to appeal under Article 128 of the Constitution of Sri Lanka, stemming from a judgment of the Court of Appeal which upheld a decision made by the High Court of Chilaw. The appellant, Upali Indrathilake Amadoru, was charged with cheating, criminal misappropriation, and criminal breach of trust in connection with fraudulent activities involving cheques issued in his favor by an insurance policyholder. The charges were filed on 24 October 2001, and the appellant maintained his innocence throughout the proceedings. A significant turning point occurred on 11 September 2002, when the Magistrate's Court of Marawila quashed the plaint against the appellant, allowing for the possibility of a fresh plaint to be filed while simultaneously ordering his release. This order led to subsequent legal challenges, as the Officer-in-Charge of the Special Investigations Unit filed new charges against the appellant on 2 July 2003. The appellant contended that the original order amounted to an acquittal under Section 314 of the Criminal Procedure Code, thus barring any further prosecution. The legal questions presented to the Supreme Court were whether the Magistrate's order was made under Section 186 of the Criminal Procedure Code and whether it constituted an acquittal. Issues The Supreme Court was tasked with addressing two primary legal questions: (i) Whether the order dated 11 September 2002 was issued under Section 186 of the Criminal Procedure Code or its proviso; (ii) If the order was made under the proviso to Section 186, whether it amounted to an acquittal in terms of Section 314 of the Criminal Procedure Act. Applicable Law Statutes: Criminal Procedure Code of Sri Lanka, Section 186, Section 314. Cases Referred to: L.I.C. de Silva v. V.M.P. Jayatillake - [67 NLR 169]; Perera v. Officer in Charge, SCIB, Kalutara - [1999] 3 SLR 407; De Silva v. Jayatilake - [67 NLR 169]; Veerappan v. the Attorney-General - [72 NLR 361]; Fernando v. Excise Inspector, Wennappuwa - [60 NLR 227]; Premadasa v. T. E. R. Assen (Inspector of Police) - [60 NLR 451]; Don Abraham v. Christoffles - [55 NLR 135]; Edwin Singho v. Nanayakkara - [61 NLR 22]; Peter v. Cotelingam - [66 NLR 468]; Fernando v. Rajasooriya - [47 NLR 399]; Sumangala Thero v. Piyatissa Thero - [1937] 39 NLR 265. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the order dated 11 September 2002 was issued under Section 186 of the Criminal Procedure Code or its proviso. Rule applied: Section 186 of the Criminal Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court meticulously analyzed the nature of the order issued by the Magistrate's Court on 11 September 2002. The Court emphasized the distinction between a mere discharge and an acquittal, noting that the former does not equate to a determination of the merits of the case. The Court referenced the procedural requirements outlined in the Criminal Procedure Code, particularly the necessity for charges to be framed and evidence to be presented before a verdict can be rendered. The Court highlighted that the Magistrate's order did not reflect an adjudication on the merits, as it was issued prior to any evidentiary proceedings. The Court further examined the legislative intent behind Section 186, which allows for a discharge at any stage of the proceedings but mandates that an acquittal can only be granted if the Magistrate is satisfied that further proceedings would not result in a conviction. The Court concluded that the order in question was a discharge rather than an acquittal, as it did not stem from a consideration of the merits of the case. Finding: The Court determined that the order dated 11 September 2002 was not made under the proviso of Section 186 and did not amount to an acquittal. Issue 2: If the order was made under the proviso to Section 186, does it amount to an acquittal in terms of Section 314 of the Criminal Procedure Act? Rule applied: Section 314 of the Criminal Procedure Act. Court's reasoning: The Court further elaborated on the implications of an acquittal under Section 314, emphasizing that for a verdict to be considered an acquittal, there must be a substantive examination of the evidence presented. The Court reiterated that the absence of any evidentiary proceedings precludes the possibility of a valid acquittal. The Court referenced previous case law to illustrate that a mere discharge does not confer the finality associated with an acquittal. The Court underscored the importance of ensuring that the rights of the accused are protected, particularly in relation to the finality of criminal proceedings. Finding: The Court concluded that an order made under the proviso of Section 186 does not fall within the ambit of Section 314, as it lacks the requisite evidentiary foundation. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in this case are intricately linked, as the determination of whether the order constituted an acquittal directly impacts the applicability of Section 314. The Court's analysis of the nature of the order informs its conclusion regarding the rights of the accused and the procedural safeguards embedded within the Criminal Procedure Code. Conclusion The Supreme Court ultimately refused the appeal, affirming the judgment of the Court of Appeal. The Court held that the order made by the Magistrate did not amount to an acquittal and that the subsequent charges against the appellant could proceed. The Court emphasized the importance of adhering to the procedural requirements outlined in the Criminal Procedure Code to ensure the integrity of the judicial process. No costs were awarded. Keywords Criminal Procedure Code — Acquittal — Discharge — Section 186 — Section 314 — Cheating — Criminal Misappropriation — Judicial Discretion — Evidentiary Proceedings — Finality of Proceedings — Rights of the Accused — Summary Trial — Framing of Charges — Legal Interpretation — Case Law
Counsel: Dulindra Weerasuriya with Sanjaya Gunasekara for the Accused-Petitioner-Petitioner-Appellant. S. Kularatne, S.S.C., for the A.G.

Parties: J.D. Fernando v. Wasath Chandrasiri Gamlath
Bench: J.A.N. De Silva, CJ; Ekanayake, J; Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: Intellectual Property Rights — Copyright Infringement — Moral Rights — Passing Off — Code of Intellectual Property Act — Damages — Original Work — Musical Composition — Teledrama — Commercial Purpose — Distortion of Work — Inheritance of Rights — Permission for Use — Unjust Enrichment
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal from the judgment of the Commercial High Court of Colombo regarding the intellectual property rights of a musical composition. The Plaintiff, J.D. Fernando, is the widow of the late Mr. C.T. Fernando, who composed and performed the song 'Pinsuduwanne.' The Defendant, Wasath Chandrasiri Gamlath, included this song in a teledrama titled 'Mal Kekulak' without obtaining permission from the Plaintiff. The Plaintiff claimed that the Defendant's actions constituted a breach of her intellectual property rights under the Code of Intellectual Property Act No. 52 of 1979. She sought a declaration affirming her rights to the tune, damages for the unauthorized use, and an order against the Defendant for unjust enrichment. The Defendant denied the allegations, asserting that he sourced the song from a textbook published by the Educational Publishing Department in 1993. After a trial, the High Court acknowledged that the composition belonged to Mr. C.T. Fernando and that the Plaintiff inherited these rights. However, it ruled that the Defendant did not infringe upon the Plaintiff's rights, leading to the appeal. Issues The primary issues in this case can be framed as follows: Whether the Defendant infringed the Plaintiff's intellectual property rights by using the song without permission? Whether the Plaintiff is entitled to damages for the unauthorized use of the song? Applicable Law Statutes: Code of Intellectual Property Act No. 52 of 1979. Cases Referred to: University of London Press v University Tutorial Press [1916] 2 Ch 601; Sawkins v Hyperion Records [2005] EWCA Civ 565; Designers’ Guild v Russell Williams [2000] UKHL 58; Francis Day & Hunter v Bron [1963] Ch.587; Confetti Records v Warner Music [2003] EWCh 1274 (Ch). Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Defendant infringed the Plaintiff's intellectual property rights by using the song without permission? Rule applied: Section 19(1) of the Code of Intellectual Property Act No. 52 of 1979. Court's reasoning: The Court began by reiterating the importance of intellectual property rights, particularly in the context of musical compositions. It emphasized that the rights of authors are protected during their lifetime and for fifty years posthumously, as outlined in Section 19(1) of the Code. The Court noted that the Plaintiff had established her claim to the rights of the song as the widow of the late Mr. C.T. Fernando. The Defendant's argument, which claimed that he sourced the song from a textbook, was critically examined. The Court highlighted that the textbook only contained the lyrics and not the musical composition, which was the crux of the Plaintiff's claim. The Defendant's admission of using the song without permission was pivotal. The Court referenced the principles of copyright infringement, which necessitate that the Plaintiff demonstrate ownership of the rights and unauthorized use by the Defendant. The Court found that the Defendant's actions constituted a clear infringement of the Plaintiff's rights, as he had utilized the composition without obtaining the necessary permissions. The judgment also drew parallels to the concept of 'passing off,' indicating that the unauthorized use of the song could mislead the public regarding the ownership and rights associated with the work. Finding: The Court concluded that the Defendant had indeed infringed the Plaintiff's intellectual property rights by using the song without her permission. Issue 2: Whether the Plaintiff is entitled to damages for the unauthorized use of the song? Rule applied: The principle of unjust enrichment and the right to claim damages under the Code of Intellectual Property Act. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the Plaintiff's claim for damages, which amounted to Rs. 25,000. It acknowledged that the Plaintiff had suffered a loss due to the Defendant's unauthorized use of her late husband's composition. The Court emphasized that damages are intended to compensate the aggrieved party for losses incurred as a result of the infringement. The Plaintiff's claim was supported by the evidence that the Defendant had profited from the use of the song in a commercial context, which further substantiated her claim for damages. The Court also considered the moral rights associated with the work, particularly the right to object to derogatory treatment of the composition. However, it noted that the Plaintiff had not sufficiently proven any distortion of the work that would warrant additional claims under moral rights. The Court ultimately determined that the Plaintiff was entitled to the damages claimed, as the Defendant's actions had unjustly enriched him at the expense of the Plaintiff's rights. Finding: The Court awarded the Plaintiff Rs. 25,000 in damages, affirming her entitlement based on the infringement of her rights. Interaction of Issues The issues of infringement and entitlement to damages are intrinsically linked. The finding of infringement directly impacts the Plaintiff's right to claim damages, as the unauthorized use of her late husband's composition resulted in financial loss. The Court's analysis of the Defendant's actions established a clear connection between the infringement and the damages sought, reinforcing the legal principle that a party who infringes upon another's rights must compensate for the resulting harm. Conclusion The Supreme Court allowed the Plaintiff's appeal, varying the High Court's judgment regarding the finding of infringement. The Court awarded the Plaintiff Rs. 25,000 in damages and costs fixed at Rs. 10,500, thereby recognizing her rights under the Code of Intellectual Property Act and affirming the importance of protecting the rights of original creators in the face of unauthorized use. The judgment serves as a significant precedent in the realm of intellectual property rights, particularly in the context of musical compositions and the rights of heirs to claim damages for infringement. Keywords Intellectual Property Rights — Copyright Infringement — Moral Rights — Passing Off — Code of Intellectual Property Act — Damages — Original Work — Musical Composition — Teledrama — Commercial Purpose — Distortion of Work — Inheritance of Rights — Permission for Use — Unjust Enrichment
Counsel: Mahinda Ralapanawa with Chandima Gamage for Plaintiff-Appellant. Sumedha Mahawanniarachchi for Defendant-Respondent.

Parties: Hidwatte and Others v. Fernando and Fernando
Bench: J.A.N. De Silva, J.; N.G. Amaratunga, J; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J
Keywords: Constructive trust — Loan transactions — Absolute transfer — Beneficial interest — Attendant circumstances — Trusts Ordinance — Mortgage bonds — Unofficial money lenders
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff, G.C. Karunaratne Perera, initiated an action seeking a declaration that the 1st Defendant held property under a constructive trust for his benefit and requested the cancellation of Deed No. 3742. The Plaintiff originally owned the property and transferred it to Weda Malini Dharmalatha via Deed No. 147 in 1987 to secure a loan of Rs. 50,000, intending to retain beneficial interest. Subsequently, the Plaintiff negotiated a loan of Rs. 75,000 from the 2nd Defendant, which resulted in the execution of Deed No. 581 in favor of the 1st Defendant, the son of the 2nd Defendant. The Plaintiff remained in possession of the property throughout these transactions, constructed a house, and obtained utility services. The 2nd Defendant later attempted to claim ownership and transferred a share of the land to himself via Deed No. 3742. The District Court ruled in favor of the Plaintiff, finding that the deeds were not absolute transfers. The Civil Appellate High Court reversed this decision, prompting the Plaintiff to appeal to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Civil Appellate High Court misdirected itself in concluding that there was no evidence to establish a constructive trust. Whether the High Court erred in law by failing to consider the attendant circumstances surrounding the transactions as evaluated by the District Court. Whether the High Court erred in law by drawing an adverse inference from the Plaintiff's failure to deposit the loan amount in court at the time of instituting the action. Applicable Law Statutes: Trusts Ordinance, Section 83 Cases Referred to: Muttamma v. Thiagaraja - [62-nlr-559] Dayawathie v. Gunasekera - [1991/v1-sri-lr-115] Analysis Issue 1: Establishment of Constructive Trust Rule applied: Section 83 of the Trusts Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that since the first transferee (Dharmalatha) did not hold absolute title, she could not convey absolute title to the 2nd Defendant. Under Section 83, where it cannot be inferred that the owner intended to dispose of the beneficial interest, the transferee holds the property in trust. The evidence showed the Plaintiff never intended to part with beneficial interest, as evidenced by his continued possession and development of the land. Finding: The High Court erred in concluding that no constructive trust existed; the transactions were loan-based, not absolute transfers. Issue 2: Consideration of Attendant Circumstances Rule applied: Principles established in Muttamma v. Thiagaraja and Dayawathie v. Gunasekera. Court's reasoning: The court held that "attendant circumstances" include events preceding or following the transfer. The Plaintiffâ€™s continued possession, construction of a house, and the 2nd Defendantâ€™s visits to collect interest were sufficient evidence to demonstrate that the Plaintiff did not intend to part with his beneficial interest. Finding: The District Court correctly identified these circumstances, and the High Court erred by ignoring them. Issue 3: Requirement to Deposit Money Rule applied: Procedural law regarding constructive trusts versus specific performance. Court's reasoning: The High Court incorrectly equated a constructive trust action with a suit for specific performance of a sales agreement. There is no legal requirement to deposit the loan amount in court at the inception of an action to declare a constructive trust. Finding: The High Court misdirected itself in law by drawing an adverse inference from the Plaintiff's failure to deposit the funds. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected because the existence of a constructive trust (Issue 1) is supported by the attendant circumstances (Issue 2). Once the court determined the transaction was a loan and not a sale, the procedural requirement to deposit funds (Issue 3) became irrelevant, as the action was not one for specific performance. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the judgment of the District Court is affirmed. The Plaintiff is entitled to the reconveyance of the property upon payment of the principal sum of Rs. 75,000 with interest at 36% per annum for a reasonable period, excluding the duration of the litigation. Keywords Constructive trust — Loan transactions — Absolute transfer — Beneficial interest — Attendant circumstances — Trusts Ordinance — Mortgage bonds — Unofficial money lenders

Parties: G.C.Karunaratne Perera v. P.U.Sanath Fernando and P.U.Navaratne Fernando
Bench: J.A.N.de Silva CJ.; N.G.Amaratunga J.; R.K.S.Suresh Chandra J.
Keywords: constructive trust — absolute transfer — Deed No. 147 — Deed No. 581 — attendant circumstances — Trusts Ordinance — Muttamma v Thiagaraja — Dayawathie v Gunasekera — loan transactions — beneficial interest — possession of property
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a dispute concerning property ownership and the nature of certain transfer deeds executed between the parties involved. The Plaintiff, G.C.Karunaratne Perera, initiated legal action against the Defendants, P.U.Sanath Fernando and P.U.Navaratne Fernando, seeking a declaration that the first Defendant was holding the property under a constructive trust in favor of the Plaintiff. The Plaintiff claimed that the property originally belonged to P.H.Rodrigo, who had subdivided it, and after his death, it was conveyed to the Plaintiff by his widow and daughter. The Plaintiff, in need of funds, executed Deed No. 147 in 1987, transferring the property to Weda Malini Dharmalatha as security for a loan of Rs. 50,000, with the understanding that the beneficial interest would remain with him until the loan was repaid. Subsequently, the Plaintiff entered into another agreement with the second Defendant, who advanced Rs. 75,000, leading to the execution of Deed No. 581, which again appeared to be an outright transfer but was claimed by the Plaintiff to be a mere security arrangement. The Defendants contended that the Plaintiff had executed absolute transfers, and thus, there was no constructive trust. The District Court initially ruled in favor of the Plaintiff, but the Civil Appellate High Court overturned this decision, prompting the Plaintiff to appeal to the Supreme Court. Issues The Supreme Court was tasked with addressing several key legal questions: (i) Whether the Civil Appellate High Court misdirected itself by concluding that there was no evidence to establish a constructive trust from Weda Malini or the Defendants? (ii) Did the High Court err in law by failing to consider the District Court's satisfaction regarding the circumstances surrounding the transactions? (iii) Has the High Court misdirected itself in law by inferring from the Plaintiff's failure to deposit the borrowed money in Court to establish his bona fides? Applicable Law Statutes: Trusts Ordinance, Section 83 Cases Referred to: Muttamma v Thiagaraja (1961) 62 N.L.R 559; Dayawathie v Gunasekera (1991) 1 Sri LR 115. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Civil Appellate High Court misdirected itself by concluding that there was no evidence to establish a constructive trust from Weda Malini or the Defendants? Rule applied: The principle of constructive trust as outlined in Section 83 of the Trusts Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the nature of the transactions between the Plaintiff and the Defendants, emphasizing the importance of the surrounding circumstances that indicated the Plaintiff's intention to retain the beneficial interest in the property. The Court noted that the District Court had found sufficient evidence to support the Plaintiff's claim of a constructive trust, highlighting that the Plaintiff had not parted with possession of the property and had continued to invest in it, including constructing a house. The Court referenced the precedent set in Muttamma v Thiagaraja, which established that transfers appearing to be absolute could still be subject to a constructive trust if the intention to retain beneficial interest could be inferred from the circumstances. The Supreme Court criticized the Civil Appellate High Court for failing to adequately consider these attendant circumstances, which included the Plaintiff's continuous possession and the nature of the loan agreements. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the Civil Appellate High Court had indeed misdirected itself and that the evidence supported the existence of a constructive trust. Issue 2: Did the High Court err in law by failing to take into consideration that the District Court was satisfied with regard to the attendant circumstances surrounding the transaction between the parties? Rule applied: The principle of considering attendant circumstances in determining the nature of property transfers. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court reiterated the significance of attendant circumstances in assessing the intent behind property transfers. It emphasized that the District Court had thoroughly evaluated the evidence and concluded that the transactions were not absolute transfers but rather loans secured by the property. The Court criticized the High Court for overlooking the District Court's findings and for not recognizing the relevance of the Plaintiff's ongoing possession and the lack of any intention to part with beneficial interest. Finding: The Supreme Court found that the High Court had erred in disregarding the District Court's conclusions regarding the attendant circumstances. Issue 3: Has the said High Court misdirected itself in law by drawing an inference from the alleged failure to deposit the money in Court to establish the bona fides of the Appellant? Rule applied: The requirement of depositing money in cases involving specific performance. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court addressed the High Court's assertion that the Plaintiff should have deposited the borrowed amount in Court to demonstrate his bona fides. The Court clarified that this requirement typically applies to specific performance cases and was not relevant in the context of a constructive trust claim. The Court noted that the Plaintiff's request for the execution of a deed did not necessitate such a deposit, as the nature of the claim was fundamentally different. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the High Court had misapplied the legal standard regarding the necessity of depositing money in this context. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in this case are interrelated, as they all revolve around the interpretation of the transactions between the Plaintiff and the Defendants and the legal implications of those transactions. The determination of whether a constructive trust existed directly impacts the validity of the transfers and the rights of the parties involved. The Supreme Court's analysis highlighted the importance of understanding the context and intent behind the transactions, which ultimately informed its conclusions on each issue. Conclusion The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, set aside the judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court, and affirmed the District Court's ruling in favor of the Plaintiff, subject to a variation regarding the interest payable on the loan. The Court recognized the broader implications of the case, noting the prevalence of unofficial money lending practices and the challenges faced by borrowers in securing loans, which often lead to disputes over property ownership. The judgment underscored the need for careful consideration of the circumstances surrounding property transactions to protect the rights of individuals in similar situations. Keywords constructive trust — absolute transfer — Deed No. 147 — Deed No. 581 — attendant circumstances — Trusts Ordinance — Muttamma v Thiagaraja — Dayawathie v Gunasekera — loan transactions — beneficial interest — possession of property
Counsel: H.Withanachchi for Plaintiff-Respondent-Appellant; Ranjan Suwandaratne for Defendants-Appellants-Respondents

Parties: Rajaratnampillai Seyon v. Airport and Aviation Service (Sri Lanka) Limited and Others
Bench: J.A.N. De Silva, J.; N.G.Amaratunga, J; R.K.S.Suresh Chandra, J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Promotion — Merit-based recruitment — Public Administration Circular 15/90 — Interview process — English language proficiency — Leadership qualities — Problem-solving skills — Discrimination
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Rajaratnampillai Seyon, is a Security Officer employed by the 1st Respondent, Airport and Aviation Services (Sri Lanka) Limited, a government-owned public company. The Petitioner joined the service in 1981 and served in various capacities, receiving promotions and commendations. In 2008, the 1st Respondent called for applications for the post of Deputy Security Marshal. The Petitioner applied for the position and participated in a written examination, where he scored 83%, and a subsequent interview. The Petitioner alleged that the recruitment process was flawed and discriminatory. He contended that the interview process deviated from established procedures, that he was unfairly denied foreign training, and that the 1st Respondent failed to adhere to Public Administration Circular 15/90. Furthermore, the Petitioner alleged that the language policy during the examination and interview process was discriminatory. The 6th and 7th Respondents were ultimately selected for the two available vacancies. The Petitioner challenged the selection process, asserting that he was more qualified than the successful candidates and that his non-selection was a violation of his fundamental rights. The 1st Respondent maintained that the selection was based on objective criteria, including performance at the interview, and that the Petitionerâ€™s claims of discrimination were unfounded. Issues Whether the 1st Respondent was legally bound to comply with Public Administration Circular 15/90 in its recruitment process. Whether the interview and selection process for the post of Deputy Security Marshal was arbitrary, discriminatory, or flawed. Whether the Petitionerâ€™s fundamental rights were violated by the 1st Respondentâ€™s failure to promote him. Applicable Law Statutes: Companies Act Cases Referred to: Amadoruge Nanda Malani Perera v. Building Materials Corporation Ltd - [unreported] Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of Public Administration Circular 15/90 Rule applied: The principle established in Amadoruge Nanda Malani Perera v. Building Materials Corporation Ltd regarding the status of public companies. Court's reasoning: The court held that once a public authority converts into a limited liability company, it ceases to be a statutory authority and functions as a commercial entity under the Companies Act. Consequently, government circulars do not have automatic application to such entities unless the Board of Directors formally adopts them. As the Petitioner failed to prove that the 1st Respondentâ€™s Board had adopted the circular, the company was entitled to utilize its own recruitment system. Finding: The 1st Respondent was not bound by Public Administration Circular 15/90. Issue 2: Fairness of the selection process Rule applied: The standard of reasonableness for recruitment processes in public companies. Court's reasoning: The court found that the written examination served only as a screening tool to shortlist candidates for the interview. The interview was designed to assess leadership, problem-solving, and English proficiency, which were legitimate criteria for the position. The court noted that other candidates who scored higher than the 6th and 7th Respondents in the written examination were also not selected, undermining the Petitioner's claim of being singled out. The court also observed that the Petitionerâ€™s challenge was significantly delayed, as he filed the application only after the appointments were finalized. Finding: The selection process was not flawed or discriminatory. Issue 3: Violation of fundamental rights Rule applied: The constitutional requirement for non-discrimination and fair administrative action. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the Petitioner failed to establish that his non-selection was based on racial grounds or any other discriminatory motive. The 1st Respondent provided valid operational reasons for the training constraints and the interview structure. Since the selection was based on a set of criteria applied to all candidates, no violation of fundamental rights occurred. Finding: No fundamental rights violation was established. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected; the determination that the 1st Respondent is a commercial entity not bound by public circulars (Issue 1) provided the legal basis for the company to implement its own recruitment criteria. This, in turn, validated the interview process (Issue 2), which ultimately led to the conclusion that the Petitionerâ€™s fundamental rights were not violated (Issue 3). Conclusion The court found no evidence of discrimination or illegality in the recruitment process. The application of the Petitioner is dismissed. No costs are ordered. Keywords Fundamental rights — Promotion — Merit-based recruitment — Public Administration Circular 15/90 — Interview process — English language proficiency — Leadership qualities — Problem-solving skills — Discrimination

Parties: Marmba Liyanage Rohana alias Loku v. The Attorney General
Bench: J.A.N. de Silva C.J.; Amaratunga J.; Ratnayake J.
Keywords: abduction — rape — Penal Code — mandatory minimum sentence — judicial discretion — High Court — appeal — conviction — sentencing — prosecutrix — emotional impulse — reluctant witness — suspended sentence — Code of Criminal Procedure Act — constitutional conflict
Summary: Facts The accused-appellant, Marmba Liyanage Rohana alias Loku, was indicted in the High Court of Anuradhapura for the serious offences of abduction and rape. The first charge alleged that on 29 April 1999, he abducted R.M. Anusha Priyadarshani, a minor under the age of 16, from the custody of her lawful guardian, an act punishable under Section 354 of the Penal Code. The second charge was for the offence of rape, which was punishable under Section 364(2)(e) of the Penal Code as amended by the Penal Code (Amendment) Act No. 22 of 1995. This amendment introduced a mandatory minimum sentence of ten years for such offences, thereby limiting the court's discretion in sentencing. The trial commenced on 25 April 2006, nearly seven years after the alleged offences occurred. During the trial, the prosecutrix, who was a student in Grade 11 at the time of the incident, testified that she had a romantic relationship with the accused. Her mother disapproved of this relationship and pressured her to end it, leading to a hostile home environment. In a moment of desperation, the prosecutrix left home in her school uniform and met the accused, asking him to take her away. She threatened to commit suicide if he refused. The accused complied and took her to his uncle's house, where they stayed together for two days, engaging in sexual intimacy. They later moved to the accused's sister's house for an additional two days before being apprehended by the police. Notably, the prosecutrix had previously stated to the Judicial Medical Officer that she went with the accused willingly. After the prosecution presented its case, the accused chose not to testify or present any evidence in his defense. The trial judge found him guilty of rape under Section 364(2)(e) and sentenced him to the mandatory minimum of ten years rigorous imprisonment and a fine of Rs. 2500, with a default term of one year. The abduction charge was not addressed in the judgment. The accused appealed to the Court of Appeal against both the conviction and the sentence. While this appeal was pending, a High Court judge submitted a reference to the Supreme Court regarding the constitutionality of the mandatory minimum sentence imposed by Section 364(2)(e). The Supreme Court determined that the mandatory minimum sentence conflicted with Articles 4(c), 11, and 12(1) of the Constitution, allowing judges to exercise discretion in sentencing. The Court of Appeal, aware of this determination, subsequently reduced the accused's sentence from ten years to five years, considering the circumstances of the case, including the nature of the relationship between the accused and the prosecutrix. Dissatisfied with this reduction, the accused sought special leave to appeal to the Supreme Court on the issue of sentencing. Issues Whether the mandatory minimum sentence imposed under Section 364(2)(e) of the Penal Code infringes upon judicial discretion in sentencing. Whether the circumstances surrounding the relationship between the accused and the prosecutrix warrant a reduction in the sentence. Applicable Law Statutes: Penal Code, Penal Code (Amendment) Act No. 22 of 1995, Code of Criminal Procedure Act. Cases Referred to: SC Reference 3/2008, H.C. Anuradhapura Case No. 333/2004. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the mandatory minimum sentence imposed under Section 364(2)(e) of the Penal Code infringes upon judicial discretion in sentencing. Rule applied: The principle of judicial discretion in sentencing and its constitutional implications. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court's determination in SC Reference 3/2008 highlighted the conflict between the mandatory minimum sentence and the constitutional provisions that safeguard judicial discretion. The Court emphasized that the imposition of a mandatory minimum sentence restricts the ability of judges to tailor sentences to the unique circumstances of each case. The Court noted that the mandatory nature of the sentence could lead to unjust outcomes, particularly in cases where mitigating factors are present. The judgment elaborated on the importance of judicial discretion, stating that it is a fundamental aspect of the justice system that allows for a nuanced approach to sentencing. The Court's unanimous opinion asserted that the minimum mandatory sentence in Section 364(2)(e) is in conflict with the Constitution, thereby affirming that judges retain the authority to impose sentences that reflect the specifics of the case at hand. This determination was pivotal in the context of the accused's appeal, as it set the stage for a more equitable consideration of his circumstances. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the mandatory minimum sentence infringes upon judicial discretion, allowing for a more individualized approach to sentencing. Issue 2: Whether the circumstances surrounding the relationship between the accused and the prosecutrix warrant a reduction in the sentence. Rule applied: Consideration of mitigating factors in sentencing. Court's reasoning: During the appeal, the accused's counsel argued that the prosecutrix had actively encouraged the accused to take her away, asserting that he acted under emotional impulse rather than malicious intent. The Court examined the nature of their relationship, noting that the prosecutrix had threatened to commit suicide if the accused did not comply with her wishes. This context was crucial in understanding the dynamics of their interaction. The Court also considered the prosecutrix's reluctance to testify against the accused, which suggested a complex emotional landscape that could not be ignored. The judgment highlighted that while the accused was technically guilty of the offence, the circumstances surrounding the case indicated that a custodial sentence might not be appropriate. The Court acknowledged the emotional turmoil experienced by both parties and the fact that the prosecutrix had since moved on with her life, becoming a mother. Ultimately, the Court found that the accused's actions, while legally culpable, did not warrant a lengthy custodial sentence given the unique circumstances of the case. Finding: The Supreme Court determined that the sentence should be reduced to two years of rigorous imprisonment, suspended for ten years, reflecting the mitigating factors present in the case. Interaction of Issues The issues of judicial discretion and the specific circumstances of the accused's relationship with the prosecutrix are interrelated. The Supreme Court's determination regarding the unconstitutionality of mandatory minimum sentences directly influenced its analysis of the accused's appeal. By affirming the importance of judicial discretion, the Court was able to consider the mitigating factors that characterized the relationship, ultimately leading to a more lenient sentence. This interaction underscores the necessity of a flexible sentencing framework that accommodates the complexities of individual cases. Conclusion The Supreme Court set aside the five-year sentence imposed by the Court of Appeal and substituted it with a two-year rigorous imprisonment sentence, suspended for ten years. The fine and default term ordered by the trial judge were affirmed. The Court directed the High Court of Anuradhapura to ensure compliance with the stipulations regarding suspended sentences, thereby concluding the matter with a focus on justice tempered by the unique circumstances of the case. Keywords abduction — rape — Penal Code — mandatory minimum sentence — judicial discretion — High Court — appeal — conviction — sentencing — prosecutrix — emotional impulse — reluctant witness — suspended sentence — Code of Criminal Procedure Act — constitutional conflict
Counsel: A.S.M. Perera, President’s Counsel with Neville Ananda for the accused-appellant. Dileepa Peiris, Senior State Counsel for the Attorney General.

Parties: Maharaja Investment Ltd. v. Cross World (Pvt.) Ltd. and Others
Bench: J.A.N. De Silva, J.; K. Sripavan, J.; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Appeal from Court of Appeal — Decree execution — Civil Procedure Code Section 337 — Limitation period — Pronouncement of decree — District Court's role — Liquidated company — Service of notice
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent Company instituted action in the District Court of Colombo in 1989 seeking recovery of money and re-possession of properties from the Defendants. The District Court entered judgment in favor of the Plaintiff on 31 October 1994. The first Defendant-Petitioner appealed this decision to the Court of Appeal. The Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal on 18 November 1996 due to the non-appearance of the Appellant. The Registrar of the Court of Appeal communicated this dismissal to the District Court on 30 November 1996. Following the liquidation of the Plaintiff-Respondent Company, a liquidator intervened in the action. On 30 April 2009, the District Court read the order of the Court of Appeal in open court. The first Defendant-Petitioner subsequently moved to abate the action under Section 402 of the Civil Procedure Code, which the District Court rejected. The first Defendant-Petitioner then challenged the Plaintiff's attempt to obtain a Writ of Execution under Section 337(1) of the Civil Procedure Code, arguing that the ten-year limitation period for execution had expired. The District Court dismissed this application, holding that the ten-year period had not lapsed because the Court of Appeal judgment was formally pronounced in the District Court on 30 April 2009. The Civil Appeal High Court affirmed this decision, leading to the present appeal. Issues Whether the District Court order dated 2 October 2009, which permitted the execution of the decree, was contrary to the ten-year limitation period prescribed in Section 337(1) of the Civil Procedure Code. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 337(1) Civil Procedure Code, Section 402 Civil Procedure Code, Section 776(4) Provincial High Court (Special Provisions) Act No. 19 of 1990 (as amended by Act No. 54 of 2006) Cases Referred to: Kamanie Alles De Silva v. Wijewardane - [2002/v3-sri-lr-236] Rajadurai v. Emerson - [1995/v2-sri-lr-30] Don Jacovis v. Perera - [9-nlr-166] Analysis Issue 1: Compliance with Section 337(1) of the Civil Procedure Code Rule applied: Section 337(1) of the Civil Procedure Code, which mandates that no application for execution of a decree shall be granted after the expiration of ten years from the date of the decree or the date of the appellate decree affirming the same. Court's reasoning: The court examined the procedural history, noting that the delay in pronouncing the Court of Appeal order in the District Court was primarily due to the first Defendant-Petitioner changing addresses without notifying the court, thereby preventing the service of notice. The court interpreted Section 776(4) of the Civil Procedure Code, which requires the Court of First Instance to conform to and execute the decree of the Court of Appeal. The court reasoned that the ten-year limitation period under Section 337(1) commences when the proceedings are returned to the Court of First Instance and the decree is formally pronounced. Since the Court of Appeal order was read in the District Court on 30 April 2009, the ten-year period had not expired at the time the application for execution was made. Finding: The order dated 2 October 2009 was not contrary to Section 337(1) of the Civil Procedure Code, as the ten-year limitation period had not lapsed. Conclusion The Supreme Court held that the ten-year period for the execution of the decree commenced upon the formal pronouncement of the Court of Appeal's order in the District Court. Consequently, the appeal of the first Defendant-Petitioner-Petitioner was dismissed without costs. Keywords Appeal from Court of Appeal — Decree execution — Civil Procedure Code Section 337 — Limitation period — Pronouncement of decree — District Court's role — Liquidated company — Service of notice

Parties: Jayasiri Edirisinghe v. City Properties (Pvt) Ltd.
Bench: Tilakawardene, J.; Sripavan, J.; Imam, J.
Keywords: Commission — Brokerage Fees — Oral Agreement — Jurisdiction — Civil Procedure Code — Amendment of Pleadings — Compliance with Court Orders — Discretion of Court — Real Issues — Justice
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent, Jayasiri Edirisinghe, initiated legal proceedings against the Defendant-Petitioner, City Properties (Pvt) Ltd., seeking to recover a sum of Rs. 12 Million as commission or brokerage fees related to the sale of a property. The case was initially filed in the District Court but was transferred to the High Court of the Western Province due to jurisdictional objections raised by the Petitioner. The Petitioner contended that the Respondent was not entitled to the claimed commission as he did not act as a broker in the transaction. The Respondent's claim was based on an alleged oral agreement with the Petitioner. During the trial, the Petitioner objected to the Respondent's issue regarding the oral agreement, arguing that the plaint failed to specify the date of the agreement and the identity of the representative of the Petitioner with whom the agreement was made. The High Court Judge ruled that the Respondent must provide this information to allow the Petitioner to prepare an adequate defense. The Respondent did not appeal this ruling but sought to amend his plaint to include the necessary details. However, the Petitioner objected to the amendment, leading to further court orders requiring clarity on the identity of the representative involved in the alleged agreement. The Respondent's failure to comply with these orders led to the Petitioner seeking leave to appeal against the High Court's decisions. Issues The primary issues in this case can be framed as follows: Whether the Respondent's failure to specify the date of the oral agreement and the identity of the representative of the Petitioner constitutes a valid ground for dismissing the claim. Whether the High Court's orders requiring the Respondent to amend his plaint were justified and within the discretion of the court. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code Cases Referred to: None explicitly mentioned in the judgment. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Respondent's failure to specify the date of the oral agreement and the identity of the representative of the Petitioner constitutes a valid ground for dismissing the claim. Rule applied: The Civil Procedure Code requires that a plaint must disclose sufficient particulars to allow the defendant to prepare a defense. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court, in its judgment, emphasized the importance of compliance with court orders and the necessity for pleadings to clearly outline the issues at hand. The court noted that the Respondent's failure to specify the date of the oral agreement and the identity of the representative was a significant omission that hindered the Petitioner’s ability to defend against the claim. The court quoted the High Court's order, which stated, "The Petitioner has objected to the Respondent’s issue No. 2 on the basis that the Respondent has no right to raise such issue as he has not specified the date on which the oral agreement was entered into." This lack of specificity was deemed critical, as it prevented the Petitioner from adequately preparing its defense. The court further elaborated that the Civil Procedure Code mandates that a copy of any written agreement must be annexed to the plaint when the cause of action arises from such an agreement. The Respondent's failure to comply with this requirement was viewed as a failure to adhere to the procedural rules that govern civil litigation. The court highlighted that the Respondent had been given ample opportunity to amend his plaint in accordance with the court's directives but failed to do so adequately. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the Respondent's non-compliance with the court's orders justified the dismissal of the claim. Issue 2: Whether the High Court's orders requiring the Respondent to amend his plaint were justified and within the discretion of the court. Rule applied: The court has the discretion to allow amendments to pleadings, provided they do not change the character of the action. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court reiterated the discretionary power of the court to amend pleadings under Section 93 of the Civil Procedure Code, which allows for amendments that facilitate the administration of justice. However, this discretion must be exercised within the confines of Section 46(2) of the same code, which prohibits amendments that would alter the nature of the action. The court noted that the High Court had acted within its discretion by requiring the Respondent to clarify the identity of the representative involved in the oral agreement. The court stated, "The effect of the clarification sought by Court was merely to find out the real dispute between the correct parties which would facilitate the task of administering justice and will not cause any injustice to the petitioner." The Supreme Court emphasized that the trial judge's discretion should not be interfered with unless there is a clear abuse of that discretion. Finding: The Supreme Court upheld the High Court's orders as justified and within the bounds of judicial discretion. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in this case are interrelated, as the Respondent's failure to comply with the court's orders directly impacted the court's ability to adjudicate the matter fairly. The requirement for specificity in pleadings is essential to ensure that both parties can adequately prepare for trial. The court's insistence on clarity regarding the oral agreement and the parties involved reflects a broader principle of ensuring that justice is served through proper procedural adherence. Conclusion The Supreme Court ultimately dismissed the appeal, affirming the High Court's orders and emphasizing the importance of compliance with court directives in civil proceedings. The court made no order as to costs, directing the Registrar to forward the case record to the High Court for expeditious trial proceedings. Keywords Commission — Brokerage Fees — Oral Agreement — Jurisdiction — Civil Procedure Code — Amendment of Pleadings — Compliance with Court Orders — Discretion of Court — Real Issues — Justice
Counsel: Harsha Amarasekera with Kanchana Pieris for Defendant-Petitioner; Kuvera de Zoysa for Plaintiff-Respondent

Parties: Hatton National Bank Limited v. Rumeco Industries Limited and Thirunavukkarasu
Bench: R.A.N.G. Amaratunge, J.; S.I. Imam, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: Appeal from High Court judgment — Guarantee bond — Rescheduling of loan — Continuing security — Novation of contract — Civil Procedure Code — Materially different case at trial
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Appellant, Hatton National Bank Limited, instituted action against the 1st Defendant, Rumeco Industries Limited, and the 2nd and 3rd Defendants, Shirani Jasintha Thirunavukkarasu and Kangasabapathy Thirunavukkarasu, to recover sums due on a term loan. The 1st and 2nd Defendants failed to appear, and an ex parte judgment was entered against them. The 3rd Defendant contested the action, denying liability. In the plaint, the Plaintiff based its claim on a term loan granted on 28th May 1995. However, during the trial against the 3rd Defendant, the Plaintiff shifted its position, arguing that the 1995 loan was a rescheduling of a 1992 facility and that a 1992 guarantee bond, marked P5, acted as a continuing security for the 1995 loan. The 3rd Defendant maintained that the 1995 term loan was a distinct transaction not covered by the 1992 guarantee. The District Court dismissed the action against the 3rd Defendant, finding that the 1995 loan was not guaranteed by the 3rd Defendant. The Provincial High Court affirmed this decision, concluding that the 1995 loan constituted a novation of the earlier contract, thereby discharging the accessory obligations of the 1992 guarantee. Issues Whether the Provincial High Court erred in failing to find that the document P6 was a rescheduling of the loan P1. Whether the Provincial High Court erred in failing to find that the guarantee bond P5 was a continuing security. Whether the 3rd Defendant is liable for the debt under P5 and P6. Whether the Plaintiff was entitled to depart from its original pleadings at trial. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 150 Cases Referred to: Y.M.B.A. v Abdul Azeez - [Bar Journal 1997 Vol.VII Part I] Palaniappa v Saminathan - [17-nlr-58] Analysis Issue 1: Rescheduling of the loan Rule applied: The court applied the principle that a party cannot make a case at trial that is materially different from the pleadings placed on record. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the plaint specifically relied on a term loan granted in 1995. The attempt to characterize the 1995 loan as a "rescheduling" of a 1992 facility was a departure from the pleadings. Furthermore, the document P6 itself contained no reference to the 1992 guarantee or the alleged rescheduling. Finding: The court found no error in the High Courtâ€™s conclusion that the 1995 loan was a new contract and not a mere rescheduling of the 1992 facility. Issue 2: Continuing security and novation Rule applied: The court applied the doctrine of novation, noting that a novation discharges the original obligation and all accessory obligations, including suretyships. Court's reasoning: The court relied on the principle that when a new agreement is accepted, prior rights are extinguished. Because the 1995 loan was a new contract, the accessory obligations of the 1992 guarantee (P5) were discharged. The court held that the guarantee could not be construed as a continuing security for a subsequent, distinct term loan. Finding: The court determined that the guarantee P5 did not extend to the 1995 loan. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the determination that the Plaintiff could not deviate from its pleadings (Issue 1) reinforced the finding that the 1995 loan was a new, independent contract. Consequently, the conclusion that the 1995 loan was a novation (Issue 2) rendered the 1992 guarantee inapplicable, thereby resolving the question of the 3rd Defendant's liability in the negative. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed, and the judgments of the High Court and the District Court are affirmed. The Plaintiff-Appellant is ordered to pay costs fixed at Rs. 31,500/-. Keywords Appeal from High Court judgment — Guarantee bond — Rescheduling of loan — Continuing security — Novation of contract — Civil Procedure Code — Materially different case at trial

Parties: Wijesekera and Others v. Lokuge and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; S.I. Imam, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: Preliminary objections — Time bar — Locus standi — Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Sports regulations — Selection committee — Captaincy appointment — Arbitrary decision making
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, H. Dilanka Wijesekera, along with fourteen others, filed a Fundamental Rights Application on 29th April 2009. The other fourteen petitioners subsequently withdrew their applications, leaving the case to proceed solely on the application of the first petitioner. The petitioner alleged that his fundamental rights were infringed by the suspension of his membership from the Sri Lanka Rugby Football Union team. This suspension allegedly occurred because the petitioner refused to participate in the Asian Rugby Football Union Five Nations Division Rugby Tournament in Dubai, citing that his captaincy had been unfairly overlooked in favor of a partisan appointment. The respondents raised three preliminary objections, arguing that the application was time-barred, that the petitioner lacked locus standi, and that the petitioner failed to demonstrate an infringement of rights under Articles 12(1), 12(2), and 14(1)(g) of the Constitution. Issues Whether the application is time-barred under Article 126(2) of the Constitution. Whether the petitioner has the necessary locus standi to maintain the application. Whether the petitioner has demonstrated a prima facie case of infringement of fundamental rights under Articles 12(1), 12(2), and 14(1)(g) to warrant a full hearing. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 12(1), 12(2), 13(4), 14(1)(g), 17, 126(1), 126(2). Cases Referred to: Gamaethige v. Siriwardena - [1988/v1-sri-lr-384] Sugathapala Mendis v. Chandrika Bandaranaike Kumarathunga - [sc-fr-352/2007] Narendrakumar v. Ziyard - [2000/v1-sri-lr-251] Bulankulama v. Secretary, Ministry of Industrial Development - [2000/v3-sri-lr-243] Jayantha Adikari Egodawele v. Dayananda Dissanayake - [frd-2-292] Kottabadu Durage Sriyani Silva v. Chanaka Iddamalgoda - [sc-fr-471/2000] Analysis Issue 1: Time-bar under Article 126(2) Rule applied: Article 126(2) of the Constitution, which requires a fundamental rights petition to be filed within one month of the alleged infringement. Court's reasoning: While the court acknowledged that the one-month rule is mandatory, it held that the violation in this case was of a "continuing nature." Relying on the principle established in Sugathapala Mendis v. Chandrika Bandaranaike Kumarathunga, the court reasoned that where a violation persists over time, the initial date of an administrative order cannot be the sole determinant for the commencement of the limitation period. Finding: The objection was dismissed; the court held the application was not time-barred due to the continuing nature of the alleged violation. Issue 2: Locus standi Rule applied: Articles 17 and 126 of the Constitution regarding the right to invoke constitutional remedies. Court's reasoning: The court rejected the narrow interpretation of standing proposed by the respondents. Citing Bulankulama v. Secretary, Ministry of Industrial Development and Jayantha Adikari Egodawele v. Dayananda Dissanayake, the court emphasized that fundamental rights often possess both individual and collective aspects. It held that in matters of public importance, particularly those affecting the youth and sports, the court must adopt a broader interpretation of "person" to ensure that the interests of justice are served. Finding: The petitioner has the necessary locus standi to maintain the application. Issue 3: Prima facie infringement Rule applied: Articles 12(1), 12(2), and 14(1)(g) of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the allegations regarding arbitrary and biased decision-making by the executive in the context of sports administration raised serious questions. It held that such matters should not be dismissed in limine without allowing the petitioner to present his case, as the rule of law requires transparency and accountability in executive actions. Finding: The petitioner is entitled to a hearing on the merits of the alleged infringements. Interaction of Issues The court's findings on standing and the nature of the violation are interconnected; by characterizing the violation as a matter of public interest affecting the youth, the court justified both the expansion of locus standi and the rejection of a strict, formalistic application of the time-bar rule. Conclusion The preliminary objections raised by the respondents are dismissed. The case is to be fixed for support to proceed to a hearing on the merits. Keywords Preliminary objections — Time bar — Locus standi — Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Sports regulations — Selection committee — Captaincy appointment — Arbitrary decision making

Parties: Habib Bank AG Zurich v. M.S.Hebtulabhoy & Co. Ltd.
Bench: R.A.N.G. Amaratunge, J.; S.I. Imam, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: Substitution of parties — Curing of defective proxy — Section 404 of the Civil Procedure Code — Jurisdictional objections — Acquiescence to jurisdiction — Delay in raising objections — Effect of substitution
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff Bank initiated an action in November 2001 against the Defendant for the recovery of 89.3 Million Rupees. The Defendant filed an answer in February 2004. In January 2005, the Substituted Plaintiff applied for substitution under Section 404 of the Civil Procedure Code, citing the transfer of the original Plaintiff's business. The Commercial High Court allowed this substitution in August 2005, and a subsequent challenge to this order by the Defendant was dismissed by the Supreme Court. In January 2006, the Defendant raised an objection regarding the validity of the original proxy filed by the Plaintiff. Following this, the Plaintiff and Substituted Plaintiff filed multiple fresh proxies to cure alleged technical defects. In January 2008, the Substituted Plaintiff filed a petition seeking the acceptance of a corrected proxy dated 9th January 2008. The Commercial High Court refused to accept this proxy on the basis that the original Plaintiff was no longer a party to the action. The Substituted Plaintiff appealed this order to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Commercial High Court erred in holding that the substitution of the Substituted Plaintiff created a legal bar to curing defects in the original proxy. Whether the Substituted Plaintiff was entitled to tender a proxy of the original Plaintiff to regularize the record. Whether a defect in a proxy can be cured if the intent to grant authority is evident. Whether a substituted party can rectify an error in the original proxy and tender a new proxy for the original party. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 27 Civil Procedure Code, Section 404 Cases Referred to: Udeshi v. Mather - [1988/v1-sri-lr-12] Paul Coir (Pvt) Ltd v. Waas - [2002/v1-sri-lr-13] Pinto v. Trelleborg Lanka (Pvt) Ltd - [2003/v3-sri-lr-214] S.P. Gunethilake v. Sunil Ekanayake - [sc-26/2009] Analysis Issue 1, 2, 3, and 4: Validity of Proxy and Curing Defects Rule applied: Section 27 of the Civil Procedure Code and the principle of acquiescence to jurisdiction. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that the Defendant raised the objection regarding the proxy approximately five years after the action was filed and only after the substitution of the Plaintiff had been affirmed by the Supreme Court. Citing S.P. Gunethilake v. Sunil Ekanayake, the Court emphasized that jurisdictional objections must be taken at the first opportunity; failure to do so constitutes acquiescence. Furthermore, the Court noted that the same Attorneys-at-Law were authorized throughout the proceedings, demonstrating a clear intent to grant authority, which is consistent with the principles established in Udeshi v. Mather, Paul Coir (Pvt) Ltd v. Waas, and Pinto v. Trelleborg Lanka (Pvt) Ltd. The Court held that the substitution of a party does not prevent the curing of technical defects in a proxy, and the High Court erred in refusing the proxy on the basis that the original Plaintiff was no longer a party. Finding: The High Court erred in its refusal to accept the proxy. The objection was raised too late, and the law allows for the curing of technical defects in proxies to ensure the progress of the trial. Interaction of Issues The issues are inextricably linked as they all pertain to the procedural validity of the representation of the Plaintiff. The Court's finding that the Defendant acquiesced to the jurisdiction by failing to raise the proxy objection at the earliest opportunity renders the technical arguments regarding the substitution's effect on the proxy moot. The court's determination that the intent to authorize the attorneys was consistent throughout the litigation reinforces the conclusion that the technical defects were curable. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the order of the Commercial High Court is set aside. The High Court is directed to proceed with the trial expeditiously, with each party bearing its own costs. Keywords Substitution of parties — Curing of defective proxy — Section 404 of the Civil Procedure Code — Jurisdictional objections — Acquiescence to jurisdiction — Delay in raising objections — Effect of substitution

Parties: Nisansala v. Rose Aberfoyle
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; K. Sripavan, J.; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Constructive trust — Trusts Ordinance — Sections 4(1) and 98 — Lawful purpose — Unlawful purpose — Breach of Revenue Law — Tax evasion — Bona fide purchaser — Financial Act
Summary: Facts The plaintiff-respondent, a foreign national, provided funds to the defendant-appellant to purchase a property in Sri Lanka. The property was purchased in the name of the defendant-appellant via Deed No. 386. The plaintiff-respondent contended that he provided the funds for the purchase on his own behalf to avoid a 100% tax imposed on foreign nationals under the Finance Act, with the intention of later transferring the property to a company he intended to incorporate. Prior to the execution of the deed, the defendant-appellant signed a document undertaking to transfer the land to the plaintiff-respondent or his nominee upon request. The defendant-appellant refused to transfer the property, claiming the purchase was a gift from the plaintiff-respondent and that she had invested significant funds into renovating and cultivating the land. The plaintiff-respondent initiated legal action to enforce the trust. The District Court of Mawanella ruled in favor of the plaintiff-respondent, and the Provincial High Court (Civil Appeal) of the Sabaragamuwa Province affirmed this decision. The defendant-appellant appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the plaintiff-respondent could plead a constructive trust in the circumstances of the case. Whether the alleged trust is contrary to the provisions of Sections 4(1) and 98 of the Trusts Ordinance. Applicable Law Statutes: Trusts Ordinance Finance Act No. 11 Cases Referred to: Muniyandy Natchie v. Kayambo - [1988/v2-calr-56] Fernando v. Ramanathan - [16-nlr-337] Mohideen v. Saibo - [17-nlr-17] Georgiades v. Klompje - [1943-tpd-15] Jajbhay v. Cassim - [1939-sa-537] Analysis Issue 1: Constructive Trust Rule applied: The definition of a trust as a relationship where a trustee is compelled in equity to hold property for the benefit of another, as referenced via Keeton (Trust, 1971). Court's reasoning: The court examined the document signed by the defendant-appellant, which explicitly stated she received funds from the plaintiff-respondent to purchase the land on his behalf and undertook to transfer it to his nominee. The court found this established the elements of a constructive trust, as the defendant-appellant held the legal title while the beneficial interest remained with the plaintiff-respondent. Finding: The plaintiff-respondent was entitled to plead a constructive trust. Issue 2: Legality under Trusts Ordinance Rule applied: Sections 4(1) and 98 of the Trusts Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The court analyzed whether the intent to avoid tax under the Finance Act rendered the trust unlawful under Section 4(1). Relying on the principles in Muniyandy Natchie v. Kayambo, the court held that a breach of revenue regulations does not automatically render a contract illegal or void. The court noted that the Finance Act provides specific mechanisms for the Commissioner of Inland Revenue to recover taxes in default, rather than invalidating the underlying property transaction. Furthermore, no evidence was presented to show the transaction was immoral, fraudulent, or contrary to public policy. Finding: The trust is not contrary to Sections 4(1) and 98 of the Trusts Ordinance. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing; because the transaction was not rendered illegal by the revenue laws (Issue 2), the court was able to recognize the validity of the constructive trust (Issue 1). The court determined that the existence of a tax liability does not preclude the equitable enforcement of a trust, as the tax authorities retain their statutory power to recover dues regardless of the trust arrangement. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed and the judgment of the Provincial High Court (Civil Appeal) of the Sabaragamuwa Province is affirmed. No order is made as to costs. Keywords Constructive trust — Trusts Ordinance — Sections 4(1) and 98 — Lawful purpose — Unlawful purpose — Breach of Revenue Law — Tax evasion — Bona fide purchaser — Financial Act

Parties: L.H.G.Elias v. Muhajid A.Cader and Anton Gajasinghe
Bench: Tilakawardane J; Amaratunga J; Suresh Chandra J.
Keywords: Power of Attorney — Legal Authority — Civil Procedure Code — Technical Objections — Damages — Construction Law — Ratification — Judicial Efficiency — Rights of Parties — Court of Justice
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a dispute between L.H.G.Elias, the Plaintiff, and the Defendants, Muhajid A.Cader and Anton Gajasinghe, regarding alleged structural damages to the Plaintiff's property caused by construction activities on the Defendants' premises. The Plaintiff, residing in Australia, initiated legal proceedings through his attorney, B.V.A.Dekker, claiming damages amounting to Rs.2,016,783/- due to deep excavations conducted by the 1st Defendant, A.Cader, on his property. The 1st Defendant denied the allegations and counterclaimed for damages, asserting that the Plaintiff's action was barred by prescription. The 2nd Defendant, Gajasinghe, who was the contractor, also denied liability and contended that the Power of Attorney submitted by the Plaintiff only authorized action against the 1st Defendant. The District Court initially ruled in favor of the Plaintiff, allowing the action against both Defendants. However, the 2nd Defendant appealed to the Civil Appellate High Court, which overturned the District Court's decision, leading to the present appeal before the Supreme Court. Issues The Supreme Court was tasked with addressing several critical issues: Whether the Provincial High Court erred in determining that the action was filed by the Plaintiff through his lawful attorney, B.V.A.Dekker. Whether the Power of Attorney marked P1 granted B.V.A.Dekker the authority to file action against the 2nd Defendant. Whether the subsequent Power of Attorney marked P12 ratified the actions of B.V.A.Dekker. Whether the objections raised by the 2nd Defendant regarding the validity of the Power of Attorney were belated. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code Cases Referred to: Gricilda Hewa v Thomas Hewa - [1998 (3) SLLR 43]; Actalina Fonseka v Dharshani Fonseka - [1989 (2) SLR 95] Analysis Issue 1: Whether the action was filed by the Plaintiff through his lawful attorney, B.V.A.Dekker. Rule applied: The validity of the Power of Attorney and its implications on the authority to institute legal proceedings. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the nature of the Power of Attorney marked P1, which explicitly authorized B.V.A.Dekker to institute action against the 1st Defendant, A.Cader. The Court noted that the Power of Attorney contained both special and general powers, with the special power specifically allowing action against the 1st Defendant. The Court emphasized that the general power granted the attorney authority to act on behalf of the Plaintiff in various matters, which could encompass actions against both Defendants. The Court further highlighted that the second Power of Attorney, marked P12, was granted out of an abundance of caution and served to ratify the actions taken under the first Power of Attorney. The Court criticized the Civil Appellate High Court's interpretation of the first Power of Attorney as inadequate, asserting that it was sufficient to authorize action against both Defendants. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the Plaintiff had the authority to maintain the action against both Defendants based on the powers conferred in the original Power of Attorney. Issue 2: Whether the objections raised by the 2nd Defendant regarding the validity of the Power of Attorney were belated. Rule applied: The procedural requirements for raising objections in civil proceedings. Court's reasoning: The Court noted that the 2nd Defendant's objections were highly technical and should have been raised at an earlier stage, specifically before filing an answer, as per Section 46(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. The Court expressed concern that such technical objections often delay justice and contribute to the backlog of cases in the judicial system. The Court emphasized the need for parties to focus on the merits of their cases rather than on procedural technicalities that do not substantially affect the rights of the parties involved. Finding: The Supreme Court found that the 2nd Defendant's objections were belated and should not have impeded the Plaintiff's right to pursue his action. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in this case are interrelated, as the validity of the Power of Attorney directly impacts the Plaintiff's ability to maintain his action against both Defendants. The Court's analysis of the authority granted to B.V.A.Dekker under the Power of Attorney also addressed the procedural propriety of the 2nd Defendant's objections, highlighting the importance of timely and relevant legal arguments in civil litigation. Conclusion The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, set aside the judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court, and affirmed the District Court's order permitting the Plaintiff to continue his action against both Defendants. The Court directed the District Court to expedite the proceedings, emphasizing the need for timely justice in civil matters. The 1st Defendant was ordered to pay Rs.10,500/- in costs, while the 2nd Defendant was ordered to pay Rs.21,500/- in costs to the Plaintiff. Keywords Power of Attorney — Legal Authority — Civil Procedure Code — Technical Objections — Damages — Construction Law — Ratification — Judicial Efficiency — Rights of Parties — Court of Justice
Counsel: V.K.Choksy for Plaintiff-Respondent-Petitioner; K.K.Farooq for 1st Defendant-Respondent-Respondent; M.Farook Thahir with N.M.Reyaz for 2nd Defendant-Petitioner-Respondent

Parties: Duraisamy and Others v. Tharmaratnam
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J; P. A. Ratnayake, P.C., J; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Appeal procedure — Civil Procedure Code — Section 754 — Rejection of plaint — Final order vs interlocutory order — Decree definition — Judicial precedent — Rajendran Chettiar case
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Appellant-Respondent instituted an action in the District Court of Jaffna against the 1st and 2nd Defendant-Respondent-Appellants and the 3rd Defendant-Respondent-Respondent to recover damages for loss of reputation. The District Judge rejected the plaint in terms of Section 46(2)(i) of the Civil Procedure Code, holding that the action was barred by a positive rule of law regarding the immunity of Attorneys-at-law for statements made in pleadings. The Respondent filed an appeal against this rejection in the Provincial High Court of the Northern Province, following the procedure set out in Section 754(1) of the Civil Procedure Code. The Appellants raised a preliminary objection, contending that the order was not a final judgment and that the Respondent should have sought leave to appeal under Section 754(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. The Provincial High Court overruled the preliminary objection and set the appeal for hearing on its merits. The Appellants subsequently sought and were granted leave to appeal to the Supreme Court against the order of the Provincial High Court. Issues Whether the order of the District Court rejecting a plaint under Section 46(2)(i) of the Civil Procedure Code constitutes a final judgment or an interlocutory order within the meaning of Section 754(5) of the Civil Procedure Code. Whether the correct procedure for appealing against an order rejecting a plaint is the procedure set out in Section 754(1) or Section 754(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code High Court of Provinces (Special Provisions) (Amendments) Act No. 54 Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka Cases Referred to: S. Rajendran Chettiar v. S. Narayanan Chettiar - [sc-appeal-101a/2009] Siriwardene v. Air Ceylon - [1984/v1-sri-lr-286] Ranjit v. Kusumawathie - [1998/v3-sri-lr-232] Salaman v. Warner - [1891/1-qb-734] Bozson v. Altrincham Urban District Council - [1903/1-kb-547] Salter Rex and Co. v. Ghosh - [1971/2-all-er-865] Shubrook v. Tufnell - [1882/9-qbd-621] Analysis Issue 1: Nature of the order Rule applied: Section 754(5) of the Civil Procedure Code and the test of whether an order finally disposes of the rights of the parties. Court's reasoning: The Court applied the precedent established in S. Rajendran Chettiar v. S. Narayanan Chettiar, which held that an order rejecting a plaint under Section 46(2) is not a final judgment. The Court reasoned that such an order does not finally dispose of the rights of the parties because Section 46(2) expressly permits a plaintiff to present a fresh plaint for the same cause of action. Finding: The order rejecting the plaint is an interlocutory order, not a final judgment. Issue 2: Correct appellate procedure Rule applied: Sections 754(1) and 754(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Court rejected the Respondentâ€™s argument that Section 754(3) creates a third gateway for appeal based on the definition of "decree" in Section 5. The Court held that the Civil Procedure Code provides only two gateways: Section 754(1) for final judgments and Section 754(2) for interlocutory orders requiring leave of court. Since the rejection of a plaint is an interlocutory order, the procedure under Section 754(2) is mandatory. Finding: The Respondent failed to follow the correct procedure; the appeal should have been filed under Section 754(2) with leave of court. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the classification of the order as interlocutory (Issue 1) necessitates the application of the leave-to-appeal procedure (Issue 2). The Courtâ€™s determination that the order is not a final judgment effectively precludes the use of the direct appeal procedure under Section 754(1), reinforcing the conclusion that the appeal was improperly constituted. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the order of the Provincial High Court dated 26th March 2009 is set aside. The appeal filed by the Respondent to the Provincial High Court is dismissed. Keywords Appeal procedure — Civil Procedure Code — Section 754 — Rejection of plaint — Final order vs interlocutory order — Decree definition — Judicial precedent — Rajendran Chettiar case

Parties: Jinadasa v. Hemamali and Others
Bench: Dr. Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; P.A. Ratnayake, P.C., J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: leave to appeal — Supreme Court Rules — preliminary objection — time limit — High Court judgment — application for leave — reasonable time — discretion of the Court — compliance with rules — mitigating factors — bereavement — legal representation — civil procedure — appeal process — mandatory provisions
