Summary: Facts This case revolves around an application for leave to appeal from a judgment delivered by the High Court of the Western Province (Civil Appeals) in Gampaha on 15 July 2008. The original plaintiff-appellant, Jamburegoda Gamage Lakshman Jinadasa, had passed away on 15 August 2008, shortly after the High Court's decision to dismiss his appeal. Following his death, his widow, Priyanthi Chandrika Jinadasa, filed an application for leave to appeal to the Supreme Court on 1 September 2008. The defendants-respondents raised a preliminary objection, asserting that the application was filed out of time, as it was submitted after the six-week period stipulated by the Supreme Court Rules of 1990. The petitioner contended that the rules did not apply to her situation and that the application should be considered within a reasonable time, especially given the circumstances surrounding her husband's death. Issues The primary issues in this case can be framed as follows: Whether the application for leave to appeal was filed within the time limit prescribed by the Supreme Court Rules of 1990? Whether the circumstances surrounding the death of the original plaintiff-appellant warrant an extension of the time limit for filing the application for leave to appeal? Applicable Law Statutes: Supreme Court Rules of 1990 Cases Referred to: Nirmala de Mel v. Seneviratne - [1982] 2 Sri L.R. 569; Jafferjee v. Perera - C.L.W. Vol. 79 pg. 81; George Stuart and Co. Ltd. v. Lankem Tea and Rubber Plantations (Pvt.) Ltd. - [2004] 1 Sri L.R. 246; L.A. Sudath Rohana v. Mohamed Zeena and others - S.C. H.C. C.A. L.A. No. 111/2010; K. Reaindran v. K. Velusomasundram - S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 298/99; N.A. Premadasa v. The People’s Bank - S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 212/99; Hameed v. Majibdeen and others - S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 38/2001; K.M. Samarasinghe v. R.M.D. Ratnayake and others - S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 51/2001; Soong Che Foo v. Harosha K. De Silva and others - S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 184/2003; C.A. Haroon v. S.K. Muzoor and others - S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 158/2006; Samantha Niroshana v. Senarath Abeyruwan - S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 145/2006; A.H.M. Fowzie and two others v. Vehicles Lanka (Pvt.) Ltd. - (2008) B.L.R. 127; Woodman Exports (Pvt.) Ltd. v. Commissioner-General of Labour - S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 335/2008. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the application for leave to appeal was filed within the time limit prescribed by the Supreme Court Rules of 1990? Rule applied: Supreme Court Rules of 1990, specifically Rule 7. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court Rules of 1990 clearly stipulate that applications for leave to appeal must be filed within six weeks of the judgment being appealed. In this case, the High Court's judgment was delivered on 15 July 2008, and the application for leave to appeal was filed on 1 September 2008, which is beyond the six-week period. The learned President’s Counsel for the respondents emphasized that the application was out of time, as it was filed after the stipulated deadline. The petitioner’s counsel argued that the Supreme Court Rules did not apply to her situation, suggesting that the application should be considered within a reasonable time due to the circumstances surrounding her husband's death. However, the Court noted that the rules are mandatory and that the petitioner was aware of the time limits but failed to act within them. The Court referenced previous cases, including George Stuart and Co. Ltd. v. Lankem Tea and Rubber Plantations (Pvt.) Ltd., which established that when no specific provision is made regarding the time frame for an application, the existing rules must be followed. The Court concluded that the petitioner did not comply with the Supreme Court Rules of 1990, as the application was filed well after the 42-day limit. Finding: The application for leave to appeal was filed out of time, and the preliminary objection raised by the respondents was upheld. Issue 2: Whether the circumstances surrounding the death of the original plaintiff-appellant warrant an extension of the time limit for filing the application for leave to appeal? Rule applied: Discretion of the Court regarding time limits. Court's reasoning: The petitioner’s counsel argued that the delay in filing the application was not intentional and was due to the bereavement of the original plaintiff-appellant. The Court acknowledged the emotional and social implications of losing a close relative, particularly in the context of Sri Lankan culture, where such events often take precedence over legal matters. However, the Court emphasized that the rules governing the filing of applications are clear and mandatory. The petitioner was aware of the time limits and failed to take necessary steps to ensure compliance. The Court distinguished the current case from Nirmala de Mel v. Seneviratne, where the circumstances were different, and there was a lacuna in the rules. The Court reiterated that the rules are designed to regulate the practice and procedure of the Supreme Court and that compliance is essential for the orderly administration of justice. Finding: The circumstances surrounding the death of the original plaintiff-appellant did not warrant an extension of the time limit for filing the application for leave to appeal. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated, as the failure to comply with the time limit directly impacts the ability to appeal. The Court's analysis of the time limit also considers the circumstances presented by the petitioner, but ultimately, the mandatory nature of the rules prevails. Conclusion The Supreme Court upheld the preliminary objection raised by the respondents and dismissed the petitioner’s application for leave to appeal, making no order as to costs. The Court emphasized the importance of adhering to procedural rules and the necessity for litigants to act within the stipulated time frames, regardless of personal circumstances. The judgment serves as a reminder of the strict adherence required to procedural rules in the legal system. Keywords leave to appeal — Supreme Court Rules — preliminary objection — time limit — High Court judgment — application for leave — reasonable time — discretion of the Court — compliance with rules — mitigating factors — bereavement — legal representation — civil procedure — appeal process — mandatory provisions
Counsel: Hemasiri Withanachchi for Petitioner; Manohara de Silva, PC, with Pubudini Wickramaratne for Defendants-Respondents-Respondents

Parties: Geethika and Others v. Dissanayake and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Equality before the law — Admission to government schools — Criteria for admission — Residence — Electoral register extracts — Documents establishing residence — National Housing Development Authority documents — Interview panel's evaluation — Arbitrary action
Summary: Facts The Petitioners, parents of the 3rd Petitioner, applied for the admission of their child to Grade 1 of D.S. Senanayake College for the year 2011 under the category of children of residents in close proximity to the school. The Petitioners submitted various documents to establish their residence and were initially informed by the interview panel that they had received 57 marks. However, the 3rd Petitioner was not selected for admission, nor was the child placed on the waiting list. The Petitioners subsequently filed an appeal, during which they were led to believe they would receive additional marks, but the final decision remained unchanged. The Respondents contended that the Petitioners were only awarded 37 marks and failed to qualify for admission as the cut-off mark was 61. The Respondents argued that the Petitioners failed to produce the required documents under the Ministry of Education circular to establish residence or ownership, specifically noting the absence of a valid deed. The Petitioners then filed this fundamental rights application alleging a violation of Article 12(1) of the Constitution, asserting that the interview panel acted arbitrarily by failing to properly evaluate the evidence of residence. Issues Whether the interview panelâ€™s failure to award marks for residence and proximity based on the documents provided by the Petitioners constituted a violation of Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the interview panel acted arbitrarily by strictly requiring title deeds to establish residence, contrary to the purpose of the Ministry of Education circular. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Ministry of Education Circular No. 2010/21 dated 31.5.2010 Cases Referred to: Haputhantirige v. Attorney General - [2007/v1-sri-lr-101] Analysis Issue 1: Evaluation of residence and proximity marks Rule applied: Clause 6.1 of Ministry of Education Circular No. 2010/21. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the Petitioners were awarded 35 marks for electoral register extracts, which established the core criterion of residence. The court reasoned that once residence is established via electoral registers, the applicant is entitled to marks under Clause 6.1(IV) regarding proximity. The interview panel had initially calculated 20 marks for proximity but struck them out due to the absence of a deed. The court found this deduction incorrect, as the circular does not mandate ownership as a prerequisite for proximity marks. Furthermore, the court found that the panel failed to properly credit the Petitioners for other documents under Clause 6.1(III), such as the childâ€™s health record and employment letters, which confirmed the genuineness of their residence. Finding: The Petitioners were entitled to additional marks for proximity and other supporting documents, which would have placed them above the cut-off mark. Issue 2: Arbitrary application of criteria Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution; Ministry of Education Circular No. 2010/21. Court's reasoning: The court held that the interview panel adopted an overly narrow and abstract interpretation of "residence" by focusing exclusively on title deeds. Relying on Haputhantirige v. Attorney General, the court emphasized that panels must consider the totality of evidence rather than merely ticking boxes for stereotyped documents. The court noted that document P17, issued by the National Housing Development Authority, sufficiently established the legitimacy of the Petitioners' residence, even if it was not a formal deed. By ignoring this and other corroborating evidence, the panel acted arbitrarily. Finding: The interview panelâ€™s decision-making process was arbitrary and violated the Petitioners' fundamental rights under Article 12(1). Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing; the courtâ€™s determination that the panel acted arbitrarily (Issue 2) directly explains why the panel failed to correctly calculate the marks (Issue 1). By viewing the circular through a rigid lens of ownership rather than residence, the panel failed to apply the marking scheme correctly, thereby violating the Petitioners' right to equality. Conclusion The court held that the Petitioners' fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution were violated. The decision of the Respondents to deny the 3rd Petitioner admission is set aside, and the Respondents are directed to admit the 3rd Petitioner to Grade 1 of D.S. Senanayake College forthwith. Keywords Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Equality before the law — Admission to government schools — Criteria for admission — Residence — Electoral register extracts — Documents establishing residence — National Housing Development Authority documents — Interview panel's evaluation — Arbitrary action

Parties: De Silva v. Wickremaratne and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Ratnayake, J; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Article 17 of the Constitution — Article 126 of the Constitution — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Time limit for filing petitions — Political victimization — Promotions in the Police Service — Kadawatharachchi Committee — Premarathne Committee — National Police Commission — Similar circumstances
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, a Superintendent of Police, filed this application alleging that his fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution were violated by the Respondents. The Petitioner contended that he was subjected to political victimization between 1981 and 1994, which delayed his promotions. Although committees such as the Kadawatharachchi Committee and the Premarathne Committee recommended that the Petitioner be promoted to the rank of Assistant Superintendent of Police with effect from 1989, the Ministry of Defence instead granted him two salary increments in 1997. The Petitioner accepted these increments but subsequently sought further relief. The Petitioner argued that he was similarly circumstanced to the 10th Respondent, who was also a victim of political victimization but was eventually granted a back-dated promotion to the rank of Senior Superintendent of Police. The Petitioner claimed that the failure of the National Police Commission to grant him similar relief constituted a violation of his right to equality. The Respondents maintained that the application was time-barred under Article 126(2) of the Constitution and that the Petitioner and the 10th Respondent were not similarly situated due to the 10th Respondent's persistent pursuit of his claims compared to the Petitioner's acceptance of the initial relief. Issues Whether the Petitionerâ€™s application was filed within the mandatory one-month time limit prescribed by Article 126(2) of the Constitution. Whether the Petitioner and the 10th Respondent were similarly situated, thereby entitling the Petitioner to the same relief granted to the 10th Respondent under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 12(1), 17, 126 Cases Referred to: Gunewardene v. Senanayake - [1-f.r.d-178] Jayawardene v. A.G - [1-f.r.d-175] Thadchamamoorthy v. A.G - [1-f.q-129] Edirisuriya v. Navaratnam - [1985/v1-sri-lr-100] Siriwardene v. Rodrigo - [1986/v1-sri-lr-384] Gamethige v. Siriwardene - [1988/v1-sri-lr-385] Deena v. India - [1983-s.c-1154] A.V. Nachane v. India - [1982-s.c-1126] Analysis Issue 1: Time limit for filing Rule applied: Article 126(2) of the Constitution, which requires applications for fundamental rights violations to be filed within one month of the alleged infringement. Court's reasoning: The court held that the alleged infringement regarding the Petitioner's promotion occurred years prior when he accepted salary increments in lieu of promotion. The court rejected the Petitioner's attempt to link his claim to the 10th Respondent's recent promotion, noting that the underlying grievance related to events from 1994. Furthermore, the court found the Petitioner's claim of ignorance regarding the 10th Respondent's promotion to be unreasonable given his position as a police officer. Finding: The application was not filed within the mandatory one-month period and was therefore time-barred. Issue 2: Similar situation Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution (Right to Equality). Court's reasoning: The court determined that the Petitioner failed to meet the burden of proving he was similarly situated to the 10th Respondent. While both were recommended for promotion by the same committees, the 10th Respondent persistently challenged the denial of his rights and rejected intermediate relief. In contrast, the Petitioner accepted the salary increments and failed to pursue further administrative remedies, such as appealing to the John Senivarathne Committee. Finding: The Petitioner and the 10th Respondent were not similarly situated; thus, no violation of the right to equality occurred. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the Petitioner was not similarly situated to the 10th Respondent undermines the Petitioner's attempt to use the 10th Respondent's recent promotion as a trigger to bypass the mandatory time limits of Article 126(2). Because the Petitioner failed to demonstrate a valid, recent infringement of his rights, the court concluded that the application lacked a legal basis. Conclusion The application is dismissed as it is time-barred and fails to establish a violation of fundamental rights. No costs are ordered. Keywords Fundamental rights — Article 17 of the Constitution — Article 126 of the Constitution — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Time limit for filing petitions — Political victimization — Promotions in the Police Service — Kadawatharachchi Committee — Premarathne Committee — National Police Commission — Similar circumstances

Parties: Wimala Perera and Premadasa Perera v. Sriyalatha
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Sripavan, J; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Leave and license — Agreement — Contract law — Offer and acceptance — Consensus ad idem — Evidence Ordinance — Admissions in police statement — Title to property — Overholding licensee — Prescriptive title — Credibility of witnesses
Summary: Facts The Appellants, M. Wimala Perera and M. Premadasa Perera, initiated an action in the District Court of Colombo seeking the ejectment of the Respondent, R.A. Kalyani Sriyalatha, from the premises at Assessment No: 147, Devos Lane, Grandpass Road, Colombo 14. The Appellants alleged that the Respondent occupied the premises under a leave and license agreement dated 1st September 1996, which was subsequently terminated by a letter dated 30th September 1997. The Appellants claimed the Respondent remained in wrongful occupation and sought damages. The Respondent denied entering into any such agreement, asserting that her signature was obtained on a blank paper by the son of the Appellant under the false pretense that it was required for bail purposes for one Matilda Gomez. The Respondent further contended that she was a tenant of Matilda Gomez, the true owner of the premises, and had subsequently purchased the property from her via a Deed of Transfer dated 1st May 1998. The District Court dismissed the Appellants' action, and the High Court of the Western Province subsequently dismissed the Appellants' appeal. The Supreme Court granted leave to appeal to examine the validity of the alleged license agreement and the findings regarding title and evidence. Issues Whether the High Court erred in its assessment of the evidence, specifically regarding the alleged leave and license agreement (Document P1) and the Respondent's statement to the police (P3). Whether the High Court erred in determining that the Respondent proved her tenancy and subsequent ownership of the premises on a balance of probabilities. Whether the High Court erred in law by addressing the title to the premises in an action for ejectment of an over-holding licensee. Whether Section 116 of the Evidence Ordinance applies to the present case. Applicable Law Statutes: Evidence Ordinance, Section 116 Cases Referred to: R. W. Pathirana v. R. E. De. S. Jayasundara - [58-nlr-169] Alvar Pillai v. Karuppan - [4-nlr-321] V. Visvalingam v. D. De S. Gajaweera - [56-nlr-111] W. M. J. Bandara v. J. Piyasena - [77-nlr-102] Muthukuda v. Sumanatwathie - [65-nlr-205] Noorbhai v. Karuppan Chetty - [27-nlr-325] Analysis Issue 1: Validity of the License Agreement Rule applied: The principles of contract law requiring consensus ad idem, intention to create legal relations, and capacity. Court's reasoning: The court found the Appellants' evidence regarding Document P1 lacked credibility. The Appellant did not testify, and the testimony of her son, who drafted the document, was inconsistent and implausible. The court accepted the Respondent's version that her signature was obtained under false pretenses. Consequently, the court concluded that no valid leave and license agreement was ever formed. Finding: The High Court did not err; the document failed to create a legally binding agreement. Issue 2: Proof of Tenancy and Ownership Rule applied: The burden of proof on a balance of probabilities in civil litigation. Court's reasoning: The court reviewed the evidence of Matilda Gomez, who confirmed she was the owner of the premises, had leased them to the Respondent's mother, and subsequently sold them to the Respondent via Deed V1. This evidence was supported by documentary proof (Deed V2). The court found this evidence credible and sufficient to establish the Respondent's lawful occupation. Finding: The High Court correctly determined that the Respondent proved her status on a balance of probabilities. Issue 3: Relevance of Title in Ejectment Rule applied: The principle that a landlord-tenant relationship must be clearly established for the rule against disputing title to apply. Court's reasoning: While the court acknowledged that a tenant generally cannot dispute a landlord's title, this rule requires a valid, unequivocal agreement. Since the court found no valid agreement existed between the parties, the prohibition against disputing title was inapplicable. Finding: The High Court did not err in its consideration of title, as the foundational requirement for the ejectment action was absent. Issue 4: Application of Section 116 of the Evidence Ordinance Rule applied: Section 116 of the Evidence Ordinance (Estoppel of tenant). Court's reasoning: The court held that Section 116 is predicated on the existence of a tenancy. Because the court found that no valid license or tenancy agreement existed between the Appellant and the Respondent, the estoppel provisions of Section 116 could not be invoked. Finding: Section 116 does not apply where the existence of the tenancy or license is not established. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the failure to establish a valid contract (Issue 1) rendered the arguments regarding title (Issue 3) and statutory estoppel (Issue 4) moot. The credibility of the evidence (Issue 2) served as the basis for rejecting the Appellants' claims across all legal questions. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The judgment of the High Court is affirmed, and the Appellants are ordered to pay costs of Rs. 5,000 to the Respondent. Keywords Leave and license — Agreement — Contract law — Offer and acceptance — Consensus ad idem — Evidence Ordinance — Admissions in police statement — Title to property — Overholding licensee — Prescriptive title — Credibility of witnesses

Parties: Attanayake v. Commissioner General of Election and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; P.A. Ratnayake, PC., J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Special leave to appeal — Supreme Court Rules 1990 — Rule 8(3) — Tender of notices — Preliminary objection — Non-compliance with rules — Rectification of defects
Summary: Facts The petitioner, Tissa Attanayake, filed an application for special leave to appeal against a judgment of the Court of Appeal, which had refused to grant relief regarding the acceptance of nomination papers for the Chilaw Pradeshiya Sabha election. Upon the matter being listed for support in the Supreme Court, the 1st, 2nd, 3rd, 28th, and 4th respondents raised a preliminary objection. The respondents contended that the petitioner failed to comply with Rule 8(3) and Rule 40 of the Supreme Court Rules 1990. The petitioner had failed to tender the required number of notices to the Supreme Court Registry for service on the respondents at the time of filing the application. Instead, the petitioner attempted to serve the documents directly upon the respondents by registered post. The petitioner did not seek an extension of time to comply with the notice requirements under Rule 40. Consequently, when the matter was listed for support, several respondents had not been properly served through the Court, and some remained unrepresented. The petitioner argued that the defect was rectifiable and that the objectives of the rules had been substantially met by the direct service of documents. Issues Whether the failure to tender notices to the Supreme Court Registry in accordance with Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules 1990 constitutes a fatal non-compliance warranting the dismissal of the application. Whether the direct service of documents by the petitioner to the respondents constitutes substantial compliance that cures the failure to follow the procedure mandated by Rule 8(3) and Rule 40 of the Supreme Court Rules 1990. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka (Article 136) Supreme Court Rules 1990 (Rule 8, Rule 40) Cases Referred to: C. Coomasaru v M/s Leechman and Co. Ltd. - [sc-applications-217/72] Nicholas v O.L.M. Macan Markar Ltd. - [1981/v2-sri-lr-1] K. Reaindren v K. Velusomasunderam - [sc-spl-la-298/99] Union Apparels (Pvt.) Ltd. v Director-General of Customs - [2000/v1-sri-lr-27] Piyadasa v Land Reform Commission - [sc-application-30/97] Kiriwanthe v Navaratne - [1990/v2-sri-lr-393] Priyani Soysa v Rienzie Arsecularatne - [1999/v2-sri-lr-179] Bank of Ceylon v The Ceylon Bank Employees Union - [2003/v1-sri-lr-47] Samantha Niroshana v Senarath Abeyruwan - [sc-spl-la-145/2006] Fowzie v Vehicles Lanka (Pvt.) Ltd. - [2008/v1-sri-lr-23] L.A. Sudath Rohana v Mohamed Cassim Mohamed Zeena - [sc-hc-ca-la-111/2010] N.A. Premadasa v The Peoples Bank - [sc-spl-la-212/99] Hameed v Majibdeen - [sc-spl-la-38/2001] K.M. Samarasinghe v R.M.D. Ratnayake - [sc-spl-la-51/2001] Soong Che Foo v Harosha K. De Silva - [sc-spl-la-184/2003] C.A. Haroon v S.K. Muzoor - [sc-spl-la-158/2006] Woodman Exports (Pvt.) Ltd. v Commissioner-General of Labour - [sc-spl-la-335/2008] Fernando v Sybil Fernando - [1997/v3-sri-lr-1] Analysis Issue 1: Non-compliance with Rule 8(3) Rule applied: Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules 1990. Court's reasoning: The Court held that Rule 8(3) is a mandatory procedural requirement designed to ensure that the Registrar of the Supreme Court facilitates the service of notice, thereby regulating the court's practice. The petitioner failed to tender the required notices and stamped envelopes to the Registry. The Court emphasized that these rules are not mere technicalities but essential components of the legal machinery. Finding: The failure to comply with Rule 8(3) is a serious procedural default. Issue 2: Substantial compliance and rectification Rule applied: Rule 40 of the Supreme Court Rules 1990. Court's reasoning: The Court rejected the petitioner's argument that direct service of documents constituted substantial compliance. It noted that Rule 40 provides a specific mechanism for seeking extensions of time, which the petitioner failed to utilize. The Court distinguished the present case from precedents cited by the petitioner, noting that those cases did not involve the specific failure to tender notices through the Registry as required by Rule 8(3). Finding: The defect was not cured by direct service, and the application was deemed non-compliant. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are interdependent; the failure to comply with the mandatory service procedure under Rule 8(3) could only have been mitigated by following the formal extension process under Rule 40. Because the petitioner ignored both the primary service rule and the secondary remedial rule, the court found no basis to exercise its discretion to overlook the procedural failure. Conclusion The preliminary objection is upheld. The petitioner's application for special leave to appeal is dismissed for non-compliance with Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules 1990. No order is made as to costs. Keywords Special leave to appeal — Supreme Court Rules 1990 — Rule 8(3) — Tender of notices — Preliminary objection — Non-compliance with rules — Rectification of defects

Parties: Tissa Attanayake v. Commissioner General of Election and Others
Bench: Dr. Shirani A. Bandaranayake, CJ.; P.A. Ratnayake, PC., J.; Priyasath Dep, PC., J.
Keywords: special leave to appeal — writ of certiorari — nomination paper — Chilaw Pradeshiya Sabha — Supreme Court Rules — preliminary objection — compliance — legal procedure — notice to respondents — mandatory rules — justice
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for special leave to appeal from a judgment of the Court of Appeal dated 04 March 2011. The petitioner, Tissa Attanayake, who is the General Secretary of the United National Party, sought to challenge the acceptance of a nomination paper by the United People’s Freedom Alliance for the Chilaw Pradeshiya Sabha elections. The petitioner filed for a writ of certiorari to quash the decision of the 2nd and 3rd respondents, which accepted the nomination paper, and also sought a writ of mandamus to compel the 1st to 3rd respondents to conduct the election following the rejection of the nomination paper. Additionally, the petitioner requested a writ of prohibition to prevent the 5th respondent and others from contesting as candidates. The Court of Appeal had refused to issue notice and interim relief on the petitioner’s application, prompting the appeal to the Supreme Court. The Supreme Court was tasked with determining whether the application for special leave to appeal should be dismissed based on preliminary objections raised regarding non-compliance with the Supreme Court Rules, specifically Rule 8(3) and Rule 40. Issues Whether the application for special leave to appeal should be dismissed for non-compliance with Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990. Whether the petitioner’s failure to tender notices to the Registry of the Supreme Court constitutes a valid ground for dismissal of the application. Applicable Law Statutes: Supreme Court Rules, 1990 Cases Referred to: C. Coomasaru v M/s Leechman and Co. Ltd. and others - [S.C. Minutes of 26.05.1976]; Nicholas v O.L.M. Macan Markar Ltd. and others - [1981] 2 Sri L.R. 1; K. Reaindren v K. Velusomasunderam - [S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 298/99 – S.C. Minutes of 07.02.2000]; Union Apparels (Pvt.) Ltd. v Director-General of Customs and others - [2000] 1 Sri L.R. 27; Piyadasa and others v Land Reform Commission - [S.C. (Application) No. 30/97 – S.C. Minutes of 08.07.1998]; Kiriwanthe and another v Navaratne and another - [1990] 2 Sri L.R. 393; Priyani Soysa v Rienzie Arsecularatne - [1999] 2 Sri L.R. 179; Bank of Ceylon v The Ceylon Bank Employees’ Union (on behalf of Karunatilake) - [2003] 1 Sri L.R. 47; Samantha Niroshana v Senarath Abeyruwan - [S.C. (Spl.) L.A. No. 145/2006 – S.C. Minutes of 02.08.2007]; Hameed v Majibdeen and others - [S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 38/2001 – S.C. Minutes of 23.07.2001]; K.M. Samarasinghe v R.M.D. Ratnayake and others - [S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 51/2001 – S.C. Minutes of 27.07.2001]; Soong Che Foo v Harosha K. De Silva and others - [S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 184/2003 – S.C. Minutes of 25.11.2003]; C.A. Haroon v S.K. Muzoor and others - [S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 158/2006 – S.C. Minutes of 24.11.2006]; A.H.M. Fowzie and two others v Vehicles Lanka (Pvt.) Ltd. - [S.C. (Spl.) L.A. Application No. 335/2008 – S.C. Minutes of 13.12.2010]. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the application for special leave to appeal should be dismissed for non-compliance with Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990. Rule applied: Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the preliminary objections raised by the learned Senior State Counsel and the learned President’s Counsel for the 4th respondent regarding the petitioner’s failure to comply with Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990. This rule mandates that the petitioner must tender a specific number of notices along with the application for special leave to appeal, ensuring that all respondents are properly notified of the proceedings. The Court noted that the petitioner had submitted an undated petition and incomplete documents, which led to a directive for the petitioner to file fresh documents. The petitioner’s failure to tender the required notices to the Registry was highlighted as a significant procedural error. The Court emphasized that the purpose of Rule 8(3) is to ensure that all necessary parties are notified, allowing for a fair hearing. The petitioner argued that the errors could be rectified and that justice would be denied if the application were dismissed on such minor mistakes. However, the Court referenced previous judgments that established the importance of adhering to procedural rules, stating that non-compliance with mandatory rules cannot be dismissed as mere technicalities. The Court also pointed out that the petitioner had not followed the proper procedure for notifying the respondents through the Registry, which is crucial for maintaining the integrity of the judicial process. The Court concluded that the petitioner’s failure to comply with Rule 8(3) was a serious breach of procedure that warranted dismissal of the application for special leave to appeal. Finding: The application for special leave to appeal was dismissed due to non-compliance with Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in this case are interrelated, as the failure to comply with procedural rules directly impacts the court's ability to hear the case. The Court's emphasis on the importance of following established procedures reflects a broader principle of ensuring fairness and justice in legal proceedings. The dismissal of the application for special leave to appeal underscores the necessity of adhering to the rules governing court procedures, which are designed to protect the rights of all parties involved. Conclusion The Supreme Court upheld the preliminary objections raised by the respondents and dismissed the petitioner’s application for special leave to appeal for non-compliance with Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990. The Court made no order as to costs, emphasizing the importance of procedural compliance in maintaining the integrity of the judicial process. Keywords special leave to appeal — writ of certiorari — nomination paper — Chilaw Pradeshiya Sabha — Supreme Court Rules — preliminary objection — compliance — legal procedure — notice to respondents — mandatory rules — justice
Counsel: Upul Jayasooriya with M. Ariyadasa for Petitioner-Petitioner. Nihal Jayamanne, PC., with Kushan D’ Alwis, K. Ratwatte, Dilshan De Silva and Chamath Fernando for 4th Respondent-Respondent. N. Pulle, SSC, for 1st, 2nd, 3rd and 28th Respondents-Respondents.

Parties: Attorney General v. Wijetunge and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: Murder — Identity of accused — Evidence Ordinance — Common murderous intention — Penal Code — Code of Criminal Procedure Act — Fundamental rights — Trial without a jury
Summary: Facts The accused, Kuruppu Arachchilage Wijetunge, Ganitha Devayalage Sunil Jayaratne, and Kuruppu Arachchilage Gamini Jayatissa, were indicted before the High Court of Kegalle for the murder of Godayalage Sadiris. The third accused died pending trial, and the case proceeded against the first and second accused. The prosecution alleged that on 23rd January 1988, the deceased and his wife, Emilin, were returning home after a police inquiry when they were intercepted by the accused. Emilin, the sole eyewitness, testified that the second accused attacked the deceased with a sword, while the first and third accused attacked him with clubs. The accused then dragged the deceased away. The deceased's body was later discovered in a nearby river, with the cause of death identified as drowning. The High Court convicted both accused and sentenced them to death. The Court of Appeal subsequently dismissed their appeal. The second accused appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the prosecution established that the accused entertained a common intention to murder the deceased as required by Section 32 of the Penal Code. Whether the Court of Appeal was justified in concluding that the trial judge's failure to explicitly consider the existence of common murderous intention did not prejudice the accused. Whether the Court of Appeal misdirected itself by applying the proviso to Section 334 of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act and the proviso to Article 138(1) of the Constitution to disregard the trial judge's failure to address common intention. Applicable Law Statutes: Penal Code (Section 31, 32, 293) Code of Criminal Procedure Act (Section 334, 335) Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka (Article 138(1)) Cases Referred to: Alwis v. Piyasena Fernando - [1993/v1-sri-lr-119] King v. Loku Nona - [19-nlr-110] Wijithasiri v. The Republic of Sri Lanka - [1990/v1-sri-lr-56] King v. Assanna - [50-nlr-324] Don Somapala v. Republic of Sri Lanka - [78-nlr-183] Sheela Sinharage v. Attorney General - [1985/v1-sri-lr-1] Analysis Issue 1: Common Intention Rule applied: Section 32 of the Penal Code and the test for common intention established in King v. Assanna. Court's reasoning: The court examined whether the accused acted with a pre-arranged plan or a common understanding. It noted that all three accused were armed, intercepted the deceased together, attacked him, and dragged him to the river. Relying on Don Somapala v. Republic of Sri Lanka, the court held that common intention can be formed at the spur of the moment. The court concluded that the acts of attacking and drowning the deceased could not have been performed without a common understanding to kill. Finding: The prosecution established the requisite common murderous intention. Issue 2: Prejudice to the Accused Rule applied: Proviso to Article 138(1) of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that while the trial judge did not explicitly use the term "common intention," her findings on the reliability of the eyewitness and the coordinated actions of the accused demonstrated that she had considered the essential elements of the crime. Therefore, the omission did not result in a miscarriage of justice or prejudice the accused. Finding: The conclusion that the failure to explicitly address common intention did not prejudice the accused was justified. Issue 3: Procedural Misdirection Rule applied: Section 335 of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act and Article 138(1) of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the Court of Appeal incorrectly cited Section 334 (applicable to jury trials) instead of Section 335 (applicable to non-jury trials). However, it held that this error was immaterial because the appellate court's duty under Section 335 is to determine if there are sufficient grounds to interfere with the judgment. Since the evidence supported the conviction, no interference was warranted. Finding: The Court of Appeal did not misdirect itself in a manner that affected the outcome. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are interdependent. The determination that common intention was substantively proven (Issue 1) directly supports the finding that the trial judge's failure to explicitly label it did not cause prejudice (Issue 2). Consequently, the procedural error regarding the citation of the Criminal Procedure Code (Issue 3) became moot, as the substantive evidence of guilt rendered the conviction sound under the appellate standards of review. Conclusion The appeal of the second accused is dismissed. The conviction and sentence imposed by the High Court are affirmed. Keywords Murder — Identity of accused — Evidence Ordinance — Common murderous intention — Penal Code — Code of Criminal Procedure Act — Fundamental rights — Trial without a jury

Parties: Illesinghe and Others v. Perera and Others
Bench: Hon. Dr. Shirani Bandaranayake, J; Hon. C. Ekanayake, J; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Ayurveda Medical Officers — Sri Lanka Ayurveda Service Minute — Public Service Commission — Contract of employment — Cadre based service — Merit order — Legitimate expectation — Establishments Code
Summary: Facts The Petitioners, sixteen Ayurvedic medical practitioners, sought a declaration that they were entitled to be appointed as Ayurvedic Medical Officers (Preliminary Grade) under the Sri Lanka Ayurveda Service Minute. The Petitioners were initially recruited on a contract basis for a pilot project in the Anuradhapura District starting in 2001. Their contracts were extended periodically until 2008. The Petitioners contended that they held a legitimate expectation of permanent appointment based on promises made by the 12th Respondent and the successful implementation of the pilot project. The Public Service Commission (PSC) subsequently initiated a recruitment process for the Sri Lanka Ayurveda Service. The Petitioners participated in an interview but were not selected for permanent positions. The PSC determined that the Petitioners did not meet the necessary criteria for permanent absorption, citing age limitations for pension eligibility and a lack of seniority in the register compared to other candidates. Consequently, the Petitioners' contracts were terminated. The Petitioners challenged this termination and the failure to appoint them to the permanent service, alleging a violation of their fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Issues Whether the termination of the Petitioners' contracts and the failure to appoint them to the Sri Lanka Ayurveda Service violated their fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the Petitioners held a legitimate expectation of permanent appointment to the Sri Lanka Ayurveda Service. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Ayurveda Act No. 31 (as amended) Establishments Code, Chapter II, Section 6 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution; Sri Lanka Ayurveda Service Minute (SLASM); Establishments Code, Chapter II, Section 6. Court's reasoning: The court found that the Sri Lanka Ayurveda Service is a cadre-based service where appointments must strictly adhere to the approved Scheme of Recruitment. The PSC, as the appointing authority, acted within its mandate by requiring that all candidates possess the necessary seniority in the register and meet age requirements for pension eligibility. The court noted that 14 of the 21 contract appointees were junior to other candidates appointed in 2007, and two exceeded the age limit. As the PSC followed the established criteria without discrimination, the court concluded that the decision was neither unreasonable, arbitrary, nor irrational. Finding: There was no infringement of Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Issue 2: Legitimate Expectation Rule applied: Principles of administrative law regarding legitimate expectation in public service appointments. Court's reasoning: The court examined the appointment letters of the Petitioners and observed that they were explicitly recruited on a contract basis. The court held that such contract appointments do not create a legitimate expectation of permanent employment, especially when the governing regulations (SLASM and the Establishments Code) mandate specific recruitment procedures that the Petitioners did not satisfy. The court further noted that the initial contract appointments were not made by the PSC, which is the sole authority for permanent appointments to the service. Finding: The Petitioners did not possess a legitimate expectation of permanent appointment. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the Petitioners lacked a legitimate expectation (Issue 2) confirms that the PSC was not legally obligated to grant them permanent status. This, in turn, supports the conclusion that the PSCâ€™s adherence to the formal recruitment criteria and the subsequent termination of the contracts did not constitute arbitrary or discriminatory conduct (Issue 1). Conclusion The application is dismissed. The court held that the Petitioners failed to establish a violation of their fundamental rights and were not entitled to the reliefs sought. No costs are ordered. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Ayurveda Medical Officers — Sri Lanka Ayurveda Service Minute — Public Service Commission — Contract of employment — Cadre based service — Merit order — Legitimate expectation — Establishments Code

Parties: Bandara v. Attorney General
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N.G. Amaratunga, J; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Appeal from Court of Appeal — Murder charge — Section 294 of the Penal Code — Culpable homicide — Exception 4 — Sudden fight — Heat of passion — Premeditation — Cruel or unusual manner
Summary: Facts The appellant, Gurudeniya Lekamgedara Nishantha Bandara, was charged with the murder of Wilson Anasley Peters and causing hurt to Bony Ignatius Peters on or about 25.09.1999 in Ampara. The incident occurred following an almsgiving at the deceased's residence. The prosecution presented evidence of three distinct incidents occurring between 10:30 p.m. and 11:45 p.m. Initially, the appellant caused a disturbance at the deceased's home, broke a glass, and injured his own hand, after which the deceased attempted to assist him. Subsequently, the appellant was seen in the compound with a club and later invited the deceased to his own house. Finally, at the gate of the appellant's house, the appellant struck the deceased on the head with a club, resulting in the deceased's death. The High Court of Ampara convicted the appellant of murder and sentenced him to death, while acquitting him of the charge of causing hurt. The Court of Appeal affirmed this conviction and sentence. The appellant was granted special leave to appeal to the Supreme Court on the question of whether the Court of Appeal misdirected itself by failing to evaluate the possibility of a sudden fight under Exception 4 to Section 294 of the Penal Code. Issues Whether the Court of Appeal misdirected itself by failing to evaluate the possibility of a sudden fight under Exception 4 to Section 294 of the Penal Code. Applicable Law Statutes: Penal Code, Section 293 Penal Code, Section 294 Cases Referred to: Surinder Kumar v Union Territory Chandigarh - [AIR (1989) SC 1094] Kikar Singh v State of Rajasthan - [AIR (1993) SC 2426] Bhagwan Munjaji Pawade v State of Maharashtra - [AIR (1979) SC 133] State of Himachal Pradesh v Wazir Chend - [AIR (1978) SC 315] Pandurang Narayana Jawalekar v State of Maharashtra - [AIR (1978) SC 1082] Jumman v State of Punjab - [AIR (1957) SC 469] Amrithalinga Nadar v State of Tamil Nadu - [AIR (1976) SC 1133] Ahmed Sher v Emperor - [AIR (1931) Lahore 513] Gajanand v State of Uttar Pradesh - [AIR (1954) SC 695] Dharman v State of Punjab - [AIR (1957) SC 324] Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of Exception 4 to Section 294 Rule applied: Exception 4 to Section 294 of the Penal Code, which requires that the act be committed without premeditation, in a sudden fight, in the heat of passion upon a sudden quarrel, and without the offender taking undue advantage or acting in a cruel or unusual manner. Court's reasoning: The Court noted that a "fight" requires a bilateral transaction where blows are exchanged. The evidence established that the deceased was unarmed and did not cause injury to the appellant during the final incident. Furthermore, the Court emphasized that a significant lapse of time occurred between the initial argument and the fatal assault, allowing the "heat of passion" to subside and providing the appellant opportunity for premeditation. The nature of the injury, caused by a club with great force, demonstrated that the appellant acted in a cruel manner. Consequently, the requirements for the exception were not met. Finding: The Court of Appeal did not misdirect itself; the appellant failed to satisfy the necessary requisites to invoke Exception 4 to Section 294 of the Penal Code. Conclusion The Supreme Court held that the Court of Appeal correctly affirmed the conviction and death sentence imposed by the High Court. The appeal is dismissed. Keywords Appeal from Court of Appeal — Murder charge — Section 294 of the Penal Code — Culpable homicide — Exception 4 — Sudden fight — Heat of passion — Premeditation — Cruel or unusual manner

Parties: Weerawarna Kurukulasooriya and Others v. Edirisinghe and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; S.I. Imam, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Z score calculation — Provisional results — Legitimate expectation — Re-scrutiny of results — University admissions — Advanced Level Examination — Revision of Z score
Summary: Facts The petitioner, a student of Thakshila Maha Vidyalaya, Horana, sat for the Advanced Level Examination in August 2008 for the second time. Upon the release of initial results on January 3, 2009, the petitioner obtained a Z score of 1.8874, which she believed qualified her for admission to a Faculty of Medicine. Relying on this belief, she did not apply for a third attempt at the examination. The Department of Examinations subsequently conducted a re-scrutiny process, which is a standard procedure following the release of examination results. On June 29, 2009, final results were issued, and the petitionerâ€™s Z score was revised downward to 1.8847. This revised score fell below the cut-off mark for the Medical Faculty, which was released by the University Grants Commission on July 2, 2009. The petitioner was instead selected for a course in Molecular Biology and Biochemistry at the University of Colombo. The petitioner challenged the revision of her Z score and the delay in releasing cut-off marks, alleging a violation of her fundamental right to equality under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Issues Whether the revision of the petitioner's Z score after the initial release of results constituted an arbitrary and discriminatory act violating Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the petitioner held a legitimate expectation that her initial Z score was final and would entitle her to admission to a Faculty of Medicine. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Cases Referred to: Schmidt v Secretary of State for Home Affairs - [1969] 1 All E.R. 904 Breen v Amalgamated Engineering Union - [1971] 1 All E.R. 1148 Re Westminster City Council - [1986] A.C. 668 Attorney General of Hong Kong v Ng Tuen Shiu - [1983] 2 All E.R. 346 Council of Civil Service Unions v Minister for the Civil Service - [1984] 3 All E.R. 935 Gauri Shankar v Union of India - [AIR (1995) SC 55] Ashutosh Gupta v State of Rajasthan - [(2002) 4 SCC 34] Western Uttar Pradesh Electric Power and Supply Co. Ltd. v State of Uttar Pradesh - [AIR (1970) SC 21] R.K. Garg v Union of India - [AIR (1981) SC 2138] Re: Special Courts Bill - [AIR (1979) SC 478] State of Uttar Pradesh v Kamla Palace - [AIR (2000) SC 633] Analysis Issue 1: Arbitrary revision of Z score Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution (Right to equality). Court's reasoning: The court noted that the Z score is a standardized statistical measure dependent on the mean and standard deviation of marks across the entire candidate pool. Because the Z score calculation is inherently subject to change following the re-scrutiny of any candidate's marks, the initial results released on January 3, 2009, were explicitly provisional. The respondents provided evidence that school principals were notified of this provisional status. The revision was a necessary consequence of the re-scrutiny process applied to all candidates, not a targeted or arbitrary act against the petitioner. Finding: The revision of the Z score was not arbitrary or discriminatory, and thus did not violate Article 12(1). Issue 2: Legitimate expectation Rule applied: Doctrine of legitimate expectation as established in Schmidt v Secretary of State for Home Affairs and Council of Civil Service Unions v Minister for the Civil Service. Court's reasoning: The court held that a legitimate expectation must arise from a clear promise, undertaking, or established regular practice. The petitioner failed to demonstrate that any such promise was made. Conversely, the respondents demonstrated that the provisional nature of the initial results was a known practice. Since the petitioner could not show that a past practice was withdrawn or that she was misled by an authority, her belief did not constitute a legally enforceable legitimate expectation. Finding: The petitioner did not possess a legitimate expectation that her initial Z score was final. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing; because the Z score system is inherently provisional until re-scrutiny is finalized, the petitioner's claim of a "legitimate expectation" fails as a matter of law. The court's determination that the process was rational and consistent with established practice negates the petitioner's argument that the respondents acted arbitrarily. Conclusion The court held that the petitioner failed to establish an infringement of her fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The application is dismissed with no order as to costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Z score calculation — Provisional results — Legitimate expectation — Re-scrutiny of results — University admissions — Advanced Level Examination — Revision of Z score

Parties: De Alwis v. Edirisinghe and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; S.I. Imam, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Legitimate expectation — Administrative action — Z score calculation — Provisional results — Re-scrutiny of results — University admissions — Advanced Level Examination
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, a student of Kalutara Balika National School, sat for the Advanced Level Examination in August 2008. Upon the release of provisional results on 03.01.2009, the Petitioner obtained a Z score of 1.8887, which she believed qualified her for admission to a Faculty of Medicine. The Petitioner alleged that the Respondents subsequently and arbitrarily reduced her Z score to 1.8860 without explanation, which resulted in her being selected for Dental Surgery instead of Medicine. The Petitioner contended that the Respondents violated her fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution by amending the Z score, delaying the release of cut-off marks, and issuing a revised schedule of results. The Respondents maintained that the initial results were provisional and subject to change following the mandatory re-scrutiny process, a procedure of which school authorities had been notified in advance. The final Z scores were calculated based on the standardized marks of all candidates after the re-scrutiny process concluded. Issues Whether the revision of the Petitioner's Z score by the Respondents constituted an arbitrary act violating the Petitioner's fundamental right to equality under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the Petitioner possessed a legitimate expectation that her initial provisional Z score would remain unchanged for the purpose of university admission. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Cases Referred to: Schmidt v. Secretary of State for Home Affairs - [1969/v1-all-er-904] Breen v. Amalgamated Engineering Union - [1971/v1-all-er-1148] Re Westminster City Council - [1986/ac-668] Attorney General of Hong Kong v. Ng Tuen Shiu - [1983/v2-all-er-346] Council of Civil Service Unions v. Minister for the Civil Service - [1984/v3-all-er-935] Gauri Shankar v. Union of India - [air-1995-sc-55] Ashutosh Gupta v. State of Rajasthan - [(2002) 4 SCC 34] Western Uttar Pradesh Electric Power and Supply Co. Ltd. v. State of Uttar Pradesh - [air-1970-sc-21] R.K. Garg v. Union of India - [air-1981-sc-2138] Re: Special Courts Bill - [air-1979-sc-478] State of Uttar Pradesh v. Kamla Palace - [air-2000-sc-633] Analysis Issue 1: Arbitrary revision of Z score Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution (Right to Equality). Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that the Z score system is a standardized statistical process dependent on the mean marks and standard deviations of all candidates. Because re-scrutiny of examination papers alters the marks of other candidates, it necessarily impacts the Z scores of all students. The court found that the Respondents followed an established, rational procedure and that the initial results were explicitly marked as provisional. Finding: The revision was not arbitrary or discriminatory; it was a necessary consequence of the standardized Z score calculation method. Issue 2: Legitimate expectation Rule applied: The doctrine of legitimate expectation as established in Schmidt v. Secretary of State for Home Affairs and Council of Civil Service Unions v. Minister for the Civil Service. Court's reasoning: The court held that a legitimate expectation must arise from a specific promise, undertaking, or established practice. In this case, the Respondents had notified school authorities that the initial results were provisional. There was no promise that the initial Z score would be final. The Petitioner failed to show that the established practice of revising scores post-re-scrutiny had been withdrawn or altered to her detriment. Finding: The Petitioner did not have a legitimate expectation that her provisional Z score would remain unchanged. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. Because the Z score calculation is a standardized, dynamic process, the court determined that the Petitioner could not reasonably hold a "legitimate expectation" of a fixed score. Consequently, the absence of such an expectation, combined with the rational nature of the administrative process, led to the conclusion that there was no violation of the right to equality under Article 12(1). Conclusion The court held that the Petitioner failed to establish an infringement of her fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The application is dismissed. No order is made as to costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Legitimate expectation — Administrative action — Z score calculation — Provisional results — Re-scrutiny of results — University admissions — Advanced Level Examination

Parties: Wijendra v. Karunawathie
Bench: Shiranee Tilakawardane, J; P.A. Ratnayake, J.; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Special Leave to Appeal — Order of the Provincial High Court — Refusal of a Commission — Examiner of Questioned Documents (EQD) — Unlisted Documents — Civil Procedure Code — Section 175(2) — Section 121(2) — Marking of Documents in Trial — Cross-Examination of Witnesses — Proviso to Section 175(2) — Pre-Trial Remedies
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent instituted an action in the District Court of Homagama seeking to set aside an Agreement to Sell and for the ejectment of the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant from the premises in dispute. During the trial, the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant attempted to mark an unlisted document during the cross-examination of the Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent. The Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent denied the signature on the document, and the trial judge refused to allow the document to be marked. Subsequently, the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant moved for a commission to be issued to the Examiner of Questioned Documents to verify the signature. The District Court refused this application by order dated 16.3.2007. The Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant appealed this decision to the Provincial High Court of Civil Appeal, which dismissed the appeal on 16.7.2007. The Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant then sought special leave to appeal to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court of Civil Appeal failed to address the refusal of the commission sought by the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant. Whether the High Court of Civil Appeal misdirected itself regarding the marking of documents versus the issuance of a commission. Whether the High Court of Civil Appeal failed to consider the proviso to Section 175 (2) of the Civil Procedure Code regarding the tendering of documents at trial. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 94 Civil Procedure Code, Section 98 Civil Procedure Code, Section 99 Civil Procedure Code, Section 100 Civil Procedure Code, Section 101 Civil Procedure Code, Section 121 (2) Civil Procedure Code, Section 175 (2) Civil Procedure Code, Section 428 Cases Referred to: Ariyaratne v. Laksiri Fernando - [2004/v1-sri-lr-184] Wickramathilake v. Marikkar - [not specified] Canapathipillai v. Adannappa Chetty - [21-nlr-217] Abnar and Co. v. Ceylon Overseas Tea Trading Co. Ltd. - [47-nlr-9] Kandiah v. Wiswanathan - [1991/v1-sri-lr-269] Casie Chetty v. Senanayake - [1999/v3-sri-lr-11] Girantha v. Maria - [50-nlr-519] Asilin Nona v. Wilbert Silva - [1997/v1-sri-lr-176] Abdul Munaf v. Mohamed Yusuf - [1997/v1-sri-lr-373] Analysis Issue 1: Refusal of the Commission Rule applied: Section 428 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court held that the issuance of a commission is a discretionary power. The Appellant failed to exercise due diligence by not listing the document or utilizing pre-trial remedies like interrogatories or notices to admit documents under Sections 94 and 101. The court found that the Appellantâ€™s attempt to seek a commission was an attempt to camouflage negligence. Finding: The refusal of the commission was correct; the issue is answered in the negative. Issue 2: Marking of Documents and Commission Rule applied: Section 121 (2) and Section 175 (2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that a commission to an Examiner of Questioned Documents is only appropriate when a document has been properly listed or marked in evidence. Since the document was unlisted and the witness denied the signature, the document could not be marked. Finding: The High Court did not misdirect itself; the issue is answered in the negative. Issue 3: Proviso to Section 175 (2) Rule applied: Proviso to Section 175 (2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: While the proviso allows unlisted documents to be used for cross-examination, the court noted that if the witness denies the document, it cannot be marked in evidence without further proof. The Appellant failed to establish the document's authenticity, and the court found no error in the lower court's application of the law. Finding: The High Court correctly considered the law; the issue is answered in the negative. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected because the failure to comply with the mandatory listing requirements under Section 121 (2) created a procedural barrier that the Appellant attempted to bypass using the proviso to Section 175 (2). Because the attempt to mark the document failed due to the witness's denial, the request for a commission became a secondary, failed attempt to remedy the initial procedural negligence. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal and upheld the orders of the lower courts. The questions of law were answered in the negative. Keywords Special Leave to Appeal — Order of the Provincial High Court — Refusal of a Commission — Examiner of Questioned Documents (EQD) — Unlisted Documents — Civil Procedure Code — Section 175(2) — Section 121(2) — Marking of Documents in Trial — Cross-Examination of Witnesses — Proviso to Section 175(2) — Pre-Trial Remedies

Parties: Gamarallage Karunawathie and Others v. Godayalage Piyasena and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; K. Sripavan, J.; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Substitution of parties — Death of a party during pendency of appeal — Section 760 A of the Civil Procedure Code — Rule 38 of the Supreme Court Rules — Abatement of appeal — Final Order of the District Court — Judgment of the High Court
Summary: Facts The appellant, Gamarallage Karunawathie, sought leave to appeal against a judgment of the High Court of the Sabaragamuwa Province, which had rejected her appeal. During the proceedings, it was noted that several parties were deceased. Specifically, the 15th respondent, who also served as the 16a respondent, died on 30.05.2004 while the matter was pending before the District Court. No steps for substitution were taken, yet the District Court proceeded to deliver a Final Order on 20.05.2005. Furthermore, the 2nd respondent died on 06.09.2007 while the matter was pending before the High Court. No substitution was effected in the High Court prior to the delivery of its judgment on 13.10.2009. The parties requested the Supreme Court to address the issue of substitution to resolve the appeal. Issues Whether Section 760 A of the Civil Procedure Code (as amended) and Rule 38 of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990 allow for the substitution of deceased parties in the Supreme Court when the record became defective in the lower courts prior to the filing of the appeal. Whether the judgments delivered by the District Court and the High Court are valid when they were rendered after the death of parties without the substitution of their legal representatives. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code (as amended) Partition Law Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka Cases Referred to: State of Punjab v Nathu Ram - [AIR 1962 SC 89] Swaran Singh Puran Singh v Ramditta Badhwa - [AIR 1969 Punjab & Haryana 216] Kanailal Manna v Bhabataran Santra - [AIR 1970 Calcutta 99] Achhar Singh v Smt. Ananti - [AIR 1971 Punjab & Haryana 477] Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of substitution rules Rule applied: Section 760 A of the Civil Procedure Code (as amended) and Rule 38 of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that Section 760 A and Rule 38 apply only when a record becomes defective due to death or change of status after the lodging of an appeal in the Supreme Court or Court of Appeal. Because the deaths of the 15th and 2nd respondents occurred while the case was pending in the District Court and High Court respectively, the record was already defective before the appeal was filed in the Supreme Court. Finding: Section 760 A and Rule 38 are inapplicable to the present circumstances. Issue 2: Validity of judgments Rule applied: Principles regarding the abatement of proceedings upon the death of a party. Court's reasoning: The court relied on the principle that a court cannot proceed with a matter without the legal representatives of a deceased party. Citing Kanailal Manna v Bhabataran Santra and Achhar Singh v Smt. Ananti, the court held that a decree passed in ignorance of the death of a party is a nullity. Since the District Court and High Court judgments were delivered after the deaths of the 15th and 2nd respondents without substitution, those judgments are ineffective. Finding: Both the District Court Final Order and the High Court judgment are nullities and must be set aside. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; because the court determined that the statutory mechanisms for substitution in the Supreme Court (Issue 1) were inapplicable due to the timing of the deaths, it necessitated the conclusion that the lower court proceedings were fundamentally flawed (Issue 2). The procedural defect rendered the lower court judgments ineffective, requiring a remand to the trial court to cure the defect from the point of abatement. Conclusion The judgments of the High Court dated 13.10.2009 and the District Court dated 20.05.2005 are set aside. The case is remanded to the District Court of Kegalle for the appellant to take necessary steps for substitution according to law. Keywords Substitution of parties — Death of a party during pendency of appeal — Section 760 A of the Civil Procedure Code — Rule 38 of the Supreme Court Rules — Abatement of appeal — Final Order of the District Court — Judgment of the High Court

Parties: Gamarallage Karunawathie v. Godayalage Piyasena and Others
Bench: Dr. Shirani A. Bandaranayake, CJ.; K. Sripavan, J.; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: substitution of parties — death of parties — Civil Procedure Code — appeal — nullity of judgment — legal representatives — partition law — ineffective decree — Supreme Court Rules — abatement of appeal — legal procedure — jurisdiction — High Court — District Court
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal filed by the 20th defendant-appellant, Gamarallage Karunawathie, against the judgment of the High Court of Sabaragamuwa Province, which had rejected her appeal on 13 October 2009. The appellant sought leave to appeal to the Supreme Court, which was granted on 5 February 2010. The case involved multiple parties, including several deceased respondents, which complicated the proceedings. The 15th respondent, Narangoda Lekamalage Kiri Mudiyanse, had died on 30 May 2004 while the case was pending in the District Court, and the 2nd respondent, Manchanayaka Arachchilage Jinaratna Banda, died on 6 September 2007 before the High Court's judgment. The absence of substitution for these deceased parties raised significant legal questions regarding the validity of the judgments rendered by both the District Court and the High Court. The appellant's counsel argued that the failure to substitute the deceased parties rendered the judgments ineffective, necessitating a review by the Supreme Court. Issues The primary issues in this case can be framed as follows: Whether the judgments of the District Court and the High Court are valid given the deaths of the 2nd and 15th respondents without proper substitution. Whether the Supreme Court has the jurisdiction to allow substitution of parties after the judgments have been rendered. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code (as amended) — Section 760 A; Supreme Court Rules, 1990 — Rule 38. Cases Referred to: State of Punjab v Nathu Ram - [AIR 1962 SC 89]; Swaran Singh Puran Singh and another v Ramditta Badhwa (dead) and others - [AIR 1969 Punjab & Haryana 216]; Kanailal Manna and Others v Bhabataran Santra and Others - [AIR 1970 Calcutta 99]; Achhar Singh and Others v Smt. Ananti - [AIR 1971 Punjab & Haryana 477]. Analysis Issue 1: Validity of Judgments Rule applied: Section 760 A of the Civil Procedure Code (as amended) and Rule 38 of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court meticulously analyzed the implications of the deaths of the 2nd and 15th respondents on the validity of the judgments rendered by the lower courts. The Court noted that the 15th respondent had died while the case was pending in the District Court, and no steps were taken for substitution at that time. This absence of substitution meant that the District Court's final order, delivered on 20 May 2005, was rendered ineffective. The Court emphasized that when a party to a case dies, the legal representatives must be brought in to continue the proceedings; otherwise, the court cannot proceed with the matter. The Court referenced Section 760 A, which allows for substitution in cases where the record becomes defective due to the death of a party after the lodging of an appeal. However, the Court found that in this case, the record had become defective before the appeal was lodged, thus complicating the application of this provision. The Court also cited several precedents, including the case of State of Punjab v Nathu Ram, which established that an appeal abates against a deceased respondent, and the appeal cannot proceed without their legal representatives. The Court concluded that both the judgments of the District Court and the High Court were ineffective and thus nullities due to the failure to substitute the deceased parties. Finding: The judgments of the District Court and the High Court were set aside as nullities. Issue 2: Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court to Allow Substitution Rule applied: Jurisdiction under Article 136 of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court further deliberated on its jurisdiction to allow substitution of parties in light of the ineffective judgments. The Court highlighted that while Section 760 A provides a mechanism for substitution, it is contingent upon the record being defective after the appeal has been lodged. In this case, the defect arose prior to the lodging of the appeal, which complicated the application of the statute. The Court underscored the importance of ensuring that all parties are properly represented in legal proceedings, particularly when a party has died. The Court referenced the necessity of adhering to procedural rules to maintain the integrity of the judicial process. The Court ultimately determined that it had the authority to address the issue of substitution, given the circumstances of the case and the need to rectify the procedural defects that had arisen. Finding: The Supreme Court affirmed its jurisdiction to allow substitution in this case. Interaction of Issues The issues of the validity of the judgments and the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court to allow substitution are intricately linked. The failure to substitute the deceased parties directly impacted the validity of the judgments rendered by the lower courts. The Court's analysis demonstrated that without addressing the substitution issue, the judgments could not stand, as they were rendered ineffective due to the absence of necessary parties. Conclusion The Supreme Court set aside the judgments of both the District Court and the High Court, declaring them nullities due to the failure to substitute deceased parties. The case was remanded to the District Court of Kegalle for the appellant to take steps for substitution according to law, with a directive for the District Court to expedite the hearing of the matter. The appeal was dismissed subject to these orders, and no costs were awarded. Keywords: substitution of parties — death of parties — Civil Procedure Code — appeal — nullity of judgment — legal representatives — partition law — ineffective decree — Supreme Court Rules — abatement of appeal — legal procedure — jurisdiction — High Court — District Court
Counsel: Buddhika Gamage for Appellant. D. Jayasinghe for Substituted Plaintiff-Respondent. Srinath Perera for 1A, 17th and 18th Respondents. Rohan Sahabandu for 6th Respondent.

Parties: Jayantha v. Divisional Secretary, Divisional Secretariat, Rajanganaya
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; P. A. Ratnayake, PC, J; S. Hettige, PC, J
Keywords: Article 125 of the Constitution — Interpretation of the Constitution — Constitutional jurisdiction — State land — Provincial Council List — Article 154 P of the Constitution — Writ of certiorari — Land Development Ordinance
Summary: Facts The petitioner, Rajapakshalage Prema Jayantha, was a permit holder for a land issued under the Land Development Ordinance. The petitioner filed an application in the Civil Appellate High Court of the North Central Province seeking a writ of certiorari against the Divisional Secretary of Rajanganaya. The petitioner alleged that the respondent acted illegally by altering the boundaries of the allocated land and violated the rules of natural justice. The respondent objected to the jurisdiction of the High Court, arguing that the subject matter concerned State land and therefore fell outside the High Court's purview. The High Court judge referred the matter to the Supreme Court under Article 125 of the Constitution to obtain an interpretation of Article 154 P regarding the High Court's jurisdiction to issue writs in such matters. The Supreme Court subsequently heard a preliminary objection raised by the Additional Solicitor General, who argued that the reference did not meet the requirements of Article 125. Issues Whether the reference made by the High Court judge to the Supreme Court under Article 125 of the Constitution was valid. Whether the question referred to the Supreme Court involved an interpretation of the Constitution that necessitated a determination by the Supreme Court. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka (Article 33(d), Article 125, Article 154 P, Ninth Schedule) Land Development Ordinance Cases Referred to: Bilimoria v. Minister of Lands and Land Development and Mahaweli Development and 2 Others - [1978/v1-sri-lr-10] SC SD 26/2003 - [sc-sd-26/2003] Analysis Issue 1: Validity of the reference Rule applied: Article 125(1) of the Constitution, which grants the Supreme Court exclusive jurisdiction to interpret the Constitution when such a question arises in other proceedings. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that for a reference to be valid under Article 125, there must be a genuine dispute regarding the interpretation of a constitutional provision. A mere reliance on a constitutional provision by a party or a request for clarification where the law is already settled does not satisfy the threshold for a mandatory reference. The court noted that the High Court judge referred the matter without a clear dispute on interpretation between the parties. Finding: The reference was invalid because it did not meet the criteria set out in Article 125. Issue 2: Necessity of constitutional interpretation Rule applied: Article 125 of the Constitution and the precedent established in Bilimoria v. Minister of Lands and Land Development and Mahaweli Development and 2 Others. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the provisions regarding State land and the jurisdiction of Provincial High Courts had been previously examined and interpreted by the Supreme Court, specifically in the determination on the Bill titled Land Ownership. Because the relevant constitutional provisions were clear and not ambiguous, there was no "question relating to the interpretation of the Constitution" that required a new determination. The court held that the High Court judge was capable of applying existing legal principles to the facts without seeking a constitutional interpretation. Finding: The question referred did not warrant an interpretation of the Constitution as the legal position was already settled. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are interdependent. The determination that the legal provisions were already settled (Issue 2) directly informs the conclusion that the reference was procedurally improper (Issue 1). Because there was no ambiguity requiring interpretation, the High Court lacked the legal basis to invoke the Supreme Court's exclusive jurisdiction under Article 125. Conclusion The preliminary objection raised by the respondent is upheld. The reference is rejected, and the learned Judge of the High Court is directed to consider the matter and make an appropriate order according to law. Keywords Article 125 of the Constitution — Interpretation of the Constitution — Constitutional jurisdiction — State land — Provincial Council List — Article 154 P of the Constitution — Writ of certiorari — Land Development Ordinance

Parties: Rajapakshalage Prema Jayantha v. Divisional Secretary
Bench: Dr. Shirani A. Bandaranayake, CJ.; P. A. Ratnayake, PC, J.; S. Hettige, PC, J.
Keywords: jurisdiction — writ of certiorari — Land Development Ordinance — Article 125 of the Constitution — State land — Provincial Council — interpretation of the Constitution — natural justice — preliminary objection — High Court jurisdiction — constitutional provisions
