Summary: Facts The case revolves around a dispute concerning the jurisdiction of the Civil Appellate High Court of the North Central Province regarding a writ application filed by Rajapakshalage Prema Jayantha, the petitioner, against the Divisional Secretary of Rajanganaya, the respondent. The petitioner, a permit holder under the Land Development Ordinance, alleged that the respondent unlawfully attempted to alter the boundaries of the land allocated to him. This action was claimed to violate the principles of natural justice, prompting the petitioner to seek a writ of certiorari to quash the respondent's decision. The respondent countered by asserting that the matter pertained to State land, thus falling outside the jurisdiction of the High Court. The High Court judge, after considering the arguments, referred the matter to the Supreme Court under Article 125 of the Constitution for clarification on the jurisdictional issues involved. The Supreme Court was tasked with determining whether the High Court had the authority to grant the relief sought by the petitioner and whether the matter warranted interpretation under Article 125. Issues The primary issues framed for consideration were: Whether the High Court had jurisdiction to issue a writ of certiorari against the Divisional Secretary concerning State land. Whether the reference made to the Supreme Court under Article 125 was appropriate given the nature of the dispute. Applicable Law Statutes: Land Development Ordinance; Article 125 of the Constitution; Article 154 P of the Constitution. Cases Referred to: Bilimoria v Minister of Lands and Land Development and Mahaweli Development and 2 Others - [(1978-79-80) 1 Sri LR 10]. Analysis Issue 1: Jurisdiction of the High Court Rule applied: Article 154 P of the Constitution; Land Development Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court began its analysis by reiterating the provisions of Article 125 of the Constitution, which grants it exclusive jurisdiction to interpret constitutional questions arising in legal proceedings. The Court emphasized that a mere reference to a constitutional provision does not automatically invoke the Supreme Court's jurisdiction unless there is a genuine dispute regarding the interpretation of that provision. The Court cited the precedent set in Bilimoria v Minister of Lands, where it was clarified that only disputes necessitating constitutional interpretation should be referred to the Supreme Court. In this case, the High Court was confronted with a matter concerning State land, specifically the legality of the Divisional Secretary's actions regarding boundary alterations. The Court noted that the High Court's jurisdiction under Article 154 P (4) includes the issuance of writs against persons exercising powers under provincial laws. However, the Supreme Court found that the question posed did not involve a dispute over the interpretation of the Constitution but rather a straightforward application of existing laws regarding State land. The Court highlighted that the High Court should have resolved the issue based on established legal principles without necessitating a Supreme Court interpretation. Finding: The Supreme Court upheld the preliminary objection raised by the respondent, concluding that the High Court should have addressed the matter without referring it to the Supreme Court. The Court directed the High Court to consider the case and issue an appropriate order according to the law. Interaction of Issues The issues presented were interrelated, as the determination of the High Court's jurisdiction directly influenced the appropriateness of the Supreme Court's involvement. The Supreme Court's analysis underscored the importance of distinguishing between mere references to constitutional provisions and actual disputes requiring interpretation. Conclusion The Supreme Court ultimately ruled in favor of the respondent, affirming the preliminary objection regarding the High Court's jurisdiction. The Court directed the High Court to proceed with the case based on its findings, emphasizing the need for clarity in jurisdictional matters concerning State land and the application of constitutional provisions. The judgment reinforced the principle that not all references to constitutional articles necessitate Supreme Court intervention, particularly when the issues can be resolved within the existing legal framework. Keywords jurisdiction — writ of certiorari — Land Development Ordinance — Article 125 of the Constitution — State land — Provincial Council — interpretation of the Constitution — natural justice — preliminary objection — High Court jurisdiction — constitutional provisions
Counsel: Mahinda Ralapanawa with Chandima Gamage, Ms. C. Herath and Nilupul Kumari Jayasundara for the Petitioner. Y.J.W. Wijayathilaka PC, ASG, with Bimba Thilakarathne, PC, ASG, A. Navaratne, DSG, Sobhitha Rajakaruna, SSC, Yuresha de Silva, SC and Bhagya Herath, SC for the Attorney General.

Parties: Kuragamage Harischandra Perera and Others v. Muniyandy Paneer Selvam
Bench: Gamini Amaratunga, J.; K. Sripavan, J.; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Urban Development Authority Law — unauthorized construction — mandatory order — delegation of powers — compliance with permits — development activities — planning procedure — judicial review — interpretation of statutes — jurisdiction of local authorities — enforcement of development plans — legal remedies — powers of the UDA — compliance notices — demolition orders
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for special leave to appeal concerning the interpretation of the Urban Development Authority Law No. 41 of 1978, particularly its amendments by Act No. 4 of 1982. The applicant, Kuragamage Harischandra Perera, representing the Municipal Engineers’ Department of the Colombo Municipal Council, sought a mandatory order for the demolition of unauthorized constructions made by the respondent, Muniyandy Paneer Selvam. The initial action was instituted in the Magistrate’s Court of Mt. Lavinia on 1st July 2005, where the applicant sought to enforce compliance with the provisions of the UDA Act. The learned Magistrate granted the order for demolition on 28th April 2006, which was subsequently upheld by the High Court on 15th November 2007. However, the Court of Appeal later reversed this decision, ruling that the applicant had acted without jurisdiction, asserting that the Urban Development Authority (UDA) could not delegate its powers to the applicant to initiate such action. The Court of Appeal's judgment was based on its interpretation of Sections 23(5) and 28(A)(3) of the UDA Act, leading to the present appeal. Issues The primary issues for determination were: (i) Whether the Court of Appeal erred in law in interpreting Section 23(5) and 28(A)(3) of the Urban Development Authority Law as amended by Act No. 4 of 1982? (ii) Whether the Court of Appeal misconstrued the provisions of the Urban Development Authority Act No. 4 of 1982? Applicable Law Statutes: Urban Development Authority Law No. 41 of 1978 as amended by Act No. 4 of 1982. Cases Referred to: Jayasinghe v. Seethawakapura Urban Council (2003) 3 S.L.R. 40. Analysis Issue 1: Interpretation of Section 23(5) and 28(A)(3) Rule applied: The interpretation of statutory provisions concerning the delegation of powers under the Urban Development Authority Law. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court meticulously analyzed the provisions of the UDA Act, particularly focusing on the legislative intent behind the amendments introduced by Act No. 4 of 1982. The Court emphasized that Section 23(5) allows the UDA to delegate its powers, duties, and functions related to planning to officers of local authorities, provided such delegation is done in consultation with the local authority. The Court noted that the delegation of powers is crucial for the effective implementation of development plans and that the UDA must ensure compliance with permits issued for development activities. The Court further elaborated that the powers conferred under Section 28A, which deals with unauthorized development activities, fall within the broader scope of planning and can thus be delegated. The judgment of the Court of Appeal was critiqued for failing to recognize the harmonious construction of the statutory provisions, which is essential for understanding the legislative framework governing urban development. The Court highlighted that the UDA's role is not merely punitive but also regulatory, aimed at ensuring compliance with development plans. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the provisions of Section 28A(3) indeed fall within the scope of planning, allowing for the delegation of powers by the UDA to local authority officers. Issue 2: Misconstruction of the Urban Development Authority Act Rule applied: The principles of statutory interpretation and the scope of powers under the UDA Act. Court's reasoning: The Court reiterated that the interpretation of statutes must align with the legislative intent and the overall objectives of the law. It emphasized that the UDA's authority to enforce compliance with development permits is integral to the planning process. The Court distinguished the present case from the precedent set in Jayasinghe v. Seethawakapura Urban Council, noting that the circumstances were markedly different as the UDA had indeed delegated powers to the local authority in this instance. The Court underscored that the legislative framework is designed to facilitate urban development while ensuring adherence to established plans and regulations. Finding: The Supreme Court found that the Court of Appeal had misconstrued the provisions of the UDA Act, leading to an erroneous conclusion regarding the delegation of powers. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as they both hinge on the interpretation of the same statutory provisions. The Court's analysis of the delegation of powers under Section 23(5) directly impacts the understanding of the enforcement mechanisms outlined in Section 28A. The resolution of these issues clarifies the scope of authority granted to local authorities in urban development matters and reinforces the legislative intent behind the UDA Act. Conclusion The Supreme Court set aside the judgment of the Court of Appeal dated 29th May 2009, affirming the mandatory order of the learned Magistrate dated 28th April 2006. The Court emphasized the importance of compliance with development regulations and the necessity for local authorities to act within the framework established by the UDA Act. No order was made as to costs, reflecting the Court's focus on the legal principles at stake rather than the parties' conduct. Keywords Urban Development Authority Law — unauthorized construction — mandatory order — delegation of powers — compliance with permits — development activities — planning procedure — judicial review — interpretation of statutes — jurisdiction of local authorities — enforcement of development plans — legal remedies — powers of the UDA — compliance notices — demolition orders
Counsel: Jacob Joseph with Sandamalee Somarathna for the 1A Substituted-Applicant-Petitioner-1st Respondent-Petitioner; V. Puvitharan with Ms. S. Kalugamage for the Respondent-Petitioner-Petitioner-Respondent; M. Gunathilleke, S.S.C. for the 2nd Respondent-Respondent-Respondent-Respondent.

Parties: Saravanamuthu v. Packiyam and Valarmathy
Bench: Shiranee Tilakawardene, J; K. Sripavan, J.; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Appeal — Per incuriam — Re-trial — Setting aside judgment — Identification of corpus — Admission of facts — Evidence Ordinance
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Petitioner instituted an action in the District Court of Kalmunai against the Defendants-Appellants-Respondents seeking a declaration of title to a land, ejectment of the defendants, and damages. In their answer, the defendants admitted their residence and the location of the land as averred in the plaint, although they provided a different description of the land based on their own deed. The District Court entered judgment in favor of the plaintiff on 07.05.2002. The defendants appealed to the Provincial High Court of the Eastern Province. On 17.12.2009, the High Court ordered a re-trial, citing a discrepancy between the schedules of the land provided in the plaint and the answer. The plaintiff subsequently filed a motion to set aside this order, arguing it was made per incuriam, but the High Court refused the application on 19.12.2010, stating it was functus officio. The plaintiff then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court order dated 17.12.2009 was made per incuriam. Whether the High Court can order a re-trial without setting aside the District Court judgment. Whether the High Court can order a re-trial when it affirms the District Court judgment. Whether the High Court erred in ordering a re-trial regarding the identification of the corpus despite the admissions in the defendants' answer. Whether the High Court can order a re-trial when the identity of the corpus was not a contested issue at trial. Applicable Law Statutes: Evidence Ordinance, Section 23 Evidence Ordinance, Section 58 High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) (Amendment) Act No. 54 of 2006, Section 5(C)(1) Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 127 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Per Incuriam Order Rule applied: The court applied the principle that judicial orders must be based on the pleadings and issues framed by the parties. Court's reasoning: The High Court ordered a re-trial based on a discrepancy in the description of the land, despite the fact that the defendants had admitted the identity of the land in their answer. By ignoring these pleadings and the lack of a framed issue regarding the corpus, the High Court acted without legal basis. Finding: Yes, the order was made per incuriam. Issue 2: Requirement to Set Aside Judgment Rule applied: Appellate procedure requiring the formal setting aside of a lower court judgment before ordering a re-trial. Court's reasoning: The High Court ordered a re-trial without formally vacating or setting aside the judgment of the District Court, which is a procedural necessity for such an order. Finding: No, the High Court cannot order a re-trial without specifically setting aside the District Court judgment. Issue 3: Affirmation and Re-trial Rule applied: Principles of appellate finality. Court's reasoning: The court noted that a re-trial is inconsistent with an affirmation of the lower court's judgment. Finding: No, the High Court cannot order a re-trial when it affirms the judgment of the District Court. Issue 4: Admissions in Pleadings Rule applied: Section 58 of the Evidence Ordinance. Court's reasoning: Under Section 58, facts admitted in pleadings need not be proved. The defendants explicitly admitted the identity of the land in paragraph 4 of their answer. The High Court ignored this admission when it ordered a re-trial to determine the identity of the corpus. Finding: Yes, the High Court made the order in forgetfulness of the admissions made by the defendants. Issue 5: Absence of Issues Rule applied: The principle that the jurisdiction of the court is limited to the dispute presented for adjudication. Court's reasoning: No issue regarding the identity of the corpus was raised before the District Court. The High Court erred by introducing a point that was not an issue between the parties. Finding: No, the High Court cannot order a re-trial when there was no issue on the question of identification of the corpus. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected as the High Courtâ€™s failure to recognize the admissions under Section 58 of the Evidence Ordinance led to the erroneous conclusion that a re-trial was necessary. Because the identity of the corpus was not a contested issue, the High Court's order for a re-trial was procedurally flawed and made per incuriam, necessitating the restoration of the District Court's original judgment. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The orders of the High Court dated 17.12.2009 and 19.02.2010 are set aside, and the judgment of the District Court of Kalmunai dated 07.05.2002 is affirmed. Parties shall bear their own costs. Keywords Appeal — Per incuriam — Re-trial — Setting aside judgment — Identification of corpus — Admission of facts — Evidence Ordinance

Parties: Dharmaratne and Others v. Ramayanayeke and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J; P. A. Ratnayake, P.C., J; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Article 126 of the Constitution — Executive or administrative action — Institute of Fundamental Studies (IFS) — State control — Agency of the State — Judicial review
Summary: Facts The Petitioners in these applications are scientists who served at the Institute of Fundamental Studies (IFS) on a contract basis. The Petitioners alleged that the Institute violated their fundamental rights guaranteed under Article 12(1) of the Constitution regarding the termination or non-renewal of their service contracts. In SC FR Application No. 73/2007, the Court initially granted leave to proceed. However, during subsequent proceedings, the Court raised a threshold question regarding whether the IFS constitutes a body exercising executive or administrative functions under Article 126 of the Constitution. Similar preliminary objections were raised by Respondents in SC FR Application No. 371/2009 and SC FR Application No. 413/2009, asserting that the IFS is not subject to the Court's fundamental rights jurisdiction. The Court consolidated these matters to determine whether the actions of the IFS are amenable to judicial review under Article 126. Issues Whether the Institute of Fundamental Studies (IFS) is an instrumentality or agency of the State, such that its actions constitute "executive or administrative action" within the meaning of Article 126(1) of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 126(1) Institute of Fundamental Studies, Sri Lanka, Act No. 55 Penal Code Bribery Act Cases Referred to: Rienzie Perera v. University Grants Commission - [1978-80/v1-sri-lr-128] Sukhdev Singh v. Bhagatram Sardar Singh Raghuvanshi - [AIR-1975-SC-1331] Rajaratne v. Air Lanka Ltd. - [1987/v2-sri-lr-128] Leo Samson v. Sri Lankan Airlines - [2001/v1-sri-lr-94] Wijeratne v. Peoples Bank - [1995/v2-sri-lr-352] Wijetunga v. Insurance Corporation - [1982/v1-sri-lr-1] Trade Exchange (Ceylon) Ltd. v. Asian Hotels Corporation Ltd. - [1981/v1-sri-lr-67] Jayakody v. Sri Lanka Insurance and Robinson Hotel Co. Ltd. - [2001/v1-sri-lr-365] Wickrematunga v. Anuruddha Ratwatte - [1998/v1-sri-lr-201] Organization of Protection of Human Rights & Rights of Insurance Employees v. Public Enterprises Reform Commission - [2007/v2-sri-lr-316] Ramana Dayaram Shetty v. International Airport Authority of India - [AIR-1979-SC-1682] Analysis Issue 1: Whether the IFS is an instrumentality of the State Rule applied: The "deep and pervasive control" test, which examines whether an entity is an agency or instrumentality of the State by evaluating statutory incorporation, financial dependence, the nature of functions, and the degree of State control over management and personnel. Court's reasoning: The Court analyzed the IFS Act and found that the Board of Governors is dominated by government representatives, including the President and Prime Minister. The Court noted that the President holds the power to remove appointed members without assigning reasons. Furthermore, evidence showed that the IFS functioned under the direct supervision of the President, with significant personnel decisions—such as contract extensions—being made by the Presidential Secretariat rather than the Board of Governors. The Court observed that the IFS enjoys special privileges, such as tax exemptions and the status of its employees as "public servants" under the Penal Code, which are indicative of sovereign characteristics. The Court concluded that the cumulative effect of these factors demonstrates that the government exercises deep and pervasive control over the Institute. Finding: The IFS is an instrumentality or agency of the State, and its actions constitute executive or administrative action subject to the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court under Article 126(1) of the Constitution. Conclusion The threshold question is answered in favor of the Petitioners, and the preliminary objections raised by the Respondents are overruled. SC FR Application No. 73/2007 shall proceed to a hearing on its merits, and the remaining applications are re-fixed for support. Keywords Fundamental rights — Article 126 of the Constitution — Executive or administrative action — Institute of Fundamental Studies (IFS) — State control — Agency of the State — Judicial review

Parties: Prof. Hapugahange Ranjith Wimalanath Dharmaratne and Dr. Maharalalage Gamini Seneviratne v. Institute of Fundamental Studies and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J.; P. A. Ratnayake, P.C., J.; S. I. Imam, J.
Keywords: fundamental rights — executive action — administrative action — Institute of Fundamental Studies — judicial scrutiny — Article 126 — public interest — State agency — deep and pervasive control — statutory incorporation — employment contracts — research and development — national development — scientific research — constitutional law
Summary: Facts The Petitioners in these applications are scientists who have served in the Institute of Fundamental Studies (IFS) at various points of time. At the time of filing these applications, the 1st Petitioner in SC FR Application No. 73/07 was an Associate Research Professor and the 2nd Petitioner to the said application as well as the Petitioner in SC FR Application No. 371/2009 had served as Senior Research Fellows at IFS. The Petitioner in SC FR Application No. 413/2009 was a very distinguished scientist who had functioned as the Director of IFS from 6th May 1998 up to 5th May 2008. They were all employed at the IFS on contract basis, and in their petitions filed in this Court in terms of Article 126(1) of the Constitution, they complain of violations of their respective fundamental rights guaranteed by Article 12(1) of the Constitution, through certain decisions taken by the Institute pertaining to the continuity of their contracts of service at IFS. When the first of these applications, namely, SC FR Application No. 73/2007 was taken up for support on 28th March 2007, after hearing submissions of learned President’s Counsel for the Petitioners, this Court granted leave to proceed. However, after the filing of objections and counter-affidavits, when this application was taken up for hearing on 15th September 2009, a differently constituted Bench of this Court noted ex mero motu that there was a “threshold question” to be decided in the case, namely whether the IFS is a body which exercises executive or administrative functions within the meaning of Article 126 of the Constitution. The Court also noted that two other applications, namely SC FR Application No. 371/2009 and SC FR Application No. 413/2009 have also been filed, in which leave to proceed had not been granted, but preliminary objection had been taken by learned Counsel for the Respondents in those cases on the basis that the said applications cannot be maintained, as the impugned actions of the IFS do not constitute “executive or administrative action” within the meaning of Article 126(1) of the Constitution. The Court directed that all these three applications should be taken up together for the purpose of determining the aforesaid threshold question and preliminary objection, which went to the jurisdiction of the court to hear and determine these cases. Thereafter, the said threshold question and preliminary objection was taken up for argument before a differently constituted Bench of this Court on 8th March 2010 and order was reserved, but due to the retirement of one of the members of the said Bench, order could not be pronounced. Consequent upon an order being made by the Chief Justice, the matter was taken up for argument afresh on the said threshold question before the presently constituted Bench on 15th February 2011. The parties to SC FR Application No. 371/2009 and SC FR Application No. 413/2009 on that day agreed that the preliminary objection taken up by the Respondents in those cases may be considered together with the threshold question raised by Court in SC FR Application No. 73/2009 on 15th September 2009. Learned Counsel appearing in all the three cases also made extensive oral submissions on the said threshold question and preliminary objection, and after considering an application made by learned Counsel appearing in SC FR Application No. 371/2009 and SC FR Application No. 413/2009, the court granted further time for the parties in those cases to file written submissions. This order will therefore bind all the parties in all the three applications before Court in regard to the said “threshold question” and preliminary objection. Executive and administrative action The only question that has to be considered in this order is whether the impugned actions of the Institute of Fundamental Studies (IFS) established by the Institute of Fundamental Studies, Sri Lanka, Act No. 55 of 1981, as amended by Act No. 5 of 1997, may be subjected to judicial scrutiny in terms of Article 126(1) of the Constitution. The said article provides that- “The Supreme Court shall have sole and exclusive jurisdiction to hear and determine any question relating to the infringement or imminent infringement by executive or administrative action of any fundamental right or language right declared and recognized by Chapter III or Chapter IV.”(Italics added) It is important to observe that while the phrase ‘executive or administrative action’ has not been defined in the Constitution, in deciding whether in a given case, the action that is sought to be challenged under the said article constitutes ‘executive or administrative action’, our courts have focused attention on the particular person, institution or body whose action is sought to be impugned. It is obvious that as Sharvananda J., (as he then was) pointed out in Rienzie Perera and Another v University Grants Commission, [1978-80] 1 Sri L R 128 at page 138- “The wrongful act of an individual, unsupported by State authority, is simply a private wrong. Only if it is sanctioned by the State or done under State authority does it constitute a matter for complaint under Article 126. Fundamental rights operate only between individuals and the State. In the context of fundamental rights, the ‘State’ includes every repository of State power” As Mathew, J once observed in his concurring judgement in Sukhdev Singh & Others v. Bhagatram Sardar Singh Raghuvanshi AIR (1975) SC 1331 : 1975 SCR (3) 619 at page 644- Our courts have emphasized that in this context the ‘State’ includes every repository of State power, and as pointed out by Sharvananda J., in Rienzie Perera and Another v University Grants Commission, supra at page 138, the said expression embraces “exertion of State power in all its forms.” Consistent with this approach, our courts have applied various tests to determine whether a particular person, institution or other body whose action is alleged to be challenged under Article 126 of the Constitution, is an emanation or agency of the State exercising executive or administrative functions. Where the body whose action is sought to be impugned is a corporate entity, these tests have focused, amongst other things, on the nature of the functions performed by the relevant body, the question whether the State is the beneficiary of its activities, the manner of its constitution, whether by statutory incorporation or otherwise, the dependence of the body whose action is sought to be challenged on State funds, the degree of control exercised by the State, the existence in it of sovereign characteristics or features, and whether it is otherwise an instrumentality or agency of the State. However, as will be seen, these tests flow into each other. The Application of the ‘tests’ One of the important tests adopted in the past decisions of this Court is whether the body whose actions are sought to be challenged under Article 126 of the Constitution performs functions of a public (or governmental) nature, or to which the functions of a government department have been transferred. Applying this test, in Wijeratne and Another v People’s Bank and Another [1995] 2 Sri LR 352, this Court concluded that the actions of the People’s Bank did not constitute administrative and executive action, as it performed functions entirely of a commercial nature, notwithstanding the fact that one of the purposes of the said Bank was to develop the co-operative movement, and despite its incorporation by a special Act of Parliament which provided for the members of its Board of Directors to be appointed by the Minister, and for a major contribution to be made towards the initial expenses for its establishment, from the Consolidated Fund. Learned President’s Counsel who appeared for the Petitioners in these cases have submitted that an examination of the provisions of the Institute of Fundamental Studies, Sri Lanka, Act No. 55 of 1981, as amended by Act No. 5 of 1997 (IFS Act), which is the statute by which the Institute of Fundamental Studies (IFS) was incorporated, will reveal that IFS performs functions of public importance and or of a governmental nature, and that its functions would benefit the State or the government, as well as the nation at large. In fact, learned President’s Counsel for the Petitioners went to the extent of suggesting that the IFS is similar to the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), which is a well known agency of the United States government engaged in specialised fundamental studies. Learned Counsel who represented the Respondents in these cases have stressed the difficulties associated with deciding whether a particular function is of a public or governmental nature or not, and have also submitted that it is clear from the jurisprudence of our courts that none of the tests adopted by them can be conclusive by themselves. However, it will be useful to consider the provisions of the IFS Act, and in fact it would be appropriate to begin with an examination of the provisions of the said Act that deal with the objectives and powers of the IFS. Section 2(2) of the IFS Act, which along with Sections 3 and 4 constitute Part I of the Act, provides that the IFS shall be “a body corporate with perpetual succession and a common seal and may sue and be sued in such name.” Section 3 of the said Act provides that “the aims and objectives of the Institute shall be to create an interest in and provide facilities for fundamental and advanced studies and in particular to- (a) initiate, promote and conduct research and original investigation into fundamental studies in general with particular emphasis on mathematics, physical and chemical sciences, life sciences, social sciences and philosophy, taken in the broadest sense; (b) arrange lectures, meetings, seminars and symposia in pursuance of its research work and for the diffusion of scientific knowledge; (c) invite scientists, in Sri Lanka and from abroad, actively engaged in creative work to deliver lectures and participate in its research activities; (d) establish and maintain liaison with scientific workers and scientific institutions in other countries and promote international co-operation in matters relating to the aims and objects of the Institute, while taking care to protect and promote the national interest; and (e) do such other acts and things as may be necessary to promote the aims and objects of the Institute.” Section 44 of the Act does not elaborate or further the definition of ‘fundamental and advanced studies’, and simply states that ‘fundamental’ and ‘advanced’ studies “include experimental investigations”. While the website of the Ministry of Technology and Research, within whose purview the Institute of Fundamental Studies falls, in terms of the assignment of subjects and functions to Minsters made under Article 44(1)(a) of the Constitution, reproduces some salient provisions of the IFS Act, the website of the IFS at the web address http://www.ifs.ac.lk/ sheds more light on its primary objective, which is to engage in basic research that would result in useful applications for national development and social welfare. These websites, which are very much in the public domain, contain useful information regarding the ongoing research projects and activities of IFS which are intended to facilitate, for instance, finding new energy sources through bio-fuel, geo-thermal and solar energy, unveiling the hidden potential in our flora, serving the nation with biological research, the provision of assistance for the disabled with artificial intelligence, finding solutions for environmental problems through scientific research and making science simple and attractive to everyone. The Institute is vested by Section 4 of the IFS Act with such powers and rights as may be necessary to achieve its aims and objects, and in particular it may, amongst other things, establish a research fund for the promotion of fundamental and advanced studies, institute Research Professorships, Associate Research Professorships and Research Fellowships, establish a research fund for the promotion of fundamental and advanced studies, and award prizes and medals, for fundamental and advanced studies. It is not easy to identify any of the aforesaid functions and powers as necessarily public or governmental functions. Learned President’s Counsel for the Respondents have contended that it is obvious from Section 3 and 4 of the IFS Act that the IFS is intended to catalyse and create an environment conducive to the highest level of scientific activity in Sri Lanka, and they have specifically referred to certain observations contained in the Cabinet Memorandum by which the approval of the Cabinet of Ministers was sought by the relevant Minister for the incorporation of the IFS. However, as submitted by learned Counsel for the Respondents, these provisions of the IFS Act do not reveal expressly, or suggest impliedly, that the IFS was created for achievement of ‘State policy’ or for fulfilling the mandate of any Ministry or Institution. In my view, the mere fact that in the United States of America, the National Science Foundation (NSF), which is an agency that supports fundamental research and education in science and engineering in areas other than those covered by the mandates of the National Institute of Health (NIH) and the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), which respectively cater to fundamental research and study in the fields of medicine and aeronautics, has been established by the US Government as its agency, does not necessarily mean that similar activity should be categorised as governmental in other jurisdictions, and in particular, in Sri Lanka. Of course, as pointed out by Learned President’s Counsel for the Petitioners, from the Sri Lankan perspective, fundamental and advanced studies further some of the directive principles of State Policy enshrined in Article 27 of the Constitution, and could be of great importance for national development, environmental protection and promoting the welfare of the People. In particular, such studies, if undertaken by the State or a governmental agency, could further infrastructure development, augment energy resources including nuclear, solar, wind and hydro power generation, contribute to the development of irrigation, agriculture, fisheries, industries, transportation, telecommunication, housing and health, and assist in the combat of natural disasters in the context of global warming and climatic change. However, in my considered opinion, it is difficult to conclude from a mere analysis of Sections 3 and 4 of the IFS Act or from information contained in the IFS website alone, that IFS was intended to be an instrumentality or agency of the State established to create an interest in and provide facilities for fundamental and advanced studies, or its actions were capable of being classified as ‘executive or administrative’ action, within the meaning of Article 126 of the Constitution. This is more so because the public or governmental character of any activity will differ from nation to nation and from time to time. As Atukorale J., observed in Rajaratne v Air Lanka Ltd [1987] 2 Sri LR 128 at page 145 - “The demands and obligations of the modern welfare State have ‘resulted’ in an alarming increase in the magnitude and range of governmental activity. For the purpose of ensuring and achieving the rapid development of the whole country by means of public economic activity the government is called upon to embark on a multitude of commercial and industrial undertakings. In fact a stage has now been reached when it has become difficult to distinguish between governmental and non-governmental functions. This distinction is now virtually non-existent.” It is necessary, in my view, to look at the other tests adopted by this Court in determining whether it should exercise jurisdiction under Article 126 in a given case. It may be useful in this connection to refer to the following observation of Sharvananda J (as he then was) in Wijetunga v Insurance Corporation and Another [1982] 1 Sri LR 1, at pages 6 to 7:- “For determining the integral relationship between the State and the Corporation, we have to examine the provisions of the statute by which the Corporation has been established. If the statute in terms answers this question, there is no need for further inquiries, but in the absence of such statutory declaration or provision, the intention of Parliament is to be gathered from the provisions of the statute constituting the Corporation. These provisions have to be judged in the light of the following:- (a) First the incorporation of the Body though not determinative is of some significance as an indication by Parliament of its intention to create a legal entity with a personality of its own, distinct from the State. (b) Secondly the degree of control exercised by the Minister on the functioning of the corporation is a very relevant factor. A complete dependence on him marks it as really a Governmental body, while comparative freedom to pursue its administration is an element negativing the intention to constitute it a government agency. (c) Third is the degree of dependence of the Corporation on the Government for its financial needs.” The mere fact that the institution or body whose action is challenged under Article 126 has been incorporated by statute, or otherwise enjoys a personality distinct from that of the State, is not necessarily conclusive in this regard. Thus, although in Trade Exchange (Ceylon) Ltd. v. Asian Hotels Corporation Ltd. [1981] 1 Sri LR 67, this Court had held that the action of a public commercial company, incorporated under the Companies Ordinance, was not amenable to the writ of certiorari and other writs despite most of its capital being contributed by the State, since such a company was a juristic person whose personality was distinct from those of its shareholders, in decisions such as Rajaratne v Air Lanka Ltd [1987] 2 Sri LR 128 and Jayakody v Sri Lanka Insurance and Robinson Hotel Co. Ltd. and Others, [2001] 1 Sri LR 365, this Court was prepared to hold that the actions of a company could amount to executive or administrative action in terms of Article 126 of the Constitution by reason of the high degree of control that was exercised over the company by the State. It is therefore necessary to consider the other provisions of the IFS Act that shed light in regard to the question as to whether the IFS came within the ambit of State control, in particular, Sections 5 to 11 of the IFS Act which constitute Part II of the Act entitled ‘The Authorities of the Institute’. According to Section 5 of the IFS Act, the authorities of the Institute are the Board of Governors and the Research Council. Section 6(1) provides that the Institute shall be administered by a Board of Governors consisting of the President of Sri Lanka, who is the Chairman, the Prime Minister, the Leader of the Opposition, the Director of the Institute, the Chairman of the University Grants Commission, the Advisor to the President on Scientific Affairs, two members elected from the Research Council and four persons appointed by the President from among persons who in his opinion are specially qualified in relation to the work of the Institute. Learned Counsel for the Respondents have contended that the Board of Governors is intended to enjoy a high degree of independence, which fact is highlighted by the provision which makes the Leader of the Opposition a member of the Board of Governors by virtue of office. On the other hand, learned President’s Counsel for the Petitioners have stressed that apart from the President of Sri Lanka, the Prime Minister, and the Advisor to the President on Scientific Affairs, who are also ex officio members of the Board of Governors, the Director of the Institute, and four other members of the Board are appointed by the President, and this brings the number of government representatives in the Board to at least seven out of a total of twelve Board members. Furthermore, while Section 6(2) to 6(5) of the IFS Act contain provisions regulating the filling of vacancies that could arise in the Board of Governors, it is significant to note that Section 6(6) of the Act expressly provides that “a member appointed by the President under this section may be removed by him at any time without reasons assigned and such removal shall not be questioned in any court.” Although I am inclined to the view that these provisions manifest a legislative intent to make the Board of Governors subservient to the executive arm of government, whether that would give the government dominance over the affairs of the IFS would depend on the other provisions of the IFS Act, particularly those that relate to the function and role of the Board of Governors and the Research Council, and the manner in which the IFS has in reality functioned. In this connection, it is significant to note that Section 9 of the IFS Act provides that, subject to the other provisions of the Act and the rules of the Institute, the powers and duties of the Institute shall be vested in the Board of Governors, and that these include the power to hold, control and administer the property and funds of the Institute, and to regulate and determine all matters concerning the Institute in accordance with the IFS Act and the rules of the Institute. For the purposes of this case, it is pertinent to note that as provided in Section 9(d) of the IFS Act, the power is specifically vested in the Board of Governors, after consideration of the recommendations of the Research Council, to institute, abolish or suspend Research Professorships, Associate Research Professorships, Fellowships, and to determine the qualifications required for appointments to such posts and to determine the emoluments payable to the holders thereof. The other internal body of the IFS is the Research Council, which consists of two persons appointed by the President from among persons who have gained eminence in the field of science, all Research Professors, Associate Research Professors, and Senior Research Fellows of the Institute, three members elected by the Research Fellows of the Institute from among their number, and five representatives of Universities in Sri Lanka who are appointed by the Board of Governors from among persons nominated by the University Grants Commission, who have excelled in fundamental research. In terms of Section 10(1) of the IFS Act, the Director of IFS also functions as the Chairman of the Council. Learned Counsel for the Respondents have relied on these provisions to argue that the Research Council is by its constitution, intended to be independent from executive or ministerial control, and is a check on the dominance of the Board of Governors in regard to the conduct of its affairs. They have also relied on Section 11(a) of the Act, which provides that “the Council shall have control and general direction of instruction, education, research and examination in the Institute”. However, it is necessary to note that this provision is expressly made subject to the other provisions of the IFS Act and the rules of the Institute, and it is manifest that on the whole, the functions of the Council, other than the power to elect two of its members to the Board of Governors, are mainly recommendatory or advisory. This becomes abundantly clear from the provisions of Part IV of the IFS Act (Sections 19 to 24) which contain elaborate provisions for the appointment and disciplinary control of Research and other staff of the Institute. In terms section 19 of the Act, the first Director of IFS was appointed directly by the President, and subsequent vacancies in said post were filled by the Board of Governors after taking into consideration the recommendations of the Research Council. The appointment of Research Professors and Associate Research Professor has to be made by the Board of Governors, after considering the recommendations of the Research Council, while Research Fellows are appointed by the Director of the IFS in consultation with the Research Professor or the Head of the Project concerned. The Board of Governors is also empowered by Section 20 to make rules defining the privileges of the Director of IFS and other research staff. It is noteworthy that, while Section 21 of the IFS Act empowers the Board of Governors to appoint to IFS such other officers and servants as the Board may deem necessary, Section 22 of the Act vests on the Board of Governors the power to dismiss and exercise disciplinary control over the staff including the research staff of the Institute and fix the wages or salary or other remuneration of such staff and determine the terms and conditions of service of such staff. It is expressly provided in the proviso to Section 22, that no member of the research staff may be dismissed except by a majority of two-thirds of the votes of the members of the Board at a meeting on the ground of gross dereliction of duty or for moral turpitude or for other good and sufficient cause to be recorded in writings. It is also noteworthy that in terms of Section 24(1) of the Act, any officer in public service may, at the request of the Board of Governors and with the consent of that officer and the Secretary to the Ministry of the Minister in charge of Public Administration, be temporarily appointed to the staff of the Institute for such period as may be determined by the Board, or with like consent be permanently appointed to such staff. These provisions of the IFS Act clearly show that the dominant organ of the IFS insofar as the appointment, conditions of service, disciplinary control and termination of service of the staff of IFS is concerned, is the Board of Governors, but whether the IFS is in reality an agency of the State whose actions constitute ‘executive or administrative’ action, would depend on the degree of control exercised by the government in regard to its affairs. In this context, it will be useful to consider to what extent the State enjoys financial control over the IFS. Our attention has been drawn by learned Counsel to the provisions of Part V of the IFS Act (Sections 25 to 31) which contain elaborate provisions relating to the IFS Provident Fund, and Part VI of the Act (Sections 32 to 36), which is headed ‘Finance’ and deals with the finances of the Institute. It is significant that in terms of to Section 33 of the Act, the Institute is entitled to receive grants from any source, whether in Sri Lanka or abroad, and to negotiate direct for such grants with any individual, institution or body of persons whether incorporate or not, for the purpose of carrying out its aims and objects. Learned President’s Counsel for the Petitioners have specifically invited our attention to Section 32 of the IFS Act, which provides that the “Government may donate to the Institute annually a grant adequate for the purpose of carrying out the aims and objects of the Institute”, and Section 34(2)(a) of the Act, which provides that all such sums of money as may be voted from time to time by Parliament for the use of the Institute shall be paid into the Fund of the IFS. However, these provisions are merely facilitative, and although it would appear from page 18 of the government publication entitled ‘Budget Estimates 2009’ that budgetary provision had been made for the President of Sri Lanka to contribute a sum of Rs. 7,227,000 to the IFS in 2007, and further sums of approximately Rs. 13,950,000 and Rs. 20,000,000 to be so contributed, respectively in the years 2008 and 2009, neither the Petitioners in these cases nor the Respondents have provided to this Court any statements of accounts or other evidence to show whether in reality any funds had been donated to the IFS by the government or voted by Parliament, and if so, whether they were so overwhelming so as to demonstrate effective financial control by the government. The IFS Act contains a few provisions that provide IFS with special favours which are generally granted only to government departments and agencies. Thus, for instance, Section 35 of the said Act provides for the exemption of the IFS from the payment of rates imposed by any local authority, and also makes provision for the Minister under whose purview the IFS falls, in consultation with the Minister in charge of the subject of Finance, to exempt the Institute from the payment of any customs duty for goods imported by the Institute for the achievement of its aims and objects. These are special favours granted to the institution by the State, which in the words of Amarasinghe J., in Wickrematunga v. Anuruddha Ratwatte and Others [1998] 1 Sri LR 201, at page 224 show “contact as well as symbiotic relationship” with the State. These features also signify the role of the IFS as a venture with some national importance. This aspect is also highlighted by Section 36 of the Act, which seek to subject the audit of accounts of the IFS to Article 154 of the Constitution conferring to the Auditor General the power to scrutinise and audit its accounts and impose surcharges and imposing on him the duty to report to Parliament about its affairs on an annual basis. It is interesting to note that Section 37, which appears in Part VI of the IFS Act entitled ‘General’, provides that the IFS is “open to all persons of either sex, of whatever race, creed or nationality and no test of religious belief or profession shall be adopted or imposed in order to entitle any person to be admitted as a Director, Professor, Member or Research Fellow of the Institute or to hold any appointment therein or to hold, enjoy or exercise any advantage or privilege therein.” Similarly, Sections 38 and 39 are intended to protect the officers of the Institute from legal proceedings and execution of writ any act which is done in good faith or on the direction of the Board, and Section 38(3) specifically entitles any officer so sued to be reimbursed by IFS for any expenses incurred in such litigation. It is also of some significance to note that Section 40 of the IFS Act provides that all officers and servants of the Institute shall be deemed to be public servants within the meaning and for the purposes of the Penal Code, and Section 41 further provides that the IFS shall be deemed to be a “scheduled institution” within the meaning of the Bribery Act. These provisions lend credence to the submission of the learned President’s Counsel for the Petitioners that the Institute was intended to be a vibrant research arm of the State playing a major role in national development and progress. These are significant pointers which appear to endow the IFS with what was described by Atukorale J., in Rajaratne v. Air Lanka Ltd. and Others [1987] 2 Sri LR 128 at page 146 as “sovereign characteristics and features”, the existence of which in the corporate body was regarded as “strongly indicative of it being an organ or agency or instrumentality of the government”. On this analysis of the provisions of the IFS Act and material contained in the official websites of the IFS as well as that of the Ministry of Technology and Research, it is manifest that at least in regard to the appointment, conditions of service and disciplinary control of the Director, Associate Directors, Deputy Directors, Research Professors Associate Research Professor and Research Fellows of the Institute, and other staff, it is the Board of Governors that is vested with and enjoys power, and that the role of the Research Council is nothing but recommendatory or advisory. Since, as already noted, the Board of Governors has in terms of the Act, very wide powers, and it consists of the President of Sri Lanka and the Prime Minister, and other representatives of the government, there can be little doubt that, as submitted by learned President’s Counsel for the Petitioners, the actions of IFS concerning the conditions of service and tenure of the senior academic staff of the Institute, constitute ‘executive or administrative’ action within the meaning of Article 126 of the Constitution. Learned Counsel for the Respondents have contended that the omission from the IFS Act of any express provision to empower the Minister to make general or special directions to the Board of Governors of IFS, manifests a legislative intent to keep the IFS free from government control. However, it is my considered view that the absence of such express provision in the IFS Act will not necessarily be conclusive, where the provisions of the relevant Act are structured so as to give the State dominance over the Institute, or it is possible to conclude from the evidence that an express provision for Ministerial control would be rendered redundant by reason of the deep and pervasive control which is in reality enjoyed by the government over its affairs. Deep and Pervasive Control In my view, in determining whether the actions of any person, institution or other body constitutes ‘executive or administrative’ action within the meaning of Article 126 of the Constitution, a mere analysis of the provisions of the incorporating statute or other relevant legislation may not always suffice, and may sometimes be misleading. In fact, in certain circumstances, a person or body may exercise executive or administrative power even in the absence of any enabling legislation. In Rajaratne v Air Lanka Ltd., [1987] 2 Sri LR 128 at page 140, this Court quoted the following words of Bhagwati J. in Ramana Dayaram Shetty v. International Airport Authority of India-AIR 1979 SC 1682-

Parties: Kahandagamage Dharmasiri Bogahahena v. The Republic of Sri Lanka
Bench: Tilakawardane, J.; Marsoof, J.; Imam, J.
Keywords: Special Leave to Appeal — Court of Appeal — High Court — Eyewitness Testimony — Credibility of Witnesses — Omission in Statements — Reasonable Doubt — Criminal Justice — Judicial Review — Article 139 of the Constitution — Forensic Evidence — Trial without Jury — Belated Evidence — Witness Intimidation — Legal Precedents
Summary: Facts The case revolves around the tragic murder of Arabada Gamage Nandawathie, which occurred on 31 March 1993 at her residence in Hambantota. The incident involved the Appellant, Kahandagamage Dharmasiri Bogahahena, who was one of three accused in the case. The prosecution presented two eyewitnesses: Kusumawathie, the mother of the deceased, and Maduranga, the deceased's son. The Appellant and the second accused were found guilty and sentenced to death by the High Court of Hambantota on 29 June 2004, while the third accused was acquitted. The Appellant's conviction was upheld by the Court of Appeal on 17 June 2008, prompting him to seek Special Leave to Appeal to the Supreme Court. The Appellant raised two primary questions of law: whether the courts failed to consider serious omissions in the eyewitness evidence and whether the Court of Appeal neglected to scrutinize the police information book for valid omissions. The background of the case is steeped in the socio-political turmoil of Sri Lanka during the late 1980s and early 1990s, particularly the JVP insurrection, which created an atmosphere of fear and intimidation. The witnesses testified that the Appellant had threatened them with death if they revealed his identity, which significantly impacted their willingness to disclose information to the police immediately after the incident. Issues The issues presented for determination were: 1. Whether the courts failed to consider serious and material omissions in the evidence of the main witnesses. 2. Whether the Court of Appeal erred in failing to scrutinize the information book of the police officers to determine if valid omissions existed. Applicable Law Statutes: Article 128 of the Constitution of Sri Lanka; Article 139 of the Constitution of Sri Lanka. Cases Referred to: Surendra Pal & Ors v. State of U.P & ANR - [2010]; Sumanasena v. Attorney-General - [1999] 3 Sri.L.R 137; Keerthi Bandara v. The Attorney General - [2000] 2 Sri.L.R 245; Banda and Others v. The Attorney General - [1999] 3 Sri. L.R, 168-174; Fattal v. Wallbrook Trustee (Jersey) Ltd, CA - [2008] EWCA Civ 427; Ambika Prasad and Another v. State (Delhi Administration) - [2000] SCC Crl.522. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the courts failed to consider serious and material omissions in the evidence of the main witnesses. Rule applied: The principle of evaluating eyewitness testimony and the implications of omissions in statements made to the police. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the testimonies of the eyewitnesses, particularly focusing on the first witness, Kusumawathie. It was noted that she did not mention the Appellant's name in her initial statement to the police, which raised questions about the reliability of her testimony. However, the Court recognized that her fear of retribution from the Appellant, who had threatened her life, was a significant factor that influenced her decision to withhold information. The Court emphasized the context of the socio-political environment during the time of the incident, which was marked by violence and intimidation. The Court cited the case of Surendra Pal & Ors v. State of U.P & ANR, highlighting that the absence of names in an inquest report does not inherently cast doubt on the presence of eyewitnesses. The Court concluded that the delay in naming the Appellant was understandable given the circumstances and did not undermine the credibility of the witnesses. Finding: The Court found that the evidence of the witnesses was credible and corroborated by forensic evidence, thus upholding the conviction. Issue 2: Whether the Court of Appeal erred in failing to scrutinize the information book of the police officers to determine if valid omissions existed. Rule applied: The obligation of the trial judge to peruse the information book when vital omissions are alleged. Court's reasoning: The Appellant contended that the Court of Appeal failed to adequately review the police information book, which could have revealed significant omissions. The Supreme Court analyzed the procedural obligations of the trial judge and the appellate court regarding the information book. It was noted that the defense counsel did not highlight any vital omissions during the trial, which diminished the necessity for the trial judge to consult the information book. The Court referenced the case of Keerthi Bandara, asserting that it is the responsibility of the trial judge to examine the information book when discrepancies are raised. The Court concluded that the Appellant's failure to mark discrepancies during the trial meant that the trial judge acted within his rights by not consulting the information book. Finding: The Court upheld the decision of the Court of Appeal, affirming that the trial judge had exercised proper judicial discretion. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as they both concern the credibility of eyewitness testimony and the procedural obligations of the courts in evaluating that testimony. The failure to consider omissions in witness statements directly impacts the assessment of the reliability of those witnesses, which in turn affects the appellate court's review of the trial court's findings. Conclusion The Supreme Court ultimately refused the appeal, affirming the judgment of the Court of Appeal and upholding the conviction of the Appellant. The Court emphasized the importance of ensuring justice for both the accused and the victim, reiterating that the judicial process must balance the rights of the accused with the need to maintain law and order in society. The Court found no grounds to overturn the previous judgments, concluding that the evidence presented was sufficient to support the conviction. Keywords Special Leave to Appeal — Court of Appeal — High Court — Eyewitness Testimony — Credibility of Witnesses — Omission in Statements — Reasonable Doubt — Criminal Justice — Judicial Review — Article 139 of the Constitution — Forensic Evidence — Trial without Jury — Belated Evidence — Witness Intimidation — Legal Precedents
Counsel: Saliya Pieris with Upul Kumarapperuma and Suranga Munasinghe for Accused-Appellant. G. Kulatunga, SSC for Respondent-Respondent.

Parties: J.H. Jacotine v. Air Lanka Limited and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J.; Marsoof, P.C., J.; Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: constructive termination — Labour Tribunal — employment law — misconduct — disciplinary action — reinstatement — vacation of post — employer-employee relationship — appeal — judicial review — back wages — costs — procedural fairness — burden of proof — employee rights
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for Leave to Appeal to the Supreme Court from an order of the Provincial High Court concerning the termination of employment of J.H. Jacotine by Air Lanka Limited. The applicant, Jacotine, claimed that his services were constructively terminated by the respondent, Air Lanka, and sought reinstatement. The background of the case indicates that Jacotine had a tumultuous employment history with Air Lanka, marked by multiple instances of misconduct, including financial discrepancies and insubordination. Following a disciplinary inquiry, Jacotine was reinstated under strict conditions, which he subsequently refused to accept, leading Air Lanka to consider him as having vacated his post. The Labour Tribunal initially dismissed Jacotine's application for relief, asserting that he had vacated his position due to his refusal to report for work under the new terms. However, the Provincial High Court later reinstated Jacotine, awarding him back wages and costs, which prompted Air Lanka to appeal to the Supreme Court. Issues The primary issues before the Supreme Court were: Whether the refusal of Jacotine to accept the conditions of reinstatement constituted a constructive termination of his employment. Whether the Labour Tribunal's finding that Jacotine had vacated his post was justified. Whether the High Court's reversal of the Labour Tribunal's decision was appropriate. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act (as amended) Cases Referred to: Nelson de Silva v. Sri Lanka State Engineering Corporation - [1996] 2 SLR 342; Hare v. Murphy Bros. - [1973] I.C.R 331; Superintendent Abottsleigh Group and Others v. Estate Services Union - [1991] 1 SLR 380; Pfizer Ltd v. Rasanayagam - [1991] 1 SLR 290. Analysis Issue 1: Constructive Termination Rule applied: The principle of constructive termination in employment law. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the circumstances surrounding Jacotine's refusal to accept the conditions of his reinstatement. The Court noted that constructive termination occurs when an employee feels compelled to resign due to the employer's conduct. In this case, Jacotine's refusal to accept a demotion and other conditions was pivotal. The Court highlighted that Jacotine had a history of misconduct, which included multiple instances of financial discrepancies and insubordination. The Labour Tribunal had found that Jacotine's refusal to report for work after being reinstated under specific conditions constituted a vacation of his post. The Court emphasized that the employer had acted within its rights to terminate the employment based on Jacotine's refusal to comply with the reinstatement terms. The Court referenced the case of Nelson de Silva, which established that both physical and mental elements must be satisfied to determine vacation of post. The Court concluded that Jacotine's actions demonstrated a clear intention not to return to work, thereby justifying the Labour Tribunal's decision. Finding: The Court found that Jacotine had not established that he was constructively terminated as alleged. Issue 2: Justification of Labour Tribunal's Decision Rule applied: The standard of review for Labour Tribunal decisions. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court scrutinized the High Court's decision to reinstate Jacotine, asserting that the Labour Tribunal was the appropriate forum for determining the facts of the case. The Court reiterated that the Labour Tribunal had acted reasonably in concluding that Jacotine's refusal to report for work amounted to a vacation of employment. The Court noted that the High Court's reversal of this finding was improper, as it failed to consider the extensive history of Jacotine's misconduct and the employer's justified actions in response. The Court emphasized the need for a careful examination of the employer-employee relationship and the principles governing constructive termination. Finding: The Court upheld the Labour Tribunal's decision, stating that the High Court's ruling was not supported by the evidence. Interaction of Issues The issues of constructive termination and the justification of the Labour Tribunal's decision are interrelated, as the determination of whether Jacotine's refusal to accept the reinstatement conditions constituted a constructive termination directly impacts the validity of the Labour Tribunal's findings. The Supreme Court's analysis highlighted the importance of the context surrounding the employment relationship and the need for a thorough examination of the facts before reaching a conclusion. Conclusion The Supreme Court set aside the High Court's judgment, affirming the Labour Tribunal's order that Jacotine had vacated his employment. The Court ordered Jacotine to pay costs to Air Lanka, emphasizing the need for accountability in employment disputes. The judgment underscored the complexities of employment law, particularly in cases involving allegations of constructive termination, and reaffirmed the Labour Tribunal's role as the primary adjudicator in such matters. Keywords constructive termination — Labour Tribunal — employment law — misconduct — disciplinary action — reinstatement — vacation of post — employer-employee relationship — appeal — judicial review — back wages — costs — procedural fairness — burden of proof — employee rights
Counsel: Ms. Manoli Jinadasa for the Respondent-Respondent-Petitioner. Lucky Wickramanayke for the Applicant-Appellant-Respondent.

Parties: Kotagala Plantations Limited v. Ranasinghe
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Saleem Marsoof, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: Industrial Disputes Act — Termination of service — Misconduct — Compensation — Just and equitable — Saleem v Hatton National Bank — Contributory fault — Burden of proof — Special circumstances
Summary: Facts The Respondent was employed by the Sri Lanka State Plantations Corporation starting in October 1975. Following the privatization of the plantations in 1992, his employment was vested with the Petitioner, Kotagala Plantations Limited, with his past service recognized. In January 1995, the Petitioner issued a charge sheet to the Respondent alleging serious misconduct. Following a domestic inquiry, the Respondent was found guilty of several charges, including negligence and irregular disposal of estate property, and his services were terminated in May 1996. The Respondent challenged his termination before the Labour Tribunal. The President of the Labour Tribunal found the Respondent guilty of multiple charges, noting that his actions caused financial losses to the Petitioner and that he had admitted to signing blank vouchers. Consequently, the Labour Tribunal dismissed the application, ruling the termination just and equitable. The Respondent appealed this decision to the Provincial High Court. The High Court affirmed the finding that the Respondent committed serious misconduct and that the termination was just and equitable, yet it awarded the Respondent two years of salary as compensation. The Petitioner subsequently appealed this award to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court erred in law by awarding compensation to the Respondent despite finding the termination of service to be just and equitable. Whether the High Court erred in law by applying the principles of Saleem v Hatton National Bank to justify the award of compensation. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act (as amended) Employment Rights Act 1996 (UK) Cases Referred to: Saleem v Hatton National Bank - [1994/v3-sri-lr-409] W. Devis & Sons Ltd v Atkins - [1977] AC 931 Morrison v Amalgamated TGWU - [1989] IRLR 361 HSBC Bank Plc v Maden - [2000] ICR 1283 Municipal Council of Colombo v Munasinghe - [71-nlr-1] Caledonian (Ceylon) Tea and Rubber Estates Limited v Hillman - [79/v1-nlr-421] Kotagala Plantations Limited v Ceylon Planters Society - [sc-appeal-144/2009] Analysis Issue 1: Awarding of compensation Rule applied: Section 31B(1) of the Industrial Disputes Act and the principle that an award must be "just and equitable" as established in Municipal Council of Colombo v Munasinghe. Court's reasoning: The Court reasoned that the Respondentâ€™s misconduct, including gross negligence and unauthorized disposal of property, contributed entirely to his dismissal. The Petitioner had provided sufficient warnings, and the Respondentâ€™s actions caused significant financial loss. The Court emphasized that the "just and equitable" mandate does not grant a tribunal the freedom to act by "whim or caprice." Since the dismissal was found to be justified, and the Respondent was the sole author of his own misfortune, there was no legal basis to grant compensation. Finding: The High Court erred in awarding compensation, as the Respondentâ€™s conduct precluded him from claiming relief for loss of career. Issue 2: Application of Saleem v Hatton National Bank Rule applied: The principle from Saleem v Hatton National Bank regarding "special circumstances" justifying relief even when termination is justified. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the Saleem principle requires the existence of "special circumstances" to warrant compensation. In this case, the Respondentâ€™s blatant neglect of duties, disregard for warnings, and intentional wrongdoing did not constitute special circumstances. The Court distinguished this case from situations where an employer acts in bad faith or fails to provide necessary utilities, noting that the Respondent had no one to blame but himself. Finding: The High Court misapplied the Saleem principle, as the facts of the case did not meet the threshold for special circumstances. Interaction of Issues The findings are intrinsically linked; the determination that the dismissal was just and equitable (Issue 1) directly negated the possibility of finding "special circumstances" (Issue 2). Because the Respondentâ€™s own conduct caused the termination, the legal justification for awarding compensation under the Saleem doctrine was entirely absent. Conclusion The appeal of the Petitioner is allowed. The judgment of the High Court is set aside, and the order of the Labour Tribunal dated 15th October 2007 is affirmed. No costs are ordered. Keywords Industrial Disputes Act — Termination of service — Misconduct — Compensation — Just and equitable — Saleem v Hatton National Bank — Contributory fault — Burden of proof — Special circumstances

Parties: Ranasinghe v. Kotagala Plantations Limited
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Saleem Marsoof, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: Industrial Disputes Act — Termination of service — Misconduct — Compensation — Just and equitable — Contributory fault — Saleem v Hatton National Bank — Burden of proof — Special circumstances
Summary: Facts The Petitioner was employed by the Sri Lanka State Plantations Corporation starting in October 1975. Following the privatization of the plantations in 1992, his employment contract was vested with the Respondent. The Petitioner continued his service without interruption, with his prior service credited to his tenure with the Respondent. In January 1995, the Respondent issued a charge sheet against the Petitioner alleging serious misconduct. Following a domestic inquiry, the Petitioner was found guilty of several charges, including negligence and unauthorized disposal of estate property, leading to the termination of his services in May 1996. The Petitioner challenged his dismissal in the Labour Tribunal. The President of the Labour Tribunal dismissed the application, finding that the Petitioner was grossly negligent, had caused financial losses to the Respondent, and had engaged in irregular activities. The Petitioner appealed this decision to the Provincial High Court. While the High Court affirmed the finding that the dismissal was justified, it nonetheless awarded the Petitioner two years of salary as compensation. The Petitioner subsequently sought leave to appeal to the Supreme Court regarding the award of compensation. Issues Whether the High Court erred in law by awarding compensation to the Petitioner despite finding the dismissal justified. Whether the High Court erred in law by applying the principles of Saleem v Hatton National Bank to the present case. Whether the High Court had jurisdiction to grant relief after rejecting the Petitioner's grounds of appeal. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act No. 32 of 1950 (as amended) Employment Rights Act 1996 (UK) Cases Referred to: Saleem v Hatton National Bank - [1994/v3-sri-lr-409] W.Devis & Sons Ltd v Atkins - [1977] AC 931 Morrison v Amalgamated TGWU - [1989] IRLR 361 HSBC Bank Plc v Maden - [2000] ICR 1283 Municipal Council of Colombo v Munasinghe - [71-nlr-202] Caledonian (Ceylon) Tea and Rubber Estates Limited v Hillman - [79/v1-nlr-421] Kotagala Plantations Limited v Ceylon Planters Society - [sc-appeal-144/2009] Analysis Issue 1: Award of compensation Rule applied: Section 31B(1) of the Industrial Disputes Act. Court's reasoning: The Court held that while the Industrial Disputes Act grants discretion to award relief, such discretion is not absolute. Compensation is intended for cases of unfair dismissal or redundancy. Where an employeeâ€™s own misconduct and gross negligence are the primary causes of dismissal, and the employer has provided sufficient warnings, the dismissal is just and equitable. The Court found that the Petitionerâ€™s actions caused significant financial loss and that he was solely responsible for his predicament. Finding: The High Court erred in awarding compensation because the Petitionerâ€™s conduct was the sole cause of his termination. Issue 2: Application of Saleem v Hatton National Bank Rule applied: The principle that compensation may be awarded in "special circumstances" even if termination is justified. Court's reasoning: The Court clarified that the principle in Saleem v Hatton National Bank is reserved for exceptional cases where equity demands relief despite a justified dismissal. In this case, the Petitionerâ€™s persistent misconduct, disregard for management instructions, and failure to heed thirteen warnings did not constitute "special circumstances." The Court emphasized that rewarding a wrongdoer would undermine the legal system. Finding: The High Court misapplied the principle, as the Petitionerâ€™s conduct did not meet the threshold for special circumstances. Issue 3: Jurisdiction to award relief Rule applied: The principle of "just and equitable" jurisdiction under the Industrial Disputes Act. Court's reasoning: The Court reasoned that the jurisdiction to grant relief is not a license to act by "whim or caprice." An award must be balanced between the parties. Since the High Court found the dismissal to be just and equitable, it lacked a rational basis to grant monetary relief to an employee who had contributed 100% to his own dismissal. Finding: The High Court exceeded the proper exercise of its discretion by awarding compensation after affirming the dismissal as just and equitable. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected because the determination that the dismissal was "just and equitable" (Issue 1) effectively negated the existence of "special circumstances" (Issue 2). Consequently, the absence of such circumstances rendered the award of compensation an improper exercise of the Court's jurisdiction (Issue 3). Conclusion The appeal of the Petitioner is denied. The judgment of the High Court is set aside, and the order of the Labour Tribunal dated 15th October 2007 is affirmed. No costs are awarded. Keywords Industrial Disputes Act — Termination of service — Misconduct — Compensation — Just and equitable — Contributory fault — Saleem v Hatton National Bank — Burden of proof — Special circumstances

Parties: Wanigaratne v. Gunawathie
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; P.A. Ratnayake, J.; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Leave to appeal — District Court order — Interim injunction — Finality of judgments — Judicial admission — Laches — Equity — Re-adjudication — Functus officio
Summary: Facts The Appellant, Dombagahawattage Ranjith Wanigaratne, initiated testamentary proceedings in the District Court of Mount Lavinia seeking probate for a Last Will dated 14.12.1984. The District Court dismissed this application on 03.08.1996, and the judgment was subsequently affirmed by the Court of Appeal and the Supreme Court. The 1st Respondent, Wijesekara Arachchige Gunawathie, had separately filed a distinct testamentary case in the same court seeking probate for a different Last Will dated 21.02.1982, which remained pending. In 2008, the 1st Respondent filed an application within the Appellant's concluded testamentary case seeking an interim and permanent injunction against the Appellant regarding the property mentioned in the Last Will. The District Court granted an interim injunction on 16.03.2009. The Appellant challenged this order before the Civil Appellate High Court of Mount Lavinia, which affirmed the District Court's decision. The Appellant subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the District Court erred in law in granting an interim injunction against the Appellant in a matter that had already been finally concluded. Whether the Last Will in favour of the 1st Respondent devised soil rights to her.
