Applicable Law Statutes: High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) (Amendment) Act No. 54 of 2006 Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Propriety of the interim injunction Rule applied: The court applied the principle that an interim injunction requires a pending trial as an indispensable condition precedent and that a court becomes functus officio once a final and conclusive judgment has been delivered. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the original testamentary case filed by the Appellant had been finalized and ratified by both the Court of Appeal and the Supreme Court years prior to the 1st Respondent's application. Because the trial had been disposed of, there was no pending litigation to support the grant of an interim injunction. The District Court lacked the legal authority or competence to re-adjudicate a matter where its functions had already been accomplished. Finding: The District Court erred in law by granting the interim injunction, as it was functus officio and there was no pending trial to justify the relief. Issue 2: Construction of the Last Will Rule applied: Not specified. Court's reasoning: The court determined that because the appeal was successfully resolved based on the legal error regarding the interim injunction, it was unnecessary to address the secondary question of law concerning the interpretation of the Last Will. Finding: The court declined to rule on this issue as it was rendered moot by the finding on the first issue. Interaction of Issues The resolution of the first issue, which established that the District Court lacked the jurisdiction to grant the injunction due to the finality of the proceedings, rendered the second issue regarding the interpretation of the Last Will unnecessary for the final determination of the appeal. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgment of the District Court dated 16.03.2009 and the order of the Civil Appellate High Court dated 03.02.2010 are set aside. No costs are awarded. Keywords Leave to appeal — District Court order — Interim injunction — Finality of judgments — Judicial admission — Laches — Equity — Re-adjudication — Functus officio

Parties: Sri Lanka Co-operative Marketing Federation Ltd. v. The State Trading Corporation of India
Bench: Tilakawardane, J.; Amarathunga, J.; Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: writ pending appeal — prescription ordinance — civil procedure code — substantial loss — written contract — international trade — document against payment — security for execution — judgment creditor — judgment debtor — appeal process — enforcement of decree — legal interest — breach of contract — limitation period
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a commercial dispute between the Sri Lanka Co-operative Marketing Federation Ltd. (the Petitioner) and the State Trading Corporation of India (the Respondent). The Petitioner had placed an order for 100 metric tons of dried chillies from the Respondent at a price of US$ 1,300 per metric ton. The shipment was made from Tuticorin to Colombo, but the mode of payment was not specified in the initial order. The Respondent asserted that the internationally accepted method of payment, Document Against Payment (DP), would apply. However, after the shipment, the Petitioner requested a change to the payment terms, asking for Documents Against Acceptance (DA) payable after 30 days, which was later extended to 60 days. The Respondent claimed that the Petitioner failed to pay for four shipments totaling US$ 130,000, leading to the Respondent filing for a writ pending appeal after the Commercial High Court ruled in favor of the Respondent. The Petitioner contested the issuance of the writ, arguing that it would incur substantial losses if the writ was allowed. The Commercial High Court ruled in favor of the Respondent, leading the Petitioner to appeal to the Supreme Court. Issues The Supreme Court was tasked with addressing several legal questions: 1) Whether the Petitioner’s action was prescribed in law. 2) Whether the courts failed to consider Section 763 of the Civil Procedure Code regarding the security to be deposited by the Respondent. 3) Whether the sum of Rs. 250,000 as security was insufficient and disproportionate to the Respondent’s claim of US$ 130,000 and legal interest from 18th February 1997. Applicable Law Statutes: Prescription Ordinance, Civil Procedure Code Cases Referred to: Coburn v Colledge [1897] 1 QB 702; Henry Boot construction Ltd v Alstom Combined Cycles Ltd [2005] 1 WLR 3850; A.D.H Perera v Gunawardena [1993] 2 SLR; Waharaka Sobitha Unnanse v Amunugama Piyaratana Unnanse, 55 NLR; Ekanayake v Ekanayake [2003] 2 Sri.L.R; Sihor Nagar Palika Bureau v Bhabhlubhai Virabhai & Co [2005] 4 SCC 1; B.P. Agarwal & anr. vs. Dhanalakshmi Bank Ltd. & ors [2008] 3 SCC 397. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Petitioner’s action was prescribed in law. Rule applied: Section 8 of the Prescription Ordinance and Section 6 of the same ordinance. Court's reasoning: The Court examined whether the transaction constituted a written contract or merely a sale of goods. The Petitioner argued that the transaction fell under Section 8, which prescribes a one-year limit for actions related to goods sold and delivered. Conversely, the Respondent contended that the transaction was based on written conditions, thus falling under Section 6, which allows six years for actions based on written contracts. The Court analyzed the documents exchanged between the parties, concluding that they constituted written agreements, thereby affirming the Respondent's position. The Court cited relevant case law to support its conclusion that the Respondent had acted within the legal time frame to institute action. Finding: The Court found that the Respondent's action was not prescribed and was valid under the law. Issue 2: Whether the courts failed to consider Section 763 of the Civil Procedure Code regarding the security to be deposited. Rule applied: Section 763 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Court noted that the burden of proof rested on the Petitioner to demonstrate substantial losses that would result from the issuance of the writ. The Petitioner failed to provide sufficient evidence to support claims of substantial loss, merely asserting that losses would occur without detailing the nature or extent of those losses. The Court referenced previous judgments that emphasized the necessity of providing concrete evidence to establish claims of substantial loss. Finding: The Court agreed with the Commercial High Court's decision, affirming that the Petitioner did not establish sufficient cause for the courts to determine an appropriate quantum of security. Issue 3: Whether the sum of Rs. 250,000 as security was insufficient and disproportionate. Rule applied: Discretion of the court regarding security amounts. Court's reasoning: The Petitioner argued that the security amount was inadequate compared to the Respondent's claim. However, the Respondent countered that the court is not legally bound to set security in direct proportion to the claim amount. The Court examined relevant case law, affirming that the discretion to set security amounts lies with the court and should be exercised judiciously based on the circumstances of each case. Finding: The Court concluded that the Commercial High Court had not erred in its judgment regarding the security amount, which was deemed sufficient to protect the interests of both parties. Interaction of Issues The issues presented were interrelated, as the determination of whether the Petitioner’s action was prescribed directly impacted the assessment of the security required and the evaluation of substantial loss. The Court's analysis of the prescription period set the stage for understanding the validity of the Respondent's claims and the subsequent implications for security and loss assessments. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal, affirming the Commercial High Court's ruling and the order for security. The Court underscored the importance of providing substantial evidence in legal proceedings and upheld the principles governing the issuance of writs pending appeal, ensuring that the interests of both parties were adequately safeguarded. The judgment reinforced the necessity for parties to substantiate their claims with detailed evidence to support their positions in court. Keywords: writ pending appeal — prescription ordinance — civil procedure code — substantial loss — written contract — international trade — document against payment — security for execution — judgment creditor — judgment debtor — appeal process — enforcement of decree — legal interest — breach of contract — limitation period.
Counsel: S.A. Parathalingam, PC with Athula Perera for Defendant-Petitioner. Kushan D’ Alwis with Ayendra Wickramasekara and Jayaruwan Wijayalath for Plaintiff-Respondent instructed by K.U. Gunasekara.

Parties: H.M. Oman Ekanayake and Others v. W. Ratranhamy
Bench: Dr. Shirani A. Bandaranayake, CJ.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Land Reform Law — Statutory Determination — Gazette Notification — Civil Procedure Code — New Evidence — Declaration of Title — Vindicatory Action — Burden of Proof — Appellate Jurisdiction — Fresh Evidence — Misdirection — Ownership — Ejectment — Prescriptive Title — Legal Principles
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a dispute over land ownership between the appellants, H.M. Oman Ekanayake and others, and the respondent, W. Ratranhamy. The appellants initiated legal action in the District Court of Embilipitiya, seeking a declaration of title to a specific piece of land and an order to eject the respondent and any parties holding under him from that land. The appellants claimed that they inherited the property from their father, relying on a Statutory Determination issued under the Land Reform Law No. 1 of 1972. The respondent contested this claim, asserting that the land belonged to B.T.A.B. Maddegama, who subsequently intervened in the case, claiming prescriptive title to the land. The District Court ruled in favor of the appellants on January 24, 2001. However, the respondent appealed this decision, and the case was transferred to the Civil Appellate High Court of the Sabaragamuwa Province following the establishment of provincial appellate courts in 2007. During the appeal process, a significant development occurred: the Statutory Determination relied upon by the appellants was canceled by Gazette Extraordinary No. 1181/19 on April 25, 2001, which was four months after the District Court's judgment. The High Court ultimately set aside the District Court's ruling on September 23, 2009, citing the Gazette Notification as the basis for forfeiting the appellants' title. The appellants sought leave to appeal to the Supreme Court, which was granted on three specific questions regarding the High Court's handling of new evidence and the validity of the Gazette Notification. Issues The Supreme Court was tasked with addressing the following issues: 1. Did the Civil Appellate High Court misdirect itself on the concept of accepting new evidence in an appeal? 2. Did the Civil Appellate High Court misdirect itself by considering the Gazette Extraordinary No. 1181/19 dated April 25, 2001, as valid? 3. Did the Civil Appellate High Court err in entering the judgment purely based on the Gazette Extraordinary No. 1181/19 dated April 25, 2001? Applicable Law Statutes: Land Reform Law No. 1 of 1972; Civil Procedure Code Section 773. Cases Referred to: Beatrice Dep v. Lalani Meemaduwa - [1997] 3 Sri L.R. 379; Ratwatte v. Bandara et al - (1966) 70 N.L.R. 231; Rev. Kiralagama Sumanaratna Thero v. Aluvihare - [1985] 1 Sri L.R. 19; Silva v. Jayawardena - (1942) 43 N.L.R. 551; De Silva v. Goonatillake - (1931) 32 N.L.R. 217; Muthusamy v. Seneviratne - (1946) 31 C.L.W. 91; Mudalihamy v. Appuhamy - (1891) 1 C.L.R. 67; Eliashamy v. Punchi Banda - (1911) 14 N.L.R. 113; Fernanodo v. Appuhamy - (1921) 23 N.L.R. 476. Analysis Issue 1: Did the High Court misdirect itself on the concept of accepting new evidence in an appeal? Rule applied: Section 773 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined whether the High Court erred in considering the Gazette Notification as new evidence. The appellants argued that the High Court's reliance on the Gazette Notification was misplaced, as it was issued after the District Court's judgment. However, the Court noted that Section 773 allows appellate courts to admit new evidence if it meets specific criteria. The Court referenced the case of Ratwatte v. Bandara, which established conditions under which new evidence could be admitted. The Court concluded that the High Court correctly accepted the Gazette Notification as it was not available during the District Court proceedings, and it significantly impacted the case's outcome. Finding: The High Court did not misdirect itself regarding the acceptance of new evidence. Issue 2: Did the High Court misdirect itself by considering the Gazette Extraordinary No. 1181/19 dated April 25, 2001, as valid? Rule applied: Validity of the Gazette Notification. Court's reasoning: The appellants contended that the Gazette Notification should not have been considered since it was issued after the District Court's judgment. However, the Supreme Court found that the High Court acted correctly in considering the Gazette Notification as it was unchallenged and had a direct bearing on the appellants' title. The Court emphasized that the appellants had not taken steps to contest the Gazette Notification's validity, which stood unchallenged at the time of the High Court's decision. Finding: The High Court did not misdirect itself by considering the Gazette Notification as valid. Issue 3: Did the High Court err in entering the judgment purely based on the Gazette Extraordinary No. 1181/19 dated April 25, 2001, as valid? Rule applied: Requirement of title in a vindicatory action. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court analyzed whether the High Court erred in basing its judgment solely on the Gazette Notification. The Court reiterated that a plaintiff must maintain title throughout the action, as established in previous cases. The cancellation of the Statutory Determination by the Gazette Notification meant that the appellants no longer had the title necessary to sustain their claim. The Court cited Voet's principles regarding the necessity of retaining title during litigation, concluding that the High Court's judgment was justified based on the Gazette Notification. Finding: The High Court did not err in entering judgment based on the Gazette Notification. Interaction of Issues The issues were interrelated, as the acceptance of new evidence directly influenced the validity of the Gazette Notification and the High Court's judgment. The Supreme Court's analysis demonstrated that the High Court's decisions were consistent with legal principles governing evidence and title in property disputes. Conclusion The Supreme Court affirmed the High Court's judgment, dismissing the appeal and upholding the decision that the appellants had lost their title to the land due to the Gazette Notification. The Court made no order as to costs, emphasizing the legal principles surrounding property rights and the importance of maintaining title throughout litigation. The judgment serves as a significant precedent in property law, particularly regarding the implications of statutory cancellations and the acceptance of new evidence in appellate proceedings.
Counsel: Chathura Galhena for Plaintiff-Respondent-Appellants. Thisath Wijayagunawardane with Prabhash Somasinghe for the Defendant-Appellant-Respondent.

Parties: Merchant Bank of Sri Lanka Limited v. Perera and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: Mortgage — Leasehold rights — Ejection of tenants — Wrongful dispossession — Civil Procedure Code — High Court of the Provinces Act — Right of appeal — Revision application
Summary: Facts The Merchant Bank of Sri Lanka Limited, as the Plaintiff, instituted a mortgage action against the 1st Defendant regarding leasehold rights to the Victoria Building. The 1st, 2nd, 3rd, and 4th Respondents were tenants in the building. Following the 1st Defendant's default on the mortgage, the Plaintiff obtained a decree and purchased the premises at a sale. The Respondents, who had previously been involved in litigation regarding their tenancy, sought to intervene in the mortgage action but were refused. The Plaintiff subsequently moved the Commercial High Court for an order to eject the occupants and obtain vacant possession. The High Court allowed this application, and the Respondents were ejected on 14.10.2005. The Respondents then filed applications for restoration of possession and damages under Section 328 of the Civil Procedure Code. The High Court allowed these applications on 15th June 2006. The Plaintiff filed a Revision Application in the Court of Appeal to challenge this order. The Respondents raised a preliminary objection, arguing that the Revision Application was not maintainable because the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996 provided a right of appeal to the Supreme Court. The Court of Appeal dismissed the Revision Application, holding that the right of appeal to the Supreme Court under Act No. 10 of 1996 was exclusive and could not be bypassed by invoking revisionary jurisdiction. The Plaintiff appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the provisions of Section 5 of the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996 are subject to the limitations of Section 329 of the Civil Procedure Code regarding orders made under Section 328 of the Civil Procedure Code. Whether the Court of Appeal correctly held that it lacked revisionary jurisdiction over orders made by the High Court in the exercise of its civil jurisdiction. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 326, 328, 329, 754 High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996, Section 2, 5, 6 Cases Referred to: Australanka Exporters Pvt Ltd v. Indian Bank - [2001/v2-sri-lr-156] Dean of Ely v. Bliss - [11-lj-ch-351] Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of Section 329 of the Civil Procedure Code Rule applied: Section 5 of the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996 and Section 329 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Court reasoned that Act No. 10 of 1996 is a special statute that confers civil jurisdiction on the High Court and establishes a specific appellate framework. Section 5 of the Act provides a right of appeal to the Supreme Court against any order made by the High Court. The Court applied the principle that when two statutes are inconsistent, the later enactment prevails. Furthermore, the Court noted that Section 329 of the Civil Procedure Code, which limits appeals in certain circumstances, was not incorporated into Act No. 10 of 1996. Therefore, the limitation in Section 329 does not restrict the right of appeal granted by Section 5 of Act No. 10. Finding: Section 329 of the Civil Procedure Code does not apply to the right of appeal granted by Section 5 of Act No. 10 of 1996. Issue 2: Revisionary Jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal Rule applied: Section 5 of the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the legislature intended for the Supreme Court to be the exclusive appellate body for matters arising from the High Court's civil jurisdiction. Allowing revisionary jurisdiction in the Court of Appeal would create an illogical two-tier review process for specific matters, whereas other matters would only be subject to a single appeal to the Supreme Court. The Court affirmed the reasoning in Australanka Exporters Pvt Ltd v. Indian Bank, confirming that appellate jurisdiction over High Court civil matters is vested exclusively in the Supreme Court. Finding: The Court of Appeal correctly determined that it lacked revisionary jurisdiction, as the exclusive right of appeal lies with the Supreme Court. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing: because Section 5 of Act No. 10 of 1996 provides a comprehensive and exclusive right of appeal to the Supreme Court, it effectively displaces the restrictive procedural bars of the Civil Procedure Code (Section 329). This renders the Court of Appeal's revisionary jurisdiction inapplicable, as the statutory scheme for High Court civil matters is self-contained and centralized in the Supreme Court. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The Court of Appeal's order is affirmed, and the Appellant is ordered to pay costs fixed at Rs. 31,500. Keywords Mortgage — Leasehold rights — Ejection of tenants — Wrongful dispossession — Civil Procedure Code — High Court of the Provinces Act — Right of appeal — Revision application

Parties: Ceylon Estate Staffs Union v. Superintendent and Pussellawa Plantations Ltd and Free Lanka Management Co. (PVT) Ltd and Others
Bench: R.A.N.G. Amaratunge, J.; Saleem Marsoof, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: Termination of services — Wrongful termination — Industrial Disputes Act — Standard of proof — Balance of probabilities — Magistrates Court decision — Labour Tribunal — Reinstatement — Compensation in lieu of reinstatement — Disciplinary inquiry — Charge sheet — Collective Agreement — Threat to superintendent — Evidence evaluation
Summary: Facts The Respondent, Tissa Nanayakkara, was employed as a Field Officer at the Sunderland Estate. On September 13, 1993, his services were terminated by the Petitioners, Pussellawa Plantations Ltd and others, following an allegation that the Respondent had threatened to assassinate the estate Superintendent and his family on September 11, 1993. No charge sheet was issued to the Respondent, and no disciplinary inquiry was conducted prior to the termination. The Respondent filed an application with the Labour Tribunal of Avissawella challenging the termination. The proceedings were laid by pending the outcome of a criminal case in the Magistrate's Court regarding the same incident. The Respondent was acquitted by the Magistrate's Court due to unsatisfactory and contradictory evidence. Following the acquittal, the Labour Tribunal inquiry commenced in 2002. The Petitioners relied on the testimony of estate staff, while the Respondent denied the allegations. The Labour Tribunal dismissed the Respondent's application. Upon appeal, the Provincial High Court set aside the Labour Tribunal's order, ruling that the Magistrate's Court judgment was binding on the Tribunal, and ordered the reinstatement of the Respondent with back wages. The Petitioners subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court erred in its interpretation of the standard of proof applicable to an application under Section 31B(1) of the Industrial Disputes Act. Whether the High Court erred in holding that the judgment of the Magistrate's Court was binding on the Labour Tribunal. Whether the termination of the Respondent's services was justifiable given the evidence presented and the lack of a disciplinary inquiry. Whether reinstatement was the appropriate remedy or if compensation in lieu of reinstatement was more suitable in the circumstances. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act No. 43 of 1950 (as amended) Cases Referred to: Superintendant, We-Oya Group v Ceylon Estate Staffs Union - [74-nlr-189] Associated Battery Manufacturers v United Engineering Workers Union - [77-nlr-541] Jayasuriya v Sri Lanka State Plantations Corporation - [1995/v2-sri-lr-379] Analysis Issue 1: Standard of Proof Rule applied: Section 31B(1) of the Industrial Disputes Act. Court's reasoning: The Court noted that the standard of proof in a criminal trial is "beyond reasonable doubt," whereas the standard in a Labour Tribunal is "proof on a balance of probabilities." The High Court failed to distinguish between these standards when evaluating the evidence. Finding: The High Court misapplied the legal principles regarding the standard of proof. Issue 2: Binding Nature of Magistrate's Court Judgment Rule applied: Section 31B(5) of the Industrial Disputes Act. Court's reasoning: While the Tribunal is required to lay by a case if proceedings are pending in another forum and must consider the decision of such proceedings, it is not legally bound by the outcome of a criminal trial. The High Court erred in holding that the Magistrate's Court judgment was binding on the Tribunal. Finding: The High Court erred in law by treating the criminal acquittal as binding. Issue 3: Justification of Termination Rule applied: Principles of natural justice and fair labor practices. Court's reasoning: The Petitioners failed to issue a charge sheet or conduct a disciplinary inquiry, violating established practices. Furthermore, the evidence presented by the Petitioners before the Tribunal was contradictory and unconvincing, particularly as the main complainant did not testify. The infirmities in the employer's case could not be cured by the Respondent's own evidence. Finding: The termination was unjustifiable as the charges were not established on a balance of probabilities. Issue 4: Appropriate Remedy Rule applied: Principles of just and equitable relief under the Industrial Disputes Act. Court's reasoning: Given the 18-year delay, the Respondent's subsequent employment as a Superintendent, and allegations of his misconduct toward witnesses, reinstatement would be subversive of discipline and not in the best interests of justice. Compensation is a more appropriate alternative. Finding: Reinstatement is denied; compensation is awarded. Interaction of Issues The findings on the standard of proof and the non-binding nature of the criminal acquittal allowed the Court to independently assess the evidence, which revealed that the employer failed to meet the burden of proof. This failure rendered the termination wrongful, necessitating a remedy. However, the assessment of the Respondent's conduct and the passage of time influenced the Court to reject reinstatement in favor of compensation. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed, and the judgment of the High Court is varied. The order for reinstatement is replaced by an order for the Petitioners to pay the Respondent compensation in the sum of Rs. 615,240. Costs are fixed at Rs. 31,500. Keywords Termination of services — Wrongful termination — Industrial Disputes Act — Standard of proof — Balance of probabilities — Magistrates Court decision — Labour Tribunal — Reinstatement — Compensation in lieu of reinstatement — Disciplinary inquiry — Charge sheet — Collective Agreement — Threat to superintendent — Evidence evaluation

Parties: Amma v. Mendis
Bench: Hon. Dr. Bandaranayake, J; R.A.N.G. Amaratunge, J.; Hon. P.A. Ratnayake, P.C.J
Keywords: Partition action — Corpus of the case — Identification of boundaries — Preliminary plan — Surveyor's report — Lis pendens — Section 18(3) of the Partition Act — Richard vs. Sieble Nona — Amalgamated land — Extent of land
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Appellant filed an action in the District Court of Anuradhapura to partition a land consisting of two allotments described as 'Helamahagaha kumbura' in schedules A and B of the amended plaint. The District Court entered judgment to partition the land as prayed for in the amended plaint. The Defendant-Appellant-Respondent appealed to the High Court of Civil Appeal of the North Central Province, which set aside the District Court judgment and ordered a retrial. The Plaintiff-Respondent-Appellant then appealed to the Supreme Court. The dispute primarily concerns the identification of the corpus. While the Defendant-Appellant-Respondent admitted the corpus as described in the schedules, she contended that the preliminary plan prepared by the licensed surveyor included portions of another land she possessed. The surveyor's plan amalgamated the two allotments into a single corpus with an extent of 2 Acres 1 Rood and 13.62 perches, which differed significantly from the extent described in the original plaint. Furthermore, there were inconsistencies between the boundaries described in the schedules and those depicted in the preliminary plan. The Plaintiff-Respondent-Appellant failed to register a fresh lis pendens for the larger extent of land identified in the preliminary plan. Issues Whether the High Court erred in law by failing to consider the explanations provided by the commissioner regarding his report 'X1'. Whether the High Court erred in determining that the Petitioner failed to identify the corpus sought to be partitioned. Whether the High Court misapplied the ratio in the decision of the Court of Appeal in Richard v. Sieble Nona [2001/v2-sri-lr-1]. Whether the High Court erred in holding that the District Court should have issued a commission to the Surveyor General under Section 18(3) of the Partition Law. Applicable Law Statutes: Partition Act No. 16 of 1951 Cases Referred to: Brampy Appuhamy v. Monis Appuhamy - [60-nlr-337] H. Sopi Nona v. P.A. Cornelis - [2010-balj-xvi-109] Richard v. Sieble Nona - [2001/v2-sri-lr-1] Analysis Issue 1: Commissioner's report and testimony Rule applied: The duty of the court to ensure the accuracy of the corpus in a partition action under the Partition Act. Court's reasoning: The surveyor's explanation regarding the discrepancy in land extent was found to be insufficient to reconcile the substantial variation between the original plaint and the survey. The court noted that the surveyor failed to explain how the two allotments could be contiguous given the conflicting boundary descriptions in the schedules. Finding: The High Court did not err; the surveyor's testimony failed to clarify the inconsistencies, and the District Court overlooked these serious lapses. Issue 2: Identification of the corpus Rule applied: The requirement for precise identification of the corpus in partition actions. Court's reasoning: The court identified conflicting evidence regarding the Northern boundary, specifically the existence of a 'Yoda Ela' reservation. While the Petitioner's witnesses testified to the reservation, the surveyor's report contradicted this. Additionally, the failure to register a lis pendens for the larger amalgamated extent created a legal infirmity. Finding: The Petitioner failed to properly identify the corpus, and the District Court's reliance on the flawed preliminary plan was unjustified. Issue 3: Application of Richard v. Sieble Nona Rule applied: The requirement to file a fresh lis pendens when a survey discloses a larger land than originally pleaded. Court's reasoning: The court affirmed that when a survey reveals a significantly larger extent, the failure to register a fresh lis pendens violates the imperative provisions of the Partition Act. The High Court correctly applied the principle that proceeding without such registration constitutes a travesty of justice. Finding: The High Court did not misapply the ratio; the Petitioner's failure to file a fresh lis pendens was a major infirmity. Issue 4: Issuance of commission to Surveyor General Rule applied: Section 18(3) of the Partition Act. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the High Court correctly identified that the District Court had the opportunity to utilize the Surveyor General's records to resolve boundary disputes, particularly regarding state reservations like the 'Yoda Ela'. Finding: The High Court was correct in its observation that the District Court should have exercised its powers to ensure proper identification. Interaction of Issues The findings on the failure to properly identify the corpus and the failure to register a fresh lis pendens are mutually reinforcing. The lack of a fresh lis pendens for the larger extent, combined with the surveyor's inability to reconcile boundary discrepancies, rendered the District Court's judgment legally unsustainable under the strict requirements of the Partition Act. Conclusion The Supreme Court answered the questions of law in the negative and dismissed the appeal. The judgment of the High Court of Civil Appeal ordering a retrial was upheld. Keywords Partition action — Corpus of the case — Identification of boundaries — Preliminary plan — Surveyor's report — Lis pendens — Section 18(3) of the Partition Act — Richard vs. Sieble Nona — Amalgamated land — Extent of land

Parties: Premasundara v. Seemasahitha Galoya Medapalatha Vivida Seva Samupakara Samithiya
Bench: R.A.N.G. Amaratunge, J.; Sripavan, J; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: Appeal from Labour Tribunal order — High Court jurisdiction — Industrial Disputes Act — High Court of Provinces Act — Maintainability of application — Circuit Tribunal — Labour Tribunal establishment
Summary: Facts The Appellant, E.P.A. Premasundara, was employed by the Respondent, Seemasahitha Galoya Medapalatha Vivida Seva Samupakara Samithiya Uhana. Following the termination of his services, the Appellant initiated proceedings before the Labour Tribunal of Ampara, which was then a circuit tribunal of the Badulla Labour Tribunal. The Respondent raised a preliminary objection regarding the maintainability of the application, which the Labour Tribunal upheld. The Appellant successfully challenged this dismissal in the High Court of Ampara via a revision application, and the matter was remanded for inquiry. The inquiry was conducted at the Ampara Labour Tribunal. During the pendency of the proceedings, a permanent Labour Tribunal was established in Ampara. The President of the Badulla Labour Tribunal, who had heard the evidence, authored the final order under the direction of the Judicial Service Commission, which was subsequently pronounced by the President of the Ampara Labour Tribunal. The Appellant appealed the resulting dismissal to the High Court of Ampara. The High Court dismissed the appeal, ruling that it lacked jurisdiction to hear the matter. The Appellant then sought leave to appeal to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court of the Eastern Province sitting at Ampara erred in law by concluding it lacked jurisdiction to hear the appeal. Whether the High Court misdirected itself by relying on the precedent of Coconut Research Board v. Fernando despite the factual distinctions in the present case. Whether the High Court failed to consider that it had previously exercised revisionary jurisdiction over the same case. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act No. 43 of 1950, Section 31C(3) High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 19 of 1990, Section 4 Cases Referred to: Coconut Research Board v. Fernando - [1993/v1-sri-lr-219] Analysis Issue 1: Jurisdiction of the High Court Rule applied: Section 31C(3) of the Industrial Disputes Act No. 43 and Section 4 of the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 19 of 1990. Court's reasoning: The statutes mandate that appeals from a Labour Tribunal must be filed in the High Court of the province where the tribunal is situated. Since the proceedings were initiated, heard, and decided at the Labour Tribunal of Ampara, the High Court of the Eastern Province (Ampara) possessed the requisite jurisdiction to hear the appeal. Finding: The High Court of Ampara erred in holding that it lacked jurisdiction. Issue 2: Applicability of Precedent Rule applied: The principle of stare decisis and the requirement to distinguish factual circumstances. Court's reasoning: The court distinguished the present case from Coconut Research Board v. Fernando. In the latter, the tribunal's location and the resulting appellate jurisdiction were ambiguous due to the lack of clear territorial designation. In the present case, the proceedings were consistently held in Ampara, and a permanent tribunal was established there before the final order was delivered. Finding: The High Court misdirected itself by failing to distinguish the facts of the present case from the cited precedent. Issue 3: Prior Exercise of Jurisdiction Rule applied: The principle of consistency in judicial proceedings. Court's reasoning: The High Court had previously exercised revisionary jurisdiction over the same case regarding a preliminary objection. It was contradictory for the same court to assert it lacked jurisdiction to hear an appeal on the final order when it had already adjudicated on the matter at an earlier stage. Finding: The High Court failed to consider its own prior exercise of jurisdiction in the same matter. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. The courtâ€™s determination that the High Court had jurisdiction (Issue 1) is supported by the fact that the High Court had already exercised jurisdiction in the same case (Issue 3) and that the reliance on Coconut Research Board v. Fernando was legally flawed due to factual differences (Issue 2). Together, these findings establish that the High Court's refusal to hear the appeal was a legal error. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the judgment of the High Court of Ampara is set aside. The High Court of Ampara is directed to hear and determine the Appellant's appeal expeditiously. Costs are fixed at Rs. 31,500/-. Keywords Appeal from Labour Tribunal order — High Court jurisdiction — Industrial Disputes Act — High Court of Provinces Act — Maintainability of application — Circuit Tribunal — Labour Tribunal establishment

Parties: Das v. Jeevananthan David and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: Appeal from Court of Appeal order — Preliminary objection — Court of Appeal (Appellate Procedure) Rules 1990 — Leave to appeal applications — Civil Procedure Code — Acknowledgment of prior invocation of jurisdiction — Filing of documents — Mutatis mutandis — Technical objections — Dispensation of justice
Summary: Facts Surendra Das, the Plaintiff-Respondent-Petitioner-Appellant, initiated an action in the District Court of Horana. The District Judge issued an interim injunction on 30 November 2006, restraining the Defendants, G. Jeevananthan David and P.E.A. Jayawickrama, who are the liquidators of Perfect Fit International (Private) Limited, from dealing with the property described in the plaint. The Defendants filed an application for leave to appeal to the Court of Appeal to set aside the District Court's order. During the proceedings in the Court of Appeal, the Appellant raised a preliminary objection, arguing that the Defendants failed to aver in their petition that they had not previously invoked the jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal. Furthermore, the Appellant contended that the Defendants failed to produce material documents or reserve the right to tender them later, which allegedly violated the Court of Appeal (Appellate Procedure) Rules, 1990. The Court of Appeal overruled these objections, holding that the Rules do not apply to leave to appeal applications, which are governed by the Civil Procedure Code. The Appellant subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the provisions of the Court of Appeal (Appellate Procedure) Rules, 1990 apply to leave to appeal applications filed in the Court of Appeal. Whether the failure to include an averment regarding the prior invocation of the Court's jurisdiction or the failure to file material documents warrants the mandatory dismissal of a leave to appeal application. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 138, 140, 141 Civil Procedure Code, Sections 757, 758, 776 Court of Appeal (Appellate Procedure) Rules, 1990, Rules 3(1)(a), 3(1)(b), 3(2), 3(15) Cases Referred to: Caderamanpulle v. Caderamanpulle - [2005/v1-sri-lr-397] [sc-appeal-50/2008] Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of the Rules Rule applied: Rule 3(15) of the Court of Appeal (Appellate Procedure) Rules, 1990, and the maxim Inclusio unius exclusio alterius. Court's reasoning: The Court reasoned that the Court of Appeal (Appellate Procedure) Rules, 1990, specifically govern applications under Articles 138, 140, and 141 of the Constitution. Leave to appeal applications are governed by the specific provisions of Sections 757 and 758 of the Civil Procedure Code. Applying the maxim Inclusio unius exclusio alterius, the Court concluded that the omission of leave to appeal applications from the scope of Rule 3(1) was intentional. Rule 3(15) allows for the application of rules mutatis mutandis to other matters, but this is subject to the court's discretion and does not override the specific procedures established by the Civil Procedure Code. Finding: The Court of Appeal (Appellate Procedure) Rules, 1990, do not apply to leave to appeal applications. Issue 2: Consequences of Procedural Omissions Rule applied: Sections 757 and 758 of the Civil Procedure Code and Rule 3(2) of the Court of Appeal (Appellate Procedure) Rules, 1990. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the Civil Procedure Code does not mandate the filing of documents or specific averments regarding prior jurisdiction for leave to appeal applications. Even if the Court of Appeal Rules were applicable, Rule 3(2) grants the court discretion regarding the dismissal of an application for failure to disclose prior invocation of jurisdiction. Mandatory dismissal for technical omissions would be a grave injustice. The Court emphasized that litigation should be decided on merits rather than technicalities. Finding: The failure to include such averments or file documents does not warrant mandatory dismissal. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing; because the Court determined that the Civil Procedure Code—not the Court of Appeal Rules—governs leave to appeal applications, the procedural requirements of the latter (such as mandatory averments and document filing) are inapplicable. This confirms that the Respondents' application was procedurally sound under the correct legal framework. Conclusion The question of law is answered in favor of the Respondents. The order of the Court of Appeal is affirmed, and the appeal is dismissed with costs fixed at Rs. 31,500. Keywords Appeal from Court of Appeal order — Preliminary objection — Court of Appeal (Appellate Procedure) Rules 1990 — Leave to appeal applications — Civil Procedure Code — Acknowledgment of prior invocation of jurisdiction — Filing of documents — Mutatis mutandis — Technical objections — Dispensation of justice

Parties: Sisira Susantha and Others v. Indian Overseas Bank Plc and Wijewickrama
Bench: R.A.N.G. Amaratunge, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: Amendment of plaint — Addition of party — Civil Procedure Code — Section 21 — Section 93(2) — Trial dates — Delay in proceedings — Irremediable injustice — Change of circumstances
Summary: Facts The Appellant, acting as the Administrator of the estate of the late J.P.I. Piyadasa, initiated an action in the District Court of Colombo seeking a declaration of ownership regarding premises located at No. 432, Sri Sangaraja Mawatha, Colombo 10. The Appellant further sought to have a mortgage bond declared void and requested an enjoining order to prevent the sale of the property by the 1st Respondent. Although an enjoining order was initially granted, it was later refused extension by the Commercial High Court. Consequently, the 1st Respondent sold the property by public auction and subsequently transferred it to the 2nd Respondent. Following the transfer, the Appellant moved to add the 2nd Respondent as a party to the action. The Commercial High Court allowed the addition of the 2nd Respondent and directed the Appellant to amend the caption of the plaint. The Appellant subsequently tendered a draft amended plaint to reflect the change in ownership and the addition of the new party. Despite the 1st Respondent offering no objection to the amendment, the Commercial High Court judge refused to accept the amended plaint, citing the requirements of Section 93(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. The Appellant appealed this refusal to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Commercial High Court erred in applying Section 93(2) of the Civil Procedure Code to an application for an amendment of the plaint necessitated by the addition of a new party under Section 21 of the Civil Procedure Code. Whether the Commercial High Court erred in refusing to accept the amended plaint after having already permitted the amendment of the caption to include the 2nd Respondent. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 21 Civil Procedure Code, Section 93(2) Civil Procedure Code, Section 402 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of Section 93(2) versus Section 21 Rule applied: Section 21 of the Civil Procedure Code governs the amendment of a plaint when a new defendant is added, while Section 93(2) governs general amendments to pleadings after the first date of trial. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that the necessity to add the 2nd Respondent arose from a change in circumstances during the pendency of the litigation, specifically the sale of the property to a third party. Because the amendment was a direct consequence of adding a necessary party, Section 21 of the Civil Procedure Code is the governing provision. The court held that the trial judge incorrectly applied the stringent requirements of Section 93(2)—which requires proof of no laches and the prevention of irremediable injustice—to a situation that was fundamentally governed by the procedural necessity of adding a party under Section 21. Finding: The Commercial High Court erred in law by requiring the Appellant to satisfy the criteria of Section 93(2) instead of applying Section 21. Issue 2: Refusal to accept the amended plaint Rule applied: Section 21 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the scope of the Appellant's original claim remained unchanged, save for the necessary inclusion of the new party and the consequential facts regarding the property's sale. Since the court had already permitted the amendment of the caption to include the 2nd Respondent, it was logically inconsistent and procedurally incorrect to reject the corresponding amended plaint. Furthermore, the 1st Respondent had not objected to the amendment, and the trial had not yet commenced. Finding: The Commercial High Court erred in refusing to accept the amended plaint. Interaction of Issues The findings are intrinsically linked; the courtâ€™s determination that Section 21, rather than Section 93(2), governs the amendment process directly informs the conclusion that the refusal to accept the amended plaint was an error. By clarifying that the procedural requirements for adding a party take precedence over the general restrictions on amending pleadings, the court established that the trial judgeâ€™s refusal was based on a misapplication of the law, thereby necessitating the reversal of the High Court's order. Conclusion The order of the Commercial High Court is set aside. The Commercial High Court is directed to accept the amended plaint and proceed to trial expeditiously. No order for costs is made. Keywords Amendment of plaint — Addition of party — Civil Procedure Code — Section 21 — Section 93(2) — Trial dates — Delay in proceedings — Irremediable injustice — Change of circumstances

Parties: Namunukula Plantations Limited v. Minister of Lands and Others
Bench: N.G. Amaratunga, J; Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Land Acquisition Act — Southern Development Authority Act — Public purpose — Mandatory condition precedent — Ultra vires — Acquisition of land — Minister's approval — Gazette notification — Disclosure of facts — Suppression of material facts
Summary: Facts The Appellant, Namunukula Plantations Limited, sought a writ of certiorari from the Court of Appeal to quash an order made by the 1st Respondent, the Minister of Agriculture and Lands, under Section 38 proviso (a) of the Land Acquisition Act. This order, published in the Gazette on 24th February 1999, initiated the acquisition of land situated within a designated area of the Southern Development Authority. The Appellant contended that the acquisition was ultra vires because it failed to comply with Section 17(1) of the Southern Development Authority of Sri Lanka Act No. 18 of 1996, which requires the Minister to approve acquisitions for the Authority's purposes via a Gazette order. During the proceedings, it was revealed that the Appellant had failed to disclose a letter dated 30th November 2000, which it had addressed to the Divisional Secretary. This letter acknowledged the existence of a declaration under Section 5 of the Land Acquisition Act published in 1999 and confirmed the Appellant's awareness of the acquisition proceedings as early as November 2000. This contradicted the Appellant's claim in its petition to the Court of Appeal that it was unaware of the acquisition until 2005. The Court of Appeal dismissed the application, and the Appellant appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the acquisition of land within a designated area for the purposes of the Southern Development Authority is invalid without an order from the Minister under Section 17(1) of the Southern Development Authority of Sri Lanka Act No. 18 of 1996. Whether an order under Section 38 proviso (a) of the Land Acquisition Act can be made in the absence of urgency. Whether the Appellantâ€™s suppression and misrepresentation of material facts disentitle it to the discretionary relief of a writ of certiorari. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 128(2) Land Acquisition Act (Cap. 460), Sections 2, 5, 38 proviso (a) Southern Development Authority of Sri Lanka Act No. 18 of 1996, Sections 3, 13, 17 Cases Referred to: Manel Fernando v. Jayaratne - [2000/v1-sri-lr-112] Fernandopulle v. E.L. Senanayake - [79-nlr-115] Gunasekera v. The Minister of Lands and Agriculture - [65-nlr-119] W.S. Alphonso Appuhamy v. L. Hettiarachchi - [73-nlr-131] King v. The General Commissioners for the Purpose of the Income Tax Acts for the District of Kensington-Ex-parte Princess Edmond de Poignac - [1917] 1 K.B. 486 Dalip Singh v. State of Uttar Pradesh - [2010/v2-scc-114] Analysis Issue 1: Validity of acquisition under Section 17(1) Rule applied: Section 17(1) of the Southern Development Authority of Sri Lanka Act No. 18 of 1996. Court's reasoning: The court distinguished between land acquired for a development project coordinated by the Authority and land acquired for a general public purpose. The court noted that Section 5(3) of the Land Acquisition Act makes a declaration under Section 5(1) conclusive evidence that the land is required for a public purpose. The Appellantâ€™s own letter of 30th November 2000 admitted the existence of such a declaration, thereby establishing that the land was acquired for a public purpose rather than a specific Authority project. Finding: The Appellant failed to prove the acquisition was for the Authority's purposes, rendering the Section 17(1) requirement inapplicable. Issue 2: Urgency under Section 38 proviso (a) Rule applied: Section 38 proviso (a) of the Land Acquisition Act. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the Appellant failed to provide evidence to substantiate its claim that there was no urgency. The burden of proof rested on the Appellant to challenge the Minister's exercise of power. Finding: The Appellant failed to discharge the burden of proving the absence of urgency. Issue 3: Suppression of material facts Rule applied: The principle of uberrima fides (utmost good faith) in applications for prerogative writs. Court's reasoning: The Appellant suppressed the letter dated 30th November 2000, which proved it had knowledge of the acquisition years earlier than claimed. This constituted a failure to make a full and fair disclosure of material facts. Citing King v. The General Commissioners for the Purpose of the Income Tax Acts for the District of Kensington-Ex-parte Princess Edmond de Poignac, the court held that a party seeking discretionary relief must come with clean hands. Finding: The Appellantâ€™s deceptive conduct and suppression of facts disentitle it to any relief. Interaction of Issues The findings on the merits (Issues 1 and 2) were reinforced by the finding on the Appellant's conduct (Issue 3). Because the Appellant suppressed evidence that would have clarified the purpose of the acquisition and the timeline of its knowledge, the court determined that the application was fundamentally tainted, making it unnecessary to further adjudicate the legal questions on the merits. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The Appellant is ordered to pay costs fixed at Rs. 35,000/- each to the 1st and 4th Respondents. Keywords Land Acquisition Act — Southern Development Authority Act — Public purpose — Mandatory condition precedent — Ultra vires — Acquisition of land — Minister's approval — Gazette notification — Disclosure of facts — Suppression of material facts

Parties: Seneviratne and Others v. Weeratunga and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; S.I. Imam, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J
Keywords: Land Reform Law — Statutory Determination — Validity of Deeds of Gift — Effect of Gazette Notification — Exceptio rei venditae et traditae — Conveyance of land — Statutory lessee
Summary: Facts Arthur Weeratunga was the original owner of agricultural lands that vested in the Land Reform Commission under the Land Reform Law No. 1 of 1972. Following the vesting, Arthur Weeratunga became a statutory lessee of the lands. While holding this status, he executed two Deeds of Gift, Nos. 1476 and 10332, in favor of his son, Tissa Indika Weeratunga, and his grandson, Rohan Tissa Anthony Weeratunga. The plaintiffs-respondents relied on these deeds to claim title to the land. The defendants-appellants, who were family members of the donor, challenged the validity of these deeds, arguing that the donor lacked title at the time of execution because the land was vested in the Land Reform Commission. The District Court ruled in favor of the plaintiffs-respondents, and the Civil Appellate High Court affirmed this decision. The defendants-appellants subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Deeds of Gift Nos. 1476 and 10332 were validly executed by Arthur Weeratunga while the subject lands were vested in the Land Reform Commission. Whether the doctrine of exceptio rei venditae et traditae applies to validate the Deeds of Gift in this case. Applicable Law Statutes: Land Reform Law No. 1 of 1972 Cases Referred to: Jinawathie v. Emalin Perera - [1986/v2-sri-lr-121] Kanapathipillai v. Vethanayagam - [66-nlr-49] Piyadasa v. Piyasena - [69-nlr-332] Esufboy v. Jeevojee - [32-nlr-356] Perera v. Perera - [57-nlr-440] Perera v. Kiri Honda - [58-nlr-545] Don Mathes v. Punchi Hamy - [Wendts Reports 122] Tissera v. William - [45-nlr-358] Analysis Issue 1: Validity of Deeds of Gift Rule applied: Sections 19 and 20 of the Land Reform Law No. 1 of 1972. Court's reasoning: The court held that under Section 20 of the Land Reform Law, a statutory determination only becomes effective upon its publication in the Gazette. Although Arthur Weeratunga received a statutory determination in 1976, the formal Gazette notification was not published until 1981. Because the deeds were executed in 1974 and 1979, they predated the effective date of the statutory determination. Consequently, at the time of execution, the donor lacked the requisite title to alienate the land, and he had failed to obtain the necessary permission from the Land Reform Commission as required by Section 14 of the Law. Finding: The Deeds of Gift were null and void as the donor lacked title at the time of execution. Issue 2: Applicability of exceptio rei venditae et traditae Rule applied: The Roman-Dutch law principle of exceptio rei venditae et traditae as interpreted in Kanapathipillai v. Vethanayagam. Court's reasoning: The court examined whether the doctrine, which allows a purchaser to claim title if a vendor subsequently acquires it, extends to donations. Relying on Kanapathipillai v. Vethanayagam and Tissera v. William, the court reaffirmed that the doctrine is limited to sales and does not apply to gifts. A donor cannot convey title they do not possess, and the subsequent acquisition of title by the donor does not retroactively validate a void deed of gift. Finding: The doctrine of exceptio rei venditae et traditae does not apply to donations, and therefore cannot validate the void deeds. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the deeds were void ab initio due to the Land Reform Law rendered the donor incapable of transferring title. The court's finding on the second issue confirms that this initial lack of capacity cannot be cured by the subsequent acquisition of title, as the legal mechanism for such a cure (exceptio rei venditae et traditae) is legally inapplicable to the category of transaction (donation) involved in this case. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, and the judgments of the District Court and the High Court are set aside. The Deeds of Gift Nos. 1476 and 10332 are declared null and void. No order is made as to costs. Keywords Land Reform Law — Statutory Determination — Validity of Deeds of Gift — Effect of Gazette Notification — Exceptio rei venditae et traditae — Conveyance of land — Statutory lessee

Parties: Galkissa Dewage Suneetha Ranasinghe v. Pattiya Dewage Madilin Nona and Others
Bench: P.A. Ratnayake, P.C., J.; Suresh Chandra, J.; Priyasath Dep, P.C., J.
Keywords: demarcation of boundaries — ejectment — Civil Procedure Code — leave to appeal — final judgment — interlocutory order — counter claim — preliminary issue — substantial rights — failure of justice — legal reasoning — appeal procedure — judgment definition — case law
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Petitioner-Respondent, Galkissa Dewage Suneetha Ranasinghe, initiated legal proceedings in the District Court of Colombo against the Defendant-Respondent-Petitioners, which included multiple parties, seeking a demarcation of the boundaries of a property and the ejectment of the Petitioners from a portion of the property that the Respondent claimed was encroached upon. The case was complex, involving multiple parties with overlapping claims and counterclaims. The Petitioners, in their response, sought the dismissal of the Respondent's case and filed a claim in reconvention, aiming to eject the Respondent from the property. During the trial, which commenced on June 4, 2007, the Respondent raised objections to the issues framed by the Petitioners based on their reconventional claim. The Petitioners requested that one of their issues be treated as a preliminary issue of law, which the District Court accepted. The District Judge ruled on February 8, 2008, in favor of the Petitioners, dismissing the Respondent's case and allowing the trial to proceed based on the Petitioners' counterclaim. The Respondent sought Leave to Appeal to the Civil Appellate High Court against this order, which led to further legal proceedings. The Civil Appellate High Court, after hearing the parties, rejected a preliminary objection raised by the Petitioners regarding the nature of the appeal, leading to the current appeal before the Supreme Court. Issues The Supreme Court was tasked with addressing several critical legal questions: Whether the High Court of Civil Appeal erred in law by deciding that Leave to Appeal lies from the dismissal of the Plaintiff's action? Whether leave to appeal lies to the High Court of Civil Appeal from that part of the order of the Learned District Judge dated 02.08.2008, pertaining to the dismissal of the action of the Plaintiff-Respondent? Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Article 128 of the Constitution Cases Referred to: S. Rajendran Chettiar and Two others vs. S. Narayanan Chettiar - SC. Appeal No. 101A/2009; Salaman v. Warner [1891] 1 QB 734; Bozson v. Altrincham Urban District Council [1903] 1 KB 547; Ranjith vs. Kusumawathie 1998 3 Sri LR 232. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the High Court of Civil Appeal erred in law by deciding that Leave to Appeal lies from the dismissal of the Plaintiff's action? Rule applied: Section 754(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the nature of the order made by the District Judge, which dismissed the Respondent's case based on a preliminary issue raised by the Petitioners. The Court referenced the definition of 'judgment' in Section 754(5) of the Civil Procedure Code, which distinguishes between final judgments and interlocutory orders. The Court noted that the District Judge's order did not dispose of the matter entirely but allowed the case to continue based on the Petitioners' counterclaim. The Court cited the precedent set in S. Rajendran Chettiar and Two others vs. S. Narayanan Chettiar, emphasizing that an order rejecting a plaint is not a final judgment but rather an interlocutory order. The Court further elaborated on the implications of the District Judge's ruling, stating that if the counterclaim were not present, the dismissal would not prevent the case from proceeding. Therefore, the Court concluded that the order in question did not have the effect of a final judgment, and thus, the Respondent was entitled to appeal under Section 754(2). Finding: The Court answered the question in the negative, affirming that the High Court of Civil Appeal did not err in its decision regarding the appeal's nature. Issue 2: Whether leave to appeal lies to the High Court of Civil Appeal from that part of the order of the Learned District Judge dated 02.08.2008, pertaining to the dismissal of the action of the Plaintiff-Respondent? Rule applied: Section 754(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court addressed the clerical error regarding the date mentioned in the Petition of Appeal, clarifying that the correct date was February 8, 2008. The Court reiterated its earlier reasoning regarding the nature of the order made by the District Judge, emphasizing that the dismissal did not constitute a final judgment. The Court also noted that the Petitioners' argument regarding the lack of comprehensive reasoning in the Civil Appellate High Court's order was not raised in the Petition of Appeal and did not affect the substantial rights of the parties involved. The Court concluded that the Respondent's right to appeal was valid under the provisions of the Civil Procedure Code. Finding: The Court answered this question in the affirmative, confirming that leave to appeal was appropriate. Interaction of Issues The issues presented were interrelated, as they both revolved around the nature of the District Judge's order and the implications for the appeal process. The determination of whether the order was final or interlocutory directly influenced the Court's conclusions on the appropriateness of the appeal to the Civil Appellate High Court. The Court's analysis highlighted the importance of understanding the procedural context and the legal definitions that govern appeals in civil matters. Conclusion The Supreme Court ultimately dismissed the appeal of the Petitioners, affirming the decisions made by the lower courts regarding the nature of the order and the Respondent's right to appeal. The Court made no order as to costs, indicating a recognition of the complexities involved in the case and the legal principles at play. The judgment underscored the significance of procedural clarity in civil litigation and the necessity for parties to adhere to established legal frameworks when pursuing appeals. Keywords demarcation of boundaries — ejectment — Civil Procedure Code — leave to appeal — final judgment — interlocutory order — counter claim — preliminary issue — substantial rights — failure of justice — legal reasoning — appeal procedure — judgment definition — case law
Counsel: S.N. Vijithsingh for the Defendant-Respondent-Petitioners. M.U.M. Ali Sabry with Shamith Fernando for the Plaintiff-Petitioner-Respondent.

Parties: Liyanage v. Somasiri and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Salary scale revision — Efficiency Bar Examinations — Satisfactory service — Establishment Code — Sri Lanka Engineering Service — Salary increments — Classification of public officers
Summary: Facts The petitioner, a Chief Irrigation Engineer in the Department of Irrigation, joined the Sri Lanka Engineering Service (SLES) on 02-12-1996. She was confirmed in her position and subsequently promoted to Class II Grade I with effect from 02-12-2006. Under the SLES Minute and Engineering Service Circular No.25, officers who completed six years of satisfactory service and passed two Efficiency Bar Examinations and a second language test were entitled to be placed on a specific salary step. The petitioner was initially placed on the salary step of Rs.124,500/- with effect from 02-12-2002. By a letter dated 29-06-2009, the 4th respondent informed the petitioner that the previous decision regarding her salary scale was cancelled and she was placed on a different salary scale with effect from 24-07-2004. The respondents further required the petitioner to refund amounts deemed to be overpaid. The petitioner challenged this decision, arguing it was arbitrary, illegal, and a violation of her fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The respondents contended that the petitioner failed to complete her first Efficiency Bar Examination within the stipulated four-year period, resulting in a delay of one year, seven months, and twenty-two days. Consequently, the respondents argued this period of delay must be excluded when calculating the "satisfactory service" required for the salary advancement. Issues Whether the cancellation of the petitioner's salary scale and the subsequent requirement to refund overpaid salary constituted a violation of the petitioner's fundamental right to equality under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the period of delay in completing the Efficiency Bar Examination must be excluded when calculating the "satisfactory service" required for salary advancement under the SLES Minute. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Establishment Code, Chapter VII, Section 10:1 Cases Referred to: Kedar Nath Bajoria v. The State of West Bengal - [A.I.R. 1953 S.C. 404] The Anant Mills Co. Ltd. v. The State of Gujarat - [A.I.R. 1975 S.C. 1234] Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution, which guarantees equality before the law and equal protection of the law. Court's reasoning: The Court held that equality does not require identical treatment for all, but rather that equals be treated equally. Reasonable classification is permissible if it is based on an intelligible differentia with a rational relation to the objective. The Court found that the respondents' classification of officers based on "satisfactory service"—defined by the timely completion of Efficiency Bar Examinations—was a valid and uniform application of the rules. Since the petitioner failed to meet the criteria for "satisfactory service" due to her delay, she was not similarly situated to those who had met the requirements. Finding: The petitioner failed to establish a violation of Article 12(1) as the respondents' actions were based on a valid, non-discriminatory classification. Issue 2: Calculation of Satisfactory Service Rule applied: Engineering Service Circular No.25 and the Establishment Code, Chapter VII, Section 10:1. Court's reasoning: The Court noted that the SLES Minute and the Establishment Code mandate the completion of Efficiency Bar Examinations within specific time frames. Section 10:1 of the Establishment Code dictates that increments are not a right but must be earned through efficient service. Because the petitioner delayed her first Efficiency Bar Examination by one year, seven months, and twenty-two days, her salary increments were legally deferred by that same period. Under the criteria for "satisfactory service," periods where increments are deferred must be excluded from the calculation. Finding: The period of delay in passing the Efficiency Bar Examination must be excluded from the calculation of the petitioner's satisfactory service. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are interdependent; the determination that the petitioner's service was not "satisfactory" due to the delay in passing the Efficiency Bar Examination (Issue 2) provided the rational basis for the classification used by the respondents (Issue 1). Because the classification was deemed rational and based on the petitioner's own failure to meet service requirements, the Court concluded that the resulting salary adjustment did not violate the constitutional right to equality. Conclusion The Court held that the respondents acted within their authority to rectify the salary scale based on the petitioner's failure to maintain "satisfactory service" as defined by the relevant regulations. The application is dismissed with no order as to costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Salary scale revision — Efficiency Bar Examinations — Satisfactory service — Establishment Code — Sri Lanka Engineering Service — Salary increments — Classification of public officers

Parties: Fernando v. Mayadunne
Bench: R.A.N.G. Amaratunge, J.; S.I. Imam, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: Substitution of parties — Civil Procedure Code Section 839 — Civil Procedure Code Section 341(1) — Survival of action — Tenancy action — Death of tenant — Rent Act No. 7 — Succession to tenancy — Next of kin
Summary: Facts The Appellant, Ms. Deepthi Fernando, filed an action in the District Court of Mount Lavinia against the Defendant, Mrs. Mayadunne, seeking her ejectment from premises located at Galle Road, Colombo 06, based on arrears of rent and damages. The action proceeded ex-parte, and a decree was entered in favor of the Appellant. Upon attempting to execute the decree, the Fiscal reported that the Defendant had died. The Appellant subsequently moved to substitute the Respondent, the nephew of the deceased Defendant, in the room of the deceased under Section 839 of the Civil Procedure Code, asserting that the Defendant died intestate, issueless, and without an administrable estate. The Respondent filed objections, contending that he was not the legal representative of the deceased, had not inherited her estate, and that the right to sue did not survive the death of the tenant. The District Court allowed the substitution, but the Civil Appellate High Court of Colombo set aside this order on appeal. The Appellant then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court erred in holding that Section 341(1) of the Civil Procedure Code was the applicable provision rather than Section 839. Whether the Appellant has the right to appoint the Respondent as a substituted defendant for the purpose of serving an ex-parte decree. Whether the action for ejectment survives the death of the tenant where no succession to the tenancy has been claimed. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 341(1) Civil Procedure Code, Section 839 Rent Act No. 7 of 1972, Section 36 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of Section 341(1) vs. Section 839 Rule applied: Section 341(1) and Section 839 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court determined that Section 341(1) is the relevant provision for substituting a deceased defendant in civil proceedings. The Appellantâ€™s attempt to invoke the inherent powers of the court under Section 839 was inappropriate because the specific procedural requirements for substitution upon the death of a party were not met. The Respondent, despite being the nephew of the deceased, had not claimed the estate or acted as an executor de son tort. Finding: The High Court did not err in identifying Section 341(1) as the applicable provision and rejecting the application under Section 839. Issue 2: Right to substitute for service of decree Rule applied: Law of contract regarding tenancy. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that the underlying action was a contract of tenancy. Upon the death of the tenant, the contract terminated. Because no steps were taken under Section 36 of the Rent Act No. 7 of 1972 to claim succession to the tenancy, the action could not proceed against a third party. Finding: The Appellant has no right to substitute the Respondent to serve a decree that became a nullity upon the death of the original tenant. Issue 3: Survival of the action Rule applied: Section 36 of the Rent Act No. 7 of 1972. Court's reasoning: The court held that a tenancy action is based on a contract, which terminates upon the death of the tenant. In the absence of a claim for succession, the action does not survive against the deceased's relatives. The decree entered against the deceased became a nullity upon her death. Finding: The action did not survive the death of the tenant, rendering the substitution of the Respondent legally impermissible. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected; the determination that the action did not survive the death of the tenant (Issue 3) confirms that the decree became a nullity, which directly informs the court's conclusion that the Appellant had no right to substitute the Respondent (Issue 2). This procedural failure reinforces the correctness of the High Courtâ€™s application of Section 341(1) over the Appellant's reliance on Section 839 (Issue 1). Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court is affirmed, and the Appellant is ordered to pay costs fixed at Rs. 21,000. Keywords Substitution of parties — Civil Procedure Code Section 839 — Civil Procedure Code Section 341(1) — Survival of action — Tenancy action — Death of tenant — Rent Act No. 7 — Succession to tenancy — Next of kin

Parties: Fernando v. Somasiri and Somasiri
Bench: R.A.N.G. Amaratunge, J.; P.A. Ratnayake, PC. J; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Appeal from High Court — Question of law — Identity of land — Surveyor General's plan — Final Colony Plan — Permit — Signature on permit — Vindicatory action — Burden of proof — Establishing corpus
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent (Respondent) instituted an action in the District Court of Polonnaruwa seeking a declaration of title to a land based on a permit issued under the Land Development Ordinance, the ejectment of the Defendant-Appellant-Petitioner (Petitioner), and damages. The Respondent claimed that he was the lawful permit holder of the land and that the Petitioner had entered the premises without authorization. The Petitioner contended that the land he occupied was a distinct allotment, specifically lot 946 of Final Colony Plan 160, which did not correspond to the land described in the Respondent's permit. The Petitioner further asserted that he had obtained permission from authorities to construct a house and was pursuing a long-term lease. During the proceedings, a representative from the District Surveyorâ€™s office testified that the Surveyor General had no record of the plan or the specific allotments cited in the Respondent's permit. Conversely, the surveyor confirmed that the boundaries of the land occupied by the Petitioner matched those of lot 946 of Final Colony Plan 160. Furthermore, the individual who served as the Divisional Secretary and Assistant Government Agent at the time the permit was purportedly issued testified that the signature on the permit was not his. The District Court entered judgment in favor of the Respondent, and the Civil Appellate High Court affirmed that decision. The Petitioner subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Respondent discharged the burden of establishing the identity of the land described in the schedule to the plaint in reference to the plans referred to therein. Whether the Civil Appellate High Court and the District Court erred in law by failing to consider that the plan FCP 160 was not referred to in the permit despite its existence at the time of issuance. Whether the Civil Appellate High Court and the District Court erred in law by failing to account for the discrepancies between the boundaries and extent described in the permit and those in the plan used to identify the corpus. Applicable Law Statutes: Land Development Ordinance High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 54 of 1996 Cases Referred to: Palisena v. Perera - [56-nlr-407] Bandaranaike v. Karunawathie - [2003/v3-sri-lr-295] Pieris v. Savunahamy - [54-nlr-207] Analysis Issue 1: Identity of the land Rule applied: The principle established in Pieris v. Savunahamy, which dictates that in a vindicatory action, the burden of proof rests upon the plaintiff to prove his title, including the precise identification of the boundaries of the corpus. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the Respondent failed to produce the plan mentioned in his permit. Furthermore, the District Surveyorâ€™s representative testified that the plan and allotment numbers cited in the permit did not exist in the Surveyor General's records. The Respondent failed to reconcile the description in his permit with the physical land, nor did he address the testimony of the official who denied signing the permit. Finding: The Respondent failed to discharge the burden of establishing the identity of the land. Issue 2: Failure to consider existing plans Rule applied: The evidentiary requirement that a plaintiff must establish a clear connection between the title document (the permit) and the physical corpus. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the plan 'V1' (Final Colony Plan 160) was in existence when the permit was issued, yet the permit made no reference to it. The lower courts failed to consider this omission, which undermined the Respondent's claim that his permit covered the land in question. Finding: The lower courts erred in law by failing to consider the significance of the existing plan 'V1' and its lack of connection to the permit. Issue 3: Discrepancies in boundaries and extent Rule applied: The requirement for certainty in a vindicatory action regarding the corpus. Court's reasoning: The court highlighted that the extent of land in the permit (1 rood) did not match the extent of the land claimed by the Petitioner (2 roods 10 perches). The Respondent provided no explanation for this discrepancy. The court found that the boundaries of the land occupied by the Petitioner matched the Surveyor General's plan 'V1', which was not the plan referenced in the permit. Finding: The lower courts erred in failing to account for the material discrepancies in the description of the corpus. Interaction of Issues The failure to identify the land (Issue 1) is inextricably linked to the failure to reconcile the permit with existing survey plans (Issue 2) and the physical discrepancies in the land's description (Issue 3). Together, these failures demonstrate that the Respondent did not meet the evidentiary threshold required for a vindicatory action. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgments of the Civil Appellate High Court and the District Court are set aside, and the action filed by the Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent is dismissed.
