Keywords Appeal from High Court — Question of law — Identity of land — Surveyor General's plan — Final Colony Plan — Permit — Signature on permit — Vindicatory action — Burden of proof — Establishing corpus

Parties: Kumarihamy and Another v. The Principal and Others
Bench: Hon.P.A. Ratnayake, PC. J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — School admissions — Marking scheme — Residence — Electoral register — Administrative action — Selection Committee — Interpretation of circular
Summary: Facts The 1st Petitioner is the mother of the 2nd Petitioner, a minor child, who sought admission to Mahamaya Balika Vidyalaya, Kandy, for Year 1 in 2009. The application for admission was made under the Ministry of Education Circular, which allocates 50% of student intake to children residing in close proximity to the school. The marking scheme for this category requires proof of residence through the electoral register, awarding 7 marks for each year the parent's name appears as the chief occupant for a five-year period. The School Selection Committee awarded the 2nd Petitioner 73 marks, falling short of the 80-mark cutoff required for admission. Specifically, the 2nd Petitioner was denied 7 marks for the year 2003 because the parents' names did not appear in the electoral register for that year. The 1st Petitioner admitted that the names were absent from the register for 2003 because the family was residing in the United Kingdom for the husband's career advancement, during which time the 2nd Petitioner was born. The Petitioners challenged the decision of the School Selection Committee, arguing that their residence should not be considered interrupted by temporary absence. The Petitioners did not file a counter-affidavit to contest the evidence provided by the 1st Respondent. Issues Whether the School Selection Committee violated Article 12(1) of the Constitution by failing to award marks for the year 2003 under the school admission circular. Whether the absence of the parents' names from the electoral register for the year 2003 precluded the School Selection Committee from exercising discretion to award marks under the circular. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Cases Referred to: Geethaka v. Dissanayake - [sc-fr-35/2011] Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution, which guarantees the right to equality before the law and equal protection of the law. Court's reasoning: The court examined whether the School Selection Committee acted arbitrarily or discriminatorily. The committee followed the objective criteria set out in the Ministry of Education Circular, which mandates the use of the electoral register to verify residence. Because the Petitioners failed to provide evidence that their names were in the register for 2003, and failed to file a counter-affidavit to challenge the Respondent's evidence, the court found no basis to conclude that the committee acted outside the scope of the circular or in a discriminatory manner. Finding: The court held that there was no violation of Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Issue 2: Discretion of the Selection Committee Rule applied: Clause i of the Marking Scheme under Paragraph 6.1 of the Ministry of Education Circular. Court's reasoning: The circular explicitly requires the electoral register to be used as proof of residence. The court reasoned that the Selection Committee possessed no discretion to award marks in the absence of the required documentation. The court noted that the Petitioners did not challenge the electoral register itself, nor did they cite the officials responsible for the register as parties to the case. Consequently, the committee was bound by the strict requirement of "no name in register, no marks." Finding: The Selection Committee acted correctly within the parameters of the circular, and no discretion existed to override the requirement of the electoral register. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. Because the Selection Committee lacked the discretion to deviate from the circular's requirements (Issue 2), their adherence to the objective marking scheme was inherently consistent with the principle of equal treatment under the law (Issue 1). The failure to satisfy the documentary requirements of the circular rendered the claim of constitutional inequality groundless. Conclusion The application is dismissed. The court made no order as to costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — School admissions — Marking scheme — Residence — Electoral register — Administrative action — Selection Committee — Interpretation of circular

Parties: Nihal and Nanayakkara (Minor) v. Panditharathne and Others
Bench: Amaratunge, J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Admission to school — False information — Residency requirements — Circulars and guidelines — Administrative action — Equality before the law
Summary: Facts The 1st Petitioner is the father of the 2nd Petitioner, a minor, on whose behalf an application was submitted for admission to Year 1 at Dharmasoka College, Ambalangoda. The application was made under the category of brothers and sisters of students currently studying in the school. The 1st Petitioner and his wife attended an interview and submitted various documents, including a lease agreement, to establish their residence. The Interview Board initially awarded the application 79.5 marks. The 2nd Petitionerâ€™s name was subsequently excluded from the temporary list of selected students. Upon inquiry, the 1st Petitioner was informed that an anonymous petition had been received alleging that the information provided regarding his residence was false. The 1st Petitioner appealed this decision. During the appeal process, it was revealed that the lessor of the premises cited by the Petitioner had objected to the application, stating that the Petitioners were not permanent residents of the premises. The 1st Respondent, the Principal of the school, further discovered that the electoral registers for the years 2005, 2006, 2007, and 2009 listed the Petitioners at a different address in Balapitiya. Consequently, the Interview Board and the Appeal Board rejected the application on the grounds that the Petitioners had provided false information regarding their residence. Issues Whether the rejection of the 2nd Petitionerâ€™s application for admission to Year 1 of Dharmasoka College violated the fundamental rights guaranteed under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the Respondents were justified in rejecting the application based on the provision of false information regarding the Petitioners' residence under Circular No. 2010/21. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 12(1), 17, 27(2)(h), 126. Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Fundamental Rights Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution, which guarantees equality before the law and equal protection of the law. Court's reasoning: The Petitioners contended that the non-selection of the 2nd Petitioner was discriminatory and arbitrary. However, the court examined the evidence provided by the 1st Respondent, which indicated that the Petitioners had submitted false information regarding their permanent residence. The court found that the administrative actions taken by the school authorities were based on the discovery of this false information rather than discriminatory intent. Finding: The court held that there was no violation of the fundamental rights of the Petitioners as the rejection was based on the discovery of false information. Issue 2: Justification of Rejection Rule applied: Circular No. 2010/21 dated 31st May 2011, specifically sections 8-2(a) and (f). Court's reasoning: The court reviewed the evidence, including the affidavit from the lessor of the premises and the electoral registers, which contradicted the address provided by the 1st Petitioner. The court noted that the circular governing school admissions explicitly stipulates that if information or documents submitted are found to be incorrect or false, the Interview Board is empowered to reject the application. The court concluded that the Respondents acted in accordance with these guidelines. Finding: The court found that the Respondents were justified in rejecting the application because the Petitioners had provided false information regarding their permanent residence. Interaction of Issues The findings are intrinsically linked; the determination that the Petitioners provided false information (Issue 2) directly negates the claim that the school's decision was arbitrary or discriminatory (Issue 1). Because the rejection was a lawful exercise of authority under the Ministry of Education's circular, the alleged violation of Article 12(1) could not be sustained. Conclusion The application is dismissed. The 1st Petitioner is ordered to pay costs of Rs. 10,000 to the State. Keywords Fundamental rights — Admission to school — False information — Residency requirements — Circulars and guidelines — Administrative action — Equality before the law

Parties: De Silva and Others v. Panditharathne and Others
Bench: Amaratunge, J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Priyasath Dep, J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Admission to school — Residency requirements — False information — Administrative action — Circulars and guidelines — Appeal Board — Interview Board
Summary: Facts The first Petitioner, Madaduwage Susil de Silva, submitted an application for the admission of his son, the second Petitioner, to the first grade at Dharmashoka Vidyalaya, Ambalangoda. The application was submitted under two categories: children of residents living in close proximity to the school and brothers or sisters of students currently studying in the school. The admission process was governed by Circular No. 2010/21 issued by the Ministry of Education. The first Petitioner attended interviews for both categories and submitted various documents, including unregistered lease agreements and extracts from electoral registers, to prove his residency. Following the publication of the temporary admission lists, the second Petitioner was not selected. The first Petitioner subsequently appealed to the Appeal Board, alleging that his residency documents were valid and that the Interview Board had incorrectly calculated his marks. The first Respondent, the Principal of the school, stated that the Interview Board received objections from local residents alleging that the Petitioners did not reside at the address provided in their application. Upon investigation, the Respondents discovered that the lessor of the premises, whose name appeared on the lease agreements provided by the Petitioner, was abroad during the period the second lease agreement was purportedly signed. Consequently, the Interview Board and the Appeal Board rejected the application on the grounds that the Petitioners had provided false information regarding their residence, in accordance with the provisions of Circular No. 2010/21. Issues Whether the rejection of the second Petitionerâ€™s application for school admission by the Interview Board and the Appeal Board was in violation of Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the Respondents were justified in rejecting the application based on the submission of false information regarding residency under Circular No. 2010/21. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 27(2)(h) Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution, which guarantees equality before the law and equal protection of the law. Court's reasoning: The Petitioners contended that the non-selection of the second Petitioner was discriminatory and arbitrary. However, the court examined the evidence regarding the residency documents submitted by the first Petitioner. The court found that the information provided to the Interview Board was false, specifically regarding the lease agreements. Because the rejection was based on the Petitioner's own submission of fraudulent documentation, the administrative action taken by the school authorities did not constitute an arbitrary or discriminatory act. Finding: There was no violation of the fundamental rights guaranteed under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Issue 2: Justification for rejection under Circular No. 2010/21 Rule applied: Circular No. 2010/21 issued by the Ministry of Education, specifically section 8-2 (a) and (f). Court's reasoning: The circular explicitly states that if documents or information submitted by applicants are found to be incorrect or false, the Interview Board is empowered to reject the application. The Respondents provided evidence, including travel records from the Controller General of Immigration and Emigration, proving that the lessor of the premises was overseas at the time the lease agreement was purportedly signed. The court concluded that the Respondents acted within the scope of their authority under the circular when they nullified the marks and rejected the application due to the discovery of false information. Finding: The Respondents were justified in rejecting the application. Conclusion The court held that the Petitioners provided false information regarding their residence, justifying the rejection of the application under the applicable Ministry of Education circular. The application is dismissed, and the first Petitioner is ordered to pay costs of Rs 10,000 to the State. Keywords Fundamental rights — Admission to school — Residency requirements — False information — Administrative action — Circulars and guidelines — Appeal Board — Interview Board

Parties: Saputhantrige Nandawathie v. Freeda Fonseka and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; S.I. Imam, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Testamentary case — Intestate succession — Scheme of Distribution — Consent of parties — Fiduciary duties of administrator — Role of the court in testamentary proceedings — Abolition of Fideicommissa and Entails Act — Last will and testament — District Court jurisdiction — High Court jurisdiction
Summary: Facts The deceased, Meemanage Wilfred Fernando, died intestate in 1984, leaving his wife, Saputhantrige Nandawathie, and three siblings as heirs. Saputhantrige Nandawathie was granted letters of administration for the estate. Following her death in 1994, Meemanage Harold Fernando was substituted as the administrator. During the pendency of the proceedings, the original first respondent, Freeda Fonseka, passed away, and her daughters, Jayani Wimalarathna and Manoja Waliwitigoda, were substituted in her place. The administrator filed final accounts and an inventory in 2006, to which the substituted respondents objected, arguing that the valuations did not reflect current market rates. The District Court directed the administrator to file an amended inventory and final accounts based on current market values. The administrator appealed this order to the High Court. The High Court set aside the District Court's order and directed the court to accept the administrator's scheme of distribution and terminate the proceedings. The substituted respondents subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court erred in law by granting relief not sought by the parties, specifically by approving a scheme of distribution that had not been considered by the District Court. Whether the court has a duty to supervise and control the administration of an estate, including the scrutiny of a scheme of distribution, regardless of whether formal objections were filed by all parties. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code Abolition of Fideicommissa and Entails Act No. 20 of 1972 Cases Referred to: [sc-appeal-177/2015] Analysis Issue 1: Approval of the Scheme of Distribution Rule applied: The court's supervisory jurisdiction under the Civil Procedure Code regarding the settlement of estates. Court's reasoning: The High Court directed the District Court to accept a scheme of distribution that had not been properly vetted or considered by the trial judge. The record indicated that the substituted respondents had not consented to this scheme, and the mere absence of an objection does not equate to legal assent. Furthermore, the scheme introduced complex legal issues regarding the devolution of property based on a separate last will that had not been produced in court. Finding: The High Court erred in law by directing the District Court to accept the scheme of distribution without allowing the respondents an opportunity to object and without the trial court conducting a proper inquiry. Issue 2: Duties of the Court and Administrator Rule applied: Chapter LIV and Chapter LV of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The administrator holds a fiduciary position and is responsible for the due administration of the estate. The court is not a "rubber stamp" but has a mandatory duty to supervise and control the administration of estates to protect the interests of all heirs and creditors. The court must ensure that the distribution is legally sound, particularly when the proposed scheme excludes assets or introduces third-party interests that deviate from standard intestate succession. Finding: The District Court is required to examine the scheme of distribution, consider any objections, and ensure the estate is administered in accordance with the law, rather than summarily accepting a scheme that may prejudice the heirs. Interaction of Issues The findings are intrinsically linked; the failure of the High Court to recognize the trial court's supervisory duty (Issue 2) led directly to the procedural error of ordering the acceptance of an unvetted scheme of distribution (Issue 1). By failing to allow for an inquiry, the High Court bypassed the essential judicial oversight required by the Civil Procedure Code. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The order of the High Court directing the District Court to accept the scheme of distribution is set aside, and the case is remanded to the District Court to allow the substituted respondents to file objections and for the court to conduct an inquiry into the scheme. Keywords Testamentary case — Intestate succession — Scheme of Distribution — Consent of parties — Fiduciary duties of administrator — Role of the court in testamentary proceedings — Abolition of Fideicommissa and Entails Act — Last will and testament — District Court jurisdiction — High Court jurisdiction

Parties: Don Udaya Shantha v. Kumaranatunga and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; K. Sripavan, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Special Leave to Appeal — Supreme Court Rules — Rule 8(3) — Rule 40 — Issuance of notices — Extension of time — Non-compliance with Rules — Preliminary objection
Summary: Facts The Petitioner filed an application for Special Leave to Appeal in the Supreme Court on 22.03.2010, challenging a judgment of the Court of Appeal dated 10.02.2010. Upon filing the petition, affidavit, and accompanying documents, the Petitioner failed to tender notices for service on the Respondents as required by the Supreme Court Rules, 1990. No further steps were taken by the Petitioner until 21.09.2010, when a motion was filed to tender additional documents and to amend the caption to reflect changes in the Respondents' official positions following the April 2010 General Elections. The Respondents filed a motion on 10.11.2010, raising a preliminary objection that the Petitioner failed to comply with Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990, by not tendering notices at the time of filing the application. The Petitioner contended that he had sought an extension of time under Rule 40 of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990, due to the impending change in government officials, and argued that the failure to comply with Rule 8(3) was a technicality that should not bar the administration of justice. The court record indicated that notices were only served on the Respondents on 25.10.2010, approximately six months after the initial filing. Issues Whether the Petitionerâ€™s failure to tender notices for service on the Respondents at the time of filing the application constituted a fatal non-compliance with Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990. Whether the Petitioner could invoke Rule 40 of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990, to obtain an extension of time for tendering notices six months after the initial filing of the application. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 136 Supreme Court Rules, 1990, Rule 8(1), Rule 8(3), Rule 8(5), Rule 40 Cases Referred to: A.H.M. Fowzie v Vehicles Lanka (Pvt) Ltd. - [(2008) B.L.R. 127] Wickramatillake v Marikar - [2-nlr-9] Annamalai Chettiar v Mangala Karunasinghe - [sc-application-69/2003] K. Reaindran v K. Velusomasunderan - [sc-appeal-298/99] N.A. Premadasa v The Peoples Bank - [sc-appeal-212/99] Hameed v Majibdeen - [sc-appeal-38/2001] K.M. Samarasinghe v R.M.D. Ratnayake - [sc-appeal-51/2001] Soong Che Foo v Harosha K. De Silva - [sc-appeal-184/2003] C.A. Haroon v S.K. Muzoor - [sc-appeal-158/2006] Samantha Niroshana v Senarath Abeyruwan - [sc-appeal-145/2006] Woodman Exports (Pvt) Ltd. v Commissioner General of Labour - [sc-appeal-335/2008] Tissa Attanayake v Commissioner General of Election - [sc-appeal-55/2011] Analysis Issue 1: Non-compliance with Rule 8(3) Rule applied: Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990, which mandates that a petitioner must tender notices for service on respondents along with the application for Special Leave to Appeal. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that the Petitioner failed to tender any notices when the application was filed on 22.03.2010. The Court emphasized that Rule 8 is designed to ensure that respondents are notified promptly to allow them to participate in the proceedings. The Court held that compliance with this rule is mandatory and not a mere technicality, as it is essential for the orderly administration of justice. Finding: The Petitioner failed to comply with the mandatory requirements of Rule 8(3). Issue 2: Applicability of Rule 40 for extension Rule applied: Rule 40 of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990, regarding the extension of time for procedural compliance. Court's reasoning: The Court reasoned that Rule 40 cannot be used to circumvent the mandatory notice requirements after a significant delay. An extension under Rule 40 must be sought either at the time of filing or after the verification process stipulated in Rule 8(5). Since the Petitioner waited six months to take any steps toward serving notice, the request for an extension was deemed invalid. Finding: The Petitioner could not invoke Rule 40 to cure the six-month delay in compliance. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are interdependent; the failure to comply with the mandatory notice requirement of Rule 8(3) at the outset could not be remedied by a belated application under Rule 40. The Courtâ€™s strict interpretation of Rule 8(3) rendered the Petitioner's attempt to use Rule 40 as a curative measure ineffective, as the delay itself undermined the procedural integrity of the application. Conclusion The preliminary objection raised by the Respondents is upheld. The Petitioner's application for Special Leave to Appeal is dismissed for non-compliance with Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990. No order is made as to costs. Keywords Special Leave to Appeal — Supreme Court Rules — Rule 8(3) — Rule 40 — Issuance of notices — Extension of time — Non-compliance with Rules — Preliminary objection

Parties: Devanagala v. Karunatilleke and Sirisena
Bench: Saleem Marsoof PC, J; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J.; Sathyaa Hettige PC, J
Keywords: Partition action — Duty of trial judge to investigate title — Examination of deeds — Pedigree and devolution of shares — Fundamental principles of partition — Section 25 of the Partition Act — Role of the Court of Appeal — Miscarriage of justice
Summary: Facts The Appellant, B.P.G. Premawathie Rukulagama Devanagala, instituted a partition action in the District Court of Kegalle regarding a land called Apullannage Watta alias Udaha Apullannage Pallehawatte. The Appellant and the Respondents, B.P.G. Karunatilleke and B.P.G. Sirisena, claimed ownership based on differing pedigrees and devolution of title. The parties agreed that the corpus was depicted in Preliminary Plan No. 869. Both sides produced various deeds and provided evidence regarding their respective claims and possession of the land and buildings thereon. The District Court accepted the pedigree and devolution of title presented by the 2nd Respondent and entered judgment accordingly. The Court of Appeal subsequently affirmed the District Court's judgment. The Appellant appealed to the Supreme Court, which granted leave to consider whether the trial judge failed to properly investigate the title and pedigree before allocating shares. Issues Whether the trial judge failed to investigate the title of the parties and establish a correct pedigree before allocating shares in a partition action as required by Section 25(1) of the Partition Act. Whether the Court of Appeal erred in law by failing to address the trial judge's failure to investigate the title and by dismissing the appeal. Applicable Law Statutes: Partition Act No. 16 of 1951 (Section 25(1)) Cases Referred to: Cooray v. Wijesuriya - [62-nlr-158] Analysis Issue 1: Failure to investigate title Rule applied: Section 25(1) of the Partition Act, which mandates that the court must examine the title of each party, receive evidence, and determine all questions of law and fact regarding the rights and shares of parties to the land. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court observed that the trial judge failed to scrutinize the deeds produced by the 2nd Respondent (2D4, 2D5, and 2D6). These deeds contained descriptions of land and boundaries inconsistent with the corpus in the Preliminary Plan. The trial judge accepted the 2nd Respondent's version without resolving these discrepancies or addressing the Appellant's challenge to the validity of these deeds. The Court held that a trial judge has a mandatory duty to examine deeds carefully to determine the correct pedigree and devolution of shares. Finding: The trial judge failed to make a finding on the correct pedigree and failed to properly investigate the title as required by law. Issue 2: Error by the Court of Appeal Rule applied: The appellate duty to review whether the trial court fulfilled its statutory obligation under Section 25(1) of the Partition Act. Court's reasoning: The Court of Appeal affirmed the District Court's judgment without addressing the Appellant's arguments regarding the discrepancies in the deeds and the failure of the trial judge to conduct a proper investigation into the title. By failing to advert its mind to these fundamental errors, the Court of Appeal perpetuated a miscarriage of justice. Finding: The Court of Appeal erred in law by dismissing the appeal and failing to address the issues regarding the investigation of title. Interaction of Issues The failure of the trial judge to investigate the title (Issue 1) directly led to an incorrect determination of shares. The Court of Appeal's failure to rectify this (Issue 2) meant that the legal errors in the trial court were left uncorrected. The Supreme Court resolved both by conducting its own investigation of the deeds and evidence to reach a final, just conclusion. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, and the judgment of the District Court is varied to reflect the corrected devolution of shares as determined by the Supreme Court. The parties shall bear their own costs. Keywords Partition action — Duty of trial judge to investigate title — Examination of deeds — Pedigree and devolution of shares — Fundamental principles of partition — Section 25 of the Partition Act — Role of the Court of Appeal — Miscarriage of justice

Parties: Mohommed Tuwan Mohommed Meezam v. Ranjith Sumanasekera and Others
Bench: Amaratunga J.; Suresh Chandra J.; Dep. PC, J.
Keywords: Civil Procedure Code — Amendment of pleadings — Addition of parties — Prescription Ordinance — Ex-parte decree — Motor traffic accident — Judicial discretion — Laches — Prejudice to parties — Cause of action — Registered owner of vehicle.
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a motor traffic accident that occurred on 2nd April 2002, involving a vehicle driven by the 2nd Defendant, Rukshana Deepama Nayomi Sumanasekera, who is the daughter of the 1st Defendant, Ranjith Sumanasekera, and the 3rd Defendant, K.P.G. Sumanasekera, who is the mother of the 2nd Defendant. The Plaintiff, Mohommed Tuwan Mohommed Meezam, filed a case in the District Court of Colombo on 29th August 2003, seeking damages amounting to Rs. 2,000,000 for injuries sustained in the accident. The case proceeded ex-parte against the Defendants, leading to a judgment in favor of the Plaintiff on 13th December 2005. The Defendants later sought to set aside this ex-parte decree, which was granted on 29th March 2007, allowing them to tender their answer. Subsequently, the Plaintiff applied to add the 3rd Defendant as a party, claiming she was the registered owner of the vehicle involved in the accident. The District Court allowed this application, leading to an appeal by the Defendants to the Civil Appellate High Court, which affirmed the District Court's decision. The Defendants then sought leave to appeal to the Supreme Court on several legal questions regarding the addition of the 3rd Defendant after the prescriptive period and the implications of laches. Issues The Supreme Court considered the following issues: Whether the District Court could permit the Plaintiff to add a party after the prescriptive period when the action is instituted against a party who had no right, title, or interest in the matter sued on? Whether the impugned order caused prejudice to the Defendants as they were unable to raise the question of prescription against the added party? Whether laches on the part of the Plaintiff disentitled him to an order in his favor? Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Prescription Ordinance No. 22 of 1871. Cases Referred to: Sherman De Silva & Co. Ltd v. Ariyalatha De Silva - [77 N.L.R. 275]; Lloyd Berenger v. F.N. De Silva and Others - [C.A. Appeal No. 884/92]. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the District Court could permit the Plaintiff to add a party after the prescriptive period. Rule applied: Section 18(1) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court analyzed the timeline of events, noting that the cause of action arose on 2nd April 2002, and the Plaintiff filed the action within the two-year prescriptive period. However, the application to add the 3rd Defendant was made on 25th July 2007, which was beyond the prescriptive period. The Court emphasized that the addition of parties must adhere to the limitations set forth in the Prescription Ordinance. The Court cited the principle that the addition of a party should not alter the nature of the action or take it out of the provisions governing limitation. The learned District Judge's reliance on Section 18(1) was deemed erroneous as it did not consider the implications of the Prescription Ordinance. Finding: The District Court could not permit the Plaintiff to add a party after the prescriptive period. Issue 2: Whether the impugned order caused prejudice to the Defendants. Rule applied: Judicial discretion in the context of adding parties. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court found that the addition of the 3rd Defendant indeed caused prejudice to the existing Defendants, as they were unable to raise the defense of prescription against her. The Court reiterated that the addition of a party must not infringe upon the rights of existing parties, especially concerning time limitations. The Court highlighted that the Defendants had a legitimate expectation that the matter would be resolved without the introduction of new parties after the expiration of the prescriptive period. Finding: The impugned order caused prejudice to the Defendants. Issue 3: Whether laches on the part of the Plaintiff disentitled him from getting an order in his favor. Rule applied: The principle of laches in civil procedure. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the Plaintiff's delay in seeking to add the 3rd Defendant, which was significant given the timeline of events. The Plaintiff's failure to ascertain the registered owner of the vehicle prior to filing the action was seen as a lack of diligence. The Court emphasized that laches could bar a party from relief if they have delayed in asserting their rights to the detriment of others. Finding: Laches on the part of the Plaintiff disentitled him from obtaining an order in his favor regarding the addition of the 3rd Defendant. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as they all stem from the Plaintiff's attempt to add a party after the expiration of the prescriptive period. The Court's findings on the first issue directly influenced the conclusions on the second and third issues, as the inability to add the 3rd Defendant after the prescriptive period inherently prejudiced the Defendants and highlighted the Plaintiff's lack of diligence. Conclusion The Supreme Court set aside the judgments of the Civil Appellate High Court and the District Court, allowing the appeal of the Defendants. The case was remitted back to the District Court for the Plaintiff to proceed against the 1st and 2nd Defendants, with a directive for expedited handling. There were no costs awarded. Keywords Civil Procedure Code — Amendment of pleadings — Addition of parties — Prescription Ordinance — Ex-parte decree — Motor traffic accident — Judicial discretion — Laches — Prejudice to parties — Cause of action — Registered owner of vehicle.
Counsel: Ranjan Suwandaratne with S. Tennakoon for the Defendants – Respondents – Petitioners - Appellants. A.H.G. Ameen with D.K.S. Kodagoda for the Plaintiff – Petitioner – Respondent – Respondent.

Parties: Lakmali v. Fernando
Bench: P.A. Ratnayake, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Maintenance Act No.37 — Special leave to appeal — High Court of Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No.19 — Condition precedent — Article 128 of the Constitution — Appeal to the Supreme Court
Summary: Facts The Respondent initiated maintenance proceedings against the Petitioner in the Magistrate's Court of Marawila. The Magistrate's Court initially ordered the Petitioner to pay Rs. 5,000 per month. The Respondent appealed this order to the High Court of Chilaw, which subsequently varied the order and increased the maintenance payment to Rs. 25,000 per month. The Petitioner then filed an application for special leave to appeal to the Supreme Court against the judgment of the High Court. A preliminary objection was raised by the Respondent regarding the maintainability of the application, asserting that the Petitioner failed to comply with the procedural requirements set forth in the Maintenance Act. Issues Whether the Petitionerâ€™s application for special leave to appeal to the Supreme Court is maintainable without first seeking leave from the High Court, pursuant to Section 14(2) of the Maintenance Act No. 37 of 1999. Whether the general appellate provisions of the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 19 of 1990 override the specific procedural requirements of the Maintenance Act No. 37 of 1999. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 128 High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 19 of 1990, Section 9 Maintenance Act No. 37 of 1999, Section 14(2) Industrial Disputes Act No. 43 of 1950, Section 31DD(1) Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Maintainability of the appeal Rule applied: Section 14(2) of the Maintenance Act No. 37 of 1999. Court's reasoning: The court interpreted Section 14(2) as establishing a condition precedent for invoking the appellate jurisdiction of the Supreme Court. The statute mandates that an aggrieved party must first seek leave to appeal from the High Court. An application to the Supreme Court is only permissible if the High Court refuses such leave. Since the Petitioner bypassed the High Court and filed directly to the Supreme Court, the mandatory procedural foundation for the court's jurisdiction was absent. Finding: The application is not maintainable as the Petitioner failed to satisfy the condition precedent required by the Maintenance Act. Issue 2: Conflict of laws Rule applied: The maxim generalia specialibus non derogant. Court's reasoning: The court held that the Maintenance Act No. 37 of 1999 is a specific statute enacted after the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 19 of 1990. Under the principle of generalia specialibus non derogant, the specific provisions of the Maintenance Act prevail over the general appellate provisions of the High Court Act. Furthermore, the court contrasted the language of the Maintenance Act with the Industrial Disputes Act, noting that the latter provides an "or" option for seeking leave, whereas the Maintenance Act uses the conjunctive "and," reinforcing the necessity of the two-step process. Finding: The specific procedural requirements of the Maintenance Act take precedence over the general provisions of the High Court Act. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing; the determination that the Maintenance Act is a specific statute (Issue 2) necessitates the strict application of its procedural requirements (Issue 1). Because the Maintenance Act provides a self-contained procedure for appeals, the Petitioner's reliance on general rules or Supreme Court procedural rules was found to be legally insufficient to bypass the statutory condition precedent. Conclusion The preliminary objection is upheld, and the Petitioner's application for special leave to appeal is dismissed. No costs are awarded. Keywords Maintenance Act No.37 — Special leave to appeal — High Court of Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No.19 — Condition precedent — Article 128 of the Constitution — Appeal to the Supreme Court

Parties: Mohammadu and Others v. Samarakoon and Kularatna
Bench: P.A. Ratnayake, J.; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J.; Sathyaa Hettige, PC, J
Keywords: Appeal from District Court — Civil Procedure Code — Notice of appeal — Petition of appeal — Addressing the court — Material prejudice — Technical objections — Cursus curiae est lex curiae
Summary: Facts The Respondent instituted an action in the District Court of Mawanella seeking a declaration of title to land, ejectment of the Appellants, and damages. The Appellants filed an answer denying the averments and seeking a dismissal of the action. The District Court entered judgment in favor of the Respondent. The Appellants subsequently appealed to the Civil Appellate High Court of Kegalle. The Civil Appellate High Court dismissed the appeal on the basis that the Notice of Appeal was invalid because it was not addressed to the original court. The Appellants then filed an application for leave to appeal to the Supreme Court, which was granted on the question of whether the High Court erred in its interpretation of Section 754(3) of the Civil Procedure Code regarding the addressing of a Notice of Appeal. Issues Whether the Notice of Appeal is invalid under Section 754(3) of the Civil Procedure Code if it is not specifically addressed to the original court. Whether the Court of Appeal has the power under Section 759(2) of the Civil Procedure Code to grant relief for a technical defect in the Notice of Appeal where no material prejudice is caused to the respondent. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 4 Civil Procedure Code, Section 754(1) Civil Procedure Code, Section 754(3) Civil Procedure Code, Section 754(4) Civil Procedure Code, Section 755(1) Civil Procedure Code, Section 755(2) Civil Procedure Code, Section 755(3) Civil Procedure Code, Section 755(4) Civil Procedure Code, Section 758(1) Civil Procedure Code, Section 759(2) Cases Referred to: Mahatun Mudali Alias Parantota v N. A. Naposingho - [1986-calr-vol-iii-318] Fernando v Sybil Fernando - [1997/v3-sri-lr-1] Wickremesinghe v De Silva - [1978-79-sllr-vol-2-65] Perera v Perera - [1981-sllr-vol-2-41] Ranin Kumar v Union Assurance Ltd Co - [2003/v2-sri-lr-92] Boyagoda v Mendis - [30-nlr-321] Jayasekara v Lakmini - [Not specified] Elias v Cader - [sc-appeal-50/2008] Analysis Issue 1: Validity of Notice of Appeal Rule applied: Sections 754(3) and 755(1) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the Civil Procedure Code does not explicitly mandate how a Notice of Appeal must be addressed. Section 754(3) only requires that the notice be "lodged" in the original court. The court applied the maxim cursus curiae est lex curiae, noting that for over two centuries, various practices regarding the addressing of such notices have existed without issue. The court held that the original court serves merely as a medium to invoke the appellate jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal. Finding: The High Court erred in law by reading an implicit requirement into the statute that the Notice of Appeal must be addressed to the original court. Issue 2: Relief for technical defects Rule applied: Section 759(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court held that the power to grant relief under Section 759(2) is wide and discretionary. Relief is barred only if the respondent suffers "material prejudice." In this case, the intention to appeal was clear, and the respondent suffered no material prejudice. The court emphasized that technical objections should be discouraged to ensure justice is served on the merits. Finding: Even if the failure to address the notice correctly were considered a defect, it is curable under Section 759(2). Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues reinforce the court's commitment to prioritizing substantive justice over procedural technicalities. The determination that the Notice of Appeal was not inherently invalid (Issue 1) is supported by the court's secondary finding that, even if a defect existed, the court possesses the discretionary power to rectify it (Issue 2), thereby ensuring the appellant's right to appeal is not defeated by formalistic interpretations. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court is set aside, and the High Court is directed to accept the Notice of Appeal and the Petition of Appeal and proceed with the hearing of the appeal on its merits. Keywords Appeal from District Court — Civil Procedure Code — Notice of appeal — Petition of appeal — Addressing the court — Material prejudice — Technical objections — Cursus curiae est lex curiae

Parties: Don Asoka Dayarathne v. A.G. Wijerathna and Others
Bench: Ratnayake, PC, J.; Suresh Chandra, J.; Sathyaa Hettige, PC, J.
Keywords: Civil Procedure Code — Notice of Appeal — Appellate Jurisdiction — Declaration of Title — Procedural Law — Substantive Law — Judicial Discretion — Technical Objections — Right to Appeal — Legal Maxims — Court Practice — Material Prejudice — Judicial Review — Interpretation of Statutes — Intent of Filing — Judicial Efficiency
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal filed by Don Asoka Dayarathne against the judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court of Kegalle, which had dismissed his appeal against a District Court ruling that favored the Respondents, A.G. Wijerathna and others. The Appellant sought a declaration of title over a piece of land, but the District Court ruled in favor of the Respondents. Following this, the Appellant filed a Notice of Appeal and a Petition of Appeal to the Civil Appellate High Court. However, the High Court rejected both on the grounds that the Notice of Appeal was improperly addressed, failing to comply with the requirements of the Civil Procedure Code. The Appellant subsequently sought leave to appeal to the Supreme Court, which was granted on several legal questions regarding the validity of the Notice of Appeal and the interpretation of relevant sections of the Civil Procedure Code. Issues The Supreme Court was tasked with addressing several key issues: Whether the learned Judges of the Civil Appellate High Court failed to consider that the Notice of Appeal had not been addressed to the Civil Appellate High Court of Kegalle? Whether the learned Judges failed to consider the intention behind filing the Notice of Appeal against the judgment in a civil case? Whether the learned Judges misinterpreted sections 754(3) and (4) of the Civil Procedure Code? Did the learned Judges ignore the remedy available under section 759(2) of the Civil Procedure Code? Whether the learned Judges erred in fact and law in this case? Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, sections 754, 755, 759 Cases Referred to: Mahatun Mudali Alias Parantota v N. A. Naposingho and another - [1986] CALR Vol III pg 318; Fernando v Sybil Fernando and Others - [1997] 3 SLR 1; Elias v Cader and another - SC Appeal No 50/2008; Boyagoda v Mendis - [30 NLR 321]. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the learned Judges of the Civil Appellate High Court failed to consider that the Notice of Appeal had not been addressed to the Civil Appellate High Court of Kegalle? Rule applied: Section 754(3) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the procedural requirements for filing a Notice of Appeal as outlined in the Civil Procedure Code. It noted that while the High Court dismissed the appeal based on the improper addressing of the Notice of Appeal, the provisions of the Code did not explicitly mandate that the Notice must be addressed to the original court. The Court emphasized that the intention of the Appellant to appeal was clear from the Notice filed, and that the procedural defect should not bar the Appellant from pursuing his right to appeal. The Court cited the principle that procedural law should not be applied in a manner that denies substantive rights, referencing the maxim 'ubi jus ibi remedium', which underscores the interdependence of substantive and procedural law. The Court also highlighted the importance of judicial discretion in allowing appeals, particularly in cases where no material prejudice was demonstrated against the Respondents. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the High Court erred in dismissing the appeal based on the addressing of the Notice of Appeal, as the intention to appeal was evident and the procedural defect did not warrant such a dismissal. Issue 2: Whether the learned Judges failed to consider the intention behind filing the Notice of Appeal? Rule applied: Judicial interpretation of procedural intent. Court's reasoning: The Court reiterated that the intention of the Appellant in filing the Notice of Appeal was to invoke the appellate jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal. It emphasized that the procedural requirements should be interpreted in a manner that facilitates justice rather than obstructs it. The Court referenced previous judgments that supported the notion that technical objections should not impede the pursuit of justice. The Court also noted that the High Court's failure to consider the intent behind the filing of the Notice of Appeal was a significant oversight that warranted correction. Finding: The Supreme Court found that the High Court's dismissal of the appeal failed to account for the Appellant's clear intention to appeal, which should have been sufficient to allow the appeal to proceed. Issue 3: Whether the learned Judges misinterpreted sections 754(3) and (4) of the Civil Procedure Code? Rule applied: Interpretation of statutory provisions. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court analyzed the relevant sections of the Civil Procedure Code, noting that they did not explicitly require the Notice of Appeal to be addressed to the original court. The Court pointed out that the High Court had misinterpreted these sections by imposing an additional requirement that was not present in the statutory language. The Court emphasized that the procedural law should be applied in a manner consistent with established practices and judicial precedents. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the High Court's interpretation of the Civil Procedure Code was erroneous and that the Notice of Appeal should not have been dismissed on the grounds cited. Issue 4: Did the learned Judges ignore the remedy available under section 759(2) of the Civil Procedure Code? Rule applied: Section 759(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the provisions of section 759(2), which allows for relief in cases of procedural defects if no material prejudice is shown to the Respondents. The Supreme Court noted that the High Court had failed to consider this provision in its judgment, which could have provided a remedy for the Appellant despite the addressing issue. The Court reiterated that the focus should be on the merits of the case rather than on technicalities that do not affect the substantive rights of the parties involved. Finding: The Supreme Court determined that the High Court's failure to consider section 759(2) constituted an error that warranted correction. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in this case are interrelated, as they all stem from the procedural handling of the Notice of Appeal and the interpretation of the relevant sections of the Civil Procedure Code. The Supreme Court's analysis highlighted the importance of judicial discretion and the need to prioritize substantive justice over procedural technicalities. The Court's findings collectively underscored the necessity of allowing the Appellant to pursue his appeal, as the procedural defects identified did not materially prejudice the Respondents. Conclusion The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, directing the Civil Appellate High Court to accept the Notice of Appeal and the Petition of Appeal filed by the Appellant. The Court emphasized that there would be no costs awarded, recognizing the significance of resolving the procedural issues in the interest of justice. The judgment reinforced the principle that technical objections should not obstruct the pursuit of justice and that courts should facilitate access to appellate remedies for aggrieved parties. Keywords Civil Procedure Code — Notice of Appeal — Appellate Jurisdiction — Declaration of Title — Procedural Law — Substantive Law — Judicial Discretion — Technical Objections — Right to Appeal — Legal Maxims — Court Practice — Material Prejudice — Judicial Review — Interpretation of Statutes — Intent of Filing — Judicial Efficiency
Counsel: Sunil Abeyrathne for the Plaintiff – Appellant – Petitioner - Appellant. D.M.G Dissanayake for Defendants – Respondents – Respondents - Respondents.

Parties: Colombo Municipal Council Employeesâ€™ Co-operative Thrift and Savings Society Limited v. Hettiarachchi
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; K. Sripavan, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Appeal from High Court judgment — Co-operative Societies Law — Co-operative Employees Commission Act — Industrial Disputes Act — Labour Tribunal jurisdiction — Section 31 B of the Industrial Disputes Act — Section 39 of the Co-operative Employees Commission Act — Estoppel — Retirement benefits — Misconduct charges
Summary: Facts The appellant is a Co-operative Society incorporated under the Co-operative Societies Law No. 5. The respondent was employed by the appellant as a clerk starting on 02-10-1972. On 22-03-2004, the appellant issued a letter of retirement to the respondent, citing her age and an alleged instance of misconduct. The respondent subsequently appealed this decision to the Co-operative Employees Commission under Section 11 (1) (e) of the Co-operative Employees Commission Act No. 12 of 1972. The parties reached a settlement during the Commission proceedings, resulting in the respondent receiving a certificate of appreciation and retirement benefits. Despite the settlement, the respondent filed an application before the Labour Tribunal seeking relief regarding her termination. The Labour Tribunal ordered the appellant to pay the respondent 24 months' salary as compensation. The appellant challenged this order in the Provincial High Court of the Western Province, which affirmed the Labour Tribunal's decision. The appellant then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the respondent was estopped from filing an application in the Labour Tribunal in view of the provisions in Section 31 B (5) of the Industrial Disputes Act and Section 39 (1) A of the Co-operative Employees Commission Act No. 12 of 1972. Applicable Law Statutes: Co-operative Societies Law No. 5 Co-operative Employees Commission Act No. 12 of 1972 Industrial Disputes Act Termination of Employment of Workmen (Special Provisions) (Amendment) Act No. 51 Cases Referred to: Independent Newspapers Limited v. Commercial and Industrial Workers Union - [1997/v3-sri-lr-197] Analysis Issue 1: Estoppel and Statutory Bar Rule applied: Section 39 (1) of the Co-operative Employees Commission Act (as amended) and Section 31 B (5) of the Industrial Disputes Act. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the statutory framework governing the duplication of actions. Section 39 (1) of the Co-operative Employees Commission Act explicitly prohibits a Labour Tribunal from entertaining an application under Section 31 B of the Industrial Disputes Act if an appeal has already been made to the Commission regarding the same or substantially the same matter. The Court distinguished this from the precedent in Independent Newspapers Limited v. Commercial and Industrial Workers Union, noting that while Section 31 B (5) of the Industrial Disputes Act generally restricts alternative legal remedies, the specific provisions of the Co-operative Employees Commission Act were designed to prevent the duplication of proceedings between the Commission and the Labour Tribunal. The respondent admitted in her Labour Tribunal application that she had already sought relief through the Commission. Consequently, the Labour Tribunal lacked the jurisdiction to entertain her application because she had already invoked the jurisdiction of the Commission. Finding: The respondent was estopped from filing the application in the Labour Tribunal, and the question was answered in the affirmative. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The order of the Labour Tribunal dated 21-02-2007 and the judgment of the High Court dated 22-06-2009 are set aside. No order is made as to costs. Keywords Appeal from High Court judgment — Co-operative Societies Law — Co-operative Employees Commission Act — Industrial Disputes Act — Labour Tribunal jurisdiction — Section 31 B of the Industrial Disputes Act — Section 39 of the Co-operative Employees Commission Act — Estoppel — Retirement benefits — Misconduct charges

Parties: Ferdinandis and Others v. Aviruppola and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; P.A. Ratnayake, PC., J; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Admission to schools — Residency criteria — Circular issued by the Ministry of Education — Lanka Sabhawa Vidyalaya — Deduction of marks — Proximity to school — Article 12(1) of the Constitution
Summary: Facts The petitioners, a minor child and her father, sought admission for the child to Grade I of Visakha Vidyalaya, Colombo 4, for the year 2011 under the category of children of chief occupants residing in close proximity to the school. During the interview process, the petitioners were initially allocated 50 marks and placed on a provisional list. Subsequently, an objection was raised, and the petitioners were called before a Board of Appeals and Objections. Following this, the child's name was removed from the permanent list of selected students. The petitioners complained to the Human Rights Commission, where it was revealed that their marks had been reduced to 45. The respondents explained that 5 marks were deducted because Lanka Sabhawa Vidyalaya was situated in closer proximity to the petitioners' residence than Visakha Vidyalaya. The petitioners contended that Lanka Sabhawa Vidyalaya was in the process of closing and that a new Tamil medium school was opened in its premises, rendering the proximity deduction invalid. The respondents maintained that the deduction was applied in accordance with the Ministry of Education Circular and that the decision to close Lanka Sabhawa Vidyalaya occurred after the interview and appeal process. The petitioners further challenged the allocation of marks regarding their residency status under the same Circular. Issues Whether the deduction of marks based on the proximity of Lanka Sabhawa Vidyalaya to the petitioners' residence violated the petitioners' fundamental right to equality under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the allocation of marks for residency under the Ministry of Education Circular was applied in a discriminatory manner, thereby violating Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1), Article 126. Cases Referred to: Ram Krishna Dalmia v. Justice Tendolkar - [A.I.R. 1958 S.C. 538] Union of India v. Valliappan - [A.I.R. 1999 S.C. 2526] Ashutosh Gupta v. State of Rajasthan - [(2002) 4 S.C.C. 41] Deena v. Union of India - [A.I.R. 1983 S.C. 1154] Analysis Issue 1: Proximity Deduction Rule applied: Clause 6(1)B(iv) of the Ministry of Education Circular regarding proximity to schools. Court's reasoning: The court found that the respondents strictly adhered to the Circular. The decision to close Lanka Sabhawa Vidyalaya was finalized on 27-12-2010, which was after the interviews and the Board of Appeals process. Furthermore, the deduction of 5 marks was applied uniformly to 146 other applicants. The court noted that even if the marks were restored, the cut-off mark for admission would have adjusted accordingly, leaving the petitioner in the same position. Finding: The deduction was not arbitrary or discriminatory and did not violate Article 12(1). Issue 2: Residency Marks Rule applied: Clause 6.1(II) of the Ministry of Education Circular. Court's reasoning: The petitioners challenged the allocation of 4 marks for a registered lease agreement. The court observed that the petitioners were effectively challenging the reasonableness of the Circular itself rather than its application. Under Article 126 of the Constitution, such a challenge must be brought within one month of the issuance of the Circular. The court found no evidence that the petitioners were singled out for hostile treatment. Finding: The respondents correctly applied the criteria set out in the Circular, and the petitioners failed to establish unequal treatment. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues reinforce the conclusion that the respondents acted within the framework of the Ministry of Education Circular. The failure of the petitioners to prove that they were treated differently from similarly circumstanced applicants, combined with the court's finding that the Circular was applied uniformly, confirms that there was no violation of the right to equality. Conclusion The petitioners failed to establish that their fundamental rights guaranteed under Article 12(1) of the Constitution were violated. The application is dismissed with no order as to costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Admission to schools — Residency criteria — Circular issued by the Ministry of Education — Lanka Sabhawa Vidyalaya — Deduction of marks — Proximity to school — Article 12(1) of the Constitution

Parties: Bhashitha Kavirathne and Others v. Pushpakumara and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N.G. Amaratunga, J; K. Sripavan, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Z-Score calculation — Advanced Level Examination — New Syllabus — Old Syllabus — Statistical method — University admission — Administrative action — Legitimate expectation
Summary: Facts The Petitioners, sixteen students who sat for the 2011 General Certificate of Education (Advanced Level) Examination, and the Ceylon Teachers Union, challenged the methodology used by the University Grants Commission to calculate Z-Scores for university admission. In 2011, students sat for the examination under two distinct syllabi: the New Syllabus and the Old Syllabus. Historically, since the introduction of the Z-Score method in 2000, candidates sitting under different syllabi were treated as separate populations for statistical ranking. For the 2011 examination, the respondents applied a common formula to calculate Z-Scores, effectively treating all candidates as a single population. The Petitioners contended that this methodology was arbitrary, irrational, and violated their legitimate expectation that the established practice of treating different syllabi as distinct populations would continue. They argued that the combined mean methodology was statistically erroneous and resulted in a gross distortion of examination results. The Respondents maintained that the examination was a single qualifying event for the 2012 academic year and that they had ensured the difficulty levels between the two syllabi were standardized, thereby justifying a pooled Z-Score approach. Issues Whether the application of a common Z-Score formula to candidates of the New and Old Syllabi violated the Petitioners' fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the classification of New and Old Syllabus candidates as a single population for Z-Score calculation satisfied the requirements of reasonable classification. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Universities Act No. 16 of 1978 Cases Referred to: Rienzie Perera v University Grants Commission - [1978/v1-sri-lr-128] Ramuppilai v Festus Perera - [1991/v1-sri-lr-11] Seneviratne v University Grants Commission - [1978/v1-sri-lr-182] Surendran v University Grants Commission - [1993/v1-sri-lr-344] Charanjit Lal Chowdhury v The Union of India - [1951-air-sc-41] State of Bombay v F.N. Balsara - [1951-air-sc-318] State of West Bengal v Anwar Ali Sarkar - [1952-air-sc-75] Kathi Raning Rawat v State of Saurashtra - [1952-air-sc-123] Lachmandas Kewalram v State of Bombay - [1952-air-sc-235] Qasim Razvi v State of Hyderabad - [1953-air-sc-156] Habeeb Mohamed v State of Hyderabad - [1953-air-sc-287] Budhan Chaudhry v State of Bihar - [1955-air-sc-191] Ram Krishna Dalmia v Justice Tendolkar - [1958-air-sc-538] Dananjanie de Alwis v Anura Edirisinghe - [sc-application-fr-578/2009] R v Criminal Injuries Compensation Board - [1997-slt-291] Allinson v General Medical Council - [1894/v1-qb-750] Ashbridge Investments Ltd. v Minister of Housing and Local Government - [1965/v1-wlr-1320] Coleen Properties Ltd. v Minister of Housing and Local Government - [1971/v1-wlr-433] Chandrasena v Kulatunga - [1996/v2-sri-lr-327] Wicremabandu v Herath - [1990/v2-sri-lr-348] Abeysinghe v Central Engineering and Consultancy Bureau - [1996/v2-sri-lr-36] Harshani S. Siriwardena v Secretary, Ministry of Health and Indigenous Medicine - [sc-application-fr-589/2009] Union of India v Hindustan Development Corporation - [1993/v3-scc-499] R.I.K. de Silva v The University Grants Commission - [2003/v1-sri-lr-261] Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution, which guarantees equality before the law and equal protection of the law. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the University Grants Commission, as a public authority, must act transparently and objectively. The change in the Z-Score calculation method without prior notice or consultation frustrated the legitimate expectation of the students, who relied on the established practice used since 2000. The Court emphasized that the right to education, while not explicitly stated, is protected under Article 12(1). Finding: The failure to maintain a transparent and consistent selection process violated the Petitioners' fundamental rights. Issue 2: Validity of Classification Rule applied: The test for permissible classification: (i) the classification must be founded on an intelligible differentia; and (ii) the differentia must have a rational relation to the object sought to be achieved. Court's reasoning: The Respondents failed to provide sufficient evidence that the New and Old Syllabi were of equal difficulty. Evidence presented by the Intervenient-Respondents showed that candidates under different syllabi had different time allocations and marking schemes for identical questions. Consequently, the "no evidence" rule applied, as the Respondents' claim of standardized difficulty was not supported by the facts. The two groups were distinct populations and could not be treated as a single class. Finding: The classification was arbitrary and failed the test of reasonable classification. Interaction of Issues The finding that the classification was arbitrary (Issue 2) directly supported the conclusion that the Petitioners' rights under Article 12(1) were violated (Issue 1). Because the Respondents failed to justify the grouping of distinct populations, the resulting Z-Score calculation was inherently discriminatory, rendering the administrative action irrational and unconstitutional. Conclusion The Court declared the Z-Scores calculated for the 2011 Advanced Level Examination null and void. The 2nd Respondent is directed to re-calculate the Z-Scores treating the New and Old Syllabi as two distinct populations and to re-issue the results accordingly. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Z-Score calculation — Advanced Level Examination — New Syllabus — Old Syllabus — Statistical method — University admission — Administrative action — Legitimate expectation

Parties: Horana Plantations Limited v. Hon. Anura Kumara Dissanayake and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane J.; Imam J.; Suresh Chandra J.
Keywords: Land Acquisition Act — Writ of Certiorari — Public Purpose — Urgency — Discretion of Minister — Administrative Law — Judicial Review — Flood Relief — Collateral Purpose — Reasonableness
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal by Horana Plantations Limited against the judgment of the Court of Appeal, which dismissed their application for a Writ of Certiorari to quash an order of land acquisition published in the Government Gazette. The acquisition was made under the Land Acquisition Act No. 09 of 1950, specifically citing urgency as the reason for the acquisition. The appellant, a Regional Plantation Company, had leased the Neuchatel Estate for 99 years and had previously had portions of the land acquired for public purposes. The controversy began when the Divisional Secretary of Bulathsinhala proposed to acquire approximately 12 acres of land from the Kokhena Division of the estate to provide alternative housing for flood victims. The appellant objected, offering alternative land from another division, which was rejected by the Divisional Secretary. The appellant argued that the acquisition was unnecessary and lacked urgency, as the land was being taken for a purpose that was not clearly defined in the notice. Issues The court was tasked with addressing two primary issues: 1) Whether a public purpose was clearly manifested in the notice under Section 2 of the Land Acquisition Act? 2) Whether the facts and circumstances warranted an order under clause (a) of the proviso to Section 38 of the Land Acquisition Act on the grounds of urgency? Applicable Law Statutes: Land Acquisition Act No. 09 of 1950 Cases Referred to: Manel Fernando v D.M. Jayaratne, Minister of Agriculture and Lands - [2000 (1) S.L.R. 112]; Katugaha v Minister of Lands and Land Development - [S.C. Appeal 68/2007, S.C. Minutes 23.7.2008]; Marie Indira Fernandopulle and Another v E.L. Senanayake, Minister of Lands, and Agriculture - [79 (II) N.L.R. 115]; Ram Dhari Jindal Memorial Trust Vs. Union of India and Others - [C.A. No. 3813 of 2007]; Padfield v Minister of Agriculture, Fisheries and Food - [1968] AC 997. Analysis Issue 1: Whether a public purpose was clearly manifested in the notice under Section 2 of the Land Acquisition Act? Rule applied: The requirement for a clear public purpose in land acquisition notices as established in Manel Fernando v D.M. Jayaratne. Court's reasoning: The court examined the notice issued under Section 2 of the Land Acquisition Act, which stated that the land was to be acquired for flood victims. However, the court noted that the actual extent of land required for the nine flood-affected families was only 90 perches, while the acquisition sought was for 4.94 hectares (approximately 12 acres). This discrepancy raised doubts about the true purpose of the acquisition. The court highlighted that the notice failed to adequately disclose the collateral purpose of providing land to individuals in other divisions, which was not justified by evidence of their need for relief. The court emphasized that the public purpose must be clearly articulated to justify the acquisition, and the lack of clarity in the notice undermined the legitimacy of the acquisition process. Finding: The court concluded that the public purpose had not been clearly manifested in the notice under Section 2 of the Land Acquisition Act. Issue 2: Whether the facts and circumstances warranted an order under clause (a) of the proviso to Section 38 of the Land Acquisition Act on the grounds of urgency? Rule applied: The burden of establishing urgency lies with the acquiring authority as per the provisions of the Land Acquisition Act. Court's reasoning: The court scrutinized the affidavits submitted by the Minister and the Divisional Secretary, which claimed urgency based on a Cabinet decision to provide relief to flood victims. However, the court found that the affidavits did not satisfactorily establish the urgency required for the acquisition. The court referenced the precedent set in Fernandopulle’s case, which stated that while the Minister has the authority to declare urgency, the courts have a duty to review the justification for such a declaration. The court noted that the affidavits lacked substantive evidence to support the urgency claim, and the burden of proof should not rest on the appellant to demonstrate the absence of urgency. The court further highlighted that the Minister's discretion is not unfettered and must be exercised with care and consideration of relevant factors. Finding: The court determined that the facts and circumstances did not warrant an order under clause (a) of the proviso to Section 38 of the Land Acquisition Act on the grounds of urgency. Interaction of Issues The issues of public purpose and urgency are interrelated, as the legitimacy of the acquisition hinges on the clarity of its stated purpose and the justification for its urgency. The court's findings on both issues indicated that the acquisition was flawed and lacked the necessary legal foundation. Conclusion The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, quashing the order of acquisition and the notices issued under Sections 2 and 4 of the Land Acquisition Act. The court emphasized the importance of transparency and justification in the land acquisition process, particularly when invoking urgency. The judgment underscored the need for public authorities to act within the bounds of the law and to ensure that the rights of individuals are not unduly compromised in the pursuit of public objectives. There shall be no costs.
Counsel: Harsha Soza, PC. with N.R. Sivendran for the Petitioner - Petitioner - Appellant; Ashan Fernando, SC. for the Respondents

Parties: Wedalage Siyaneris and Others v. Dharmathileke and Others
Bench: Marsoof, PC J; R.K.S. Suresh Chandra, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Partition action — Co-ownership — Transfer of rights — Deeds of transfer — Boundaries of land — Identity of parties — Provincial High Court — District Court — Civil Procedure Code
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff initiated a partition action on 22 April 1982 to terminate co-ownership of a land described in the second schedule to the Plaint. The original owner, Wedalage Undia, had three heirs, namely Punchikira, Singho, and Lapaya, each inheriting one-third of the land. The Plaintiff and the first Defendant derived their respective interests from the shares of Punchikira and Singho. The dispute centered on the devolution of the one-third share belonging to Lapaya, who had four heirs: Babanis, Rankira, Siyadoris, and Piyadasa. The fifth Defendant contended that Babanis and Rankira transferred their respective interests to Siyadoris via deeds 5V1 and 5V2, and that Siyadoris subsequently gifted his interest to the fifth Defendant via deed 5V3. The contesting Defendants did not challenge the execution of these deeds but argued that the land described therein was not the subject matter of the partition action. The District Court concluded that the deeds related to the corpus and allocated shares accordingly. The Provincial High Court (Civil Appeal) subsequently varied this judgment, holding that Babanis had transferred his interest to an unknown party named Sidorisa, rather than Siyadoris, and consequently awarded shares to the heirs of Babanis. The fifth Defendant appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Provincial High Court misdirected itself in law by concluding that the transferee "Sidorisa" in deed 5V1 was a different person from "Siyadoris" in the absence of evidence or a dispute regarding identity at the trial. Whether the District Court failed to comply with Section 187 of the Civil Procedure Code by allegedly failing to answer issue number 25. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 187 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Identity of the Transferee Rule applied: The court applied the principle that a trial courtâ€™s findings on evidence should not be disturbed when the identity of a party was not contested during the trial. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court observed that the identity of Siyadoris was never challenged during the District Court trial, and the proceedings were conducted on the mutual understanding that Sidorisa and Siyadoris referred to the same individual. The High Court erred by introducing a new theory regarding the identity of the transferee that was not supported by evidence or raised by the parties. Furthermore, the High Courtâ€™s decision to allocate shares to the heirs of Babanis based on the assumption that the transferee was an unknown person was legally inconsistent; if the transferee were truly unknown, the shares should have remained unallotted rather than being awarded to the heirs of the transferor. Finding: The High Court misdirected itself; the District Court correctly identified the parties and the devolution of title based on the evidence presented. Issue 2: Compliance with Section 187 of the Civil Procedure Code Rule applied: Section 187 of the Civil Procedure Code, which requires the court to state its findings on each issue. Court's reasoning: The High Court held that the District Court failed to answer issue number 25. Upon review, the Supreme Court found that issue number 11 and issue number 25 were identical in substance. Since the District Court had provided a clear finding on issue number 11, the omission to formally label the answer as issue number 25 did not result in any prejudice to the parties or a failure of justice. Finding: The District Courtâ€™s judgment was in substantial compliance with the law, and the High Courtâ€™s finding of procedural error was incorrect. Interaction of Issues The resolution of the identity issue directly impacted the share allocation, as the High Courtâ€™s erroneous finding regarding the transfereeâ€™s identity necessitated an incorrect redistribution of property shares. The procedural issue regarding Section 187 was secondary, as the High Court used it to justify setting aside a judgment that was substantively correct. By correcting the error regarding the identity of the transferee, the Supreme Court restored the original share allocation, rendering the High Court's procedural critique moot. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgment of the Provincial High Court (Civil Appeal) of Sabaragamuwa is set aside, and the judgment of the District Court of Kegalle dated 15 July 2005 is affirmed. Keywords Partition action — Co-ownership — Transfer of rights — Deeds of transfer — Boundaries of land — Identity of parties — Provincial High Court — District Court — Civil Procedure Code

Parties: Phoenix Ventures Limited v. Superior Palayakat (Private) Limited
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J.; Sripavan, J.; Suresh Chandra, J.
Keywords: Interim Injunction — Specific Performance — Mortgage Bond — Sale and Purchase Agreements — Prima Facie Case — Balance of Convenience — Irreparable Loss — Commercial High Court — Legal Rights — Recovery of Monies
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a dispute between Phoenix Ventures Limited (the Plaintiff) and Superior Palayakat (Private) Limited (the Defendant) regarding the sale of 17 apartments in a condominium project. The Defendant, having acquired ownership of the property through Deeds of Transfer, intended to develop a condominium named 'Palais Maharaj'. The Plaintiff agreed to purchase 17 apartments for a total of Rs.119 million, paying Rs.86,700,000 upfront. The Sale and Purchase Agreements included a clause allowing the Plaintiff to seek specific performance or terminate the agreements if the Defendant failed to deliver possession of the apartments. After granting an extension for completion, the Defendant failed to meet the deadline, prompting the Plaintiff to terminate the agreements and demand possession. The Defendant's refusal led the Plaintiff to file an action in the Commercial High Court, which granted an interim injunction preventing the Defendant from placing anyone in possession of the property. The Defendant appealed this decision, questioning the validity of the injunction. Issues Whether the Interim Injunction issued by the Commercial High Court was granted according to established legal principles. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Mortgage Act Cases Referred to: Felix Dias Bandaranayake v. The State Film Corporation and another - [1981(2) SLR 287] Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Interim Injunction was justified. Rule applied: The principles for granting an interim injunction as established in Felix Dias Bandaranayake v. The State Film Corporation. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the criteria for granting an interim injunction, which includes establishing a strong prima facie case, assessing the balance of convenience, and considering the conduct of the parties. The Plaintiff demonstrated a prima facie case by showing that the Defendant had failed to honor the Sale and Purchase Agreements and had executed a mortgage bond as security for the repayment of Rs.170 million in case of breach. The Court noted that the Plaintiff's claim was not merely for monetary recovery but also to secure their rights regarding the mortgaged property.
