The Defendant's argument that the claim was solely for money and thus did not warrant an injunction was dismissed. The Court emphasized that the Plaintiff's interest in preventing the property from being transferred to third parties was legitimate and necessary to protect their rights. The Defendant's assertion that the balance of convenience favored them was also rejected. The Court highlighted that the Defendant had not completed the construction of the apartments and that the injunction did not prevent them from continuing development. The Commercial High Court's conclusion that the balance of convenience lay with the Plaintiff was upheld, as the injunction aimed to protect the Plaintiff's interests in the ongoing dispute. Finding: The Court affirmed the Commercial High Court's decision to grant the interim injunction, concluding that it was justified based on the established legal principles. Interaction of Issues The issues are interconnected as the justification for the interim injunction hinges on the Plaintiff's ability to establish a prima facie case and the balance of convenience. The Court's analysis of these factors demonstrated that the Plaintiff's rights were at risk, necessitating the injunction to prevent irreparable harm. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the Defendant's appeal, affirming the Commercial High Court's order regarding the interim injunction. The Court found that the injunction was appropriately granted to protect the Plaintiff's interests in the property and to ensure that their rights under the Sale and Purchase Agreements were not compromised. In light of the circumstances, no order was made regarding costs. Keywords Interim Injunction — Specific Performance — Mortgage Bond — Sale and Purchase Agreements — Prima Facie Case — Balance of Convenience — Irreparable Loss — Commercial High Court — Legal Rights — Recovery of Monies
Counsel: Romesh De Silva PC with Geethaka Gunewardena for the Defendant-Appellant. Kushan De Alwis with Manoj Bandara for the Plaintiff–Respondent.

Parties: Omatte Thilakaratne Mudiyanse Ralahamillage Wilfred Bandara Kalawana v. Sirisena Nawalage and O.T.M.R.U. Bandara Kalawana
Bench: Dr. Shirani A. Bandaranayake, CJ.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Priyasath Dep, P.C., J.
Keywords: rei vindicatio — ownership — possession — title — compensation — co-ownership — legal principles — appeal — judgment — evidence — improvements — lawful possession — rights — land dispute — inheritance
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a land dispute involving Omatte Thilakaratne Mudiyanse Ralahamillage Wilfred Bandara Kalawana (the Plaintiff) and Sirisena Nawalage and O.T.M.R.U. Bandara Kalawana (the Defendants). The Plaintiff initiated legal action in the District Court of Ratnapura on 23 February 1988, seeking to declare himself the lawful owner of a 10-acre land parcel located in Kalawana, which he claimed was rightfully inherited from his ancestors. The Plaintiff's claim was based on a series of deeds that traced the title back to his grandfather, O.T.M.R. Tilakaratne Bandara Kalawana, who had gifted the land to his wife in 1943. The Defendants contested the claim, asserting that the land belonged to a larger co-owned estate known as Kalawana Nindagama, and that they had purchased the land from other co-owners after the Plaintiff had initiated his action. The District Court dismissed the Plaintiff's action, leading to an appeal to the High Court of Ratnapura, which overturned the District Court's decision and ruled in favor of the Plaintiff. The Defendants subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court, raising several legal questions regarding the High Court's judgment. Issues Whether the High Court failed to give effect to previous Supreme Court decisions regarding the burden of proof in rei vindicatio actions. Whether the High Court erred in not considering the Defendants' right to compensation for improvements made to the land. Whether the High Court erred in stating that the Defendants should have appealed the District Court's failure to adjudicate on their rights. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code Cases Referred to: De Silva v. Gunathilake - [32 NLR 217]; Muthusamy v. Seneviratne - [31 CLW 91]; Wanigaratne v. Juwanis Appuhamy - [65 NLR 167]; Harriet v. Padmasiri - [1996 1 SLR 358]; Fernando v. Fernando and Others - [2006 BLR 28]; Talagune v. De Livera - [1997 1SLR 253]; Dharmadasa v. Jayasena - [1997 3 SLR 327]. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the High Court failed to give effect to the decision in De Silva v. Gunathilake, Muthusamy v. Seneviratne, and others. Rule applied: The principle of rei vindicatio requires the Plaintiff to prove ownership of the property in question. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the High Court's judgment, noting that the Plaintiff had produced four deeds (P1-P4) establishing a clear chain of title to the land since 1943. The Court emphasized that the identity of the land was not in dispute and that the Plaintiff's predecessors had possessed the land until the death of his father in 1984. The Court highlighted that the Defendants' claims of ownership were based on a deed executed after the Plaintiff's action was initiated, which could not be considered valid under the law. The Supreme Court reiterated that the burden of proof lies with the Plaintiff to establish his title, and in this case, the Plaintiff had successfully done so. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the High Court did not err in its judgment and that the Plaintiff had established his title to the land. Issue 2: Whether the High Court erred in failing to consider the Defendants' right to compensation for improvements. Rule applied: The principle of jus retentionis, which allows a possessor to retain possession until compensated for improvements made. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court noted that the Defendants had entered the land unlawfully and were not bona fide possessors. The Court stated that since the Defendants had occupied the land unlawfully for over two decades, they were not entitled to compensation for any improvements made. The Court emphasized that the Defendants had taken the risk of making improvements without lawful possession and thus could not claim compensation. Finding: The Supreme Court affirmed that the Defendants were not entitled to compensation for improvements made to the land. Issue 3: Whether the High Court erred in stating that the Defendants should have appealed the District Court's failure to adjudicate on their rights. Rule applied: The necessity of appealing a judgment when rights are not adjudicated. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court clarified that since the District Court had dismissed the Plaintiff's action, there was no need for the Defendants to appeal regarding compensation, as they were not ejected from the land. The Court found that the High Court's statement on this point was erroneous but did not affect the overall judgment. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the High Court's finding on this point did not impact the outcome of the case. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in this case are interrelated, as they all stem from the central question of ownership and the rights of the parties involved. The determination of whether the Plaintiff established his title directly impacts the question of compensation for the Defendants, as their claims are contingent upon their lawful possession of the land. The Supreme Court's analysis of the burden of proof in rei vindicatio actions is crucial in understanding the legal framework governing property disputes in Sri Lanka. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal filed by the Defendants, affirming the High Court's judgment in favor of the Plaintiff. The Court ordered the Defendants to pay costs to the Plaintiff, thereby reinforcing the Plaintiff's rightful ownership of the land in question. The judgment serves as a significant precedent in property law, particularly concerning the principles of ownership, possession, and the rights of co-owners in Sri Lanka. Keywords rei vindicatio — ownership — possession — title — compensation — co-ownership — legal principles — appeal — judgment — evidence — improvements — lawful possession — rights — land dispute — inheritance
Counsel: Gamini Marapana PC with Navin Marapana and Nishanthi Mendis for the 1st Defendant – Respondent – Petitioner-Appellant. Chandana Premathilake with Niluka Dissanayake for the Plaintiff - Appellant- Respondent – Respondent.

Parties: Nazeem v. Gunasekera and Others
Bench: Shiranee Tilakawardene, J; K. Sripavan, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Residency requirements — School admissions — Site inspections — Administrative action — Circular No. 18/2011 — Proof of residency — Arbitrariness
Summary: Facts Mohamed Azvin Nazeem submitted an application for the admission of his son, Mohamed Uzman Nazeem, to Grade One at Royal College, Colombo, for the year 2012 under the category of children of residents in close proximity to the school. The selection process was governed by Circular No. 18/2011 issued by the Ministry of Education. Although the petitionerâ€™s son was awarded 69 marks, which exceeded the cut-off mark of 52, his name was omitted from the provisional and final admission lists. Upon appealing the decision, the petitioner was informed by the Appeals and Protests Board that his son was not selected because the petitioner failed to establish his residency at the address provided during spot inspections conducted by school authorities. The respondents maintained that site inspections were mandatory under Clause 8.3(c) of Circular No. 18/2011. Evidence presented to the court indicated that multiple site inspections conducted by Royal College and D.S. Senanayake College failed to confirm the petitioner's residency at the stated address. The petitioner subsequently filed a fundamental rights application alleging that the omission was arbitrary, capricious, and in violation of Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Issues Whether the decision to exclude the petitionerâ€™s son from the admission list due to a failure to confirm residency during spot inspections violated the petitioner's fundamental right to equality under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the court should consider additional documents submitted by the petitioner after the filing of the petition to prove residency. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Constitution of Sri Lanka, Article 17 Constitution of Sri Lanka, Article 126 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Clause 8.3(c) of Circular No. 18/2011, which mandates spot inspections to confirm residency for applicants in the "close proximity" category. Court's reasoning: The court found that the respondents acted in accordance with the established circular. The contemporaneous notes from multiple site inspection teams across two different schools consistently indicated that the petitioner was not residing at the provided address. The court held that it is bound by these official inspection notes absent evidence of mala fides or arbitrariness. The petitioner failed to demonstrate that the inspection teams acted unfairly, and the court noted that the petitioner himself had previously characterized the omission as a "genuine mistake" rather than an act of bad faith. Finding: The petitioner failed to establish that his fundamental rights under Article 12(1) were infringed, as he did not meet the residency requirements verified through the mandatory inspection process. Issue 2: Admissibility of additional documents Rule applied: The selection criteria and marking scheme established in Circular No. 18/2011. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that the selection of students must be based on the documents furnished at the time of the interview, the veracity of which is then verified by site inspections. Allowing the submission of new documents after the petition was filed would undermine the integrity of the selection process and the verification mechanism. Finding: The court refused to consider the additional documents tendered by the petitioner after the commencement of the legal proceedings. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing; the court's determination that the residency requirement was a mandatory, verified condition (Issue 1) directly informs the decision to exclude late-filed evidence (Issue 2). Because the residency status was a factual condition precedent to admission, the failure to prove it at the designated time rendered subsequent attempts to provide documentation irrelevant to the legality of the initial administrative decision. Conclusion The application is dismissed. There will be no costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Residency requirements — School admissions — Site inspections — Administrative action — Circular No. 18/2011 — Proof of residency — Arbitrariness

Parties: Asian Hotels & Properties PLC v. Benjamin and Others
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Appeal from Court of Appeal — Industrial Disputes Act — Arbitration proceedings — Domestic inquiry proceedings — Marking of evidence — Audi alteram partem rule — Natural justice — Just and equitable relief — Evidence Ordinance — Role of the Arbitrator
Summary: Facts The first respondent was employed by a subsidiary of the appellant, Asian Hotels & Properties PLC, as a Manager of Apartments Leasing and Rentals. Following a domestic inquiry, the appellant terminated the services of the first respondent. The third respondent subsequently referred the dispute regarding the justification of this termination and the entitlement to an annual bonus to the fourth respondent for arbitration. During the arbitration proceedings, the first respondent did not attend any sittings, reportedly due to medical treatment abroad. The appellant sought to produce the record of the domestic inquiry proceedings through the testimony of Mr. F.N. de Silva, a retired President of the Labour Tribunal who had conducted the inquiry. Although the first respondent initially raised no objection, he later opposed the production of these proceedings, citing the audi alteram partem rule. The fourth respondent upheld this objection, ruling that the domestic inquiry proceedings could not be marked until the witnesses who testified at that inquiry were called to testify before the arbitrator. The appellant challenged this order in the Court of Appeal, which dismissed the application. The appellant then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the domestic inquiry proceedings should be allowed to be marked in arbitration and Labour Tribunal proceedings irrespective of whether the witnesses who testified at the domestic inquiry are summoned to give evidence. Whether the refusal of the arbitrator to mark the domestic inquiry proceedings constituted a violation of the rules of natural justice. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act No. 43 of 1950 (as amended) Evidence Ordinance Cases Referred to: Kalamazoo Industries Ltd v. Minister of Labour and Vocational Training - [1998/v1-sri-lr-235] The Bharat Bank Ltd. v. The Employees of the Bharat Bank Ltd. - [A.I.R. 1950 S.C. 188] United Engineering Workers Union v. K.W. Devanayagan - [69-nlr-289] Daniel v. Rickett, Cockrell and Co. - [1938 2 K.B. 322] The Ceylon Workers Congress v. The Superintendent, Kallebokka Estate - [Not specified] The Batticaloa Multi-Purpose Co-operative Societies Union Ltd v. V. Velupillai - [76-nlr-60] Analysis Issue 1: Admissibility of domestic inquiry proceedings Rule applied: Section 17(1) of the Industrial Disputes Act and the principle that Labour Tribunals and arbitrators are not bound by the strict rules of the Evidence Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The Court noted that arbitrators and Labour Tribunals are tasked with providing just and equitable relief and are not bound by the rigid rules of evidence. Relying on The Batticaloa Multi-Purpose Co-operative Societies Union Ltd v. V. Velupillai, the Court held that a tribunal may act on evidence led at a domestic inquiry provided it is satisfied that the evidence was properly recorded and the workman had a fair opportunity to meet the allegations. The Court found that the arbitrator has an unfettered discretion to admit such material to determine what is just and equitable. Finding: Domestic inquiry proceedings may be marked and admitted in arbitration proceedings without the mandatory requirement of summoning all witnesses who testified at the domestic inquiry. Issue 2: Violation of natural justice Rule applied: The principles of natural justice, specifically the audi alteram partem rule. Court's reasoning: The Court reasoned that the arbitratorâ€™s refusal to mark the proceedings on the basis that witnesses were not summoned was an artificial restriction that hindered the adjudication process. Since the appellant was required to justify the termination, the domestic inquiry record was essential evidence. The Court observed that the first respondent had the opportunity to call any witness he deemed necessary for his case. By preventing the marking of the record, the arbitrator failed to exercise his discretion properly, thereby violating the principles of natural justice. Finding: The arbitrator's refusal to mark the domestic inquiry proceedings was incorrect and constituted a violation of the rules of natural justice. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. By establishing that the arbitrator has the discretion to admit domestic inquiry records under the Industrial Disputes Act (Issue 1), the Court clarified that the arbitratorâ€™s refusal to do so was not merely a procedural error but a failure to fulfill the duty to provide just and equitable relief, which is a core requirement of natural justice (Issue 2). Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgment of the Court of Appeal dated 02-07-2010 and the order of the arbitrator dated 14-11-2008 are set aside. Keywords Appeal from Court of Appeal — Industrial Disputes Act — Arbitration proceedings — Domestic inquiry proceedings — Marking of evidence — Audi alteram partem rule — Natural justice — Just and equitable relief — Evidence Ordinance — Role of the Arbitrator

Parties: M.D.Gunasena & Co. Ltd. v. Somaratne Gamage
Bench: Shiranee Tilakawardana, J; S.I. Imam, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Industrial Disputes Act — Termination of employment — Reinstatement — Compensation — Misconduct — Domestic inquiry — Evidence evaluation — Just and equitable order — Back wages — Retirement age
Summary: Facts The Applicant-Respondent-Respondent was employed as the Manager of the Horana Branch of the Employer-Appellant-Appellant starting from December 27, 1994. His services were suspended on September 18, 2000, and subsequently terminated effective from that date by a letter dated November 24, 2001. The employer alleged that the termination was justified following a domestic inquiry where the Applicant was found guilty of misappropriating company funds by failing to pay exhibition sales commissions to the company and to a sales promotion officer. The Applicant challenged the termination in the Labour Tribunal of Panadura under Section 31B of the Industrial Disputes Act. During the tribunal proceedings, the employer called the sales promotion officer to testify, but the witness passed away before his cross-examination was concluded. The Labour Tribunal President overruled an objection to expunge this evidence. Ultimately, the Labour Tribunal held that the Applicant was not guilty of misconduct, noting that the funds in question were deposited in the company account and that the Applicant had acted prudently by withholding payments due to the officer's failure to return company property. The tribunal ordered reinstatement and one year's salary as compensation. The employer appealed to the Provincial High Court of Kalutara, which affirmed the order of reinstatement. Given that the Applicant had only one year of service remaining until retirement at the time of the High Court judgment, the court ordered the employer to pay four years' salary as compensation or alternatively reinstate the Applicant for four years. The employer then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the orders of the Labour Tribunal and the High Court were vitiated by failing to be "just and equitable" under the Industrial Disputes Act, particularly regarding the relief granted. Whether the Labour Tribunal and the High Court failed to judicially evaluate the evidence led at the inquiry. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act Cases Referred to: Pearce v. Foster - [(1886)17QBD 536] Shalimar Rope Works Mazdoor Union v. Shalimar Rope Works Ltd. - [1953(2) LLJ 876] Laws v. London Chronical Ltd. - [1959-2 ALL ER 285] Clouston and Co. Ltd. v. Cory - [1906 AC 122] Sharda Prasad Tiwari and others v. Divisional Superintendent, Central Railway, Nagpur Division - [1961 AIR Bombay 150] Analysis Issue 1: Just and Equitable Relief Rule applied: The Industrial Disputes Act, which mandates that orders must be "just and equitable." Court's reasoning: The Applicant had specifically prayed for reinstatement and back wages, or in the alternative, adequate compensation. The court found that the Labour Tribunal's order for reinstatement and compensation was within its statutory powers. Because the termination was found to be unjust and the Applicant was out of work since 2000, the High Court's order for four years' salary as compensation was a reasonable exercise of discretion to provide a just and equitable remedy, especially since the Applicant could have served that duration had he been reinstated earlier. Finding: The orders were not vitiated and were consistent with the requirement to be just and equitable. Issue 2: Judicial Evaluation of Evidence Rule applied: The test for misconduct as established in Pearce v. Foster and Laws v. London Chronical Ltd., requiring that misconduct be inconsistent with the fulfillment of the express or implied conditions of service. Court's reasoning: The court evaluated the evidence regarding the withheld commission. It found that the Applicant deposited the money into the company account rather than misappropriating it. The decision to withhold payment from the sales promotion officer was a prudent financial measure taken because the officer had failed to return company books. The court concluded that this conduct did not constitute misconduct, let alone the serious misconduct required to justify dismissal. Finding: The lower courts correctly evaluated the evidence, and the Applicant was not guilty of misconduct. Interaction of Issues The findings on the evidence (Issue 2) directly supported the conclusion on the relief granted (Issue 1). Because the court determined that the termination was not justified by any misconduct, the employer's argument that the relief was not "just and equitable" failed, as the Applicant was entitled to compensation for the unlawful termination of his services. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The judgment of the Provincial High Court is affirmed, subject to the variation that the alternative relief of four years of employment is set aside due to the strained relations and the Applicant having passed retirement age. The employer is ordered to pay four years' salary as compensation and costs of Rs. 75,000. Keywords Industrial Disputes Act — Termination of employment — Reinstatement — Compensation — Misconduct — Domestic inquiry — Evidence evaluation — Just and equitable order — Back wages — Retirement age

Parties: Ceylon Quartz Industries (Private) Limited v. The Director General of Customs and H.M.H.B. Seneviratne and Attorney General
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; N.G. Amaratunga, J; P.A. Ratnayake, PC., J
Keywords: Agreement X8 — Customs duty — Export control — BOI status — Silica Quartz export — Investment agreements — Role of the BOI — Arbitration — Schedule B of Law No.4 — Verification of goods
Summary: Facts The Appellant, Ceylon Quartz Industries (Private) Limited, was incorporated to mine and process Silica Quartz for export. The Appellant entered into an agreement with the Board of Investment (BOI) under Section 17 of the Greater Colombo Economic Commission Law No. 4 of 1978, which granted the enterprise specific exemptions from export duties and customs controls. The Appellant subsequently attempted to export a consignment of semi-processed Silica Quartz. The Respondents, the Director General of Customs and an Assistant Director of Customs, inspected the consignment and determined that the goods were subject to export duty because they did not conform to the specific form of processed Silica Powder permitted under the agreement. The Respondents initiated an inquiry into the matter and required the Appellant to provide a bank guarantee before the consignment could be exported. The Appellant challenged this action by filing a writ application in the Court of Appeal, seeking writs of prohibition to stop the Customs inquiry and certiorari to quash the order requiring a bank guarantee. The Court of Appeal dismissed the application, leading to the present appeal. Issues Whether the Customs authority has the power to interpret the nature of goods permitted for export under the terms of the BOI Agreement (X8). Whether the power of the Customs authority is restricted to verifying whether the goods exported conform to the goods declared by the exporter. Applicable Law Statutes: Greater Colombo Economic Commission Law No. 4 of 1978 Customs Ordinance Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Interpretation of goods under the BOI Agreement Rule applied: Clause 10(ix) of the BOI Agreement (X8) and Section 17 of the Greater Colombo Economic Commission Law No. 4 of 1978. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that the BOI was established as a central facilitation point for investors, granting them specific concessions, including exemptions from customs and export controls. Clause 10(ix) of the agreement explicitly states that goods produced by the enterprise may be exported free of export duty and customs control. While the agreement allows governmental authorities to examine the correctness of declarations, such power must be exercised in the manner directed by the BOI. Evidence showed that the BOI had not directed the Customs to conduct an inquiry into the nature of the goods. Therefore, the Customs authority lacked the jurisdiction to unilaterally interpret the agreement or the nature of the goods permitted thereunder. Finding: The Customs cannot interpret the nature of the goods that can be exported under and in terms of the Agreement X8. Issue 2: Scope of Customs authority Rule applied: Clause 10(ix) of the BOI Agreement (X8). Court's reasoning: The court determined that the role of the Customs authority is limited to verifying the physical conformity of the exported goods to the declaration made by the exporter. Because the BOI Agreement provides a comprehensive framework for the enterprise's operations and includes an arbitration clause for disputes, the Customs authority cannot bypass this framework to impose duties or penalties based on its own interpretation of the agreement. Any dispute regarding the nature of the goods or compliance with the agreement must be handled through the mechanisms established by the BOI or via arbitration. Finding: The power of the Customs is restricted to verifying whether the goods exported conform to the goods said to be exported by exporters. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are mutually reinforcing. By establishing that the Customs authority lacks the power to interpret the BOI Agreement, the court necessarily limits the scope of the Customs' authority to mere verification. This prevents the Customs from overstepping its role and ensures that the special concessions granted to BOI-approved enterprises are not undermined by unauthorized administrative inquiries. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the judgment of the Court of Appeal is set aside. The court grants a writ of prohibition restraining the Respondents from continuing the inquiry and a writ of certiorari quashing the order requiring the Appellant to provide a bank guarantee. Keywords Agreement X8 — Customs duty — Export control — BOI status — Silica Quartz export — Investment agreements — Role of the BOI — Arbitration — Schedule B of Law No.4 — Verification of goods

Parties: K. Dharmasiriwardena v. Pure Beverages Company Ltd.
Bench: Shiranee Tilakawardena, J.; K. Sripavan, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Exclusive Distributor Agreement — Termination — Notice Requirement — Clause Interpretation — Damages — Procedural Irregularity — Contractual Obligations — Legal Precedents — Fairness in Administration — Intent of Parties
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a dispute between K. Dharmasiriwardena (the Plaintiff-Respondent) and Pure Beverages Company Ltd. (the Defendant-Appellant) regarding the termination of an Exclusive Distributor Agreement (the Agreement). The Plaintiff instituted action against the Defendant on 26 February 1999, claiming damages amounting to Rs. 3.5 Million, asserting that the Defendant unlawfully terminated the Agreement effective from 8 December 1995, via a notice dated 8 November 1995. The Plaintiff contended that the notice did not provide the requisite one month's notice as stipulated in Clause 6 of the Agreement, as it was received only a few days before the termination date. Furthermore, the Plaintiff argued that the notice failed to specify the reasons for termination, which he believed rendered the termination invalid. The Defendant, on the other hand, maintained that the Plaintiff's objections were overly technical and did not substantively affect the validity of the notice. The Defendant also argued that it had the right to terminate the Agreement under Clause 8 due to the Plaintiff's failure to maintain satisfactory distribution and for actions detrimental to the Defendant's interests. The Commercial High Court initially ruled in favor of the Plaintiff, awarding damages and interest. However, the Defendant appealed this decision. Issues The primary issues before the Supreme Court were: Whether the notice of termination issued by the Defendant was valid under Clause 6 of the Agreement. Whether the Plaintiff was entitled to damages despite the termination being deemed valid. Applicable Law Statutes: The relevant statutes include the principles of contract law as applied in Sri Lanka. Cases Referred to: The judgment referenced several cases, including Re D.S.E.F.R. Senanayake 75 N.L.R. 215 and Union of India vs. Komal Chand (1966, M.P.L.J. Notes, page 150). Analysis Issue 1: Validity of Notice Rule applied: Clause 6 of the Exclusive Distributor Agreement Court's reasoning: The Court examined the validity of the notice of termination issued by the Defendant under Clause 6 of the Agreement. It noted that Clause 6 required one month's written notice for termination, and the Plaintiff argued that the notice was not valid as it did not provide sufficient time. However, the Court emphasized that the notice was sent via registered post to the Plaintiff's address, thus fulfilling the requirements of Clause 13 of the Agreement, which stated that notice would be deemed sufficient if sent to the respective addresses. The Court highlighted that the Plaintiff did not return the notice or object to its validity at the time of receipt, indicating acceptance of the notice. The Court also pointed out that the Plaintiff's subsequent correspondence did not contest the notice's validity, further reinforcing the notion that the Plaintiff accepted the notice as valid. The Court concluded that the notice was indeed valid, as it complied with the procedural requirements set forth in the Agreement. Finding: The notice of termination was valid under Clause 6 of the Agreement. Issue 2: Entitlement to Damages Rule applied: Clause 10 of the Exclusive Distributor Agreement Court's reasoning: The Court then turned to the issue of whether the Plaintiff was entitled to damages following the valid termination of the Agreement. It referenced Clause 10, which explicitly stated that if the Agreement was terminated under Clause 6 or 8, the Company would not be liable to pay any damages or compensation to the Distributor. The Court emphasized the importance of the parties' intentions as expressed in the Agreement. It noted that the language of Clause 10 was clear and unambiguous, indicating that the parties had agreed that no damages would be payable upon termination. The Court further stated that the Plaintiff had acknowledged this provision during the proceedings, which reinforced the Defendant's position. The Court concluded that the Plaintiff was not entitled to any damages due to the explicit terms of the Agreement. Finding: The Plaintiff was not entitled to damages following the termination of the Agreement. Interaction of Issues The issues of the validity of the notice and the entitlement to damages were closely intertwined. The determination that the notice was valid directly influenced the conclusion regarding the Plaintiff's entitlement to damages. Since the notice was deemed valid, the subsequent claim for damages was rendered moot by the explicit terms of the Agreement, which precluded any compensation upon termination. Conclusion The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, set aside the judgment of the Commercial High Court, and dismissed the Plaintiff's action. The Court did not order costs, considering the circumstances of the case. The judgment underscored the importance of adhering to the terms of contractual agreements and the necessity for parties to act within the framework established by their contracts. Keywords Exclusive Distributor Agreement — Termination — Notice Requirement — Clause Interpretation — Damages — Procedural Irregularity — Contractual Obligations — Legal Precedents — Fairness in Administration — Intent of Parties
Counsel: Chandana Premathilake for the Defendant-Appellant; Lakshman Perera with Upendra Walgampaya for the Plaintiff-Respondent.

Parties: Karunawathie Wickramasinghe Samaranayake v. Ranjani Warnakulasuriya
Bench: N.G. Amaratunga, J.; Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J.; C. Ekanayake, J.
Keywords: Leave to Appeal — Time Limit — High Court of Provinces — Special Provisions Act — Supreme Court Rules — Reasonableness — Jurisdiction — Civil Matters — Appeal Process — Mandatory Compliance — Legislative Intent
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for leave to appeal from a judgment delivered by the High Court of the Central Province in Kandy on 15 March 2010. The Plaintiff, Karunawathie Wickramasinghe Samaranayake, had initially filed a case in the District Court of Nuwara Eliya seeking recovery of possession of state land held under an annual permit and for damages. The District Court dismissed her action, prompting her to appeal to the High Court, which subsequently overturned the District Court's decision and granted relief to the Plaintiff. The Defendant, Ranjani Warnakulasuriya, sought leave to appeal this High Court judgment, claiming that the application was lodged out of time, specifically beyond the six-week limit set by the Supreme Court Rules. The core issue was whether the application for leave to appeal was time-barred, as the Defendant's petition was filed 56 days after the High Court's judgment. Issues The primary issues framed in this case are: Whether the application for leave to appeal was filed within the stipulated time frame as per the Supreme Court Rules? Whether the absence of an explicit time limit in the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) (Amendment) Act No. 54 of 2006 affects the applicability of the Supreme Court Rules? Applicable Law Statutes: High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) (Amendment) Act No. 54 of 2006; Supreme Court Rules, 1990; Article 154P of the Constitution. Cases Referred to: L.A. Sudath Rohana and Another v. Mohamed Zeena - [S.C. H.C. C.A. L.A. No.111/2010]; Tea Small Factories Ltd. v. Weragoda - [1994 (3) SLR 353]; Mahaweli Authority of Sri Lanka v. United Agency Construction (Pvt) Ltd - [2002 1 SLR 8]; George Stuart & Company Limited v. Lankem Tea and Rubber Plantation Ltd. - [2004 1 SLR 246]; Jamburegoda Gamage Lakshman Jinadasa v. Pilitthu Wasam Gallage Pathma Hemamali and Others - [S.C. H.C. C.A. L.A. No.99/2008]. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the application for leave to appeal was filed within the stipulated time frame as per the Supreme Court Rules? Rule applied: Supreme Court Rules, 1990, Rule 7. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the timeline of events leading to the application for leave to appeal. The judgment of the High Court was delivered on 15 March 2010, and the application for leave to appeal was filed on 10 May 2010, which was 56 days later. The Respondent's counsel raised a preliminary objection, asserting that the application was time-barred as it exceeded the six-week limit specified in Rule 7 of the Supreme Court Rules. The Court noted that while the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) (Amendment) Act did not explicitly impose a time limit for filing an application for leave to appeal, the Supreme Court Rules provided a clear framework for such applications. The Court emphasized that the legislative intent behind the Act sought to equate the High Court of the Province with the Court of Appeal, thereby implying that the time limits applicable to the Court of Appeal should also apply to the High Court of the Province. The Court referenced previous judgments, including L.A. Sudath Rohana and Another v. Mohamed Zeena, which established that applications for leave to appeal must be filed within a reasonable time, and the absence of a specific time limit does not negate the necessity for compliance with established rules. The Court concluded that the application for leave to appeal was indeed time-barred, as the six-week period was mandatory and must be strictly adhered to. Finding: The application for leave to appeal was dismissed as it was filed out of time. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in this case are interrelated, as the determination of whether the application was time-barred directly impacts the court's ability to entertain the appeal. The absence of a specific time limit in the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) (Amendment) Act raised questions about the applicability of the Supreme Court Rules, but the Court ultimately held that the established time limits must be respected to maintain the integrity of the judicial process. Conclusion The Supreme Court upheld the preliminary objection raised by the Respondent's counsel, concluding that the application for leave to appeal was time-barred. Consequently, the application was dismissed without an order for costs, reflecting the Court's recognition of the procedural requirements that govern appeals in the Sri Lankan legal system. The judgment underscores the importance of adhering to established timelines in the pursuit of legal remedies, reinforcing the principle that procedural compliance is essential for the effective functioning of the judiciary. Keywords Leave to Appeal — Time Limit — High Court of Provinces — Special Provisions Act — Supreme Court Rules — Reasonableness — Jurisdiction — Civil Matters — Appeal Process — Mandatory Compliance — Legislative Intent
Counsel: Hiran de Alwis with C. Jayamaha for the Defendant-Respondent-Petitioner; Harsha Soza, PC with N. Jayasena for Plaintiff-Appellant-Respondent

Parties: Associated Newspapers of Ceylon Ltd v. Pituwana Liyanage Shantha Chandraguptha Amarasinghe
Bench: Tilakawardane, J.; Marsoof, P.C., J; Hettige, P.C., J
Keywords: Intellectual Property Rights — Copyright — Economic Rights — Moral Rights — Damages — Judicial Notice — Employment Contract — Berne Convention — Media Law — Journalistic Rights — Public Interest — Legal Precedents — Evidence Ordinance — High Court Jurisdiction — Appeal
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a dispute between Associated Newspapers of Ceylon Ltd (the Appellant) and Pituwana Liyanage Shantha Chandraguptha Amarasinghe (the Respondent) concerning the unauthorized publication of photographs taken by the Respondent during the communal riots of July 1983. The Respondent, a photographer, alleged that his intellectual property rights were violated when the Appellant published nine of his photographs in the 'Daily News' and 'Dinamina' newspapers on July 24, 1999, without his consent. The Respondent claimed that he had economic rights to these photographs under the now-repealed Code of Intellectual Property Act No. 52 of 1979, specifically citing Sections 10 and 11, which pertain to economic and moral rights. The Respondent sought damages amounting to Rs. 2,500,000, asserting that the Appellant's actions not only infringed his rights but also failed to acknowledge him as the source of the photographs. The Appellant contended that the High Court erred in its judgment, particularly in awarding damages and in its reliance on a Gazette submitted by the Respondent without prior notice. The High Court had previously ruled in favor of the Respondent, awarding him Rs. 1,000,000 in damages. Issues The primary issues before the Supreme Court were: Whether the Learned High Court Judge erred in holding that damages occurred to the Respondent based on his economic rights to the photographs? Whether the Learned High Court Judge erred in awarding damages to the Respondent in the sum of Rs. 1,000,000? Whether the Learned High Court Judge improperly relied on a Gazette submitted by the Respondent without notice to the Appellant? Applicable Law Statutes: Code of Intellectual Property Act No. 52 of 1979 (repealed) Cases Referred to: Jayawickrama Vs. Silva - [76 NLR 427]; Leechman Co Ltd., Vs. Rangalle Consolidated Ltd. - [1981 (2) SLR 373] Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Learned High Court Judge erred in holding that damages occurred to the Respondent based on his economic rights to the photographs? Rule applied: The economic rights of authors as defined under the Code of Intellectual Property Act. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the High Court's determination regarding the Respondent's ownership of the photographs and whether he had consented to their publication by the Appellant. The Court noted that the Appellant conceded that the Respondent took the photographs during the riots and that they were published without his authorization. The Court emphasized that the Respondent's capacity to consent was a factual question that could not be revisited at the appellate level, as it required new evidence that was not presented during the trial. The Court reiterated that the High Court's findings were based on the evidence presented, which established that the Respondent retained ownership of the photographs. The Court also highlighted the importance of the Respondent's personal risk in capturing the images during a dangerous period, which underscored his claim to economic rights. The Court concluded that the High Court did not err in its finding that the Respondent had economic rights to the photographs and was entitled to damages. Finding: The Supreme Court affirmed the High Court's ruling that the Respondent had economic rights to the photographs and was entitled to damages. Issue 2: Whether the Learned High Court Judge erred in awarding damages to the Respondent in the sum of Rs. 1,000,000? Rule applied: The assessment of damages in intellectual property infringement cases. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court agreed with the High Court's assessment of damages, noting the unique and historical significance of the photographs taken during the riots. The Court acknowledged the Respondent's testimony regarding the dangers he faced while capturing the images, which added to their value. The Court also considered the lack of acknowledgment by the Appellant when publishing the photographs, which further justified the damages awarded. The Court referenced the Respondent's previous sale of one photograph for Rs. 10,000, but emphasized that the value of the photographs was not solely determined by market transactions. The Court concluded that the damages awarded were appropriate given the circumstances and the nature of the infringement. Finding: The Supreme Court upheld the damages awarded by the High Court. Issue 3: Whether the Learned High Court Judge improperly relied on a Gazette submitted by the Respondent without notice to the Appellant? Rule applied: Judicial notice of laws and regulations under the Evidence Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court found that the High Court was within its rights to take judicial notice of the Gazette in question, as it was a document of statutory importance. The Court noted that the Evidence Ordinance mandates that courts take notice of laws having the force of law without requiring prior notice to the parties involved. The Court concluded that the absence of notice did not constitute a violation of due process and that the High Court's reliance on the Gazette was justified. Finding: The Supreme Court ruled that the High Court did not err in its reliance on the Gazette. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in this case are interrelated, as they all stem from the central question of the Respondent's rights to the photographs and the Appellant's actions in publishing them without consent. The determination of economic rights directly influenced the assessment of damages and the validity of the High Court's reliance on the Gazette. The Court's analysis highlighted the importance of protecting intellectual property rights, particularly in the context of journalism and the risks faced by journalists in their work. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal by the Appellant, affirming the High Court's judgment that the Respondent possessed economic rights to the photographs and was entitled to damages of Rs. 1,000,000. The Court emphasized the need for legal protections for journalists and the importance of acknowledging their contributions to society. The Appellant was ordered to pay costs amounting to Rs. 25,000. Keywords Intellectual Property Rights — Copyright — Economic Rights — Moral Rights — Damages — Judicial Notice — Employment Contract — Berne Convention — Media Law — Journalistic Rights — Public Interest — Legal Precedents — Evidence Ordinance — High Court Jurisdiction — Appeal
Counsel: Mr. Kushan D' Alwis with Prasanna de Silva and Kanchana Ratwatte for Defendant-Appellant. Mr. Saliya Pieris with Upul Kumarapperuma, Irusha Kalidasa and Varuna de Saram for Plaintiff-Respondent.

Parties: Lokuge v. Gunasekera and Others
Bench: P. A. Ratnayake PC, J; S.I. Imam, J.; P. Dep PC, J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — School admissions — Circular interpretation — Proximity criteria — Electoral register — Appeals and Protest Board — Arbitrary decision-making — Unfair treatment
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Ravidu Viduranga Lokuge, applied for admission to Grade 1 at Royal College, Colombo 7, for the 2011 academic year under the proximity criteria of Circular 2010/21. Following an initial interview, the Petitioner was awarded 54 marks and placed 119th on the provisional list. Subsequently, the Petitioner was summoned to a protest inquiry, but the nature of the objection and the identity of the objector were not disclosed. The Petitioner was later excluded from the final admission list, despite 21 students ranked below him in the provisional list being admitted. The 1st Respondent, the Principal of Royal College, contended that the Petitioner's marks were reduced to 42 following an appeal and protest board review. This reduction was based on the absence of the Petitioner's name in an electoral CD for 2006 and the identification of intervening schools between the Petitioner's residence and the school. The Petitioner challenged the legality of the mark reduction, arguing that the protest board failed to follow the re-scrutiny procedures mandated by Circular 2010/21 and that the assessment of intervening schools was arbitrary. Issues Whether the reduction of the Petitioner's marks by the Appeals and Protest Board violated the procedural requirements of Clause 10.6 of Circular 2010/21. Whether the exclusion of the Petitioner from the final admission list constituted an infringement of his fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1), Article 126(2) Cases Referred to: Haputhanthrige v. Attorney General - [2007/v1-sri-lr-101] Ceylon Paper Sacks Ltd. v. Janatha Estate Development Board - [BLR 1993 Vol. V part 1] Analysis Issue 1: Procedural compliance of the Appeals and Protest Board Rule applied: Clause 10.6 of Circular 2010/21. Court's reasoning: The court found that Clause 10.6 restricted the Appeals and Protest Board to re-examining documents submitted at the initial interview. The board improperly relied on an electoral CD that was not available for re-scrutiny by the Petitioner's next friend. Furthermore, the court determined that the deduction of marks for intervening schools was based on an unfair and inconsistent interpretation of proximity, particularly regarding schools that did not offer advanced level education. Finding: The reduction of marks by the Appeals and Protest Board was incorrect, contrary to the provisions of Circular 2010/21, and procedurally unfair. Issue 2: Infringement of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The court held that the Respondents failed to apply the criteria in Circular 2010/21 fairly and reasonably. By arbitrarily reducing the Petitioner's marks and excluding him from the final list while admitting candidates who were marked outside the established scheme, the Respondents denied the Petitioner the equal protection of the law. The court rejected the Respondents' argument that the challenge was time-barred, noting that the infringement occurred only when the Petitioner was denied admission. Finding: The Petitioner's fundamental rights under Article 12(1) were infringed by the 1st Respondent's decision to omit him from the final admission list. Interaction of Issues The procedural failures identified in Issue 1 directly caused the substantive violation of the Petitioner's rights addressed in Issue 2. Because the Appeals and Protest Board acted outside the scope of its authority under Circular 2010/21, the resulting mark reduction was illegitimate, which in turn rendered the Petitioner's exclusion from the final list an act of unequal treatment. Conclusion The court declared that the Petitioner's fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution were infringed. The 1st and 3rd Respondents were directed to admit the Petitioner to Royal College. No costs were ordered. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — School admissions — Circular interpretation — Proximity criteria — Electoral register — Appeals and Protest Board — Arbitrary decision-making — Unfair treatment

Parties: Municipal Council of Moratuwa and Others v. Weerahennadige Shian Hiresh Fernando
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Priyasath Dep, P.C., J.
Keywords: Special Leave to Appeal — Supreme Court Rules — Time Bar — Writ of Mandamus — Judicial Pronouncement — Affidavit Requirements — Public Interest — Substantial Questions of Law — Procedural Compliance — Legal Sanctity — Judgment Pronouncement — Municipal Council Ordinance — Defective Affidavits — Legal Representation — Court of Appeal Judgments
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for Special Leave to Appeal under Article 128 of the Constitution of Sri Lanka, initiated by the Municipal Council of Moratuwa and its officials against Weerahennadige Shian Hiresh Fernando. The Municipal Council had been aggrieved by a judgment of the Court of Appeal which had issued a Writ of Mandamus in favor of the Respondent, compelling the Council to take action regarding certain constructions deemed unauthorized. The Respondent, a private individual, had sought the writ on the grounds that the Municipal Council failed to act against these constructions. The Municipal Council contended that the Court of Appeal's decision was flawed and sought to appeal the judgment. The case was marked by preliminary objections raised by the Respondent regarding the timeliness of the application for leave to appeal and the inclusion of extraneous material in the petition without supporting affidavits. The applications for special leave were filed on 24 June 2011, which the Petitioners argued was within the six-week period stipulated by the Supreme Court Rules. Issues Whether the applications for special leave to appeal were filed in compliance with the time limits set out in Rule 7 of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990. Whether the inclusion of material outside the judgment of the Court of Appeal without supporting affidavits contravened Rule 6 of the Supreme Court Rules. Applicable Law Statutes: Article 128 of the Constitution of Sri Lanka; Supreme Court Rules, 1990. Cases Referred to: Tea Small Factories Ltd. v. Weragoda [1994] 3 SLR 353; Sitamparanathan v. Premaratne and Others [1996] 2 SLR 202; Selenchina v. Mohamed Marikkar and Others (2000) 3 SLR 100. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the applications for special leave to appeal were filed in compliance with the time limits set out in Rule 7 of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990. Rule applied: Supreme Court Rules, 1990, Rule 7. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the timeline of events leading to the filing of the applications for special leave to appeal. The key issue was the date from which the six-week period for filing should be calculated. The Petitioners argued that the date on the judgment (12 May 2011) should be used, while the Respondent contended that the actual pronouncement date (13 May 2011) should apply. The Court noted that the judgments were not legally binding until pronounced in court, thus the six-week period should commence from the date of pronouncement. The Court referenced definitions from legal dictionaries and previous case law to support its conclusion that the applications were timely filed. Finding: The applications for special leave to appeal were deemed to have been filed within the prescribed time limit, and the preliminary objection regarding timeliness was overruled. Issue 2: Whether the inclusion of material outside the judgment of the Court of Appeal without supporting affidavits contravened Rule 6 of the Supreme Court Rules. Rule applied: Supreme Court Rules, 1990, Rule 6. Court's reasoning: The Court addressed the Respondent's objection regarding the inclusion of extraneous material in the Petitioners' application. It was noted that the Petitioners had submitted affidavits from the Mayor and Commissioner of the Municipal Council, who had personal knowledge of the matters at hand. The Court found that the objections raised did not prevent the application from being considered for special leave, as the core questions raised could be supported by the existing affidavits. The Court emphasized that the purpose of Rule 6 was to ensure that allegations of fact were verifiable, and in this case, the affidavits provided sufficient support for the Petitioners' claims. Finding: The preliminary objection regarding the inclusion of extraneous material was overruled. Interaction of Issues The issues were interrelated as the determination of the timeliness of the application directly impacted the Court's ability to consider the merits of the case. The resolution of the preliminary objections allowed the Court to proceed with the substantive issues raised in the applications for special leave to appeal. Conclusion The Supreme Court overruled both preliminary objections raised by the Respondent, allowing the applications for special leave to appeal to proceed. The Court ordered costs to be paid by the Respondent to the Petitioners, setting the stage for further proceedings on the substantive issues raised in the appeal. The judgment underscored the importance of adhering to procedural rules while also ensuring that substantive justice is served in cases involving public interest and significant legal questions. Keywords Special Leave to Appeal — Supreme Court Rules — Time Bar — Writ of Mandamus — Judicial Pronouncement — Affidavit Requirements — Public Interest — Substantial Questions of Law — Procedural Compliance — Legal Sanctity — Judgment Pronouncement — Municipal Council Ordinance — Defective Affidavits — Legal Representation — Court of Appeal Judgments
Counsel: Rasika Dissanayake for the 1st, 2nd and 3rd Petitioners; Chrismal Wanasuriya with Sanjaya Wilson Jayasekera for the Respondent.

Parties: Mapalagamage Leelawathie and Olukarada v. Perera
Bench: Shirani A. Bandaranayake, J.; K. Sripavan, J.; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Land Development Ordinance — Permit-holder definition — Succession to land — Failure to enter possession — Section 68 of the Ordinance — Section 19A of the Ordinance
Summary: Facts The appellant, Mapalagamage Leelawathie, initiated an action in the District Court of Anuradhapura seeking a declaration of title to possession of a disputed property and the ejectment of the respondent, P.P.R.A.S. Perera. The appellant contended that her deceased husband was a permit-holder of the land under the Land Development Ordinance, having received possession from the Mahaweli Authority. Upon her husband's death, the appellant claimed she became the successor permit-holder. The respondent contested this, asserting he had been in possession of the land since 1985 and denying that the appellant's husband ever held a valid permit. The District Court ruled in favor of the appellant. The respondent appealed this decision to the Civil Appellate High Court of the North Central Province, which set aside the District Court judgment. The appellant subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. Evidence established that the document provided by the Mahaweli Authority to the appellant's husband was not a permit in the prescribed form under the Land Development Ordinance. Furthermore, the appellant admitted she had not been in possession of the land since her husband's death in 1988, having left the area during an insurrection and only returning in 1992, while the respondent had been cultivating the land during that period. Issues Whether the appellant's deceased husband qualified as a "permit-holder" under Section 2 of the Land Development Ordinance, thereby allowing the appellant to claim succession. Whether the appellant is barred from claiming succession to the land under Section 68 of the Land Development (Amendment) Act due to her failure to enter into possession within six months of her husband's death. Applicable Law Statutes: Land Development Ordinance Land Development (Amendment) Act No. 16 Cases Referred to: Kassim Hameedu Lebbe v. M. Sultan Samoon - [71-nlr-452] Gunawardena v. Rosalin - [62-nlr-213] Analysis Issue 1: Status of permit-holder Rule applied: Section 2 of the Land Development Ordinance, which defines a permit-holder as a person to whom a permit has been issued or a person in occupation of land alienated on a permit, even if no permit was formally issued. Court's reasoning: The court examined document P1 and determined it was not a permit in the prescribed form required by Section 25 of the Ordinance. However, under Section 2, the deceased husband could be considered a permit-holder if he was in occupation of land alienated to him. The court found that because the husband was in occupation and cultivating the land until his death, he qualified as a permit-holder under the second limb of Section 2. Finding: The deceased husband was a permit-holder under the Ordinance. Issue 2: Failure of succession Rule applied: Section 68 of the Land Development (Amendment) Act, which stipulates that a spouse fails to succeed to a holding if they do not enter into possession within six months of the permit-holder's death. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the appellant's husband died in 1988. The evidence demonstrated that the appellant did not enter into possession of the land following his death; instead, the respondent cultivated the field from 1988 onwards. The appellant was absent from the area until 1992 and did not institute legal action until 2001. Applying the principle in Gunawardena v. Rosalin, the court held that the statutory requirement to enter into possession within six months is mandatory for succession. Finding: The appellant failed to satisfy the requirements for succession under Section 68. Interaction of Issues The findings are sequential; while the court acknowledged the husband's status as a permit-holder under the broad definition in Section 2, this status did not automatically vest rights in the appellant. The failure to comply with the temporal requirements of Section 68 regarding entry into possession rendered the appellant's claim to succession legally untenable, regardless of the husband's initial status. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed and the judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court is affirmed. The appellant is not entitled to claim succession to the land. Keywords Land Development Ordinance — Permit-holder definition — Succession to land — Failure to enter possession — Section 68 of the Ordinance — Section 19A of the Ordinance

Parties: Arachchilage Gedara Anulawathi v. Wadiyaralalage Weerasinghe Bandara
Bench: Dr. Shirani A. Bandaranayake, CJ.; P.A. Ratnayake, PC., J.; Priyasath Dep, PC., J.
Keywords: Civil Procedure Code — Notice of Appeal — Petition of Appeal — Jurisdiction — Long-standing Practice — Judicial Authority — Appeal Process — District Court — Civil Appellate High Court — Legal Precedent — Procedural Compliance
Summary: Facts The present appeal arises from a judgment delivered by the Civil Appellate High Court of the Sabaragamuwa Province, which rejected the Notice of Appeal and Petition of Appeal filed by the defendant-appellant-appellant (hereinafter referred to as the appellant) against a judgment of the District Court of Mawanella. The plaintiff-respondent-respondent (hereinafter referred to as the respondent) had instituted action in the District Court, which ruled in favor of the respondent. The appellant, dissatisfied with this outcome, filed a Notice of Appeal and a Petition of Appeal. However, the Civil Appellate High Court dismissed these filings on the grounds that they were improperly addressed to the Court of Appeal instead of the original District Court, as mandated by Section 754 (3) of the Civil Procedure Code. The appellant subsequently sought leave to appeal to the Supreme Court, which was granted. During the proceedings, both parties acknowledged that similar legal questions were under consideration in another case pending before the Supreme Court, specifically S.M. Samarakoon and S.M. Kularatne v. M.S. Fajurdeen. Issues The primary issues for determination in this appeal are: Whether the Notice of Appeal and Petition of Appeal were validly filed in accordance with the provisions of the Civil Procedure Code? Whether the long-standing practice of addressing Notices of Appeal to the Court of Appeal should be recognized despite the statutory requirements? Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Sections 754 (3) and 754 (4) Cases Referred to: Boyagoda v. Mendis - (1929) 30 N.L.R. 321; Babapulle v. Domingo - (1892) 2 C.L.R. 96; S.M. Samarakoon and S.M. Kularatne v. M.S. Fajurdeen - S.C. Appeal No. 06/2012 Analysis Issue 1: Validity of Notice of Appeal and Petition of Appeal Rule applied: Section 754 of the Civil Procedure Code Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court, in considering the appeal, focused on the procedural requirements outlined in the Civil Procedure Code, particularly Section 754, which stipulates that a Notice of Appeal must be addressed to the original court from which the appeal is made. The Civil Appellate High Court had correctly identified that the Notice of Appeal and Petition of Appeal were improperly addressed to the Court of Appeal, which rendered them invalid. The Court emphasized that adherence to procedural rules is essential for the integrity of the judicial process. The appellant's counsel argued that for over a century, it had been a common practice to address Notices of Appeal to the Court of Appeal, and thus this practice should be recognized. The Court referenced the case of Boyagoda v. Mendis, where it was established that long-standing practices that have been accepted and recognized by judicial authority should not be easily disregarded. The Court noted that the practice of addressing Notices of Appeal to the Court of Appeal had been prevalent, and the legal community had operated under this understanding for many years. However, the Court also highlighted that the statutory provisions must be followed to ensure that the judicial process remains orderly and predictable. The Court ultimately concluded that while the historical practice was significant, it could not override the explicit requirements of the law. Finding: The Supreme Court found that the Notice of Appeal and Petition of Appeal were invalid due to improper addressing, but acknowledged the long-standing practice in the legal community. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in this appeal are interrelated, as the validity of the Notice of Appeal directly impacts the procedural compliance required by the Civil Procedure Code. The recognition of long-standing practices in the legal system must be balanced against the necessity of adhering to statutory requirements to maintain the rule of law. Conclusion The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, set aside the judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court dated 25.10.2011, and directed the Civil Appellate High Court of Kegalle to accept the Notice of Appeal and Petition of Appeal filed by the appellant and to proceed with the appeal. The Court emphasized the importance of following procedural rules while also recognizing the historical context of legal practices. There will be no costs.
Counsel: Rohan Sahabandu with Hasitha Amarasinghe for Defendant-Appellant-Appellant; Saliya Peiris with Thanuka Nandasiri for Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent

Parties: Hussain and Others v. Wimalasuriya Tilakaratne
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Sripavan, J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Partition Law — Description of land — Deeds — Undivided land — Physical metes and bounds — Prescription Ordinance — Gift of transfer — Signature verification — Title registration system
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Petitioner, Noor Nazima alias Noorul Naxima Hussain, instituted a partition action in the District Court of Kandy regarding an allotment of land at Uda Mahaiyawa, Kandy. The District Court entered judgment in favor of the Plaintiff, finding that she was the owner of the land and that the Deed bearing No. 31540, through which the 2nd Defendant-Appellant-Respondent, Shantha Wimalasuriya Tilakaratne, claimed title, was invalid as the Plaintiff had not signed it. The 2nd Defendant appealed this decision to the Provincial High Court of the Central Province, which set aside the District Court judgment. The Plaintiff subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the description of the land in Deed No. 6257 is conclusive evidence that the land is a defined and distinct portion. Whether the transfer of land in the relevant deed constituted an undivided share rather than a defined allotment. Whether the Plaintiffâ€™s action was barred by the three-year limitation period under Section 10 of the Prescription Ordinance. Whether the 2nd Defendant acquired valid title to the property through a deed based on a forged signature. Applicable Law Statutes: Partition Law No. 21 of 1977 Partition Ordinance Prescription Ordinance (Section 10) Cases Referred to: Ranbanda v. Yatagama - [3-acr-40] Jayawickrama v. Silva - [76-nlr-427] Leechman Co Ltd. v. Rangalle Consolidated Ltd. - [1981/v2-sri-lr-373] Analysis Issue 1: Conclusiveness of Deed Description Rule applied: Section 4(b) of the Partition Law No. 21 of 1977 and the interpretation of Section 2 of the repealed Partition Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the description of land in a deed is not necessarily conclusive. By analogy to the Partition Ordinance, the court held that where a deed lacks sufficient detail to enable exact identification, the court may rely on other evidence, such as survey plans, to determine if the land is identified. Finding: The description in the deed was not conclusive; the court must look to the facts and circumstances to determine if the land is defined. Issue 2: Defined vs. Undivided Land Rule applied: The principle that if a grantâ€™s language is insufficient to permit exact demarcation of boundaries, it must be interpreted as conveying an undivided share. Court's reasoning: The court examined the documents and found that the original transfer to the Plaintiff was of an undivided allotment. Regarding the deed in question, the court found it failed to provide clear language indicating the boundaries of the 10 perches purportedly conveyed. Finding: The transfer was of an undivided share, not a defined allotment. Issue 3: Prescription Rule applied: Section 10 of the Prescription Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the cause of action accrued in 1994, only after the criminal investigation into the fraud proved futile. Finding: The action was commenced within the statutory time limit, and the Respondent could not rely on the Prescription Ordinance. Issue 4: Validity of Title Rule applied: Principles of evidence regarding the Examiner of Questioned Documents (EQD) report. Court's reasoning: The EQD provided evidence that the signature on the deed (P2) did not match the Plaintiffâ€™s specimen signatures. The court concluded on the balance of probabilities that the signature was forged. Consequently, the person from whom the 2nd Defendant purchased the land had no soil rights to convey. Finding: The deed was void, and the 2nd Defendant possessed no valid title or locus standi to contest the partition. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected: the determination that the land was undivided (Issue 2) supported the application of the Partition Law. This, combined with the finding that the 2nd Defendantâ€™s title deed was void due to forgery (Issue 4), rendered the 2nd Defendantâ€™s claims legally unsustainable, thereby reinforcing the Plaintiffâ€™s right to the property. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the judgment of the District Court dated 23.10.2002 is affirmed. The judgment of the High Court dated 01.07.2008 is set aside. Keywords Partition Law — Description of land — Deeds — Undivided land — Physical metes and bounds — Prescription Ordinance — Gift of transfer — Signature verification — Title registration system

Parties: Senasinghe Perera v. Dissanayake
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; P.A. Ratnayake, J.; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Leave to appeal — Land Development Ordinance — Section 19(2) — Mahaweli Authority permit — Proof of documents — Secondary evidence — Section 65(3) of the Evidence Ordinance — Destruction of documents — Validity of permit — State land
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent, Dissanayake Mudiyanselage Kithsiri Bandara Dissanayake, claimed possession of a land allotment based on a permit issued under the Land Development Ordinance. The Respondent alleged that he had employed the Defendant-Appellant-Petitioner-Appellant, Pelana Pathiranalage Rahula Ananda Senasinghe Perera, as a watcher and temporarily allowed him access to the land. The Respondent contended that the Appellant subsequently obstructed his access and remained in unlawful possession of the property. The Appellant denied the existence of the permit and asserted his own right to possess and cultivate the land. The District Court of Anuradhapura ruled in favor of the Respondent. The Appellant appealed this decision to the Provincial High Court of Civil Appeal of the North Central Province, which dismissed the appeal. The Appellant then sought leave to appeal to the Supreme Court. During the trial, the Respondent produced a permit marked P-1 and a ledger page marked P-2 to establish his title. A Land Officer from the Mahaweli Authority testified that the original records had been destroyed in a fire during an insurrection and that the documents produced were reconstructed, genuine records. The Appellant admitted during the trial that he held no license or permit for the land in dispute. Issues Whether the documents P-1 and P-2 were validly proved in accordance with the law. Whether the secondary evidence provided for the permit P-1 was admissible under the Evidence Ordinance. Applicable Law Statutes: Land Development Ordinance Evidence Ordinance Cases Referred to: Kandasamy v. Sinnahamby - [1985-2-slr-249] Analysis Issue 1: Validity of proof for P-1 and P-2 Rule applied: The Evidence Ordinance, specifically regarding the production and authentication of official documents. Court's reasoning: The Respondent produced P-1 and P-2 through the testimony of a Land Officer from the Mahaweli Authority. The officer confirmed the authenticity of the signatures and handwriting on the documents. Furthermore, the officer explained that the original records were destroyed in a fire and subsequently reconstructed using official office records and permits held by permit holders. The Appellant failed to object to the admission of these documents when they were tendered at trial. Finding: The documents P-1 and P-2 were duly proved as genuine and valid. Issue 2: Admissibility of secondary evidence Rule applied: Section 64 and Section 65(3) of the Evidence Ordinance. Court's reasoning: Section 64 allows for secondary evidence when primary evidence is unavailable. Section 65(3) specifically permits secondary evidence when the original document has been destroyed or lost through no fault of the party. The court relied on the precedent in Kandasamy v. Sinnahamby to support the application of Section 65(3). Since the original permit was destroyed in a fire, the testimony of the Land Officer, who could verify the authenticity of the reconstructed document, satisfied the legal requirements for secondary evidence. Finding: The secondary evidence was admissible, and the permit P-1 was validly established. Interaction of Issues The findings on the admissibility of secondary evidence directly supported the conclusion that the documents were validly proved. Because the secondary evidence was deemed admissible under the Evidence Ordinance, the Respondent successfully established his title, which rendered the Appellant's lack of a permit or legal authority to possess the land the decisive factor in the dispute. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed, and the judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court of the North Central Province dated 18.03.2010 is affirmed. No costs are ordered. Keywords Leave to appeal — Land Development Ordinance — Section 19(2) — Mahaweli Authority permit — Proof of documents — Secondary evidence — Section 65(3) of the Evidence Ordinance — Destruction of documents — Validity of permit — State land

Parties: Sithy Zulfika v. Seelavangsha
Bench: Shiranee Thilakawardene, J.; S.I. Imam, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Appeal against High Court judgement — Questions of law — Documents establishing money lending transactions — Intention to dispose of beneficial interest in property — Contradictory evidence — Attornment of tenant — Constructive trust — Valuation of property — Husband's employment as a moneylender — Deed of Transfer — Notary Public's role
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent instituted an action in the District Court of Gampola seeking a declaration of title to a property, the eviction of the Defendant-Appellant-Petitioner, and damages for unlawful occupation. The Respondent relied on Deed of Transfer No. 2572 dated 06.03.1986 to establish an outright transfer of the property from the Appellant. The Appellant contested the action, alleging that the deed was executed merely to secure a loan of LKR 11,000 and that the property was held on trust until the loan was repaid. The Appellant further claimed that the loan had been repaid, although she later stated she would repay it if the judgment were in her favor. The Civil Appellate High Court dismissed the Appellant's appeal, finding her evidence contradictory and lacking creditworthiness. The High Court noted that the Appellant provided inconsistent accounts regarding the alleged loan repayments. Furthermore, the property was occupied by a tenant, T.P. Assen, at the time of the transfer. The Respondent explained that she permitted the tenant to remain until her husband retired, at which point she took possession. The Appellant alleged the property was undervalued in the deed to reflect the loan amount, but evidence showed the property value in the deed was higher than a previous deed from 1985. The Appellant also alleged the Respondentâ€™s husband was a moneylender, but failed to provide sufficient evidence of a systematic business of money lending. Issues Whether the documents D1 (a) - (j) establish that the Respondent was engaging in money lending transactions. Whether the Civil Appellate High Court misdirected itself in assessing the attendant circumstances regarding the Appellant's intent to dispose of the beneficial interest in the property. Whether the Civil Appellate High Court misdirected itself in holding that the Appellant's evidence was contradictory. Whether the Respondent failed to establish that the tenant, T.P. Assen, had attorned to her, and whether the Appellant is entitled to judgment based on a constructive trust. Applicable Law Statutes: Not specified Cases Referred to: Edgelow v. Mac Elwee - [1918] 1 KB 205 Dias Nagahawatte v. Alwis Appuhamy - [1987] 288 SLR Hameed v. Anamalay - [47-nlr-1] Analysis Issue 1: Money lending transactions Rule applied: The definition of a moneylender as articulated in Edgelow v. Mac Elwee and Dias Nagahawatte v. Alwis Appuhamy, requiring a "business" of money lending characterized by system, repetition, and continuity. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the Appellant failed to provide evidence of a systematic business. The receipts submitted (V1) were inconsistent, and one was dated after the commencement of legal proceedings, rendering it lacking in credibility. Finding: The documents did not establish that the Respondent was engaged in a money lending business. Issue 2: Intent to dispose of beneficial interest Rule applied: The principle that an outright deed of transfer executed before a Notary Public reflects the parties' intentions unless proven otherwise by cogent evidence. Court's reasoning: The Court compared the disputed deed (P1) with a previous deed (P2) and found the property was not undervalued. The Court reasoned that if the transaction were a loan, the parties would have executed a mortgage bond rather than an outright transfer. Finding: There were no attendant circumstances suggesting the transaction was anything other than an outright transfer of beneficial interest. Issue 3: Contradictory evidence Rule applied: The appellate standard of review regarding the evaluation of evidence and witness credibility by the trial court.
