Court's reasoning: The High Court conducted an incisive evaluation of the facts and identified material inconsistencies in the Appellant's testimony, particularly regarding the alleged repayment of the loan. The Supreme Court found no error in this evaluation. Finding: The High Court did not misdirect itself; the Appellant's evidence was indeed contradictory and lacked creditworthiness. Issue 4: Attornment and constructive trust Rule applied: The principle from Hameed v. Anamalay that a purchaser of land succeeds to all rights of the vendor on a lease without special assignment, and the principle that attornment need not be express. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the tenant's rent payments to the owner (the Respondent) were sufficient to establish the transfer of tenancy. Regarding the constructive trust, the Court found no evidence in the deed or the surrounding circumstances to support the existence of such a trust. Finding: The Respondent established her rights as the new owner, and no constructive trust was created. Interaction of Issues The findings on the nature of the transaction (Issue 2) and the lack of evidence for a money lending business (Issue 1) reinforce the conclusion that the deed was an outright transfer. These findings, combined with the rejection of the Appellant's contradictory testimony (Issue 3), negate the basis for the Appellant's claim of a constructive trust (Issue 4). Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court is affirmed. Keywords Appeal against High Court judgement — Questions of law — Documents establishing money lending transactions — Intention to dispose of beneficial interest in property — Contradictory evidence — Attornment of tenant — Constructive trust — Valuation of property — Husband's employment as a moneylender — Deed of Transfer — Notary Public's role

Parties: Daya Abeyawicsrama v. Dias and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Right of way — Abandonment of servitude — Non-user of property — Soil rights — Obstruction of right of way — Deliberate and intentional abandonment — Roman-Dutch Law — Servitude created by grant
Summary: Facts The deceased Plaintiff initiated an action in the District Court of Mount Lavinia against the Defendants seeking a declaration of title to commonly possess Lot A3, an order for the removal of obstructions on said lot, and a permanent injunction against the Defendants. The Plaintiff asserted ownership of the property and a right of access over Lot A3 based on Deed No. 5301 and Plan No. 903. The Defendants contested the claim, arguing that the Plaintiff never utilized the right of way and had abandoned it by constructing walls that separated the Plaintiff's land from the access path. The Defendants further claimed that the right of way was used exclusively for access to their own properties. The District Court entered judgment in favor of the Plaintiff. Upon appeal, the Civil Appellate High Court of Mount Lavinia reversed this decision, ruling in favor of the Defendants. The Substituted Plaintiff-Respondent-Appellant subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court to set aside the judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court and restore the District Court's decision. Issues Whether a person holding soil rights to a property can be prevented from using a right of way over that property. Whether the non-user or abandonment of a servitude created by a notarial grant constitutes a loss of rights under Sri Lankan law. Whether the construction of a gate by the owner of the soil constitutes an actionable obstruction to a right of way. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) (Amendment) Act No. 54 Cases Referred to: Saparamadu v. Melder - [2004/v3-sri-lr-148] Fernando v. Wickremasinghe - [1998/v3-sri-lr-37] Suntangala v. Appuhamy - [46-nlr-137] Senathiraja v. Marimuttu - [53-nlr-5] Nagamani v. Vinayagamoorthy - [24-nlr-438] Paramount Investments Ltd. v. Carder - [1986/v2-sri-lr-309] Jayasekera Hamine v. Agida Hamine - [46-nlr-38] de la Harpe v. Wickremasinghe - [58-nlr-310] Analysis Issue 1: Soil rights and servitude Rule applied: The principle that an action for the use of a servitude can be filed by the owner of the soil rights, as established in Saparamadu v. Melder and Fernando v. Wickremasinghe. Court's reasoning: The court found that the Substituted Appellant established ownership of the soil rights to Lot A3 via Deed P1. Since a servitude restricts the owner's free use of their land, the owner retains the right to enforce their interests unless a valid abandonment is proven. Finding: The Substituted Appellant, as the owner of the soil, has the legal standing to assert rights over the property. Issue 2: Abandonment and non-user Rule applied: The Roman-Dutch Law requirement of 33 and 1/3 years for loss of rights by non-user, and the requirement that abandonment of a notarial grant must be deliberate and intentional (Nagamani v. Vinayagamoorthy; Paramount Investments Ltd. v. Carder). Court's reasoning: The court determined that the Respondents failed to prove deliberate or intentional abandonment. Furthermore, the period of non-user did not meet the 33 and 1/3 year threshold required under Roman-Dutch Law, as the action was initiated well within that timeframe from the date of the deed. Finding: The right of way was not abandoned or lost through non-user. Issue 3: Obstruction of the right of way Rule applied: The principle that an owner of the soil may protect their land provided they do not substantially obstruct the dominant owner's free use of the path (de la Harpe v. Wickremasinghe; Jayasekera Hamine v. Agida Hamine). Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that a gate is permissible if it does not hinder the free passage of the dominant owner. However, any construction that prevents the full and free use of the path is actionable. Finding: The Substituted Appellant is permitted to maintain a gate provided it does not obstruct the Respondents' free use of the right of way. Interaction of Issues The findings are reinforcing: the establishment of soil rights (Issue 1) provides the legal basis for the Appellant to control the land, while the failure of the Respondents to prove abandonment (Issue 2) preserves the servitude. The court's ruling on obstruction (Issue 3) balances the Appellant's ownership rights with the Respondents' right of access, ensuring that the final order protects the servitude without granting the Respondents excessive control over the Appellant's property. Conclusion The judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court is set aside and the judgment of the District Court of Mount Lavinia is affirmed. A permanent injunction is granted restraining the Respondents from constructing temporary or permanent structures within the road way. Keywords Right of way — Abandonment of servitude — Non-user of property — Soil rights — Obstruction of right of way — Deliberate and intentional abandonment — Roman-Dutch Law — Servitude created by grant

Parties: Mohamed Haniffa Sithy Zulfika v. Chandrani Seelavangsha
Bench: Tilakawardena, J.; Imam, J; Dep, P.C., J.
Keywords: money lending — constructive trust — beneficial interest — deed of transfer — eviction — unlawful possession — attornment — contradictory evidence — legal proceedings — property rights
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a property dispute between Mohamed Haniffa Sithy Zulfika (the Appellant) and Chandrani Seelavangsha (the Respondent). The Respondent initiated legal action in the District Court of Gampola on September 11, 1995, seeking a declaration of title to a property, eviction of the Appellant, and damages for unlawful occupation. The Respondent claimed ownership based on a Deed of Transfer (P1) dated March 6, 1986, which purportedly transferred the property from the Appellant to the Respondent. The Respondent asserted that she had been unlawfully deprived of possession for over 12 years. The Appellant contested this, arguing that the transfer was merely a security for a loan of LKR 11,000 provided by the Respondent, and that she had no intention of transferring the beneficial interest in the property. The Appellant's position was complicated by her contradictory statements regarding the repayment of the loan, which she initially claimed had been settled but later suggested would only be repaid if the judgment favored her. The Civil Appellate High Court dismissed the Appellant's appeal, leading to the current Supreme Court appeal. Issues The Supreme Court considered several key legal questions: 1. Whether the documents D1 (a) - (j) establish that the Respondent was engaging in money lending transactions. 2. Whether the Civil Appellate High Court misdirected itself by failing to properly assess the circumstances surrounding the Appellant's intention regarding the beneficial interest in the property. 3. Whether the Civil Appellate High Court erred in finding the Appellant's evidence contradictory. 4. (a) Whether the Respondent failed to establish that the tenant, T.P. Assen, had attorned to her. (b) If not, whether the Appellant is entitled to judgment based on a constructive trust. Applicable Law Statutes: The judgment references various legal principles related to property law, contracts, and the nature of money lending. Cases Referred to: Edgelow v. Mac Elwee [1918] 1 KB 205; Dias Nagahawatte Vs. Alwis Appuhamy (1987) 288 SLR; Hameed Vs. Anamalay (1946) 47 NLR 558. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the documents establish that the Respondent was engaging in money lending transactions. Rule applied: The definition of money lending as requiring a business of lending with system, repetition, and continuity. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the evidence presented regarding the Respondent's alleged status as a money lender. The Appellant's claims were based on the assertion that the Respondent's husband was a money lender, which was not substantiated by credible evidence. The Court noted that the receipts provided by the Appellant were inconsistent and lacked credibility, particularly as one receipt dated September 1997 was issued after legal proceedings had commenced. The Court emphasized that the definition of a money lender requires more than isolated transactions; it necessitates a systematic approach to lending. The Court found that the evidence did not support the Appellant's claims, concluding that the Respondent was not engaged in money lending as defined by law. Finding: The Court determined that the evidence did not establish the Respondent as a money lender. Issue 2: Whether the Civil Appellate High Court misdirected itself regarding the Appellant's intention. Rule applied: The principle of beneficial interest and the intention behind property transfers. Court's reasoning: The Court scrutinized the findings of the Civil Appellate High Court, which had concluded that the Appellant's evidence was contradictory and lacked credibility. The Appellant's claims regarding the nature of the transfer were inconsistent, particularly her statements about the repayment of the loan. The Court highlighted that the Appellant's assertion that the transfer was merely a security for a loan was undermined by her own contradictory statements. The Court reiterated that the intention behind the transfer must be clear and that the evidence presented did not support the Appellant's claims of a constructive trust. Finding: The Court upheld the Civil Appellate High Court's findings, affirming that the Appellant's intention was not credible. Issue 3: Whether the Civil Appellate High Court erred in finding the Appellant's evidence contradictory. Rule applied: The assessment of evidence credibility and consistency. Court's reasoning: The Court analyzed the Appellant's testimony and the inconsistencies therein. The Civil Appellate High Court had noted that the Appellant contradicted herself regarding the repayment of the loan and the nature of the transfer. The Court found that the Appellant's statements lacked coherence and credibility, particularly in light of the evidence presented by the Respondent and the Urban Council representative. The Court emphasized that the Appellant's failure to provide a consistent narrative undermined her position. Finding: The Court agreed with the Civil Appellate High Court's assessment of the Appellant's evidence as contradictory. Issue 4: (a) Whether the Respondent failed to establish that the tenant had attorned to her. (b) If not, whether the Appellant is entitled to judgment based on a constructive trust. Rule applied: The principles of attornment and constructive trusts. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the evidence regarding the tenant, T.P. Assen, and the payments made to the Urban Council. The Court noted that even if Assen was unaware of the ownership transfer, the payments made were intended for the new landlord, the Respondent. The Court cited legal precedents establishing that a purchaser of land subject to a lease succeeds to the rights of the vendor without a special assignment. The Court concluded that the evidence indicated that Assen had accepted the Respondent as the owner, and thus the issue of attornment was satisfied. Regarding the constructive trust, the Court found no evidence supporting the Appellant's claim that the transfer was intended as a security for a loan. The Deed of Transfer did not indicate any intention to create a constructive trust. Finding: The Court found that the Respondent had established her ownership and that the Appellant's claims of a constructive trust were unfounded. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in this case are interrelated, as they all revolve around the central question of the nature of the property transfer and the credibility of the Appellant's claims. The determination of whether the Respondent was engaged in money lending directly impacts the interpretation of the Deed of Transfer and the Appellant's assertions regarding the nature of the transaction. The inconsistencies in the Appellant's evidence further complicate the matter, as they undermine her credibility and the validity of her claims. Conclusion The Supreme Court affirmed the decision of the Civil Appellate High Court, concluding that the Appellant's claims lacked merit and that the Respondent had established her ownership of the property. The Court found no basis for the Appellant's assertions regarding a constructive trust or the nature of the transfer. The appeal was dismissed with no costs.
Counsel: Faisz Musthapha, PC with Kamran Aziz for Defendant-Appellant-Petitioner. Lal Wijenayake for Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent.

Parties: Pallehagoda v. Singho and Others
Bench: Shiranee Tilakawardane, J; P.A. Ratnayake, PC. J; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Appeal from Civil Appellate High Court — Identification of corpus — Land Development Ordinance — Survey plan vs. permit — Boundaries of land — Extent of land — Role of the Surveyor — Failure to identify the corpus
Summary: Facts The Respondent instituted a vindicatory action in the District Court of Walasmulla seeking a declaration of title to a land, the ejectment of the Appellants, and damages. The Respondent claimed title to the land via a permit issued under the Land Development Ordinance. The Appellants had entered a portion of the land and were in possession, a status previously reinforced by an order under the Primary Courts Procedure Act. The District Court dismissed the action primarily due to the failure of the Respondent to properly identify the subject matter of the suit. Upon appeal, the Civil Appellate High Court set aside the District Court judgment and granted the reliefs sought by the Respondent. The Appellants subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the court erred in concluding that the Respondent established title to the corpus when the description in the plaint was not properly identified. Whether the court erred in relying on permits where the boundaries were contradictory. Whether the court erred in failing to consider that the land surveyed was larger than the land described in the plaint. Applicable Law Statutes: Land Development Ordinance Chap. 464 Primary Courts Procedure Act No. 44 Cases Referred to: Wanigaratne v. Juwanis Appuhamy - [65-nlr-167] [sc-appeal-104/05] Analysis Issue 1: Identification of the corpus Rule applied: The principle that in a vindicatory action, the identity of the property is as fundamental as the proof of ownership. Court's reasoning: The court noted a substantial discrepancy between the 2-acre extent stated in the permit and the 3 acres, 1 rood, and 23.12 perches depicted in the surveyor's plan. The surveyor admitted during cross-examination that he could not positively confirm the land surveyed was the land described in the permit. The court held that the surveyor's uncertainty, combined with the significant difference in extent, constituted a failure to identify the corpus. Finding: The court answered the question of law in the affirmative, finding that the corpus was not properly identified. Issue 2: Contradictory boundaries Rule applied: The court evaluated the evidentiary weight of permits 'P1' and 'P2' and the testimony of the District Land Officer. Court's reasoning: While the northern and southern boundaries in permit 'P1' were reversed compared to the survey plan, the District Land Officer testified that 'P1' and 'P2' were issued for the same land. The court reasoned that this specific boundary error was a clerical mistake that did not inherently invalidate the identity of the land. Finding: The court found that the boundary discrepancy alone did not vitiate the identity of the corpus. Issue 3: Discrepancy in extent Rule applied: The requirement for clear identification of property in a vindicatory action as established in [sc-appeal-104/05]. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the surveyor included an additional area based on the Plaintiff's instructions, leading to the substantial difference in extent. Because the Plaintiff cannot succeed solely by attacking the weakness of the Defendant's title, the failure to reconcile the survey plan with the permit rendered the identification insufficient. Finding: The court answered the question of law in the affirmative, concluding that the failure to identify the land correctly prevented the Respondent from succeeding. Interaction of Issues The issues regarding boundary descriptions and the total extent of the land are intrinsically linked to the overarching requirement of identifying the corpus. While the court found the boundary errors to be reconcilable, the failure to resolve the discrepancy in the land's extent—compounded by the surveyor's own uncertainty—rendered the identification of the subject matter legally insufficient, thereby necessitating a re-survey. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court is set aside. The judgment of the District Court is also set aside, and the case is remanded to the District Court for a re-hearing with directions to issue a new commission for a survey to properly identify the corpus. Keywords Appeal from Civil Appellate High Court — Identification of corpus — Land Development Ordinance — Survey plan vs. permit — Boundaries of land — Extent of land — Role of the Surveyor — Failure to identify the corpus

Parties: Rajawila and Others v. Kamalawathie and Piyasena
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC. J; S.I. Imam, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC. J
Keywords: Land Development Ordinance — Permits — Validity of permits — Cancellation of permits — Government Agent's actions — Land ownership — District Court judgment — Civil Appellate High Court judgment
Summary: Facts The original Plaintiff, Wimala Herath, filed an action in the District Court of Polonnaruwa seeking a declaration of title to land described in two schedules to the plaint and the ejectment of the Defendants. The first schedule comprised a block of land measuring 2A. 1R. 26P. held under Permit No. 156 issued pursuant to the Land Development Ordinance. The second schedule comprised a 30-perch block of land that was situated within the boundaries of the larger block described in the first schedule. The Defendants contended that the first Defendant held a valid permit, No. 156A, for the 30-perch block of land. The District Court held that the Plaintiff was the lawful owner of both blocks, declared that any other permits issued for the said lands were null and void, and ordered the ejectment of the Defendants while awarding them compensation for improvements. The Defendants appealed to the Civil Appellate High Court, which set aside the District Court judgment and dismissed the plaint. The Plaintiff-Respondent-Appellants subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Civil Appellate High Court erred in law by holding that the Respondents were entitled to the land described in Permit No. 156A, which formed part of the land already held by the Petitioners under Permit No. 156. Whether the Civil Appellate High Court erred in holding that it was unnecessary to cancel Permit No. 156 prior to the issuance of Permit No. 156A. Whether the Civil Appellate High Court erred in its evaluation of the provisions of the Land Development Ordinance No. 19 as amended. Applicable Law Statutes: Land Development Ordinance No. 19 as amended Cases Referred to: Seenithambi v. Ahamadulebbe - [74-nlr-222] Analysis Issue 1: Validity of overlapping permits Rule applied: Land Development Ordinance No. 19 as amended. Court's reasoning: The court observed that Permit No. 156 was admitted by both parties to be legal and valid. Under the Land Development Ordinance, there is no provision to expunge a portion of land already granted under a permit to issue a separate permit for that portion. The court reasoned that since the 30-perch block (Permit 156A) was situated within the larger block (Permit 156), the issuance of the second permit was legally impermissible while the first permit remained valid. Finding: Permit No. 156A is illegal and void. Issue 2: Necessity of permit cancellation Rule applied: Land Development Ordinance No. 19 as amended; Seenithambi v. Ahamadulebbe - [74-nlr-222]. Court's reasoning: The court relied on the principle established in Seenithambi v. Ahamadulebbe, which held that strict proof of the due cancellation of an existing permit is a prerequisite before the title of the original permit holder can be defeated. The court found that the Civil Appellate High Court misinterpreted this precedent. Because Permit No. 156 was never lawfully cancelled, the government agent had no legal authority to issue a new permit for any portion of that land. Finding: The Civil Appellate High Court erred in law by holding that the cancellation of the original permit was unnecessary. Issue 3: Evaluation of the Land Development Ordinance Rule applied: Land Development Ordinance No. 19 as amended. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the government agent acted in violation of the Ordinance by issuing Permit No. 156A without first following the lawful procedure for cancelling the existing permit. The court rejected the argument that consent from the original permit holder or long possession could validate a permit that was issued in contravention of the statutory framework. Finding: The Civil Appellate High Court failed to correctly apply the provisions of the Land Development Ordinance. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the illegality of Permit No. 156A (Issue 1) is a direct consequence of the failure to follow the mandatory statutory procedure for permit cancellation (Issue 2). The courtâ€™s interpretation of the Land Development Ordinance (Issue 3) confirms that the absence of a lawful cancellation renders the subsequent permit void, thereby reinforcing the conclusion that the original permit holderâ€™s title remained undisturbed. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court is set aside, and the judgment of the District Court of Polonnaruwa is restored. Keywords Land Development Ordinance — Permits — Validity of permits — Cancellation of permits — Government Agent's actions — Land ownership — District Court judgment — Civil Appellate High Court judgment

Parties: Wakachiku Construction Co. Ltd. v. Road Development Authority
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J.; K. Sripavan, J.; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: inherent jurisdiction — arbitration — appointment of arbitrators — party autonomy — Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 — High Court jurisdiction — legal principles — judicial review — due process — statutory provisions — legal remedies — construction contracts — dispute resolution — Sri Lankan law — foreign arbitrators — legal representation
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Wakachiku Construction Co. Ltd., is a foreign construction company engaged in a contract with the Road Development Authority (RDA) of Sri Lanka. Disputes arose during the execution of the contract, leading the Petitioner to refer the matter to an Engineer and subsequently to an Adjudicator as per the contract's provisions. Dissatisfied with the Adjudicator's decision, the Petitioner sought to initiate arbitration by nominating three arbitrators and requesting the RDA to select one within a stipulated timeframe. The RDA, however, refused to comply, insisting on the appointment of Sri Lankan arbitrators instead. The Petitioner proceeded to appoint one of the nominated arbitrators after the RDA failed to respond within the agreed period. The RDA then sought the intervention of the High Court, claiming that the Petitioner had unilaterally appointed an arbitrator contrary to the contract and the Arbitration Act. The High Court ruled in favor of the RDA, appointing a different arbitrator, which prompted the Petitioner to seek the Supreme Court's intervention to set aside the High Court's order, arguing that the High Court lacked jurisdiction to entertain the matter. Issues Whether the High Court had jurisdiction to entertain the RDA's application regarding the appointment of an arbitrator. Whether the Petitioner was entitled to invoke the inherent jurisdiction of the Supreme Court to set aside the High Court's order. Applicable Law Statutes: Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 Cases Referred to: Merchant Bank of Sri Lanka Ltd. vs. D.V.D.A. Tillekeratne - [2001] B.A.L.R. 71; Ganeshanathan vs. Vivienne Gunawardene - [1984] 1 S.L.R. 319; Mohamed v. Annamalai Chettiar - [1932] Ceylon Law Recorder; Alice Kotalawela vs. W.H. Perera - [1937] 1 CLJ 58. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the High Court had jurisdiction to entertain the RDA's application regarding the appointment of an arbitrator. Rule applied: Section 7 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the provisions of the Arbitration Act, particularly Section 7, which allows parties to agree on a procedure for appointing arbitrators. The Court noted that the RDA's application to the High Court was based on the assertion that the Petitioner had violated the contractual obligations by unilaterally appointing an arbitrator. The Petitioner contended that the High Court's intervention was unwarranted since the parties had already established a clear procedure for appointing arbitrators in their contract. The Court emphasized the principle of party autonomy in arbitration, which is a fundamental tenet of arbitration law, as highlighted in the case of Merchant Bank of Sri Lanka Ltd. vs. Tillekeratne. The Supreme Court reiterated that the parties are free to select arbitrators of any nationality, and the High Court's ruling undermined this principle. The Court further noted that the RDA's refusal to select an arbitrator from the Petitioner’s list was not justified under the contract terms. The High Court's conclusion that there was no agreement between the parties regarding the appointment of arbitrators was found to be erroneous, as the contract explicitly provided a mechanism for such appointments. Finding: The Supreme Court found that the High Court had overstepped its jurisdiction by intervening in a matter where the parties had already agreed on a procedure for appointing arbitrators. Issue 2: Whether the Petitioner was entitled to invoke the inherent jurisdiction of the Supreme Court to set aside the High Court's order. Rule applied: Inherent jurisdiction of the Supreme Court. Court's reasoning: The Petitioner argued that the absence of a statutory provision allowing for an appeal against the High Court's order placed it in a precarious position, necessitating the invocation of the Supreme Court's inherent jurisdiction. The Court acknowledged the inherent powers of the Supreme Court to ensure justice and rectify situations where statutory provisions may not provide adequate remedies. However, the Court also emphasized that inherent powers should be exercised cautiously and only when justified by the facts of the case. The Supreme Court referenced previous judgments that established the limits of inherent jurisdiction, noting that it cannot be invoked when a statutory remedy exists. The Court concluded that since the Arbitration Act provided specific remedies for challenging arbitral awards, the Petitioner should have pursued those remedies rather than seeking inherent jurisdiction. Finding: The Supreme Court determined that it was unnecessary to exercise its inherent jurisdiction in this case, as the statutory framework provided adequate avenues for addressing the Petitioner’s grievances. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as they both concern the jurisdictional boundaries of the High Court and the Supreme Court in arbitration matters. The determination of whether the High Court had jurisdiction directly impacts the Petitioner’s ability to invoke the Supreme Court's inherent powers. The Supreme Court's analysis highlighted the importance of adhering to established legal frameworks and the principle of party autonomy in arbitration. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the Petitioner’s application, affirming the High Court's order and emphasizing the need to respect the contractual agreements between parties in arbitration. The Court underscored the importance of statutory provisions in arbitration law and the limitations of inherent jurisdiction in the absence of statutory gaps. The ruling reinforced the principle that parties must adhere to the agreed-upon procedures for dispute resolution as outlined in their contracts. Keywords inherent jurisdiction — arbitration — appointment of arbitrators — party autonomy — Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 — High Court jurisdiction — legal principles — judicial review — due process — statutory provisions — legal remedies — construction contracts — dispute resolution — Sri Lankan law — foreign arbitrators — legal representation
Counsel: K. Kanag-Isvaran P.C. with S. Kanag-Isvaran for the Petitioner. A.H.M.D. Nawaz D.S.G. for the Respondent.

Parties: Srivaratharajan Pirashanthan v. University of Peradeniya and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Sathya Hettige, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — University appointments — Selection process — Qualifications for appointment — Experience requirement — Administrative action
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Srivaratharajan Pirashanthan, challenged the selection process for the post of Lecturer (Probationary)/Senior Lecturer Grade II/I in the Department of Tamil at the University of Peradeniya. The University advertised the vacancy on 12.08.2008, and the Petitioner, along with the 23rd Respondent, applied for the position. Both candidates possessed the basic qualifications for the post of Lecturer (Probationary). The Petitioner further contended that he met the requirements for the post of Senior Lecturer Grade II, whereas the 23rd Respondent did not. The selection committee evaluated the candidates and determined that the Petitioner was unsuitable for the Senior Lecturer position, citing a lack of required experience. Although the 7th Respondent later filed an affidavit alleging the Petitionerâ€™s past attendance as a temporary lecturer was unsatisfactory, this reason was not reflected in the official selection proceedings document. The Petitioner argued that he had completed the mandatory six years of experience by the application deadline of 17.09.2008. The Court found that the Petitioner was more qualified than the 23rd Respondent, who was ultimately selected for the post. Issues Whether the non-selection of the Petitioner for the post of Lecturer (Probationary)/Senior Lecturer Grade II/I constituted an infringement of the fundamental right to equality guaranteed under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the Petitioner satisfied the threshold requirement of six years of experience for the post of Senior Lecturer Grade II by the application deadline. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Infringement of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that the Petitioner possessed higher qualifications than the 23rd Respondent, who was selected for the post. The Court noted that the reasons provided in the 7th Respondent's affidavit regarding the Petitioner's past performance were not documented in the official selection proceedings. Consequently, the arbitrary exclusion of a more qualified candidate in favor of a less qualified one violated the principle of equality. Finding: The actions of the interview board members in selecting the 23rd Respondent over the Petitioner constituted an infringement of the Petitioner's fundamental rights under Article 12(1). Issue 2: Threshold experience requirement Rule applied: University Grants Commission Circular No. 721 dated 21st November 1997. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the timeline of the recruitment process. While the Petitioner was six or seven days short of the six-year experience requirement on the date of the advertisement (12.08.2008), he had successfully completed the full six years of experience by the application deadline of 17.09.2008. The Court concluded that the Petitioner met the threshold requirement within the stipulated timeframe. Finding: The Petitioner was suitably qualified for the post as he met the experience requirement by the application deadline. Interaction of Issues The findings are intrinsically linked; the determination that the Petitioner met the objective experience threshold (Issue 2) directly supported the conclusion that his non-selection was arbitrary and discriminatory (Issue 1). Because the Petitioner was objectively more qualified than the successful candidate, the selection process failed to adhere to the standards of equality required by the Constitution. Conclusion The Court declared that the actions of the relevant Respondents in selecting the 23rd Respondent infringed the Petitioner's fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The Court further declared that the Petitioner is entitled to be considered for the post of Lecturer (Probationary)/Senior Lecturer Grade II/I. No order was made as to costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — University appointments — Selection process — Qualifications for appointment — Experience requirement — Administrative action

Parties: Consolidated Steel Industries (Pvt) Ltd. v. People's Bank
Bench: Tilakawardene J; Saleem Marsoof, P.C. J; Imam J
Keywords: Civil Procedure Code — Service of Summons — Registered Office — Ex Parte Judgment — Default of Appearance — Jurisdiction — Corporate Entities — Reasonable Grounds — Burden of Proof — Interests of Justice — Appeal — High Court — Commercial Matters
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal by Consolidated Steel Industries (Pvt) Ltd. against an ex parte judgment and decree entered by the Commercial High Court on 31st August 2001. The appellant, a limited liability company, contended that it did not receive proper service of summons as mandated by the Civil Procedure Code, specifically Section 59(2)(a), which requires that summons be served at the registered office of a corporate entity. The respondent, People's Bank, claimed that summons had been served at the factory premises of the appellant. The appellant argued that the failure to appear in court was due to the lack of proper service, as the summons was not delivered to its registered office at No. 3 Fredrica Road, Colombo 6, but rather at its factory located at No. 237/4, Hekitta Road, Wattala. The appellant's Managing Director testified that the registered office was indeed at the Fredrica Road address, and no summons was served there. The case highlights the importance of proper service of process in ensuring that a court has jurisdiction over a defendant. Issues The primary issue for determination was whether the High Court erred in refusing to set aside the ex parte judgment and decree against the appellant. Specifically, the court needed to consider: 1. Whether the summons was served at the registered office of the appellant as required by law. 2. Whether the appellant had reasonable grounds for its default in appearance. 3. Whether the burden of proof regarding the service of summons lay with the appellant or the respondent. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code Ordinance No. 12 of 1895, as amended; Companies Act No. 7 of 2007. Cases Referred to: David Appuhamy v Yassasi Thero (1987) 1 SLR 253; Bandaranike vs. Times of Ceylon Ltd. (1984) 1 SLR 178; The Bank of Chettinad Ltd. v Thambiah et al 35 NLR 190; Ittepana v Hemawathie (1981) 1 SLR 476; L.M. Gladwin D. Mel v J. A.Neethasinghe [1994] Vol. V Part II BALR 24. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the summons was served at the registered office of the appellant. Rule applied: Section 59(2)(a) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court examined the evidence presented regarding the service of summons. The appellant's Managing Director asserted that the registered office was at No. 3 Fredrica Road, Colombo 6, and that no summons had been served there. The respondent claimed that summons was served at the factory on 27th April 2001. However, the Fiscal Officer's testimony raised doubts about the accuracy of the service. The court noted that the summons must be served at the registered office unless otherwise sanctioned by the court. The court emphasized that the respondent, as a state bank, should have known the correct registered address of the appellant and should have ensured proper service. The court also highlighted the importance of the registered office in corporate law, as it serves as the domicile of the company for legal purposes. The court found that the respondent failed to comply with the requirements of the Civil Procedure Code regarding service of summons. Finding: The court concluded that the summons was not properly served at the registered office, and thus the ex parte judgment should be set aside. Issue 2: Whether the appellant had reasonable grounds for its default in appearance. Rule applied: Section 86(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court considered whether the appellant had demonstrated reasonable grounds for its failure to appear. The appellant argued that the lack of proper service constituted a valid excuse for its default. The court noted that the burden of proof lay with the appellant to show that it had reasonable grounds for its default. The court found that the appellant had sufficiently demonstrated that it was not served at its registered office, which was crucial for establishing jurisdiction. The court emphasized that the interests of justice required that the appellant be given an opportunity to defend itself against the claims made by the respondent. Finding: The court determined that the appellant had reasonable grounds for its default and should be allowed to purge its default. Interaction of Issues The issues of proper service of summons and reasonable grounds for default are interrelated. The failure to serve summons at the registered office directly impacted the appellant's ability to appear in court. The court's analysis of the service of summons was critical in determining whether the appellant could be held accountable for its absence. The court's findings on both issues ultimately led to the conclusion that the ex parte judgment was unjust and should be set aside. Conclusion The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, set aside the order of the High Court dated 10th June 2003, and the ex parte judgment and decree dated 31st August 2001. The court directed the High Court to permit the appellant to file an answer and defend the action instituted by the respondent. The court did not make any order for costs, considering the circumstances of the case. Keywords Civil Procedure Code — Service of Summons — Registered Office — Ex Parte Judgment — Default of Appearance — Jurisdiction — Corporate Entities — Reasonable Grounds — Burden of Proof — Interests of Justice — Appeal — High Court — Commercial Matters
Counsel: Shantha Jayawardena with Duleeka Imbuldeniya for the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant; Kushan D’Alwis P.C with Hiran Jayasuriya for the Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent

Parties: Dissanayake v. Bodhinagoda
Bench: Thilakawardane, J; S.I. Imam, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Rule under Section 42(2) of the Judicature Act — Professional misconduct — Deceit — Malpractice — Notaries Ordinance — Attorney-at-Law — Disciplinary proceedings — Standard of proof — False attestation — Fraudulent deeds — Title search — Notarial duties
Summary: Facts The Respondent, an Attorney-at-Law, was issued a Rule under Section 42(2) of the Judicature Act No. 2 of 1978 to show cause why he should not be suspended or removed from practice for deceit and malpractice. The proceedings originated from a complaint by Mr. D.M.A.J. Dissanayake, who alleged that the Respondent facilitated the purchase of a land in Boralesgamuwa through fraudulent deeds. The Complainant was introduced to the Respondent by the purported seller, Eranga Lanka Jayasekera, who claimed the Respondent handled his family's legal matters. The Respondent assured the Complainant that the title was clear and that he had conducted a title search. Relying on this, the Complainant entered into an agreement and subsequently executed Deed No. 998, attested by the Respondent. It was later discovered that the land was actually owned by one Anura S. Hewawasam, who never sold the property. The Respondent had also attested a prior deed, No. 975, which falsely listed Hewawasam as the seller. The Complainant suffered significant financial loss, having to re-purchase the land from the actual owner. Although the Respondent initially agreed to settle the matter by paying the Complainant, he failed to honor the settlement. Evidence from the Land Registry confirmed that the Respondent failed to register the deeds in the proper sequence, provided erroneous prior registrations, and failed to submit the required monthly lists or nil lists to the Land Registry. The Respondent did not testify or lead evidence at the inquiry. Issues Whether the Respondentâ€™s conduct in attesting fraudulent deeds and providing a false title report constitutes deceit and malpractice under Section 42(2) of the Judicature Act No. 2 of 1978. Whether the Respondentâ€™s failure to comply with the Notaries Ordinance and his breach of the settlement agreement constitute professional misconduct warranting removal from the roll of Attorneys-at-Law. Applicable Law Statutes: Judicature Act No. 2 of 1978 Notaries Ordinance No. 1 of 1907 Supreme Court (Conduct of and Etiquette of Attorneys-at-Law) Rules 1988 Cases Referred to: Daniel v. Chandradeva - [1994/v2-sri-lr-1] In Re Arthenayake, Attorney-at-Law - [1987/v1-sri-lr-314] In Re Srilal Herath - [1987/v1-sri-lr-57] Analysis Issue 1: Deceit and malpractice Rule applied: Section 42(2) of the Judicature Act No. 2 of 1978, which empowers the Court to discipline an Attorney-at-Law for deceit or malpractice. Court's reasoning: The Court applied the standard of "comfortable satisfaction" as established in Daniel v. Chandradeva. The evidence, including the testimony of the Complainant and the actual owner, Anura S. Hewawasam, proved that the Respondent attested two fraudulent deeds and provided a false title report. The Respondent's failure to justify his actions or provide evidence despite the opportunity to do so led the Court to conclude that the Respondent intentionally misled the Complainant. Finding: The Respondentâ€™s actions constitute clear deceit and malpractice. Issue 2: Professional misconduct and removal Rule applied: Rules 60 and 61 of the Supreme Court (Conduct of and Etiquette of Attorneys-at-Law) Rules 1988 and Section 31 of the Notaries Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the Respondent failed to perform mandatory duties under the Notaries Ordinance, such as conducting proper title searches and submitting monthly lists to the Land Registry. Furthermore, his failure to honor the settlement agreement reached before the Bar Association of Sri Lanka demonstrated dishonorable conduct. Citing In Re Srilal Herath, the Court reasoned that an attorney who misappropriates funds and refuses to make restitution is unfit to be entrusted with the interests of clients. Finding: The Respondentâ€™s conduct is disgraceful and unworthy of an Attorney-at-Law, necessitating his removal from the roll. Interaction of Issues The findings of deceit and malpractice in the execution of fraudulent deeds directly inform the assessment of the Respondent's professional character. The failure to adhere to the Notaries Ordinance and the subsequent breach of the settlement agreement reinforce the conclusion that the Respondentâ€™s conduct was not a mere oversight but a pattern of professional dishonesty that renders him unfit to practice. Conclusion The Court finds the Respondent guilty of deceit and malpractice. The Respondent is removed from the roll of Attorneys-at-Law, and the Registrar of the Supreme Court is directed to remove his name accordingly. Keywords Rule under Section 42(2) of the Judicature Act — Professional misconduct — Deceit — Malpractice — Notaries Ordinance — Attorney-at-Law — Disciplinary proceedings — Standard of proof — False attestation — Fraudulent deeds — Title search — Notarial duties

Parties: International Dresses (Private) Limited v. Seneviratne and Others
Bench: S. Tilakawardane, J; S.I. Imam, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Industrial Disputes Act — Arbitration — Award — Judicial determination of facts — Re-instatement — Back wages — Workmen Compensation — Fairness and equity — Error of Law
Summary: Facts International Dresses (Private) Limited, the Petitioner-Appellant, sought a Writ of Certiorari to quash an Arbitral Award dated 10.01.2007. The Arbitrator, the 5th Respondent, had been appointed under the Industrial Disputes Act to resolve a dispute regarding the termination of services of the 6th to 26th Respondents. The Appellant alleged that the 13th Respondent had behaved in an unruly manner under the influence of alcohol and that other employees had subsequently engaged in violent behavior, obstructed production, and threatened management. Following internal disciplinary inquiries, the Appellant terminated the services of the 6th to 20th Respondents, while the 21st to 26th Respondents were treated as having vacated their posts for failing to report for work. The Respondents contended that the dispute arose due to the Appellant's opposition to the formation of a trade union branch. They argued that the Arbitrator had properly evaluated the evidence and that the terminations were acts of victimization. The Arbitrator concluded that the terminations were unfair and ordered the reinstatement of the 6th to 26th Respondents with back wages. The Court of Appeal affirmed this Award on 28.06.2010, leading the Appellant to appeal to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether an Arbitrator's observation that parties exaggerated facts is sufficient to establish that the findings are supported by evidence. Whether the Court of Appeal erred in failing to conclude that the Award was irrational for not offering the Appellant the option of paying compensation in lieu of reinstatement. Whether the Arbitrator failed to duly consider the evidence before making the order. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act Cases Referred to: Hayleys Ltd. v. De Silva - [64-nlr-130] Municipal Council of Colombo v. Munasinghe - [71-nlr-223] Ceylon Transport Board v. Ceylon Transport Workers Union - [71-nlr-158] Best Footwear (Pvt.) Ltd. v. The Minister of Labour - [1997/v2-sri-lr-137] Analysis Issue 1: Sufficiency of evidence Rule applied: The requirement for an Arbitrator to act judicially and decide based on the evidence before them. Court's reasoning: The Court noted that while the Arbitrator observed that both parties exaggerated their positions, this did not imply a failure to evaluate the evidence. The Arbitrator had analyzed the underlying causes of the dispute, including the management's hostility toward the union and the specific circumstances surrounding the 13th Respondent's salary advance. The Arbitrator's conclusion was based on a careful scrutiny of the events and the conduct of both parties. Finding: The observation regarding exaggeration did not invalidate the findings, as the Arbitrator had sufficiently considered the evidence. Issue 2: Option of compensation in lieu of reinstatement Rule applied: The principle that an Award must be "Just and Equitable" under the Industrial Disputes Act. Court's reasoning: The Court found that while the Arbitrator correctly determined that termination was too severe a punishment, the award of full back wages and allowances was unreasonable given that the employees' conduct had contributed to the unrest. Furthermore, the Court recognized that reinstatement might not always be feasible due to potential business closures or lack of vacancies. Finding: The Court varied the Award to allow for compensation based on basic salary in lieu of reinstatement if the Appellant is unable to reinstate the workmen. Issue 3: Failure to consider evidence Rule applied: The duty of an Industrial Court/Arbitrator to consider every material question involved in the dispute. Court's reasoning: The Court reviewed the domestic inquiry records and the Arbitrator's findings. It concluded that the Arbitrator had properly identified that the employees were victimized for trade union activities and that the "vacation of post" claim was unsupported by evidence. Finding: The Arbitrator did not fail to consider the evidence; the findings were supported by the record. Interaction of Issues The findings on the sufficiency of evidence and the evaluation of the facts reinforce the conclusion that the Arbitrator acted within his jurisdiction. The determination that the employees were unfairly terminated (Issue 3) necessitated the relief of reinstatement, but the Court's assessment of the "Just and Equitable" nature of the award (Issue 2) required a modification of the financial components to balance the interests of both parties. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed, and the Judgment of the Court of Appeal is affirmed subject to the variation that the back wages are limited to the basic salary and that the Appellant may pay compensation in lieu of reinstatement if reinstatement is not feasible. The heirs of the deceased respondent are entitled to compensation based on the basic salary for a period of years. Keywords Industrial Disputes Act — Arbitration — Award — Judicial determination of facts — Re-instatement — Back wages — Workmen Compensation — Fairness and equity — Error of Law

Parties: Competent Authority Pugoda Textiles Lanka Ltd. and Others v. Ratwatte and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, J.; Sripavan, J; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Special leave to appeal — Time bar — Amendment of petition — Jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal — Writ of mandamus — Supreme Court Rules — Notices to respondents — Acquiescence — Preliminary objections
Summary: Facts The Petitioners-Respondents, W.A. Richard Ratnasiri Pelpita and two thousand sixty-two others, obtained a writ of mandamus from the Court of Appeal against the Respondents-Appellants, Sumith Ediriwickrama and Charitha Ratwatte, to compel the payment of salary arrears for the period between May 1997 and December 1999. The Appellants filed an application for special leave to appeal against this judgment on 9 December 2003. Subsequently, the Appellants sought and were granted permission by the Supreme Court to file an amended petition, which was filed on 30 November 2004. Special leave to appeal was granted by the Supreme Court on 9 December 2004. Following the grant of leave, the matter underwent several postponements and attempts at amicable resolution, including an ex gratia payment of 10 million rupees by the Appellants to the Respondents. During the proceedings before the Supreme Court, the Respondents raised two preliminary objections regarding the non-compliance with Supreme Court Rules. Specifically, the Respondents contended that the amended petition was filed outside the mandatory time limit and that the Appellants failed to serve notices on all respondents as required by the rules. The Appellants argued that the amendments were made with court permission and that the Respondents had participated in the proceedings for nearly a decade, thereby waiving their right to object. Issues Whether the filing of an amended petition for special leave to appeal outside the six-week period prescribed by Rule 7 of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990, renders the appeal time-barred. Whether the failure to tender notices for service on all respondents in accordance with Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990, warrants the dismissal of the appeal in limine. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 128 Judicature Act, Section 39 Rehabilitation of Public Enterprises Act No. 29 Supreme Court Rules, 1990, Rules 2, 3, 7, and 8(3) Cases Referred to: A.H.M. Fowzie v. Vehicles Lanka (Pvt) Ltd - [2008] BLR 127 Tissa Attanayake v. The Commissioner General of Election - [sc-appeal-155/2011] Analysis Issue 1: Time-bar of the amended petition Rule applied: Rule 7 of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990, which mandates that applications for special leave to appeal be made within six weeks of the Court of Appeal judgment. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the original petition was filed within the statutory time limit. The amended petition was filed with the express permission of the court granted on 10 November 2004. Furthermore, the court observed that the Respondents failed to raise any objection to the amended petition at the time it was filed or during the subsequent years of litigation. The court held that raising such a technical objection nearly a decade later, after the case had proceeded to the merits stage, was impermissible. Finding: The preliminary objection regarding the time-bar is overruled. Issue 2: Non-compliance with notice requirements Rule applied: Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990, regarding the mandatory tendering of notices for service on respondents. Court's reasoning: While acknowledging that Rule 8(3) is mandatory and that non-compliance can lead to dismissal, the court emphasized that the Respondents had entered caveats and were represented by counsel throughout the proceedings. The court found that the Respondents had acquiesced to the process for nearly ten years and had even engaged in settlement negotiations. Consequently, the court determined that the Respondents were estopped by their conduct from raising this objection at such a late stage. Finding: The preliminary objection regarding non-compliance with Rule 8(3) is overruled. Interaction of Issues The courtâ€™s findings on both issues are reinforced by the principle of laches and the conduct of the parties. By failing to raise these procedural objections at the earliest opportunity and participating in the merits of the appeal for years, the Respondents effectively waived their right to challenge the procedural validity of the filings. The court viewed both objections as belated and technical, concluding that the interests of justice and the smooth functioning of the legal machinery required the case to proceed to a hearing on the merits. Conclusion The preliminary objections raised by the Respondents are overruled. The matter is to be fixed for hearing on the merits before a bench to be nominated by the Chief Justice. Keywords Special leave to appeal — Time bar — Amendment of petition — Jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal — Writ of mandamus — Supreme Court Rules — Notices to respondents — Acquiescence — Preliminary objections

Parties: Atapattu v. Janashakthi General Insurance Co. Ltd.
Bench: Hon. N.G. Amaratunga, J; Hon. Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Arbitration Act — Enforcement of arbitral awards — Setting aside arbitral awards — Fraud — Standard of proof — Public policy — Double insurance — Costs of arbitration — Certification of copies of arbitration agreement and award
Summary: Facts The Appellant, Kiran Atapattu, was the proprietor of Colombo Engineering Enterprises and a partner in Soul Enterprises. He obtained three insurance policies from the Respondent, Janashakthi General Insurance Co. Ltd., covering a Nissan lorry and various musical instruments and sound equipment. The Appellant claimed that while transporting these items from Kandy to Colombo, the lorry caught fire due to an electrical defect, resulting in the total destruction of the vehicle and its contents. The Respondent repudiated the claims, alleging that the Appellant had deliberately set the vehicle on fire and that the insured items were not present in the lorry at the time of the incident. The dispute was referred to an arbitral tribunal, which unanimously upheld the Appellant's claims, though the arbitrators differed on the quantum of the awards. The Respondent subsequently moved the High Court under Section 32 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 to set aside the awards, while the Appellant sought enforcement under Section 31. The High Court consolidated the applications and set aside the arbitral awards, ruling that the insurance policies were not properly certified and that the tribunal had applied the wrong standard of proof regarding the allegation of fraud. The Appellant appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court erred in law by rejecting the insurance policies on the ground that they were uncertified. Whether the arbitral tribunal applied an incorrect standard of proof regarding the allegation of fraud. Whether the arbitral awards were contrary to public policy due to alleged double insurance and the award of three sets of costs. Applicable Law Statutes: Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 Cases Referred to: Kristley (Pvt) Ltd. v The State Timber Corporation - [2002/v1-sri-lr-225] Janson v. Driefontein Consolidated Gold Mines Ltd - [1902] AC 484 Richardson v. Mellish - [1824] 2 Bing 229 Enderby Town Football Club Ltd. v. Football Association. Ltd. - [1971] Ch. 591 Oil & Natural Gas Corporation Ltd v. Saw Pipes Ltd - [AIR-2003-SC-2629] Beresford v. Royal Insurance Co - [1938] A.C. 586 Heyman v. Darwins - [1942] A.C. 356 Lakshmanan Chettiar v. Muttiah Chettiar - [50-nlr-337] Yoosoof v. Rajaratnam - [74-nlr-9] Associated Battery Manufacturers (Ceylon) Ltd. v. United Engineering Workers Union - [77-nlr-541] Caledonian Estate Ltd. v. Hilaman - [79-1-nlr-421] Re H (Minors) - [1996] AC 563 Francis Samarawickrema v. Dona Enatto Hilda Jayasinghe - [2009/v1-sri-lr-293] Soysa v. Sanmugam - [10-nlr-355] R v. Perera - [31-nlr-449] Gratiaen Perera v. The Queen - [61-nlr-522] Samarakoon v. Public Trustee - [65-nlr-100] Cresswall v. Jackson - [1860] F & F 24 Abinger v. Ashton - [1874] LR 17 Light Weight Body Armour Ltd. v. Sri Lanka Army - [2007/balr-10] Analysis Issue 1: Certification of documents Rule applied: Section 31(2) of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. Court's reasoning: The High Court erred by strictly rejecting the insurance policies for lack of formal certification. Following the precedent in Kristley (Pvt) Ltd. v. The State Timber Corporation, the court held that Section 31(2) allows for certification to the satisfaction of the court. Since the documents were admitted by both parties at the arbitration and relied upon by the Respondent in its own application, the High Court should have accepted them. Finding: The High Court misdirected itself in law; the documents were sufficiently authenticated. Issue 2: Standard of proof for fraud Rule applied: Principles of evidence regarding fraud in civil proceedings. Court's reasoning: The High Court incorrectly held that the tribunal applied an overly strict standard of proof. The tribunal correctly recognized that while the civil standard of balance of probabilities applies, the degree of proof required is higher for serious allegations like fraud. The tribunal's evaluation of the expert evidence and the Appellant's testimony was consistent with established legal principles. Finding: The tribunal did not err in its application of the law; the High Court's interference was unjustified. Issue 3: Public policy challenges Rule applied: Sections 32(1)(b)(ii) and 34(1)(b)(ii) of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. Court's reasoning: The court found no evidence of double insurance after comparing the itemized lists in the policies. Regarding costs, the court held that an arbitral tribunal has the discretion to award costs, and the amounts granted were reasonable despite the consolidation of claims. Finding: The awards were not contrary to public policy. Interaction of Issues The findings on the standard of proof and the admissibility of evidence reinforce the conclusion that the arbitral tribunal acted within its jurisdiction and in accordance with the law. The High Court's failure to properly assess the evidence led to an erroneous conclusion that the awards violated public policy. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgment of the High Court is set aside, and the Respondent's application to set aside the arbitral awards is refused. The Appellant's application for enforcement is allowed, and the High Court is directed to enter decree in terms of the arbitral awards. Keywords Arbitration Act — Enforcement of arbitral awards — Setting aside arbitral awards — Fraud — Standard of proof — Public policy — Double insurance — Costs of arbitration — Certification of copies of arbitration agreement and award

Parties: Alles and Vithanage v. Road Passenger Services Authority of the Western Province and Others
Bench: Marsoof, P.C., J; Sripavan, J; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Time bar — Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka Act — Preliminary objection — Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Cancellation of interview — Transparency in promotions — Disciplinary investigations
Summary: Facts The Petitioners, Amura Deshapriya Alles and Gamunu Thissa Lankathillaka Vithanage, were employees of the 1st Respondent, the Road Passenger Services Authority of the Western Province. In September 2007, the 1st Respondent called for internal applications for the post of Assistant Manager (Transport) Grade 4. The Petitioners applied and participated in an interview held on 8th May 2008. The Petitioners alleged that they obtained the highest marks at this interview. Subsequently, without notice of cancellation of the previous process, the Petitioners were directed to attend another interview on 4th December 2008. The Petitioners later discovered that the 17th and 18th Respondents were selected for the posts and their appointments were ante-dated to 19th January 2009. The Petitioners filed a complaint with the Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka on 19th February 2009 and subsequently invoked the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court under Article 126 of the Constitution on 8th June 2009. The 17th and 18th Respondents raised a preliminary objection at the hearing, contending that the application was time-barred. Issues Whether the application is time-barred under Article 126(2) of the Constitution, given the failure of the 17th and 18th Respondents to raise the objection in their pleadings or written submissions. Whether the selection process for the post of Assistant Manager (Transport) Grade 4 violated the Petitioners' fundamental right to equality guaranteed by Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1), Article 126 Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka Act No. 21 of 1996, Section 13(1), Section 14 Cases Referred to: De Silva v. Wickramarathne - [2011/v2-blr-360] Romesh Corray v. L.A.S. Jayalath - [2008/v2-blr-169] Analysis Issue 1: Time-bar Rule applied: Supreme Court Rules 1990, Rules 30(4), 30(6), 30(7), 45(6), 45(7), and 45(8). Court's reasoning: The Court held that preliminary objections must be raised at the earliest opportunity, specifically in the statement of objections or written submissions, to avoid surprising the opposing party and the Court. The 17th and 18th Respondents failed to provide notice of the time-bar objection in their pleadings or prior written submissions. Relying on Romesh Corray v. L.A.S. Jayalath, the Court emphasized that the purpose of the Rules is to ensure transparency and fairness. Furthermore, Section 13(1) of the Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka Act tolls the time limit for filing a fundamental rights application while a complaint is pending before the Commission. Finding: The preliminary objection is overruled due to procedural failure by the Respondents and the statutory tolling of the time period. Issue 2: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the 1st Respondent admitted in proceedings before the Human Rights Commission that the Petitioners had obtained the highest marks at the initial interview. The subsequent interview held on 4th December 2008 lacked transparency, as no valid reason was provided for discarding the first interview results. The Respondents failed to produce the marking sheet for the first interview, and the Court found the justifications for the second interview to be unsubstantiated and inconsistent with the evidence. Finding: The fundamental rights of the Petitioners under Article 12(1) were violated. Interaction of Issues The procedural failure of the Respondents to raise the time-bar in their pleadings prevented them from effectively challenging the factual basis of the Petitioners' compliance with the Human Rights Commission Act. By failing to follow the Supreme Court Rules, the Respondents were precluded from asserting a technical defense that would have otherwise required the Petitioners to produce specific documentation regarding their complaint to the Commission. Conclusion The Court declares that the fundamental rights of the Petitioners under Article 12(1) of the Constitution were violated. The appointments of the 17th and 18th Respondents are quashed, and the 1st Respondent is directed to hold a fresh, proper interview process for the post. No order for costs or compensation is made. Keywords Time bar — Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka Act — Preliminary objection — Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Cancellation of interview — Transparency in promotions — Disciplinary investigations

Parties: Fernando v. Dayawansa and Others
Bench: Shiranee Tilakawardane, J; P. A. Ratnayake PC, J; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Article 11 of the Constitution — Torture — Cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment — Arrest — Police investigation — Medical evidence — Burden of proof
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Warnakulasooriya Sunil Asoka Harischandra Fernando, was employed as a machine operator at Mangalika Oil Mill. Following a theft of desiccated coconut at the mill, the Petitioner alleged that he was arrested at his home on 02.07.2010 by the 1st Respondent and another police officer from the Madampe Police Station. The Petitioner claimed that he was subsequently detained and subjected to severe torture by the 1st Respondent on 03.07.2010, including being stripped, suspended, and assaulted with a baton, and having his face covered with a bag containing chili powder. The Petitioner and another laborer, Leon Singho, were produced before the Chilaw Magistrate on 05.07.2010 and remanded. The Petitioner alleged that he informed a prison officer of the assault upon his arrival at the remand prison. After being released on bail on 15.07.2010, the Petitioner was admitted to the Chilaw Government Hospital, where he was treated for fractured ribs and other injuries. The 1st, 2nd, and 3rd Respondents denied the allegations, asserting that the Petitioner was arrested on 04.07.2010 based on information from a private informant and that no torture occurred. Issues Whether the Petitioner was subjected to torture, cruel, inhuman, or degrading treatment or punishment in violation of Article 11 of the Constitution. Whether the Petitioner met the required standard of proof to establish a violation of Article 11 of the Constitution by the 1st to 3rd Respondents. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 11 Penal Code, Section 369 Penal Code, Section 443 Cases Referred to: Premadasa v. O/C Hakmana - [sc-appeal-127/94] Lucas Appuhamy v. Maturata - [1994/v1-sri-lr-401] Thadchanamoorthi v. AG - [1980/v1-frd-129] Velumurugu v. Attorney General - [1981/v1-frd-180] Goonewardene v. Perera - [1983/v1-sri-lr-305] Kapugeekiyana v. Hettiarachchi - [1984/v2-sri-lr-153] Malinda Channa Pieris v. Attorney General - [1994/v1-sri-lr-6] Jeganathan v. Attorney General - [1982/v1-sri-lr-302] Namasivayam v. Gunawardena - [1989/v1-sri-lr-401] Edward Sivalingam v. S.I. Jayasekera - [sc-fr-326/2008] Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 11 Rule applied: Article 11 of the Constitution, which prohibits torture or cruel, inhuman, or degrading treatment or punishment. Court's reasoning: The court examined the medical evidence provided by the Judicial Medical Officer. While the Petitioner alleged severe torture, the medical report and admission forms did not substantiate these claims. The injuries noted, such as superficial skin exfoliation and wrist abrasions, were consistent with the application of handcuffs during a standard arrest rather than the brutal torture described by the Petitioner. Furthermore, the court noted discrepancies in the dates of the alleged assault within the medical documentation. Finding: The Petitioner failed to establish that he was subjected to treatment that constitutes a violation of Article 11. Issue 2: Standard of Proof Rule applied: The civil standard of persuasion, requiring a high degree of certainty on a balance of probabilities, as established in Malinda Channa Pieris v. Attorney General and Edward Sivalingam v. S.I. Jayasekera. Court's reasoning: The court held that allegations of torture against public officers carry serious consequences and must be strictly proved. In the absence of independent corroborative evidence, the court relied on the testimonial creditworthiness of the Petitioner. Given the conflicting accounts of the arrest date and the lack of medical evidence supporting the specific allegations of torture, the court found the Petitioner's version of events lacked the necessary degree of probability. Finding: The Petitioner failed to meet the high threshold of proof required to establish a violation of fundamental rights. Interaction of Issues The failure to meet the standard of proof (Issue 2) directly resulted in the court's inability to find a substantive violation of Article 11 (Issue 1). The lack of credible evidence regarding the circumstances of the arrest and the nature of the injuries meant that the Petitioner could not satisfy the legal requirements to trigger the protections of Article 11. Conclusion The application of the Petitioner is dismissed. No costs are awarded. Keywords Fundamental rights — Article 11 of the Constitution — Torture — Cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment — Arrest — Police investigation — Medical evidence — Burden of proof

Parties: Ratnayake v. Administrative Appeals Tribunal and Others
Bench: Marsoof, P.C., J; Ratnayake, J; S.I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Special leave to appeal — Article 61A of the Constitution — Administrative Appeals Tribunal Act No.4 — Ouster clause — Jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal — Article 140 of the Constitution — Public Service Commission — Appellate tribunal — Constitutional validity
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Mr. A.M. Ratnayake, was subjected to a disciplinary inquiry following the issuance of a charge sheet in April 2003. Upon being found guilty of the charges, the Public Service Commission dismissed the Petitioner from service on 12 January 2007. The Petitioner appealed this dismissal to the Administrative Appeals Tribunal, which affirmed the decision of the Public Service Commission. Following a previous legal challenge where the parties agreed to refer the matter back to the Administrative Appeals Tribunal due to issues regarding its constitution, the Tribunal reheard the appeal. On 22 February 2011, the Administrative Appeals Tribunal issued an order dismissing the Petitioner's appeal and upholding the termination of his services. The Petitioner subsequently filed an application for writs of certiorari and mandamus in the Court of Appeal to challenge the order of the Administrative Appeals Tribunal. The Court of Appeal refused to issue notice on the application. The Petitioner then sought special leave to appeal to the Supreme Court against the decision of the Court of Appeal. Issues Whether the ouster of jurisdiction contained in Article 61A of the Constitution applies to decisions made by the Administrative Appeals Tribunal. Whether the Court of Appeal possesses the jurisdiction to hear and determine an application challenging a decision of the Administrative Appeals Tribunal. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka (Article 59(1), Article 61A, Article 126, Article 128, Article 140) Administrative Appeals Tribunal Act No. 4 (Section 8(2)) Interpretation Ordinance No. 21 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of Article 61A Rule applied: Article 61A of the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka. Court's reasoning: The court observed that Article 61A specifically restricts the jurisdiction of courts regarding orders or decisions made by the Public Service Commission, a Committee, or a public officer acting under delegated authority. The court reasoned that the Administrative Appeals Tribunal is an independent appellate body established under Article 59(1) of the Constitution, not a body exercising powers delegated by the Public Service Commission. Therefore, the constitutional ouster of jurisdiction provided in Article 61A does not extend to the decisions of the Administrative Appeals Tribunal. Finding: The preliminary objection based on Article 61A is overruled as it does not apply to the Administrative Appeals Tribunal. Issue 2: Jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal Rule applied: Article 140 of the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka. Court's reasoning: The court determined that because the Administrative Appeals Tribunal is an appellate body with the authority to alter, vary, or rescind decisions of the Public Service Commission, it operates outside the scope of the constitutional restrictions placed on the Commission itself. Consequently, the Court of Appeal retains its inherent jurisdiction under Article 140 to review the decisions of the Administrative Appeals Tribunal. The court noted that the state's objection was limited solely to Article 61A and did not address the potential impact of Section 8(2) of the Administrative Appeals Tribunal Act or Section 22 of the Interpretation Ordinance. Finding: The Court of Appeal possesses the necessary jurisdiction to hear and determine the Petitioner's application. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are interdependent; by determining that the constitutional ouster in Article 61A is inapplicable to the Administrative Appeals Tribunal, the court effectively removed the primary legal barrier preventing the Court of Appeal from exercising its supervisory jurisdiction. This resolution confirms that the Petitioner's challenge is not barred by the specific constitutional provision cited by the Respondents. Conclusion The preliminary objection raised by the Respondents is overruled. The application for special leave to appeal is to be fixed for support on a date convenient to the court, with no order for costs. Keywords Special leave to appeal — Article 61A of the Constitution — Administrative Appeals Tribunal Act No.4 — Ouster clause — Jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal — Article 140 of the Constitution — Public Service Commission — Appellate tribunal — Constitutional validity

Parties: Kiran Atapattu v. Janashakthi General Insurance Co. Ltd.
Bench: N.G. Amaratunga, J.; Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J.; S. I. Imam, J.
Keywords: Arbitration Act — Insurance Claims — Fraud — Burden of Proof — Public Policy — Double Insurance — Costs of Arbitration — Enforcement of Awards — Standard of Proof — Civil Proceedings — Good Faith — Evidence — Expert Testimony — Arbitral Tribunal — Judicial Review
Summary: Facts In this case, Kiran Atapattu, the Claimant-Petitioner-Appellant, sought to challenge the consolidated judgment of the High Court which set aside three arbitral awards made in his favor by a tribunal of three arbitrators. The awards were related to claims made under three insurance policies issued by Janashakthi General Insurance Co. Ltd., the Respondent. The background of the case involves a Nissan lorry insured under a comprehensive motor vehicle policy, along with two other policies covering musical instruments and sound equipment. On July 5, 1998, the lorry, carrying these items to a dinner dance in Kandy, caught fire on its return journey, resulting in the destruction of the vehicle and its contents. The Appellant claimed a total of Rs. 7,531,500 from the Respondent, which included the value of the lorry and the musical equipment. However, the Respondent repudiated the claim, alleging that the fire was deliberately set by the Appellant. The dispute was referred to arbitration, where the arbitrators unanimously upheld the Appellant's claims, although they differed on the quantum of the awards. The Respondent subsequently moved the High Court to set aside the awards, leading to the impugned judgment that the Appellant now appeals against. Issues The main issues for determination by the Supreme Court include: Whether the High Court Judge misdirected himself in law regarding the burden of proof of fraud. Whether the High Court Judge misapplied the burden of proof for establishing fraud in civil proceedings. Whether the High Court Judge erred in rejecting the insurance policies on the grounds of them being uncertified. Whether the High Court Judge failed to consider the evidence led in the arbitration proceedings.
