The Registrar was directed to communicate the suspension to the relevant authorities to ensure compliance with the order. The Court did not make an order for costs, reflecting the serious nature of the proceedings and the focus on upholding professional integrity. Keywords Judicature Act No 02 of 1978 — Attorney-at-Law — Professional Misconduct — Forgery — Fraudulent Deeds — Ethics Committee — Disciplinary Action — Notaries Ordinance No 1 of 1907 — Conviction — Suspension — Legal Profession — Integrity — Malpractice — Deceit — Sentencing Principles
Counsel: Rohan Sahabandu, PC, for the Bar Association of Sri Lanka; Ms. M.N.B. Fernando, Deputy Solicitor General for the Attorney General; G.R.D. Obeysekara for the Respondent.

Parties: Ven. Walahahangunawewa Dhammarathana Thero and Rajamaha Viharaya Mihintale v. Mahanama and WeerarathnaWaduge and Inspector General of Police and Attorney General
Bench: Marsoof, P.C., J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Arrest without warrant — Reasonable suspicion — Credible information — Bailable offences — Bail Act — Remand — Role of the Magistrate — Human Rights Commission — Self-defence
Summary: Facts The 1st Petitioner is the Viharadhikari of the Mihintale Rajamaha Viharaya, and the 2nd Petitioner is a samanera bhikku. On 12.03.2009, the 2nd Petitioner observed the 2nd Respondent and another individual interfering with the bags of pilgrims. When the 2nd Petitioner intervened, he was assaulted and threatened with a spray can, leading him to strike the 2nd Respondent with an eckle broom in self-defense. The 2nd Respondent subsequently filed a complaint with the Mihintale Police. On 14.03.2009, police officers arrived at the temple and insisted that the Petitioners accompany them to the police station. The 1st Petitioner, who had previously implicated senior police officers in a separate legal matter, expressed concern regarding bias. Eventually, the Petitioners voluntarily attended the police station with legal counsel. Upon arrival, the 1st Respondent, the Officer-in-Charge of the Mihintale Police, ordered their arrest and detention despite the availability of bail. The Petitioners were produced before an Acting Magistrate, who remanded them until 18.03.2009. A permanent Magistrate later granted bail on 16.03.2009. The Petitioners alleged that their fundamental rights under Articles 12(1), 13(1), and 13(2) of the Constitution were violated. Issues Whether the arrest and detention of the Petitioners by the 1st Respondent were in accordance with the procedure established by law under Section 32(1) of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979. Whether the refusal to grant bail and the subsequent remand of the Petitioners violated the principles established by the Bail Act No. 30 of 1997. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 12(1), 13(1), 13(2) Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979, Section 32(1) Penal Code, Sections 89, 314, 316 Bail Act No. 30 of 1997, Sections 2, 6, 16 Cases Referred to: Muttusamy v. Kannangara - [52-nlr-324] Corea v. The Queen - [55-nlr-457] Analysis Issue 1: Lawfulness of the arrest Rule applied: Section 32(1) of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979. Court's reasoning: The court held that a police officer's power to arrest without a warrant requires a "reasonable complaint" or "reasonable suspicion." The mere receipt of a complaint is insufficient; the officer must objectively verify the credibility of the information. In this case, the 1st Respondent failed to investigate the Petitioners' claims of self-defense and alibi before effecting the arrest. The court noted that the 1st Respondent's actions appeared motivated by a desire to remand the Petitioners rather than to conduct a lawful investigation. Finding: The arrest and detention were not in accordance with the law and violated Article 13(1) of the Constitution. Issue 2: Compliance with the Bail Act Rule applied: Bail Act No. 30 of 1997, Sections 2, 6, and 16. Court's reasoning: The court emphasized that under Section 2 of the Bail Act, bail is the rule and refusal is the exception. Section 6 mandates that for bailable offenses, police should release suspects on a personal undertaking rather than producing them for remand. The 1st Respondent failed to consider these provisions, and the Acting Magistrate failed to provide reasons for the refusal of bail as required by Section 16. The court found that the 1st Respondent effectively dictated the remand order to the Acting Magistrate. Finding: The refusal of bail and the resulting remand were contrary to the guiding principles of the Bail Act. Interaction of Issues The court's findings on the lack of a "reasonable complaint" (Issue 1) directly informed the conclusion that the subsequent remand (Issue 2) was an abuse of process. The failure to investigate the credibility of the complaint meant there was no objective basis for the arrest, which in turn rendered the refusal of bail an arbitrary exercise of power, reinforcing the violation of the Petitioners' fundamental rights. Conclusion The court held that the arrest and detention of the Petitioners violated their fundamental rights under Article 13(1) of the Constitution. The 1st Respondent is ordered to pay Rs. 25,000 as compensation to each of the Petitioners. Keywords Fundamental rights — Arrest without warrant — Reasonable suspicion — Credible information — Bailable offences — Bail Act — Remand — Role of the Magistrate — Human Rights Commission — Self-defence

Parties: Najimuddin and Shahabdeen v. Nageshwari and Others
Bench: Marsoof, PC.J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Appeal from High Court — District Court judgment — Subletting — Ex-parte judgment — Admission of facts — Evidence Ordinance — Affidavit evidence — Heresay evidence — Landlord's rights — Tenancy agreement — Wrongful subletting
Summary: Facts The Plaintiffs initiated an action in the District Court of Kandy to eject the 1st, 2nd, 3rd, and 4th Defendants from business premises on the grounds of unauthorized subletting. The 1st Defendant was the original tenant, who sublet the premises to the 2nd Defendant. The 2nd and 3rd Defendants subsequently sublet the premises to the 4th Defendant. All Defendants filed answers in the District Court. The 1st, 2nd, and 3rd Defendants admitted the subletting in their respective pleadings but failed to appear at the trial, resulting in an ex-parte judgment against them. The 4th Defendant contested the matter inter partes, claiming he had begun paying rent to a third party, the Natha Devale, after discovering the ownership status of the premises. The Plaintiffs led evidence through the 2nd Plaintiff and the record keeper of the Primary Court, who produced records from a prior Primary Court case involving the 2nd, 3rd, and 4th Defendants. The 4th Defendant did not testify or lead evidence at the trial. The District Court entered judgment in favor of the Plaintiffs, finding that subletting was established. The 4th Defendant appealed to the Civil Appellate High Court, which set aside the judgment against the 4th Defendant and, additionally, set aside the ex-parte judgment against the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd Defendants. The Plaintiffs appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Civil Appellate High Court acted in excess of its jurisdiction by setting aside the ex-parte judgment against the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd Defendants. Whether the Civil Appellate High Court erred in holding that the affidavits tendered by the 4th Defendant in previous proceedings constituted inadmissible hearsay evidence. Whether the Civil Appellate High Court erred in disregarding evidence placed by the Plaintiffs that was not challenged by the defense at trial. Applicable Law Statutes: Primary Court Act No. 44 of 1979 Evidence Ordinance Rent Act Cases Referred to: Perera v. Seneviratne - [77-nlr-403] Ratnaweera v. Nandawathie Fernando - [1998/v2-sri-lr-299] Edrick de Silva v. Chandradasa de Silva - [70-nlr-169] Sri Lanka Ports Authority v. Jugolinja-Boal East - [1981/v1-sri-lr-18] Balapitiya Gunananda Thero v. Talalle Methananda Thero - [1997/v2-sri-lr-101] Analysis Issue 1: Jurisdiction regarding ex-parte judgment Rule applied: The principle that facts admitted in pleadings do not require proof. Court's reasoning: The 1st, 2nd, and 3rd Defendants admitted the subletting in their pleadings and did not appeal the District Court's ex-parte judgment. The High Court lacked a basis to set aside a judgment against parties who had admitted the cause of action and had not challenged the original decree. Finding: The High Court acted in excess of its jurisdiction by setting aside the ex-parte judgment against the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd Defendants. Issue 2: Admissibility of affidavits Rule applied: The principle that statements made in prior judicial proceedings are relevant to contradict a party's current position. Court's reasoning: The High Court incorrectly applied the Evidence Ordinance to exclude affidavits that were relevant to the 4th Defendant's prior admissions. These documents were admissible to establish the standing taken by the parties in previous litigation. Finding: The High Court erred in rejecting the affidavits as hearsay. Issue 3: Disregarding unchallenged evidence Rule applied: The principle that documents admitted into evidence without objection at trial constitute lawful evidence. Court's reasoning: The Plaintiffs' documents (P1 to P13) were read into evidence without objection from the 4th Defendant. Furthermore, the 4th Defendant failed to testify or provide any evidence to contradict the Plaintiffs' case. Under the rule in Edrick de Silva v. Chandradasa de Silva, the failure to lead evidence to contradict the Plaintiff's case strengthens the Plaintiff's position. Finding: The High Court erred in disregarding the evidence, as the facts of subletting were either admitted in pleadings or supported by unchallenged documentary evidence. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing: because the subletting was admitted in the pleadings, the necessity for strict proof was diminished. The High Court's failure to recognize these admissions led to the erroneous exclusion of supporting documentary evidence and the improper interference with the ex-parte judgment against the non-appealing defendants. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court is set aside, and the judgment of the District Court dated 05.03.2003 is affirmed. The Plaintiffs are granted the reliefs prayed for in the plaint, with costs awarded to the Appellants. Keywords Appeal from High Court — District Court judgment — Subletting — Ex-parte judgment — Admission of facts — Evidence Ordinance — Affidavit evidence — Heresay evidence — Landlord's rights — Tenancy agreement — Wrongful subletting

Parties: Peoples Bank v. Good Fellows (Pvt) Ltd.
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Letters of credit — Documentary credits — Uniform Customs and Practices — Guarantees — Contract of sale — Issuing bank — Negotiating bank — Reimbursement — Recourse against seller — International Chamber of Commerce — Unjust enrichment
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Appellant, People's Bank, initiated legal action against the Defendant-Respondent, Good Fellows (Pvt) Ltd., in the Commercial High Court of Colombo to recover funds paid out under two Letters of Credit. The Respondent had exported goods to a buyer, Speed Control New York Inc., and the Appellant Bank negotiated the relevant bills and paid the Respondent. The Respondent executed guarantees in favor of the Appellant Bank to ensure repayment of these monies. The Appellant Bank alleged that the Respondent failed to repay the sums despite the issuing bank, Marine Midland Bank of New York, failing to remit payment. The Respondent contended that the Appellant Bank was solely responsible for seeking reimbursement from the issuing bank and that the Respondent was not liable. Furthermore, the Respondent argued that the matter was subject to arbitration before the International Chamber of Commerce and that the Appellant Bank could not pursue the Respondent until that process concluded. The Commercial High Court dismissed the Appellant Bank's case, ruling that the bank could only seek recovery from the issuing bank. The Appellant Bank appealed this decision, asserting that the action was based on the independent guarantees provided by the Respondent rather than solely on the Letters of Credit. Issues Whether the Appellant Bank is precluded from seeking recovery from the Respondent due to the existence of the Letters of Credit and the potential liability of the issuing bank. Whether the action instituted by the Appellant Bank is maintainable as a claim based on the guarantees provided by the Respondent. Whether the pending arbitration before the International Chamber of Commerce bars the Appellant Bank from pursuing the current litigation. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 754 (1) High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996 Cases Referred to: Goldets v. Czarnikow - [1979] All ER 726 Bankers Trust Co. v. State Bank of India - [1991] 2 Lloyds Rep 443 Analysis Issue 1: Recourse against the Respondent Rule applied: The court applied the principle that a bank may hold recourse against both the principal borrower (issuing bank) and the guarantor (the Respondent). Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that the transaction functioned similarly to a loan. While the Appellant Bank had a right of recourse against the issuing bank, it also held a contractual right of recourse against the Respondent as the seller/guarantor. The court clarified that while the bank may proceed against either or both, it is prohibited from recovering the total amount from both parties simultaneously, thereby avoiding unjust enrichment. Finding: The Appellant Bank is not restricted to seeking recovery solely from the issuing bank. Issue 2: Basis of the action Rule applied: Contractual principles governing guarantees. Court's reasoning: The court examined the documents marked P10 and P11. It determined that the Respondent had provided specific guarantees to the Appellant Bank, which included an unconditional undertaking to reimburse the bank and a waiver of the right to contest the nature of the claim. The court concluded that the action was fundamentally based on these guarantees rather than the underlying Letters of Credit. Finding: The action is maintainable as it is founded on the guarantees provided by the Respondent. Issue 3: Impact of arbitration Rule applied: Contractual waiver of rights. Court's reasoning: The court considered the Respondent's argument regarding the International Chamber of Commerce ruling. It found that the Respondent had explicitly waived its right to contest the amount or nature of the Appellant Bank's claim in the guarantees (P10 and P11). Consequently, the pending arbitration did not preclude the Appellant Bank from enforcing the guarantees. Finding: The pending arbitration does not bar the current litigation. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected; the determination that the action is based on independent guarantees (Issue 2) reinforces the conclusion that the bank has a right of recourse against the Respondent (Issue 1) regardless of the status of the Letters of Credit or the arbitration proceedings (Issue 3). The waiver of rights within the guarantees effectively neutralized the Respondent's procedural objections. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, and the judgment of the Commercial High Court is set aside. The Respondent is ordered to pay the Appellant Bank the sums claimed in the Plaint, including interest and statutory charges, with costs awarded to the Appellant Bank. Keywords Letters of credit — Documentary credits — Uniform Customs and Practices — Guarantees — Contract of sale — Issuing bank — Negotiating bank — Reimbursement — Recourse against seller — International Chamber of Commerce — Unjust enrichment

Parties: Araliya Impex (Private) Limited and Others v. Bank of Ceylon
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Appeal from High Court — Jurisdiction of High Court — Personal guarantee — Director's duties — Breach of fiduciary duty — Interest rate on loans — Evidence assessment — Commercial High Court
Summary: Facts Araliya Impex (Private) Limited, the first defendant-appellant, applied for credit facilities from the Bank of Ceylon, the plaintiff-respondent, on September 2, 1998. The company subsequently obtained a hypothecation loan secured by a mortgage bond and a joint and several guarantee provided by the second and third defendants-appellants, who were directors of the company. The guarantee bond was executed on the same date as the loan application. The company later borrowed additional funds under the hypothecation loan through several letters of request. The bank initiated legal action in the Commercial High Court of the Western Province to recover outstanding debts. The Commercial High Court found in favor of the bank on all issues. The defendants-appellants appealed this decision to the Supreme Court, challenging the court's jurisdiction, the nature of the personal guarantees provided by the directors, and the calculation of the interest rates and total sums due. Issues Whether the Commercial High Court had the jurisdiction to hear and determine the matter. Whether the second and third defendants-appellants provided personal guarantees for the hypothecation loan. Whether the amounts claimed by the plaintiff-respondent were supported by evidence and whether the interest rate of 26% was arbitrarily applied. Applicable Law Statutes: High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996 Companies Act No. 7 of 2007 Cases Referred to: Lister v. Romford Ice and Cold Storage Co. Ltd. - [1957] A.C. 555 Cornel and Company Ltd. v. Mitsui and Company Ltd. and Others - [2000/v1-sri-lr-57] Analysis Issue 1: Jurisdiction Rule applied: Section 2(1) of the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the Act grants the High Court exclusive jurisdiction over debt-related cases involving banking where the cause of action arises within the province and the amount exceeds the statutory threshold of Rs. 3 million. Since the parties were located in the Western Province and the claim exceeded the threshold, the Commercial High Court possessed the requisite authority. Finding: The Commercial High Court had proper jurisdiction to hear the matter. Issue 2: Personal Guarantees Rule applied: Section 189(a) of the Companies Act No. 7 of 2007; fiduciary duties of directors. Court's reasoning: The court examined the guarantee bond and observed that the signatories did not append the title "Director" to their signatures. Furthermore, the court held that directors have a fiduciary duty to read and understand documents signed on behalf of the company. The court reasoned that a guarantee signed by directors in their personal capacity is distinct from the company's obligations, and the failure to read the terms of the bond constituted a breach of their duty to act with reasonable skill and care. Finding: The second and third defendants-appellants provided personal guarantees and are liable for the debt up to the specified limit. Issue 3: Evidence and Interest Rule applied: Contractual terms of the Hypothecation Loan (P1) and Mortgage Bond (P3). Court's reasoning: The court found that the loan documents allowed the bank to vary interest rates. The companyâ€™s own correspondence requesting loans explicitly acknowledged the 26% interest rate. The court reviewed the statements of account and found no evidence to support the appellants' claim that interest figures were inserted post-signature. The appellants failed to object to the interest rates in writing during the loan period. Finding: The interest rate was not arbitrary, and the amounts claimed were supported by the evidence presented. Interaction of Issues The findings on the validity of the personal guarantees and the accuracy of the debt calculation reinforce the court's conclusion that the defendants-appellants were fully aware of their contractual obligations. The determination that the directors failed in their fiduciary duty to scrutinize the loan documents supports the finding that the interest rates and debt amounts were binding and not subject to arbitrary imposition. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The judgment of the Commercial High Court is affirmed, and the defendants-appellants are ordered to pay costs in the sum of Rs. 100,000 to the plaintiff-respondent. Keywords Appeal from High Court — Jurisdiction of High Court — Personal guarantee — Director's duties — Breach of fiduciary duty — Interest rate on loans — Evidence assessment — Commercial High Court

Parties: People's Bank v. Good Fellows (Pvt) Ltd.
Bench: Tilakawardane, J.; Ekanayake, J.; Dep, P.C., J.
Keywords: Letters of Credit — Guarantees — Recovery of Monies — Issuing Bank — Unjust Enrichment — International Chamber of Commerce — Commercial High Court — Civil Procedure Code — Contractual Obligations
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Appellant, People's Bank, initiated legal proceedings against the Defendant-Respondent, Good Fellows (Pvt) Ltd., in the Commercial High Court of Colombo, seeking to recover two sums totaling US $ 636,135.88 based on two Letters of Credit. The Appellant Bank claimed that it had negotiated these Letters of Credit and made payments to the Respondent, who subsequently failed to reimburse the amounts owed. The Respondent contested the claim, asserting that any liability for payment lay solely with the issuing bank, Marine Midland Bank of New York, and not with them. The High Court dismissed the Appellant's case on 10 June 2004, leading to this appeal. The Appellant contended that the learned judge erred in law by misinterpreting the nature of the action, which was based on guarantees provided by the Respondent, rather than solely on the Letters of Credit. The Respondent argued that the Appellant could not pursue this action while a related dispute was pending before the International Chamber of Commerce (ICC). Issues Whether the Appellant Bank has the right to recover the sums due from the Respondent based on the guarantees provided, despite the Respondent's claim that liability lies only with the issuing bank. Whether the Appellant can maintain this action while a ruling from the ICC is pending. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996. Cases Referred to: Goldets V Czarnikow (1979) All ER 726; Bankers Trust Co. V State Bank of India (1991) 2 Lloyds Rep 443. Analysis Issue 1: Right to Recover from the Respondent Rule applied: The principle of recourse in contractual obligations and guarantees. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the nature of the transaction between the Appellant and the Respondent, emphasizing that the Appellant's claim was based on the guarantees marked P10 and P11, which were executed by the Respondent. The Court noted that the learned High Court judge had incorrectly interpreted the Appellant's action as solely based on the Letters of Credit, overlooking the guarantees that provided a basis for recovery. The Court highlighted that the guarantees created a contractual obligation for the Respondent to reimburse the Appellant for the sums paid under the Letters of Credit. The Appellant Bank argued that the transaction was akin to a loan, where it could pursue either the principal borrower (the issuing bank) or the guarantor (the Respondent). The Court agreed, stating that the Appellant had a right of recourse against both the issuing bank and the Respondent, but could not recover from both simultaneously. The Court also addressed the Respondent's assertion that the Appellant could only seek recovery from the issuing bank, clarifying that the guarantees provided a valid basis for the Appellant's claim against the Respondent. Finding: The Court concluded that the Appellant Bank had the right to recover the sums due from the Respondent based on the guarantees. Issue 2: Maintenance of Action Pending ICC Ruling Rule applied: The principle of arbitration and the effect of pending disputes on legal actions. Court's reasoning: The Respondent contended that the Appellant could not maintain the action while awaiting a ruling from the ICC regarding the Letters of Credit. The Supreme Court analyzed the implications of the ICC proceedings and the nature of the guarantees provided by the Respondent. The Court noted that the guarantees explicitly stated that the Respondent would reimburse the Appellant unconditionally, regardless of any disputes regarding the Letters of Credit. The Court emphasized that the existence of the ICC proceedings did not preclude the Appellant from pursuing its claim against the Respondent, as the guarantees created an independent obligation to pay. The Court further clarified that the learned High Court judge's concerns regarding potential unjust enrichment of the Appellant were unfounded, as the Appellant could only recover from one party, either the issuing bank or the Respondent, but not both. Finding: The Court determined that the Appellant could maintain the action despite the pending ICC ruling. Interaction of Issues The issues of the Appellant's right to recover from the Respondent and the maintenance of the action pending the ICC ruling are interrelated. The determination of the Appellant's right to recover hinges on the interpretation of the guarantees, which were independent of the Letters of Credit and the ICC proceedings. The Court's analysis clarified that the guarantees provided a valid basis for recovery, irrespective of the ongoing arbitration. Conclusion The Supreme Court set aside the judgment of the learned High Court and ruled in favor of the Appellant Bank, ordering the Respondent to pay the total sums claimed, along with interest and costs. The Court affirmed the Appellant's right to recover based on the guarantees, emphasizing the independent nature of the obligations created by the guarantees. The appeal was allowed, and the Respondent was ordered to pay the Appellant Bank a total of US $ 636,135.88, along with interest at the rate of 9% per annum and statutory charges. Keywords Letters of Credit — Guarantees — Recovery of Monies — Issuing Bank — Unjust Enrichment — International Chamber of Commerce — Commercial High Court — Civil Procedure Code — Contractual Obligations
Counsel: Kushan D Alwis, PC, with Kaushalya Nawaratne and Hiran Jayasuriya for the Plaintiff-Appellant. M.U.M. Ali Sabry, PC, with Sanjeewa Dassanayake for the Defendant-Respondent.

Parties: Araliya Impex (Private) Limited and Others v. Bank of Ceylon
Bench: Tilakawardane, J.; Ekanayake, J.; Dep, P.C., J.
Keywords: Commercial High Court — Jurisdiction — Personal Guarantee — Hypothecation Loan — Interest Rate — Directors' Duties — Companies Act — Evidence Evaluation — Debt Recovery — Banking Law — Liability of Guarantors — Civil Procedure Code — High Court of Provinces (Special Provisions) Act — Appeal Process — Legal Principles
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal made by Araliya Impex (Private) Limited and its directors, Mylvaganam Rajkumar and Liyanage Mahesh Paul De Silva, against the Bank of Ceylon regarding a hypothecation loan. The Appellant Company applied for credit facilities amounting to Rs. 30 million on 2nd September 1998 and subsequently obtained a hypothecation loan secured by a mortgage bond and personal guarantees from its directors. The Commercial High Court found in favor of the Bank, leading to the appeal. The Appellants contested the findings of the High Court, arguing that the amounts claimed were arbitrary and that the court lacked jurisdiction. They also questioned the validity of the personal guarantees provided by the directors. The Appellants claimed that the interest rate charged was incorrect and that the guarantees were not sought at any stage. The Commercial High Court had previously ruled on these matters, leading to the current appeal. Issues The issues before the Supreme Court were framed as follows: 1. Whether there was evidence in support of the amounts claimed by the Plaintiff-Respondent and whether the amounts claimed had been arrived at arbitrarily. 2. Whether personal guarantees were sought from the 2nd and 3rd Defendants-Appellants at any stage. 3. Whether the Commercial High Court had jurisdiction to hear and determine this matter. Applicable Law Statutes: High Court of Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996; Civil Procedure Code; Companies Act No. 7 of 2007. Cases Referred to: Cornel and Company Ltd. Vs. Mitsui and Company Ltd. and Others - [2000] Vol.1 S.L.R. 57; Lister Vs. Romford Ice and Cold Storage Co. Ltd. - [1957] A.C. 555. Analysis Issue 1: Whether there was evidence in support of the amounts claimed by the Plaintiff-Respondent and whether the amounts claimed had been arrived at arbitrarily. Rule applied: The principle of evidence evaluation in civil proceedings. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the evidence presented in the Commercial High Court, including the hypothecation loan agreement and the personal guarantees signed by the directors. The Court noted that the Appellants had borrowed money under the hypothecation loan and that the guarantees provided by the 2nd and 3rd Appellants were valid and enforceable. The Court emphasized that the Appellants had acknowledged the interest rate of 26% in their correspondence with the Bank, which undermined their claim that the interest rate was arbitrary. The Court also highlighted that the Commercial High Court had thoroughly evaluated the evidence, including the statements of account, and had arrived at a reasonable conclusion regarding the amounts owed. The Court found that the Appellants had failed to provide sufficient evidence to support their claims of arbitrary amounts. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the Commercial High Court had correctly assessed the evidence and that the amounts claimed were not arbitrary. Issue 2: Whether personal guarantees were sought from the 2nd and 3rd Defendants-Appellants at any stage. Rule applied: The legal principle regarding the validity of personal guarantees. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court analyzed the guarantee bond signed by the 2nd and 3rd Appellants, which clearly indicated their liability as guarantors for the hypothecation loan. The Court noted that the guarantees were signed on the same date as the loan application, and the language of the guarantee bond indicated that the Appellants were providing personal guarantees, not acting in their capacity as directors. The Court emphasized that directors have a fiduciary duty to understand the agreements they sign and that failing to read the terms of the guarantee bond would constitute a breach of their duties. The absence of the title 'Director' under their signatures further supported the finding that they acted as personal guarantors. Finding: The Court affirmed that the 2nd and 3rd Appellants had indeed provided personal guarantees for the hypothecation loan. Issue 3: Whether the Commercial High Court had jurisdiction to hear and determine this matter. Rule applied: Jurisdictional provisions under the High Court of Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the jurisdictional claims made by the Appellants, referencing Section 7 of the High Court of Provinces (Special Provisions) Act. The Court noted that the High Court had exclusive jurisdiction over cases involving debts exceeding Rs. 3 million, and since the parties were located in the Western Province, the Commercial High Court in Colombo had the authority to hear the case. The Court cited the case of Cornel and Company Ltd. Vs. Mitsui and Company Ltd. and Others to support its conclusion regarding jurisdiction. Finding: The Supreme Court held that the Commercial High Court had the necessary jurisdiction to hear the case. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in this case are interrelated, as the validity of the personal guarantees directly impacts the determination of the amounts owed and the jurisdiction of the Commercial High Court. The Court's findings on the guarantees established the liability of the directors, which in turn validated the claims made by the Bank regarding the amounts owed. The assessment of the evidence and the jurisdictional authority of the Commercial High Court were also linked, as the Court's affirmation of jurisdiction was based on the nature of the claims and the amounts involved. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal, affirming the findings of the Commercial High Court. The Court ordered the Appellants to pay costs amounting to Rs. 100,000 to the Plaintiff-Respondent. The judgment reinforced the principles surrounding personal guarantees, the evaluation of evidence in civil proceedings, and the jurisdictional authority of the Commercial High Court in matters involving significant debts. Keywords Commercial High Court — Jurisdiction — Personal Guarantee — Hypothecation Loan — Interest Rate — Directors' Duties — Companies Act — Evidence Evaluation — Debt Recovery — Banking Law — Liability of Guarantors — Civil Procedure Code — High Court of Provinces (Special Provisions) Act — Appeal Process — Legal Principles
Counsel: M. Javed Mansoor for the Defendants-Appellants. S. Rajaratnam, DSG, with Fazly Razik, SC for the Plaintiff-Respondent.

Parties: Arasaratnam v. Wijewardena
Bench: N.G. Amaratunga, J; Saleem Marsoof, J.; S. Hettige, PC, J
Keywords: Partition Law — Delivery of possession — Restoration of possession — Section 52 of the Partition Law — Section 52A of the Partition Law — Interference with possession — Dispossession — Time limit — Monthly tenancy — Acquisition of title
Summary: Facts The Appellant instituted a partition action in the District Court of Colombo seeking to partition a land where he claimed an undivided half share, while his two brothers held the remaining shares. The District Court entered an interlocutory decree for the sale of the property, and the Appellant subsequently acquired the entirety of the corpus through the purchase of his brothers' shares. The Respondent, who occupied a house on the corpus, claimed tenancy rights and compensation for improvements. Following a final decree, the Appellant sought an order for delivery of possession under Section 52(1) of the Partition Law. When the Fiscal attempted to deliver possession, the Respondent resisted, citing his alleged tenancy. The Appellant unsuccessfully attempted to use Section 325(1) of the Civil Procedure Code to obtain possession, which the District Court rejected as an inappropriate procedure. Subsequently, the Appellant filed a fresh application for delivery of possession under Section 52(2)(a) of the Partition Law. The Respondent raised a preliminary objection, arguing that the application was time-barred under Section 52A of the Partition Law because it was filed more than twelve months after the initial resistance to the Fiscal. The District Court overruled the objection, but the Civil Appellate High Court allowed the Respondent's appeal, holding that the application was indeed time-barred under Section 52A. The Appellant then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the twelve-month time frame prescribed in Section 52A of the Partition Law applies to an application for delivery of possession under Section 52(2)(a) where the applicant has never been in physical possession of the corpus. Whether the Appellantâ€™s application for delivery of possession was time-barred under Section 52A of the Partition Law. Applicable Law Statutes: Partition Law, No. 21 of 1977 Civil Procedure Code Prevention of Frauds Ordinance Rent Act, No. 1 of 1972 Cases Referred to: Virasinghe v. Virasinghe - [2002/v1-sri-lr-1] Virasinghe v. Virasinghe - [2002/v1-sri-lr-264] The Ceylon Brewery Limited v. Jax Fernando - [2001/v1-sri-lr-270] Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of Section 52A time limit Rule applied: Section 52 and Section 52A of the Partition Law, No. 21 of 1977. Court's reasoning: The Court distinguished between the two remedies provided by the Partition Law. Section 52 governs the initial delivery of possession to a party declared entitled by a final decree. Section 52A, however, provides a remedy for "restoration of possession" specifically for those who were previously in possession and were subsequently dispossessed or whose possession was interfered with. The Court reasoned that Section 52 does not contain a time limit, whereas Section 52A imposes a mandatory twelve-month limit for restoration. Because the Appellant never held physical possession of the corpus, his application fell under Section 52(2)(a) for initial delivery, not Section 52A for restoration. Finding: The twelve-month time limit in Section 52A is inapplicable to applications for initial delivery of possession under Section 52. Issue 2: Whether the application was time-barred Rule applied: Section 52A of the Partition Law, No. 21 of 1977. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the Respondentâ€™s reliance on Section 52A was misplaced. The Appellantâ€™s application was not for "restoration" of a lost possession but for the "delivery" of possession to which he was entitled by the final decree. Since the Appellant had never enjoyed possession, the jurisdictional time bar of twelve months stipulated in Section 52A for restoration of possession did not apply to his application. Finding: The application was not time-barred as it was correctly brought under Section 52(2)(a), which contains no such limitation. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing; by determining that the Appellantâ€™s application was for initial delivery of possession (Issue 1), the Court effectively excluded the application of the jurisdictional time bar (Issue 2). The legal characterization of the remedy sought dictated the inapplicability of the statutory limitation period. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court is set aside, and the order of the District Court of Colombo is affirmed. The Respondent is ordered to pay the Appellant a sum of Rs. 100,000 as costs of the appeal. Keywords Partition Law — Delivery of possession — Restoration of possession — Section 52 of the Partition Law — Section 52A of the Partition Law — Interference with possession — Dispossession — Time limit — Monthly tenancy — Acquisition of title

Parties: Dharmasiri v. Provincial Director of Health Services of the Sabaragamuwa Province and Others
Bench: N.G. Amaratunga, J; Saleem Marsoof, J.; S. E. Wanasundera, P.C., J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Tender procedure — Administrative action — Price determination — Public procurement — Tender conditions — Market price
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, H.R.S. Dharmasiri, submitted tenders for the supply of food items to the Karawanella Base Hospital and the Kithulgala District Hospital for the year 2010. The tender conditions were governed by documents marked P2 and P3. The Petitioner alleged that his tenders were the lowest in price for most items. Following the tender closing date, the Petitioner was requested to attend a discussion with the 2nd Respondent, where he was asked to supply food items at prices determined by the Departmental Prices Committee, which were lower than the prices he had quoted. The Petitioner refused to agree to these lower prices, asserting that it was uneconomical and impossible for him to do so. The 1st and 2nd Respondents issued letters of conditional acceptance of the tenders, insisting that the Petitioner supply items at the prices specified by the Prices Committee. When the Petitioner failed to sign the agreements or commence supply, the 1st Respondent informed him that his failure was unsatisfactory and threatened to award the tender to the second lowest bidder, forfeit his deposits, and blacklist him from future tenders. The Petitioner subsequently filed this application alleging that the conduct of the Respondents violated his fundamental right to equality under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The Respondents maintained that they acted in accordance with the tender conditions, specifically clause 5 of the Instructions to Tenderers, which allowed for price adjustments if quoted prices deviated significantly from market rates. Issues Whether the conditional acceptance of the Petitioner's tenders and the subsequent demand to supply food items at prices determined by the Prices Committee constituted a violation of the Petitioner's fundamental right to equality under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the Respondents' actions were in compliance with the established tender procedure and the specific conditions set out in the Instructions to Tenderers (P3). Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution of Sri Lanka, which guarantees the right to equality before the law and equal protection of the law. Court's reasoning: The Court examined whether the Respondents' insistence on price adjustments was discriminatory or arbitrary. The evidence showed that the Respondents acted based on a pre-existing tender condition that allowed for the amendment of prices found to be unusually higher than market rates. The Court found no evidence of malice or discriminatory intent, noting that the Respondents were attempting to secure supplies at reasonable rates after the Petitioner's initial quotes were deemed inconsistent with market prices. Finding: The Court found no violation of the Petitioner's fundamental right to equality. Issue 2: Compliance with Tender Procedure Rule applied: Clause 5 of the Instructions to Tenderers and Additional Conditions (P3). Court's reasoning: Clause 5 explicitly required tenderers to agree to amend prices if they were found to be unusually higher or lower than current market prices, as determined by the Departmental Prices Committee. The letters of acceptance (P8 and P9) clearly stated that the tender award was subject to these price amendments. The Court noted that the Petitioner failed to provide sufficient material to challenge the reasonableness of the Prices Committee's rates or to prove that his own quotes were compliant with the tender requirements. Finding: The Respondents acted in accordance with the tender procedure and the contractual conditions governing the bidding process. Interaction of Issues The findings on the two issues are mutually reinforcing. Because the Respondents acted within the scope of the express tender conditions (Issue 2), their actions were deemed reasonable and non-arbitrary, which directly led to the conclusion that there was no infringement of the Petitioner's fundamental right to equality (Issue 1). Conclusion The application is dismissed. No order for costs is made. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Tender procedure — Administrative action — Price determination — Public procurement — Tender conditions — Market price

Parties: Lionair (Private) Limited v. Ceylinco Leasing Corporation Limited
Bench: N.G. Amaratunga, J; Saleem Marsoof, J.; K. Sripavan, J.
Keywords: Motor vehicle possession — Entitlement to possession — Bona fide purchaser
Summary: Facts Ceylinco Leasing Corporation Limited (Plaintiff-Respondent) instituted an action in the Commercial High Court against Lionair (Private) Limited (Defendant-Appellant) to recover an aggregate sum of Rs. 132,523,149.86, representing outstanding payments on 12 separate loans. The Plaintiff-Respondent alleged that these loans were granted to the Defendant-Appellant, and as security, the Defendant-Appellant issued 12 promissory notes. The Defendant-Appellant filed an answer denying any contractual liability to the Plaintiff-Respondent, asserting that any financial assistance contemplated under a Strategic Alliance Agreement was provided by Ceylinco Capital Investment Co (Pvt) Ltd, not the Plaintiff-Respondent. At trial, the Plaintiff-Respondent called one witness, an Assistant Managing Director, who testified that the loans were granted and were evidenced by the promissory notes and a request letter. The Defendant-Appellant did not call any evidence. The Commercial High Court held that the Plaintiff-Respondent had proved its case on a balance of probabilities, characterizing the action as a regular action for the recovery of money lent, where the promissory notes served as evidence of the underlying loan transactions. The Defendant-Appellant appealed this decision. Issues Whether the Plaintiff-Respondent successfully proved the existence of the underlying loan transactions and their terms, notwithstanding the reliance on promissory notes. Whether the failure to comply with the procedural requirements of the Bills of Exchange Ordinance No. 25 of 1927 precludes recovery in an action framed as a regular action for money lent. Whether the letters of demand issued by the Plaintiff-Respondent were sufficient to satisfy the requirements for a demand for repayment under Roman-Dutch law. Applicable Law Statutes: Bills of Exchange Ordinance No. 25 of 1927 Civil Procedure Code Evidence Ordinance No. 14 of 1895 Cases Referred to: Seylan Bank Limited v. Intertrade Garments (Private) Limited - [2005/v1-sri-lr-80] L.B. Finance Ltd v. Manchanayake - [2000/v2-sri-lr-142] Narendra v. Seylan Bank Limited - [2003/v2-sri-lr-1] Fradd v. Brown & Co. Ltd. - [18-nlr-382] Abdul Sathar v. Bogtstra - [54-nlr-102] Alwis v. Piyasena Fernando - [1993/v1-sri-lr-119] In re Romer and Haslam - [1893] 2 Q.B. 286 Maung Chit v. Roshan N.M.A Kareem Oomer & Co. - [AIR 1934 Rangoon 389] Goddard & Son v. Q'Brien - [1882] 9 Q.B.D. 37 Day v. McLea - [1889] 22 Q.B.D. 610 Analysis Issue 1: Proof of loan transactions Rule applied: Roman-Dutch law principles of mutuum (loan for consumption). Court's reasoning: The court noted that the Plaintiff-Respondent established the loans through the testimony of its witness and the production of promissory notes and a request letter. The court held that the promissory notes were not the sole consideration but evidence of the underlying loan transactions. The Defendant-Appellant's failure to lead evidence to rebut the Plaintiff-Respondent's case allowed the court to infer the existence of the loans on a balance of probabilities. Finding: The Plaintiff-Respondent sufficiently proved the existence and terms of the loan transactions. Issue 2: Applicability of Bills of Exchange Ordinance Rule applied: Principles of conditional payment regarding negotiable instruments. Court's reasoning: The court held that the action was a regular action for money lent, not a summary action on promissory notes. Under the principle of conditional payment, if a promissory note is not the sole consideration, the creditor may sue on the original consideration if the note is not paid. The procedural requirements of the Bills of Exchange Ordinance do not apply to the underlying loan contract. Finding: The action was maintainable as a regular action for money lent, and the Bills of Exchange Ordinance did not bar recovery. Issue 3: Sufficiency of demand Rule applied: Roman-Dutch law requirements for notice of demand. Court's reasoning: The court determined that while the letters of demand referenced the promissory notes, they functioned as a notice to return the money lent. Given that the loans were repayable on demand, the letters provided sufficient notice to the borrower. Finding: The letters of demand were adequate to perfect the cause of action. Interaction of Issues The court's findings are interconnected: by characterizing the action as one for "money lent" rather than a suit on "promissory notes," the court bypassed the strict procedural requirements of the Bills of Exchange Ordinance. This characterization allowed the court to rely on the promissory notes as evidence of the underlying loan, which, when combined with the Defendant-Appellant's failure to provide rebuttal evidence, satisfied the burden of proof under Roman-Dutch law. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The judgment of the Commercial High Court is affirmed, and the Defendant-Appellant is ordered to pay costs of Rs. 100,000.00. Keywords Motor vehicle possession — Entitlement to possession — Bona fide purchaser

Parties: Ceylinco Leasing Corporation Limited v. Lionair (Private) Limited
Bench: N.G. Amaratunga, J.; Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J.; K. Sripavan, J.
Keywords: Loan Recovery — Promissory Notes — Strategic Alliance Agreement — Civil Jurisdiction — Commercial High Court — Bills of Exchange Ordinance — Demand Letters — Evidence — Roman-Dutch Law — Contractual Obligations — Balance of Probabilities — Legal Interest — Cause of Action — Written Contracts — Oral Agreements
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal by Lionair (Private) Limited against a judgment delivered by the Commercial High Court of the Western Province, which upheld the claim of Ceylinco Leasing Corporation Limited for the recovery of an aggregate sum of Rs. 132,523,149.86, allegedly due on 12 separate loans. The loans were claimed to have been granted to Lionair under a Strategic Alliance Agreement dated 24th September 2003, which Ceylinco Leasing was not a party to. Ceylinco Leasing contended that it provided financial assistance to Lionair based on this agreement, while Lionair denied any contractual obligation to Ceylinco Leasing, asserting that any financial assistance was provided by Ceylinco Capital, not Ceylinco Leasing. The trial involved extensive evidence, including affidavits and cross-examinations, with the only witness being the Assistant Managing Director of Ceylinco Leasing, who testified about the loans and the promissory notes issued by Lionair as security. The Commercial High Court found in favor of Ceylinco Leasing, determining that the evidence presented was sufficient to establish the existence of the loans and the obligations of Lionair. Issues The primary issues framed in this case include: Whether Ceylinco Leasing had a valid cause of action against Lionair based on the loans granted. Whether the promissory notes were enforceable and constituted sufficient evidence of the loan transactions. Whether the letters of demand issued by Ceylinco Leasing were adequate to establish the cause of action. Applicable Law Statutes: Bills of Exchange Ordinance No. 25 of 1927, as amended. Cases Referred to: Seylan Bank Limited v. Intertrade Garments (Private) Limited [2005] 1 SLR 80; L.B. Finance Ltd v. Manchanayake [2000] 2 SLR 142; Fradd v. Brown & Co. Ltd. 18 NLR 382 (SC); Abdul Sathar v. Bogtstra 54 NLR 102 (PC); Alwis v. Piyasena Fernando (1993) 1 SLR 119 (SC). Analysis Issue 1: Valid Cause of Action Rule applied: The existence of a valid cause of action based on loan agreements. Court's reasoning: The court examined the nature of the loans and the evidence presented by Ceylinco Leasing. It was established that the loans were documented through promissory notes, which were signed by Lionair's directors. The court emphasized that the Strategic Alliance Agreement provided context but did not negate the existence of the loans. The court noted that the promissory notes served as evidence of the loan transactions, and the absence of a formal written contract did not preclude the existence of an oral agreement. The court highlighted that the letters of demand sent to Lionair were consistent with the requirements of the law, and the action was filed within the statutory period, thus affirming the validity of the cause of action. Finding: The court concluded that Ceylinco Leasing had established a valid cause of action against Lionair based on the loans granted. Issue 2: Enforceability of Promissory Notes Rule applied: The enforceability of promissory notes as evidence of loan transactions. Court's reasoning: The court analyzed the nature of the promissory notes and their role in the loan agreements. It was determined that the promissory notes were executed as security for the loans and embodied the terms of the agreements. The court noted that the absence of a written contract did not undermine the enforceability of the promissory notes, as the evidence indicated that the loans were advanced based on mutual understanding and agreement. The court also addressed the argument that the letters of demand were insufficient, clarifying that the action was not solely based on the promissory notes but rather on the underlying loan transactions. Finding: The court affirmed that the promissory notes were enforceable and constituted valid evidence of the loan transactions. Issue 3: Adequacy of Demand Letters Rule applied: The sufficiency of demand letters in establishing a cause of action. Court's reasoning: The court scrutinized the letters of demand issued by Ceylinco Leasing, which requested payment within a specified timeframe. The court held that these letters served as adequate notice for the repayment of the loans, as they were issued in accordance with the terms of the promissory notes. The court emphasized that the demand letters were consistent with the legal requirements and that the action was initiated after a reasonable period following the demands. Finding: The court concluded that the demand letters were sufficient to establish the cause of action for the recovery of the loans. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in this case are interrelated, as the validity of the cause of action hinges on the enforceability of the promissory notes and the adequacy of the demand letters. The court's analysis demonstrated that the existence of the loans, as evidenced by the promissory notes, supported the claims made by Ceylinco Leasing. The court's findings on each issue reinforced the overall conclusion that Ceylinco Leasing was entitled to recover the amounts due from Lionair. Conclusion The Supreme Court upheld the judgment of the Commercial High Court, affirming that Ceylinco Leasing had proven its case on a balance of probabilities. The appeal by Lionair was dismissed, and Lionair was ordered to pay the costs of the appeal. The court's decision underscored the importance of evidence in establishing loan transactions and the enforceability of promissory notes as valid instruments in commercial dealings. Keywords Loan Recovery — Promissory Notes — Strategic Alliance Agreement — Civil Jurisdiction — Commercial High Court — Bills of Exchange Ordinance — Demand Letters — Evidence — Roman-Dutch Law — Contractual Obligations — Balance of Probabilities — Legal Interest — Cause of Action — Written Contracts — Oral Agreements
Counsel: Chandaka Jayasundere with Tharindu Rajakaruna for the Defendant-Appellant. I.S. de Silva with D. Perera for the Plaintiff-Respondent.

Parties: Solaimuthu Rasu v. The Superintendent and Others
Bench: Mohan Pieris, P.C., C.J.; Sripavan, J.; Wanasundera, P.C., J.
Keywords: Special Leave to Appeal — Jurisdiction — State Lands — Provincial Councils — Thirteenth Amendment — Unitary State — Legislative Power — Encroachment — Alienation of Land — National Land Commission — Court of Appeal — High Court — Ownership Rights — Land Tenure — Administrative Control — Legal Interpretation
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for special leave to appeal against a judgment delivered by the Court of Appeal on 8 August 2012, which had set aside a prior judgment from the Provincial High Court dated 25 October 2000. The petitioner, Solaimuthu Rasu, a resident of Dickson Corner Colony, Stafford Estate, Ragala, challenged the decisions regarding the jurisdiction of the Provincial High Court in matters concerning state lands. The respondents included the Superintendent of Stafford Estate, a plantation expert from the Ministry of Plantation Industries, and the Attorney General. The core of the dispute involved whether the Provincial High Court had the authority to adjudicate cases involving the disposition, encroachment, or alienation of state lands, particularly in light of the provisions set forth in the Thirteenth Amendment to the Constitution. The Supreme Court, in its deliberation, sought to clarify the extent of jurisdiction granted to the Provincial High Courts concerning state lands and the implications of the Thirteenth Amendment on such matters. Issues Whether the Court of Appeal erred in determining that the Provincial High Court had jurisdiction to hear cases involving the disposition or encroachment of state lands. Whether the interpretation of the Thirteenth Amendment allows for the Provincial High Court to adjudicate on matters related to state land. Applicable Law Statutes: Thirteenth Amendment to the Constitution of Sri Lanka. Cases Referred to: Magor and St. Mellons RDC v. Newport Corporation - [1950] 2 AER 1226; The Thirteenth Amendment to the Constitution (1987) 2 Sri. LR 312; Weragama v. Eksath Lanka Wathu Kamkaru Samitiya and Others - [1994] 4 Sri. LR 293. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Court of Appeal erred in determining that the Provincial High Court had jurisdiction to hear cases involving the disposition or encroachment of state lands. Rule applied: The interpretation of the Thirteenth Amendment and its implications on the jurisdiction of the Provincial High Court. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court, particularly through the judgment authored by Justice Wanasundera, emphasized the importance of understanding the legislative intent behind the Thirteenth Amendment. The Court referenced Lord Denning's perspective on judicial interpretation, stressing that judges should aim to fulfill the intentions of Parliament rather than dissecting the language to the point of absurdity. The judgment highlighted that the devolution of state lands to the provinces is limited and must adhere to the overarching authority of the Republic. The Court noted that while the Provincial Councils have certain powers regarding state lands, these powers are not absolute and must align with national policies as determined by the National Land Commission. The judgment further clarified that the President retains the authority to grant and dispose of state lands, and any rights of ownership are subject to the limitations imposed by the Constitution. The Court underscored that the Provincial High Court's jurisdiction does not extend to matters of state land unless explicitly provided for in the legislative framework. Finding: The Court concluded that the Court of Appeal had indeed erred in its determination regarding the jurisdiction of the Provincial High Court over state land matters. Interaction of Issues The issues presented are interrelated, as the determination of jurisdiction directly impacts the interpretation of the Thirteenth Amendment. The Court's analysis of the legislative intent and the powers granted to Provincial Councils is crucial in understanding the limitations placed on the Provincial High Court's authority. The relationship between state land management and the constitutional framework is central to resolving the jurisdictional questions raised in this case. Conclusion The Supreme Court ultimately ruled that the Provincial High Court does not possess the jurisdiction to hear cases involving the disposition or encroachment of state lands, reaffirming the supremacy of the central government in matters concerning state land. The judgment serves to clarify the boundaries of authority between the central and provincial governments in Sri Lanka, particularly in the context of land management and ownership rights. The Court's decision underscores the importance of adhering to the constitutional provisions that govern the administration of state lands, ensuring that the legislative intent behind the Thirteenth Amendment is respected and upheld. Keywords Special Leave to Appeal — Jurisdiction — State Lands — Provincial Councils — Thirteenth Amendment — Unitary State — Legislative Power — Encroachment — Alienation of Land — National Land Commission — Court of Appeal — High Court — Ownership Rights — Land Tenure — Administrative Control — Legal Interpretation

Parties: Solaimuthu Rasu v. The Superintendent and Others
Bench: Mohan Pieris, P.C., C.J.; Sripavan, J.; Wanasundera, P.C., J.
Keywords: State Lands — Provincial High Court Jurisdiction — Writ of Certiorari — Encroachment — Alienation of State Lands — 13th Amendment — Constitutional Law — Legislative Powers — National Land Commission — Recovery of Possession — Administrative Control
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for special leave to appeal against a judgment delivered by the Court of Appeal on 8 August 2012, which set aside a prior judgment of the Provincial High Court of Kandy dated 25 October 2000. The petitioner, Solaimuthu Rasu, sought to challenge a quit notice issued to him under the State Lands (Recovery of Possession) Act No. 7 of 1979. The quit notice was issued by the Superintendent of Stafford Estate, which the petitioner claimed was unlawful. The petitioner sought a writ of certiorari to quash the quit notice, a writ of prohibition to prevent further eviction actions, and a writ of mandamus to protect his possession of the land. The respondents, including the Superintendent and a plantation expert, contended that the land in question was state land and that the Provincial High Court lacked jurisdiction to hear the case. The Provincial High Court initially ruled in favor of the respondents, stating it did not have jurisdiction over matters concerning state land. The petitioner appealed to the Court of Appeal, which ruled that the Provincial High Court did have jurisdiction, leading to the current appeal to the Supreme Court. Issues The Supreme Court was tasked with determining two primary issues: (i) Whether the Court of Appeal erred in deciding that the Provincial High Court had jurisdiction to hear cases involving dispossession or encroachment of state lands. (ii) Whether the Court of Appeal failed to consider the right of appeal against the High Court's dismissal of the application for lack of jurisdiction. However, during the hearing, the parties agreed to limit their submissions to the first issue. Applicable Law Statutes: State Lands (Recovery of Possession) Act No. 7 of 1979 — 13th Amendment to the Constitution of Sri Lanka. Cases Referred to: Weragama Vs. Eksath Lanka Wathu Kamkaru Samithiya & Others (1994) 1 S.L.R. 293 — Determination of the Agrarian Services (Amendment) Bill [S.C. Special Determination 2/91 and 4/91] — Re The Thirteenth Amendment to the Constitution [(1987) 2 S.L.R. 312]. Analysis Issue 1: Jurisdiction of the Provincial High Court Rule applied: The jurisdiction of the Provincial High Court concerning state lands is governed by the provisions of the Constitution and the State Lands (Recovery of Possession) Act. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the constitutional framework surrounding the devolution of powers to Provincial Councils, particularly in relation to state lands. It noted that while state land is included in the Provincial Council List, it remains under the ultimate control of the Republic. The Court emphasized that the Provincial Councils do not possess sovereign legislative powers and are subordinate to Parliament. The Court referenced the 13th Amendment, which delineates the powers of Provincial Councils and highlighted that any legislation concerning state lands must comply with national policy as formulated by the National Land Commission. The Court further analyzed the specific provisions of the State Lands (Recovery of Possession) Act and concluded that the Provincial High Court lacked jurisdiction to issue writs concerning state land unless such land had been made available to the Provincial Council for a Provincial Council subject. The Court cited previous judgments, including the case of Weragama, to reinforce its interpretation of the legislative intent behind the 13th Amendment. Finding: The Court concluded that the Court of Appeal erred in holding that the Provincial High Court had jurisdiction to issue a writ of certiorari regarding the quit notice issued under the State Lands (Recovery of Possession) Act. The order of the Court of Appeal was set aside, and the order of the Provincial High Court was affirmed. Interaction of Issues The issues presented were interrelated, as the determination of jurisdiction directly impacted the ability to appeal the High Court's decision. However, since the parties agreed to limit their focus to the jurisdictional question, the second issue was not addressed in this judgment. Conclusion The Supreme Court ultimately ruled in favor of the respondents, affirming the Provincial High Court's original decision and clarifying the jurisdictional limitations concerning state lands. The judgment underscored the importance of adhering to constitutional provisions and the legislative framework governing land matters in Sri Lanka.

Parties: Solaimuthu Rasu v. The Superintendent and Others
Bench: Mohan Pieris, P.C., C.J.; Sripavan, J.; Wanasundera, P.C., J.
Keywords: State Lands — Jurisdiction — Provincial High Court — Writ Jurisdiction — 13th Amendment — Recovery of Possession — Constitutional Law — Land Ownership — Legal Precedents — Administrative Law — Competent Authority — Quit Notice — Appeal Process — Legal Interpretation
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for special leave to appeal against a judgment delivered by the Court of Appeal on 08 August 2012, which set aside a prior judgment from the Provincial High Court dated 25 October 2000. The petitioner-appellant, Solaimuthu Rasu, was involved in a dispute regarding the illegal occupation of state land in Ragala, Halgranaoya. The competent authority, represented by the second petitioner, initiated proceedings in the Magistrate's Court of Nuwara Eliya under the State Lands (Recovery of Possession) Act No. 7 of 1979 to recover possession of the state land. Rasu filed an application in the Provincial High Court seeking a writ of certiorari to quash the quit notice issued by the competent authority. The High Court ruled that it lacked jurisdiction to hear the case, leading Rasu to appeal to the Court of Appeal, which ultimately ruled that the Provincial High Court did have jurisdiction to hear such cases involving state land. This appeal to the Supreme Court was based on two key legal questions regarding the jurisdiction of the Provincial High Court in matters of state land. Issues The primary issues before the Supreme Court were: (i) Whether the Court of Appeal erred in deciding that the Provincial High Court has jurisdiction to hear cases involving dispossession or encroachment of state lands. (ii) Whether the Court of Appeal failed to consider the right of appeal against the High Court's order dismissing the application for lack of jurisdiction. Applicable Law Statutes: State Lands (Recovery of Possession) Act No. 7 of 1979 — 13th Amendment to the Constitution of Sri Lanka. Cases Referred to: Vasudeva Nanayakkara v. Choksy and Others - {2008} 1 Sri.LR 134. Analysis Issue 1: Jurisdiction of the Provincial High Court Rule applied: The jurisdiction of the Provincial High Court in matters concerning state land is governed by the provisions of the 13th Amendment to the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court engaged in a detailed analysis of the constitutional provisions regarding state land, particularly focusing on the 13th Amendment. The Court emphasized that state land is primarily a subject of the central government, as outlined in List II of the Constitution, which reserves state lands for the Republic. The Court noted that while the Provincial Councils have certain powers over land, these are limited and do not extend to matters of state land dispossession or encroachment. The Court referenced the specific provisions of the 13th Amendment, which delineate the powers of the Provincial Councils and the central government regarding land. It was concluded that the Provincial High Court does not possess jurisdiction to issue writs concerning quit notices issued under the State Lands (Recovery of Possession) Act, as such matters do not fall within the scope of the Provincial Council List. The Court further criticized the Court of Appeal for misinterpreting the constitutional framework and for failing to recognize the overarching authority of the central government over state lands. Finding: The Supreme Court found that the Court of Appeal erred in its judgment, affirming the decision of the Provincial High Court that it lacked jurisdiction in this matter. Interaction of Issues The issues presented were interrelated, as the determination of the Provincial High Court's jurisdiction directly impacted the appeal process and the rights of the parties involved. The Supreme Court's analysis clarified the legal framework governing state land and the limitations placed on the Provincial Councils, thereby resolving the jurisdictional question. Conclusion The Supreme Court set aside the Court of Appeal's judgment and upheld the Provincial High Court's ruling, affirming that the Provincial High Court did not have jurisdiction to hear the case concerning the quit notice issued under the State Lands (Recovery of Possession) Act. The Court's decision reinforced the central government's authority over state lands and clarified the jurisdictional boundaries established by the 13th Amendment. Keywords State Lands — Jurisdiction — Provincial High Court — Writ Jurisdiction — 13th Amendment — Recovery of Possession — Constitutional Law — Land Ownership — Legal Precedents — Administrative Law — Competent Authority — Quit Notice — Appeal Process — Legal Interpretation
