Parties: Janaki v. Kariyawasam and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof P C, J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Sathyaa Hettige P C, J
Keywords: Leave to appeal — High Court order — District Judge order — Interim injunctions — Prima facie case — Balance of convenience — Conduct of parties — Wrongful possession — Status quo — Rent Act
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent instituted action in the District Court of Colombo seeking a declaration of title and the ejectment of the Defendants from the premises at No. 19, Avissawella Road, Kirulapona. The Plaintiff claimed ownership via a deed of gift and asserted that the tenancy, originally held by one Francis and subsequently his son, had passed to the 1st Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant. The Plaintiff alleged that the 1st Defendant had unlawfully permitted the 2nd and 3rd Defendants to occupy the premises as subtenants and conduct business activities therein without authorization. The District Court issued interim injunctions against the Defendants, restraining them from subletting or conducting business activities that would alter the status quo of the premises. The 1st Defendant challenged this order in the High Court of Civil Appeal, which refused leave to appeal. The 1st Defendant subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court, contending that the lower courts misdirected themselves regarding the evidence of subletting and the necessity of an interim injunction. Issues Whether the High Court and District Court misdirected themselves in finding that the 1st Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant had sublet the premises to the 2nd and 3rd Defendant-Respondents, thereby forfeiting her tenancy. Whether the inference of subletting drawn by the lower courts was against the weight of the documentary evidence, thereby rendering the issuance of interim injunctions unjustified. Applicable Law Statutes: Mediation Boards Act No. 72 of 1988 Civil Procedure Code Cases Referred to: Martin Burn Ltd. v. R.N.Banerjee - [AIR 1958 SC 79] Jinadasa v. Weerasinghe - [31-nlr-33] F.D.Bandaranaike v. State Film Corporation - [1981/v2-sri-lr-287] Gulam Hussain v. Cohen - [1995/v2-sri-lr-1] Dissanayake v. Agricultural and Industrial Corporation - [1962-64-nlr-283] Seelawathie Mellawa v. Millie Keerthiratna - [1982/v1-sri-lr-384] Subramaniam v. Shabdeen - [1984/v1-sri-lr-48] Analysis Issue 1: Finding of Subletting Rule applied: The court applied the principle that an applicant for an interim injunction must establish a prima facie case, meaning there is a serious question to be tried and a probability that the plaintiff will succeed. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the Tenancy Certificate (P4) identified the 1st Defendant as the sole tenant of the entire premises. The evidence indicated that the 2nd and 3rd Defendants were in occupation of portions of the premises. The 1st Defendant failed to provide any explanation for how these parties came into possession of the premises. Consequently, the court found that the lower courts were justified in concluding that the 1st to 3rd Defendants were in wrongful possession in violation of the Rent Act. Finding: The court answered the question of law in the negative, finding no misdirection by the lower courts. Issue 2: Justification for Interim Injunctions Rule applied: The court applied the principle that an injunction is an equitable remedy used to stop a wrongdoer from obtaining benefits from their own wrongful conduct, as established in Seelawathie Mellawa v. Millie Keerthiratna and Subramaniam v. Shabdeen. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that if a party is in unlawful possession, they should be restrained from taking benefits arising from that possession to prevent the court from preserving a position of advantage gained through wrongdoing. The court determined that the balance of convenience favored the Plaintiff, as the potential damage to the Plaintiff by refusing the injunction outweighed the potential hardship to the Defendants. Finding: The court answered the question of law in the negative, affirming that the injunctions were properly granted to preserve the status quo. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are interdependent; the determination that the Defendants were in wrongful possession (Issue 1) provided the necessary legal basis for the court to exercise its discretion to grant equitable relief (Issue 2). The court's conclusion that the 1st Defendant failed to explain the presence of the other Defendants reinforced the finding that the Plaintiff had established a strong prima facie case, thereby justifying the protective measures of the interim injunction. Conclusion The Supreme Court answered the questions of law in the negative and dismissed the appeal. The order of the High Court of Civil Appeal affirming the issuance of the interim injunctions was upheld. Keywords Leave to appeal — High Court order — District Judge order — Interim injunctions — Prima facie case — Balance of convenience — Conduct of parties — Wrongful possession — Status quo — Rent Act

Parties: Fernando v. Perera
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Rent Act No. 07 of 1972 — Section 22(1)(d) — Deterioration of premises — Structural alteration — Repair vs. improvement — Expert evidence — Reduction of property value — Tenant's duties — Notice to Quit — Grama Sevaka report
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Appellant-Respondent instituted an action in the District Court of Panadura seeking the ejectment of the Defendant-Respondent-Petitioner from the premises located at Galle Road, Panadura. The Plaintiff alleged that the Defendant had caused the premises to deteriorate in violation of Section 22(1)(d) of the Rent Act No. 07 of 1972. The dispute arose after the Plaintiff terminated the tenancy via a notice to quit. The Defendant claimed that following heavy rains in 1991, the roof and walls of the premises were damaged, and the Plaintiff failed to perform necessary repairs. Consequently, the Defendant replaced the original Sinhala half-round tiles with asbestos sheets. The District Court dismissed the action, finding that the repairs were necessary and did not constitute deterioration. The Plaintiff appealed to the Western Province Civil Appellate High Court, which set aside the District Court judgment. The Defendant then appealed to the Supreme Court. Expert evidence from a Chartered Architect indicated that the Defendant had reduced the height of the walls by two feet to accommodate the asbestos roofing, resulting in a loss of valuable timber and a distortion of the architectural character of the premises. Issues Whether the repairs made by the Defendant caused deterioration to the premises under Section 22(1)(d) of the Rent Act No. 07 of 1972. Whether the replacement of Sinhala tiles with asbestos sheets and the associated structural changes constituted deterioration of the premises. Whether the Plaintiff is entitled to the relief of ejectment. Applicable Law Statutes: Rent Act No. 07 of 1972 Cases Referred to: A. C. T. Constructions Ltd. v. Customs Excise Commrs - [1982/v1-all-er-84] Barakathulla v. Hinniappuhamy - [1982/v2-sri-lr-463] Musthapa Thamby Lebbe v. Ruwanpathirane - [1986/v1-sri-lr-201] De Zoysa v. Victor De Silva - [73-nlr-576] De Alwis v. Wijewardena - [59-nlr-36] W. A. S. de Silva v. L. Gooneratne - [1-mlr-6] C. A. No. 30/79 (F) - [1984] Analysis Issue 1: Deterioration under the Rent Act Rule applied: Section 22(1)(d) of the Rent Act No. 07 of 1972. Court's reasoning: The court held that "deterioration" requires proof that the tenantâ€™s acts made the premises worse. Relying on expert evidence, the court found that the structural alterations—specifically the reduction of wall height and the removal of original timber—diminished the value and character of the property. The court rejected the argument that deterioration is limited to physical damage, noting that a reduction in commercial value and the necessity for future restoration costs also constitute "making the premises worse." Finding: The repairs caused deterioration within the meaning of the Rent Act. Issue 2: Structural alterations and improvements Rule applied: Principles of "useful improvements" versus "structural alterations" as discussed in Barakathulla v. Hinniappuhamy and Musthapa Thamby Lebbe v. Ruwanpathirane. Court's reasoning: While the replacement of a roof can be a useful improvement, the court found that the Defendantâ€™s actions were not improvements because they fundamentally altered the structure and appearance of the building. The court distinguished this case from W. A. S. de Silva v. L. Gooneratne, noting that here, significant physical changes were made that reduced the property's value. The court emphasized that a tenant has a duty to restore the property in its original condition, reasonable wear and tear excepted, and may not alter or destroy the substance of the property. Finding: The alterations were not improvements but were structural changes that damaged the premises. Interaction of Issues The findings on the nature of the structural alterations directly informed the interpretation of "deterioration." Because the court determined the alterations were not "useful improvements" but rather destructive changes, the legal threshold for "deterioration" under Section 22(1)(d) was satisfied. The lack of evidence regarding the alleged wall collapse further undermined the Defendant's justification for the structural changes, reinforcing the conclusion that the tenant acted in breach of their duty to maintain the premises. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed, and the judgment of the High Court is confirmed. The court found that the Defendant's unauthorized structural alterations constituted deterioration of the premises under the Rent Act. Keywords Rent Act No. 07 of 1972 — Section 22(1)(d) — Deterioration of premises — Structural alteration — Repair vs. improvement — Expert evidence — Reduction of property value — Tenant's duties — Notice to Quit — Grama Sevaka report

Parties: Peiris and Others v. Weerakoon and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, J.; Hon. C. Ekanayake J, J.; Hon. E. Wanasundera PC, J
Keywords: Substitution of petitioner — Death of original petitioner — Fundamental rights application — Locus standi — Article 126 of the Constitution — Rule 38 of the Supreme Court Rules — Cause of action — Litis contestatio — Right to life
Summary: Facts The original Petitioner, Malalage Chaminda Tissa Peiris, filed a fundamental rights application alleging violations of Articles 11, 13(1), and 13(2) of the Constitution following his arrest and alleged torture by police officers at the Anuradhapura Police Station. This Court granted leave to proceed solely regarding the alleged violation of Article 11. During the pendency of the proceedings, the original Petitioner committed suicide. Following his death, his father, Malalage Gunadasa Peiris, filed an application to be substituted as the Petitioner. This Court allowed the substitution on 27th July 2012. When the matter was subsequently taken up for hearing, the Respondents challenged the validity of the substitution, arguing that the cause of action was personal and did not survive the death of the original Petitioner. Issues Whether the fundamental rights application, specifically regarding an alleged violation of Article 11, survives the death of the Petitioner after the stage of litis contestatio has been reached. Whether the substitution of the father of the deceased Petitioner is valid in law. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 11, 13(1), 13(2), 17, 126. Supreme Court Rules 1990, Rule 38. Cases Referred to: Somawathie v. Weerasinghe - [1990/v2-sri-lr-121] Shriyani Silva v. Iddamalgoda - [2003/v1-sri-lr-14] Fernando v. Livera - [29-nlr-246] Podisingho v. Jayatu - [30-nlr-169] Vangadasalam v. Karuppan - [79/v2-nlr-150] Jayasooriya v. Samaranayake - [1982/v2-sri-lr-460] Atapattu v. Peoples Bank - [1997/v1-sri-lr-208] Leelawathie v. Manel Ratnayake - [1998/v3-sri-lr-349] Stella Perera v. Margret Silva - [2002/v1-sri-lr-169] John Fernando v. Satarasinghe - [2002/v2-sri-lr-113] Muheeth v. Nadarajapilla - [19-nlr-461] Analysis Issue 1: Survival of the cause of action Rule applied: The principle of litis contestatio (close of pleadings) and the maxim actio personalis moritur cum persona. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that while fundamental rights applications are generally personal, the maxim actio personalis moritur cum persona does not apply once litis contestatio is reached. Following the precedent in Atapattu v. Peoples Bank, the Court determined that litis contestatio occurs when pleadings are closed. In this case, the Petitioner filed counter-affidavits on 30th June 2009, effectively closing the pleadings well before his death. Therefore, the right to seek relief for the alleged violation of Article 11 survived the Petitioner's death. Finding: The cause of action survived the death of the original Petitioner because the stage of litis contestatio had been reached. Issue 2: Validity of substitution Rule applied: Rule 38 of the Supreme Court Rules 1990. Court's reasoning: Rule 38 allows the Court to substitute a proper person when the record becomes defective due to the death of a party. The Court found that the Substituted Petitioner, being the father of the deceased, was a proper person to continue the action. The mother and brothers of the deceased provided affidavits consenting to the substitution. Finding: The substitution of Malalage Gunadasa Peiris was valid and in accordance with Rule 38. Interaction of Issues The resolution of the first issue regarding the survival of the cause of action provided the legal foundation for the second issue. Because the Court determined that the right to seek redress for the fundamental rights violation did not lapse upon the Petitioner's death due to the completion of litis contestatio, the procedural mechanism under Rule 38 could be validly invoked to permit the substitution. Conclusion The objections to the substitution are overruled, and the substitution of Malalage Gunadasa Peiris in place of the deceased original Petitioner is upheld. The case shall proceed to hearing before the same Bench. Keywords Substitution of petitioner — Death of original petitioner — Fundamental rights application — Locus standi — Article 126 of the Constitution — Rule 38 of the Supreme Court Rules — Cause of action — Litis contestatio — Right to life

Parties: Liyanage v. Bandara
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Forcible dispossession — Abuse of process of Court — Status quo — Enjoining order — Withdrawal of action — Constructive trust — Civil Appellate High Court — District Court — Restoration of possession
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Petitioner instituted action in the District Court seeking a declaration of title to certain lands, alleging the Defendant-Respondent held the property under a constructive trust despite a prior transfer. The Plaintiff-Petitioner obtained an ex-parte enjoining order restraining the Defendant-Respondent from alienating the land or forcibly entering it. The Defendant-Respondent, who had been in occupation of the property and residing in a house thereon since purchasing the land, was forcibly evicted by the Plaintiff-Petitioner under the guise of the enjoining order. The eviction was carried out by the Plaintiff-Petitioner with the assistance of police personnel and other individuals rather than through court officers. Following the eviction, the Plaintiff-Petitioner moved to withdraw the action, which the District Court allowed, dismissing the case. The Defendant-Respondent immediately filed a motion in the District Court seeking restoration of possession, which the District Court refused on the grounds that the action was no longer pending. The Defendant-Respondent subsequently filed a revision and leave to appeal application in the Civil Appellate High Court. The High Court ruled in favor of the Defendant-Respondent and ordered his restoration to possession. The Plaintiff-Petitioner appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Civil Appellate High Court was justified in ordering the restoration of the property to the Defendant-Respondent without holding a formal inquiry into the complaint of forcible dispossession. Whether the court possesses the inherent power to restore the status quo ante when an interim order has been abused to facilitate an illegal eviction. Applicable Law Statutes: Not specified Cases Referred to: Sivapathalingam v. Sivasubramaniam - [1990/v1-sri-lr-378] Analysis Issue 1: Necessity of a formal inquiry Rule applied: The court applied the principle that a Superior Court has inherent jurisdiction to remedy an injury caused by its own process or the abuse thereof. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that the record contained sufficient evidence, including affidavits from the Grama Sevaka, an incumbent priest, and other witnesses, alongside police complaints, to establish that the Defendant-Respondent was in lawful possession prior to the eviction. Given this substantial evidentiary record, the Court determined that a further formal inquiry into the dispossession was unnecessary at the High Court stage. The Court emphasized that the District Court should have acted immediately upon the motion filed by the Defendant-Respondent to restore the status quo. Finding: The Civil Appellate High Court was justified in ordering restoration without a further inquiry because the evidence of forcible dispossession and abuse of process was already manifest on the record. Issue 2: Inherent power to restore status quo Rule applied: The court relied on the precedent in Sivapathalingam v. Sivasubramaniam, which affirms that a court has the inherent power to make restitution when its act or process has caused injury to a suitor. Court's reasoning: The Court reasoned that an enjoining order is a tool for preservation, not an instrument for eviction. By using an ex-parte order to forcibly evict a party already in possession, the Plaintiff-Petitioner committed an abuse of the process of court. The Court held that when an action is withdrawn, it is the duty of the court to restore the parties to the position they occupied before the interim relief was granted. Failure to do so allows litigants to weaponize interim orders to achieve illegal ends. Finding: The court has the inherent power and duty to restore the status quo ante when an interim order has been misused to facilitate an illegal eviction. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the court possesses inherent power to restore the status quo (Issue 2) provides the legal basis for the court's decision to bypass a redundant inquiry (Issue 1). Because the abuse of process was clear and the court's duty to restore the status quo was absolute, the court was able to provide an immediate remedy without the delay of further proceedings. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court is confirmed, and the Defendant-Respondent is granted costs in all courts. Keywords Forcible dispossession — Abuse of process of Court — Status quo — Enjoining order — Withdrawal of action — Constructive trust — Civil Appellate High Court — District Court — Restoration of possession

Parties: Kumarasinghe v. Weliveriya
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Sripavan, J; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Special Leave to Appeal — Order of Ejectment — State Lands (Recovery of Possession) Act — Appeal against Magistrate's order — Revision Application — Writ Application — Punitive costs — Abuse of process of Court — Willful ignorance of Court procedure — Disclosure of parallel proceedings
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Leon Peris Kumarasinghe, sought special leave to appeal against a judgment of the Court of Appeal, which had dismissed his challenge to an Order of Ejectment issued by the Magistrateâ€™s Court of Nuwara Eliya. The Order of Ejectment was originally granted to the Respondent, the Director General of the Sri Lanka Broadcasting Corporation, under the State Lands (Recovery of Possession) Act No. 7 of 1979. Following the ejectment order, the Petitioner initiated a series of legal actions, including a direct appeal and a revision application in the High Court, a writ application, and an appeal to the Court of Appeal. The Petitioner failed to disclose the existence of parallel proceedings to the courts and pursued an appeal despite the statutory prohibition against appeals in the governing Act. The Supreme Court considered whether these actions constituted an abuse of the process of court and whether the imposition of punitive costs was warranted. Issues Whether the Petitionerâ€™s conduct in instituting multiple, simultaneous, and non-disclosed legal proceedings constitutes an abuse of the process of court. Whether the Court is justified in awarding punitive costs against the Petitioner for his conduct and for attempting to appeal an order where no right of appeal exists under the State Lands (Recovery of Possession) Act No. 7 of 1979. Applicable Law Statutes: State Lands (Recovery of Possession) Act No. 7 of 1979 Cases Referred to: Wilkes v. Woods - [98-ER-489] Smith v. Wade - [461-U.S.-30] Kwan v. Kaplan - [ZAGPJHC-36] Mohapi and Others v. Magashule and Others - [ZAFSHC-45] Makuwa v. Poslson - [3-SA-84] Khan v. Mzovuyo Investments (Pty) Ltd - [3-SA-47] Washaya v. Washaya - [4-SA-41] Rookes v. Barnard - [UKHL-1] Rustom K. Karanjia and Anr. v. Krishnaraj M.D. Thackersey and Ors - [72-BOMLR-94] Gunarathna v. Thambinayagam - [2-SLR-355] Reliance Mobile v. Hari Chand Gupta - [CPJ-73-NC] Polye v. Papaki and Another - [1-LRC-170] Analysis Issue 1: Abuse of process Rule applied: The court applied the principle that the institution of multiple, simultaneous, and misleading proceedings that impede the administration of justice and squander judicial resources constitutes an abuse of the process of court. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the Petitioner filed simultaneous appeal and revision applications in the High Court without disclosing their existence to the respective benches. Furthermore, the Petitioner withdrew a writ application after failing to pursue it, then attempted to challenge the underlying Notice to Quit through collateral proceedings. The court found that the Petitionerâ€™s failure to disclose parallel proceedings and his haphazard litigation strategy caused unnecessary delays and wasted judicial time. Finding: The Petitionerâ€™s conduct constitutes a blatant abuse of the process of court. Issue 2: Punitive costs Rule applied: Section 10(2) of the State Lands (Recovery of Possession) Act No. 7 of 1979, which prohibits appeals against ejectment orders, and the common law principle that punitive costs serve to punish the guilty and deter future misconduct. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the Petitioner pursued an appeal despite Section 10(2) of the Act explicitly stating that no appeal shall lie against an order of ejectment. Relying on Gunarathna v. Thambinayagam, the court affirmed that the right of appeal is purely statutory. Given the Petitionerâ€™s willful ignorance of procedure, his misleading of the court, and his attempt to challenge matters already waived, the court determined that punitive costs were necessary to mark its displeasure and deter similar conduct. Finding: The court is justified in awarding punitive costs of Rs. 200,000 against the Petitioner. Interaction of Issues The findings are deeply interconnected; the abuse of process identified in the first issue provides the factual foundation for the necessity of the punitive costs awarded in the second. The courtâ€™s determination that the Petitioner acted in bad faith by ignoring statutory bars to appeal and misleading the court directly informs the quantum and purpose of the punitive costs as a deterrent. Conclusion The application for special leave to appeal is dismissed. The Petitioner is directed to pay Rs. 200,000 as punitive costs to the Respondent within one month. Keywords Special Leave to Appeal — Order of Ejectment — State Lands (Recovery of Possession) Act — Appeal against Magistrate's order — Revision Application — Writ Application — Punitive costs — Abuse of process of Court — Willful ignorance of Court procedure — Disclosure of parallel proceedings

Parties: Silva v. Aleckman
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Default of appearance — Proxy — Attorney-at-law — Ex-parte trial — Negligence — Mistake — Evidence Ordinance — Admissibility of evidence — Civil Procedure Code — Writ of execution — Damages
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent-Respondent (Respondent) was previously ejected from premises at No. 11, Negombo Road, Kochikade, pursuant to an action instituted by the mother of the Substituted-Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant-Appellant (Petitioner). The original action resulted in a judgment for the Petitioner's mother, which was affirmed by the Court of Appeal. Subsequently, the Respondent sued the Petitioner's mother, alleging that the writ of the District Court was wrongfully issued and that the Petitioner was liable for damages caused to the Respondent's business and machinery by the Fiscal Officer during the ejectment. Upon receiving summons, the Petitioner's mother retained an Attorney-at-Law, Mr. Panditharatne, and paid him to file an answer. Mr. Panditharatne mistakenly recorded the date for filing the answer as 05.03.1993 instead of 05.02.1993. Consequently, the Petitioner failed to appear on the correct date. Upon discovering the error, the Attorney attempted to obtain the Respondent's consent to vacate the ex-parte trial date, but the request was refused. At the ex-parte trial on 27.04.1993, the Attorney offered to pay costs and requested leave to file an answer, which was also rejected. The District Court entered judgment for the Respondent based on uncontradicted oral evidence. The Petitioner's subsequent applications to vacate the ex-parte decree and for revision were dismissed by the lower courts. Issues Whether the Court of Appeal erred in holding that there were no reasonable grounds for the default of appearance and that the principle in Kathiresu v Sinniah was inapplicable. Whether the Court of Appeal erred in holding that legally admissible evidence was led at the ex-parte trial and in refusing to act in revision. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 27(1) Civil Procedure Code, Section 85(1) Evidence Ordinance, Section 59 Evidence Ordinance, Section 91 Cases Referred to: Kathiresu v Sinniah - [71-nlr-450] L.J. Peiris and Co. Limited v L.C.H. Peiris - [74-nlr-261] Paul Coir (Pvt) Ltd v Waas - [2002/v1-sri-lr-13] Udeshi v Mather - [1988/v1-sri-lr-12] Tillekeratne v Wijesinghe - [11-nlr-270] Punchihamy v Rambukpotha - [16-times-of-ceylon-law-reports] Packiyanathan v Singarajah - [2003/v2-sri-lr-205] Schareguivel v Orr - [11-nlr-302] Pakir Mohideen v Mohamadu Cassim - [4-nlr-299] Wimalasiri and another v Premasiri - [2003-sri-lr-330] Queen v Murugan Ramasamy - [64-nlr-433] Ranesinghe v Henry - [1-nlr-303] Analysis Issue 1: Default of appearance Rule applied: Section 27(1) of the Civil Procedure Code and the principle that a lawyer's mistake in noting dates constitutes an excusable error rather than negligence. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the proxy was signed on 05.01.1993, establishing the agency relationship. Relying on L.J. Peiris and Co. Limited v L.C.H. Peiris and Udeshi v Mather, the Court held that proxy irregularities are curable and that Section 27(1) is directory. Furthermore, the Court distinguished this case from those involving gross negligence, finding that the Attorney's error in recording the date was a "mistake" similar to that in Kathiresu v Sinniah, and that the Petitioner took all feasible measures to rectify the error upon discovery. Finding: The Court of Appeal erred; the default was due to an excusable mistake, and the proxy was valid. Issue 2: Admissibility of evidence Rule applied: Section 91 of the Evidence Ordinance regarding primary evidence. Court's reasoning: The Respondent relied on oral evidence to prove damages arising from a prior court writ without producing the actual court decision. Under Section 91, where a matter is required to be in a document, the document itself must be produced. The Court held that "personal knowledge" cannot override the requirement for primary documentary evidence. Additionally, the Court noted that the Petitioner could not be held liable for the Fiscal Officer's actions during the execution of a writ, citing Ranesinghe v Henry. Finding: The evidence led at the ex-parte trial was inadmissible, and the District Court failed to satisfy itself of the claim's merits. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the default was an excusable mistake allowed the Court to set aside the ex-parte decree, while the finding that the evidence presented at that trial was legally inadmissible demonstrated that the ex-parte judgment itself was substantively flawed and could not be sustained. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, and the ex-parte judgment is set aside. No costs are ordered. Keywords Default of appearance — Proxy — Attorney-at-law — Ex-parte trial — Negligence — Mistake — Evidence Ordinance — Admissibility of evidence — Civil Procedure Code — Writ of execution — Damages

Parties: Jayasuriya v. Manikkaratnam and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Marsoof, PC, J; Sripavan, J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Article 11 of the Constitution — Torture or cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment — Article 13(1) of the Constitution — Arrest according to procedure established by law — Medical evidence — Credibility of evidence — Possession of heroin
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Ediriweera Arukpatabandige Sugath Rohan Jayasuriya, filed this application alleging that the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd Respondents, who were police officers at the Tissamaharama Police Station, violated his fundamental rights under Articles 11 and 13(1) of the Constitution. The Petitioner claimed that on 29 December 2007, he was assaulted by the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd Respondents, resulting in him becoming unconscious. The Respondents contended that the incident occurred at a road block where the Petitioner, who was intoxicated, fell off his motorcycle. Upon being apprehended, the 1st Respondent discovered two packets of heroin in the Petitioner's possession. The Respondents maintained that the Petitioner's injuries were consistent with a fall from his motorcycle, which also sustained damage. The Petitioner was subsequently arrested for being intoxicated and in possession of heroin. The Petitioner later challenged the legality of his arrest and the veracity of the evidence regarding the heroin possession, noting that he was discharged in the Magistrate's Court proceedings. Issues Whether the Petitioner was subjected to torture or cruel, inhuman, or degrading treatment in violation of Article 11 of the Constitution. Whether the Petitionerâ€™s arrest was conducted in violation of the procedure established by law under Article 13(1) of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 11, 12(1), 13(1), 17, 126 Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979, Sections 32(1)(a), 32(1)(b), 188 Cases Referred to: Channa Peris v. Attorney General - [1994/v1-sri-lr-1] Nadasena v. Chandradasa - [2006/v1-sri-lr-207] Namasivayam v. Gunawardena - [1989/v1-sri-lr-394] Soogrim v. Trinidad and Tobago - [Communication No. 362/1989] G. Jeganathan v. Attorney General - [1982/v1-sri-lr-294] Velmurugu v. Attorney General - [1981/v1-sri-lr-406] Kapugeekiyana v. Hettiarachchi - [1984/v2-sri-lr-153] Grant v. Jamaica - [Communication No. 353/1988] Fillastre v. Bolivia - [Communication No. 336/1988] Tomasi v. France - [15 EHRR 1] Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 11 Rule applied: Article 11 of the Constitution; the principle that a high degree of certainty and cogent medical evidence is required to prove allegations of torture. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the Petitioner's medical reports were inconsistent. The initial report recorded the Petitioner's admission that his injuries resulted from a fall while intoxicated. Subsequent claims of assault were made eight days later and were not corroborated by the nature of the injuries, which were consistent with a motorcycle accident. The court found the Petitioner's credibility undermined by his shifting narrative and his history of substance abuse. Finding: The Petitioner failed to discharge the burden of proof; no violation of Article 11 was established. Issue 2: Violation of Article 13(1) Rule applied: Article 13(1) of the Constitution; Section 32(1)(b) of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979. Court's reasoning: The court found that the police had credible information regarding the commission of a cognizable offence (possession of heroin). The arrest note (IR 6) confirmed that the Petitioner was informed of the reasons for his arrest at the time of apprehension. The court rejected the argument that the Petitioner's discharge in the Magistrate's Court invalidated the arrest, noting that a discharge is not an acquittal and does not negate the existence of reasonable suspicion at the time of the arrest. Finding: The arrest was conducted in accordance with the procedure established by law; no violation of Article 13(1) was established. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues were reinforced by the court's assessment of the Petitioner's credibility. The inconsistencies in the Petitioner's account regarding the alleged assault (Article 11) mirrored the discrepancies in his claims regarding the circumstances of his arrest (Article 13). The court's determination that the Petitioner's injuries were self-inflicted due to a fall corroborated the police version of events, which in turn supported the legality of the arrest. Conclusion The application is dismissed as the Petitioner failed to prove the alleged violations of his fundamental rights. No costs are ordered. Keywords Fundamental rights — Article 11 of the Constitution — Torture or cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment — Article 13(1) of the Constitution — Arrest according to procedure established by law — Medical evidence — Credibility of evidence — Possession of heroin

Parties: Kapugeekiyana v. Tennakone and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Marsoof, P.C., J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Land Acquisition Act — Public purpose — Section 2 notice — Disclosure of purpose — Section 38(a) — Urgency — Section 39A — Divesting order — Compensation — Improvements to land
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, D.F.A. Kapugeekiyana, challenged the acquisition of his land by the Ministry of Lands under the Land Acquisition Act. The acquisition was initiated under Section 38(a) of the Act, with a notice issued under Section 2 and a vesting order published in the Government Gazette on 08.01.1986 following an order of urgency dated 02.01.1986. The Petitioner sought a writ of certiorari to quash the acquisition order, alleging a failure to disclose a clear public purpose and a lack of urgency. Alternatively, the Petitioner sought a writ of mandamus to compel the divestment of the land under Section 39A of the Act, claiming the land remained unused for its intended purpose. The Court of Appeal refused the application, finding that the acquisition was valid and that a supervening public purpose existed. The Petitioner appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Evidence presented indicated that the land was intended for urban development and subsequently designated for the construction of married quarters for the Special Task Force. The Respondents demonstrated that significant development work, including land filling and preparation, had been conducted on the property. The Petitioner had previously requested compensation for the land and had not initially challenged the acquisition on the basis of an undisclosed public purpose. Issues Whether the acquisition was void ab initio due to the failure to disclose a specific public purpose in the Section 2 notice. Whether the Court of Appeal erred in law by upholding the acquisition based on a supervening public purpose. Whether the Court of Appeal erred in its factual findings regarding the existence of an urgent public purpose and the adequacy of the development work carried out on the land. Whether the Petitioner satisfied the statutory requirements under Section 39A of the Land Acquisition Act to warrant a divesting order. Applicable Law Statutes: Land Acquisition Act (Section 2, Section 4, Section 5, Section 7, Section 38(a), Section 39A, Section 40) Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka (Article 128(2)) Cases Referred to: Manel Fernando v D.M. Jayarathne, Minister of Agriculture and Lands - [Not specified] De Silva v Athukorale, Minister of Lands, Irrigation and Mahaweli Development - [1993/v1-sri-lr-283] Analysis Issue 1: Disclosure of Public Purpose Rule applied: Section 2(1) and 2(2) of the Land Acquisition Act. Court's reasoning: The Court affirmed that a Minister must have a clear public purpose in mind when issuing a Section 2 notice. However, upon reviewing the documentation, the Court found that the Petitioner had not raised the issue of lack of clarity regarding the public purpose in his initial communications, but had instead sought compensation. The Court determined that the purpose of "urban development" was sufficiently communicated and that the Petitioner failed to provide evidence that the purpose was deceptive or nonexistent. Finding: The acquisition was not void ab initio as the public purpose was sufficiently established and disclosed. Issue 2: Supervening Public Purpose Rule applied: Section 39A of the Land Acquisition Act and the principles established in De Silva v Athukorale. Court's reasoning: The Court held that even if the original purpose of an acquisition is not fulfilled, the Minister may decline to divest if a new, real, and present supervening public purpose exists. The Court found that the land was utilized for the construction of married quarters for the Special Task Force, which constituted a valid supervening public purpose. Finding: The Court of Appeal did not err in upholding the acquisition based on a supervening public purpose. Issue 3: Urgency and Development Rule applied: Land Acquisition Act (Section 38(a)) and guidelines from Institute for Construction Training and Development (Publication No: SCA/3/3). Court's reasoning: The Court examined photographic and documentary evidence showing that the land had been transformed from marshy/paddy land into a site prepared for construction. The Court concluded that the extensive filling and preparation work demonstrated both the necessity of the acquisition and the active pursuit of the public purpose. Finding: The Court of Appeal correctly determined that the acquisition was warranted by urgency and that significant development had occurred. Issue 4: Statutory Requirements for Divestment Rule applied: Section 39A(2) of the Land Acquisition Act. Court's reasoning: To qualify for a divesting order, the Petitioner must satisfy four cumulative conditions: no compensation paid, no use for public purpose, no improvements made, and consent of interested parties. The Court found that compensation had been paid and significant improvements had been made to the land. Finding: The Petitioner failed to satisfy the mandatory conditions under Section 39A(2), rendering him ineligible for a divesting order. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected; the failure to satisfy the statutory requirements for divestment (Issue 4) is reinforced by the court's factual finding that the land was indeed developed for a valid supervening public purpose (Issues 2 and 3). Because the land was actively being used for a public purpose, the Petitioner's claim that the acquisition was void due to lack of purpose (Issue 1) was rendered untenable. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The Petitioner is ordered to pay costs in the sum of Rs 50,000/- to the Respondents. Keywords Land Acquisition Act — Public purpose — Section 2 notice — Disclosure of purpose — Section 38(a) — Urgency — Section 39A — Divesting order — Compensation — Improvements to land

Parties: Gunawardana Weththasinghe and Others v. Ariyawathi Mudalige and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Marasinghe, J
Keywords: Partition action — District Court judgment — Civil Appellate High Court decision — Transfer of land — Undivided share — Deed of Sale — Agreement to Sell — Interpretation of documents — Vendor's rights — Boundaries of land — Extent of corpus
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Appellant-Petitioner instituted a partition action in the District Court of Matugama regarding a land known as Egallawedeniya, specifically Lot 4 of Plan No. 119. The corpus was previously subject to a partition decree in D.C. Kalutara Case No. 28363, which allocated 2/3 of the land to Dona Louisa Edirimanna Hamine and 1/3 to Kalutara Muhandiramge Misilin Rodrigo. The Plaintiff-Appellant claimed title through a transfer from Don Ilian Somapala Ranawaka, who held a 1/3 share of the corpus. The 3rd Defendant-Respondent claimed a portion of the land based on a subsequent Deed of Conveyance No. 304. The Civil Appellate High Court of Kalutara previously dismissed the Appellant's appeal and affirmed a judgment that allocated 15 perches to the 3rd Defendant-Respondent, which the Appellant challenged on the basis that the vendor had already divested all his rights to the Appellant via prior deeds. Issues Whether the judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court was supported by the evidence regarding the allocation of shares to the 3rd Defendant-Respondent. Whether the vendor, Don Ilian Somapala Ranawaka, retained any legal interest in the corpus to transfer to the 3rd Defendant-Respondent after executing Deed P8 in favor of the Plaintiff-Appellant. Applicable Law Statutes: Not specified Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Evidence supporting the allocation of shares Rule applied: The court applied the principle that a partition decree must accurately reflect the extent of the corpus and the valid title of the parties. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the High Court judgment allocated shares that, when totaled, exceeded the actual extent of the corpus by 15 perches. The court found this calculation to be a perverse finding of fact, as it resulted in an inaccurate distribution of the land that did not align with the established ownership rights of the parties. Finding: The judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court was not supported by the evidence and contained a mathematical error regarding the total extent of the corpus. Issue 2: Validity of the transfer to the 3rd Defendant-Respondent Rule applied: The court applied the principle of nemo dat quod non habet (no one gives what they do not have) regarding the transfer of property rights. Court's reasoning: The court examined Deed P8, through which the vendor transferred his entire interest in the corpus to the Plaintiff-Appellant. The court determined that the vendor did not reserve any right, title, or interest in the land at the time of that transfer. Consequently, the vendor had no remaining legal interest to convey to the 3rd Defendant-Respondent via the subsequent Deed 3V1. The court rejected the 3rd Defendant-Respondent's claim because the vendor had already divested himself of all rights to the property. Finding: The 3rd Defendant-Respondent acquired no rights through Deed 3V1, as the vendor had no remaining interest to transfer. Interaction of Issues The findings are intrinsically linked; the court's determination that the vendor had no remaining interest to transfer (Issue 2) directly explains why the High Court's allocation of 15 perches to the 3rd Defendant-Respondent was factually and legally erroneous (Issue 1). By resolving the title dispute in favor of the Appellant, the court corrected the mathematical error in the partition distribution. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court dated 03.05.2011 is set aside. The judgment of the Additional District Judge of Matugama dated 31.01.2003 is affirmed subject to the variations regarding the share allocations. No costs are ordered. Keywords Partition action — District Court judgment — Civil Appellate High Court decision — Transfer of land — Undivided share — Deed of Sale — Agreement to Sell — Interpretation of documents — Vendor's rights — Boundaries of land — Extent of corpus

Parties: Abeysinghe v. Heen Manike and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Leave to Appeal — Lawful title — Tenancy rights — Dependants — Rent Act No. 7 — Section 36(2) — Illegitimate child — Spouse — Family connection — Burden of proof
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Appellant-Petitioner, D. K. Abeysinghe, instituted action in the District Court of Mount Lavinia seeking a declaration of title to the land described as Lot 1B of Plan No. 2023, the ejectment of the 1st and 2nd Defendants-Respondents, and the recovery of damages for wrongful occupation. The premises were originally occupied by K. P. Peter Perera, who was a tenant of the Appellant's father. Following the death of the Appellant's father, the Appellant became the landlord. K. P. Peter Perera remained in occupation until his death on 14 May 1990. The 1st Respondent, who cohabited with the deceased, and the 2nd Respondent, the deceased's son, claimed tenancy rights following the death of K. P. Peter Perera. The District Court entered judgment for the Respondents, finding them to be lawful tenants by operation of law. The Provincial High Court of Civil Appeal of Mount Lavinia subsequently dismissed the Appellant's appeal. Issues Whether the dismissal of the Appellant's action was erroneous in law given the Appellant's established title to the premises. Whether the 1st Respondent, having admitted she was not married to the deceased tenant, qualified as a "dependant" under Section 36(2) of the Rent Act No. 7 of 1972. Whether the lower courts erred in finding that both Respondents were lawful tenants of the Appellant. Applicable Law Statutes: Rent Act No. 7 of 1972 Rent Act No. 26 of 2002 Workman's Compensation Ordinance No. 19 of 1935 Cases Referred to: Kodithuwakku Arachchi v. Wadugodapitiya - [1994/v3-sri-lr-29] Smelting Co. of Australia v. The Commissioner of Inland Revenue - [1897/v1-qb-175] Analysis Issue 1: Appellant's Title and Action Rule applied: The court applied the principles of property ownership and the statutory requirements for tenancy succession under the Rent Act. Court's reasoning: The court acknowledged the Appellant's lawful title to the premises. It reasoned that the dismissal of the action was predicated on the erroneous finding that the Respondents held valid tenancy rights. Once the tenancy claim was invalidated, the Appellant's right to possession and ejectment was restored. Finding: The dismissal of the action was erroneous. Issue 2: Status of 1st Respondent as Dependant Rule applied: Section 36(2)(a) of the Rent Act No. 7 of 1972 and the doctrine of ejusdem generis. Court's reasoning: The court held that "dependant" must be interpreted in the context of the preceding words (spouse, child, parent, brother, or sister), requiring a familial connection. The 1st Respondent admitted the deceased was married to another woman who was alive at the time of his death, disqualifying the 1st Respondent as a spouse. Furthermore, she failed to prove any exceptional circumstances to qualify as a dependant. Finding: The 1st Respondent was not a dependant under the Rent Act. Issue 3: Lawful Tenancy of Respondents Rule applied: Section 36(2)(a) of the Rent Act No. 7 of 1972. Court's reasoning: The 2nd Respondentâ€™s claim was derivative, based on the 1st Respondentâ€™s status. Since the 1st Respondent was not a lawful tenant, the 2nd Respondentâ€™s claim failed. Additionally, the 2nd Respondent was an adult at the time of the tenant's death and did not meet the criteria for a dependant. Finding: The Respondents were not lawful tenants. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the resolution of the 1st Respondent's status as a non-dependant directly invalidated the 2nd Respondent's derivative claim. Consequently, the absence of a valid tenancy succession rendered the Respondents' occupation unlawful, thereby necessitating the reversal of the lower courts' decisions regarding the Appellant's claim for ejectment. Conclusion The judgment of the High Court is set aside. Judgment is entered in favour of the Plaintiff-Appellant-Petitioner as prayed for in the plaint, with costs fixed at Rs. 30,000/-. Keywords Leave to Appeal — Lawful title — Tenancy rights — Dependants — Rent Act No. 7 — Section 36(2) — Illegitimate child — Spouse — Family connection — Burden of proof

Parties: Tangerine Beach Hotel v. Ryan William Smith and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J.; Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J.; Dep, P.C., J.
Keywords: vicarious liability — tort law — Motor Traffic Act No. 14 of 1951 — admissibility of evidence — corporate veil — distinct legal entity — negligence — proof of documents — judicial precedent — liability of vehicle owner — evidence corroboration
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal made by the 1st Defendant-Appellant-Petitioner, Tangerine Beach Hotel, against a judgment delivered by the Provincial High Court of Civil Appeal of the Western Province on 26 February 2013. The appeal concerns two primary legal questions regarding vicarious liability and the admissibility of certain documents as evidence. The incident that led to the appeal involved a vehicular accident where a vehicle owned by the Petitioner collided with a train, resulting in injuries to three passengers. The Petitioner contended that it should not be held vicariously liable for the actions of the driver, who was an employee of a separate entity, Tangerine Tours Limited. The Respondent, Ryan William Smith, argued that as the legal owner of the vehicle, the Petitioner was vicariously liable for the driver's actions. The case also involved the admissibility of medical reports and other documents that the Petitioner claimed were not proven and thus should not have been considered by the court. Issues The issues raised for consideration by the Supreme Court were: 1. Whether the Provincial High Court of Civil Appeal of the Western Province misdirected itself in holding the Petitioner vicariously liable for the actions of the 3rd Defendant. 2. Whether the Provincial High Court misdirected itself by failing to recognize that the documents marked by the Respondent were admitted into evidence subject to proof and were allegedly not proven. Applicable Law Statutes: - Motor Traffic Act No. 14 of 1951 - High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 19 of 1990 - Civil Procedure Code Cases Referred to: - Krishnan Nalinda Priyadarshana v Kandana Arachchcige Nilmini Dhammika Perera - Ellis v Paranavitana - Rafina and Another v The Port (Cargo) Corporation and Another - Jafferjee v Munasinghe - Seattle v Stone - T. H. I. De Silva v Trust Co Ltd - Ormrod v Crossville Motor Services Ltd - Silva v Kindersley - Sri Lanka Ports Authority and Another v Jugolinija – Boat East - Samson v Aitchison Analysis Issue 1: Vicarious Liability Rule applied: The principle of vicarious liability in tort law. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the nature of vicarious liability, which holds an employer liable for the negligent acts of an employee performed within the course of employment. The Petitioner argued that the driver was not an employee and thus no vicarious liability should attach. However, the Court noted that the relationship between the Petitioner and the driver was complex, as both the Petitioner and Tangerine Tours Limited shared common ownership and management. The Court cited the case of Krishnan Nalinda Priyadarshana, emphasizing that vicarious liability arises when there is a direct nexus between the employer and employee. The Petitioner attempted to distance itself from the driver by asserting that the driver was not under its control. However, the Court found that the Petitioner had not sufficiently demonstrated that it had relinquished control over the driver. The evidence indicated that the Petitioner played an active role in selecting drivers for its vehicles, which further complicated the assertion of no liability. The Court also referenced the Motor Traffic Act, which imposes prima facie liability on vehicle owners for accidents involving their vehicles. The Petitioner’s argument that the corporate veil should be lifted was also considered, with the Court noting that the close relationship between the two companies suggested that the Petitioner had sufficient interest in the driver's actions. Finding: The Court concluded that the Petitioner had not provided adequate evidence to absolve itself of vicarious liability for the driver's actions. Issue 2: Admissibility of Evidence Rule applied: The rules governing the admissibility of evidence as outlined in the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Petitioner contested the admissibility of certain documents presented by the Respondent, claiming they were not proven and thus should not have been considered by the court. The Court examined the relevant provisions of the Civil Procedure Code, particularly Section 154, which outlines the requirements for formally tendering documents as evidence. The Court noted that the Respondent had tendered the documents subject to proof and had subsequently provided evidence to support their validity. The Petitioner failed to demonstrate that it had objected to the documents at the time they were presented, which is a prerequisite for contesting their admissibility on appeal. The Court referenced several precedents, including Silva v Kindersley, which established that documents not objected to at the time of admission are considered legally admissible. The Court emphasized that the Petitioner had not specified the grounds for its objection nor provided a reasonable basis for questioning the validity of the evidence. Finding: The Court determined that the documents were admissible and had been properly considered in the lower court's judgment. Interaction of Issues The issues of vicarious liability and the admissibility of evidence are interrelated in this case. The determination of whether the Petitioner is vicariously liable hinges on the evidence presented, including the documents that the Petitioner sought to challenge. If the evidence supporting the Respondent's claims is deemed admissible and credible, it strengthens the argument for vicarious liability. Conversely, if the evidence were to be excluded, it could potentially weaken the Respondent's case. Conclusion The Supreme Court denied the application for leave to appeal, affirming the decision of the Provincial High Court. The Court ordered the Petitioner to pay costs amounting to Rs. 100,000 to the Respondents. The judgment underscores the principles of vicarious liability and the importance of adhering to procedural rules regarding the admissibility of evidence in civil proceedings. The ruling reinforces the notion that vehicle owners may bear responsibility for the actions of those who operate their vehicles, particularly when a close relationship exists between the parties involved. Keywords vicarious liability — tort law — Motor Traffic Act No. 14 of 1951 — admissibility of evidence — corporate veil — distinct legal entity — negligence — proof of documents — judicial precedent — liability of vehicle owner — evidence corroboration

Parties: Tangerine Beach Hotel v. Rodney Errol Smith and Others

Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal made by the 1st Defendant-Appellant-Petitioner, Tangerine Beach Hotel, against a judgment delivered by the Provincial High Court of Civil Appeal of the Western Province. The appeal was prompted by a motor vehicle accident involving a vehicle owned by the Petitioner, which collided with a train, resulting in injuries to three passengers. The Petitioner contended that it should not be held vicariously liable for the actions of the driver, who was not an employee but rather an employee of a related entity, Tangerine Tours Limited. The Respondent, Rodney Errol Smith, argued that the Petitioner, as the legal owner of the vehicle, should be held liable for the driver's actions. The case raises significant questions regarding the principles of vicarious liability, the admissibility of evidence, and the responsibilities of vehicle owners in the context of tort law. Issues The Court identified two primary issues for consideration: 1. Whether the Provincial High Court misdirected itself in holding the Petitioner vicariously liable for the actions of the 3rd Defendant, the driver. 2. Whether the Provincial High Court erred in failing to recognize that the documents submitted by the Respondent were admitted into evidence subject to proof and were allegedly not proven. Applicable Law Statutes: High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No 19 of 1990 — Motor Traffic Act No. 14 of 1951 — Civil Procedure Code. However, the Court noted that the Petitioner, as the owner of the vehicle, had a prima facie responsibility for the actions of anyone using the vehicle. However, the Court found that the Petitioner had not provided sufficient evidence to demonstrate that it had relinquished control over the driver. The relationship between the Petitioner and Tangerine Tours Limited, which employed the driver, was scrutinized. The Court noted that both entities shared common directors and interests, suggesting a closer relationship than merely that of separate legal entities. The Court also highlighted that the Petitioner had a vested interest in the driver's actions, as the vehicle was being used for purposes related to the Petitioner’s business. The Court concluded that the Petitioner had not successfully rebutted the presumption of vicarious liability. Finding: The Court upheld the Provincial High Court's finding of vicarious liability. Issue 2: Admissibility of Evidence Rule applied: The standards for the admissibility of evidence as outlined in the Civil Procedure Code. The Court noted that the Respondent had tendered the documents subject to proof and had provided evidence of the injuries sustained by the passengers. The Petitioner claimed that it had objected to the documents' admission; however, the Court found no substantial evidence of such an objection being raised at the appropriate time. The Court referenced previous case law, including Silva v Kindersley and Sri Lanka Ports Authority v Jugolinija, which established that failure to object to the admissibility of evidence at trial precludes raising such objections on appeal. The Court concluded that the Petitioner had not adequately demonstrated any grounds for questioning the validity of the evidence presented by the Respondent. Finding: The Court ruled that the documents were admissible and had been properly considered by the Provincial High Court. Interaction of Issues The issues of vicarious liability and the admissibility of evidence are interrelated in this case. The determination of whether the Petitioner is liable for the actions of the driver hinges on the evidence presented regarding the driver's employment status and the relationship between the parties. The Court's analysis of the admissibility of evidence directly impacts the findings on liability, as the evidence presented by the Respondent was crucial in establishing the circumstances surrounding the accident and the injuries sustained. Conclusion The Supreme Court denied the application for leave to appeal, affirming the decision of the Provincial High Court. The Court ordered the Petitioner to pay costs amounting to Rs. 100,000 to the Respondents. The judgment underscores the principles of vicarious liability in tort law and the importance of evidentiary standards in civil proceedings, reinforcing the notion that vehicle owners bear a significant responsibility for the actions of those who operate their vehicles, regardless of the employment relationship. Parties: Tangerine Beach Hotel v. Andrew Errol Smith and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J.; Marsoof, P.C., J.; Dep, P.C., J.
Keywords: vicarious liability — negligence — Motor Traffic Act — admissibility of evidence — burden of proof — corporate veil — distinct legal entity — tort law — legal ownership — statutory liability — evidence ordinance — judicial precedent — appeal process — damages — control over driver
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal made by the 1st Defendant-Appellant-Petitioner, Tangerine Beach Hotel, against a judgment delivered by the Provincial High Court of Civil Appeal of the Western Province on 26 February 2013. The appeal concerns two primary legal questions regarding vicarious liability and the admissibility of evidence. The incident that led to the appeal involved a vehicular accident where a vehicle owned by the Petitioner collided with a train, resulting in injuries to three passengers. The Petitioner contends that it should not be held vicariously liable for the actions of the driver, the 3rd Defendant, as he was not an employee of the Petitioner but rather of a separate entity, Tangerine Tours Limited. The Respondent, Andrew Errol Smith, argues that the Petitioner, as the legal owner of the vehicle, is vicariously liable for the driver's actions. The case also involves the admissibility of certain documents presented by the Respondent, which the Petitioner claims were not properly proven and should not have been considered by the court. Issues The court identified two key issues for determination: 1. Whether the Provincial High Court misdirected itself in holding the Petitioner vicariously liable for the actions of the 3rd Defendant. 2. Whether the Provincial High Court erred in failing to recognize that the documents marked by the Respondent were admitted into evidence subject to proof and were allegedly not proven. Applicable Law Statutes: Motor Traffic Act No. 14 of 1951; Evidence Ordinance; Civil Procedure Code. Cases Referred to: Krishnan Nalinda Priyadarshana v. Kandana Arachchcige Nilmini Dhammika Perera - [SC. Appeal 67/2012]; Jafferjee v. Munasinghe - [51 NLR 313]; Ellis v. Paranavitana - [58 NLR 373]; Rafina and Another v. The Port (Cargo) Corporation and Another - [1980] 2 SLR 189; T. H. I. De Silva v. Trust Co Ltd - [55 NLR 241]; Ormrod v. Crossville Motor Services Ltd - [1953] 2 AER 755; Silva v. Kindersley - [1914] 18 N.L.R. 85; Sri Lanka Ports Authority and Another v. Jugolinija – Boat East - [1981] 1 Sri LR 18. Analysis Issue 1: Vicarious Liability Rule applied: The principle of vicarious liability in tort law. Court's reasoning: The court examined the relationship between the Petitioner and the 3rd Defendant, focusing on whether the 3rd Defendant was an employee of the Petitioner or if there was a sufficient nexus to establish vicarious liability. The Petitioner argued that the 3rd Defendant was not under its control and cited the case of Krishnan Nalinda Priyadarshana, which emphasized that vicarious liability arises only when the wrongful act is performed within the scope of employment. The Petitioner further referenced Salmond's principles on vicarious liability, asserting that liability only attaches to employers of the driver. However, the Respondent countered this by highlighting that the Petitioner, as the owner of the vehicle, bears prima facie responsibility for the actions of the driver. The court noted that the Petitioner and Tangerine Tours Limited shared common ownership and management, which could establish a connection that undermines the argument of distinct legal entities. The court also referenced various cases that support the notion that vehicle owners can be held liable for the actions of those they allow to operate their vehicles, regardless of the employment status of the driver. Finding: The court concluded that the Petitioner had not sufficiently demonstrated that it had relinquished control over the driver, thus affirming the vicarious liability ruling. Issue 2: Admissibility of Evidence Rule applied: The rules governing the admissibility of evidence as per the Evidence Ordinance and Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Petitioner contested the admissibility of documents presented by the Respondent, arguing that they were not proven and should not have been considered by the court. The court examined the procedural requirements for admitting evidence, particularly focusing on whether the Petitioner had raised objections to the documents at the trial level. The court cited the Evidence Ordinance, which stipulates that documents must be formally tendered and that failure to object at the time of admission typically waives the right to contest their admissibility later. The court found that the Petitioner had not provided adequate evidence of any objection to the documents during the trial, thus rendering the objection invalid on appeal. The court also referenced judicial precedents that support the notion that unobjected documents become admissible evidence. Finding: The court determined that the Petitioner could not challenge the admissibility of the documents on appeal due to the lack of timely objection. Interaction of Issues The issues of vicarious liability and the admissibility of evidence are interrelated in this case, as the determination of liability hinges on the evidence presented. The court's analysis of the vicarious liability issue was influenced by the evidence that was deemed admissible, which included documents that supported the Respondent's claims regarding the injuries sustained in the accident. The court's ruling on the admissibility of evidence ultimately reinforced its finding on vicarious liability, as the evidence presented by the Respondent was critical in establishing the connection between the Petitioner and the actions of the driver. Conclusion The Supreme Court denied the application for leave to appeal, affirming the decision of the Provincial High Court regarding vicarious liability and the admissibility of evidence. The court ordered the Petitioner to pay costs amounting to Rs. 100,000 to the Respondents. The judgment underscores the importance of maintaining proper evidentiary procedures and the implications of ownership in establishing liability in tort cases. The court's ruling reflects a commitment to uphold the principles of justice while ensuring that legal standards are met in the adjudication of civil disputes.

Parties: Noon and Others v. University Grants Commission and Others
Bench: Mohan Pieris, J.; K. Sripavan, J.; E. Wanasundera, P.C., J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — University admission — Special quota — Foreign qualifications — University Grants Commission — Admission criteria — Statutory power — Ultra vires doctrine
Summary: Facts The Petitioners, Ms. A.M. Noon and K.P. Noon, sought admission for the first Petitioner to a Sri Lankan university for the 2008/2009 academic year under a special quota for students with foreign qualifications. The University Grants Commission manual and handbook stipulated that children of Sri Lankan diplomatic personnel must have received education abroad for at least three years in the six-year period immediately preceding the qualifying examination to be eligible for this quota. The first Petitioner accompanied her father, the second Petitioner, on foreign postings to Indonesia and the Maldives between 2002 and 2006. Upon returning to Sri Lanka in 2006, the first Petitioner enrolled in a local school and sat for the G.C.E. (A/L) examination in 2008. Consequently, she had only studied abroad for two years prior to the qualifying examination, failing to meet the three-year requirement. The Petitioners argued that the first Petitioner should be admitted based on precedents where other students were admitted despite not meeting the criteria. Issues Whether the first Petitioner was entitled to admission under the special quota despite failing to meet the three-year foreign education requirement stipulated in the University Grants Commission manual and handbook. Whether the Petitioners' right to equality under Article 12(1) of the Constitution was violated by the refusal of admission, given that other students had allegedly been admitted in similar circumstances. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Universities Act No. 16 of 1978 Cases Referred to: C.W. Mackie and Company Ltd. v. Hugh Molagoda Commissioner General of Inland Revenue - [1986/v1-sri-lr-300] Gamaethige v. Siriwardene - [1988/v1-sri-lr-384] Dissanayake v. Priyal de Silva - [2007/v2-sri-lr-134] Patrick Lowe v. Commercial Bank of Ceylon Ltd. - [2001/v1-sri-lr-280] Ashbury Railway Carriage & Iron Co. Ltd. v. Hector Riche - [Not specified] Attorney-General v. The Great Eastern Railway - [Not specified] Analysis Issue 1: Eligibility under the special quota Rule applied: Clause 18(d) of the University Grants Commission Manual and Clause 2.2(b) of the Handbook for the Academic Year 2008/2009. Court's reasoning: The court found that the governing criteria required a candidate to have received education abroad for at least three years in the six-year period immediately preceding the qualifying examination. The first Petitioner returned to Sri Lanka in 2006 and studied locally for two years before sitting the 2008 examination. As she did not satisfy the three-year requirement, she was ineligible for the quota. Finding: The first Petitioner failed to meet the mandatory eligibility criteria. Issue 2: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution and the principle that equality cannot be invoked to compel an illegal act. Court's reasoning: The court held that Article 12(1) guarantees equal treatment in the performance of lawful acts, not the equal violation of the law. Citing C.W. Mackie and Gamaethige, the court emphasized that previous illegal admissions do not create a legal right for others to be admitted illegally. The University Grants Commission is bound by its own statutory criteria and cannot act in an ad hoc manner. Finding: There was no violation of Article 12(1) as the Petitioners could not rely on past administrative errors to compel the commission of a further wrong. Interaction of Issues The findings are intrinsically linked; the determination that the first Petitioner was ineligible under the specific regulatory criteria (Issue 1) rendered the claim of discriminatory treatment (Issue 2) legally unsustainable. Because the criteria were validly established under the Universities Act, the court's rejection of the equality argument reinforced the necessity of strict adherence to the statutory admission framework. Conclusion The Petitioners failed to establish a violation of their fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The petition is dismissed. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — University admission — Special quota — Foreign qualifications — University Grants Commission — Admission criteria — Statutory power — Ultra vires doctrine

Parties: Orient Financial Services Corporation Ltd. v. Range Forest Officer and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Forest Ordinance — Section 40 — Forfeiture of vehicles — Registered owner — Absolute owner — Section 433A of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act — Hire purchase agreement — Lease agreement — Prevention of forest offences — Audi alteram partem — Burden of proof
Summary: Facts The Appellant, Orient Financial Services Corporation Ltd., entered into a lease agreement for a vehicle with D.P. Anura Kumara, who became the registered owner. The registered owner was charged in the Magistrate Court of Ampara for transporting timber without a permit, an offence under the Forest Ordinance, and pleaded guilty. Following the conviction, an inquiry was held regarding the forfeiture of the vehicle under Section 40A of the Forest Ordinance. The Appellant claimed the vehicle as the absolute owner, asserting that it had taken necessary precautions to prevent the offence and that the offence occurred without its knowledge. A representative of the Appellant provided evidence, including the registration book and lease agreement. The Magistrate ordered the confiscation of the vehicle, reasoning that the absolute owner could recover losses from the registered owner or guarantors and that returning the vehicle to the Appellant might result in it being returned to the accused, thereby defeating the deterrent purpose of the Forest Ordinance. The High Court of Ampara affirmed this order upon revision. The Court of Appeal subsequently dismissed the Appellant's petition without issuing notice, holding that the "owner" contemplated by the statute is the person with control over the vehicle, which is the registered owner. Issues Whether the Court of Appeal erred in law by holding that the "owner" contemplated under the Forest Ordinance is the registered owner rather than the absolute owner. Whether the Court of Appeal erred in considering the definition of "owner" under the Forest Ordinance when that issue was not raised by the Respondents in the lower courts. Whether the Magistrateâ€™s order of confiscation was in accordance with the law. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 128 Forest Ordinance, Section 25(1), Section 40, Section 40A Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979, Section 433A, Section 425-433 Cases Referred to: Manawadu v. Attorney General - [1987/v2-sri-lr-30] Mercantile Investment Ltd. v. Mohamed Mauloom - [1998/v3-sri-lr-32] The Finance Private Ltd. v. Agampodi Mahapedige Priyantha Chandana - [sc-appeal-105a/2008] Analysis Issue 1: Definition of "owner" Rule applied: Section 40 of the Forest Ordinance and Section 433A of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the term "owner" includes both the registered and absolute owner. However, Section 433A deems the absolute owner as the person entitled to possession. Despite this, the right to possession is not absolute; it remains subject to the court's discretion during forfeiture proceedings. The person in actual possession and control is best positioned to prove they took precautions to prevent the offence. Finding: The Court of Appeal erred in strictly limiting the definition to the registered owner, as the absolute owner also has a right to be heard. Issue 2: Raising issues of law Rule applied: Appellate jurisdiction principles. Court's reasoning: The Court determined that the interpretation of "owner" under the Forest Ordinance is a pure question of law. Appellate courts are entitled to consider such questions even if not explicitly raised by the parties in lower courts. Finding: The Court of Appeal did not err in considering this legal question. Issue 3: Legality of confiscation Rule applied: Section 40 of the Forest Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The Magistrate provided the Appellant an opportunity to be heard. The Court found that the legislative intent of Section 40 is to deter forest offences. Releasing the vehicle to an absolute owner who could return it to the offender would undermine this deterrent effect. The absolute owner has civil remedies against the registered owner for breach of contract, which mitigates the impact of the forfeiture. Finding: The Magistrateâ€™s order was consistent with the law and the legislative intent of the Forest Ordinance. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected: while the absolute owner has a legal right to be heard (Issue 1), the courtâ€™s discretion to order forfeiture (Issue 3) is guided by the legislative intent to prevent environmental harm. The procedural correctness of the Magistrate in hearing the Appellant (Issue 3) satisfies the requirements of natural justice, rendering the specific definition of "owner" (Issue 1) secondary to the court's ultimate power to confiscate to ensure deterrence. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The order of the Court of Appeal affirming the Magistrate's confiscation of the vehicle is upheld. No costs are awarded. Keywords Forest Ordinance — Section 40 — Forfeiture of vehicles — Registered owner — Absolute owner — Section 433A of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act — Hire purchase agreement — Lease agreement — Prevention of forest offences — Audi alteram partem — Burden of proof

Parties: Jayawardana and Others v. Dissanayaka and Others
Bench: Marsoof, PC. J; Sripavan, J; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Admission to school — Residency requirements — State land occupation — Administrative action — Circular interpretation — Right to education
Summary: Facts The Petitioners are the parents of a minor child and the minor child himself. The Petitioners sought admission for the minor child to Grade 1 at D.S. Senanayake College for the 2012 academic year. During the interview process, the child was awarded 78 marks, which included marks for residency within the school's feeder area as stipulated by Circular No. 2011/18. Despite achieving these marks, the child was excluded from the admission list. Other children who received lower marks than the 3rd Petitioner were admitted to the school. The Respondents subsequently contended that the Petitioners were ineligible for admission because they resided on State land, characterizing their occupation as unlawful. The Petitioners provided documentation, including birth certificates, marriage certificates, electoral lists, and electricity bills, to demonstrate that they had been continuous residents of the feeder area for a significant period. The Respondents did not communicate the reason for the exclusion to the Petitioners until the commencement of the legal proceedings. Issues Whether the exclusion of the 3rd Petitioner from admission to D.S. Senanayake College based on the alleged unlawful occupation of State land violated the fundamental right to equality guaranteed under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the term "resident" in Circular No. 2011/18 should be interpreted to require "lawful" residency. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 126 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution, which guarantees equality before the law and equal protection of the law. Court's reasoning: The court found that the Respondents acted arbitrarily and unreasonably by excluding the 3rd Petitioner despite him having qualified for admission based on the marks awarded by the interview board. The Respondents failed to provide any justification for the exclusion at the time of the decision, only raising the issue of land occupation during the litigation. This inconsistent and opaque conduct deprived the child of his right to education and constituted a violation of the Petitioners' fundamental rights.
