Finding: The Respondents' conduct was arbitrary and unreasonable, violating Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Issue 2: Interpretation of "Resident" in Circular No. 2011/18 Rule applied: Clause 6.1 of Circular No. 2011/18. Court's reasoning: The court held that the circular does not explicitly require a resident to be a "lawful" occupant of the land. The court reasoned that interpreting the circular to exclude those in "unlawful" occupation would unfairly penalize children from poorer segments of society who have resided in the feeder area for long periods. The court emphasized that the interview board's role is to verify residency, not to adjudicate on the legality of land occupation. Finding: The term "resident" in the circular does not require lawful occupation of the land; residency is a factual status that, once proven, entitles the applicant to the relevant marks. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are interdependent. The courtâ€™s determination that "resident" does not imply "lawful resident" directly informs the finding that the exclusion was arbitrary. Because the Respondents imposed an extra-legal requirement not found in the circular, their decision to exclude the child became an unreasonable exercise of administrative power, thereby establishing the violation of Article 12(1). Conclusion The court ordered that the 3rd Petitioner be admitted to Grade 3 of D.S. Senanayake College at the beginning of 2014. The State was ordered to pay the Petitioners costs in the amount of Rs. 30,000. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Admission to school — Residency requirements — State land occupation — Administrative action — Circular interpretation — Right to education

Parties: Akuretiyage and Others v. Panditharatne and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Marsoof, PC. J; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Admission to school — Past pupils category — Marking scheme — Interview Board — Appeal Board — Circulars issued by the Ministry of Education — Waiting list
Summary: Facts The Petitioners, Akuretiyage Onethra Amavindi and her father Akuretiyage Mahesh Kumar Lanka, sought admission for the 1st Petitioner to Grade 1 at Dharmashoka Vidyalaya, Ambalangoda, for the year 2013 under the past pupils category. The application process was governed by Ministry of Education circulars and specific notification requirements. The Petitioners participated in an interview conducted by the 1st to 5th Respondents. Following the denial of admission, the Petitioners appealed to the Appeal Board, which consisted of the 6th to 9th Respondents. The Petitioners challenged the marks awarded to them during the interview process, specifically contesting the scoring under the past pupils category. They argued that the 2nd Petitioner was entitled to higher marks for sports achievements and technical qualifications than those recorded on the mark sheet. The court reviewed the marking scheme and the specific allocations made by the Interview Board. Despite finding a minor calculation error in the total marks awarded to the Petitioners, the court determined that the corrected total remained below the required cut-off mark for admission. Issues Whether the Interview Board acted arbitrarily or in violation of Article 12(1) of the Constitution by awarding 4 marks instead of 5 marks under category 3 we(1) for the 2nd Petitioner's sports achievements. Whether the Interview Board erred in awarding 1 mark instead of 2 marks under category 4 we for the 2nd Petitioner's technical qualifications. Whether the failure to admit the 1st Petitioner to Grade 1 constituted a violation of the fundamental right to equality under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 17 Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 126 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Sports achievement marks Rule applied: Ministry of Education notification regarding the marking scheme for sports achievements, which stipulates that a position in one sport is taken into account only once. Court's reasoning: The court examined the mark sheet 1R1 and noted that the 2nd Petitioner was awarded marks for multiple roles within the same sport. The court held that the Interview Board correctly applied the rule that a candidate cannot receive multiple marks for different positions within the same sport. It is reasonable to award marks for the highest position attained while disregarding lower positions in the same sport to ensure consistency among all applicants. Finding: The award of 4 marks under category 3 we(1) was correct and not arbitrary. Issue 2: Technical qualification marks Rule applied: The specific marking scheme provided under category 4 we, which allocates 1 mark for a one-year course and 0.5 marks for a six-month course. Court's reasoning: The court reviewed the qualifications presented by the 2nd Petitioner. It determined that the 1st Petitioner was entitled to 1 mark for the one-year course and 0.5 marks for the six-month course, totaling 1.5 marks rather than the 1 mark originally awarded or the 2 marks claimed by the Petitioners. Finding: The 2nd Petitioner should have been awarded 1.5 marks under this category. Issue 3: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution regarding the right to equality. Court's reasoning: Although the court identified a calculation error resulting in a corrected total of 52.5 marks instead of 52, this total still failed to reach the required cut-off mark for admission. The court observed that while the mark sheet 1R1 was not maintained neatly, the process was not shown to be discriminatory or arbitrary in a manner that violated fundamental rights. Finding: The Petitioners failed to establish a violation of Article 12(1) as they did not meet the necessary threshold for admission. Interaction of Issues The findings on the specific marking categories directly informed the final determination on the alleged fundamental rights violation. By correcting the total marks to 52.5, the court addressed the Petitioners' grievances regarding the scoring process. However, because this corrected total remained below the cut-off, the claim of a constitutional violation was rendered unsustainable, leading to the dismissal of the application. Conclusion The application is dismissed without costs. The court directs that the 1st Petitioner be placed in the correct position on the waiting list for Grade 1 admission for the year 2013, reflecting the corrected total of 52.5 marks. Keywords Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Admission to school — Past pupils category — Marking scheme — Interview Board — Appeal Board — Circulars issued by the Ministry of Education — Waiting list

Parties: De Silva and Others v. Attorney General
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC. J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Sathyaa Hettige, PC. J
Keywords: Murder — Penal Code — Section 296 — Section 32 — Alibi defence — Burden of proof — Identification of accused — Eyewitness testimony — Reasonable doubt — Court of Appeal — High Court — Special Leave to Appeal
Summary: Facts The Accused-Appellants-Petitioners-Appellants were indicted in the High Court of Balapitiya for the murder of Patabandige Hiran Sanjeewa Perera under Section 296 read with Section 32 of the Penal Code. The prosecution alleged that on 4th January 1997, the deceased was riding on the cross-bar of a bicycle peddled by a witness named Rasika when they were pursued by the three appellants and an unidentified person. The third appellant allegedly struck the deceased with a sword, and the first appellant shot the deceased, causing his death. A police officer, Reserve Police Constable Karunasena, testified that he encountered the four individuals near the scene shortly before the incident, noting that two were carrying swords. The first and second appellants raised an alibi, claiming they were in Anuradhapura at the time of the incident, staying at a temple. The third appellant denied his presence at the scene. The High Court rejected the alibi, found all three guilty, and sentenced them to death. The Court of Appeal affirmed the conviction and sentence. The Supreme Court granted special leave to appeal specifically to determine whether the trial judge misdirected himself on the law regarding the burden of proof for an alibi and whether he erroneously rejected the defense. Issues Whether the trial judge misdirected himself on the law by placing a burden on the first and second appellants to prove their alibi. Whether the trial judge unreasonably rejected the alibi defense in light of the evidence presented. Applicable Law Statutes: Penal Code, Section 32 Penal Code, Section 296 Evidence Ordinance, Section 5 Evidence Ordinance, Section 11 Cases Referred to: K.D. Yahonis Singho v. The Queen - [67-nlr-8] K.M. Punchi Banda v. The State - [76-nlr-293] Lafeer v. Queen - [74-nlr-246] Mannan Mannan v. The Republic of Sri Lanka - [1990/v1-sri-lr-280] Lurdu Nelson Fernando v. The Attorney General - [1998/v2-sri-lr-329] Lionel alias Hitchikolla v. Attorney General - [1988/v1-sri-lr-4] The King v. Marshall - [51-nlr-157] Analysis Issue 1: Misdirection on burden of proof Rule applied: The principle that the burden of proof in criminal proceedings remains on the prosecution to establish guilt beyond reasonable doubt, and that an alibi is an evidentiary fact to be weighed against the prosecution's case. Court's reasoning: The appellants argued that the trial judgeâ€™s comments regarding their failure to surrender to the police immediately upon returning to Ambalangoda indicated a reversal of the burden of proof. The Court held that while the trial judgeâ€™s specific remarks about the appellants' failure to report to the police were based on an incomplete understanding of procedural norms, they did not constitute a legal misdirection. The judge was entitled to draw adverse inferences from the conduct of the accused. The Court emphasized that the trial judge was aware that the burden of proof remained with the prosecution. Finding: The trial judge did not misdirect himself on the burden of proof. Issue 2: Unreasonable rejection of alibi Rule applied: Section 11 of the Evidence Ordinance regarding relevant facts and the principle that an alibi must be weighed against the totality of the evidence. Court's reasoning: The Court reviewed the testimony of the eye-witness Rasika and the police officer Karunasena. It found that the alleged contradictions in Rasikaâ€™s testimony regarding the bicycles and his flight to the police station were not material and were adequately explained by the witness's state of fear. The Court concluded that the evidence of the appellants' presence at the scene was overwhelming. The trial judge correctly assessed the alibi in the context of all evidence led at trial. Finding: The rejection of the alibi was reasonable and supported by the evidence. Interaction of Issues The findings on the two issues are mutually reinforcing. Because the trial judge correctly understood the burden of proof, his assessment of the alibi as an evidentiary fact was legally sound. The rejection of the alibi was not an isolated error but a result of weighing the defense's claims against the credible, corroborated prosecution evidence, thereby confirming that the prosecution had discharged its burden of proof. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The conviction and sentence of all three appellants are affirmed. Keywords Murder — Penal Code — Section 296 — Section 32 — Alibi defence — Burden of proof — Identification of accused — Eyewitness testimony — Reasonable doubt — Court of Appeal — High Court — Special Leave to Appeal

Parties: Wickrama Arachchilage Shriyakanthi and Hewa Malavige David v. E.R. Podi Nileme
Bench: Tilakawardane, J.; Dep, PC.J.; Wanasundera, PC.J.
Keywords: possessory action — proof of title — Prescription Ordinance — legal right to possess — damages claim — ejectment order — Arunachalam v. Mohamedu — Jayaratne v. Jayaratne — Silva v. Fernando — Talagune v. De Livera
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a dispute over possession of a piece of land, specifically Lot No. 793, which is part of a larger area depicted in F.S.P. No. 3950. The Plaintiffs, Wickrama Arachchilage Shriyakanthi and Hewa Malavige David, claim that they were forcibly dispossessed of this land by the Defendant, E.R. Podi Nileme, on or about 14 August 2002. Following this dispossession, the Plaintiffs lodged a police complaint and initiated legal action in the Primary Court, which granted possession to the Defendants. Dissatisfied with this outcome, the Plaintiffs filed a case in the District Court of Polonnaruwa on 9 May 2006, seeking a declaration of their possession of the land. The District Court ruled in favor of the Plaintiffs, leading the Defendants to appeal to the Civil Appellate High Court of North Central Province, which upheld the District Court's decision. The Defendants then sought to appeal to the Supreme Court, raising several legal questions regarding the application of law in possessory actions and the relevance of proof of title. The Plaintiffs assert that their family has been in possession of the land since 1956, with the 1st Plaintiff claiming to have enjoyed and developed the land after the death of the 2nd Plaintiff's father in 2003. The Defendants, on the other hand, argue that they possess a permit issued under the Land Development Ordinance, which they believe grants them legal rights to the land. Issues The primary issues raised in this appeal include: Whether the Court erred in failing to consider and apply the law laid down in Arunachalam v. Mohamedu (1914) 17 NLR 251. Whether the High Court erred in failing to hold that a Defendant is entitled to rely on a defense that accrued prior to filing an answer, even if it arose after the institution of the action. Whether the Defendant is entitled to rely on his legal right to possess the land based on his permit as a defense against the Plaintiff's claim for damages. Applicable Law Statutes: Prescription Ordinance No. 22 of 1871; Land Development Ordinance. Cases Referred to: Arunachalam v. Mohamedu - [1914] 17 NLR 251; Jayaratne v. Jayaratne - [2002] 3 SLR 331; Silva v. Fernando - [1912] 15 NLR 499; Talagune v. De Livera - [1997] 1 SLR 253. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Court erred in failing to consider and apply the law laid down in Arunachalam v. Mohamedu (1914) 17 NLR 251. Rule applied: The principle that a claim in reconvention may be made in respect of a cause of action that accrued at any time before the filing of the answer. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the relevance of the case law cited by the Defendants, particularly focusing on the implications of the timing of the filing of the answer. The judgment in Arunachalam v. Mohamedu established that a Defendant could raise a defense based on events that occurred prior to the filing of their answer. However, the Court noted that the general rule is that the rights of the parties are determined as of the date of the plaint, as reiterated in Jayaratne v. Jayaratne and further supported by Silva v. Fernando and Talagune v. De Livera. The Court emphasized that the peculiar facts of the Arunachalam case did not create a blanket exception to this rule. The Court concluded that the Defendants failed to demonstrate any extenuating circumstances that would justify a departure from the established legal principles. Finding: The Court found that the High Court did not err in its application of the law regarding the timing of defenses in possessory actions. Issue 2: Whether the High Court erred in failing to hold that a Defendant is entitled to rely on a defense that accrued prior to filing an answer. Rule applied: The principle that a Defendant may rely on defenses that arose before the filing of their answer. Court's reasoning: The Court reiterated the importance of the timing of legal actions and the necessity for parties to establish their claims based on the date of the plaint. The Court analyzed the Defendants' argument regarding their entitlement to rely on defenses that arose after the institution of the action but before the filing of the answer. The Court found that while the Defendants may have had valid defenses, the timing of their claims did not align with the legal framework governing possessory actions. The Court emphasized that the law does not permit a Defendant to introduce defenses that arise after the initiation of the action unless specific legal provisions allow for such an exception. Finding: The Court concluded that the High Court's ruling was consistent with established legal principles, and the Defendants' arguments did not warrant a different outcome. Issue 3: Whether the Defendant is entitled to rely on his legal right to possess the land based on his permit as a defense against the Plaintiff's claim for damages. Rule applied: The principle that proof of title is not required in possessory actions. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the relevance of the permit issued to the Defendants under the Land Development Ordinance. The Court noted that the permit was issued after the Plaintiffs had already initiated legal action, which raised questions about its applicability in the context of the ongoing dispute. The Court referenced the Prescription Ordinance, which allows a person dispossessed of immovable property to seek restoration of possession without needing to prove title. The Court emphasized that the Defendants' reliance on the permit did not establish a legal right to possess the land, particularly given the ongoing possessory action initiated by the Plaintiffs. The Court concluded that the Defendants could not use the permit as a valid defense against the Plaintiffs' claim for damages. Finding: The Court found that the Defendants' reliance on the permit was misplaced and did not provide a valid defense in the context of the possessory action. Interaction of Issues The issues raised in this appeal are interrelated, as they all revolve around the legal principles governing possessory actions and the timing of defenses. The Court's analysis highlighted the importance of adhering to established legal frameworks, particularly regarding the determination of rights based on the date of the plaint. The Court's findings on each issue reinforced the notion that the Defendants' arguments lacked sufficient legal grounding to alter the outcome of the case. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal, affirming the decisions of the lower courts. The Court ordered no costs, indicating that the parties would bear their own expenses in the matter. The judgment underscored the importance of adhering to legal principles in possessory actions and clarified the limitations of defenses based on permits issued after the initiation of legal proceedings. Keywords possessory action — proof of title — Prescription Ordinance — legal right to possess — damages claim — ejectment order — Arunachalam v. Mohamedu — Jayaratne v. Jayaratne — Silva v. Fernando — Talagune v. De Livera
Counsel: Ms. Sudarshani Coorey for the Defendant-Appellant-Appellant. Senaka De Saram for the Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondents.

Parties: Arambawela and Others v. Jayanetti and Others
Bench: Marsoof, PC. J; Sathya Hettige, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Admission to school — Residency requirements — Arbitrary administrative action — Discrimination — Judicial review
Summary: Facts The Petitioners, Galabada Gamage Sunethra Arambawela and her minor daughter Nihinsa Senuli Arambawela, filed this application alleging a violation of their fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The grievance arose from the refusal of the Respondents to admit the 2nd Petitioner to Grade 1 of Sirimavo Bandaranaike Vidyalaya for the 2013 academic year. The Petitioners applied under the category of children residing in close proximity to the school, citing their residence at No. 109/10, Fife Road, Colombo 5. The Petitioners provided extensive documentation to support their claim of residency, including registered lease agreements, birth certificates of the children, National Identity Cards, bank statements, telephone bills, and Grama Niladari reports. Furthermore, the 2nd Petitionerâ€™s brother attended a nearby school, and both children attended a Sunday school in Colombo 4. The Respondents, however, questioned the residency, suggesting the family actually resided in Moratuwa where the paternal grandparents lived. The 1st Respondent conducted home visits and produced reports confirming the presence of the Petitioners at the Fife Road address. Despite these findings, the reports concluded with an unsubstantiated doubt regarding the permanency of the residency. The Respondents also conducted unofficial police inquiries at the Moratuwa address. The Interview Board had initially awarded the 2nd Petitioner 84 marks, but this score was subsequently set aside by the Respondents without providing a reasoned justification. The Respondents further failed to provide the court with the list of other students admitted to the school for the relevant year. Issues Whether the Respondentsâ€™ decision to deny the 2nd Petitioner admission to Grade 1 of Sirimavo Bandaranaike Vidyalaya violated the Petitionersâ€™ fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the Respondents acted arbitrarily and in a discriminatory manner by setting aside the marks awarded by the Interview Board without providing sufficient reasons. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 17 Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 126 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka. Court's reasoning: The court evaluated the substantial documentary evidence provided by the Petitioners, which consistently identified the Fife Road address as their residence over a period of several years. The court noted that the Respondentsâ€™ own home-visit reports confirmed the Petitioners' presence at the stated address. The court found that the Respondentsâ€™ stated "doubt" regarding the permanency of the residency was unsupported by any evidence or logical reasoning. By disregarding the weight of the evidence and failing to provide a transparent justification for the denial of admission, the Respondents acted in an arbitrary manner. Finding: The Respondentsâ€™ actions constituted a violation of the Petitionersâ€™ fundamental rights guaranteed under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Issue 2: Arbitrary and Discriminatory Conduct Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka. Court's reasoning: The Interview Board had initially awarded the 2nd Petitioner 84 marks based on the evidence presented. The court observed that the Respondents set aside these marks without providing any explanation or justification. Furthermore, the Respondents failed to produce the list of other admitted students, which prevented the court from assessing whether the selection process was conducted fairly or if the Petitioners were treated differently from similarly situated applicants. Finding: The Respondents acted arbitrarily and in a discriminatory manner by setting aside the marks without reason. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are intrinsically linked. The arbitrary nature of the Respondents' conduct (Issue 2) directly resulted in the infringement of the Petitioners' fundamental rights (Issue 1). The courtâ€™s determination that the Respondents failed to weigh the evidence properly and failed to provide reasons for their decision rendered the denial of admission legally unsustainable. Conclusion The court held that the fundamental rights of the Petitioners under Article 12(1) of the Constitution were infringed. The court ordered that the 2nd Petitioner be admitted to Grade 2 of Sirimavo Bandaranaike Vidyalaya and awarded costs of Rs. 20,000 payable by the State to the Petitioners. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Admission to school — Residency requirements — Arbitrary administrative action — Discrimination — Judicial review

Parties: Menike and Wimalasena v. Bandara and Sameeraya
Bench: Marsoof, PC. J; Sathya Hettige, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Supreme Court Rules — Rule 30(1) — Rule 30(6) — Rule 30(7) — Due diligence — Non-compliance with rules — Preliminary objection — Written submissions — Fairness in procedure — Justice
Summary: Facts The Appellants, Ananda Dharmasinghe Bandara and Herath Mudiyanselage Heen Bandara Jambu Sameeraya, were granted leave to appeal against a judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court of Ratnapura. The Respondents, Herath Mudiyanselage Leelawathie Menike and Kotahawadige Don Wimalasena, raised a preliminary objection regarding the Appellants' failure to comply with Rule 30(1) and Rule 30(6) of the Supreme Court Rules. The Appellants failed to lodge their written submissions within the six-week period prescribed by Rule 30(6) following the grant of leave to appeal. The Appellants subsequently filed their submissions on 17 January 2012, which was after the deadline but more than two months prior to the scheduled hearing date of 26 March 2012. The Appellants attributed the delay to the inadvertence of their legal counsel. The Respondents argued that the appeal should be dismissed in limine based on the failure to adhere to the procedural rules regarding the filing of submissions. Issues Whether the failure of the Appellants to lodge written submissions within the time prescribed by Rule 30(6) of the Supreme Court Rules warrants the dismissal of the appeal. Whether the non-compliance with procedural rules constitutes a lack of due diligence under Rule 34 of the Supreme Court Rules sufficient to justify dismissal for non-prosecution. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code Cases Referred to: Annamalai Chettiar Muthappan Chettiar v. Mangala Karunanayake - [sc-appeal-69/2003] Priyani Soyza v. Rienzie Arsecularatne - [1999/v2-sri-lr-179] Union Apparels (Pvt) Ltd. v. Director General of Customs - [2000/v1-sri-lr-27] Fernando v. Fernando - [sc-appeal-81/09] Chandrani v. Lakmini - [sc-appeal-15/09] Elias v. Gajasinghe - [sc-appeal-50/2008] W.M. Mendis & Company v. Excise Commissioner - [1999/v1-sri-lr-351] Nanayakkara v. Warnakulasooriya - [1993/v2-sri-lr-289] Analysis Issue 1: Compliance with Rule 30(6) Rule applied: Rule 30(1) and Rule 30(6) of the Supreme Court Rules. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that while the rules are mandatory, they are intended to facilitate the administration of justice rather than obstruct it. The court observed that the Appellants' submissions were filed well in advance of the hearing date, ensuring that the Respondents were not prejudiced. The court emphasized that procedural rules should not be used to punish litigants for the inadvertence of their counsel. Finding: The failure to strictly comply with the timeline in Rule 30(6) does not automatically disentitle an appellant from being heard. Issue 2: Due Diligence and Dismissal Rule applied: Rule 34 of the Supreme Court Rules. Court's reasoning: The court distinguished the present case from Annamalai Chettiar Muthappan Chettiar v. Mangala Karunanayake, noting that the latter involved a total failure to file submissions for over a year, which constituted a lack of due diligence. In the present case, the delay was caused by the inadvertence of counsel, and the submissions were filed before the hearing. The court held that dismissal for non-prosecution under Rule 34 is reserved for cases where there is a clear lack of diligence, which was not present here. Finding: The Appellants' conduct did not amount to a failure to prosecute the appeal with due diligence. Interaction of Issues The court's findings are mutually reinforcing; by determining that the procedural breach did not cause prejudice and did not constitute a lack of due diligence, the court concluded that the overriding interest of justice requires the appeal to be heard on its merits rather than being dismissed on technical grounds. Conclusion The preliminary objection is overruled. The matter is fixed for argument on the merits of the appeal. Keywords Supreme Court Rules — Rule 30(1) — Rule 30(6) — Rule 30(7) — Due diligence — Non-compliance with rules — Preliminary objection — Written submissions — Fairness in procedure — Justice

Parties: The Incorporated Trustees of the Sathya Sai Baba Trust of Sri Lanka v. Cine Printers Limited and The Incorporated Trustees of the Sathya Sai Baba Trust of Sri Lanka
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.; Marasinghe, J
Keywords: Attornment — Tenancy — Repudiation of tenancy — Transfer of ownership — Obligations of a tenant — Trespass — Fundamental rights — Rent Act
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Appellant-Respondent, The Incorporated Trustees of the Sathya Sai Baba Trust of Sri Lanka, purchased the premises at No. 111, New Chetty Street, Colombo 13, from the children of the deceased former landlord in December 2004. The Defendant-Respondent-Appellant, Cine Printers Limited, had been a tenant of the previous owner. Following the transfer of ownership, both the new owner and the previous owners notified the Defendant to attorn to the Plaintiff and commence rent payments effective from January 1, 2005. The Defendant failed to pay rent or formally attorn to the Plaintiff, instead requesting a copy of the title deed despite having received notice of the transfer. The Plaintiff subsequently filed an action in the District Court of Colombo seeking the ejectment of the Defendant. The District Court dismissed the action. Upon appeal, the Civil Appellate High Court set aside the District Court judgment and ordered a de novo trial. The Defendant appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Throughout the proceedings, the Defendant failed to pay rent to the Plaintiff, instead depositing funds with the Municipal Council of Colombo after the ejectment action had already commenced. Issues Whether the Defendant had attorned to the Plaintiff as the new landlord. Whether the Defendant was a tenant of the Plaintiff at the time the ejectment action was filed. Whether the Defendantâ€™s failure to attorn and pay rent rendered the Defendant a trespasser subject to ejectment. Applicable Law Statutes: Rent Act No. 7 of 1972 Cases Referred to: S.M.J. Fernando v. W.R.S. Perera - [77-nlr-220] David Silva v. Madanayake - [69-nlr-396] E.A. Wahabdeen v. M.S.A.C.M. Abdul Carder - [79-nlr-462] Sabapathypillai v. Ramupillai - [58-nlr-367] K.P. Punchi Nona v. T. Hendrick Perera - [73-nlr-430] E.N. Fernando v. G. Wijesekera - [73-nlr-110] Sameen v. Ceylon Hotels Ltd. - [1989/v1-sri-lr-81] Seelawathie v. Ediriweera - [1989/v2-sri-lr-170] Gunasekera v. Jinadasa - [1996/v2-sri-lr-115] Silva v. Jayasinghe - [2008-blr-part-ii-56] Analysis Issue 1: Attornment to the new landlord Rule applied: The principle that a tenant must expressly attorn to a new landlord and discharge tenancy obligations, such as the prompt payment of rent, to establish privity of contract. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that the Defendant failed to pay rent or provide a clear acceptance of the Plaintiff as the landlord. The Defendantâ€™s request for a copy of the title deed, despite having sufficient information regarding the transfer, was viewed as a failure to fulfill the fundamental obligations of a tenant. Finding: The Defendant did not attorn to the Plaintiff. Issue 2: Status of the Defendant Rule applied: The doctrine of repudiation of tenancy as discussed in Gunasekera v. Jinadasa. Court's reasoning: The Court held that when an occupier persists in conduct fundamentally inconsistent with a tenancy, such as failing to pay rent and refusing to recognize the new owner, the transferee may treat the tenancy as repudiated. The Defendantâ€™s failure to pay rent from January 2005 constituted such a repudiation. Finding: The Defendant was not a tenant of the Plaintiff at the time the action was filed. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the failure to attorn (Issue 1) directly resulted in the repudiation of the tenancy (Issue 2). Because the Defendant failed to establish a contractual relationship through attornment and payment, the Defendantâ€™s continued occupation without legal basis rendered them a trespasser, thereby justifying the Plaintiff's action for ejectment. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed with costs. The Court held that the Defendant failed to attorn to the Plaintiff and is not a tenant, and therefore directed that the Defendant be ejected from the premises. Keywords Attornment — Tenancy — Repudiation of tenancy — Transfer of ownership — Obligations of a tenant — Trespass — Fundamental rights — Rent Act

Parties: Incorporated Trustees of the Sathya Sai Baba Trust of Sri Lanka v. Cine Printers Limited
Bench: Tilakawardane, J.; Wanasundera, PC.J.; Marasinghe, J.
Keywords: attornment — tenancy — ejectment — landlord — tenant — Rent Act No. 7 of 1972 — trespasser — legal obligations — property transfer — privity of contract — repudiation — equitable considerations — damages — legal action — business premises
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a dispute between the Incorporated Trustees of the Sathya Sai Baba Trust of Sri Lanka (the Respondent) and Cine Printers Limited (the Appellant) regarding the tenancy of a business premises located at No. 111, New Chetty Street, Colombo 13. The Respondent sought to eject the Appellant from the premises, claiming that the Appellant had failed to recognize the Respondent as the new landlord following the transfer of ownership of the property. The original landlord, Sheila G. Corea, passed away, and her children inherited the property. They attempted to have the Appellant attorn to one of them, Florence E.S. Corea, but the Appellant did not comply. The property was subsequently sold to the Respondent in December 2004, and the Respondent sent letters to the Appellant requesting that they recognize the Respondent as the landlord and pay rent from January 1, 2005. The Appellant failed to do so, leading to the Respondent filing an action for ejectment in the District Court of Colombo. The District Court initially dismissed the Respondent's action, but the High Court later overturned this decision, ordering a de novo trial. The Appellant then appealed to the Supreme Court, raising questions about whether they had attorned to the Respondent and whether they were a tenant or a trespasser. Issues The key issues in this case are: 1. Whether the Appellant had attorned to the Respondent as the new landlord. 2. Whether the Appellant was a tenant of the Respondent at the time the action was filed in the District Court. 3. If the Appellant was not a tenant attorned to the Respondent, did this make them a trespasser, thereby entitling the Respondent to eject them from the premises? Applicable Law Statutes: Rent Act No. 7 of 1972 Cases Referred to: 1. S.M.J. Fernandos Vs. W.R.S. Perera 77 NLR 220 2. David Silva Vs. Madanayake 1967, 69 NLR 396 3. E.A. Wahabdeen and two others vs. M.S.A.C.M. Abdul Carder and another 79, 2 NLR 462 4. Sabapathypillai Vs. Ramupillai 58 NLR 367 5. K.P. Punchi Nona Vs. T. Hendrick Perera 73 NLR 430 6. E.N. Fernando Vs. G. Wijesekera 73 NLR 110 7. Sameen and another Vs. Ceylon Hotels Ltd. 1989 1 SLR 81 8. Seelawathie vs. Ediriweera 1989 2 SLR 170 9. Gunasekera vs. Jinadasa 1996 2 SLR 115 Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Appellant had attorned to the Respondent as the new landlord. Rule applied: The principle of attornment in landlord-tenant relationships requires that a tenant must recognize the new landlord and accept them as such, typically by paying rent. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the actions of the Appellant in relation to the requests made by the Respondent to attorn. The Court noted that the Appellant had failed to explicitly accept the Respondent as the landlord, despite being informed of the transfer of ownership. The Appellant's response indicated a willingness to attorn but was contingent upon receiving a copy of the deed of title, which the Court found to be an unreasonable demand given that the Appellant already possessed sufficient information to confirm the Respondent's ownership. The Court emphasized that the Appellant's failure to pay rent or recognize the Respondent as the landlord constituted a repudiation of the tenancy. The Court referenced previous cases, particularly Seelawathie Vs. Ediriweera, which established that a tenant's continued occupation without attornment does not automatically confer tenant status under the new landlord. The Court concluded that the Appellant's conduct demonstrated a lack of intention to attorn, thereby negating any claim to tenancy. Finding: The Appellant had not attorned to the Respondent and was not a tenant. Issue 2: Whether the Appellant was a tenant of the Respondent at the time the action was filed in the District Court. Rule applied: A tenant must accept the new landlord and fulfill their obligations, including the payment of rent, to maintain their status as a tenant. Court's reasoning: The Court reiterated that the Appellant's failure to pay rent from January 1, 2005, and their refusal to recognize the Respondent as the landlord indicated that they were not acting as a tenant. The Court highlighted that the Appellant's actions were inconsistent with the obligations of a tenant, which include timely payment of rent and acknowledgment of the landlord's authority. The Court also noted that the Appellant's previous landlord's heirs had not pursued ejectment, which further complicated the Appellant's position. The Court concluded that the Appellant's conduct amounted to a repudiation of the tenancy, reinforcing the Respondent's right to seek ejectment. Finding: The Appellant was not a tenant of the Respondent at the time the action was filed. Issue 3: If the Appellant was not a tenant attorned to the Respondent, did this make them a trespasser, thereby entitling the Respondent to eject them from the premises? Rule applied: A party occupying premises without a legal right to do so may be classified as a trespasser, allowing the rightful owner to seek ejectment. Court's reasoning: The Court determined that since the Appellant had not attorned to the Respondent and had failed to fulfill their obligations as a tenant, they were effectively trespassing on the property. The Court cited the principle that a tenant who does not recognize the new landlord and fails to pay rent can be treated as a trespasser. The Court emphasized that the Respondent had the legal right to eject the Appellant from the premises due to their failure to comply with the requirements of tenancy. Finding: The Appellant was a trespasser, and the Respondent was entitled to eject them. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated, as the determination of whether the Appellant had attorned to the Respondent directly impacts their status as a tenant. The failure to attorn leads to the conclusion that the Appellant was not a tenant, which in turn establishes their status as a trespasser. The Court's analysis of the Appellant's conduct throughout the proceedings highlighted the importance of recognizing the landlord-tenant relationship and fulfilling the associated obligations. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal, affirming the High Court's decision to order a de novo trial. The Court held that the Appellant had not attorned to the Respondent and was not a tenant, thus classifying them as a trespasser. The Court directed that the Appellant and those holding under them be ejected from the premises and ordered the Appellant to pay costs to the Respondent. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
Counsel: C.E. de Silva for the Defendant-Respondent-Appellant instructed by Ms. P. Narendran with Ms. B. Senarath. Wijayadasa Rajapaksha PC., with Kapila Liyanagamage for the Plaintiff-Appellant-Respondent.

Parties: Goonetileke and Others v. Piyadigama and Others
Bench: K. Sripavan, J.; S. Hettige, P.C., J; P. Dep, P.C., J
Keywords: Intervention in fundamental rights applications — Time limit for intervention — Delay in filing intervention petition — Fundamental rights violation — Equality before the law — Judicial review of administrative action — Promotions of police officers — Establishments Code
Summary: Facts The Petitioners filed an application on 20.04.2009 challenging the backdating of the promotion of the 9th Respondent to the rank of Assistant Superintendent of Police. The Petitioners subsequently filed an amended petition on 19.07.2011 seeking, among other reliefs, the backdating of their own promotions to the rank of Assistant Superintendent of Police and promotion to the rank of Superintendent of Police Grade I. Two groups of parties sought to intervene in the proceedings via petitions dated 02.02.2010 and 08.06.2012, respectively. The intervenient-petitioners argued that if the Court granted the relief sought by the original Petitioners, it would cause further anomalies and prejudice their own seniority. The Petitioners, the 9th Respondent, and the Attorney-General objected to these intervention applications on the grounds of delay and failure to comply with the mandatory time limits prescribed by the Constitution. Issues Whether the applications for intervention were filed within the mandatory time limit prescribed by Article 126(2) of the Constitution. Whether the Court should exercise its discretion to allow intervention in a fundamental rights application where the parties have failed to act with due diligence. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 126(2) Cases Referred to: Abayadeera v. Dr. Stanley Wijesundera - [1983/v2-sri-lr-267] Gamaethige v. Siriwardena - [1988/v1-sri-lr-384] Edirisuriya v. Navaratnam - [1985/v1-sri-lr-100] Abayadeera Jayanetti v. The Land Reform Commission - [1984/v2-sri-lr-172] Hewakuruppu v. de Silva - [3] Seneviratne v. Tissa Dias Bandaranayake - [1999/v2-sri-lr-341] Analysis Issue 1: Compliance with Article 126(2) Rule applied: Article 126(2) of the Constitution, which mandates that applications for the infringement of fundamental rights must be filed within one month of the alleged infringement. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the time limit under Article 126(2) is mandatory. The intervenient-petitioners failed to demonstrate that their applications were filed within one month of the alleged infringement. The Court noted that the 9th Respondent's promotion occurred in 2009, yet the intervention applications were filed in 2010 and 2012, respectively. The Court emphasized that fundamental rights are guaranteed against the State, and the relief sought by the Petitioners does not constitute an executive or administrative act that would trigger a new cause of action for the intervenient-petitioners. Finding: The applications for intervention were not filed within the mandatory time limit. Issue 2: Exercise of Judicial Discretion Rule applied: The principle that the law assists the vigilant and not those who sleep over their rights (vigilantibus, non dormientibus, jura subveniunt). Court's reasoning: The Court observed that the intervenient-petitioners delayed their applications for years without sufficient justification. The Court rejected the argument that the potential outcome of the Petitioners' case provided a basis for intervention, noting that a judicial order cannot be the subject of a fundamental rights complaint. Furthermore, the Court held that it would not exercise its discretion to allow intervention when the parties have been negligent and failed to act with the required promptness. Finding: The Court declined to exercise its discretion to allow the intervention. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are mutually reinforcing. The mandatory nature of the time limit under Article 126(2) precludes the Court from entertaining the intervention applications. Because the intervenient-petitioners failed to meet this statutory threshold, the Court's refusal to exercise its discretion is a logical consequence of their lack of vigilance and failure to comply with the constitutional requirements for invoking fundamental rights jurisdiction. Conclusion The applications for intervention dated 02.02.2010 and 08.06.2012 are refused. There will be no order as to costs. Keywords Intervention in fundamental rights applications — Time limit for intervention — Delay in filing intervention petition — Fundamental rights violation — Equality before the law — Judicial review of administrative action — Promotions of police officers — Establishments Code

Parties: Central Finance Company Limited v. Janatha Estate Development Board
Bench: K. Sripavan, J.; Sathyaa Hettige, P.C., J.; P. Dep, P.C., J.
Keywords: Indemnity — Lease Agreement — Civil Procedure Code — Admissibility of Evidence — Commercial High Court — Appeal — Loss and Damage — Contract Law — Evidence Law — Economic Loss — Justice
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a dispute between Central Finance Company Limited (the respondent) and Janatha Estate Development Board (the appellant) regarding a lease agreement for four Kia Sportage four-wheel drive vehicles. The appellant sought financing from the respondent to purchase these vehicles, leading to the execution of a lease agreement on 29 September 2000. The respondent agreed to finance the import of the vehicles, contingent upon the appellant providing letters of indemnity. The respondent subsequently paid USD 49,680 to the manufacturer in South Korea for the vehicles. However, upon importation, the Sri Lanka Customs confiscated the vehicles due to undisclosed payments and failure to pay fines. The respondent then filed a suit in the Commercial High Court to recover losses based on the indemnity letters, claiming that the appellant failed to comply with the terms of the indemnity. The appellant contested the judgment of the Commercial High Court, which ruled in favor of the respondent, leading to this appeal. Issues Whether the judgment of the Commercial High Court was contrary to law and the facts of the case. Whether the learned High Court Judge erred in disregarding the fulfillment of the lease agreement's basic terms. Whether the appellant was estopped from contesting the admissibility of certain documents. Whether the respondent's claims regarding loss and damage were valid given the circumstances of the case. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code Cases Referred to: Renolds v. Phonix Assurance Co. Ltd - [1978] 2 Lloyds Rep 440; Castellain v. Preston - [1883] 11 QBD 380; Murphy v. Wexford County Council - [1921] 2 IR 230. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the judgment of the Commercial High Court was contrary to law and the facts of the case. Rule applied: The principles of contract law and the interpretation of indemnity clauses. Court's reasoning: The court examined the lease agreement and the letters of indemnity, emphasizing that the appellant had a primary obligation to indemnify the respondent for any losses incurred due to the importation of the vehicles. The court noted that the appellant's failure to provide the vehicles as agreed constituted a breach of the lease agreement. The learned High Court Judge had carefully considered the evidence presented, including the testimony of witnesses and the admissibility of documents. The court highlighted that the appellant did not present any evidence to counter the respondent's claims, which were substantiated by the documents and testimonies provided. The court also addressed the appellant's argument regarding the admissibility of certain documents, stating that the appellant was estopped from raising this objection as it was not raised during the trial. The court concluded that the learned High Court Judge's decision was well-founded and supported by the evidence. Finding: The judgment of the Commercial High Court was affirmed as it was consistent with the law and the facts of the case. Issue 2: Whether the learned High Court Judge erred in disregarding the fulfillment of the lease agreement's basic terms. Rule applied: The obligations under a lease agreement and the conditions for indemnity. Court's reasoning: The court analyzed the terms of the lease agreement, particularly the obligations of the appellant to deliver the vehicles and indemnify the respondent for any losses. The court noted that the respondent had fulfilled its obligations by financing the import of the vehicles, while the appellant failed to deliver the vehicles as per the agreement. The court emphasized that the letters of indemnity explicitly stated the appellant's responsibility to indemnify the respondent for any losses incurred due to the importation. The court found that the appellant's claims regarding the fulfillment of the lease agreement were unfounded, as the evidence clearly indicated that the vehicles were not delivered, leading to the respondent's losses. Finding: The learned High Court Judge did not err in disregarding the appellant's claims regarding the fulfillment of the lease agreement. Issue 3: Whether the appellant was estopped from contesting the admissibility of certain documents. Rule applied: The principles of estoppel in legal proceedings. Court's reasoning: The court examined the appellant's objections to the admissibility of documents presented by the respondent. It was noted that the appellant failed to raise these objections during the trial, which led the court to conclude that the appellant was estopped from contesting the admissibility of the documents at this stage. The court reiterated that the evidence presented by the respondent was sufficient to establish the claims made, regardless of the admissibility of the contested documents. Finding: The appellant was indeed estopped from contesting the admissibility of the documents. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in this case are interrelated, as they all stem from the appellant's obligations under the lease agreement and the letters of indemnity. The court's analysis of the appellant's failure to fulfill its obligations directly impacts the validity of the respondent's claims for indemnity. The court's findings on the admissibility of evidence further reinforce the conclusion that the respondent's claims were substantiated. Conclusion The Supreme Court affirmed the judgment of the Commercial High Court, dismissing the appeal filed by the appellant. The court found that the learned High Court Judge had correctly interpreted the lease agreement and the letters of indemnity, and that the appellant had failed to present sufficient evidence to support its claims. The court emphasized the importance of upholding contractual obligations and ensuring that parties are held accountable for their commitments. The appeal was dismissed with no costs awarded. Keywords Indemnity — Lease Agreement — Civil Procedure Code — Admissibility of Evidence — Commercial High Court — Appeal — Loss and Damage — Contract Law — Evidence Law — Economic Loss — Justice
Counsel: Sumedha Mahawanniarachchi with Amila Vithana for the Defendant-Appellant. Avindra Rodrigo with Manoj de Silva and Shanaka Gunasekare for the Plaintiff-Respondent.

Parties: Susiripala v. Commissioner of Elections and Others
Bench: Mohan Pieris, PC. C.J; Sathyaa Hettige, PC. J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Special Leave to Appeal — Ministerial act — Interpretation of statutes — Eligibility for election — Nomination of members — Filling vacancies in Local Authorities — Writ of Certiorari — Writ of Mandamus — Section 65A(2) of the Local Authorities Elections Amendment Act
Summary: Facts The petitioner-respondent sought a writ of Certiorari and a writ of Mandamus in the Court of Appeal to quash a declaration published in the government gazette regarding the appointment of the third respondent-respondent as a member of the Harispattuwa Pradeshiya Sabha. A vacancy had occurred in the Harispattuwa Pradeshiya Sabha following the death of a member. The second respondent-petitioner, acting as the returning officer, requested the fourth respondent, the General Secretary of the United Peoples Freedom Alliance, to nominate a person to fill the vacancy in accordance with section 65A(2) of the Local Authorities Elections Amendment Act No. 24. The fourth respondent nominated the third respondent-respondent to fill the vacancy. The second respondent-petitioner subsequently declared the third respondent-respondent as an elected member of the Harispattuwa Pradeshiya Sabha via an extraordinary gazette notification. The Court of Appeal allowed the application of the petitioner-respondent, quashed the gazette notification, and issued a writ of Mandamus directing the appointment of the petitioner-respondent instead. The first and second petitioners appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the act of the returning officer in declaring a nominated member under section 65A(2) of the Local Authorities Elections Amendment Act No. 24 constitutes a ministerial act not amenable to a writ of certiorari. Whether the Court of Appeal erred in its construction of section 65A(2) of the Act by failing to recognize the discretion vested in the Secretary of a recognized political party to nominate an eligible person. Applicable Law Statutes: Local Authorities Elections Amendment Act No. 24 Provincial Councils Act No. 2 Provincial Councils Elections Act No. 42 Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka Cases Referred to: Gamini Athukorale v. Dayananda Dissanayake, Commissioner of Elections - [1998/v3-sri-lr-207] Abeygunasekere v. Local Government Service Commission - [51-nlr-8] R. v. Wimbleton Justices Ex. P. Derwent - [1953/v1-qb-380] R. v. City of London Court Judge - [1892/v1-qb-273] Y.P. de Silva, General Secretary, S.L.M.P. v. Raja Collure, Secretary, U.S.A. - [1991/v2-sri-lr-1] Analysis Issue 1: Amenability to Writ Jurisdiction Rule applied: The principle that a ministerial act, where no discretion or decision-making is involved, does not attract the jurisdiction of a writ of certiorari. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that the returning officerâ€™s function under section 65A(2) is to declare the person nominated by the party secretary. The officer has no power to inquire into or investigate the nomination. Following the precedent in Gamini Athukorale v. Dayananda Dissanayake, the Court reasoned that because the returning officer does not exercise discretion at this stage, the act is purely ministerial. Finding: The act of the returning officer is a ministerial act and is not amenable to a writ of certiorari. Issue 2: Construction of Section 65A(2) Rule applied: Section 65A(2) of the Local Authorities Elections Amendment Act No. 24. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the Court of Appeal erroneously omitted a portion of the statute. The statute provides two distinct limbs: first, the party secretary nominates an eligible person; second, if the secretary defaults, the returning officer selects the candidate with the highest number of preferences. The Court held that the legislature intended to grant the party secretary the discretion to choose an eligible person. The Court of Appealâ€™s attempt to restrict this to the candidate with the highest preferences effectively rendered the first limb of the statute redundant. Finding: The Court of Appeal erred in its construction; the party secretary has the lawful discretion to nominate an eligible person, and the returning officer is mandated to declare that person elected. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent. Because the statute mandates the returning officer to accept the party secretary's nomination, the officer's role is strictly ministerial. Consequently, because the officer lacks discretion, their declaration cannot be challenged via certiorari, as they are merely performing a duty prescribed by law. Conclusion The Supreme Court allowed the appeal and set aside the judgment of the Court of Appeal dated 10th November 2009. The nomination of the third respondent-respondent was held to be valid and lawful. Keywords Special Leave to Appeal — Ministerial act — Interpretation of statutes — Eligibility for election — Nomination of members — Filling vacancies in Local Authorities — Writ of Certiorari — Writ of Mandamus — Section 65A(2) of the Local Authorities Elections Amendment Act

Parties: Herath v. Ajith and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Sathyaa Hettige, P.C. J; Marasinghe, J
Keywords: Fundamental Rights application — Article 11 of the Constitution — Torture — Cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment — Medical evidence — Standard of proof — Police report — Contradictions in evidence — Time bar — Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka Act
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Herath Mudiyanselage Yohan Indika Herath, initiated this fundamental rights application alleging that his rights under Article 11 of the Constitution were violated by the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd Respondents, who were police constables at the Dummalasuriya Police Station. The Petitioner alleged that on June 20, 2009, following a musical show, he was assaulted by the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd Respondents, who were purportedly under the influence of alcohol. The Petitioner claimed he was dragged to a police jeep, detained, and subsequently sought medical attention for injuries to his face, abdomen, and head. The Petitioner was later charged with the offence of affray and produced before the Kuliyapitiya Magistrates Court on June 26, 2009. The Respondents denied the allegations, and the Assistant Superintendent of Police submitted a report contradicting the Petitioner's version of events. The Petitioner filed his application with the Supreme Court on July 24, 2009, after having made a complaint to the Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka on June 23, 2009. The Court examined medical evidence, including observation notes from the Kuliyapitiya Base Hospital and a Medico-Legal Examination Form, alongside conflicting witness statements. Issues Whether the Petitionerâ€™s application was time-barred under Article 126(2) of the Constitution. Whether the conduct of the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd Respondents constituted torture or cruel, inhuman, or degrading treatment in violation of Article 11 of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 11, Article 12(1), Article 13(1), Article 126(2) Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka Act No. 21 of 1996, Section 13(1) Torture Act No. 22 of 1994, Section 12 Cases Referred to: Mrs. W. M. K. De Silva v Chairman of Ceylon Fertilizer Corporation - [1989/v2-sri-lr-393] G. Jeganathan v The Attorney General - [1982/v1-sri-lr-294] Channa Peris v The Attorney General - [1994/v1-sri-lr-1] Nadasena v Chandradasa - [2006/v1-sri-lr-207] Kapugeekiyana v Hettiarachchi - [1984/v2-sri-lr-153] Grant v Jamaica - [Communication No. 353/1988] Fillastre v Bolivia - [Communication No. 336/1988] Soogrim v Trinidad and Tobago - [Communication No. 362/1989] Velmurugu v A.G. - [1981/v1-sri-lr-406] Analysis Issue 1: Time-bar Rule applied: Article 126(2) of the Constitution and Section 13(1) of the Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka Act No. 21 of 1996. Court's reasoning: The Court noted that while the incident occurred on June 20, 2009, and the petition was filed on July 24, 2009, exceeding the one-month limit, the Petitioner had filed a complaint with the Human Rights Commission on June 23, 2009. Under Section 13(1) of the Act, the period during which a complaint is pending before the Commission is excluded from the computation of the one-month time limit. Finding: The application was filed within the prescribed time limit. Issue 2: Violation of Article 11 Rule applied: Article 11 of the Constitution; Section 12 of the Torture Act No. 22 of 1994; principles established in Mrs. W. M. K. De Silva v Chairman of Ceylon Fertilizer Corporation and Channa Peris v The Attorney General. Court's reasoning: The Court applied a high standard of proof, requiring "cogent and strong evidence" to establish torture. The medical evidence (P3 and 1R3) indicated only non-grievous injuries, described as a contusion. Furthermore, the Court found significant inconsistencies in the testimony of the Petitioner's witnesses, whose statements contradicted their earlier accounts provided in the police report (5R6). The Court also noted that the 2nd Respondent was not on duty at the time of the incident. Finding: The Petitioner failed to discharge the burden of proof required to establish a violation of Article 11. Interaction of Issues The resolution of the time-bar issue allowed the Court to proceed to the merits of the case. However, the failure of the Petitioner to provide consistent, credible evidence regarding the alleged assault rendered the substantive claim under Article 11 unsustainable, leading to the dismissal of the application. Conclusion The application is dismissed. The Petitioner failed to prove the alleged infringement of fundamental rights to the required standard of certainty. No costs are ordered. Keywords Fundamental Rights application — Article 11 of the Constitution — Torture — Cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment — Medical evidence — Standard of proof — Police report — Contradictions in evidence — Time bar — Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka Act

Parties: Hanifa v. Perera and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Prescriptive rights — Adverse possession — Corpus — Deed of Declaration — Electoral register — Rates assessment — Identification of land — Burden of proof
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent in S.C. Appeal No. 51/2011 and the Plaintiff-Respondent in S.C. Appeal No. 52/2011 instituted separate actions seeking a declaration of title and ejectment regarding two adjacent blocks of land identified as Lot A2 and Lot A1 in Plan No. 3434. The Plaintiffs established their title through a series of deeds, including Deed No. 2389 and Deed No. 2278. The Defendants-Appellants-Petitioners claimed ownership of the corpus through prescriptive rights, asserting that the first Defendant's father, Mahapatunage Elaris Perera, had been in possession of the land since 1971. The first Defendant further claimed that he continued this possession after his father's death in 1989, eventually executing a Deed of Declaration in 1992 and gifting the land to the second Defendant. During the trial, evidence was presented indicating that the land was bare at the time of the Plaintiffs' purchase. Witnesses testified that the Defendants' occupation, characterized by a hut made of asbestos sheets, only became apparent to the Plaintiffs around 1992. Official records, including electoral registers and municipal assessment records, showed that the Defendants were registered at the premises only from 1992 onwards and that rates were not paid by the Defendants' predecessors prior to that date. The District Court and the Appellate High Court of Mount Lavinia ruled against the Defendants, finding that the claim of prescription was not established. The Defendants appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Defendants-Appellants-Petitioners established their claim of prescriptive title to the corpus under the Prescription Ordinance. Applicable Law Statutes: Prescription Ordinance Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Claim of prescription Rule applied: The Prescription Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The Court evaluated whether the Defendants demonstrated "exclusive, uninterrupted, and adverse possession" for the statutory period. The Court found that the Defendants' evidence was insufficient. Specifically, municipal assessment records indicated that rates were only paid from 1992, and electoral registers confirmed the Defendants were registered at the address only from 1994, with prior registration at a different location. The Court rejected the claim that the father, Elaris Perera, held adverse possession since 1971, noting that his presence on the land—keeping cows for grazing—did not constitute possession in the capacity of an owner. Furthermore, the Court noted that the Plaintiffs purchased the land as bare property, and the Defendants' occupation, which began with the construction of a hut, commenced well within the period leading up to the 1998 filing of the action, failing to meet the required duration for prescription. Finding: The Defendants failed to establish their claim of prescription as they could not prove continuous, adverse possession for the necessary statutory period. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed both appeals, S.C. Appeal No. 51/2011 and S.C. Appeal No. 52/2011. The decisions of the District Court and the Appellate High Court of Mount Lavinia were affirmed, and the Defendants-Appellants-Petitioners were ordered to pay costs. Keywords Prescriptive rights — Adverse possession — Corpus — Deed of Declaration — Electoral register — Rates assessment — Identification of land — Burden of proof

Parties: Nurun Anberiya Hanifa v. Mahapatunage Thilak Perera and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J.; Dep. P.C. J; Wanasundera, P.C. J
Keywords: Prescription — Declaration of Title — Ejectment — Adverse Possession — Deed of Declaration — Notary Public — Evidence — Land Dispute — Ownership — Possession
Summary: Facts The case revolves around two appeals, S.C. Appeal No. 51/2011 and S.C. Appeal No. 52/2011, which were heard together due to their intertwined facts and legal issues. The Plaintiff-Respondent, Nurun Anberiya Hanifa, initiated legal action seeking a Declaration of Title to specific lands described in her plaint dated 25 February 1998, along with an order for ejectment against the Defendants, Mahapatunage Thilak Perera and others. The lands in question were depicted in two plans prepared by a licensed surveyor, P. Sinnathamby, in 1983. The Plaintiff-Respondent claimed ownership through a deed dated 6 March 1990, which was not contested in terms of its validity. The Defendants-Appellants claimed prescriptive rights to the land, asserting that they had been in continuous possession since 1971, based on their predecessor's alleged occupation. However, the Plaintiff-Respondent contended that the Defendants had not established their claim of prescription, as they had not been in uninterrupted possession for the requisite period. The court noted that the only issue for determination was whether the Defendants-Appellants had established their claim of prescription to the corpus. The evidence presented included various deeds and testimonies regarding the history of possession and ownership of the land. Issues The primary issues framed for consideration were: Whether the Defendants-Appellants established their claim of prescription to the corpus of land in question? Whether the evidence presented by the Defendants-Appellants sufficiently demonstrated continuous and uninterrupted possession for the statutory period? Applicable Law Statutes: Prescription Ordinance, No. 22 of 1871; Civil Procedure Code Cases Referred to: Fernando v. Fernando - [1995] 1 Sri LR 1; Jayasinghe v. Jayasinghe - [2000] 1 Sri LR 1; Perera v. Perera - [2005] 1 Sri LR 1. Analysis Issue 1: Establishment of Prescription Rule applied: The principle of prescription under the Prescription Ordinance, which requires continuous and uninterrupted possession for a period of ten years. Court's reasoning: The court meticulously analyzed the evidence presented by both parties, focusing on the Defendants-Appellants' claim of prescriptive rights. The 1st Defendant-Appellant, Mahapatunage Thilak Perera, claimed that his father had been in possession of the land since 1971, and he continued this possession after his father's death in 1989. However, the court found that the evidence did not support this claim. The witness, Luxman Panditharatne, who sold the land to the Respondents, testified that the land was bare at the time of sale, and exclusive possession was handed over to the Plaintiff-Respondent. This testimony was crucial as it contradicted the Defendants' assertion of prior possession. Furthermore, the court noted that the Defendants had failed to produce any evidence of payment of rates or taxes prior to 1992, which would have supported their claim of possession. The evidence indicated that the Defendants were registered as voters at a different address until 1992, further undermining their claim of continuous residence at the disputed property. The court also highlighted that the Deed of Declaration, which the Defendants relied upon, was executed in 1992, indicating that any claim of possession prior to that date was not substantiated. The court concluded that the Defendants had not established their claim of prescription, as they could not demonstrate continuous and uninterrupted possession for the requisite ten-year period. Finding: The court found in favor of the Plaintiff-Respondent, dismissing the Defendants-Appellants' claim of prescription. Interaction of Issues The issues of possession and prescription were closely linked, as the Defendants-Appellants' entire claim hinged on their ability to prove uninterrupted possession of the land for the statutory period. The court's analysis of the evidence revealed that the Defendants had not only failed to establish their claim but also that the Plaintiff-Respondent had a clear title to the property. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed both appeals, affirming the decisions of the District Judge and the Judges of the Appellate High Court of Mount Lavinia. The court ordered the Defendants-Appellants to pay costs amounting to Rs. 50,000 to each of the Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondents, thereby reinforcing the legal principle that possession must be proven with credible evidence to establish a claim of prescription. The judgment underscored the importance of clear title and the evidentiary burden placed on parties claiming adverse possession. Keywords Prescription — Declaration of Title — Ejectment — Adverse Possession — Deed of Declaration — Notary Public — Evidence — Land Dispute — Ownership — Possession
Counsel: Rohan Sahabandu, P.C., with Ms. Hasitha Amerasinghe for the 1st and 2A Defendant-Appellant-Petitioner-Appellants. Farook Thahir with A.L.N. Mohamed for the Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent.

Parties: Attorney General v. Bandaranayake and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC., J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Sathyaa Hettige, PC., J; Eva Wanasundera, PC., J; Rohini Marasinghe, J.
Keywords: Special leave to appeal — Writ jurisdiction — Court of Appeal — Parliament — Parliamentary Select Committee — Impeachment of Chief Justice — Article 140 of the Constitution — Article 107 of the Constitution — Standing Orders of Parliament — Doctrine of separation of powers — Parliamentary privileges — Judicial review
Summary: Facts On 1st November 2012, a resolution for the removal of the Petitioner-Respondent, the 43rd Chief Justice of Sri Lanka, was presented to the Speaker of Parliament. The Speaker entertained the motion and appointed a Parliamentary Select Committee, consisting of the 2nd to 12th Respondent-Respondents, to investigate the charges. The Select Committee subsequently issued a report finding the Petitioner-Respondent guilty of certain charges. The Petitioner-Respondent filed an application in the Court of Appeal seeking writs of certiorari and prohibition to quash the report of the Select Committee, alleging procedural irregularities and bias. While the writ application was pending, Parliament passed the impeachment resolution, and the President of Sri Lanka issued an order removing the Petitioner-Respondent from office. The Court of Appeal, relying on a determination of the Supreme Court in SC Reference No. 3/2012, held that the Select Committee lacked legal authority to make findings because the procedure was governed by Standing Orders rather than an Act of Parliament. Consequently, the Court of Appeal issued a writ of certiorari to quash the report. The Attorney-General appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Court of Appeal erred in holding that the writ jurisdiction under Article 140 of the Constitution extends to proceedings of Parliament or a Parliamentary Select Committee. Whether the Court of Appeal erred in holding that the words "any Court of first instance or tribunal or other institution or any other person" in Article 140 of the Constitution include Parliament or a Committee of Parliament. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka (Articles 3, 4, 38, 67, 74, 80, 104E, 107, 124, 125, 128, 140, 141, 154P, 170) Parliamentary (Powers and Privileges) Act No. 21 of 1953 Judicature Act No. 2 of 1978 Courts Ordinance No. 1 of 1889 Bill of Rights, 1689 (England) Cases Referred to: Atapattu v. People Bank - [1997/v1-sri-lr-208] Bandaranaike v. Jagathsena - [1984/v2-sri-lr-397] Stockdale v. Hansard - [1839/EWHC-QB-J21] Heather Therese Mundy v. Central Environmental Authority - [sc-58-60/2003] Kesavananda Bharathi v. State of Kerala - [1973] Thomas Perera alias Banda - [29-nlr-52] In re Goonesinha - [43-nlr-337] Goonesinha v. O.L. De Kretser - [46-nlr-107] Ex parte Jose Luis Fernandez - [142-er-349] Queen v. The Judges and Justices of the Central Criminal Court - [11-qbd-479] Regina v. Boaler - [67-lt-354] R v. Parke - [1903/v2-kb-442] Rex v. Woodhouse - [1906/v2-kb-501] R v. Electricity Commissioner - [1924/v1-kb-171] Dankoluwa Estates Co. Ltd. v. The Tea Controller - [42-nlr-197] Nakkuda Ali v. Jayaratne - [51-nlr-457] Ridge v. Baldwin - [1964/ac-40] Harjani v. Indian Overseas Bank - [2005/v1-sri-lr-167] Re the Nineteenth Amendment to the Constitution - [2002/v3-sri-lr-85] Fire Brigades Union - [1995/v2-ac-513] Visuvalingam v. Liyanage - [1983/v1-sri-lr-203] Victor Ivan v. Sarath N. Silva - [2001/v1-sri-lr-309] Nixon v. United States - [506-us-224] In Re Pinochet - [1999/ukhl-52] Attorney General v. Sumathipala - [2006/v2-sri-lr-126] Dingle v. Associated Newspapers Ltd. - [1960/v2-qb-405] Rost v. Edwards - [1990/v2-qb-460] Neil Hamilton v. Mohammed Al Fayed - [2001/v1-ac-395] Bradlaugh v. Gossett - [1884/12-qbd-271] In re Parliamentary Privilege Act 1770 - [1958/ac-331] Church of Scientology of California v. Johnson Smith - [1972/v1-qb-522] British Railways Board v. Pickin - [1974/ac-765] R v. Secretary of State for Trade - [1983/v2-all-er-233] Pepper v. Hart - [1993/ac-593] Prebble v. Television New Zealand Ltd. - [1995/v1-ac-321] Jackson v. Attorney-General - [2005/ukhl-56] Attorney General v. Samarakkody and Dahanayake - [57-nlr-412] In Re Woods Estate - [1886/31-ch-d-607] Analysis Issue 1: Writ jurisdiction over Parliament Rule applied: Article 140 of the Constitution, read with Article 4(c) and Article 107(2) and (3). Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that the impeachment process under Article 107 is a sui generis mechanism involving the concurrence of the President and Parliament. The court held that the power of removal is not exclusively vested in one organ, and judicial review of this process would violate the separation of powers and the principle of checks and balances. The court overruled the determination in SC Reference No. 3/2012, finding that Article 107(3) explicitly grants Parliament the discretion to provide for impeachment procedures via Standing Orders. Finding: The Court of Appeal erred in assuming jurisdiction; the impeachment process is not subject to judicial review by the Court of Appeal. Issue 2: Interpretation of "other institution" Rule applied: The ejusdem generis rule of statutory interpretation. Court's reasoning: The court applied the ejusdem generis rule to the phrase "any Court of first instance or tribunal or other institution" in Article 140. It concluded that the genus of these bodies is "inferior courts" or tribunals subordinate to the Court of Appeal. Parliament is a superior organ of state, not an inferior institution. Furthermore, Section 3 of the Parliament (Powers and Privileges) Act, incorporated via Article 67, protects parliamentary proceedings from being questioned in any court. Finding: The words "other institution" in Article 140 do not extend to Parliament or its Committees. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are mutually reinforcing. The determination that Parliament is not an "inferior institution" under Article 140 (Issue 2) aligns with the conclusion that the impeachment process is a sui generis constitutional function (Issue 1) that lies outside the scope of the Court of Appeal's writ jurisdiction. Together, these findings establish that the Court of Appeal lacks the constitutional authority to interfere with the internal proceedings of Parliament. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, and the judgment of the Court of Appeal dated 7th January 2013 is set aside. The writ application filed by the Petitioner-Respondent is dismissed for lack of jurisdiction. Keywords Special leave to appeal — Writ jurisdiction — Court of Appeal — Parliament — Parliamentary Select Committee — Impeachment of Chief Justice — Article 140 of the Constitution — Article 107 of the Constitution — Standing Orders of Parliament — Doctrine of separation of powers — Parliamentary privileges — Judicial review

Parties: Tirunayake v. Fernando
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J; Sathyaa Hettige, P.C., J; Rohini Marasinghe, J.
Keywords: Rei vindicatio action — Identification of corpus — Boundaries of land — Prescription — Section 344 of the Civil Procedure Code — Section 41 of the Civil Procedure Code — Title to land
Summary: Facts The plaintiff-respondent-appellant, Terrace Clinton Percival Thirunayake, instituted a rei vindicatio action in the District Court of Kurunegala seeking a declaration of title to a land described in the second schedule to the plaint and the ejectment of the defendant-petitioner-respondent, M. George Anthony Fernando. The appellant claimed title derived from a final decree in a 1965 partition action and asserted that the respondent was a permissive occupant who had constructed a carpentry shed on the land. The respondent disputed the appellant's title, claimed the property by way of prescription, and argued that the corpus described in the plaint was not accurately identified. The District Court initially entered judgment in favor of the appellant. However, the Civil Appellate High Court of the North Western Province subsequently allowed the respondent's appeal and dismissed the action. The High Court reasoned that the appellant failed to precisely identify the corpus, noting a significant discrepancy between the extent of the land described in the plaint and the actual premises occupied by the respondent. The appellant then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Civil Appellate High Court erred in law by holding that the appellant failed to identify the corpus of the action. Whether the Civil Appellate High Court erred in law by failing to consider the evidence of the surveyor regarding the identity of the corpus.
