Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 41 Civil Procedure Code, Section 344 Cases Referred to: Silva v. Sellohamy - [25-nlr-113] Peiris v. Saunhamy - [54-nlr-207] Abeykoon Hamine v. Appuhamy - [52-nlr-49] Analysis Issue 1: Identification of the corpus Rule applied: Section 41 of the Civil Procedure Code and the principle that in a rei vindicatio action, the plaintiff bears the burden of proving title and identifying the land with precision and definite boundaries. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the appellant failed to describe the land with the required precision. The schedule to the plaint described a larger land of one rood and 30 perches, whereas the respondent occupied a specific premises of only 12.05 perches. The court emphasized that the appellant failed to establish the correct boundaries, which is a paramount duty in a rei vindicatio action. Finding: The High Court did not err in law; the appellant failed to identify the corpus as required by law. Issue 2: Consideration of surveyor evidence Rule applied: The evidentiary requirement for identifying the corpus in a rei vindicatio action. Court's reasoning: The appellant failed to call for a commission to a surveyor to identify the corpus in the present action. Instead, the appellant attempted to rely on a plan from a different case record that lacked the necessary judicial authentication. The court found that this evidence was insufficient to establish the identity of the land in dispute. Finding: The High Court correctly concluded that the appellant failed to provide sufficient evidence to identify the land. Interaction of Issues The failure to identify the corpus (Issue 1) is directly linked to the failure to produce valid, authenticated surveyor evidence (Issue 2). Because the appellant did not properly utilize a surveyor to define the boundaries as required by Section 41 of the Civil Procedure Code, the court could not reconcile the discrepancy between the land described in the plaint and the land actually occupied by the respondent, rendering the rei vindicatio claim unsustainable. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court of the North Western Province dated 05.11.2008 is affirmed. Keywords Rei vindicatio action — Identification of corpus — Boundaries of land — Prescription — Section 344 of the Civil Procedure Code — Section 41 of the Civil Procedure Code — Title to land

Parties: Mod Tec Lanka (Pvt) Ltd v. Forest Glen Hotel & Spa (Pvt) Ltd
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Sathya Hettige, J.; Marasinghe, J
Keywords: Appeal for non-prosecution — Due diligence — Supreme Court Rules 1990 — Change of address — Notice to parties — Misuse of court procedures — Delay in proceedings
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent, Forest Glen Hotel & Spa (Pvt) Ltd, initiated legal action against the Defendant-Appellant, Mod Tec Lanka (Pvt) Ltd, in the High Court of the Western Province for the recovery of funds following the termination of a construction contract. The Respondent claimed a sum of Rs. 24,954,466/=, representing the unutilized portion of an advance payment made for the construction of a hotel in Nuwara Eliya. The High Court proceedings were marked by the Appellant's failure to present evidence or attend hearings, leading to an ex-parte judgment in favor of the Respondent on 03.11.2010. The Appellant subsequently filed an appeal to the Supreme Court. Throughout the appellate process, the Appellant consistently failed to appear or remain represented, despite multiple notices sent via registered post. The Appellantâ€™s instructing attorney passed away during the proceedings, and no fresh proxy was filed despite opportunities granted by the Court. Furthermore, the Appellant failed to notify the Court of a change in the company's address, requiring the Respondent to identify the new address and inform the Court. The Appellant remained absent and unrepresented at the final hearing on 13.01.2014. Issues Whether the Appellant failed to exercise due diligence in prosecuting the appeal as required by the Supreme Court Rules 1990. Whether the Appellantâ€™s failure to notify the Court of a change of address constitutes a misuse of judicial process and an undue burden on the Respondent. Applicable Law Statutes: High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996 Civil Procedure Code Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka Supreme Court Rules 1990 Cases Referred to: Daniel v. Chandradeva - [1994/v2-sri-lr-1] Priyani E. Soysa v. Rienzie Arsecularatne - Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Due diligence in prosecution Rule applied: Rule 34 of the Supreme Court Rules 1990. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that the Appellant engaged in dilatory tactics in both the High Court and the Supreme Court. Under Rule 34, a party must demonstrate "due diligence," defined as the prudence and activity expected of a reasonable person. The Appellant failed to file a fresh proxy after the death of their attorney and repeatedly failed to appear for hearings. The Court reasoned that such neglect undermines the administration of justice. Finding: The Appellant failed to show due diligence, warranting dismissal of the appeal. Issue 2: Change of address and judicial process Rule applied: The principle that the onus to notify the Court of a change of address lies with the party changing the address. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the burden of updating contact information rests solely with the party changing their address. Failure to do so forces the opposing party to engage in a "voyage of discovery" to locate them, which wastes judicial time and resources. The Court noted that the Respondent was forced to inform the Court of the Appellant's new address, which was an unnecessary burden. Finding: The Appellantâ€™s failure to update their address constituted a misuse of the Court's time and an unnecessary delay in the delivery of justice. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues reinforce the conclusion that the Appellantâ€™s conduct was a calculated or negligent misuse of the judicial process. The lack of due diligence in prosecuting the appeal (Issue 1) was compounded by the Appellant's failure to maintain proper communication with the Court (Issue 2), collectively demonstrating a disregard for the judicial process that necessitated the dismissal of the appeal. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed for non-prosecution due to the Appellant's failure to exercise due diligence and their disregard for court procedures. No costs are ordered. Keywords Appeal for non-prosecution — Due diligence — Supreme Court Rules 1990 — Change of address — Notice to parties — Misuse of court procedures — Delay in proceedings

Parties: Mod Tec Lanka (Pvt) Ltd v. Forest Glen Hotel & Spa (Pvt) Ltd
Bench: Tilakawardane, J.; Hettige, P.C., J; Marasinghe, J.
Keywords: Civil Procedure Code — High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996 — due diligence — non-prosecution — change of address — judicial process — misuse of court procedures — legal interest — contract termination — appeal dismissal
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent, Forest Glen Hotel & Spa (Pvt) Ltd, initiated legal proceedings against the Defendant-Appellant, Mod Tec Lanka (Pvt) Ltd, in the High Court of the Western Province, Colombo, seeking recovery of a sum of Rs. 28,704,466 along with legal interest. This claim arose from a terminated contract for the construction of a hotel in Elk Plain, Nuwara Eliya. The Respondent had advanced Rs. 28,246,400 to the Appellant, but upon termination, only Rs. 3,291,934 had been utilized, leaving a balance of Rs. 24,954,466 owed to the Respondent. The Respondent's motion filed on 02 September 2009 indicated that they would not call any further witnesses. However, when the trial commenced on 16 December 2009, the Appellant was unprepared, leading to a series of adjournments. On 04 December 2009, neither the Appellant nor their counsel appeared, prompting the Respondent to seek an ex-parte order. The High Court ruled in favor of the Respondent on 03 November 2010. Subsequently, the Appellant filed a Petition of Appeal to the Supreme Court, which was assigned Appeal number 19/2011 on 31 December 2010. Notices were issued to both parties, but the notice to the Appellant was returned undelivered, indicating they had 'left the place.' The Appellant's counsel passed away, and subsequent attempts to notify the Appellant of hearings were unsuccessful, leading to further delays. The Respondent informed the Court of a change in the Appellant's address, which the Appellant had failed to communicate. The Appellant's absence and lack of representation were noted throughout the proceedings, raising concerns about their diligence in pursuing the appeal. Issues Whether the Appellant demonstrated due diligence in prosecuting the appeal. Whether the Appellant's failure to notify the Court of a change of address constituted a misuse of court procedures. Applicable Law Statutes: High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996; Civil Procedure Code; Articles 127 & 128(4) of the Constitution. Cases Referred to: Daniel v. Chandradeva (1994) 2 SLR 1; Priyani E. Soysa v. Rienzie Arsecularatne. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Appellant demonstrated due diligence in prosecuting the appeal. Rule applied: Rule 34 of the Supreme Court Rules 1990. Court's reasoning: The Court emphasized the importance of due diligence in prosecuting appeals, as outlined in Rule 34 of the Supreme Court Rules 1990. The Court noted that the Appellant had failed to take necessary steps to advance their case, which was evident from their repeated absences and lack of representation. The judgment highlighted that the Appellant's actions, or lack thereof, were detrimental not only to the Respondent but also to the judicial process. The Court quoted Black's Law Dictionary to define due diligence as the measure of prudence expected from a reasonable person under the circumstances. The Appellant's failure to act in a timely manner and their neglect of the proceedings were viewed as a serious disregard for the Court's time and resources. The Court referenced the case of Daniel v. Chandradeva, which underscored the consequences of failing to demonstrate due diligence. The Appellant's conduct was characterized as a misuse of court procedures, which the Court does not tolerate. Finding: The Court concluded that the Appellant had not acted with due diligence, leading to the dismissal of the appeal. Issue 2: Whether the Appellant's failure to notify the Court of a change of address constituted a misuse of court procedures. Rule applied: Obligation to notify the Court of address changes. Court's reasoning: The Court reiterated that it is the responsibility of a party to inform the Court of any changes to their address. The failure to do so can result in significant delays and complications for the opposing party, as well as for the Court itself. The Respondent's proactive approach in informing the Court of the Appellant's address change was noted, contrasting sharply with the Appellant's negligence. The Court cited the case of Priyani E. Soysa v. Rienzie Arsecularatne, emphasizing that it is unreasonable to expect the opposing party to ascertain the current address of a party who has failed to communicate such changes. The Court expressed concern that the Appellant's actions not only wasted judicial resources but also undermined the integrity of the judicial process. Finding: The Court determined that the Appellant's failure to notify the Court of their address change constituted a misuse of court procedures, further justifying the dismissal of the appeal. Interaction of Issues The issues of due diligence and the obligation to notify the Court of address changes are interrelated, as both reflect the Appellant's overall lack of engagement with the judicial process. The Appellant's failure to demonstrate diligence in prosecuting their appeal was compounded by their negligence in communicating essential information to the Court. This combination of factors illustrated a broader pattern of disregard for the legal process, ultimately leading to the dismissal of the appeal. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal, citing the Appellant's failure to demonstrate due diligence and their neglect in notifying the Court of a change of address. The Court emphasized that such conduct undermines the judicial process and is unacceptable. No costs were awarded. Keywords Civil Procedure Code — High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996 — due diligence — non-prosecution — change of address — judicial process — misuse of court procedures — legal interest — contract termination — appeal dismissal
Counsel: Dr. Wickrama Weerasooriya with B.U Jayaweera for the Respondent. Appellant is absent and unrepresented.

Parties: The Peopleâ€™s Bank v. Jayasuriya and Others
Bench: Marsoof, P.C. J; Sathya Hettige, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Finance Act No. 11 — Vesting order — Section 72(3) — Tenants of original owner — Tenants of new owner — Encumbrances — Mortgage bond — District Court order — Court of Appeal judgment — Special Leave to Appeal
Summary: Facts The Peoples Bank initiated summary proceedings under section 72 of the Finance Act No. 11 of 1963 to evict the second and third respondents from premises that had been vested in the Bank by an order of the Minister of Finance. The original owner of the property, H.M. Ariyapala, had mortgaged the premises, leading to a default and a subsequent public auction where the property was purchased by H.M. Sumanasiri and later transferred to the first respondent. Throughout these ownership changes, the second and third respondents remained in occupation as tenants. Following the vesting of the property in the Peoples Bank, the Bank sought to take possession. The second and third respondents objected, claiming lawful tenancy rights. The District Court of Matara ordered the eviction of the respondents, ruling that the vesting order extinguished all encumbrances, including tenancy rights, pursuant to section 72(3) of the Finance Act as amended by Act No. 19 of 1990. The third respondent appealed this decision to the Court of Appeal, which set aside the District Court judgment. The Court of Appeal held that section 72(3) should not be interpreted to include long-standing tenants of the original owner, as doing so would cause grave injustice and absurdity. The Peoples Bank subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Court of Appeal erred in its interpretation of section 72(3) of the Finance Act No. 11 of 1963 by excluding tenants of the original owner from the scope of the vesting order. Whether the Court of Appeal was correct in exercising its revisionary jurisdiction despite the delay in filing the application. Applicable Law Statutes: Finance Act No. 11 of 1963 Finance (Amendment) Act No. 19 of 1990 Civil Procedure Code Debt Recovery (Special Provisions) Act No. 2 of 1990 Mortgage (Amendment) Act No. 3 of 1990 Recovery of Loans by Banks (Special Provisions) Act No. 4 of 1990 Cases Referred to: [ca-revision-757/94] Analysis Issue 1: Interpretation of Section 72(3) Rule applied: Section 72(3) of the Finance Act No. 11 of 1963, as amended by the Finance (Amendment) Act No. 19 of 1990. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that the statute explicitly states that upon the publication of a vesting order, the premises vest in the Bank "absolutely... free from all encumbrances." The amendment specifically declared that rights conferred by the Rent Act and the Protection of Tenants (Special Provisions) Act constitute encumbrances. The Court rejected the Court of Appeal's distinction between tenants of the original owner and subsequent owners, noting that the legislative language is clear and unambiguous. The Court held that there is no rational basis to exclude original tenants from the effect of a vesting order, and that affected parties must seek remedies through compensation or revocation of the order under the Act, rather than through judicial modification of clear statutory language. Finding: The Court of Appeal erred in law by excluding the tenants of the original owner from the effect of the vesting order. Issue 2: Revisionary Jurisdiction and Delay Rule applied: Principles governing the exercise of revisionary jurisdiction by the Court of Appeal. Court's reasoning: The Bank argued that the revision application should have been dismissed in limine due to a five-year delay. The respondents contended that the delay was caused by the pendency of a prior appeal and the systemic delays of the legal process. The Court noted that the Court of Appeal possesses wide discretion in revisionary matters and may act ex mero motu to correct errors. Given the presence of a substantial question of law, the Court found that the Court of Appeal was justified in entertaining the application despite the delay. Finding: The Court of Appeal did not err in exercising its revisionary jurisdiction. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are distinct but complementary. While the Court upheld the Court of Appeal's procedural decision to hear the revision application, it simultaneously corrected the Court of Appeal's substantive interpretation of the Finance Act. The resolution of the procedural issue allowed the Supreme Court to address the critical statutory interpretation issue, which ultimately led to the reversal of the Court of Appeal's judgment. Conclusion The judgment of the Court of Appeal dated 31.03.2010 is set aside, and the judgment of the District Court dated 06.09.2001 is affirmed. The appeal is allowed with no order as to costs. Keywords Finance Act No. 11 — Vesting order — Section 72(3) — Tenants of original owner — Tenants of new owner — Encumbrances — Mortgage bond — District Court order — Court of Appeal judgment — Special Leave to Appeal

Parties: National Development Bank PLC v. Rupasinghe and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Sripavan, J; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Mortgage — Guarantor — Borrower — Certificate of Sale — Hypothecary action — Recovery of Loans by Bank Act — Parate execution — Validity of auction — Fundamental rights
Summary: Facts The 1st Appellant obtained a loan from the Respondent-Bank for a tea replanting project and mortgaged her lands to secure the debt. The 2nd Appellant, acting as a third-party mortgagor, mortgaged her own properties to provide additional security for the same loan. Following a default on the loan capital, the Respondent-Bank initiated parate execution proceedings under the Recovery of Loans by Banks (Special Provisions) Act No. 4 of 1990. The Respondent-Bank auctioned the lands belonging to both the 1st and 2nd Appellants, purchased them for one million rupees, and registered a Certificate of Sale in the Land Registry on 20.08.2003. Subsequently, the Supreme Court delivered a judgment in 2005 clarifying that the Recovery of Loans by Banks (Special Provisions) Act No. 4 of 1990 did not permit the sale of properties belonging to third-party mortgagors or guarantors. Relying on this precedent, the Respondent-Bank unilaterally declared the Certificate of Sale a nullity and initiated a new hypothecary action in the Commercial High Court to recover the outstanding debt. The Appellants contended that the Respondent-Bank, having registered the Certificate of Sale and assumed ownership of the lands, was precluded from filing a subsequent hypothecary action. The Commercial High Court ruled in favor of the Respondent-Bank, leading to the present appeal. Issues Whether the Respondent-Bank is precluded from instituting a hypothecary action against the Appellants after having registered a Certificate of Sale for the mortgaged properties. Whether the parate execution proceedings conducted by the Respondent-Bank were valid in respect of the properties belonging to the 2nd Appellant, a third-party mortgagor. Applicable Law Statutes: Recovery of Loans by Banks (Special Provisions) Act No. 4 of 1990 High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996 Cases Referred to: Chelliah Ramachandran v. Hatton National Bank - [sc-appeal-05/2004] V. Anandasiva v. Hatton National Bank - [sc-appeal-9/2004] C. Ukwatte v. DFCC Bank - [sc-spl-la-31/2004] M.D. Karunawathie v. DFCC Bank - [sc-spl-la-32/2004] Analysis Issue 1: Preclusion of hypothecary action Rule applied: The principles of parate execution under the Recovery of Loans by Banks (Special Provisions) Act No. 4 of 1990 and the legal effect of a registered Certificate of Sale. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that the Respondent-Bank had registered the Certificate of Sale, effectively becoming the owner of the lands in the eyes of the public and the Land Registry. While the Bank attempted to unilaterally declare the sale a nullity to pursue a hypothecary action, the Court held that the Bank could not simply ignore the legal consequences of the registered sale. However, the Court determined that the Bank could only maintain its claim against the borrower (1st Appellant) regarding the lands legally subject to valid parate execution. Finding: The Respondent-Bank is entitled to maintain the hypothecary action only to the extent that the prior sale was valid, necessitating an amendment to the Certificate of Sale to reflect the exclusion of the 2nd Appellant's lands. Issue 2: Validity of parate execution against a third-party mortgagor Rule applied: The interpretation of the Recovery of Loans by Banks (Special Provisions) Act No. 4 of 1990 as established in Chelliah Ramachandran v. Hatton National Bank. Court's reasoning: The Court applied the precedent that the Act does not authorize the sale of properties belonging to a third-party mortgagor or guarantor. The Court found that the Respondent-Bank exceeded its statutory powers by including the 2nd Appellant's lands in the auction. Consequently, the sale of the 2nd Appellant's properties was invalid. Finding: The parate execution against the 2nd Appellant was legally void, and the Certificate of Sale must be amended to release the 2nd Appellant's lands from the Respondent-Bank's ownership. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the invalidity of the parate execution against the 2nd Appellant (Issue 2) necessitates the partial nullification of the Certificate of Sale. This partial nullification clarifies the scope of the Respondent-Bank's remaining claims, allowing the hypothecary action (Issue 1) to proceed only against the 1st Appellant's properties, which were the only assets validly subject to the Bank's initial recovery efforts. Conclusion The judgment of the Commercial High Court is set aside. The Certificate of Sale No. 443 is to be amended to include only the 1st Appellant's lands, thereby releasing the 2nd Appellant's lands from the Respondent-Bank's ownership. The 1st Appellant is awarded costs of Rs. 100,000 payable by the Respondent-Bank. Keywords Mortgage — Guarantor — Borrower — Certificate of Sale — Hypothecary action — Recovery of Loans by Bank Act — Parate execution — Validity of auction — Fundamental rights

Parties: National Development Bank PLC v. Nelka Rupasinghe alias Rupasinghe and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J.; Sripavan, J.; Wanasundera, PC.J.
Keywords: Certificate of Sale — hypothecary action — mortgage — guarantor — borrower — parate execution — ownership — legal nullity — statutory power — Recovery of Loans by Bank (Special Provisions) Act No. 4 of 1990 — auction — land registry — Civil Commercial Jurisdiction — Supreme Court — Provincial High Court
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a loan agreement between the National Development Bank PLC (the Respondent-Bank) and the 1st Appellant, Nelka Rupasinghe, who sought a loan of 7 million rupees to replant tea on her land. The 1st Appellant mortgaged her land to secure the loan, and the 2nd Appellant, Ahangama Gamage Nandawathie, also mortgaged her property as additional security for the same loan. The Respondent-Bank disbursed only 3.8 million rupees of the loan, and the 1st Appellant failed to repay the loan capital, leading the Respondent to auction both Appellants' lands. The Respondent-Bank purchased the lands for 1 million rupees and issued a Certificate of Sale, which was registered in the Land Registry. The Appellants contested the validity of the Certificate of Sale, arguing that it prohibited the Respondent-Bank from filing a hypothecary action to recover the outstanding loan. The Respondent-Bank contended that the Certificate of Sale was a nullity based on previous Supreme Court judgments. The case was heard in the Commercial High Court, which had jurisdiction due to the agreement that payments would be made at the Respondent-Bank's head office. Issues Whether the Certificate of Sale obtained by the Respondent-Bank prohibits it from filing a hypothecary action against the Appellants. Whether the Respondent-Bank's claim to ownership of the lands is valid in light of the previous Supreme Court judgments. Applicable Law Statutes: Recovery of Loans by Bank (Special Provisions) Act No. 4 of 1990 Cases Referred to: Chelliah Ramachandran and another vs. Hatton National Bank and 3 others - [SC. Appeal No. 05/2004]; V. Anandasiva and 12 others Vs. Hatton National Bank and 3 others - [SC. Appeal No. 9/2004]; C. Ukwatte and another Vs. DFCC Bank and another - [SC. Spl. LA. No. 31/2004]; M.D. Karunawathie and 5 others Vs. DFCC Bank and another - [SC. Spl. LA. No. 32/2004] Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Certificate of Sale obtained by the Respondent-Bank prohibits it from filing a hypothecary action against the Appellants. Rule applied: The legal principle regarding the ownership of mortgaged property and the rights of the lender to recover debts. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the implications of the Certificate of Sale issued to the Respondent-Bank, which indicated that the Bank had become the owner of the mortgaged lands. The Court noted that the Respondent-Bank had registered the Certificate of Sale in the Land Registry, which established its ownership in the eyes of the law. However, the Court also referenced previous judgments that clarified the limits of a bank's authority to auction properties belonging to guarantors. The Court emphasized that the Respondent-Bank's actions were inconsistent with the legal framework established by the Recovery of Loans by Bank (Special Provisions) Act No. 4 of 1990, which restricts the ability of banks to execute against the properties of guarantors. The Court highlighted that the Respondent-Bank's decision to auction the lands of the 2nd Appellant was legally flawed, as the auction should have been limited to the properties of the borrower, the 1st Appellant. The Court concluded that the Respondent-Bank could not pursue a hypothecary action against the Appellants after having acquired ownership of the lands through the Certificate of Sale. Finding: The Certificate of Sale did not prohibit the Respondent-Bank from filing a hypothecary action, but the auction of the 2nd Appellant's lands was invalid. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as they both concern the legal implications of the Certificate of Sale and the rights of the Respondent-Bank to recover the loan amount. The determination of whether the Certificate of Sale is valid directly impacts the Respondent-Bank's ability to pursue further legal action against the Appellants. The Court's analysis of the ownership established by the Certificate of Sale also informs its conclusions regarding the validity of the hypothecary action. Conclusion The Supreme Court set aside the judgment of the Commercial High Court, affirming that the Respondent-Bank's auction of the 2nd Appellant's lands was invalid. The Court ordered that the Certificate of Sale be amended to reflect only the lands of the 1st Appellant and awarded costs to the 1st Appellant. The judgment underscores the importance of adhering to statutory provisions governing the rights of lenders and the limitations on their ability to execute against guarantors' properties. Keywords Certificate of Sale — hypothecary action — mortgage — guarantor — borrower — parate execution — ownership — legal nullity — statutory power — Recovery of Loans by Bank (Special Provisions) Act No. 4 of 1990 — auction — land registry — Civil Commercial Jurisdiction — Supreme Court — Provincial High Court
Counsel: Rohan Sahabandu PC for 1st & 2nd Defendant-Appellants. Romesh de Silva, PC. with Geethaka Goonewardane for Plaintiff-Respondent.

Parties: Thenuwara and Others v. Rajapakse and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC., J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Sathyaa Hettige, PC., J; Eva Wanasundera, PC., J; Rohini Marasinghe, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Parliamentary Select Committee — Standing Orders of Parliament — Impeachment of a Judge — Judicial review — Executive or administrative action — Locus standi — Justiciability
Summary: Facts The Petitioners, including various professionals and office bearers of the Bar Association of Sri Lanka, filed these fundamental rights applications following the presentation of a notice of resolution to the Speaker of Parliament for the removal of the 43rd Chief Justice of Sri Lanka. The Petitioners challenged the constitutionality of Standing Order 78A of the Standing Orders of Parliament, which governed the procedure for the impeachment of Superior Court Judges. They contended that the appointment of a Parliamentary Select Committee under this Standing Order was ultra vires the Constitution, specifically alleging violations of Articles 4(c) and 107(3). The Court granted leave to proceed limited to the alleged violation of the fundamental right to equality under Article 12(1) of the Constitution, specifically focusing on the prayer for a declaration that Standing Order 78A is null and void. Several parties were permitted to intervene in the proceedings. The Respondents, represented by the Attorney General, and the Intervenient-Petitioners-Respondents raised several preliminary objections regarding the maintainability of the applications, including the lack of locus standi, the absence of executive or administrative action, the failure to disclose a violation of fundamental rights, and the non-justiciability of the matters raised. Issues Whether the appointment of a Parliamentary Select Committee under Standing Order 78A constitutes "executive or administrative action" amenable to the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court under Article 126 of the Constitution. Whether the Petitioners possess the necessary locus standi to challenge the impeachment process of a Chief Justice. Whether the petitions disclose a prima facie violation of the Petitioners' fundamental rights. Whether the challenge to the validity of Standing Orders of Parliament is justiciable by the Supreme Court. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka (Articles 3, 4, 12, 14, 17, 67, 74, 75, 107, 125, 126) Parliament (Powers and Privileges) Act No. 21 Cases Referred to: Victor Ivan v. Hon. Sarath N. Silva - [2001/v1-sri-lr-309] Faiz v. The Attorney General - [1995/v1-sri-lr-372] Weerawansa v. The Attorney General - [2000/v1-sri-lr-387] Dayananda v. Weeratunga - [Fundamental Rights Decisions, Vol. 2] Kumarasinghe v. A. G. - [sc-application-54/82] Leo Fernando v. Attorney-General - [1985/v2-sri-lr-341] Ramupillai v. Perera - [1991/v1-sri-lr-11] Jayathevan v. A.G. - [sc-application-192/91] R v. Liyanage - [64-nlr-313] Edward F William Silva v. Shirani Bandaranayake - [1997/v1-sri-lr-92] Somawathie v. Weerasinghe - [1990/v2-sri-lr-121] Mediwake v. Dayananda Dissanayake - [2000/v1-sri-lr-177] Sunila Abeysekera v. Ariya Rubasinghe - [2001/v1-sri-lr-315] Leader Publications v. Ariya Rubasinghe - [2001/v1-sri-lr-315] Lilanthi De Silva v. Attorney General - [2003/v1-sri-lr-155] Bulankulama v. Secretary, Ministry of Industrial Development - [2000/v3-sri-lr-243] The Attorney General v. Hon. (Dr.) Shirani Bandaranayke - [sc-appeal-67/2013] The Attorney General v. Samarakkody and Dahanayake - [57-nlr-412] Analysis Issue 1: Executive or administrative action Rule applied: Articles 17 and 126 of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Court examined whether the Speaker's act of appointing a Select Committee was executive or administrative. It distinguished between legislative, judicial, and administrative acts, noting that the appointment was not a legislative or judicial act. The Court held that the act of the Speaker in appointing the committee was an administrative act essential to the impeachment process, thus bringing it within the scope of Article 126. Finding: The preliminary objection regarding the lack of executive or administrative action was overruled. Issue 2: Locus standi Rule applied: Articles 17 and 126(2) of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Court noted that while it has adopted a liberal approach to standing in public interest litigation, the Petitioners must still demonstrate a direct grievance. The Court found that the Petitioners were challenging the impeachment process while the Chief Justice was already defending herself in that process. The Court held that the Petitioners lacked the necessary standing to challenge the constitutionality of the committee's appointment at that time. Finding: The preliminary objection regarding lack of locus standi was upheld. Issue 3: Disclosure of violation Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Court determined that the Petitioners' argument—that Standing Order 78A was ultra vires because it was not an Act of Parliament—was based on an erroneous interpretation of Article 107(3). The Court relied on its decision in The Attorney General v. Hon. (Dr.) Shirani Bandaranayke to conclude that Parliament is empowered to regulate impeachment procedures via Standing Orders. Finding: The petitions failed to disclose any violation of fundamental rights; the objection was upheld. Issue 4: Justiciability Rule applied: Section 3 of the Parliament (Powers and Privileges) Act No. 21 and Article 67 of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the impeachment process falls within the exclusive domain of Parliament. Under Section 3 of the Parliament (Powers and Privileges) Act, proceedings in Parliament cannot be questioned in any court. The Court concluded that the challenge to the validity of Standing Orders, which regulate internal parliamentary proceedings, is not justiciable. Finding: The objection regarding non-justiciability was upheld. Interaction of Issues The findings on locus standi, the failure to disclose a violation, and non-justiciability are mutually reinforcing. Because the Court determined that the impeachment process is a sui generis function of Parliament protected by privilege, the Petitioners' attempt to challenge the Standing Orders was fundamentally flawed. The lack of standing and the failure to show a violation of rights were direct consequences of the non-justiciable nature of the parliamentary proceedings. Conclusion The preliminary objections regarding locus standi, failure to disclose a violation of fundamental rights, and non-justiciability were upheld. Consequently, the petitions filed by the Petitioners in all cases are dismissed. No order for costs is made. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Parliamentary Select Committee — Standing Orders of Parliament — Impeachment of a Judge — Judicial review — Executive or administrative action — Locus standi — Justiciability

Parties: Hewage v. Bandaranayake
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC., J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Sathyaa Hettige, PC., J; Eva Wanasundera, PC., J; Rohini Marasinghe, J.
Keywords: Intervention in legal proceedings — Court of Appeal jurisdiction — Parliamentary Select Committee — Writ application — Attorney General's role — Judicial independence — Public interest — Article 107 of the Constitution
Summary: Facts Sumudu Kantha Hewage, the Intervenient-Petitioner-Petitioner-Appellant, sought to intervene in a writ application filed by the then Chief Justice, Hon. (Dr.) Shirani Anshumala Bandaranayake, in the Court of Appeal. The original writ application challenged the findings of a Parliamentary Select Committee and sought to restrain further steps regarding a resolution under Article 107 of the Constitution. The Appellant claimed that as a concerned citizen and Attorney-at-Law, he represented the public interest and intended to challenge the jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal to entertain the writ application. The Court of Appeal dismissed the intervention application on January 3, 2013. The court reasoned that the outcome of the writ application would not directly or indirectly impact the Appellant. Furthermore, the court noted that it had already decided to notice the Attorney General to appear as amicus curiae, rendering the Appellantâ€™s intervention unnecessary for the assistance of the court. Following this dismissal, the Court of Appeal proceeded to hear the matter and issued a judgment, which was subsequently set aside by the Supreme Court in a separate appeal on the grounds that the Court of Appeal lacked jurisdiction. The Appellant then appealed the refusal of his intervention to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Court of Appeal erred in law by refusing the Appellantâ€™s application to intervene in the writ proceedings. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 107(2), 107(3), 128(2), 140. Cases Referred to: The Attorney General v. Hon. (Dr.) Shirani Bandaranayake and Others - [sc-appeal-67/2013] Analysis Issue 1: Refusal of intervention Rule applied: The court applied the principles governing the exercise of judicial discretion in allowing or refusing intervention in pending litigation, specifically regarding the necessity of the party's presence for the court's assistance and the impact of the judgment on the party. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court observed that the Court of Appeal correctly exercised its discretion by determining that the Appellant would not be adversely affected by the outcome of the writ application. The Court of Appeal had already secured the assistance of the Attorney General, the Chief Law Officer of the State, to represent the public interest and assist the court. The Supreme Court found that the Court of Appealâ€™s reasoning was sound and that the presence of the Attorney General sufficiently addressed the concerns regarding the representation of public interest. Furthermore, the Supreme Court noted that because the original proceedings in the Court of Appeal had already concluded and the final judgment had been set aside in a separate appeal, granting the Appellant relief would serve no practical or useful purpose. Finding: The Court of Appeal did not err in law in refusing the intervention, and the appeal is dismissed as it serves no useful purpose. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. No costs are ordered. Keywords Intervention in legal proceedings — Court of Appeal jurisdiction — Parliamentary Select Committee — Writ application — Attorney General's role — Judicial independence — Public interest — Article 107 of the Constitution

Parties: Centre for Policy Alternatives (Guarantee) Ltd. and Saravanamuttu v. Jayaratne and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC. J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Sathyaa Hettige, PC. J; Eva Wanasundera, PC. J; Rohini Marasinghe, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Equality before the law — Parliamentary Council — Appointment of Chief Justice — Presidential immunity — Article 35(1) of the Constitution — Judicial process — Impeachment of a Chief Justice — Article 107(3) of the Constitution — Role of Parliament — Role of the President
Summary: Facts The Petitioners, Centre for Policy Alternatives (Guarantee) Ltd. and Dr. Paikiasothy Saravanamuttu, filed this application alleging a violation of Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The application arose following the impeachment of the 43rd Chief Justice, Hon. (Dr.) Shirani Bandaranayake, and the subsequent appointment of the 6th Respondent as the 44th Chief Justice. The Petitioners challenged the validity of the Parliamentary Select Committee report and the parliamentary resolution that led to the removal of the former Chief Justice. They sought declarations and restraining orders to prevent the 6th Respondent from accepting or exercising the functions of the office of Chief Justice. The 7th Respondent, the Attorney General, raised preliminary objections regarding the immunity of the President under Article 35(1) of the Constitution and the court's lack of jurisdiction to grant relief that would effectively remove a sitting Chief Justice. The Petitioners, through their counsel, subsequently indicated they did not wish to participate in the further disposal of the matter. The Court proceeded to determine the preliminary objections based on the pleadings and the law. Issues Whether the relief sought by the Petitioners is barred by the immunity of the President under Article 35(1) of the Constitution. Whether the Court has the jurisdiction to grant relief that would have the effect of removing the 6th Respondent from the office of Chief Justice. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka (Articles 12(1), 17, 35, 41A, 44, 107, 126, 134) Companies Act No. 7 Cases Referred to: Mallikarachchi v. Shiva Pasupathy, Attorney-General - [1985/v1-sri-lr-74] Edward F William Silva v. Shirani Bandaranayake - [1997/v1-sri-lr-92] Victor Ivan v. Sarath N. Silva - [2001/v1-sri-lr-309] Karunathilaka v. Dayananda Dissanayake - [1999/v1-sri-lr-157] Visuvalingam v. Liyanage - [1983/v1-sri-lr-203] The Attorney General v. Shirani Bandaranayke - [sc-appeal-67/2013] Analysis Issue 1: Presidential Immunity Rule applied: Article 35(1) of the Constitution, which provides that no proceedings shall be instituted or continued against the President while in office in respect of official or private acts. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the appointment of the 6th Respondent was an act of the President under Article 107(1). Following the principles in Mallikarachchi v. Shiva Pasupathy, the President enjoys absolute immunity for official acts. The Attorney General was not cited under the proviso to Article 35(3) to defend an act of the President in a ministerial capacity, but rather as a formal party under Article 126. Therefore, the challenge to the President's appointment power is barred by Article 35(1). Finding: The preliminary objection regarding Presidential immunity is upheld. Issue 2: Jurisdiction to Remove Chief Justice Rule applied: Article 107(2) and (3) of the Constitution, which prescribes the procedure for the removal of a Superior Court Judge. Court's reasoning: The Court reasoned that the power to remove a Chief Justice is not vested in the judiciary but requires the concurrence of Parliament and the President. The Court lacks the constitutional jurisdiction to grant any order that would directly or indirectly remove a sitting Chief Justice from office. Finding: The preliminary objection regarding the Court's lack of jurisdiction to remove the 6th Respondent is upheld. Interaction of Issues The findings on the two issues are mutually reinforcing. The immunity of the President prevents the Court from reviewing the validity of the appointment itself, while the constitutional framework for the removal of a judge prevents the Court from interfering with the 6th Respondent's tenure. Together, these findings render the Petitioners' requested relief legally unattainable. Conclusion The preliminary objections raised by the 7th Respondent are upheld. Consequently, the application is dismissed. No order for costs is made. Keywords Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Equality before the law — Parliamentary Council — Appointment of Chief Justice — Presidential immunity — Article 35(1) of the Constitution — Judicial process — Impeachment of a Chief Justice — Article 107(3) of the Constitution — Role of Parliament — Role of the President

Parties: DFCC Bank and Others v. Perera and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC., J; Sathyaa Hettige, PC., J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Appeal from Court of Appeal — Recovery of Loans by Bank (Special Provisions) Act — Borrowers — Directors of a Corporate entity — Lifting the veil of incorporation — Parate execution — Interim relief — Fundamental rights — Equality before the law
Summary: Facts The Petitioner-Respondent, Weliwita Don Kusumitha Muditha Perera, filed a writ application in the Court of Appeal seeking to restrain the 1st Respondent-Appellant, DFCC Bank, from selling his property by public auction. The property had been mortgaged under Mortgage Bond No. 1811 to secure a loan of Rs. 25,000,000 granted by the Bank to the 3rd Respondent-Respondent, Sewagama Rice Products (Pvt) Ltd. The Petitioner and Weliwita Don Neel Perera were directors of the 3rd Respondent company and co-owners of the mortgaged property. The Bank initiated parate execution proceedings against the property following a default on the loan. The Court of Appeal granted interim relief to the Petitioner, finding that he had established a prima facie case that he was not a "borrower" under the relevant statute and that he had provided sufficient reasons for not citing the Board of Directors as respondents. The Bank appealed this order to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Court of Appeal erred in determining that the Petitioner was not a "borrower" within the meaning of the Recovery of Loans by Bank (Special Provisions) Act No. 4 of 1990. Whether the Court of Appeal erred in determining that the Petitioner provided cogent reasons for not citing the members of the Board of Directors as respondents in the writ application, contrary to the principle in Ukwatte v. DFCC Bank. Applicable Law Statutes: Recovery of Loans by Bank (Special Provisions) Act No. 4 of 1990 Mortgage Act No. 6 of 1949 Banking Act No. 30 of 1988 Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka Cases Referred to: HNB v. Jayawardena - [2007] BALJR 50 Ramachandran v. Hatton National Bank - [2006/v1-sri-lr-393] Ukwatte v. DFCC Bank - [2004/v1-sri-lr-164] Salomon v. A. Salomon and Co. Ltd. - [1897] AC 22 Atlas Maritime Co SA v. Avalon Maritime Ltd. The Coral Rose - [1991] 4 All ER 769 Analysis Issue 1: Status as a borrower Rule applied: The Recovery of Loans by Bank (Special Provisions) Act No. 4 of 1990, as interpreted in Ramachandran v. Hatton National Bank. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the Act restricts parate execution to property mortgaged by the actual borrower. Following the majority decision in Ramachandran, the Court emphasized that the "borrower" is the person to whom the loan is granted. The Court distinguished HNB v. Jayawardena, noting that the latter involved specific circumstances where the corporate veil was lifted because the directors were the alter ego of the company and benefited from the loan. In the present case, the Petitioner was a third-party mortgagor, and the Court found no basis to apply the narrow principle of lifting the corporate veil to classify him as a borrower. Finding: The Court of Appeal did not err in determining that the Petitioner was not a borrower for the purpose of interim relief. Issue 2: Non-joinder of the Board of Directors Rule applied: The principle of separate legal personality as established in Salomon v. A. Salomon and Co. Ltd. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the Bank, as a corporate entity, is the party that benefits from parate execution and is the proper party to be sued. The Court held that the decision in Ukwatte v. DFCC Bank is irreconcilable with the fundamental principle of separate legal personality. Furthermore, the Court noted that the Petitioner had made reasonable efforts to obtain information regarding the Board's resolution, which was exclusively within the Bank's knowledge, justifying the naming of the Bank as the sole respondent. Finding: The Court of Appeal did not err in its determination regarding the non-joinder of the Board of Directors. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. By correctly identifying the Petitioner as a third-party mortgagor rather than a borrower, the Court established that the Bank's attempt to exercise parate execution was potentially outside its statutory jurisdiction. This jurisdictional challenge, combined with the finding that the Bank is the proper legal entity to be named in a writ application, confirms that the interim relief was appropriately granted to prevent irreparable loss. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed, and the order of the Court of Appeal dated 17th September 2010 is upheld. The case is remitted to the Court of Appeal for expeditious conclusion, with the interim injunction restraining the sale of the property to remain in effect until the final determination of the application. No costs are ordered. Keywords Appeal from Court of Appeal — Recovery of Loans by Bank (Special Provisions) Act — Borrowers — Directors of a Corporate entity — Lifting the veil of incorporation — Parate execution — Interim relief — Fundamental rights — Equality before the law

Parties: Warnasooriya v. Sellahewa
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC.J; Sathyaa Hettige, PC.J; Eva Wanasundera, PC.J
Keywords: Conditional transfer — Delivery of deed — Dominium — Consideration — Escrow — Conveyance of title — Mortgage bond — Cancellation of deed — Agreement to sell
Summary: Facts The Defendant-Appellant, Dayawathi Sellahewa, agreed to sell her property in Moratuwa to the Plaintiff-Respondent, Kapila Warnasooriya, for a total price of Rs. 445,000. The Plaintiff-Respondent paid an initial sum of Rs. 100,000 upon signing the agreement to sell and a further Rs. 105,000 on 23.04.1991. The balance of Rs. 240,000 was to be paid by 27.05.1991, primarily through a loan from the State Mortgage & Investment Bank. To facilitate this loan, the Defendant-Appellant signed the deed of transfer (P5/6517) on 23.04.1991. The Plaintiff-Respondent failed to secure the loan or pay the balance purchase price by the agreed date. Consequently, the Defendant-Appellant remained in possession of the property and was unable to complete a separate transaction to purchase a new residence in Malabe. The Plaintiff-Respondent subsequently filed an action in the District Court seeking a declaration of title and the ejectment of the Defendant-Appellant. The District Court ruled in favor of the Plaintiff-Respondent, and the Court of Appeal affirmed this decision, though it set aside the order for damages. The Defendant-Appellant appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the mere execution and delivery of the deed of transfer (P5) sufficed to pass dominium of the property to the Plaintiff-Respondent in the absence of full payment of the purchase price. Whether the Plaintiff-Respondent could succeed in an action for declaration of title without a proper conveyance of title. Whether the Court of Appeal erred in its treatment of the payment of the balance purchase price and the legal status of the transaction. Applicable Law Statutes: Not specified Cases Referred to: Appuhamy v. Appuhamy - [3-scc-61] Gunatillake v. Fernando - [21-nlr-257] Baiya v. Karunasekera - [56-nlr-265] Analysis Issue 1: Passing of Dominium Rule applied: The Roman-Dutch law principle that the mere signing of a deed does not automatically pass title; delivery of the deed is a prerequisite, and a deed may be delivered as an escrow if it is subject to a condition. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that the deed was signed on the implied condition that it would only become operative upon the payment of the full consideration. Because the balance of Rs. 240,000 was never paid, the intention to convey title was absent at the time of signing. The court characterized the deed as an "escrow," meaning it was not intended to be operative until the condition of payment was satisfied. Finding: The mere execution and delivery of the deed did not suffice to pass dominium to the Plaintiff-Respondent. Issue 2: Action for Declaration of Title Rule applied: The requirement for a valid conveyance of title to maintain a rei vindicatio action. Court's reasoning: Since the court found that the deed of transfer was not operative due to the failure of consideration and the conditional nature of the agreement, the Plaintiff-Respondent never acquired legal title to the property. Consequently, he lacked the necessary standing to maintain an action for declaration of title and ejectment. Finding: The Plaintiff-Respondent could not succeed in his action for declaration of title. Issue 3: Treatment of Purchase Price and Transaction Rule applied: Principles of contract law regarding the failure of consideration and the nullity of conditional transactions. Court's reasoning: The court determined that because the loan was never granted and the balance was never paid, the mortgage bond registered in favor of the bank was a nullity. The court held that the entire transaction failed due to the non-performance of the conditions agreed upon by the parties. Finding: The Court of Appeal erred in its conclusions; the deed of transfer is to be cancelled, and the mortgage is declared null and void. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent: the determination that the deed was an escrow (Issue 1) directly invalidates the Plaintiff-Respondent's claim to title (Issue 2). Because the title never passed, the underlying transaction and the associated mortgage are rendered void (Issue 3), necessitating the cancellation of the deed and the restitution of the advance payment. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The deed of transfer (P5) is to be cancelled, and the mortgage bond is declared null and void. The Defendant-Appellant is directed to deposit Rs. 205,000 in the District Court for return to the Plaintiff-Respondent, upon which the Registrar shall execute a deed of cancellation. Keywords Conditional transfer — Delivery of deed — Dominium — Consideration — Escrow — Conveyance of title — Mortgage bond — Cancellation of deed — Agreement to sell

Parties: International Water Management Institute v. Jayakody
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC. J; Sathyaa Hettige, PC. J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Diplomatic Privileges Act — International Water Management Institute — Immunity from process — Minister's order — Gazette publication — Parliamentary approval — Fundamental rights — International agreements — Labour Tribunal jurisdiction
Summary: Facts The Appellant, the International Water Management Institute, was incorporated by the International Irrigation Management Institute Act No. 6 of 1985, as amended by Act No. 50 of 2000. The Respondent filed an application in the Labour Tribunal, Kaduwela, seeking relief for alleged unlawful termination of services. The Appellant raised a preliminary objection asserting immunity from legal process based on the Diplomatic Privileges Act No. 9 of 1996 and the International Irrigation Management Institute Act No. 6 of 1985. The Labour Tribunal initially overruled the plea of immunity, a decision later set aside by the High Court due to inadequacy of reasons. Upon remand, the Labour Tribunal issued a reasoned order again overruling the plea, citing the lack of evidence that the Ministerâ€™s order granting immunity had been placed before Parliament as required by Section 5(1) of the Diplomatic Privileges Act. The High Court affirmed this decision, holding that the Ministerâ€™s order was ineffective without parliamentary approval and that the Tribunalâ€™s jurisdiction could not be ousted by executive order alone. The Appellant appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court erred in rejecting the plea of immunity based on the Ministerâ€™s order dated 10th December 1997, issued under Section 4 of the Diplomatic Privileges Act No. 9 of 1996. Whether the High Court erred in failing to find that the International Irrigation Management Institute Act No. 6 of 1985, as amended, demonstrated a legislative intent to grant immunity. Applicable Law Statutes: International Irrigation Management Institute Act No. 6 of 1985 International Irrigation Management Institute (Amendment) Act No. 50 of 2000 Industrial Disputes Act No. 43 of 1950 Diplomatic Privileges Act No. 9 of 1996 Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Immunity under the Diplomatic Privileges Act Rule applied: Section 4(1) and 4(2) of the Diplomatic Privileges Act No. 9 of 1996. Court's reasoning: The Court held that an order made by the Minister under Section 4(1) comes into force upon publication in the Gazette. The Court rejected the argument that the failure to place the order before Parliament under Section 5(1) invalidated the immunity. The Court further determined that the Appellant, as an agency established to carry out the objectives of the Memorandum of Agreement, fell within the scope of "agents" entitled to immunity under the Act. The Court found that the Ministerâ€™s order clearly incorporated the terms of the Memorandum of Agreement, which explicitly granted the Institute immunity from every form of legal process. Finding: The High Court erred in rejecting the plea of immunity; the Appellant is entitled to immunity under the Diplomatic Privileges Act. Issue 2: Immunity under the IIMI Act Rule applied: Section 33 of the International Irrigation Management Institute Act No. 6 of 1985. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that Section 33 imposed a legal obligation on the Government to ensure the Institute was accorded privileges and immunities as agreed upon in the Memorandum of Agreement. While the Court expressed doubt that Section 33 alone was sufficient to confer immunity from suit in a municipal tribunal, it acknowledged that the Act reflected a clear legislative intent to grant such status. Finding: The High Court erred in not recognizing the legislative intent to grant immunity, though Section 33 by itself did not independently confer immunity from suit. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues reinforce the conclusion that the Appellant is entitled to immunity. The resolution of the first issue regarding the Diplomatic Privileges Act provides the primary legal basis for the immunity, while the second issue confirms that this status is consistent with the legislative intent expressed in the Institute's founding Act. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgment of the High Court and the order of the Labour Tribunal are set aside, and the Respondent's application in the Labour Tribunal is dismissed for lack of jurisdiction. Keywords Diplomatic Privileges Act — International Water Management Institute — Immunity from process — Minister's order — Gazette publication — Parliamentary approval — Fundamental rights — International agreements — Labour Tribunal jurisdiction

Parties: Athula Chandraguptha Thenuwara v. Chamal Rajapakse and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J; Sathyaa Hettige, P.C., J.; Eva Wanasundera, P.C., J.; Rohini Marasinghe, J.
Keywords: fundamental rights — impeachment — judicial independence — administrative action — locus standi — ultra vires — Standing Orders — constitutional law — separation of powers — justiciability — parliamentary privileges — public interest — rule of law — judicial accountability — constitutional interpretation
Summary: Facts The present case involves a series of fundamental rights applications filed under Articles 17 and 126 of the Constitution of Sri Lanka, following the presentation of a resolution for the removal of the 43rd Chief Justice, Hon. (Dr.) Shirani Bandaranayake. The petitioners, comprising individuals and representatives of the Bar Association of Sri Lanka, sought to challenge the constitutionality of Standing Order 78A, which was invoked to appoint a Parliamentary Select Committee to investigate the allegations against the Chief Justice. The petitioners claimed that the appointment of the Select Committee violated their fundamental rights to equality and due process as enshrined in Articles 12(1) and 12(2) of the Constitution. The Court granted leave to proceed on the basis of the alleged violation of fundamental rights, focusing specifically on the legality of Standing Order 78A. The petitioners argued that the Standing Order was ultra vires the Constitution, particularly in light of Article 107(3), which mandates that the procedure for the removal of judges must be provided by law. The case was marked by significant public interest, as it involved the integrity of the judiciary and the independence of the legal profession in Sri Lanka. Issues The primary issues before the Court were: Whether the actions of the Speaker of Parliament in appointing the Parliamentary Select Committee constituted 'executive or administrative action' under Article 126 of the Constitution. Whether the petitioners had locus standi to challenge the constitutionality of the Standing Order and the appointment of the Select Committee. Whether the petitions disclosed any violation of the fundamental rights of the petitioners. Whether the matters sought to be determined were justiciable in the context of parliamentary privileges. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka Articles 17, 126, 12(1), 12(2), 107(2), 107(3), 4(c) Parliament (Powers and Privileges) Act No. 21 of 1953 Cases Referred to: Faiz v. The Attorney General (1995) 1 SLR 372; Weerawansa v. The Attorney General (2000) 1 Sri LR 387; Victor Ivan and Others v. Hon. Sarath N. Silva and Others (2001) 1 Sri LR 309; Edward F William Silva and Others v. Shirani Bandaranayake and Others (1997) 1 Sri LR 92; The Attorney General v. Hon. (Dr.) Shirani Bandaranayake and Others SC Appeal No. 67/2013. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the actions of the Speaker of Parliament in appointing the Parliamentary Select Committee constituted 'executive or administrative action' under Article 126 of the Constitution. Rule applied: Article 126 of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the nature of the actions taken by the Speaker of Parliament in appointing the Select Committee, determining that such actions fell within the realm of 'executive or administrative action' as defined by Article 126. The Court emphasized that the appointment was not merely a legislative act but involved administrative functions necessary for the impeachment process. The Court referenced previous judgments that distinguished between legislative, judicial, and administrative actions, asserting that the Speaker's role in this context was administrative in nature. The Court further noted that the impeachment process, while involving Parliament, also required adherence to constitutional provisions that safeguard fundamental rights. The Court concluded that the actions of the Speaker were indeed administrative, thus satisfying the criteria for justiciability under Article 126. Finding: The Court found that the actions of the Speaker constituted 'executive or administrative action' as per Article 126, allowing the petitioners to invoke the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court. Issue 2: Whether the petitioners had locus standi to challenge the constitutionality of the Standing Order and the appointment of the Select Committee. Rule applied: Locus standi principles under Article 17 and 126 of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Court considered the arguments presented regarding the locus standi of the petitioners, particularly focusing on whether they had a direct interest in the matter at hand. The petitioners contended that they were acting in the public interest and on behalf of the general public to safeguard the independence of the judiciary. The Court acknowledged the evolving interpretation of locus standi in Sri Lankan jurisprudence, which has become more liberal over time, allowing individuals to challenge violations of fundamental rights even if they are not directly aggrieved. However, the Court ultimately concluded that the petitioners did not possess sufficient standing to challenge the constitutionality of the Standing Order at the time of filing their applications, as the Chief Justice herself had not yet contested the proceedings before the Select Committee. Finding: The Court upheld the preliminary objection regarding locus standi, concluding that the petitioners lacked the necessary standing to challenge the constitutionality of the Standing Order and the appointment of the Select Committee. Issue 3: Whether the petitions disclosed any violation of the fundamental rights of the petitioners. Rule applied: Fundamental rights provisions under Articles 12(1) and 12(2) of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the petitions to determine if they disclosed any prima facie violation of the petitioners' fundamental rights. The Court noted that the essence of the petitioners' claims revolved around the alleged unconstitutionality of the Standing Order and the impeachment process. However, the Court found that the petitions did not adequately demonstrate how the actions of the Speaker or the Select Committee infringed upon the petitioners' rights. The Court emphasized that the mere invocation of fundamental rights did not suffice; the petitioners needed to establish a clear link between the alleged violations and their own rights. Finding: The Court concluded that the petitions did not disclose any violation of the fundamental rights of the petitioners, thereby upholding the preliminary objection on this ground. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in this case were interrelated, as the determination of locus standi directly impacted the Court's ability to consider the substantive claims regarding the constitutionality of the Standing Order and the actions of the Speaker. The Court's findings on the nature of the actions taken by the Speaker also influenced the assessment of justiciability, as the Court had to navigate the delicate balance between parliamentary privileges and the protection of fundamental rights. Conclusion The Supreme Court ultimately dismissed the petitions filed by the petitioners, upholding the preliminary objections raised regarding locus standi, the failure to disclose violations of fundamental rights, and the justiciability of the matters in question. The Court emphasized the importance of safeguarding the independence of the judiciary while also respecting the constitutional framework that delineates the powers and privileges of Parliament. The Court did not make any order for costs, reflecting the complex nature of the proceedings and the significant public interest involved. Keywords fundamental rights — impeachment — judicial independence — administrative action — locus standi — ultra vires — Standing Orders — constitutional law — separation of powers — justiciability — parliamentary privileges — public interest — rule of law — judicial accountability — constitutional interpretation
Counsel: Suren Fernando for the Petitioner in SC Application 665/12 (FR). Viran Corea and Ms. Sarita de Fonseka for the Petitioner in SC Application 666/12 (FR). Illangage for the Intervenient-Petitioner-Respondent in SC Application 665/12 (FR), 666/12 (FR), 667/12 (FR) and 672/12 (FR), Koggala Wellala Bandula. Prof. H.M. Zafrullah with B. Manawadu for the Intervenient – Petitioner-Respondent in SC Application 666/12 (FR) and 667/12 (FR), Jayasooriya Alankarage Peter Nelson Perera. Razik Zarook, P.C. with B. Manawadu for the Intervenient – Petitioner-Respondent in SC Application 672/12 (FR), Arumapperuma Arachchige Sudima Chandani. Palitha Fernando, P.C. Parties: Samarasinghe v. Perera and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC., J; Sathyaa Hettige, PC., J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Public service appointments — Interview process — Marking scheme — Direct relevant experience — Administrative action
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Dr. P.W.S.M. Samarasinghe, was an officer of the Sri Lanka Agriculture Service who served as the Deputy Director (Potato Development) at the Seed and Planting Material Development Centre. The Petitioner challenged the appointment of the 15th Respondent to the post of Director of the Seed and Planting Material Development Centre, which occurred on 3rd November 2008. The Petitioner alleged that this appointment violated her fundamental rights to equality under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Both the Petitioner and the 15th Respondent retired from service during the pendency of the application. The Petitioner sought a declaration that the appointment was null and void and requested to be appointed to the post retrospectively. During the proceedings, it was noted that the Petitioner had been ranked third at the interview, while the 15th Respondent was ranked first with a significantly higher score. The Petitioner contended that the interview panel misdirected itself by failing to award her marks for her previous research experience, which she argued constituted direct relevant experience for the post. The Respondents maintained that the marking scheme specifically prioritized production experience over research experience. Issues Whether the appointment of the 15th Respondent to the post of Director of the Seed and Planting Material Development Centre violated the Petitioner's fundamental right to equality under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the interview panel misdirected itself in the application of the marking scheme regarding the assessment of "direct relevant experience." Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 12(1), 17, and 126. Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the Petitioner was ranked third at the interview. Even if the Petitioner were to succeed in her claim that she was unfairly treated, she failed to challenge the position of the candidate ranked second. Furthermore, the 15th Respondent possessed greater seniority in Class I of the Sri Lanka Agriculture Service. Given that the Petitioner admitted the application would yield no monetary benefit due to her retirement, the court found no evidence of discriminatory conduct that would warrant a constitutional remedy. Finding: The Petitioner failed to establish a violation of Article 12(1). Issue 2: Misdirection in marking Rule applied: The marking scheme (P6) annexed to the petition. Court's reasoning: The Petitioner argued that her research experience should have been credited as "direct relevant experience." The court analyzed the marking scheme, which defined the criteria as farm planning, management, and seed production. The court reasoned that these criteria specifically emphasize production-oriented work and exclude research-related work. Even if the Petitioner's research experience were considered as indirect experience, the resulting marks would be insufficient to overcome the 60.85-point gap between her and the 15th Respondent. Finding: The interview panel did not misdirect itself; the Petitioner's research experience did not meet the criteria for direct relevant experience under the established scheme. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the interview panel correctly applied the marking scheme (Issue 2) directly supports the conclusion that the appointment process was not discriminatory (Issue 1). Because the Petitioner could not demonstrate that she was entitled to higher marks under the objective criteria, her claim of a fundamental rights violation based on the selection process necessarily failed. Conclusion The Petitioner failed to discharge the burden of proof required to establish a violation of her fundamental rights. The application is dismissed without costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Public service appointments — Interview process — Marking scheme — Direct relevant experience — Administrative action

Parties: Subramaniam Sivapalanathan v. Attorney General and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Marsoof, PC, J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Cheating under section 400 of the Penal Code — Misappropriation under section 386 of the Penal Code — Advance rent — Notice to vacate — Issuing a cheque without funds — Deception — Essential elements of charges — Debt Recovery Act
Summary: Facts The Complainant, Selladorai Sellamma, entered into a rental agreement with the Accused, Subramaniam Sivapalanathan, for a room at a monthly rent of Rs. 6,000. The Complainant paid an advance of Rs. 72,000 for one year. The Complainant occupied the premises for three months before vacating due to disputes with the Accused. Upon vacating, the Complainant requested a refund of the balance rent. The Accused provided a post-dated cheque for Rs. 42,500, which was subsequently dishonored because the bank account had been closed. The Accused was charged in the Magistrates Court of Colombo with cheating under section 400 of the Penal Code and, in the alternative, criminal misappropriation under section 386 of the Penal Code. The Accused contended that the Complainant failed to provide the required one-month notice under the agreement, thereby forfeiting the balance. The Magistrate convicted the Accused on both counts and imposed a sentence of one year of rigorous imprisonment. The Provincial High Court affirmed the conviction and sentence. The Accused appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the conviction for cheating under section 400 of the Penal Code was justified by the evidence. Whether the conviction for criminal misappropriation under section 386 of the Penal Code was justified by the evidence. Whether the sentence of one year of rigorous imprisonment was appropriate under the circumstances. Applicable Law Statutes: Penal Code, Section 386 Penal Code, Section 400 Debt Recovery Act, Section 25 Constitution, Article 128 High Court of Western Province Act No. 9 of 1996 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Cheating Rule applied: Section 400 of the Penal Code, which requires proof of deception to induce a person to deliver property or perform an act. Court's reasoning: The court found that the essential element of deception was absent. The Complainant had already vacated the premises of her own volition before the cheque was issued. Therefore, the issuance of the dishonored cheque did not induce the Complainant to vacate the premises, as the act of vacating had already occurred. Finding: The conviction for cheating was set aside, and the Accused was acquitted on the first count. Issue 2: Misappropriation Rule applied: Section 386 of the Penal Code regarding the dishonest misappropriation of property. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the Accused had received the advance rent and was obligated to return the balance after the Complainant vacated. Even if the Complainant failed to provide the required notice, the Accused was only entitled to retain one month's rent. By failing to return the remaining balance and issuing a cheque from a closed account, the Accused demonstrated dishonest intent to misappropriate the funds. Finding: The conviction for criminal misappropriation was affirmed. Issue 3: Sentencing Rule applied: Judicial discretion in sentencing under the Penal Code. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the Magistrate had improperly considered a subsequent 2005 conviction as a basis for a custodial sentence for an offence committed in 1998. Given the age of the Accused and the nature of the offence, a custodial sentence was deemed inappropriate. Finding: The sentence of one year of rigorous imprisonment was suspended for a period of five years. Interaction of Issues The resolution of the cheating charge as a failure of proof regarding the timing of the deception clarified that the criminal liability rested solely on the retention of funds. By setting aside the cheating conviction, the court narrowed the scope of the Accused's liability to the misappropriation of the balance rent, which allowed for a more proportionate sentencing approach. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed in part. The conviction for cheating is set aside, the conviction for misappropriation is affirmed, and the sentence of one year of rigorous imprisonment is suspended for five years. Keywords Cheating under section 400 of the Penal Code — Misappropriation under section 386 of the Penal Code — Advance rent — Notice to vacate — Issuing a cheque without funds — Deception — Essential elements of charges — Debt Recovery Act

Parties: Wanasinghe v. Janashakthi Insurance Company Limited
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC, J; Sathyaa Hettige, PC, J; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J
Keywords: Preliminary objections — Suppression of fact — Prior invocation of jurisdiction — Final appeal — Interlocutory order — Jurisdiction of Commercial High Court — Arbitration Act No 11 — Civil Procedure Code — Leave to appeal
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Petitioner, Wajira Prabath Wanasinghe, filed an action in the Commercial High Court against the Defendant-Respondent, Janashakthi Insurance Company Limited. The Commercial High Court dismissed the action on 13th July 2012, ruling that it lacked jurisdiction to entertain the matter due to the provisions of Section 5 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. The Plaintiff-Petitioner subsequently filed an application for leave to appeal in the Supreme Court on 27th July 2012. The Defendant-Respondent raised preliminary objections, alleging that the Petitioner had suppressed the fact that he had previously invoked the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court by way of a final appeal and that the impugned order could only be canvassed by way of a final appeal rather than an application for leave to appeal. Issues Whether the Plaintiff-Petitioner suppressed the fact that he had previously invoked the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court by way of a final appeal. Whether the order of the Commercial High Court dated 13th July 2012 is an interlocutory order subject to an application for leave to appeal or a final judgment requiring a final appeal. Applicable Law Statutes: Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 Civil Procedure Code High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996 Cases Referred to: Wickremasinghe v. De Silva - [1978/79/v2-sri-lr-65] Salaman v. Warner - [1891/v1-qb-734] Bozson v. Altrincham Urban District Council - [1903/v1-kb-547] Siriwardena v. Air Ceylon Ltd. - [1984/v1-sri-lr-286] Ranjit v. Kusumawathie - [1998/v3-sri-lr-232] Rajendran Chettiar v. S. Narayanan Chettiar - [2011-baljr-25] Analysis Issue 1: Suppression of prior invocation of jurisdiction Rule applied: Section 754(4) and Section 755(3) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that the mere lodging of a Notice of Appeal in the original court does not invoke the jurisdiction of the appellate court. Jurisdiction is only invoked when the Notice of Appeal meets all statutory requirements and is followed by a Petition of Appeal. In this case, the Petitioner filed his application for leave to appeal on 27th July 2012, while the Petition of Appeal was only filed on 10th September 2012.
