Therefore, no final appeal was pending before the Supreme Court at the time the leave to appeal application was filed. Finding: The preliminary objection regarding suppression of facts is overruled. Issue 2: Nature of the impugned order Rule applied: The test established in Salaman v. Warner, as adopted by the five-judge bench in Rajendran Chettiar v. S. Narayanan Chettiar. Court's reasoning: The court applied the "Salaman test," which asks whether the decision, if given in one way, would finally dispose of the matter, or if given in the other, would allow the action to proceed. The court noted that if the Commercial High Court had ruled against the Respondent on the preliminary issue of jurisdiction, the trial would have continued to a final conclusion. Because the decision to dismiss the action based on the Arbitration Act was contingent upon the resolution of a preliminary issue, the order is interlocutory in nature. Finding: The impugned order is an interlocutory order, and the application for leave to appeal is maintainable. Interaction of Issues The resolution of the second issue confirms that the Petitioner followed the correct procedural path for an interlocutory order, while the resolution of the first issue confirms that the Petitioner did not engage in procedural misconduct or misrepresentation. Together, these findings allow the court to proceed to the merits of the leave to appeal application. Conclusion The preliminary objections raised by the Defendant-Respondent are overruled. The application for leave to appeal is to be re-fixed for support. No order for costs is made. Keywords Preliminary objections — Suppression of fact — Prior invocation of jurisdiction — Final appeal — Interlocutory order — Jurisdiction of Commercial High Court — Arbitration Act No 11 — Civil Procedure Code — Leave to appeal

Parties: Wewalwalahewage AriyaKumara v. Dona Bernadeth and Another
Bench: Marsoof, PC, J; Sathya Hettige, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Special leave to appeal — Jurisdiction of High Court — Appellate jurisdiction — Revisionary jurisdiction — Article 154P of the Constitution — High Court of Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 19 of 1990 — Court of Appeal — Supreme Court
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Wewalwalahewage Hemantha AriyaKumara, filed an application for special leave to appeal to the Supreme Court against a judgment of the High Court of Negombo. The High Court had previously exercised its appellate jurisdiction in Case No. HCA 217/11, which originated from a maintenance case in the Magistrate's Court. Upon the matter being taken up for support, the Respondent, Kaluappu Kankanamalage Dona Bernadeth Yamuna Rani Karunarathne, raised a preliminary objection regarding the maintainability of the appeal. The Respondent contended that the Supreme Court lacked jurisdiction to hear the appeal, arguing that under Article 154P(6) of the Constitution, appeals from the High Court must be directed to the Court of Appeal. The Petitioner maintained that the Supreme Court possessed the requisite jurisdiction because the High Court had exercised its appellate jurisdiction rather than its revisionary jurisdiction. Issues Whether the Supreme Court has jurisdiction to hear an appeal against a judgment of the Provincial High Court when that court has exercised its appellate jurisdiction under Article 154P(3)(b) of the Constitution. Whether the preliminary objection regarding the maintainability of the appeal should be upheld. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 138, Article 154P(3)(b), Article 154P(6) High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 19 of 1990, Section 9, Section 10 Cases Referred to: Abeywardana v. Ajith de Silva - [1998/v1-sri-lr-134] Gunaratne v. Thambinayagam - [1993/v2-sri-lr-355] Wickremasekara v. Officer in Charge, Police Station, Ampara - [2004/v1-sri-lr-257] Analysis Issue 1: Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court Rule applied: Section 9 and Section 10 of the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 19 of 1990. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the constitutional framework and the subsequent legislative changes introduced by Act No. 19 of 1990. While Article 154P(6) generally directs appeals from the High Court to the Court of Appeal, Section 9 of Act No. 19 of 1990 specifically grants a right of appeal to the Supreme Court against final orders or judgments of a High Court when it exercises its appellate jurisdiction. The Court clarified that this statutory provision distinguishes between the High Court's original criminal or revisionary jurisdiction—where appeals lie to the Court of Appeal—and its appellate jurisdiction, where appeals lie to the Supreme Court. Finding: The Supreme Court has jurisdiction to hear appeals from the High Court when the High Court has exercised its appellate jurisdiction. Issue 2: Maintainability of the Appeal Rule applied: Section 9 of the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 19 of 1990 and the precedent established in Gunaratne v. Thambinayagam. Court's reasoning: The Court noted that the Petitioner sought to appeal a judgment rendered by the High Court in the exercise of its appellate jurisdiction. Following the established legal position in Gunaratne v. Thambinayagam, which was affirmed in subsequent cases, the Court determined that the Petitioner had correctly invoked the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court. Finding: The preliminary objection is overruled, and the appeal is maintainable. Interaction of Issues The resolution of the jurisdictional question (Issue 1) directly determined the outcome of the preliminary objection (Issue 2). Because the Court found that the statutory framework grants the Supreme Court appellate authority over High Court decisions made in an appellate capacity, the Respondent's objection regarding the maintainability of the application was necessarily dismissed. Conclusion The preliminary objection raised by the Respondent is overruled. The Petitioner is permitted to proceed with the application for special leave to appeal. Keywords Special leave to appeal — Jurisdiction of High Court — Appellate jurisdiction — Revisionary jurisdiction — Article 154P of the Constitution — High Court of Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 19 of 1990 — Court of Appeal — Supreme Court

Parties: Ambewela Livestock Co. Ltd. v. Sri Lanka Co-operative Marketing Federation Ltd. and Sri Lanka Co-operative Marketing Federation Ltd.
Bench: Thilakawardena, J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Sathyaa Hettige, PC, J
Keywords: Contractual transaction — Potato seedlings supply — Breach of contract — Prescription — Novation — Successor company — Asset recovery
Summary: Facts The plaintiff, Ambewela Livestock Co. Ltd., is a company incorporated to succeed the business of the Ambewela Farm, previously managed by the National Livestock Development Board. The plaintiff sought to recover a balance sum of Rs. 4,694,000/- from the defendant, Sri Lanka Co-operative Marketing Federation Ltd. (Markfed), for the supply of 100 Metric Tons of potato seedlings. The plaintiff alleged that the defendant requested the seedlings, acknowledged receipt, and failed to pay the full amount despite partial payments. The defendant denied the existence of a contract, claiming it acted only as an intermediary between the National Livestock Development Board and various Farmers' Societies. The defendant argued that its obligation to remit payments was conditional upon receiving funds from said societies and that it had already remitted all funds received. The defendant further contended that the claim was prescribed and that the transaction had been subject to novation. The Commercial High Court of Colombo ruled in favor of the plaintiff, prompting the defendant to appeal to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether a valid contractual obligation existed between the plaintiff and the defendant for the supply of potato seedlings. Whether the plaintiff's claim was barred by the Prescription Ordinance. Whether the contract was extinguished or replaced by novation due to the change in the plaintiff's corporate structure and share ownership. Applicable Law Statutes: State Agriculture Corporations Act No. 11 of 1972 Prescription Ordinance Cases Referred to: Muthukuda v. Sumanawathie - [65-nlr-205] Noorbhai v. Karuppan Chetty - [27-nlr-325] Karthikesu v. Ponnachchy - [14-nlr-486] Analysis Issue 1: Existence of a contract Rule applied: The court applied the principles of contract formation, specifically the requirement of offer, acceptance, and consensus ad idem. Court's reasoning: The court examined documentary evidence, including letters of request from the defendant and a promissory note executed by the defendant's officers. The court found that these documents constituted a clear manifestation of mutual assent and a written promise to pay the specified sum. The defendant's failure to contradict this evidence during trial supported the finding of a valid contract. Finding: A valid written contract existed between the parties. Issue 2: Prescription Rule applied: Section 6 of the Prescription Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the plaintiff was a government institution until its incorporation as a private company in 1999. Furthermore, the defendant acknowledged the debt in correspondence sent as late as 2002, after the share sale agreement. Since the action was filed in 2003, it fell within the statutory period for recovery of a debt based on a written promise. Finding: The claim was not prescribed. Issue 3: Novation Rule applied: The definition of novation as the substitution of a new obligation or party, requiring the consent of all parties to extinguish the prior obligation. Court's reasoning: The court held that the defendant failed to provide evidence of a new agreement or mutual consent to discharge the original debt. The change in the plaintiff's corporate name and the sale of shares to Lanka Milk Foods (CWE) Limited did not extinguish the underlying debt or the defendant's liability. The court emphasized that a mere change in the creditor's identity or management does not constitute novation without an express or implied agreement to replace the original obligation. Finding: No novation occurred; the defendant remained liable for the debt. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. The establishment of a valid contract (Issue 1) provided the basis for the debt, which the court found was not extinguished by novation (Issue 3). Because the debt remained valid and enforceable, the court's determination that the action was filed within the statutory period (Issue 2) ensured that the plaintiff's recovery of its assets was legally sound. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The judgment of the Commercial High Court is affirmed, and the defendant-appellant is ordered to pay costs fixed at Rs. 50,000/- to the plaintiff-respondent. Keywords Contractual transaction — Potato seedlings supply — Breach of contract — Prescription — Novation — Successor company — Asset recovery

Parties: de Silva and Others v. Mathew and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC, J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Categorization of employees — Salary restructuring — Management Assistants — Middle Management — Junior Management — Promotional prospects — Public Administration Circular — Article 12(1) of the Constitution
Summary: Facts The petitioners are employees of the Industrial Development Board holding the post of Enterprise Promotion Managers, Executive Grade V. They were recruited through an open competitive examination and interview process. Under the promotion scheme existing prior to the impugned circular, these employees were entitled to promotion to Enterprise Promotion Manager, Executive Grade IV upon completing seven years of service. Following the issuance of Public Administration Circular No. 06/2006 and Management Services Circular No. 30, the Salaries and Cadres Commission issued a schedule of grades for the Industrial Development Board. This schedule categorized the petitioners as Management Assistants under salary scale MA 5.2. The petitioners contend that this categorization effectively demoted them from an executive/managerial grade to a non-executive/non-managerial grade, thereby abolishing their promotional prospects. The respondents argued that the categorization was solely for salary restructuring purposes and did not alter the status of the employees. The Industrial Development Board did not file objections and consented to abide by the court's decision. Issues Whether the categorization of the petitioners as Management Assistants under the new salary scheme constitutes a violation of the fundamental right to equality guaranteed under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the abolition of the promotional grade of Enterprise Promotion Manager, Executive Grade IV, constitutes an imminent infringement of the petitioners' rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Public Administration Circular No. 06/2006 Management Services Circular No. 30 Cases Referred to: Gunaratne v. Sri Lanka Telecom - [1993/v1-sri-lr-109] Analysis Issue 1: Categorization as Management Assistants Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution and the definition of "Management Assistant Services" in Clause 3.2 of Annexure II of Management Services Circular No. 30. Court's reasoning: The court rejected the respondents' argument that "MA" was merely a salary code acronym. Based on dictionary definitions, the court determined "MA" is an abbreviation for "Management Assistant." Under Circular No. 30, Management Assistants are defined as those providing supportive services to executive and managerial personnel. By placing the petitioners—who previously held executive status—into this category, the respondents effectively demoted them to a non-executive, supportive role, which is inconsistent with their qualifications and previous duties. Finding: The categorization of the petitioners as Management Assistants is null and void as it violates Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Issue 2: Abolition of Promotional Prospects Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution and the principle established in Gunaratne v. Sri Lanka Telecom. Court's reasoning: The court found that the petitioners had a legitimate expectation of promotion based on the recruitment conditions prevailing at the time of their entry. The abolition of the Enterprise Promotion Manager, Executive Grade IV, removed the next logical step in their career progression. Citing Gunaratne v. Sri Lanka Telecom, the court reasoned that a scheme that negatively affects promotions in an existing service is inherently discriminatory and constitutes an infringement of fundamental rights. Finding: The abolition of the promotional grade and the failure to place the petitioners in Middle or Junior Management categories constitutes a violation of Article 12(1). Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing; the improper categorization of the petitioners as Management Assistants (Issue 1) directly caused the loss of their promotional prospects (Issue 2). Because the court determined the categorization was a substantive demotion rather than a mere salary adjustment, the resulting abolition of promotional rungs was found to be an inherently discriminatory act that violated the petitioners' constitutional rights. Conclusion The court declared that the 1st to 21st respondents violated the petitioners' fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The decision to categorize the petitioners as Management Assistants was declared null and void, and the court directed the respondents to categorize the petitioners as either Middle Management or Junior Management. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Categorization of employees — Salary restructuring — Management Assistants — Middle Management — Junior Management — Promotional prospects — Public Administration Circular — Article 12(1) of the Constitution

Parties: Wajira Prabath Wanasinghe v. Janashakthi Insurance Company Limited
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J.; Sathyaa Hettige, P.C., J.; Eva Wanasundera, P.C., J.
Keywords: leave to appeal — jurisdiction — Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 — interlocutory order — Civil Procedure Code — Notice of Appeal — Petition of Appeal — final appeal — suppression of fact — maintainability — Commercial High Court — preliminary objections — judicial decisions — Sri Lankan law — legal proceedings
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for leave to appeal filed by Wajira Prabath Wanasinghe against the Janashakthi Insurance Company Limited. The Petitioner sought to challenge an order from the Commercial High Court dated 13th July 2012, which dismissed his action on the grounds of lack of jurisdiction, citing Section 5 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. The Petitioner contended that the Commercial High Court had no authority to hear the matter due to the existence of an arbitration agreement between the parties. The Respondent raised preliminary objections, arguing that the Petitioner had previously invoked the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court through a final appeal and that the impugned order could only be contested via a final appeal, not through an application for leave to appeal. The Supreme Court had to first address these preliminary objections before considering the merits of the leave to appeal application. Issues Whether the Petitioner suppressed the fact of prior invocation of the Supreme Court's jurisdiction. Whether the impugned order of the Commercial High Court can only be canvassed by way of a final appeal. Applicable Law Statutes: Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995; Civil Procedure Code; High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996. Cases Referred to: Wickremasinghev v. De Silva - [1978/79] 2 SLR 65; Siriwardena v. Air Ceylon Ltd. - [1984] 1 Sri LR 286; Ranjit v. Kusumawathie - [1998] 3 Sri LR 232; Rajendran Chettiar and Two Others v. S. Narayanan Chettiar - 2011 BALJR 25. Analysis Issue 1: Suppression of Fact Rule applied: The principle of full disclosure in legal proceedings. Court's reasoning: The Respondent's first preliminary objection was based on the assertion that the Petitioner had suppressed the fact that he had previously invoked the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court by way of a final appeal. The Respondent claimed that the Petitioner had filed a Notice of Appeal on 23rd July 2012 and a Petition of Appeal on 10th September 2012, thus invoking the Supreme Court's jurisdiction. However, the Court noted that the Petitioner had explicitly stated in his application that he had not previously invoked the Court's jurisdiction. The Court examined the timeline of events and found that while the Notice of Appeal was indeed lodged, it did not constitute a valid invocation of the Supreme Court's jurisdiction until the Petition of Appeal was filed. The Court emphasized that the mere lodging of a Notice of Appeal does not invoke the appellate court's jurisdiction unless all procedural requirements are met. The Court referenced Wickremasinghev v. De Silva, where it was held that non-compliance with mandatory requirements results in the rejection of the Petition of Appeal. The Court concluded that since the Petition of Appeal was filed after the leave to appeal application, there was no suppression of fact by the Petitioner. Finding: The preliminary objection regarding suppression of fact was overruled. Issue 2: Impugned Order Capable of Being Canvassed Only by Way of Final Appeal Rule applied: Distinction between final and interlocutory orders. Court's reasoning: The second preliminary objection raised by the Respondent was that the impugned order could only be challenged through a final appeal, not through an application for leave to appeal. The Court analyzed the nature of the order issued by the Commercial High Court, which dismissed the action based on jurisdictional grounds. The Court referred to the definitions of final and interlocutory orders, citing various judicial decisions, including those from England and Sri Lanka. The Court noted the conflicting opinions on what constitutes a final order versus an interlocutory order. Ultimately, the Court adopted the approach of Lord Esher in Bozson v. Altrincham Urban District Council, which posits that the determination of whether an order is final or interlocutory depends on whether it disposes of the rights of the parties. The Court concluded that since the Commercial High Court's order effectively ended the action, it was an interlocutory order, and thus the application for leave to appeal was maintainable. Finding: The preliminary objection regarding the nature of the impugned order was rejected. Interaction of Issues The two issues are interrelated as they both concern the procedural aspects of the appeal process. The first issue addresses whether the Petitioner had previously invoked the Supreme Court's jurisdiction, which would affect the maintainability of the leave to appeal application. The second issue examines the nature of the impugned order and whether it can be challenged through an application for leave to appeal. Both issues highlight the importance of adhering to procedural rules and the implications of jurisdictional challenges in legal proceedings. Conclusion The Supreme Court overruled both preliminary objections raised by the Respondent, allowing the application for leave to appeal to proceed. The Court directed that the application be re-fixed for support on 28th April 2014, without making any order for costs. The judgment underscores the significance of procedural compliance and the distinction between final and interlocutory orders in the context of appeals in Sri Lankan law.
Counsel: Harsha Amarasekera for the Plaintiff-Petitioner. Nigel Hatch, P.C. with Ms. P. Abeywickrama and Ms. S. Illangage for the Defendant-Respondent.

Parties: Kumarasiri and Others v. Bandara and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, J.; K. Sripavan, J.; B. Aluwihare, P.C., J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Public Service Commission — Selection criteria — Gazette Notification — Special Cadre — Information Technology — Mathematics — Special Education — Administrative action — Judicial review
Summary: Facts The Petitioners applied for the Open Competitive Examination for recruitment to Class III of the Sri Lanka Educational Administrative Service (SLEAS) pursuant to a Gazette Notification. The examination consisted of two parts, and candidates were required to have studied and passed subjects relevant to their chosen field of the Special Cadre at the degree level. Following the examination, the Petitioners were called for interviews where they were informed of an addendum to the original Gazette Notification that introduced specific degree and diploma requirements for the fields of Special Education and Planning. The Petitioners were subsequently denied appointment to the Special Cadre on the grounds that they either lacked the requisite academic qualifications in their chosen fields or failed to meet the necessary cut-off marks. The Petitioners challenged these actions, alleging arbitrary changes to the selection criteria and discriminatory non-selection. Issues Whether the challenge to the addendum (P6) is time-barred under Article 126(2) of the Constitution. Whether the non-appointment of the 1st and 3rd Petitioners to the SLEAS violated their fundamental right to equality under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the non-appointment of the 2nd and 4th through 16th Petitioners was based on reasonable criteria and sufficient evidence regarding their academic qualifications and examination marks. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 126(1) Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 126(2) Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Time Bar Rule applied: Article 126(2) of the Constitution, which imposes a one-month time limit for invoking the fundamental rights jurisdiction of the Supreme Court. Court's reasoning: The Petitioners became aware of the addendum (P6) during their interviews in September 2008. By participating in the interview process without protest and waiting seven months to file their application, the Petitioners failed to invoke the court's jurisdiction promptly. Finding: The challenge to the addendum (P6) is time-barred and cannot be entertained. Issue 2: Equality of 1st and 3rd Petitioners Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution (Right to Equality). Court's reasoning: The Public Service Commission conceded that the 3rd Petitionerâ€™s degree was relevant to his field. The 1st Petitioner, who scored 254 marks (above the cut-off of 242), possessed relevant degree subjects. Denying her appointment while accepting the 3rd Petitioner, despite similar qualifications, constitutes a violation of the principle of equal protection of the law. Finding: The non-appointment of the 1st and 3rd Petitioners violated Article 12(1). Issue 3: Non-appointment of other Petitioners Rule applied: The requirement in the Gazette Notification (P1) that candidates must have studied and passed subjects relevant to the field applied for at the degree level. Court's reasoning: The court reviewed the academic records and marks of the remaining Petitioners. It found that they either failed to meet the required cut-off marks for their chosen fields or lacked the necessary academic background in the subjects applied for. The court held that it should not substitute its own view for that of the Public Service Commission unless the decision is shown to be grossly unreasonable or mala fide. Finding: The non-appointment of the 2nd and 4th through 16th Petitioners was not unreasonable or unjust. Interaction of Issues The court's finding that the challenge to the addendum was time-barred limited the scope of review to the original Gazette Notification (P1). Within that framework, the court determined that while most Petitioners failed to meet the objective criteria of P1, the 1st and 3rd Petitioners were treated inconsistently, necessitating a remedy under Article 12(1) to ensure fairness and equality. Conclusion The court declares that the fundamental rights of the 1st and 3rd Petitioners were violated and directs the Public Service Commission to appoint them to Class III of the SLEAS with full seniority and benefits. The applications of the 2nd and 4th through 16th Petitioners are dismissed. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Public Service Commission — Selection criteria — Gazette Notification — Special Cadre — Information Technology — Mathematics — Special Education — Administrative action — Judicial review

Parties: Ambewela Livestock Co. Ltd. v. Sri Lanka Co-operative Marketing Federation Ltd. Markfed
Bench: Thilakawardena, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Sathyaa Hettige, PC, J.
Keywords: contract law — novation — prescription — unjust enrichment — liability — evidence — commercial transactions — legal obligations — state corporations — debt recovery
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal from the judgment of the Commercial High Court of Colombo, where the plaintiff, Ambewela Livestock Co. Ltd., sought to recover a sum of Rs. 4,694,000 from the defendant, Sri Lanka Co-operative Marketing Federation Ltd. Markfed. The plaintiff, incorporated on 19 July 1999, succeeded the business of Ambewela Farm, previously managed by the National Livestock Development Board (NLDB). The plaintiff's claim was based on a contract for the supply of 100 Metric Tons of potato seedlings, which the defendant had requested and for which it had agreed to pay Rs. 7 million. The plaintiff delivered the seedlings, but the defendant only paid Rs. 2,307,810, leaving a balance of Rs. 4,694,000 unpaid. The defendant denied the existence of the contract and claimed that the action was prescribed. The Commercial High Court ruled in favor of the plaintiff, leading to the defendant's appeal. Issues The primary issues raised in this case include: Whether a valid contract existed between the plaintiff and the defendant for the sale of potato seedlings? Whether the plaintiff's claim was prescribed under the Prescription Ordinance? Whether there was a novation of the contract that would discharge the defendant from its obligations? Applicable Law Statutes: State Agriculture Corporations Act No. 11 of 1972; Prescription Ordinance. Cases Referred to: Muthukuda v. Sumanawathie (1962) 65 NLR 205; Noorbhai v. Karuppan Chetty (1925) 27 NLR 325; Karthikesu v. Ponnachchy 14 NLR 486. Analysis Issue 1: Existence of Contract Rule applied: The essentials of a valid contract. Court's reasoning: The court examined the evidence presented, including letters and a promissory note, which indicated a clear agreement between the parties. The defendant's letters acknowledged the request for seedlings and the obligation to pay, demonstrating mutual assent. The court emphasized that a contract requires an offer and acceptance, and the evidence showed that the defendant had accepted the terms of the sale. The court cited C.G. Weeramantry's work on contract law, highlighting that the intention to create a legal obligation was evident from the correspondence. The court concluded that the documents presented by the plaintiff constituted a valid contract, as they reflected a consensus ad idem between the parties. Finding: The court found that a valid contract existed between the plaintiff and the defendant for the sale of the seedlings. Issue 2: Prescription of Claim Rule applied: Prescription Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The defendant argued that the plaintiff's claim was prescribed, asserting that the action should be dismissed. However, the court noted that the plaintiff had been a government institution until 2001, and thus, the prescription period did not apply until it became a private company. The plaintiff filed the action within two years of becoming a private entity, and the court found that the claim was timely. The court referenced the relevant provisions of the Prescription Ordinance, confirming that the plaintiff's claim was not barred by prescription. Finding: The court concluded that the plaintiff's claim was not prescribed. Issue 3: Novation of Contract Rule applied: Definition and requirements of novation. Court's reasoning: The defendant contended that there was a novation of the contract, which would discharge its obligations. The court examined the definition of novation and found that the defendant had failed to provide evidence of a new agreement or the substitution of parties. The court noted that the defendant's position was contradictory, as it denied the existence of a contract while simultaneously claiming that the contract had been novated. The court emphasized that for novation to occur, there must be mutual consent to replace the original obligation, which was not established in this case. The court found that the plaintiff had consistently asserted its claim for the debt, indicating no intention to discharge the defendant's obligations. Finding: The court determined that there was no novation of the contract, and the defendant remained liable for the debt. Interaction of Issues The issues of contract existence, prescription, and novation are interrelated. The determination of whether a valid contract existed directly impacts the prescription argument, as a valid contract would allow the plaintiff to pursue its claim within the appropriate time frame. Additionally, the novation argument hinges on the existence of the original contract; without a valid contract, the claim of novation fails. The court's analysis of these issues demonstrated a comprehensive understanding of contract law and the principles governing obligations and liabilities. Conclusion The Supreme Court upheld the Commercial High Court's judgment, affirming that the plaintiff was entitled to recover the sum of Rs. 4,694,000 from the defendant. The appeal was dismissed with costs fixed at Rs. 50,000, reinforcing the defendant's liability for the debt owed to the plaintiff. The court's decision clarified the legal principles surrounding contract formation, prescription, and novation, providing a detailed analysis of the evidence and legal standards applicable to the case. Keywords contract law — novation — prescription — unjust enrichment — liability — evidence — commercial transactions — legal obligations — state corporations — debt recovery
Counsel: Harsha Soza, PC with Athula Perera for Defendant-Appellant. V.K. Choksy for Plaintiff-Respondent.

Parties: Navaratnarajavel and Kangadevi v. Naganathan and Others
Bench: Mohan Pieris, J; Sathyaa Hettige, J; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Thesawalamai Pre-Emption Ordinance — Right of pre-emption — Undivided share — Sole ownership — Co-ownership — Amicable partition — Conveyance of land — Deeds of gift — Survey plans — Dominium
Summary: Facts The Appellants instituted an action in the District Court of Mallakam to enforce a right of pre-emption under the Thesawalamai Pre-emption Ordinance following the sale of land by the 1st and 2nd Respondents to the 3rd, 4th, and 5th Respondents. The 2nd Appellant was the original owner of the land described as Item 4 in the schedule of a deed dated 19.3.1983, which contained a condition requiring her to transfer the southern half of said land to her sister, the 2nd Respondent, upon the latter reaching the age of majority. The 2nd Appellant subsequently transferred the southern half of the property to the 2nd Respondent via a dowry deed dated 29.08.1988. This deed defined the boundaries and extent of the southern portion, which was thereafter registered in a separate folio at the Land Registry. Following this transfer, the 2nd Respondent exercised exclusive control over the southern portion, including leasing it to third parties, with all subsequent transactions registered in the new folio. In 2004, the 1st and 2nd Respondents sold this southern portion to the 3rd, 4th, and 5th Respondents. The Appellants challenged this sale, asserting a right of pre-emption on the basis that the land remained co-owned. The Civil Appellate High Court ruled against the Appellants, leading to the present appeal. Issues Whether the Southern part of the land identified as item 4 in the Schedule to the Plaint was a divided allotment of land. Whether the Appellants possessed a right of pre-emption under Section 2(1) of the Thesawalamai Pre-emption Ordinance No. 59 of 1947 regarding the sale of the property. Applicable Law Statutes: Thesawalamai Pre-emption Ordinance No. 59 of 1947 Cases Referred to: Githohamy v. Karanagoda - [56-nlr-250] Nagaratnam v. Sunmugam - [69-nlr-389] Sivagurunathan v. Visaladchi - [56-nlr-376] Analysis Issue 1: Status of the land as divided Rule applied: The court applied the principle that co-ownership is terminated when shareholders amicably agree to possess separate parcels of land, even in the absence of a formal plan or deed of partition. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the 1988 dowry deed specifically defined the boundaries and extent of the southern portion. The registration of this portion in a separate folio, coupled with the 2nd Respondent's subsequent exclusive acts of ownership—such as leasing the land to third parties—demonstrated that the parties intended to and did effectively divide the land. The court held that the lack of a formal survey plan at the time of the initial division did not negate the reality of the partition, as the parties clearly understood and accepted their respective portions. Finding: The southern portion was a divided allotment of land, and co-ownership had ceased to exist as of 1988. Issue 2: Right of pre-emption Rule applied: Section 2(1) and Section 4 of the Thesawalamai Pre-emption Ordinance No. 59 of 1947. Court's reasoning: Section 4 stipulates that the right of pre-emption applies only to undivided shares and is not permitted where property is held in sole ownership. Since the court determined that the land was divided and held in sole ownership by the 2nd Respondent, the statutory requirements for pre-emption were not met. The court noted that for pre-emption to apply, a co-owner must be able to exercise plenum dominium over the entirety of the common property, which was not the case here. Finding: The Appellants did not have a right to exercise pre-emption against the 2nd Respondent. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the determination that the land was legally divided (Issue 1) directly precluded the existence of the co-ownership required to trigger the statutory right of pre-emption (Issue 2). Because the land was held in sole ownership, the legal basis for the Appellants' claim under the Ordinance was extinguished. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed and the judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court of the Northern Province is confirmed. No costs are ordered. Keywords Thesawalamai Pre-Emption Ordinance — Right of pre-emption — Undivided share — Sole ownership — Co-ownership — Amicable partition — Conveyance of land — Deeds of gift — Survey plans — Dominium

Parties: Haniffa and Others v. Mohamed Idroos
Bench: Mohan Pieris, PC.C.J; Sathyaa Hettige, PC.J; Eva Wanasundera, PC.J
Keywords: Re-listing application — Registered Attorney — District Court — Court of Appeal — Civil Procedure Code — Final appeal — Proxy — Leave to Appeal application — Revision application — Attorney-at-Law on record
Summary: Facts The subject matter of this litigation was land, with proceedings originating in the District Court of Kalmunai in 1982. Following a judgment in favor of the Plaintiff on 18.11.1998, the Defendant appealed to the Court of Appeal. On 15.10.2009, the Court of Appeal affirmed the District Court judgment, having heard the matter on its merits despite the absence of the Defendant-Appellant. The Fiscal subsequently executed a writ, and the Plaintiff took possession of the land. Approximately six months after the Court of Appeal judgment, the Defendant-Appellant filed a re-listing application in the Court of Appeal seeking to have the appeal reheard. The Court of Appeal dismissed this application on 23.02.2011, ruling that such an application must be made by the registered Attorney-at-Law on record and could not be filed by a different Attorney-at-Law. The Petitioners, being the substituted defendants, appealed this order to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Court of Appeal erred in law by deciding that a re-listing application for a final appeal must be made exclusively by the registered Attorney-at-Law on record in the District Court. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 756(4) Administration of Justice Law No. 44 of 1973 Cases Referred to: Jeevani Investments (Pvt) Ltd. v. Wijesena Perera - [2008/v1-sri-lr-207] Saravanapavan v. Kandasamydurai - [1984/v1-sri-lr-268] Wace v. Angage Helena Hami - [1881/v4-scc-48] Romanis Baas v. Revenna Kader Mohideen - [1881/v4-scc-61] Gunasekera v. De Zoysa - [52-nlr-357] Analysis Issue 1: Authority to file a re-listing application Rule applied: The court applied the principle that a party cannot change their instructing attorney without leave of court and that the registered attorney in the original court must remain the attorney-at-law for all subsequent steps in the proceedings, unless the matter originates in the appellate court. Court's reasoning: The court distinguished between proceedings that originate in the appellate court (such as Leave to Appeal or Revision applications) and those that are a continuation of the original District Court proceedings (such as a Final Appeal and its ancillary re-listing application). The court reasoned that because a re-listing application is directly connected to the final appeal, it is not a separate mechanism but a continuation of the original case. Therefore, it must be signed by the registered attorney on record. The court rejected the rationale in Jeevani Investments (Pvt) Ltd. v. Wijesena Perera, noting that a re-listing application has a definite bearing on the original court proceedings, unlike Revision or Leave to Appeal applications, which are independent of the original court's record. Finding: The court held that a re-listing application of a final appeal must be made by the registered attorney on record in the District Court. Conclusion The court answered the question of law in the negative, affirming the order of the Court of Appeal. The appeal is dismissed with costs fixed at Rs. 10,000/- to be paid by the Petitioners to the Respondent. Keywords Re-listing application — Registered Attorney — District Court — Court of Appeal — Civil Procedure Code — Final appeal — Proxy — Leave to Appeal application — Revision application — Attorney-at-Law on record

Parties: Dissanayake and Another v. Officer-in-Charge Police Station, Warakapola and Another
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Plea of alibi — Ellenborough dictum — Strong prima facie case — Reasonable doubt — False implication — Evidence evaluation — Criminal trial — Conviction — Sentence
Summary: Facts The Accused-Appellants, Dissanayake Rallage Ranasingha Karuppattiya and Dissanayake Rallage Wijesinghe, were charged alongside Dissanayake Rallage Amarasinghe in the Magistrate Court of Warakapola. The charges arose from an incident on 7th December 2003, involving allegations of causing grievous hurt with a sword, causing hurt with a club, stabbing, and robbery of a gold chain from the complainants, Lal Anura Kumara and A.R. Rathnasena. The prosecution evidence relied on the testimony of the two injured brothers, who alleged that the accused attacked them near the house of the second accused, Amarasinghe. The prosecution did not call Susantha Pieris, who was present at the scene, arguing that his criminal disposition rendered his testimony unnecessary. During the trial, the second accused, Amarasinghe, passed away. The first and third accused testified under oath, asserting they were not present at the scene and were being falsely implicated due to their familial relationship with the deceased second accused. This alibi was supported by the testimony of the wife of the second accused. The Magistrate convicted the first and third accused on all counts, and the High Court subsequently affirmed these convictions and sentences. The accused then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the learned Magistrate and the High Court failed to properly evaluate the evidence and the plea of alibi raised by the first and third accused. Whether the trial court and the High Court erroneously applied the Ellenborough dictum in the absence of a strong prima facie case against the accused. Applicable Law Statutes: Penal Code Evidence Ordinance Cases Referred to: Walimunage John v. The State - [76-nlr-488] Yahonis Singho v. Queen - [67-nlr-8] Chandradasa v. Queen - [72-nlr-160] Punchi Banda v. State - [76-nlr-293] Rex v. Cochrane - [Garneys Reports, page 479] The King v. L. Seeder de Silva - [41-nlr-1] The King v. Geekiyanage John Silva - [46-nlr-73] Queen v. Seetin - [68-nlr-316] Republic v. Illangathilaka - [1984/v2-sri-lr-1] James Silva v. The Republic of Sri Lanka - [1980/v2-sri-lr-167] Jayasena v. The Queen - [72-nlr-313] Analysis Issue 1: Evaluation of evidence and alibi Rule applied: The principles established in Yahonis Singho v. Queen, Chandradasa v. Queen, and Punchi Banda v. State regarding the evaluation of an alibi. Court's reasoning: The court noted that if an alibi is true, the accused must be acquitted; if it is probably true or creates a reasonable doubt, the accused is entitled to an acquittal. The court found that the prosecution's case contained significant infirmities, including the failure of one witness to identify the first accused. The court determined that the trial judge failed to properly evaluate the alibi evidence, which, when considered alongside the possibility of false implication due to the second accused's involvement, created a reasonable doubt. Finding: The evidence of the accused raised a reasonable doubt, necessitating an acquittal. Issue 2: Application of the Ellenborough dictum Rule applied: The dictum in Rex v. Cochrane, which permits an adverse inference only when a strong prima facie case has been established. Court's reasoning: The court held that the Ellenborough dictum is only applicable where the prosecution has established a strong prima facie case. In this instance, the court found that no such strong case existed against the first and third accused. Therefore, the reliance on this dictum by the lower courts to draw adverse inferences against the accused was unwarranted and erroneous. Finding: The application of the Ellenborough dictum was improper. Interaction of Issues The courtâ€™s finding that the prosecution failed to establish a strong prima facie case (Issue 2) directly undermined the lower court's reliance on the Ellenborough dictum. This, combined with the failure to properly weigh the alibi evidence (Issue 1), meant that the conviction could not be sustained as the prosecution failed to prove its case beyond a reasonable doubt. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The convictions and sentences imposed on the first and third accused are quashed, and they are acquitted of all charges. Keywords Plea of alibi — Ellenborough dictum — Strong prima facie case — Reasonable doubt — False implication — Evidence evaluation — Criminal trial — Conviction — Sentence

Parties: Jayathilaka v. Peoplesâ€™ Bank and Others
Bench: Tilkawardane, J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Industrial Disputes Act — Time limit for applications — Amendment of Act — Retrospective effect — Labour Tribunal — High Court — Revision Application — Exceptional circumstances — Section 31B(7) — Termination of services — Effective date of termination
Summary: Facts The Applicant-Petitioner-Appellant filed an application in the Labour Tribunal under section 31B of the Industrial Disputes Act alleging that his services were terminated unlawfully and unjustly by the Respondent. The Respondent raised a preliminary objection that the application was time-barred. The President of the Labour Tribunal upheld the objection and dismissed the application. The Applicant subsequently filed a revision application in the High Court of the Western Province. The Respondent raised several preliminary objections, including that the application was bad in law for citing the incorrect section, failing to annex material documents, failing to file an appeal, and failing to establish exceptional circumstances. The High Court affirmed the order of the Labour Tribunal, holding that the application was filed out of time. The Applicant then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the revisionary jurisdiction of the Provincial High Court was properly invoked by the Petitioner despite the citation of an incorrect section and the availability of an alternative remedy. Whether the High Court erred in law by failing to apply the Industrial Disputes (Amendment) Act No. 21 of 2008, which extended the time limit for filing applications to the Labour Tribunal to six months. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act No. 43 of 1950 Industrial Disputes (Amendment) Act No. 32 of 1990 Industrial Disputes (Amendment) Act No. 21 of 2008 Industrial Disputes (Special Provisions) Act No. 53 of 1973 Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka Cases Referred to: Peiris v. Commissioner of Inland Revenue - [65-nlr-457] Kumarathunge v. Samarasinghe - [1983/v2-sri-lr-63] Edirisuriya v. Navarathnam - [1985/v1-sri-lr-100] Ranjith v. Kusumawathi - [1998/v3-sri-lr-232] Rustom v. Hapangama - [1978-79/v2-sri-lr-225] Rasheed Ali v. Mohamad Ali - [1988/v1-sri-lr-262] Ameed v. Rasheed - [6-clw-8] Alima Natchair v. Marikar - [47-nlr-81] Ceylon Electricity Board v. Ranjith Fonseka - [2008/balr-part-ii] Gamaethige v. Siriwardena - [1988/v1-sri-lr-384] De Silva v. Wickramarathne - [2011/v2-blr-360] Attorney General v. Uplands Bus Company Ltd - [56-nlr-248] Hadji Omar v. Bodhidasa - [1994/v2-sri-lr-191] De Silva v. Weerasinghe - [1978-79-80/v1-sri-lr-334] Analysis Issue 1: Invocation of Revisionary Jurisdiction Rule applied: The principle that an exercise of power is referable to a jurisdiction that confers validity upon it, and the court's discretionary power to act in revision despite the availability of an appeal in cases of public importance. Court's reasoning: The court held that citing the wrong section in the caption was a curable defect because the application was filed in the correct forum under the correct Act. Regarding the alternative remedy, the court noted that the Labour Tribunal's order was a preliminary order rather than a final order, making revision an appropriate mechanism. Furthermore, because the case involved an important question of law regarding statutory interpretation, the court exercised its discretion to entertain the revision application. Finding: The revisionary jurisdiction was properly invoked. Issue 2: Application of the Amending Act Rule applied: The principle that statutes of limitation are procedural and the law in force at the date of the institution of the suit applies, provided the action was not already time-barred under the previous law. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the effective date of termination was the date the Applicant received the notice, not the date on the letter. Since the application was not time-barred under the three-month limit when the Amendment Act No. 21 of 2008 came into force, the Applicant was entitled to the benefit of the extended six-month period. Finding: The High Court erred; the application was not time-barred. Interaction of Issues The resolution of the first issue allowed the court to reach the merits of the second issue. By determining that the revisionary jurisdiction was properly invoked, the court was able to correct the High Court's erroneous interpretation of the limitation period, thereby ensuring that the Applicant's substantive right to seek relief was not forfeited due to a procedural misapplication of the law. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgments of the High Court and the Labour Tribunal are set aside, and the Labour Tribunal is directed to proceed with the inquiry under section 31C of the Industrial Disputes Act. Keywords Industrial Disputes Act — Time limit for applications — Amendment of Act — Retrospective effect — Labour Tribunal — High Court — Revision Application — Exceptional circumstances — Section 31B(7) — Termination of services — Effective date of termination

Parties: Galagamage Indrawansa Kumarasiri and Others v. W.M.M. Kumarihami and Others
Bench: Tilakawardane, J.; Ekanayake, J.; Sathyaa Hettige, P.C., J.; Priyasath Dep, P.C., J.; Wanasundera, P.C., J.
Keywords: Conspiracy — Abduction — Murder — Penal Code — Fair Trial — Evidence — Witness Credibility — Dock Statement — Procedural Irregularity — Criminal Procedure Code
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal by four accused individuals, Galagamage Indrawansa Kumarasiri, Thumbedura Vitty Newton, Jayaratnage Dhammika Nihal Jayaratne, and Godapitiyawatte Arachchilage Janapriya Senaratne, who were convicted by a Trial-at-Bar for conspiracy to abduct and murder two individuals, Muthuthanthri Bastiange Dinesh Tharanga Fernando and Goniamalimage Dhanushka Udayakantha Aponso. The events leading to their conviction began on the night of 12 August 2009, when a complaint was lodged at the Angulana Police Station regarding two individuals allegedly posing as army officers. The accused, who were police officers and a home guard, were involved in the subsequent actions that led to the abduction and murder of the two youths. The prosecution's case was built on the testimonies of several police officers who were present during the events, detailing how the accused conspired to abduct the victims, assaulted them, and ultimately shot them. The accused raised multiple legal issues regarding their conviction, including claims of procedural irregularities, lack of fair trial, and the admissibility of evidence. Issues The primary issues raised in the appeal included: Whether the necessary matters were considered in the conviction of the accused on the charges based on conspiracy and common intention? Whether the first accused was deprived of a fair trial due to the glossing over of favorable matters by the Trial-at-Bar judges? Whether the unsworn dock statement of a co-accused was impermissibly allowed as evidence against another accused? Applicable Law Statutes: Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979 as amended by Act No. 21 of 1988; Penal Code. Cases Referred to: Abdul Sammen v. The Bribery Commissioner (1991) (1 SLR 76); King v. Pieris Appuhamy (1942) (43 NLR 412); The Queen v. Ariyawantha (1957) (59 NLR 241); Prabath de Saram v. Republic of Sri Lanka (2002); The Queen v. Buddhrakkita Thera and 2 Others (1962) (63 NLR 433). Analysis Issue 1: Procedural Irregularity Rule applied: Section 450(4) of the Code of Criminal Procedure. Court's reasoning: The court examined the argument that the indictment was signed by the Registrar of the High Court rather than the Attorney General, which the defense claimed vitiated the trial. The court noted that the Attorney General had exhibited information against the accused under the relevant section of the Code, and the Registrar's signing was in accordance with the court's direction. The court found that the procedure followed was lawful and did not constitute a ground for vitiating the conviction. Finding: The court upheld the conviction, affirming that the procedural requirements were met. Issue 2: Fair Trial Rule applied: Right to a fair trial. Court's reasoning: The accused argued that their trial was unfair due to the failure of the Trial-at-Bar judges to consider evidence favorable to them and the lack of calling crucial witnesses. The court analyzed the testimonies of the prosecution witnesses and the circumstances under which they were taken. It concluded that the witnesses were credible and their testimonies corroborated each other, despite the defense's claims of inconsistencies. Finding: The court found no violation of the right to a fair trial. Issue 3: Admissibility of Dock Statements Rule applied: Evidence Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The defense contended that the unsworn dock statements made by the accused were improperly used against each other. The court clarified that while dock statements are considered evidence, they are subject to the infirmity of not being given under oath. The court evaluated the content of the dock statements and found them lacking in credibility, particularly due to evasive language and contradictions with other evidence. Finding: The court ruled that the dock statements were inadmissible as evidence against the other accused. Interaction of Issues The issues of procedural irregularity, fair trial, and the admissibility of evidence were interrelated, as they all pertained to the integrity of the trial process and the rights of the accused. The court's analysis demonstrated that despite the defense's claims, the trial was conducted in accordance with legal standards, and the evidence presented was sufficient to uphold the convictions. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal, affirming the convictions of the accused for conspiracy to abduct and murder. The court found that the prosecution had established its case beyond a reasonable doubt, and the procedural and evidentiary challenges raised by the defense were without merit. The court's decision underscored the importance of maintaining the rule of law and ensuring accountability for serious criminal offenses. Keywords Conspiracy — Abduction — Murder — Penal Code — Fair Trial — Evidence — Witness Credibility — Dock Statement — Procedural Irregularity — Criminal Procedure Code
Counsel: Anil Silva P.C., with Lasith Muhandiramge and Manoj Nanayakkara for the 1st Accused-Appellant. Saliya Pieris with Upul Kumarapperuma, Suranga Munasinghe, Thanuka Nandasiri and Silyna Pieris for the 2nd Accused-Appellant. D.P. Kumarasinghe P.C., with M.A. Kumarasinghe, and Vajira Ranasinghe for the 3rd Accused-Appellant. Amila Palliyage with Eranda Sinharage for the 4th Accused-Appellant. Jayantha Jayasuriya P.C., ASG, with Chaminda Atukorale SC, for the Attorney General.

Parties: Warnakulasuriyage Charlert, Kusumawathi Kulasuriya v. Gunathilaka
Bench: Shiranee Tilakawardane, J; Sathyaa Hettige, PC, J; Marasinghe, J
Keywords: Constructive trust — Section 83 of the Trusts Ordinance — Section 92 of the Evidence Ordinance — Bona fide purchaser — Attendant circumstances — Parole evidence — Section 65(1) of the Trusts Ordinance — Section 66(1) of the Trusts Ordinance — Res judicata
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Warnakulasuriyage Charlert Kusumawathi Kulasuriya, and another party transferred a block of land to one Gamini Sarathchandra Mohotti via Deed No. 7948 dated 15.07.1987 for a consideration of Rs. 10,000. Subsequently, Mohotti transferred the same property to the Respondent, Mastiyage Don Wimal Harischandra Gunathilake, via Deed No. 10944 dated 12.09.1990 for Rs. 40,000. The Petitioner later entered the premises, removed part of a wire fence, and began constructing a structure. The Respondent filed action in the District Court of Panadura (Case No. 1290/L) seeking a declaration of title and an order of ejectment. The Petitioner contended that the original transfer to Mohotti was not an absolute sale but a security for a loan, and that Mohotti held the property in a constructive trust for her. She alleged that the subsequent transfer to the Respondent was fraudulent. The District Court dismissed the Respondent's action, but the High Court of Civil Appeals in Kalutara allowed the Respondent's appeal. The Petitioner then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court erred in finding that no constructive trust was created by the transaction in Deed No. 7948 under Section 83 of the Trusts Ordinance. Whether the Respondent is a bona fide purchaser for value without notice, thereby precluding the Petitioner from claiming the property even if a constructive trust existed. Whether the dismissal of the Petitioner's earlier action in District Court of Panadura Case No. 551/L operates as res judicata against the Petitioner. Applicable Law Statutes: Evidence Ordinance, Section 92 Trusts Ordinance, Section 3(j), Section 65(1), Section 66(1), Section 83, Section 98 Cases Referred to: Perera v. Fernando - [17-nlr-486] Somasunderam Chetty v. Todd - [1-pronchihamy-v-don-davith] Dayawathie v. Gunasekara - [1991/v1-sri-lr-115] Muttammah v. Thiyagaraja - [62-nlr-559] Ehiya Lebbe v. Majeed - [48-nlr-357] Carthelis v. Ranasinghe - [2002/v2-sri-lr-359] Thisa Nona and Three Others v. Premadasa - [1997/v1-sri-lr-169] Carthelis v. Perera - [32-nlr-19] Premawathi v. Gnanawathi - [1994/v2-sri-lr-171] Chulalankara Thero v. Lavendris and Others - [1981/v1-sri-lr-226] M. Kandavanam v. V. Kandaswamy - [58-nlr-413] Pannaloka Thero v. Saranankara Thero - [1983/v2-sri-lr-523] Keokuk Ry. Co. v. Donnell - [77-iowa-221] Karunaratna v. Amarisa - [66-nlr-567] Ibrahim Baay v. Abdul Rahim - [12-nlr-177] Fernando v. Menika - [3-bal-115] Annamalai Chetty v. Thornhill - [34-nlr-381] Sivakolunthu v. Kamalambal - [56-nlr-52] Dingiri Menika v. Punchi Mahatmaya - [13-nlr-59] Dingiri Amma v. Appuhamy - [72-nlr-347] Jayawardene v. Arnolishamy - [69-nlr-497] Analysis Issue 1: Existence of a Constructive Trust Rule applied: Section 83 of the Trusts Ordinance and Section 92 of the Evidence Ordinance. Court's reasoning: While Section 92 of the Evidence Ordinance generally prohibits oral evidence to contradict a deed, Section 83 of the Trusts Ordinance allows for the consideration of "attendant circumstances" to determine if a transferor intended to pass the beneficial interest. The Court examined factors such as the adequacy of consideration, continued possession, and the existence of non-notarial agreements. The Court found the consideration of Rs. 10,000 was not grossly inadequate for the 15.95 perches. Furthermore, the Petitioner failed to produce the Notary or witnesses to substantiate the loan claim, and her evidence of continued possession was inconsistent and contradicted by the Respondent's erection of a fence. Finding: No constructive trust was established. Issue 2: Bona Fide Purchaser Rule applied: Section 66(1)(a) and Section 98 of the Trusts Ordinance. Court's reasoning: Even if a trust existed, the Respondent purchased the property for Rs. 40,000. There was no evidence that the Respondent had actual or constructive notice of the alleged agreement between the Petitioner and Mohotti. The Deed of Transfer appeared to be a genuine sale, and a title search would not have revealed any encumbrances or conditions. Finding: The Respondent is a bona fide purchaser for value without notice, and equity cannot intervene to divest his title. Issue 3: Res Judicata Rule applied: The doctrine of res judicata as summarized in Karunaratna v. Amarisa. Court's reasoning: The previous action (Case No. 551/L) was dismissed due to the Petitioner's failure to pay costs, not upon an adjudication of the merits of the claim. As there was no judicial determination of the rights in dispute, the doctrine does not apply. Finding: The plea of res judicata is rejected. Interaction of Issues The failure to establish a constructive trust (Issue 1) is the primary finding. However, the Court's determination that the Respondent is a bona fide purchaser (Issue 2) serves as an independent, alternative ground for dismissing the Petitioner's claim, ensuring that even if the trust had been proven, the Petitioner would still not be entitled to the property. The rejection of res judicata (Issue 3) confirms that the Court decided the case on its substantive merits rather than procedural bars. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed, and the judgment of the High Court is affirmed. The Respondent is awarded costs in the sum of Rs. 25,000. Keywords Constructive trust — Section 83 of the Trusts Ordinance — Section 92 of the Evidence Ordinance — Bona fide purchaser — Attendant circumstances — Parole evidence — Section 65(1) of the Trusts Ordinance — Section 66(1) of the Trusts Ordinance — Res judicata

Parties: Dayawathie and Dayarathne v. Sirisena
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Notarial attestation — Duress — Fraud — Evidence Ordinance — Section 92 — Invalidation of documents — Burden of proof — Trial judge's evaluation of evidence
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent instituted an action in the District Court of Mount Lavinia seeking a declaration of title to a property, an order for the ejectment of the Defendants-Appellants-Appellants, and monthly damages. The Plaintiff claimed ownership based on a deed of transfer executed by the Defendants. The Defendants admitted to signing the deed but contended that the execution was obtained under duress. They alleged that the Plaintiff, accompanied by others, threatened them with death and forced them to attend the Notaryâ€™s office to sign the document. The Notary Public who attested the deed testified that the parties signed voluntarily and he observed no reluctance at the time of execution. However, he noted that three days later, the Defendants informed him of the alleged duress and requested that he not register the deed. The Defendants also filed a complaint with the police after the execution. During the trial, evidence emerged regarding the violent nature of the 2nd Defendant. The District Judge disbelieved the Defendants' version of events, finding that they failed to discharge the burden of proving duress. The High Court of Civil Appeal subsequently affirmed the District Court's judgment. Issues Whether a party is permitted to adduce evidence against the contents of a notarially executed deed to prove fraud, duress, or intimidation. Whether the learned Trial Judge failed to evaluate the evidence of the defense in accordance with the law. Whether the Defendants are entitled to the judgment prayed for in their answer. Applicable Law Statutes: Evidence Ordinance, Section 92 High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Amendment Act No. 54 of 2006 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Admissibility of evidence against a deed Rule applied: Section 92 of the Evidence Ordinance, specifically proviso (1). Court's reasoning: While Section 92 generally prohibits oral evidence inconsistent with the terms of a written document, proviso (1) explicitly allows for evidence to be led to invalidate a document on grounds such as fraud, intimidation, illegality, or want of consideration. The court noted that the trial judge correctly permitted the Defendants to lead evidence to challenge the validity of the deed. Finding: Yes, a party is permitted to lead such evidence to invalidate a deed. Issue 2: Evaluation of evidence Rule applied: General principles of judicial evaluation of witness testimony and burden of proof. Court's reasoning: The court found that the burden of proof shifted to the Defendants to establish duress once the execution of the deed was admitted. The Defendants failed to discharge this burden. The court highlighted several factors: the Notaryâ€™s testimony that the signing was voluntary, the belated nature of the police complaint, the failure to take proactive steps to set aside the deed until the filing of the answer, and the inherent improbability of the Defendants' claims given the 2nd Defendantâ€™s own history of violence. The trial judge, having observed the demeanor of the witnesses, was best positioned to assess credibility. Finding: The trial judge did not fail to evaluate the evidence; the evaluation was proper and supported by the record. Issue 3: Entitlement to judgment Rule applied: Law of contract and evidence regarding the validity of deeds. Court's reasoning: Because the Defendants failed to establish the defense of duress or want of consideration, the deed remained valid. Consequently, the Plaintiff was entitled to the relief sought. Finding: The Defendants are not entitled to the judgment prayed for in their answer. Interaction of Issues The resolution of Issue 1 in favor of the Defendants (allowing them to lead evidence) did not assist their case because, as determined in Issue 2, the evidence they presented was insufficient to meet the legal burden of proof. The failure to prove the allegations of duress rendered the challenge to the deed unsuccessful, thereby necessitating the dismissal of the appeal as addressed in Issue 3. Conclusion The Supreme Court affirmed the judgment of the District Court of Mount Lavinia and the High Court of Civil Appeal. The appeal was dismissed with no order as to costs. Keywords Notarial attestation — Duress — Fraud — Evidence Ordinance — Section 92 — Invalidation of documents — Burden of proof — Trial judge's evaluation of evidence

Parties: Arangallage v. Herath and Bank of Ceylon
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Marasinghe, J; Aluwihare, PC, J
Keywords: Leave to Appeal — Prima facie case — Auction sale — Doctrine of laesio enormis — Sale under authority of Court — Material irregularity — Civil Procedure Code — Fiscal's sale — Writ of Execution
Summary: Facts The Petitioner and his wife became owners of a property via Deed No. 1129. The Petitioner subsequently mortgaged this property to the 2nd Respondent to secure a loan. The 1st Respondent initiated legal action against the Petitioner in the Commercial High Court, which resulted in a judgment against the Petitioner for a sum of Rs. 3,215,586.48 plus interest. An appeal to the Supreme Court was dismissed due to the Petitioner's failure to appear. Following the judgment, the 1st Respondent obtained a writ of execution and seized the Petitioner's half-share of the property. The Petitioner attempted to stay the auction by filing a petition in the High Court, but failed to comply with the court's order to deposit Rs. 4 million. Consequently, the auction proceeded on 02.09.2005, and the 1st Respondent purchased the property for Rs. 8,025,000. The Petitioner did not raise objections within the statutory period, and the court confirmed the sale on 08.11.2005. A Fiscal Conveyance was subsequently issued. In 2008, the Petitioner filed an action in the District Court seeking to declare the conveyance void based on the principle of laesio enormis. The District Court refused an interim injunction, and the High Court of Civil Appeal subsequently refused leave to appeal against that order. The Petitioner then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the auction sale in question constitutes an ordinary sale by public auction that attracts the doctrine of laesio enormis. Whether the Petitioner has disclosed a prima facie case against the 1st Respondent. Whether the passage from C. G. Weeramantryâ€™s Law of Contracts citing Voet 18.5.16 regarding laesio enormis is applicable in the context of modern law. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 282(1) Civil Procedure Code, Section 282(2) Cases Referred to: Silva v. Dias et al. - [13-nlr-125] Tasadduk Rasul Khan v. Ahamad Husain - [ILR. 21. Cal. 66] Muttukumaraswamy v. Nannitamby - [4-tam-34] Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of laesio enormis Rule applied: The doctrine of laesio enormis does not apply to sales made under the authority of a court or judicial decree. Court's reasoning: The court examined the nature of the sale, noting it was conducted via a Writ of Execution, managed by the Fiscal, and confirmed by the court. Relying on authorities including Voet and Weeramantry, the court held that judicial sales are exempt from the doctrine of laesio enormis. The court further noted that the 1st Respondent was not a mala fide purchaser, as the sale followed a legitimate legal process. Finding: The auction was a judicial sale, not an ordinary sale, and therefore the doctrine of laesio enormis does not apply. Issue 2: Existence of a prima facie case Rule applied: Section 282(2) of the Civil Procedure Code requires that any application to set aside a sale due to material irregularity must be made within thirty days of the receipt of the Fiscal's report. Court's reasoning: The Petitioner failed to object within the thirty-day window prescribed by the Civil Procedure Code. Furthermore, the Petitioner failed to provide evidence that the alleged low price was the result of a material irregularity, as required by the principles in Silva v. Dias et al.. The court emphasized that mere inadequacy of price does not establish an irregularity. Finding: The Petitioner failed to establish a prima facie case due to procedural non-compliance and lack of evidence regarding irregularity. Issue 3: Application of Voet 18.5.16 Rule applied: The principle that laesio enormis does not lie in cases of sales made under the authority of the court. Court's reasoning: The court affirmed that while the principle of laesio enormis exists in contract law, it is subject to the specific exception for judicial sales. The court rejected the Petitioner's argument that modern law renders this exception obsolete, confirming that the distinction between ordinary sales and judicial sales remains valid. Finding: The passage remains applicable and serves to exclude the current transaction from the scope of the doctrine. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the sale was a judicial one (Issue 1) directly supports the conclusion that the doctrine of laesio enormis is inapplicable. This, combined with the Petitioner's failure to comply with the statutory time limits under Section 282 of the Civil Procedure Code (Issue 2), confirms that the Petitioner has no legal basis to challenge the conveyance. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed, and the orders of the High Court and District Court are affirmed. The Appellant is ordered to pay costs in the sum of Rs. 100,000. Keywords Leave to Appeal — Prima facie case — Auction sale — Doctrine of laesio enormis — Sale under authority of Court — Material irregularity — Civil Procedure Code — Fiscal's sale — Writ of Execution

Parties: Amaradasa Liyanage and Others v. Sampath Bank PLC
Bench: Tilakawardane, J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Recovery of Loans by Banks Act — Certificate of Sale — Validity of Certificate — Board of Directors signature — Conclusive proof — Notice of sale — Upset price — Statutory pre-conditions — Administrative action
Summary: Facts Amaradasa Liyanage, carrying on business as Kosmodara Tea Factory, obtained credit facilities from Sampath Bank PLC and hypothecated property as security. Following default on payments, the Bankâ€™s Board of Directors passed a resolution to sell the property under the Recovery of Loans by Banks (Special Provisions) Act No. 4 of 1990. The resolution was published in the Dinamina newspaper and the Gazette. The Appellant unsuccessfully sought to stay the auction in the Commercial High Court and subsequently failed to pursue an appeal. A notice of auction was published, and the Appellant was notified of the sale scheduled for April 10, 2007. At the auction, the Bank purchased the property. Upon the Appellant's refusal to vacate, the Bank initiated proceedings in the District Court under Section 16 of the Act to obtain possession. The District Court refused the application, but the High Court of Civil Appeals subsequently made the order absolute. The Appellant appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Certificate of Sale was validly issued under Section 15(2) of the Recovery of Loans by Banks (Special Provisions) Act No. 4 of 1990 if signed by only two members of the Board rather than the entire Board. Whether the Certificate of Sale constitutes conclusive proof of compliance with the statutory provisions of the Act. Whether the Bank complied with the statutory requirements for notice and publication under Sections 8 and 9 of the Act. Applicable Law Statutes: Recovery of Loans by Banks (Special Provisions) Act No. 4 of 1990 State Mortgage and Investment Bank Law No. 13 of 1975 National Development Bank Act No. 2 of 1979 Bank of Ceylon Ordinance Peoples Bank Act No. 43 of 1961 Cases Referred to: National Development Bank v. Serendib Asia (Pvt) Ltd - [1999/v2-sri-lr-56] Hatton National Bank v. Marimuttu - [2004-bar-association-law-report] Haji Omar v. Wickramasinghe - [2002/v1-sri-lr-113] Analysis Issue 1: Validity of the Certificate of Sale Rule applied: Section 15(2) and Section 22 of the Recovery of Loans by Banks (Special Provisions) Act No. 4 of 1990. Court's reasoning: The Appellant argued that the Act requires the entire Board to sign the Certificate of Sale. The Court noted that while the statute defines "Board" as the body charged with management, the signature of two members constitutes a minor procedural irregularity. The Court held that invalidating the sale for this reason would be disproportionate and that such irregularities are remediable. Finding: The Certificate of Sale is valid despite being signed by only two members of the Board. Issue 2: Conclusive effect of the Certificate Rule applied: Section 15(2) of the Recovery of Loans by Banks (Special Provisions) Act No. 4 of 1990. Court's reasoning: The Court relied on the principle that the Certificate of Sale serves as conclusive proof that all statutory provisions relating to the sale have been complied with. Distinguishing National Development Bank v. Serendib Asia (Pvt) Ltd, the Court held that the Certificate precludes challenges to the procedural compliance of the sale, as established in Haji Omar v. Wickramasinghe and Hatton National Bank v. Marimuttu. Finding: The Certificate of Sale is conclusive proof of compliance with the Act. Issue 3: Compliance with notice requirements Rule applied: Sections 8 and 9 of the Recovery of Loans by Banks (Special Provisions) Act No. 4 of 1990. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the Appellantâ€™s claims of lack of notice were inconsistent with his own prior pleadings in the Commercial High Court. Evidence confirmed that the Bank published the notice of sale and dispatched a letter to the Appellant. Furthermore, the Appellant had nearly two years between the resolution and the auction to settle the debt. Finding: The Bank complied with the statutory notice and publication requirements. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing: the validity of the Certificate (Issue 1) and its conclusive nature (Issue 2) prevent the Appellant from reopening the sale based on alleged procedural defects. The Courtâ€™s determination that the Bank actually complied with notice requirements (Issue 3) further confirms that the "conclusive proof" provided by the Certificate is factually supported, thereby preventing an abuse of the court process. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed, and the judgment of the High Court of Civil Appeals is affirmed. The Appellant is ordered to pay costs in the sum of Rs. 75,000 to the Bank. Keywords Recovery of Loans by Banks Act — Certificate of Sale — Validity of Certificate — Board of Directors signature — Conclusive proof — Notice of sale — Upset price — Statutory pre-conditions — Administrative action

Parties: Karunarathne v. Senaratne and Others
Bench: K. Sripavan, J.; E. Wanasundera, P.C., J
Keywords: Article 126 of the Constitution — Fundamental rights — Time limit for application — Retirement benefits — Resignation — Political Victimization Committee — Human Rights Commission recommendations — Cabinet decision — Administrative action
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Dr. Vickrambahu Karunarathne, was a former employee of the University of Ceylon and later the University of Peradeniya. In 1982, the Petitioner sought to retire under the provisions of the Universities Act No. 16 of 1978. The Vice-Chancellor of the University of Peradeniya informed the Petitioner by letter dated 21 October 1982 that he could not retire under the Act but that his resignation was accepted effective 1 November 1982, subject to Council approval. The Petitioner subsequently sought relief based on a recommendation from a Political Victimization Committee, which was approved by the Cabinet in a letter dated 25 October 2001. The Petitioner also relied on recommendations from the Human Rights Commission and a settlement reached in a separate fundamental rights application, S.C. (F.R.) 260/2002, to which he was not a party. The Petitioner filed the current application alleging that the Respondents failed to grant him retirement benefits. The Respondents raised a preliminary objection regarding the maintainability of the application, specifically citing the time limit prescribed by Article 126(2) of the Constitution. Issues Whether the application is maintainable given the time limit prescribed by Article 126(2) of the Constitution. Whether the Petitioner is entitled to retirement benefits despite the University having accepted his resignation in 1982. Whether the recommendations of the Human Rights Commission or a settlement in a separate fundamental rights case can form the basis for an infringement of fundamental rights under Article 126.
