Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 126 Universities Act No. 16 of 1978 Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka Act No. 21 of 1996 Cases Referred to: Gamaethige v. Siriwardene - [1988/v1-sri-lr-384] Analysis Issue 1: Maintainability and Time Bar Rule applied: Article 126(2) of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Court noted that the Petitioner filed the application approximately 12 years after the Cabinet decision of 2001 and significantly longer after his resignation in 1982. The Court held that the pursuit of other administrative or judicial remedies does not interrupt the operation of the constitutional time limit. Finding: The preliminary objection is upheld; the application is time-barred. Issue 2: Entitlement to Retirement Benefits Rule applied: Universities Act No. 16 of 1978. Court's reasoning: The Petitionerâ€™s resignation was accepted by the University in 1982, and he failed to provide evidence that he was formally retired. The Court found no legal basis to grant retirement benefits when the employment relationship ended via resignation rather than retirement. Finding: The Petitioner failed to establish a legal entitlement to retirement benefits. Issue 3: Reliance on External Recommendations and Settlements Rule applied: Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka Act No. 21 of 1996; Article 126 of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Court held that findings of the Human Rights Commission are recommendatory and not binding on the Court. Furthermore, the Petitioner was not a party to the settlement in S.C. (F.R.) 260/2002, and judicial orders in other cases cannot serve as the basis for an executive or administrative infringement of fundamental rights. Finding: The Petitioner cannot rely on these external documents to invoke the fundamental rights jurisdiction of the Court. Interaction of Issues The findings are reinforcing: the Court determined that the Petitioner lacked a substantive legal claim to retirement benefits because he resigned, and even if he had a claim, the application was procedurally barred by the lapse of time and the inability to use non-binding recommendations to circumvent constitutional time limits. Conclusion The preliminary objection raised by the Respondents is sustained. Leave to proceed is refused, and the application is dismissed. Keywords Article 126 of the Constitution — Fundamental rights — Time limit for application — Retirement benefits — Resignation — Political Victimization Committee — Human Rights Commission recommendations — Cabinet decision — Administrative action

Parties: Suvinipala v. The Peoplesâ€™ Bank
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC. J; K. Sripavan, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC.J
Keywords: Appeal from Provincial High Court — Labour Tribunal order — Termination of employment — Unlawful termination — Compensation — Excessive compensation — Evaluation of evidence — Disciplinary action — Retirement benefits — Inquiry without opportunity to defend
Summary: Facts The Applicant, W. Suvinipala, was employed as a Manager at the Peoples Bank. Upon reaching the retirement age of 55, the Applicant was granted an extension of service until 15-06-2002. Shortly before this date, the Petitioner Bank discovered alleged irregularities regarding overdrafts granted by the Applicant. The Petitioner Bank interdicted the Applicant on 03-06-2002 and subsequently issued a letter on 19-12-2003, retiring the Applicant with effect from 03-06-2002. During the period of interdiction, the Applicant received half-salary. The Applicant filed an application in the Labour Tribunal seeking reinstatement or compensation, which was dismissed on the basis that he had accepted retirement benefits. The Applicant appealed to the Provincial High Court, which set aside the Labour Tribunal's order and awarded compensation equivalent to the full salary for the period of interdiction, less the amounts already paid. The Petitioner Bank appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Provincial High Court erred in law in its evaluation of evidence and failed to consider the totality of the evidence regarding the termination of the Applicant's services. Whether the order for compensation granted by the Provincial High Court was excessive or erroneous in law. Applicable Law Statutes: Not specified Cases Referred to: Ceylon Planters Society v. Bogawanthalawa Plantation Ltd. - [2004/v1-sri-lr-88] Piyasena Silva v. Ceylon Fisheries Corporation - [1994/v2-sri-lr-292] Saleem v. Hatton National Bank - [1994/v3-sri-lr-409] Jayasooriya v. Sri Lanka State Plantation Corporation - [1995/v2-sri-lr-379] Analysis Issue 1: Evaluation of evidence and lawfulness of termination Rule applied: The court applied the principle that disciplinary authority must be exercised by the competent body (Chairman and Board of Directors) and that an employee must be afforded an opportunity to defend themselves. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the termination letter was issued by a committee without the involvement of the lawful disciplinary authority, the Chairman and Board of Directors. Furthermore, the Applicant was denied an opportunity to defend himself at an inquiry. The court found that the Petitioner Bank treated the Applicant as an employee until 19-12-2003 by paying him half-salary during the interdiction period. Consequently, the court agreed with the lower findings that the termination was unlawful and unjustifiable. Finding: The Provincial High Court did not err in its evaluation of evidence; the termination was indeed unlawful and unjustifiable. Issue 2: Appropriateness of compensation Rule applied: The court applied the principle that compensation should be granted in lieu of reinstatement when the latter is not an option, and that wrongful termination denies an employee the opportunity to seek other employment. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that since the Petitioner Bank had kept the Applicant in service during the interdiction period by paying half-salary, it was improper to deprive him of full salary upon the conclusion of the disciplinary process. The court rejected the argument that the receipt of a pension justified reducing the compensation, noting that the Bank could not take contradictory positions. Finding: The compensation awarded by the Provincial High Court, representing the full salary for the period of interdiction, was not excessive or erroneous. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the termination was procedurally unlawful and substantively unjustifiable (Issue 1) established the liability of the Petitioner Bank. This finding directly necessitated the calculation of appropriate compensation (Issue 2), which the court found to be reasonable given the Bank's own conduct in maintaining the employment relationship during the interdiction period. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed and the judgment of the Provincial High Court is affirmed. No costs are ordered. Keywords Appeal from Provincial High Court — Labour Tribunal order — Termination of employment — Unlawful termination — Compensation — Excessive compensation — Evaluation of evidence — Disciplinary action — Retirement benefits — Inquiry without opportunity to defend

Parties: W. Suvinipala v. The Peoples’ Bank
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC. J.; K. Sripavan, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC.J.
Keywords: compensation — unlawful termination — employment law — Labour Tribunal — Provincial High Court — disciplinary action — retirement age — evidence evaluation — reinstatement — constructive dismissal — pension rights — judicial review — employer-employee relationship — procedural fairness — appeal
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal to the Supreme Court concerning the employment status and termination of W. Suvinipala, who was employed as a manager at the People's Bank. The Respondent, Suvinipala, filed an application in the Labour Tribunal seeking relief for what he claimed was an unlawful termination of his services. The Labour Tribunal dismissed his application, stating that he had retired and collected his retirement benefits, thus preventing him from claiming wrongful termination. However, upon appeal, the Provincial High Court awarded him compensation, leading to the current appeal by the People's Bank. The Respondent was due to retire at the age of 55, as per the Bank's circular, but was granted extensions. Just before his retirement, the Bank discovered alleged irregularities in his conduct and decided to interdict him, which led to a series of legal proceedings. The Respondent contended that he was wrongfully terminated and sought compensation for the period he was interdicted. The Labour Tribunal found that his termination was unlawful, a finding that was upheld by the Provincial High Court. Issues The key issues presented to the Supreme Court were: (a) Whether the order of the Provincial High Court for compensation was excessive and/or erroneous in law. (b) Whether the Provincial High Court erred in law in the evaluation of evidence and made an order without considering the totality of the evidence. Applicable Law Statutes: Labour Relations Act, Employment Ordinance Cases Referred to: Ceylon Planters Society vs. Bogawanthalawa Plantation Ltd - [2004, 1 SLR 88]; Piyasena Silva Vs. Ceylon Fisheries Corporation - [1994, 2 SLR 292]; Saleem Vs. Hatton National Bank - [1994, 3 SLR 409]; Jayasooriya Vs. Sri Lanka State Plantation Corporation - [1995, 2 SLR 379]. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the order of the Provincial High Court for compensation was excessive and/or erroneous in law. Rule applied: The principle of unlawful termination and the rights of employees to compensation for wrongful dismissal. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the circumstances surrounding the Respondent's termination, noting that he was due to retire on 15-06-2002 but was made to retire earlier on 03-06-2002. The Court highlighted that the Respondent was not given a fair opportunity to defend himself during the disciplinary inquiry, which was a significant procedural flaw. The Court emphasized that the Labour Tribunal had found the termination to be unlawful and unjustifiable, a conclusion that was supported by the evidence presented. The Supreme Court noted that the Provincial High Court had appropriately evaluated the evidence and determined that the Respondent was entitled to compensation for the period he was unlawfully deprived of his full salary. The Court referenced the Labour Tribunal's findings, which indicated that the termination was not executed by the proper authority, further reinforcing the Respondent's claim of wrongful termination. The Court concluded that the compensation awarded was not excessive, as it reflected the Respondent's loss of earnings during the period of unlawful termination. Finding: The Supreme Court found that the compensation awarded by the Provincial High Court was justified and not excessive. Issue 2: Whether the Provincial High Court erred in law in the evaluation of evidence and made an order without considering the totality of the evidence. Rule applied: The requirement for a comprehensive evaluation of evidence in employment disputes. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court scrutinized the evaluation process undertaken by the Provincial High Court, asserting that it had indeed considered the totality of the evidence presented. The Court noted that both the Labour Tribunal and the Provincial High Court had arrived at similar conclusions regarding the unlawfulness of the termination. The Supreme Court reiterated that the Respondent had been denied procedural fairness, which was a critical factor in determining the legality of the termination. The Court emphasized that the evidence indicated that the Respondent was effectively employed until the date of the termination notice, and the delay in communicating the termination further complicated the matter. The Supreme Court agreed with the findings of the lower courts that the Respondent's rights had been violated, and the evaluation of evidence was thorough and justified. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the Provincial High Court did not err in its evaluation of the evidence and that its findings were well-founded. Interaction of Issues The issues presented were interrelated, as the determination of whether the compensation was excessive was directly linked to the evaluation of evidence regarding the lawfulness of the termination. The Supreme Court's analysis highlighted that both issues stemmed from the same factual matrix, where the Respondent's rights as an employee were central to the court's considerations. The Court's findings on the unlawfulness of the termination directly influenced its conclusions regarding the appropriateness of the compensation awarded. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal by the People's Bank, affirming the judgment of the Provincial High Court. The Court found that the compensation awarded was justified and that the Respondent's termination was unlawful. The Court ordered no costs, considering the circumstances of the case. The judgment reinforced the principles of employee rights and the necessity for fair procedures in employment termination cases. Keywords compensation — unlawful termination — employment law — Labour Tribunal — Provincial High Court — disciplinary action — retirement age — evidence evaluation — reinstatement — constructive dismissal — pension rights — judicial review — employer-employee relationship — procedural fairness — appeal
Counsel: Manoli Jinadasa with Tharanga Ambepitiya for the Respondent-Respondent-Appellant. Indra Ladduwahetty for the Applicant-Appellant-Respondent.

Parties: Ariyaratne and Others v. Attorney General
Bench: K. Sripavan, J.; P. Dep, P.C., J; Rohini Marasinghe, J.
Keywords: Special leave to appeal — Court of Appeal ex parte dismissal — Opportunity to be heard — Inadvertent omission by counsel — Bona fide mistake — Justice manifestly seen to be done — Legal representation — Fair dispensation of justice
Summary: Facts The Accused Appellant Petitioner, Arumabadadurage Ariyaratne, was indicted before the High Court of Hambantota on charges of kidnapping and rape. The High Court found the Appellant guilty on both counts and imposed sentences of rigorous imprisonment, which were ordered to run concurrently. The Appellant subsequently appealed the conviction and sentence to the Court of Appeal. During the appellate proceedings, the matter was fixed for argument on several occasions. On 02.02.2009, when the case was called, the Appellant was absent and unrepresented. Consequently, the Court of Appeal proceeded to hear the matter ex parte and dismissed the appeal. The Appellant thereafter sought special leave to appeal to the Supreme Court. Counsel for the Appellant filed an affidavit explaining that the absence on 02.02.2009 was due to a bona fide mistake in recording the date of the hearing. The Respondent did not object to the granting of special leave to appeal. Issues Whether the Court of Appeal should have provided the Appellant an opportunity to be heard, either by himself or through Counsel, in the interest of fair play and justice. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 128 Penal Code, Section 354 Penal Code, Section 364(2) Cases Referred to: Burgoine v. Taylor - [9-ch-d-1] Pett v. Greyhound Racing Association Ltd. - [1968/v2-all-e-r-545] Analysis Issue 1: Opportunity to be heard Rule applied: The Court applied the principle that justice must not only be done but must be seen to be done, and that legal representation is an elementary feature of the fair dispensation of justice. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the affidavit filed by the Appellant's Counsel, which admitted that the failure to appear on 02.02.2009 was an inadvertent error in noting the hearing date. The Court reasoned that Counsel should not be punished for bona fide mistakes that are not motivated by malice or an intent to abuse the court process. It emphasized that the right to legal representation is essential because a party may lack the ability to effectively defend themselves or cross-examine witnesses. The Court noted that the Appellant was effectively denied a real and reasonable opportunity for defense by the ex parte dismissal. It concluded that the interests of justice are best served by allowing the appeal to be heard on its merits rather than maintaining a dismissal based on a procedural slip. Finding: The Court answered the question of law in the affirmative, holding that the Appellant should have been given an opportunity to be heard. Conclusion The judgment of the Court of Appeal dated 02.02.2009 is set aside. The matter is remitted to the Court of Appeal to be heard afresh on the merits after providing the Appellant an opportunity to be heard either by himself or through Counsel. Keywords Special leave to appeal — Court of Appeal ex parte dismissal — Opportunity to be heard — Inadvertent omission by counsel — Bona fide mistake — Justice manifestly seen to be done — Legal representation — Fair dispensation of justice

Parties: The British High Commission v. Jansen
Bench: Mohan Pieris, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Eva Wanasundera, P.C., J
Keywords: State immunity — Sovereign immunity — Diplomatic immunity — Labour Tribunal jurisdiction — Contract of employment — Waiver of immunity — Acts jure imperii — Acts jure gestionis — Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations — Employment contract — Security Assistant
Summary: Facts The Applicant-Appellant-Respondent, Ricardo Wilhelm Michael Jansen, was employed by the British High Commission, the Respondent-Respondent-Appellant, as a Security Assistant starting on 02.04.2001. After an initial period of service, the Respondent was made a permanent employee. On 03.02.2010, the Appellant terminated the Respondent's services for alleged misconduct, specifically for sleeping while on guard duty, which was cited as a breach of the employment contract and Post Security Regulations. The Respondent filed an application in the Labour Tribunal seeking relief for unlawful termination. The Appellant filed an answer asserting state immunity, arguing that the British High Commission represents the United Kingdom and is not subject to Sri Lankan laws. The Appellant did not participate in the subsequent Labour Tribunal proceedings, which were held ex parte. The Labour Tribunal upheld the plea of immunity and dismissed the application. The Respondent appealed to the Provincial High Court, which reversed the Labour Tribunal's order, finding that the contractual clause subjecting the Respondent's service conditions to Sri Lankan Labour Law constituted an express waiver of immunity. The British High Commission subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the British High Commission is entitled to sovereign immunity regarding the employment contract of a Security Assistant under the principles of customary international law. Whether the inclusion of a clause in the employment contract stating that conditions of service are subject to Sri Lankan Labour Law constitutes an express waiver of sovereign immunity. Applicable Law Statutes: Diplomatic Privileges Act No. 9 of 1996 Cases Referred to: Maclaine Watson v. Department Trade - [1988] 3 All ER 257 Philippine Admiral - [1976] 1 All ER 71 Trendex Trading Corporation v. Bank of Nigeria - [1977] QB 529 I Congreso del Partido - [1983] AC 244 Sengupta v. Republic of India - [65-ilr-325] Littrell v. United States of America (No 2) - [1994] 2 All ER Holland v. Lampen Wolfe - [2000] 1 WLR 1573 United States of America v. The Public Service Alliance of Canada - [94-ilr-264] Kuwait Airways Corporation v. Iraqi Airways Co - [1995] 1 WLR 1147 Libyan American Oil Company (Lamco) v. Socialist Peoples Libyan Arab Jamahirya - [1981] Ybk Comm.Arb 89 International Tin Council v. Amalgamated Inc - [1988] NYS 2d 1971 R v. Madhan - [1961] 2 QB 1 Aziz v. Republic of Yemen - [2005] EWCACiv 745 Malaysian Industrial Redevelopment Authority v. Jeyasingham - [1998] ICR 307 Egypt v. Gamal-Eldin - [1996] 2 All ER 237 Ahmed v. Government of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia - [1996] 2 All ER 248 Mills v. USA - [120-ilr-162] The Superintendent, Deeside Estate Maskeliya v. Illankai Tholar Kazhakam - [70-nlr-279] Analysis Issue 1: Sovereign Immunity Rule applied: The doctrine of restrictive immunity under customary international law, distinguishing between jure imperii (sovereign acts) and jure gestionis (private/commercial acts). Court's reasoning: The Court applied the test from I Congreso del Partido, examining the nature and purpose of the employment. It determined that the Respondent's duties as a Security Assistant were integral to the inviolability and security of the diplomatic mission. Because these duties were performed in the interest of the public service of the United Kingdom, the contract was an act jure imperii rather than a private commercial act. Finding: The British High Commission is entitled to sovereign immunity as the employment contract constitutes a sovereign activity. Issue 2: Waiver of Immunity Rule applied: Section 2(3) of the Diplomatic Privileges Act No. 9 of 1996 and the principle that a waiver must be made by the Head of Mission or an authorized representative. Court's reasoning: The Court held that a contractual clause stating that service conditions are subject to local labour law is merely a choice of law provision and does not constitute an express waiver of immunity from the jurisdiction of the courts. Furthermore, such a waiver must be explicitly granted by the Head of Mission, which did not occur in this case. Finding: There was no valid waiver of sovereign immunity. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. Because the employment contract was classified as an act jure imperii, the Appellant was entitled to immunity. The absence of a valid waiver meant that this immunity remained intact, thereby depriving the Labour Tribunal of the jurisdiction to adjudicate the dispute. Conclusion The appeal of the British High Commission is allowed. The order of the Provincial High Court is set aside, and the order of the Labour Tribunal is affirmed to the extent that it lacked jurisdiction to hear the application. Keywords State immunity — Sovereign immunity — Diplomatic immunity — Labour Tribunal jurisdiction — Contract of employment — Waiver of immunity — Acts jure imperii — Acts jure gestionis — Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations — Employment contract — Security Assistant

Parties: Waduge Somasiri v. Attorney General
Bench: Mohan Pieris, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Rape — Sexual assault — Evidence — Medical Legal Report — Letter evidence — Dock statement — Evaluation of dock statement — Miscarriage of justice — Proviso to section 334 of the Criminal Procedure Code — Proviso to Article 138 of the Constitution — Burden of proof
Summary: Facts The Accused-Appellant, Gusthinggna Waduge Somasiri, was indicted in the High Court of Anuradhapura for the rape of a fifteen-year-old girl, Devika Iranganie, under Section 364(2)(e) of the Penal Code. The prosecutrix testified that the accused, a distant relative who assisted her with studies, entered her room, locked the door, and forcibly had intercourse with her while her parents were at work. She did not report the incident immediately due to threats of acid attacks and academic interference. The incident came to light after the accused delivered a letter to the prosecutrix, which was intercepted by her brother and subsequently read by her mother. Medical evidence from Dr. Wilson confirmed a partially healed hymenal tear consistent with the history provided by the prosecutrix. The accused elected to make a statement from the dock, denying the allegations and claiming he was falsely implicated due to financial disputes with the family. The High Court rejected the dock statement, found the prosecution's case proved beyond reasonable doubt, and convicted the accused. The Court of Appeal affirmed the conviction, acknowledging that the trial judge had misdirected himself regarding the evaluation of the dock statement but applying the proviso to Section 334(1) of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act and the proviso to Article 138 of the Constitution to conclude that no miscarriage of justice had occurred. Issues Whether the Court of Appeal erred in law by applying the proviso to Section 334(1) of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act and the proviso to Article 138 of the Constitution to affirm a conviction despite the trial judge's misdirection regarding the evaluation of a dock statement. Whether the proviso to Section 334(1) of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act is applicable to non-jury trials where a grievous misdirection on a material concept of law has occurred. Applicable Law Statutes: Penal Code (as amended by Act No. 22 of 1995) Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979 Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka Administration of Justice Law No. 44 of 1973 Court of Criminal Appeal Ordinance No. 23 of 1938 Cases Referred to: Queen v. Kularatne - [71-nlr-529] Somasiri v. AG - [1983/v2-sri-lr-225] Lionel v. AG - [1988/v1-sri-lr-4] Gunapala v. Republic of Sri Lanka - [1994/v3-sri-lr-180] Mannar Mannan v. The Republic of Sri Lanka - [1990/v1-sri-lr-1] Sheela Sinharage - [1985/v1-sri-lr-1] Moses v. State - [1999/v3-sri-lr-401] Wijerathne v. The Attorney General - [2010/v1-blr-169] Analysis Issue 1: Application of the Proviso to Dock Statement Misdirection Rule applied: Proviso to Section 334(1) of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act and the proviso to Article 138(1) of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The court acknowledged that the trial judge failed to follow the established principle in Queen v. Kularatne, which requires a judge to treat a dock statement as evidence and inform the jury (or consider in a bench trial) that it may raise a reasonable doubt. However, citing Mannar Mannan v. The Republic of Sri Lanka, the court held that the proviso allows an appellate court to dismiss an appeal despite a misdirection if there is no substantial miscarriage of justice. Because the trial judge had explicitly rejected the dock statement as false and the remaining evidence was credible and sufficient to prove the case beyond reasonable doubt, the error did not vitiate the conviction. Finding: The Court of Appeal did not err in applying the proviso, as the conviction was supported by overwhelming evidence. Issue 2: Applicability of the Proviso to Non-Jury Trials Rule applied: Section 334(1) and Section 335(1) of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act. Court's reasoning: The court rejected the argument that the proviso to Section 334(1) is limited to jury trials. It noted that while Section 334 refers to jury trials, the underlying principle—that an appellate court should not interfere if there is no miscarriage of justice—is a general principle of appellate jurisdiction. The court relied on Wijerathne v. The Attorney General, which confirmed that these principles apply to non-jury trials. Even without an explicit proviso in Section 335(1), the court possesses the inherent authority to affirm a conviction if the prosecution has proved its case beyond reasonable doubt, thereby avoiding unnecessary retrials. Finding: The proviso is applicable to non-jury trials. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues reinforce each other by establishing that the appellate court's primary duty is to prevent a miscarriage of justice. The resolution of the first issue confirms that a legal misdirection does not automatically mandate an acquittal if the evidence is sufficient. The second issue confirms that this standard of review is uniform across both jury and non-jury trials, ensuring consistency in the administration of justice. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The conviction and sentence imposed by the High Court and affirmed by the Court of Appeal are upheld. Keywords Rape — Sexual assault — Evidence — Medical Legal Report — Letter evidence — Dock statement — Evaluation of dock statement — Miscarriage of justice — Proviso to section 334 of the Criminal Procedure Code — Proviso to Article 138 of the Constitution — Burden of proof

Parties: Ranbanda v. Peoplesâ€™ Bank
Bench: Marsoof, P.C., J; Sripavan, J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Labour Tribunal order — Wrongful termination of employment — Pension benefits — Excessive punishment — Appeals from High Court — Special Leave to Appeal — Leave to Appeal — Industrial Disputes Act — High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act — Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court — Maintainability of application — Non-compliance with Supreme Court Rules — Questions of law fit for review
Summary: Facts The Applicant-Respondent-Petitioner, Dassanayake Mudiyanselage Ranbanda, filed an application in the Labour Tribunal alleging that his services were wrongfully and unjustly terminated by the Respondent-Appellant-Respondent, Peoples Bank. The President of the Labour Tribunal held that the Respondent Bank failed to prove the charges against the Applicant-Petitioner and that the termination was an excessive punishment. The Tribunal granted pension benefits to the Applicant-Petitioner but did not order reinstatement as he had reached retirement age. The Respondent Bank appealed this order to the High Court. The High Court set aside the order of the Labour Tribunal and dismissed the application. The Applicant-Petitioner subsequently filed an application for Special Leave to Appeal in the Supreme Court. The Respondent Bank raised preliminary objections regarding the maintainability of the application, citing non-compliance with relevant laws and Supreme Court Rules, specifically noting the failure to specify the governing statute in the caption and the failure to strictly adhere to procedural requirements for leave to appeal. Issues Whether the failure to specify the governing statute in the caption of the application for Special Leave to Appeal is a fatal defect or a curable irregularity. Whether the application for leave to appeal from the High Court to the Supreme Court in labour matters must be filed under the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 19 of 1990 or the Industrial Disputes (Amendment) Act No. 32 of 1990. Whether the Applicant-Petitioner substantially complied with the Supreme Court Rules regarding the statement of questions of law. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka (13th Amendment) High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 19 of 1990 Industrial Disputes Act No. 43 of 1950 (as amended by Act No. 32 of 1990) Maintenance Act No. 37 of 1999 Cases Referred to: Martin v. Wijewardena - [1989/v2-sri-lr-409] Gunarathne v. Thambinayagam - [1993/v1-sri-lr-355] Malegoda v. Joachim - [1997/v1-sri-lr-88] The Ceylon Electricity Board v. Ranjith Fonseka - [2008/balr-part-11] Attanayake v. Commissioner General of Elections - [2011/v2-blr] Priyani Soyza v. Rienzie Arsacularatne - [1999/v2-sri-lr-179] Kiriwantha v. Nawaratne - [1990/v2-sri-lr-393] Analysis Issue 1: Caption Omission Rule applied: Supreme Court Rules 1990. Court's reasoning: The court distinguished this case from The Ceylon Electricity Board v. Ranjith Fonseka, where the application was teeming with fatal irregularities. Here, the title of the petition was correct, and the omission of the specific statute in the caption was deemed a technical defect rather than a substantive failure. The court emphasized that litigants should not be penalized for drafting ambiguities caused by overlapping legislation. Finding: The omission is a curable technical irregularity; the Applicant-Petitioner is permitted to amend the caption. Issue 2: Governing Statute Rule applied: High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 19 of 1990; Industrial Disputes (Amendment) Act No. 32 of 1990. Court's reasoning: The court found that both statutes provide for a right of appeal and confer jurisdiction on the Supreme Court. While the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 19 is the primary enabling statute, the Industrial Disputes (Amendment) Act No. 32 also provides a valid basis for appeal. The court held that these provisions coexist and that the use of different terminology ("special leave" vs. "leave") does not invalidate the application. Finding: The application is maintainable under either statute, as they provide concurrent paths for appeal in labour matters. Issue 3: Compliance with Supreme Court Rules Rule applied: Supreme Court Rules 1990 Part 1 (3). Court's reasoning: The Respondent Bank argued that the failure to use the exact phrase "fit for review" was fatal. The court noted that the Applicant-Petitioner had pleaded questions of law and averred they were "fit and proper" for consideration. Relying on Kiriwantha v. Nawaratne, the court held that rules must be complied with, but they do not mandate automatic dismissal for minor defaults. Finding: The Applicant-Petitioner substantially complied with the rules. Interaction of Issues The court's findings on the governing statute and the technical nature of the caption omission are mutually reinforcing. By determining that the legislative framework is ambiguous and that the statutes provide overlapping remedies, the court concluded that strict adherence to specific terminology is not required to invoke the court's jurisdiction. This interpretation prevents the dismissal of the application on purely technical grounds, ensuring that the substantive right of appeal is preserved. Conclusion The preliminary objections raised by the Respondent Bank are overruled. The application is deemed maintainable, and the matter shall be listed for support for the granting of leave. No costs are ordered. Keywords Labour Tribunal order — Wrongful termination of employment — Pension benefits — Excessive punishment — Appeals from High Court — Special Leave to Appeal — Leave to Appeal — Industrial Disputes Act — High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act — Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court — Maintainability of application — Non-compliance with Supreme Court Rules — Questions of law fit for review

Parties: Ranjith Wagaarachchi v. Union Assurance P.L.C
Bench: Wanasundera, P.C., J.; Marasinghe, J.; Aluwihare, P.C., J.
Keywords: Leave to appeal — High Court — Civil Procedure Code — Time limit — Interlocutory order — Appeal procedure — Legislative intent — Mandatory provisions — Jurisdiction — Absurdity — Legal interpretation
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for leave to appeal filed by Ranjith Wagaarachchi against Union Assurance P.L.C. The application was made under Section 5(2) of the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996, in conjunction with Chapter LVIII of the Civil Procedure Code and relevant constitutional provisions. The plaintiff, Wagaarachchi, sought to challenge an interlocutory order made by the High Court of the Western Province. The crux of the matter arose when the defendant raised a preliminary objection, asserting that the application was filed out of time, specifically beyond the fourteen-day period mandated by law. The High Court's order, which the petitioner sought to appeal, was delivered on 7 May 2014, while the application to the Supreme Court was lodged on 10 June 2014, a clear thirty-three days after the order was pronounced. The petitioner contended that the procedural requirements were not strictly obligatory due to the permissive language used in the relevant statutes. Issues The primary issues for determination were: Whether the application for leave to appeal was filed within the stipulated time frame as per the Civil Procedure Code. Whether the provisions of Chapter LVIII of the Civil Procedure Code are mandatory or permissive in nature when applied to appeals to the Supreme Court under the 1996 Act. Applicable Law Statutes: High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996 — Civil Procedure Code Chapter LVIII — Constitution of Sri Lanka. Cases Referred to: Haji Omar Vs. Wickramasinghe & Others - [2001 3 S.L.R 61] Kamkaru Sevana and others V. Kingsly Perera and others - [SC. H.C.(L.A) 86/12] George Stuart & Co. Ltd Vs. Lankem Tea & Rubber Plantations Ltd - [2004 1 S.L.R 246]. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the application for leave to appeal was filed within the stipulated time frame as per the Civil Procedure Code. Rule applied: Section 757(1) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court meticulously examined the timeline of events leading to the filing of the application for leave to appeal. The court noted that the High Court's order was delivered on 7 May 2014, and the application was filed on 10 June 2014, which was beyond the fourteen-day limit prescribed by Section 757(1). The court emphasized that the statutory requirement for filing an appeal is not merely procedural but a substantive right that ensures timely justice. The court referred to the legislative intent behind the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act, particularly Sections 5 and 6, which outline the appeal process. The court highlighted that the use of the word 'shall' in Section 5(3) indicates a mandatory requirement, while Section 6's phrase 'as nearly as may be' was interpreted to allow for minor procedural adjustments but not to disregard the essential timelines. The court further elaborated that if the petitioner's interpretation were accepted, it would lead to an absurd situation where appeals could be filed indefinitely, undermining the finality of court orders and the principle of legal certainty. The court cited previous judgments that reinforced the necessity of adhering to the prescribed timelines, noting that any delay must be justified under the principle of 'lex non cogit ad impossibilia.' Finding: The court concluded that the application for leave to appeal was indeed filed out of time, and thus the preliminary objection raised by the respondent was upheld. The application was dismissed without costs. Interaction of Issues The issues presented were interrelated, as the determination of whether the application was filed within the time limit directly impacted the court's jurisdiction to hear the appeal. The court's interpretation of the statutory provisions also influenced the broader understanding of procedural compliance in appeals to the Supreme Court. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the application for leave to appeal, affirming the necessity of adhering to the statutory time limits set forth in the Civil Procedure Code. The court underscored the importance of procedural discipline in the judicial process, ensuring that parties cannot unduly delay appeals and that the integrity of court orders is maintained. The ruling serves as a reminder of the critical nature of compliance with procedural requirements in the pursuit of justice. Keywords Leave to appeal — High Court — Civil Procedure Code — Time limit — Interlocutory order — Appeal procedure — Legislative intent — Mandatory provisions — Jurisdiction — Absurdity — Legal interpretation
Counsel: Ian Fernando with Saman Liyanage for the Plaintiff-Petitioner. S.A. Parathalingam, P.C. with N. Parathalingam for the Defendant-Respondent.

Parties: Menike v. Dalugama Multi Purpose Cooperative Society Ltd.
Bench: Eva Wanasundera PC, J; B Aluwihare PC, J; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Termination of employment — Unjust and unreasonable termination — Reinstatement — Back wages — Labour Tribunal — High Court — Evidence at inquiry — Breach of discipline — Assault
Summary: Facts The Applicant-Appellant was employed as an accountant by the Respondent society. In April 1997, she prepared a profit and loss account for a New Year Fair, which she subsequently revised following written instructions from the General Manager. During a Board of Directors meeting held on 30.7.1997, a dispute occurred between the Applicant-Appellant and the Chairman of the Respondent society. The Chairman assaulted the Applicant-Appellant, threatened her with death, and forcibly removed her from the premises. The Applicant-Appellant filed complaints with the police regarding these incidents. Consequently, the Officer-in-Charge of the Peliyagoda Police Station initiated criminal proceedings against the Chairman in the Magistrate Court of Colombo for offences under the Penal Code. The Chairman eventually settled the criminal case by paying compensation to the Applicant-Appellant. The Applicant-Appellant filed an application in the Labour Tribunal of Colombo challenging her termination. The Labour Tribunal President determined that the termination was unjust and unreasonable and ordered the payment of seven years' salary as compensation but denied reinstatement. The Applicant-Appellant appealed this order to the High Court. The High Court Judge affirmed that the termination was unjust and unreasonable and ordered the reinstatement of the Applicant-Appellant but denied her back wages. The Applicant-Appellant subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court seeking full back wages. Issues Whether the High Court Judge erred in law by failing to provide reasons for denying back wages to the Applicant-Appellant despite ordering her reinstatement. Whether the denial of back wages to the Applicant-Appellant was justified given the finding that her termination was unjust and unreasonable. Applicable Law Statutes: Penal Code Cases Referred to: Millers Limited v. Ceylon Mercantile Industrial and General Workers Union - [1993/v1-sri-lr-179] Analysis Issue 1: Failure to provide reasons Rule applied: The principle that judicial orders must be fair to all parties and grounded in the evidence. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that the High Court Judge failed to provide any justification or reasoning for the decision to deny back wages while simultaneously ordering reinstatement. The Court noted that the Applicant-Appellant was deprived of her income through no fault of her own, as the termination resulted from the unlawful conduct of the Chairman of the Respondent society. Finding: The High Court Judge erred in law by failing to provide a reasoned basis for the denial of back wages. Issue 2: Justification for denial of back wages Rule applied: The principle established in Millers Limited v. Ceylon Mercantile Industrial and General Workers Union, which requires orders to be fair to all parties in the interest of discipline. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the Applicant-Appellant had not breached any disciplinary standards of the Respondent society. Conversely, the Chairman of the Respondent society had engaged in violent conduct, which led to criminal charges that he eventually settled. The Court rejected the Respondent society's argument regarding economic hardship, noting a lack of supporting evidence. Given that the termination was found to be unjust and unreasonable by both the Labour Tribunal and the High Court, the Court concluded that there was no justification to deprive the Applicant-Appellant of her salary, allowances, and increments. Finding: The denial of back wages was unreasonable and unjustified. Interaction of Issues The findings are intrinsically linked; the lack of reasoning identified in the first issue highlights the absence of a substantive legal or factual basis for the denial of back wages. By establishing that the termination was entirely the fault of the Respondent society and that the Applicant-Appellant was blameless, the Court reinforced that the failure to award back wages was not only procedurally flawed but also substantively inequitable. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The Court orders the reinstatement of the Applicant-Appellant without a break in service, including the payment of full back wages from the date of termination on 17.10.1997. Keywords Termination of employment — Unjust and unreasonable termination — Reinstatement — Back wages — Labour Tribunal — High Court — Evidence at inquiry — Breach of discipline — Assault

Parties: Wijialudchumi Ramesh v. Wigneswaran and Others
Bench: Mohan Pieris, J.; Rohini Marasinghe, J.; Buwaneka Aluwihare, J.
Keywords: Withdrawal of circular — Ultra vires — Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) — Article 14(1)(g) — Provincial Council Act No 42 of 1987
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Wijialudchumi Ramesh, filed an application under Article 126 of the Constitution alleging that the 1st Respondent, the Chief Minister of the Northern Provincial Council, infringed her fundamental rights under Articles 12(1) and 14(1)(g) of the Constitution. The dispute arose from the issuance of Administrative Standing Instructions No. 1/2014, a circular designated as P10, which the Petitioner contended was ultra vires the powers of the 1st Respondent. Upon supporting the petition, this Court granted leave to proceed and issued an interim order suspending the operation of the circular, while clarifying the legal parameters governing the appointment, transfer, and disciplinary control of public officers. Subsequently, the 1st Respondent filed an affidavit informing the Court that the impugned circular P10 had been withdrawn. While the 1st Respondent asserted that this withdrawal was made in deference to the Court, the Court examined the nature of this action. A later statement filed by the Attorney-at-Law for the 1st Respondent attempted to qualify the withdrawal, but the Court rejected this, finding that the initial withdrawal was a conscious, deliberate, and unconditional act. Issues Whether the issuance of the circular P10 by the 1st Respondent was ultra vires his powers. Whether the unconditional withdrawal of the circular P10 renders the fundamental rights application infructuous. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1), 14(1)(g), 126 Provincial Council Act No. 42 of 1987 Cases Referred to: Abeywicrema v. Pathirana - [1986/v1-sri-lr-120] Analysis Issue 1: Vires of the Circular P10 Rule applied: The principle of ultra vires regarding the exercise of administrative power under the Provincial Council Act No. 42 of 1987. Court's reasoning: The Court initially determined that the circular P10 was, on its face, ultra vires the powers of the 1st Respondent. This finding formed the basis for the interim order suspending the circular. The Court noted that the 1st Respondentâ€™s subsequent unconditional withdrawal of the circular served as a representation that he was mistaken regarding the legal effect of the circular. Finding: The Court accepted the withdrawal as an admission that the 1st Respondent had stepped outside the four corners of his powers, creating an estoppel against the 1st Respondent. Issue 2: Infructuousness of the Application Rule applied: The doctrine of mootness in fundamental rights litigation under Article 126 of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Court reasoned that the gravamen of the Petitioner's complaint was the issuance of the circular and the resulting imminent infringement of her rights. Because the 1st Respondent unconditionally withdrew the circular, the Petitionerâ€™s fear of imminent infringement was removed. Consequently, the Court determined that any further investigation into the vires of P10 or the collateral issues regarding the Petitioner's tenure would be superfluous and serve no practical purpose. Finding: The application was rendered infructuous due to the withdrawal of the impugned circular. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are intrinsically linked. The Courtâ€™s initial determination that the circular was ultra vires prompted the 1st Respondent to withdraw it. This withdrawal, in turn, satisfied the Court that the legal controversy had been resolved, thereby rendering the substantive determination of the constitutional infringement unnecessary and the proceedings infructuous. Conclusion The Court held that the unconditional withdrawal of the circular P10 by the 1st Respondent rendered the proceedings infructuous. Accordingly, the application is terminated. Keywords Withdrawal of circular — Ultra vires — Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) — Article 14(1)(g) — Provincial Council Act No 42 of 1987

Parties: Don Piyasena Owitigala and Others v. Karunasena and Others
Bench: Eva Wanasundera, PC.J; Aluwihare, PC, J; Sarath De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Trust Ordinance — Section 83 — Section 91 of the Evidence Ordinance — Constructive trust — Attendant circumstances — Intention to transfer beneficial interest — Deed of transfer — District Court judgment — High Court judgment
Summary: Facts The original Plaintiff, Hewage Don Piyasena, filed an action in the District Court of Matugama seeking the re-transfer of a land parcel, claiming that a deed of transfer executed in 1987 was intended to create a trust as security for a loan rather than an outright sale. The Plaintiff alleged that he remained in possession of the land and continued to pay interest on the loan to the Defendant, Karunasena Hathurusinghe, who was a rice trader. The Plaintiff further contended that the consideration stated in the deed was significantly lower than the prevailing market value of the land at the time. The District Court ruled in favor of the Plaintiff, finding that the transaction constituted a constructive trust. The Defendant appealed to the Provincial High Court of the Civil Appeals of the Western Province, which initially affirmed the District Court's judgment. However, following a Supreme Court order regarding a procedural failure to substitute the deceased Plaintiff's heirs, the High Court reheard the matter and issued a second judgment setting aside the District Court's decision, holding that the transaction was an outright sale. The substituted Plaintiffs appealed this second High Court judgment to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court erred in its interpretation and application of Section 83 of the Trusts Ordinance regarding the creation of a constructive trust. Whether the High Court failed to properly evaluate the attendant circumstances and evidence, including the adequacy of consideration and the conduct of the parties, in determining the nature of the transaction. Applicable Law Statutes: Trusts Ordinance, Section 83 Evidence Ordinance, Sections 91 and 92 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Interpretation of Section 83 of the Trusts Ordinance Rule applied: Section 83 of the Trusts Ordinance allows for the formation of a constructive trust if it can be proven through attendant circumstances that a donor did not intend to transfer the beneficial interest, notwithstanding the terms of a written document. Court's reasoning: The Court noted that while Sections 91 and 92 of the Evidence Ordinance generally restrict the admission of evidence to contradict written documents, Section 83 of the Trusts Ordinance provides a specific exception where the true intent of the parties regarding beneficial interest is at issue. The Court found that the High Court had unduly restricted the scope of this provision by failing to look beyond the face of the deed. Finding: The High Court erred in law by failing to correctly apply Section 83 to the facts, which demonstrated that the beneficial interest was never intended to pass to the Defendant. Issue 2: Evaluation of Attendant Circumstances Rule applied: The Court applied the principle that attendant circumstances, such as the disparity in land value and the conduct of the parties regarding possession and interest payments, are admissible to determine the existence of a constructive trust. Court's reasoning: The Court evaluated the evidence, noting that the consideration of Rs. 40,000 for 158 perches was less than half the market value at the time. Furthermore, the Defendantâ€™s testimony was found to be inconsistent and lacked credibility, particularly regarding his reasons for purchasing the land and his failure to produce original documentation. The Court accepted the Plaintiff's evidence that the transaction was a security arrangement for a loan, supported by the fact that the Plaintiff remained in possession and paid monthly interest. Finding: The High Court failed to properly evaluate the evidence and the attendant circumstances, which clearly indicated that the transaction was not an outright sale but a constructive trust. Interaction of Issues The findings on the legal interpretation of Section 83 and the factual evaluation of attendant circumstances are inextricably linked. The Courtâ€™s conclusion that the High Court misapplied the law (Issue 1) was reinforced by the High Courtâ€™s failure to properly weigh the factual evidence (Issue 2). By failing to appreciate the evidence of the loan arrangement and the undervaluation of the land, the High Court erroneously concluded that the statutory requirements for a constructive trust were not met. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgment of the Provincial High Court of Kalutara dated 12-01-2012 is set aside, and the judgment of the District Court of Matugama dated 05-11-2002 is affirmed. The Defendant-Appellant-Respondent-Respondent is ordered to pay costs of Rs. 90,000 to the substituted Plaintiff-Respondents-Petitioners-Appellants. Keywords Trust Ordinance — Section 83 — Section 91 of the Evidence Ordinance — Constructive trust — Attendant circumstances — Intention to transfer beneficial interest — Deed of transfer — District Court judgment — High Court judgment

Parties: de Silva v. Principal, Dharmashoka College, Ambalangoda and Others
Bench: K. Sripavan, J.; Rohini Marasinghe, J.; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Admission to school — Difficult school — Zonal Director certification — Ministry of Education circular — Administrative action — Arbitrary decision
Summary: Facts The petitioner, a teacher, applied for the admission of his child to Year 1 at Dharmashoka College, Ambalangoda, for the 2014 academic year. Following an interview, the petitioner was awarded 33 marks, which fell below the required cut-off mark of 43. The petitioner appealed the decision, but the Appeals Board maintained the original marks. The petitioner alleged that he was entitled to an additional 12 marks under clause 6.4.iii of the Ministry of Education circular P2, based on his service in a difficult school from 1991 to 1995. The petitioner submitted a letter of appointment and a letter from the Principal of Madakumbura Maha Vidyalaya to support his claim of service in a difficult school. However, the Zonal Director of Education, Elpitiya, issued a letter confirming that Madakumbura Maha Vidyalaya was not categorized as a difficult school during the relevant period. The petitioner contended that the respondents' failure to award the additional marks and admit his child was arbitrary, illegal, and a violation of his fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Issues Whether the petitioner was entitled to additional marks under clause 6.4.iii of the Ministry of Education circular P2 for service in a difficult school. Whether the respondents violated the petitioner's fundamental rights guaranteed under Article 12(1) of the Constitution by denying his child admission to the school. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Entitlement to additional marks Rule applied: Clause 6.4.iii of the Ministry of Education circular P2, which requires certification by the Zonal Director of Education that a school is categorized as a "difficult school" to qualify for additional marks. Court's reasoning: The court examined the evidence provided by the petitioner, including his appointment letter and a letter from the Principal of Madakumbura Maha Vidyalaya. The court noted that the Principal's letter only referred to the area as "difficult" rather than the school itself. Crucially, the Zonal Director of Education, Elpitiya, explicitly confirmed in letter 1R10 that the school was not categorized as a difficult school during the relevant period. The court rejected the argument that the Zonal Director's prior endorsement on a document constituted a formal certification of the school's status, especially given the subsequent clarification in 1R10. Finding: The petitioner failed to establish that he served in a school categorized as a difficult school and was therefore not entitled to the additional marks. Issue 2: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka regarding the right to equality and equal protection of the law. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the decision to deny admission was based on the petitioner's failure to meet the required cut-off marks after a fair assessment process. Since the petitioner was not entitled to the additional marks he claimed, the decision of the interview and appeal boards was not arbitrary, illegal, or capricious. Finding: The respondents did not infringe upon the petitioner's fundamental rights under Article 12(1). Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the failure of the petitioner to satisfy the evidentiary requirements for additional marks under the Ministry of Education circular directly resulted in the court finding that the admission process was conducted fairly and legally, thereby negating the claim of a fundamental rights violation. Conclusion The court dismissed the petition. No order for costs was made. Keywords Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Admission to school — Difficult school — Zonal Director certification — Ministry of Education circular — Administrative action — Arbitrary decision

Parties: Kanagaratnam and Others v. Sri Bodhiraja Foundation and Others
Bench: Mohan Pieris, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Rohini Marasinghe, J.
Keywords: Animal sacrifice — Butchers Ordinance — Cruelty to Animals Act — Religious freedom — Fundamental rights — Writ of mandamus — Trade definition — Slaughter of animals — Public order — Protection of public health
Summary: Facts The Petitioners, being the 7th, 8th, and 9th Respondents in the original Court of Appeal proceedings, appealed against a judgment dated 29.08.2013. The original application was filed by the Respondents, who sought writs of prohibition and mandamus regarding animal sacrifices conducted at the Sri Bhadra Kali Amman Kovil in Munneswaram. The Court of Appeal issued a writ of mandamus directing the relevant authorities to prevent the slaughter of animals at the Kovil if the Petitioners did not possess a license under the Butchers Ordinance or if they violated the provisions of the Cruelty to the Animals Act. The Petitioners contended that the annual animal sacrifice is a long-standing religious ritual protected under the Constitution and does not constitute a trade. They argued that the Court of Appeal erred in its interpretation of the term trade within the Butchers Ordinance. The Respondents maintained that the slaughter of animals at the Kovil, which involves the distribution of meat as alms, falls under the regulatory scope of the Butchers Ordinance and the Cruelty to the Animals Act. The Supreme Court considered whether the Court of Appeal correctly interpreted the relevant legislation and whether the religious nature of the practice exempted the Petitioners from compliance with these laws. Issues Whether the Court of Appeal erred in law by interpreting the term "trade" in Section 4(1) of the Butchers Ordinance to include the ritual slaughter of animals at the Kovil. Whether the requirement to obtain a license under the Butchers Ordinance and compliance with the Cruelty to the Animals Act constitutes an unreasonable interference with the Petitioners' fundamental rights under Articles 10 and 14(1)(e) of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Butchers Ordinance Cruelty to the Animals Act Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka Cases Referred to: Balasunderam v. Kalimuththu - [79/v1-nlr-361] Premalal Perera v. Weerasuriya - [1985/v2-sri-lr-177] R v. Minister for the Civil Service ex p Council of Civil Service Unions - [1985] Analysis Issue 1: Interpretation of "trade" Rule applied: Section 4(1) and Section 2 of the Butchers Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the definition of "butcher" in Section 2 is inclusive, covering any person who slaughters animals or exposes meat for sale. The Court reasoned that the slaughter of animals at the Kovil, which involves turning carcasses into meat for distribution as alms, cannot be divorced from the process of trade. The Court rejected a restrictive interpretation of "trade," finding that the legislative intent in the domestic context is expansive and covers the entire process of slaughter and preparation. Finding: The Court of Appeal did not err in law; the ritual slaughter constitutes "trade" under the Butchers Ordinance, necessitating a license. Issue 2: Fundamental rights and legislative compliance Rule applied: Articles 10, 14(1)(e), and 15(7) of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Court acknowledged that while religious beliefs are protected, the Butchers Ordinance and the Cruelty to the Animals Act are neutral, non-religion-specific enactments. The Court held that the freedom to manifest religion under Article 14(1)(e) is not absolute. Under Article 15(7), fundamental rights may be restricted by law in the interest of public health, morality, or the general welfare. The Court concluded that the state's regulatory framework for animal welfare constitutes a valid restriction. Finding: The requirement to comply with the law is not an unconstitutional interference with religious freedom. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. By determining that the ritual slaughter falls within the definition of "trade" (Issue 1), the Court established that the activity is subject to secular regulatory oversight. This legal classification allows the Court to apply the limitations permitted under Article 15(7) of the Constitution (Issue 2), confirming that religious practice does not grant immunity from neutral, public-interest legislation. Conclusion The Supreme Court affirmed the judgment of the Court of Appeal and dismissed the appeal. The Court held that the ritual slaughter of animals at the Kovil is subject to the licensing requirements of the Butchers Ordinance and the provisions of the Cruelty to the Animals Act. Keywords Animal sacrifice — Butchers Ordinance — Cruelty to Animals Act — Religious freedom — Fundamental rights — Writ of mandamus — Trade definition — Slaughter of animals — Public order — Protection of public health

Parties: Wijewardhana v. Kurunegala Plantations Limited and Others
Bench: Mohan Pieris, J; Priyasath Dep, J; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Executive and administrative action — Contract law — State entities — Arbitrary action — Lease agreements — Judicial review
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Kalidasage Roshan Chaminda Wijewardhana, entered into a lease agreement with the 1st Respondent, Kurunegala Plantations Limited, on December 2, 2009, to operate a quarry on a rocky site. The Petitioner paid the full lease rental for the three-year term. Although the agreement provided for an additional month to obtain necessary permits, the Petitioner faced significant delays in commencing operations due to the obstructionist behavior of the Estate Superintendent, who was an employee of the 1st Respondent. Despite the Petitioner's complaints, the 1st Respondent failed to intervene effectively for a prolonged period. The Petitioner was only able to enter the site on August 8, 2011, after the 1st Respondent replaced the Superintendent. Upon the expiration of the lease term, the Petitioner requested an extension of the agreement to compensate for the time lost due to the initial obstruction. The 1st Respondent refused this request, relying on the terms of the contract. The Petitioner subsequently invoked the fundamental rights jurisdiction of the Supreme Court, alleging that the 1st Respondent's refusal to extend the lease and the prior conduct of the Respondents constituted an infringement of his rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The Respondents raised a preliminary objection, contending that the dispute was purely contractual and did not constitute executive or administrative action subject to fundamental rights jurisdiction. Issues Whether the actions of a state entity in the performance or non-performance of a contract constitute "executive or administrative action" under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the 1st Respondent's refusal to extend the lease agreement is subject to judicial review under the fundamental rights jurisdiction of the Supreme Court. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 12(1), 12(2), 12(3), 17, 126. Cases Referred to: Roberts v. Ratnayake - [1986/v2-sri-lr-36] Wijenaike v. Air Lanka - [1990/v1-sri-lr-293] Gunaratne v. Ceylon Petroleum Corporation - [1996/v1-sri-lr-315] Wickrematunga v. Anuruddha Ratwatte - [1998/v1-sri-lr-201] Wickremasinghe v. Ceylon Petroleum Corporation - [2001/v2-sri-lr-409] Analysis Issue 1: Nature of executive and administrative action in contract Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution regarding the right to equality and the principle that public authorities must conform to constitutional requirements even in the sphere of contract. Court's reasoning: The Court reviewed the evolution of its jurisprudence, noting a shift from the restrictive "threshold" approach in Roberts v. Ratnayake and Wijenaike v. Air Lanka to a broader interpretation. Relying on Gunaratne v. Ceylon Petroleum Corporation, Wickrematunga v. Anuruddha Ratwatte, and Wickremasinghe v. Ceylon Petroleum Corporation, the Court held that public authorities cannot evade constitutional duties by disguising their activities as private contracts. The Court reasoned that the principle of equality applies to state entities regardless of the stage of the contract. Finding: The actions of the Respondents, as a state entity, fall within the scope of executive and administrative action, and the dispute is not excluded from fundamental rights jurisdiction merely because it arises from a contract. Issue 2: Reviewability of the refusal to extend the lease Rule applied: The principle that state entities must act without arbitrariness and in accordance with the constitutional guarantee of equality. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that the 1st Respondent acted in an irresponsible and arbitrary manner by failing to address the Superintendent's obstruction, which prevented the Petitioner from utilizing the land for which he had already paid. The Court determined that the refusal to extend the lease should not be viewed through the narrow lens of contract law but through the broader perspective of the exercise of power by a state agency. Finding: The 1st Respondent's refusal to extend the lease is subject to review under Article 12(1) because the state entity exercised its power in an arbitrary manner. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the determination that the Respondents' conduct constitutes "executive or administrative action" (Issue 1) provides the necessary jurisdictional basis to review the specific act of refusing the lease extension (Issue 2). By rejecting the dichotomous approach of pre- and post-contractual stages, the Court established that the arbitrary nature of the 1st Respondent's conduct throughout the contractual relationship justifies the exercise of its constitutional jurisdiction. Conclusion The preliminary objection raised by the Respondents is overruled. The Court held that the matter is justiciable under Article 12(1) of the Constitution and directed that the application proceed to be heard on its merits. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Executive and administrative action — Contract law — State entities — Arbitrary action — Lease agreements — Judicial review

Parties: Lanka Kect (Pvt) Limited v. Wickramasinghe and Others
Bench: Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.; Buwaneka Aluwihare, J.; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Contract of hire — Contract of supply — Acceptance of tender — Breach of contract — Rental payments — Option to purchase — Delivery of goods — Port of Colombo — Unilateral conversion of contract
Summary: Facts Lanka Kect (Pvt) Limited, the Plaintiff, entered into a contract with the 1st Defendant, the Director of Buildings, for the design, supply, installation, and operation of a prop-up assembly for the reconstruction of the Jaffna library. The Plaintiff submitted a bid offering to supply the equipment on a rental basis, stipulating that rental charges would commence upon delivery to the port of Colombo and continue until the equipment was returned to the same location. The 1st Defendant accepted these terms, and a formal contract was executed. The 1st Defendant subsequently defaulted on rental payments. Although a deposit was initially set off against arrears, the 1st Defendant failed to make further payments and refused to return the equipment to the Plaintiff. The Plaintiff initiated legal action for breach of contract, seeking recovery of overdue rentals and the return of the equipment or its value. The 1st Defendant contended that the agreement was a contract of supply or a hire contract with an option to purchase, and further alleged that the Plaintiff had delayed the delivery of the equipment. The Commercial High Court ruled in favor of the Plaintiff, prompting the 1st Defendant and the Attorney General to appeal the decision. Issues Whether the 1st Defendant was entitled to unilaterally convert the contract of hire into a contract of supply or purchase. Whether the Plaintiff breached the contract by delaying the delivery of the equipment. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 755(3) High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996, Section 5(1) Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Conversion of contract Rule applied: The court applied the principles of contractual interpretation, specifically examining the express terms of the written agreement (P14) and the parties' intentions. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the contract (P14) contained no clause granting the 1st Defendant an option to purchase the equipment. Testimony from the 1st Defendantâ€™s own witness confirmed that the department opted for a hire arrangement because the equipment was not of long-term utility. Furthermore, the 1st Defendantâ€™s attempt to seek the Plaintiff's permission to convert the contract into a supply contract in 2001 contradicted the claim that such an option already existed. Finding: The 1st Defendant had no legal basis to unilaterally convert the hire contract into a contract of supply or purchase. Issue 2: Alleged delay in delivery Rule applied: The court interpreted the specific performance obligations defined in the contract regarding the place of delivery. Court's reasoning: The contract explicitly limited the Plaintiffâ€™s obligation to delivering the equipment to the port of Colombo. The responsibility for transporting the goods from the port to the work site in Jaffna rested solely with the 1st Defendant. Evidence provided by the 1st Defendantâ€™s own witness confirmed that there was no delay by the Plaintiff in fulfilling the delivery obligation to the port of Colombo. Finding: The contention that the Plaintiff delayed the performance of the contract was rejected. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the contract was strictly one of hire—and not a purchase—solidified the Plaintiff's right to demand the return of the equipment and the payment of rentals. Simultaneously, the rejection of the delay claim removed any justification for the 1st Defendantâ€™s refusal to pay or return the equipment, thereby confirming the breach of contract. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed with costs. The judgment of the Commercial High Court is upheld. Keywords Contract of hire — Contract of supply — Acceptance of tender — Breach of contract — Rental payments — Option to purchase — Delivery of goods — Port of Colombo — Unilateral conversion of contract

Parties: K.H.S Pushpadeva v. Senok Trade Combine Ltd.
Bench: Saleem Marsoof P.C., J.; Rohini Marasinghe, J.; Aluwihare P.C., J.
Keywords: affidavit — preliminary objections — Civil Procedure Code — Industrial Disputes Act — personal knowledge — technical irregularities — validity of affidavit — Oaths Ordinance — appeal process — legal representation — due diligence — justice — procedural compliance — capacity to swear an affidavit — principal officer
Summary: Facts This case revolves around an application for leave to appeal against a judgment delivered by the High Court of Colombo, which exercised appellate jurisdiction over an order from the Labour Tribunal of Colombo. The applicant, K.H.S Pushpadeva, challenged the validity of an affidavit submitted by the respondent, Senok Trade Combine Ltd., on several grounds. The affidavit in question was purportedly filed by Jerome Anil Ratnayake, who claimed to be the Director in charge of Human Resources and Administration of the respondent company. The applicant raised preliminary objections regarding the affidavit's validity, arguing that it did not conform to the legal requirements set forth in the Civil Procedure Code, particularly Form 75. The applicant contended that the affidavit lacked proper affirmation, did not specify the affirmant's religion, and contained discrepancies in the address of the deponent. The applicant further argued that the affirmant did not have the capacity to swear the affidavit on behalf of the company, as he was not a director as defined by the Companies Act. The case was argued on 5 March 2014, with written submissions filed subsequently. The Supreme Court had to determine whether the affidavit was valid and whether the preliminary objections raised by the applicant warranted dismissal of the appeal. Issues The primary issues in this case can be framed as follows: Whether the affidavit filed by the respondent was valid in law. Whether the preliminary objections raised by the applicant regarding the affidavit's compliance with Form 75 of the Civil Procedure Code were justified. Whether the affirmant had the capacity to swear the affidavit on behalf of the respondent company. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code — Industrial Disputes Act — Oaths Ordinance Cases Referred to: Senanayake v. Commissioner of National Housing and Others - [2005 1 SLR 182] De Silva and others v. L.B. Finance Ltd - [1993 1 SLR 371] Trico Freighters (PVT) Ltd v. Yang Civil Engineering Lanka (PVT) Ltd - [2000 2 SLR 136] Sooriya Enterprises (International) Limited v. Michael White & Company Ltd - [2002 3 SLR 371] Kobbekaduwa v. Jayawardene - [1983 1 SLR 419] Distilleries Company v. Kariyawasam & Others - [2001 SLR 119] Mohamed Facy v. Mohamed Azath Sanoon Sally and Others - [S.C. Appeal 4/2004] Analysis Issue 1: Whether the affidavit filed by the respondent was valid in law. Rule applied: The validity of affidavits is governed by the provisions of the Civil Procedure Code and the Oaths Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The court examined the objections raised by the applicant regarding the affidavit's validity. The applicant argued that the affidavit did not commence with the required affirmation and that the affirmant's religion was not specified. The court noted that while the affidavit did not strictly adhere to Form 75, the essential requirement of affirmation was present in the jurat of the affidavit. The court cited the case of De Silva and others v. L.B. Finance Ltd, where it was held that the absence of the word 'affirmed' in the jurat does not invalidate the affidavit. The court emphasized that the fundamental obligation of a deponent is to tell the truth, and the purpose of the oath or affirmation is to reinforce that obligation. The court also addressed the applicant's contention regarding the incorrect address in the affidavit, stating that such irregularities are technical in nature and do not affect the validity of the affidavit. The court concluded that the affidavit was valid despite the technical deficiencies pointed out by the applicant. Finding: The court found that the affidavit filed by the respondent was valid in law. Issue 2: Whether the preliminary objections raised by the applicant regarding the affidavit's compliance with Form 75 of the Civil Procedure Code were justified. Rule applied: Compliance with procedural rules is essential, but technical irregularities should not prevent the court from dispensing justice. Court's reasoning: The court acknowledged the importance of adhering to procedural rules but emphasized that not all irregularities warrant dismissal of an application. Citing the case of Distilleries Company v. Kariyawasam & Others, the court reiterated that technical irregularities should not frustrate civil proceedings if they do not cause prejudice to the other party. The court found that the errors in the affidavit were minor and did not impact the substantive rights of the parties involved. The court also noted that the applicant had not demonstrated any prejudice resulting from the alleged irregularities. Finding: The court concluded that the preliminary objections raised by the applicant were not justified and did not warrant dismissal of the appeal. Issue 3: Whether the affirmant had the capacity to swear the affidavit on behalf of the respondent company. Rule applied: Section 183A(b) of the Civil Procedure Code allows principal officers of a corporation to make affidavits if they have personal knowledge of the facts. Court's reasoning: The court examined the qualifications of the affirmant, Jerome Anil Ratnayake, and determined that he was indeed a principal officer of the respondent company. The court noted that the definition of a director under the Companies Act does not preclude someone holding a position such as Director of Human Resources and Administration from being considered a principal officer. The court emphasized that the key requirement is that the affirmant must have personal knowledge of the facts of the case, which Ratnayake did. The court cited relevant case law to support its position that the capacity to swear an affidavit is not limited to formal titles but includes individuals who hold significant positions within the organization. Finding: The court found that the affirmant had the capacity to swear the affidavit on behalf of the respondent company. Interaction of Issues The issues raised in this case are interrelated, as the validity of the affidavit directly impacts the court's ability to consider the appeal. The preliminary objections regarding the affidavit's compliance with procedural rules and the affirmant's capacity to swear the affidavit are all part of the broader question of whether the court can accept the affidavit as valid evidence in the appeal process. The court's analysis of these issues demonstrates a commitment to ensuring that technicalities do not obstruct the pursuit of justice. Conclusion The Supreme Court ultimately rejected the preliminary objections raised by the applicant and held that the affidavit filed by the respondent was valid.
