The court emphasized the importance of substance over form, allowing the appeal to proceed despite the technical irregularities identified. The judgment underscores the principle that courts should not dismiss cases based on minor procedural errors that do not affect the rights of the parties involved. The court's decision reflects a balanced approach to legal technicalities, prioritizing the pursuit of justice while maintaining the integrity of the legal process. Keywords: affidavit — preliminary objections — Civil Procedure Code — Industrial Disputes Act — personal knowledge — technical irregularities — validity of affidavit — Oaths Ordinance — appeal process — legal representation — due diligence — justice — procedural compliance — capacity to swear an affidavit — principal officer
Counsel: Suren de Silva instructed by Ms. M.N.T Pieris for Respondent-Respondent-Petitioner. A. Sri Nammuni for Applicant-Appellant-Respondent.

Parties: Lanka Kect (Pvt) Limited v. DA Wickramasinghe and Others
Bench: Eva Wanasundera PC, J.; Buwaneka Aluwihare PC, J.; Sisira J de Abrew J.
Keywords: breach of contract — tender acceptance — rental agreement — delivery obligations — contract interpretation — hire vs. purchase — evidence evaluation — appellate review — judgment confirmation — legal principles
Summary: Facts This case revolves around an appeal by the defendant-appellants against a judgment delivered by the Commercial High Court on 11 January 2008. The plaintiff, Lanka Kect (Pvt) Limited, claimed that the first defendant, DA Wickramasinghe, who was the Director of Buildings at the Buildings Department, was in breach of a contract regarding the design, supply, installation, and operation of a prop-up assembly for the reconstruction of the Jaffna library. The plaintiff submitted a bid on 27 August 1999, which was accepted by the first defendant on 6 September 1999. The contract stipulated that the plaintiff would supply equipment on a rental basis, with specific payment terms including advance rental and daily rental charges from the time of delivery to the port of Colombo. The first defendant was responsible for transporting the equipment to Jaffna and returning it to Colombo. However, the first defendant defaulted on rental payments, leading the plaintiff to file a case for breach of contract, seeking overdue rentals and the return of the equipment or its value. The High Court ruled in favor of the plaintiff, leading to the appeal by the defendants. Issues The primary issues in this case are: Whether the first defendant could unilaterally convert the contract of hire into a contract of supply or purchase. Whether there was any delay on the part of the plaintiff in delivering the equipment. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No 10 of 1996. Cases Referred to: Lanka Kect (Pvt) Limited v. DA Wickramasinghe - [SC CHC 19/2008]. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the first defendant could unilaterally convert the contract of hire into a contract of supply or purchase. Rule applied: The principle of contract interpretation and the necessity of mutual consent for contract modification. Court's reasoning: The court examined the terms of the contract and the evidence presented. It was established that the contract was explicitly for hire, with no provisions allowing for conversion to a sale or supply contract. The first defendant's argument that he could unilaterally convert the contract was rejected based on the lack of any such clause in the contract. The court noted that the first defendant's own witness admitted there was no clause permitting such a conversion. Furthermore, the first defendant's actions, including seeking permission to convert the contract, indicated that he understood the contract to be one of hire. The court emphasized that the intention of the parties at the time of contract formation was crucial, and the evidence supported that the parties intended to enter into a rental agreement, not a sale. Finding: The court concluded that the first defendant could not unilaterally convert the contract of hire into a contract of supply or purchase. Issue 2: Whether there was any delay on the part of the plaintiff in delivering the equipment. Rule applied: The obligations of the parties under the contract and the standard of performance required. Court's reasoning: The court considered the evidence regarding the delivery obligations. It was established that the plaintiff's responsibility was limited to delivering the equipment to the port of Colombo, and there was no obligation to deliver to Jaffna. The first defendant's witness confirmed that there was no delay in the plaintiff's performance of the contract. The court found that the first defendant's failure to transport the equipment was not the plaintiff's responsibility. The court reiterated that the terms of the contract clearly delineated the responsibilities of both parties, and the plaintiff had fulfilled its obligations. Finding: The court determined that there was no delay on the part of the plaintiff in delivering the equipment. Interaction of Issues The issues are interconnected as the first defendant's claim of conversion of the contract was partly based on the assertion of delay in delivery. The court's findings on both issues reinforced the conclusion that the first defendant was in breach of the contract and could not escape liability by claiming a conversion that was not supported by the contract terms. Conclusion The Supreme Court upheld the judgment of the High Court, dismissing the appeal with costs. The court found no grounds to interfere with the lower court's decision, affirming that the contract was one of hire and that the plaintiff had fulfilled its obligations under the contract. The appeal was dismissed, confirming the plaintiff's right to claim overdue rentals and the return of the equipment. Keywords breach of contract — tender acceptance — rental agreement — delivery obligations — contract interpretation — hire vs. purchase — evidence evaluation — appellate review — judgment confirmation — legal principles
Counsel: Viraj Dyaratne DSG for the Defendant-Appellants; GJT Alagaratnam PC with Lasantha Gurusinghe for the Plaintiff-Respondent

Parties: Bandara v. Ekanayake and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC. J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC.J
Keywords: Pradeshiya Sabha Act — Inquiry into misconduct — Extensions of time — Mandatory vs. directory provisions — Ultra vires — Minister's powers — Good governance — Judicial review
Summary: Facts Sarath Dharma Siri Bandara was the Chairman of the Pathahewaheta Pradeshiya Sabha. The Minister in Charge of Local Authorities, Central Province, Sarath Ekanayake, initiated an inquiry into allegations of incompetence and mismanagement against the Chairman. Pursuant to Section 185(2) of the Pradeshiya Sabha Act No. 15, the Minister appointed an Inquiring Officer to investigate and report on these matters. The Chairman was suspended from office pending the outcome of this inquiry. The inquiry process exceeded the initial three-month period stipulated in the statute. The Inquiring Officer requested, and was granted, four extensions of time to conclude the proceedings. The Chairman participated in the inquiry throughout these extended periods without raising objections. In fact, the Chairmanâ€™s own representative requested that the inquiry be held only once a week, which necessitated the extensions. Following the conclusion of the inquiry and the submission of the report, the Minister removed the Chairman from office. The Chairman challenged this removal in the Court of Appeal, which quashed the Minister's order on the basis that the inquiry exceeded the mandatory three-month time limit, rendering the report and the subsequent removal illegal and ultra vires. The Minister appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the three-month period stipulated in Section 185(2) of the Pradeshiya Sabha Act No. 15 for the conclusion of an inquiry is mandatory or directory. Whether the extensions of time granted to the Inquiring Officer rendered the resulting report and the Minister's subsequent decision to remove the Chairman ultra vires and illegal. Applicable Law Statutes: Pradeshiya Sabha Act No. 15 Commission of Inquiry Act No. 17 Cases Referred to: Nagalingam v. Lakshman de Mel - [78-nlr-237] Mohamed Ishak v. Morais - [1996/v1-sri-lr-145] Mohamed v. H Jayaratne - [2002/v3-sri-lr-169] Analysis Issue 1: Nature of the time limit Rule applied: Section 185(2) of the Pradeshiya Sabha Act No. 15. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the intention of the legislature in enacting Section 185(2) was to ensure good governance and transparency. Interpreting the three-month limit as mandatory would defeat this purpose, as it could lead to the rejection of a report based on merits simply due to procedural delays, or encourage parties to manipulate the timeline to avoid an adverse finding. The Court noted that the provision is directory, as the primary objective is to facilitate an informed decision by the Minister. Finding: The three-month time limit in Section 185(2) is directory, not mandatory. Issue 2: Validity of extensions and the Minister's decision Rule applied: Principles of acquiescence and statutory interpretation of directory provisions. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the Chairman participated in the inquiry without objection and even requested the schedule that necessitated the extensions. Under the principle of acquiescence, a party cannot participate in proceedings and later challenge the jurisdiction of the authority based on time limits they helped create. Furthermore, because the provision is directory, the extensions granted by the Minister were not ultra vires. The Minister acted within his authority by considering the report submitted after the initial three-month period. Finding: The extensions were lawful, and the Minister's decision based on the report was valid and not ultra vires. Interaction of Issues The findings are intrinsically linked; because the Court determined that the time limit is directory rather than mandatory, the extensions granted by the Minister were necessarily valid. This legal characterization of the statute effectively nullified the basis for the Court of Appeal's decision, as the procedural delay did not strip the Inquiring Officer of the authority to report or the Minister of the authority to act upon that report. Conclusion The Supreme Court set aside the judgment of the Court of Appeal and dismissed the Writ application. The appeal was allowed, and the Petitioner-Respondent was ordered to pay costs of Rs. 50,000 to the Respondent-Petitioner. Keywords Pradeshiya Sabha Act — Inquiry into misconduct — Extensions of time — Mandatory vs. directory provisions — Ultra vires — Minister's powers — Good governance — Judicial review

Parties: Fernando v. Indrajith and Fernando
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC. J; Sripavan, J; Eva Wanasundera, PC. J
Keywords: Road accident — Negligence — Damages — Contributory negligence — Quantum of damages — Mitigation of damages — Prospective pecuniary loss — Birth right — Usability of limb
Summary: Facts The Appellant, a student in Grade 10, was involved in a road accident while traveling in a school van in Panadura. A lorry driven by the 1st Respondent and owned by the 2nd Respondent collided with the van, causing the Appellant to suffer a fracture in his right arm and hand. The Appellant underwent three operations and extensive physiotherapy to restore the functionality of his limb. The 1st Respondent pleaded guilty to negligent driving in the Magistrate's Court. The Appellant subsequently filed a civil action in the District Court, which awarded him damages of Rs. 800,000. The Respondents appealed this decision to the Provincial High Court of Civil Appeal, arguing that there was contributory negligence and that the damages were excessive. The High Court rejected the claim of contributory negligence but reduced the quantum of damages to Rs. 600,000, reasoning that the Appellant's subsequent pursuit of higher studies abroad served as a factor to mitigate the impact of his injuries. The Appellant then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Appellant's subsequent pursuit of higher studies abroad should be reckoned as a factor to mitigate the quantum of damages for the injuries sustained. Whether the reduction of damages by the Provincial High Court of Civil Appeal was legally justified. Applicable Law Statutes: Not specified Cases Referred to: Gafoor v. Wilson - [1990/v1-sri-lr-142] Analysis Issue 1: Mitigation of damages through subsequent achievements Rule applied: The court applied the principles of the Aquilian action, which allows for compensation for loss in respect of property, business, or prospective gains, and the principle that damages must be assessed based on the detriment suffered by the victim rather than subsequent improvements in the victim's circumstances. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that the victim's birth right to an intact limb was violated by the Respondents' negligence. It held that subsequent achievements, such as pursuing higher studies, are not relevant factors to reduce the liability of a tortfeasor. The court emphasized that the focus must remain on the actual suffering, the cost of medical treatment, and the loss of functionality of the limb. It rejected the notion that a victim's determination or success following an injury should benefit the party responsible for the injury. Finding: The Appellant's proceeding abroad for higher studies should not have been reckoned to mitigate the effect of the injury. Issue 2: Justification for reduction of damages Rule applied: The court applied the principle that damages for personal injury must address the victim's loss of capability and bodily/mental suffering caused by the tortious act. Court's reasoning: The court found that the High Court erred by considering the Appellant's academic success as a mitigating factor. It determined that the High Court's reasoning for the reduction was baseless. The court clarified that only the negative consequences (the "worse things") resulting from the accident are relevant to the assessment of damages, not the positive outcomes that may occur despite the injury. Finding: The reduction of damages from Rs. 800,000 to Rs. 600,000 was incorrect. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are interdependent; the determination that the Appellant's academic success was legally irrelevant to the assessment of damages directly invalidated the High Court's basis for reducing the award. By resolving the first issue in favor of the Appellant, the court effectively restored the original assessment of damages made by the District Court. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, and the judgment of the Provincial High Court of Civil Appeal is set aside. The judgment of the District Court is affirmed, and the Appellant is entitled to the original award of Rs. 800,000, along with legal interest from the date of the District Court judgment and costs of suit in all courts. Keywords Road accident — Negligence — Damages — Contributory negligence — Quantum of damages — Mitigation of damages — Prospective pecuniary loss — Birth right — Usability of limb

Parties: Alagaratnam v. Chandrasiri and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC., Acting C.J, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC., J
Keywords: Preliminary objection — Time bar — Article 126(2) of the Constitution — Section 13 of the Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka Act No. 21 — Transfer of public servant — Complaint to Human Rights Commission — Fundamental rights application
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Alagaratnam Manoranjan, was employed as the Deputy Chief Secretary/Finance in the Provincial Treasury of the Northern Provincial Council. By a letter dated 8 January 2013, the Petitioner was informed that he was temporarily attached to the Chief Secretaryâ€™s Secretariat with immediate effect. The Petitioner complied with this order and reported to the Chief Secretaryâ€™s Secretariat. Subsequently, the Petitioner wrote to the 1st Respondent on 27 February 2013 requesting to be reinstated in his former position. On 4 April 2013, the Petitioner was issued a letter directing him to the Training Centre, Management Development and Training Institute, Mannar. The Petitioner filed a complaint with the Human Rights Commission on 10 April 2013. The Petitioner later filed the present fundamental rights application before the Supreme Court, contending that the transfer was wrongful and that the period spent before the Human Rights Commission should be excluded from the computation of the one-month time limit for filing a fundamental rights application under Article 126(2) of the Constitution. The Respondents raised a preliminary objection, asserting that the application was time-barred as it was filed more than one month after the initial transfer on 8 January 2013. Issues Whether the Petitionerâ€™s fundamental rights application is time-barred under Article 126(2) of the Constitution. Whether the Petitioner is entitled to the benefit of Section 13(1) of the Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka Act No. 21 of 1996 to exclude the period of inquiry before the Human Rights Commission from the computation of the one-month limitation period. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 126(2) Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka Act No. 21 of 1996, Section 13(1) Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka Act No. 21 of 1996, Section 14 Cases Referred to: Roshan Mahesh Ukwatta v. Sub Inspector Marasinghe - [sc-appeal-252/2006] Namasivayam v. Gunawardena - [1989/v1-sri-lr-394] Subasinghe v. IGP - [sc-spl-application-16/1999] Divalage Upalika Ranaweera v. Sub Inspector Vinisias - [sc-application-654/2003] Kariyawasam v. Southern Provincial Road Development Authority - [2007/v2-sri-lr-33] Analysis Issue 1: Time-bar under Article 126(2) Rule applied: Article 126(2) of the Constitution requires an application for the infringement of fundamental rights to be filed within one month of the alleged infringement. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the act complained of—the transfer from the position of Deputy Chief Secretary/Finance—occurred on 8 January 2013. The Petitionerâ€™s subsequent correspondence and actions confirmed his awareness and compliance with this transfer. The court rejected the Petitioner's argument that the later letter dated 4 April 2013 constituted the relevant date of infringement, noting that the Petitioner was merely being moved from his temporary attachment. Finding: The application was filed well beyond the one-month period stipulated by the Constitution. Issue 2: Applicability of Section 13(1) of the Human Rights Commission Act Rule applied: Section 13(1) of the Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka Act No. 21 of 1996 allows for the exclusion of time spent in an inquiry before the Commission, provided the complaint was made to the Commission within one month of the alleged infringement. Court's reasoning: The court found that the Petitioner failed to file his complaint with the Human Rights Commission within one month of the 8 January 2013 transfer. Furthermore, the Petitioner failed to provide evidence that an inquiry was actually pending before the Commission at the time the Supreme Court application was filed. The court emphasized that statutory provisions cannot be used to circumvent constitutional time limits when the initial complaint to the Commission was itself filed out of time. Finding: The Petitioner is not entitled to the benefit of Section 13(1) of the Act. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; because the Petitioner failed to file a timely complaint with the Human Rights Commission, he could not satisfy the statutory requirements to toll the limitation period under Section 13(1). Consequently, the failure to meet the threshold for the statutory exception rendered the application subject to the strict one-month limitation period prescribed by Article 126(2) of the Constitution. Conclusion The preliminary objection is upheld, and the application is dismissed in limine. No order for costs is made. Keywords Preliminary objection — Time bar — Article 126(2) of the Constitution — Section 13 of the Human Rights Commission of Sri Lanka Act No. 21 — Transfer of public servant — Complaint to Human Rights Commission — Fundamental rights application

Parties: Perera and Others v. Gunasekera and Others
Bench: K. Sripavan, J.; E.Wanasundera, P.C., J; B. Aluwihare, P.C., J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Administrative action — Supreme Court Rules 1990 — Rule 44(1)(b) — Necessary parties — Maintainability of application — Impleading of authorities — Due diligence
Summary: Facts The Petitioners, Kariyawasam Widanarachilage Gathidu Ugeeshwara Perera and Kariyawasam Widanarachilage Dimuthu Sanjeewa Perera, filed this application seeking a direction for the admission of the first Petitioner to Grade 1 at Royal College, Colombo, for the year 2011. The Petitioners alleged that the refusal to admit the first Petitioner violated their fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The admission process was governed by a circular issued by the Ministry of Education, which involved an interview panel and an appeal board. The Petitioners were not selected for admission and subsequently filed this application, naming the Principal of Royal College, the Director of National Schools, the Secretary of the Ministry of Education, and the Attorney-General as Respondents. The Petitioners did not name the individual members of the interview panel or the appeal board as respondents, although they included a prayer in their petition requesting the court to direct the first Respondent to provide the names of those panel members. The Petitioners failed to support this specific prayer for the disclosure of names prior to the matter being taken up for hearing. Issues Whether the failure to name the members of the interview panel and the appeal board as respondents constitutes a fatal irregularity under Rule 44(1)(b) of the Supreme Court Rules 1990. Whether the Petitioners' lack of diligence in pursuing the disclosure of the identities of the panel members warrants the dismissal of the application. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 126(2) Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 136 Supreme Court Rules 1990, Rule 44(1)(b) Cases Referred to: Sudath Rohana v. Mohamed Zeena - [sc-hc-cala-11/2010] Batugahage Don Udaya Shantha v. Jeevan Kumaratunga - [sc-spl-la-49/2010] Analysis Issue 1: Non-joinder of necessary parties Rule applied: Rule 44(1)(b) of the Supreme Court Rules 1990, which mandates that a petitioner must name as respondents the Attorney-General and the person or persons who have infringed or are about to infringe the fundamental right. Court's reasoning: The court interpreted the word "shall" in the rule as an obligatory command rather than a discretionary power. Because the alleged prejudice to the Petitioners stemmed directly from the decisions of the interview panel and the appeal board, these bodies were necessary parties. The court reasoned that it cannot adjudicate the validity of an administrative act in the absence of the authority that performed that act. Finding: The failure to implead the interview board and the appeal board constitutes a fatal irregularity. Issue 2: Lack of diligence Rule applied: The courtâ€™s equitable jurisdiction and the requirement for due diligence in fundamental rights litigation. Court's reasoning: While the court acknowledged its power to add parties, it emphasized that the Petitioners failed to support their prayer for the disclosure of the panel members' names at any point, including when leave to proceed was granted. Given that three years had elapsed since the grant of leave, the court determined that the Petitioners failed to exercise the necessary diligence to pursue their remedy. Finding: The Petitioners' lack of diligence and the resulting delay justify the rejection of the petition. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are mutually reinforcing. The failure to comply with the procedural requirement of naming necessary parties (Issue 1) was exacerbated by the Petitioners' failure to utilize available procedural mechanisms to identify those parties in a timely manner (Issue 2). Together, these failures rendered the application procedurally defective and incapable of adjudication. Conclusion The preliminary objection raised by the Respondents is upheld. The application is dismissed without costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Administrative action — Supreme Court Rules 1990 — Rule 44(1)(b) — Necessary parties — Maintainability of application — Impleading of authorities — Due diligence

Parties: Senasinghe v. Cabraal and Others
Bench: K. Sripavan, J.; Rohini Marasinghe, J.; Sarath De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Investment decisions — Foreign exchange reserves — Monetary Law Act — Arbitrary exercise of power — Foreign Exchange Reserves Management Guidelines — Central Bank of Sri Lanka — Risk management strategy
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Sujeewa Arjune Senasinghe, filed an application under Article 126 of the Constitution alleging that the 1st and 3rd to 6th Respondents acted in an unlawful, irresponsible, and arbitrary manner regarding the investment of funds in Greece Government Bonds. The Petitioner contended that the Monetary Board failed to comply with Clause 5.2.9 of the Foreign Exchange Reserves Management Guidelines. Upon the direction of the Court, the Deputy Solicitor General produced the relevant guidelines, which revealed that the specific clause relied upon by the Petitioner did not exist. The Respondents maintained that the investment was a strategic decision made within the scope of the Monetary Law Act to manage international reserves. Evidence indicated that the investment in Greece and Ireland bonds constituted less than 1.0 percent of the Central Bank's reserves. Although the Central Bank incurred a loss of U.S. $ 6.6 million on the Greece bonds following a downturn in the Euro Zone, the bank recorded a total net profit of U.S. $ 430.2 million on international reserve management for the year 2011. The Petitioner subsequently attempted to raise new arguments regarding non-compliance with other clauses of the guidelines during the hearing, which had not been pleaded in the original petition. Issues Whether the investment in Greece Government Bonds by the Monetary Board constituted an arbitrary or unlawful exercise of power. Whether the Petitioner is entitled to raise new grounds of non-compliance with the Foreign Exchange Reserves Management Guidelines that were not included in the original petition. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 126 Monetary Law Act Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Arbitrary or unlawful exercise of power Rule applied: Section 66 and Section 67 of the Monetary Law Act; Clause 2.2.1 of the Foreign Exchange Reserves Management Guidelines. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the Monetary Board is empowered to maintain international reserves and that foreign government securities are recognized assets. The Governor, as the Chief Executive Officer, possesses delegated authority to manage these reserves. The Court emphasized that economic policy decisions are complex and empiric, and judicial review is limited to determining if a decision is arbitrary or serves a collateral purpose. The investment was a small portion of the portfolio (0.6%) and part of a broader risk management strategy that resulted in an overall net profit for the year. Finding: The Court found that the Respondents did not act in an arbitrary, unreasonable, or fraudulent manner. Issue 2: Raising new grounds Rule applied: Article 126(2) of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Petitioner sought to argue at the hearing that Clauses 5.2.1 and 3.3.2 of the guidelines were violated. The Court noted that these objections were not raised in the original petition, nor did the Petitioner seek to amend the petition after the guidelines were made available to them in September 2012. Consequently, these new matters fall outside the time limit prescribed by the Constitution. Finding: The Court held that the new grounds could not be considered. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the investment was a legitimate exercise of discretion under the Monetary Law Act negates the claim of arbitrariness. Furthermore, the procedural failure to timely plead specific violations of the guidelines precluded the Court from entertaining additional arguments, thereby confirming that the Petitioner failed to establish a prima facie case of constitutional infringement. Conclusion The Court refused to grant leave to proceed. The application is dismissed with no costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Investment decisions — Foreign exchange reserves — Monetary Law Act — Arbitrary exercise of power — Foreign Exchange Reserves Management Guidelines — Central Bank of Sri Lanka — Risk management strategy

Parties: Freeda Doreen Peiris v. Namal Rohitha Peiris and Sunil Jackson Peiris
Bench: Mohan Pieris, P.C., C.J.; K. Sripavan, J.; E. Wanasundera, P.C., J.
Keywords: Supreme Court Rules 1990 — Leave to Appeal — Preliminary Objection — Non-compliance — Jurisdiction — Mandatory Requirements — Service of Notice — Civil Appellate Jurisdiction — Appeal Period — Discretion of Court
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for leave to appeal filed by the Defendants-Petitioners, Namal Rohitha Peiris and Sunil Jackson Peiris, against the Plaintiff-Respondent, Freeda Doreen Peiris (after marriage Gunathilaka). The Plaintiff is a resident of Australia, while the Defendants reside in Kotte, Sri Lanka. The initial proceedings began in the District Court of Nugegoda, where the Defendants were aggrieved by a decision made by the High Court of Mt. Lavinia on 28 February 2012. The Defendants filed their petition of appeal, affidavit, and supporting documents on 9 April 2012, but failed to tender the requisite notices for service on the Respondent at that time. The notices were only submitted to the Registry on 3 May 2012, which was beyond the six-week period allowed for filing an appeal as per the applicable rules. The Plaintiff's counsel raised a preliminary objection regarding the non-compliance with Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules 1990, which mandates that notices must be filed along with the application for leave to appeal. Issues The primary issues in this case can be framed as follows: Whether the Defendants-Petitioners complied with Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules 1990 when filing their application for leave to appeal? Whether the Court has the discretion to overlook the non-compliance with the rules regarding the filing of notices? Applicable Law Statutes: Supreme Court Rules 1990 — Article 127 of the Constitution of Sri Lanka Cases Referred to: Ediriwickrema Vs. Ratnasiri - [S.C. Appeal No. 85/2004 – S.C. Minute of 22.2.13] Priyani E. Soysa Vs. Rienzie Arsecularatne - [1999] 2 S.L.R. 179 Priyanthie Chandrika Jinadasa Vs. Pathma Hemamali & Others - [2011] 1 S L R 337 Hon. A.H.M. Fowzie & two Others Vs. Vehicles Lanka (Pvt.) Ltd. - [2008] B.L.R. 127 Analysis Issue 1: Compliance with Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules 1990 Rule applied: Supreme Court Rules 1990, Rule 8(3) Court's reasoning: The Court examined the procedural requirements set forth in Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules 1990, which explicitly states that a petitioner must tender the requisite number of notices for service on the respondents at the time of filing the application for leave to appeal. The Plaintiff's counsel argued that the Defendants had failed to comply with this mandatory requirement, which is designed to ensure that respondents are duly notified of the application lodged against them. The Court noted that the Defendants filed their petition on 9 April 2012 but did not submit the necessary notices until 3 May 2012, which was outside the six-week period allowed for appeals. The Court referenced the case of Ediriwickrema Vs. Ratnasiri, where a similar preliminary objection was raised, but distinguished it on the grounds that the objection in that case was raised after submissions had been made on the merits, whereas in the current case, the objection was raised before the matter was taken up for support. The Court emphasized that the Defendants had not provided a satisfactory explanation for their failure to comply with the rule at the time of filing. The Court reiterated that the rules of procedure are not mere formalities but are essential for the orderly conduct of judicial proceedings. Finding: The Court upheld the preliminary objection raised by the Plaintiff's counsel and concluded that the Defendants had failed to comply with Rule 8(3) of the Supreme Court Rules 1990. Issue 2: Discretion of the Court to Overlook Non-compliance Rule applied: Discretionary powers of the Court Court's reasoning: The Defendants' counsel argued that the Court should exercise its discretion to overlook the non-compliance with the rules, citing the need to maintain the discipline of the law and the context of the rule. However, the Court found that the Defendants had not taken any steps to rectify their failure to comply with the rules in a timely manner. The Court highlighted that the purpose of Rule 8(3) is to ensure that respondents are notified of the application for leave to appeal, and this requirement is mandatory. The Court also noted that the Defendants had not made any application for an extension of time to comply with the rule, which further weakened their position. The Court emphasized that the integrity of the judicial process must be upheld, and allowing the appeal to proceed despite the non-compliance would undermine the established rules and procedures. Finding: The Court determined that it could not exercise its discretion in favor of the Defendants due to their failure to comply with the mandatory requirements of the Supreme Court Rules. Interaction of Issues The issues of compliance with Rule 8(3) and the Court's discretion to overlook non-compliance are interrelated. The failure to comply with the procedural requirements directly impacts the Court's ability to exercise discretion. The Court's insistence on adherence to procedural rules underscores the importance of maintaining the integrity of the judicial process. Conclusion The Supreme Court upheld the preliminary objection raised by the Plaintiff's counsel and dismissed the Defendants' application for leave to appeal. The Court's decision reinforces the necessity of compliance with procedural rules and the importance of timely notification to respondents in judicial proceedings. The ruling serves as a reminder that the Court will not overlook non-compliance with established rules, thereby ensuring the orderly conduct of justice. Keywords Supreme Court Rules 1990 — Leave to Appeal — Preliminary Objection — Non-compliance — Jurisdiction — Mandatory Requirements — Service of Notice — Civil Appellate Jurisdiction — Appeal Period — Discretion of Court
Counsel: Dr. Sunil Coorey with Henmantha Boteju, H.A.M. Dayaratna for the 1st and 2nd Defendants-Petitioners-Petitioners. Athula Bandara Herath with Shashika de Silva instructed by Sanath Wijewardene for the Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent.

Parties: Bandara v. Perera and Others
Bench: Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC.J
Keywords: Discharge from service — Air Force Act — Summary trial — Court Martial — Ultra vires — Proportionality — Base standing orders — Natural justice — Violation of regulations
Summary: Facts The Petitioner-Respondent, K.H.M.S. Bandara, was an Officer Cadet in the Sri Lanka Air Force. Following an incident where the Petitioner and a colleague entered a residential quarter without authority to locate an electric fan, a complaint was lodged by Flt. Lt. C.J.C. de Silva. An investigation was conducted by a board, and a subsequent inquiry was held on 29.11.2004. The Petitioner was found guilty of entering premises without authority and criminal trespass. He was initially sentenced to detention for both charges, but the first charge was later exonerated, leaving only the 14-day detention for the second charge. After serving the detention, the Petitioner was orally informed on 21.12.2004 that he was discharged from the Air Force. An internal document, 1R7, confirmed his discharge with effect from 22.12.2004. The Petitioner challenged this discharge in the Court of Appeal, which issued a Writ of Certiorari quashing the decision of the 1st Respondent-Appellant and awarded costs. The Respondents-Appellants subsequently appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Court of Appeal erred in holding that the inquiry and discharge were arbitrary and outside the provisions of the law. Whether the Court of Appeal erred in applying the concept of proportionality to the discharge of the Petitioner. Whether the Petitioner was subject to the provisions of the Air Force Act, thereby rendering the summary trial and subsequent discharge ultra vires. Applicable Law Statutes: Air Force Act Penal Code Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Legality of the inquiry and discharge Rule applied: Air Force Act, specifically Sections 102(1), 129, and 133. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the Petitioner was tried summarily under Regulation 126 of the base standing orders. However, the charges of criminal trespass and entering premises without authority fall under Sections 129 and 102(1) of the Air Force Act, which require a trial by Court Martial. The court noted that discharge from service is a punishment that can only be imposed by a Court Martial under Section 133. Consequently, the summary trial and subsequent discharge were procedurally flawed and contrary to the Act. Finding: The discharge was ultra vires and the inquiry was conducted outside the provisions of the law. Issue 2: Proportionality of the discharge Rule applied: General principles of administrative law regarding reasonableness and proportionality. Court's reasoning: The court found that the Petitioner had already been punished with 14 days of detention for the incident. Ordering a discharge from service after the completion of this sentence effectively amounted to a second punishment for the same offense, which is impermissible. The court characterized the discharge as totally disproportionate to the offense and unreasonable. Finding: The discharge was arbitrary and disproportionate. Issue 3: Applicability of the Air Force Act Rule applied: Air Force Act. Court's reasoning: The court rejected the Appellants' argument that the Petitioner, as a trainee, was not subject to the Air Force Act. The court pointed to the enlistment documents, which explicitly stated that the Petitioner was subject to the Air Force Act and liable for punishment thereunder. Furthermore, the base regulations used to try the Petitioner were themselves derived from the Air Force Act. Finding: The Petitioner was subject to the Air Force Act, and the Appellants were bound by its procedural requirements. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing; because the Petitioner was subject to the Air Force Act, the Appellants were required to follow the statutory procedures for trial by Court Martial. By bypassing these procedures through a summary trial, the Appellants acted ultra vires. This procedural illegality, combined with the imposition of a second punishment, rendered the discharge both legally invalid and disproportionate. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed, and the judgment of the Court of Appeal quashing the discharge is upheld. The order for costs of Rs. 25,000/- against the 1st Respondent-Appellant is set aside, and no costs are ordered for the appeal. Keywords Discharge from service — Air Force Act — Summary trial — Court Martial — Ultra vires — Proportionality — Base standing orders — Natural justice — Violation of regulations

Parties: Adamjee Lukmanjee & Sons Limited v. Premasiri and Others
Bench: Eva Wanasundera, PC.J; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.; Sarath De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Writ of Execution — Section 343 of the Civil Procedure Code — Residential premises — Special Leave to Appeal — Supreme Court Rules — Filing of written submissions — Due diligence — Failure to prosecute — Preliminary objection
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Judgment-Creditor-Respondent-Respondent, Adamjee Lukmanjee & Sons Limited, obtained a consent judgment against the Defendant-Judgment-Debtor-Petitioner-Appellant, Samarasinghe Arachchige Premasiri, for the recovery of a decreed sum. Upon the Appellant's default in payment, a Writ of Execution was issued on 26.03.2003. The Appellant challenged the seizure of his residential premises in the Commercial High Court, which ruled against him on 21.08.2008. The Appellant subsequently sought leave to appeal to the Supreme Court. Leave to appeal was granted on 18.06.2009. The Appellant failed to file written submissions within the six-week period prescribed by the Supreme Court Rules. Although the Appellant eventually filed written submissions on 19.11.2009, he failed to file them by the initial hearing date of 24.09.2009. Furthermore, the Appellant failed to produce certified copies of documents marked X1 to X7, which he had undertaken to provide in his petition and affidavit. The Respondent raised a preliminary objection regarding the Appellant's failure to comply with Supreme Court Rules 30 and 34, arguing that the appeal should be dismissed for lack of due diligence in prosecution. Issues Whether the Supreme Court Rules regarding the filing of written submissions apply to appeals originating from the Commercial High Court. Whether the Appellant's failure to file written submissions within the prescribed time and failure to produce promised documents constitutes a lack of due diligence warranting dismissal under Rule 34 of the Supreme Court Rules. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka Civil Procedure Code High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996 Cases Referred to: A.C. Muthappan Chettiar v. M.R. Karunanayake - [sc-appeal-69/2003] Tissa Attanayake v. Commissioner General of Elections - [sc-spl-la-55/2011] Ananda Dharmasinghe Bandara v. Herath Mudiyanselage Leelawathie Menike - [sc-appeal-172/2011] Elias v. Gajasinghe - [sc-appeal-50/2008] Fernando v. Fernando - [sc-appeal-81/2009] Fernando v. Sybil Fernando - [1997/v3-sri-lr-1] Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of Supreme Court Rules Rule applied: Section 6 of the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996 and Supreme Court Rules. Court's reasoning: The Court rejected the Appellant's contention that Supreme Court Rules do not apply to appeals from the Commercial High Court. The Court reasoned that once a case enters the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court, it is governed by the Supreme Court Rules, regardless of whether the matter originated in an original court or an appellate court. The High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996 regulates procedure only until the case leaves the High Court. Finding: The Supreme Court Rules are mandatory and applicable to the present appeal. Issue 2: Due Diligence and Non-Prosecution Rule applied: Supreme Court Rules 30(1), 30(6), and 34. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the Appellant failed to file written submissions within the mandatory six-week period and failed to provide certified copies of documents X1 to X7 as promised. Unlike in Ananda Dharmasinghe Bandara v. Herath Mudiyanselage Leelawathie Menike, where the Court exercised discretion because no prejudice was caused and the delay was minimal, the Appellant here provided no valid reason for the delay and failed to seek an extension of time. The Court emphasized that the rules are essential for the smooth functioning of the legal machinery and that the Appellantâ€™s conduct demonstrated a lack of due diligence. Finding: The Appellant failed to prosecute the appeal with due diligence. Interaction of Issues The findings are reinforcing: the determination that the Supreme Court Rules are mandatory (Issue 1) provides the legal framework under which the Appellant's failure to comply (Issue 2) is judged. The lack of diligence in following these mandatory rules necessitates the dismissal of the appeal. Conclusion The preliminary objection is upheld. The appeal is dismissed for failure to prosecute diligently in accordance with the Supreme Court Rules. No costs are ordered. Keywords Writ of Execution — Section 343 of the Civil Procedure Code — Residential premises — Special Leave to Appeal — Supreme Court Rules — Filing of written submissions — Due diligence — Failure to prosecute — Preliminary objection

Parties: Arpico Finance Company PLC v. Richard Pieris Arpico Finance Limited
Bench: Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Aluwihare PC, J; Sarath De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Trade name infringement — Intellectual Property Act No. 36 — Unfair competition — Passing off — Confusion in the market — Interim injunction — Prima facie case — Balance of convenience — Goodwill and reputation — Descriptive names
Summary: Facts The Respondent, Arpico Finance Company PLC, was incorporated in 1951 and has operated as a finance company under the trade names Arpico Finance and Arpico Finance Company PLC. In 1967, the Respondentâ€™s shareholding was purchased by Alliance Finance Company Ltd, separating it from the Richard Pieris & Company group. The Respondent alleges that it has established significant goodwill and a substantial deposit base under its trade names. The Petitioner, Richard Pieris Arpico Finance Limited, was incorporated later. The Respondent initially objected to the Registrar General of Companies regarding the Petitioner's original name, Arpico Financial Services Limited, which led to the name change to Richard Pieris Arpico Finance Limited. The Respondent subsequently instituted action in the Commercial High Court, alleging that the use of the word Arpico Finance by the Petitioner constitutes unfair competition and passing off under the Intellectual Property Act No. 36 of 2003. The High Court granted an interim injunction restraining the Petitioner from using the name Arpico Finance or any confusingly similar name. The Petitioner appealed this order to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court Judge misdirected himself in the application of Sections 122, 144, and 160 of the Intellectual Property Act No. 36 of 2003 regarding the likelihood of public confusion. Whether the High Court Judge correctly applied the criteria for granting an interim injunction under Section 54 of the Judicature Act No. 2 of 1978. Applicable Law Statutes: Intellectual Property Act No. 36 of 2003 Judicature Act No. 2 of 1978 Companies Act No. 7 of 2007 Cases Referred to: Indrani v. The Municipal Board Imphal - [AIR-1958-Manupuri-27] Jinadasa v. Weerasinghe - [31-nlr-33] Bhauroo Singh v. Mst Dulari And Anor - [1992/v1-western-law-cases-636] Ceylon Insurance Corporation v. United Ceylon Insurance Company - [48-nlr-1] North Chesire and Manchester Brewery Company Ltd v. Manchester Brewery Co. Ltd - [1899-ac-83] Parle Products Pvt Ltd v. Parle Agro Pvt Ltd - [2009-fsr-18] Adrema v. Adrema-Werke - [1958-rpc-323] HFC Bank PLC v. Midland Bank PLC - [200-rfs-176] First National Bank in Sioux Falls v. First National Bank South Dakota SPC. INC - [2008-dsd-9] Reddaway v. Banham - [1896-ac-199] Reckitt & Coleman Products Ltd v. Borden - [1990-rpc-341] Analysis Issue 1: Likelihood of confusion and unfair competition Rule applied: Sections 144 and 160 of the Intellectual Property Act No. 36 of 2003. Court's reasoning: The court noted that "Arpico" is an invented name, distinguishing it from common descriptive terms. Relying on North Chesire and Manchester Brewery Company Ltd v. Manchester Brewery Co. Ltd, the court reasoned that the use of an invented name by a rival entity in the same sector creates a high likelihood of confusion. The court rejected the Petitioner's argument that the inclusion of "Richard Pieris" sufficiently distinguished the entities, finding that the similarity in the core trade name could lead the public to believe the companies had amalgamated. Finding: The High Court did not misdirect itself in concluding that the Petitioner's use of the name was likely to cause confusion and constitute unfair competition. Issue 2: Criteria for interim injunction Rule applied: Section 54 of the Judicature Act No. 2 of 1978. Court's reasoning: The court applied the three-fold test: existence of a prima facie case, balance of convenience, and equitable considerations. Citing Jinadasa v. Weerasinghe, the court held that the Respondent established a serious question to be tried. Regarding the balance of convenience, the court observed that the Respondent had long-standing, established business operations, whereas the Petitioner was of recent vintage with a smaller deposit base. Finding: The High Court correctly exercised its discretion in granting the interim injunction as the Respondent satisfied the necessary legal criteria. Interaction of Issues The findings on the likelihood of confusion (Issue 1) directly supported the court's determination that the Respondent had established a prima facie case (Issue 2). By confirming that the Petitioner's trade name created a risk of misrepresentation, the court reinforced the necessity of maintaining the status quo via an interim injunction to prevent irreparable harm to the Respondent's goodwill. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed and the order of the High Court granting the interim injunction is upheld. The court vacated its previous stay order and directed the High Court to prioritize the final determination of the action. Keywords Trade name infringement — Intellectual Property Act No. 36 — Unfair competition — Passing off — Confusion in the market — Interim injunction — Prima facie case — Balance of convenience — Goodwill and reputation — Descriptive names

Parties: Adamjee Lukmanjee & Sons Limited v. Samarasinghe Arachchige Premasiri and Others
Bench: Eva Wanasundera, PC.J.; Sisira J De Abrew, J.; Sarath de Abrew, J.
Keywords: Civil Procedure Code — Writ of Execution — Judgment-Debtor — Appeal — Supreme Court Rules — Non-compliance — Diligence — Preliminary Objection — Leave to Appeal — Certified Copies
Summary: Facts This case arises from an application under Chapter LIII of the Civil Procedure Code concerning a Writ of Execution issued against the Defendant-Judgment-Debtor, Samarasinghe Arachchige Premasiri, for the recovery of a decreed sum of approximately Rs. 3.7 million. The Plaintiff, Adamjee Lukmanjee & Sons Limited, had entered into a consent judgment with the Defendant, wherein the Defendant was to pay Rs. 1.85 million in monthly installments by 31 July 2004. However, the Defendant defaulted on these payments, leading to the issuance of the Writ of Execution on 26 March 2003. The Defendant claimed that the property seized under the Writ was his residential premises and sought its release. The Commercial High Court of Colombo ruled on 21 August 2008 that the property was not the Defendant's residential premises and thus could not be released from seizure under Section 343(1) of the Civil Procedure Code. The Supreme Court granted leave to appeal on 18 June 2009, allowing the Defendant to challenge the lower court's ruling. The case was marked by significant procedural delays, including the Defendant's failure to file written submissions by the required deadlines, which became a central issue in the appeal. Issues Whether the Defendant-Judgment-Debtor-Petitioner-Appellant's failure to file written submissions within the stipulated time constitutes grounds for dismissing the appeal for non-prosecution? Whether the Supreme Court Rules apply to appeals from the Commercial High Court, and if so, whether the Defendant's non-compliance with these rules affects the appeal? Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code; High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996. Cases Referred to: A.C. Muthappan Chettiar Vs. M.R. Karunanayake and another - [SC. Appeal 69/2003]; Tissa Attanayake Vs. Commissioner General of Elections and 27 others - [SC. Spl, LA. No. 55/2011]; Ananda Dharmasinghe Bandara and Another Vs. Herath Mudiyanselage Leelawathie Menike and Another - [SC. Appeal 172/2011]; Elias Vs. Gajasinghe & another - [SC. Appeal 50/2008]; Fernando Vs. Fernando - [SC. Appeal 81/2009]. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Defendant-Judgment-Debtor-Petitioner-Appellant's failure to file written submissions within the stipulated time constitutes grounds for dismissing the appeal for non-prosecution? Rule applied: Supreme Court Rules 30 and 34. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court emphasized the importance of adhering to procedural rules to ensure the efficient functioning of the court system. The Appellant's failure to file written submissions within the six-week timeframe mandated by Rule 30(6) was deemed a significant lapse. The Court noted that the Appellant had not sought an extension of time or provided valid reasons for the delay, which indicated a lack of diligence in prosecuting the appeal. The Court referenced previous judgments, including Muthappan Chettiar, which established that non-compliance with mandatory rules could lead to dismissal for non-prosecution. The Appellant's argument that the Supreme Court Rules did not apply to his case was rejected, as the Court clarified that once an appeal reaches the Supreme Court, it is subject to its rules regardless of the originating court. Finding: The Court upheld the preliminary objection raised by the Respondent, concluding that the Appellant's failure to comply with the Supreme Court Rules warranted dismissal of the appeal. Interaction of Issues The issues of non-compliance with procedural rules and the applicability of the Supreme Court Rules to appeals from the Commercial High Court are interrelated. The Court's determination that the Appellant's failure to file written submissions constituted grounds for dismissal was rooted in the understanding that all appeals to the Supreme Court must adhere to its procedural framework. The Appellant's argument regarding the nature of the appeal did not exempt him from the obligation to comply with the rules, reinforcing the principle that procedural diligence is essential in the judicial process. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal due to the Appellant's failure to prosecute diligently, as evidenced by his non-compliance with the Supreme Court Rules regarding the filing of written submissions. The Court ordered no costs, emphasizing the importance of adherence to procedural requirements in maintaining the integrity of the judicial system. Keywords Civil Procedure Code — Writ of Execution — Judgment-Debtor — Appeal — Supreme Court Rules — Non-compliance — Diligence — Preliminary Objection — Leave to Appeal — Certified Copies
Counsel: Saliya Pieris, with Palitha Yaggahawita for Defendant-Judgment-Debtor-Petitioner-Appellant. M. Adamaly with J. Abeysundera for Plaintiff-Judgment-Creditor-Respondent-Respondent.

Parties: Arpico Finance Company PLC v. Richard Pieris Arpico Finance Limited
Bench: Priyasath Dep, P.C., J.; Aluwihare, P.C., J.; Sarath de Abrew, J.
Keywords: Intellectual Property Act — Unfair Competition — Passing Off — Trade Name Infringement — Interim Injunction — Goodwill — Consumer Confusion — Prima Facie Case — Balance of Convenience — Misrepresentation — Legal Standards — Judicial Discretion — Commercial Activities — Trade Name Protection
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a dispute between two finance companies, Arpico Finance Company PLC (the Respondent) and Richard Pieris Arpico Finance Limited (the Petitioner), regarding the use of the trade name 'Arpico Finance'. The Respondent, incorporated in 1951, has been providing financial services and claims to have established substantial goodwill and reputation associated with its trade name. The Respondent alleges that the Petitioner’s use of a similar name constitutes unfair competition and infringes upon its rights under the Intellectual Property Act No. 36 of 2003. The Respondent sought various forms of relief, including a declaration of infringement, a permanent injunction against the Petitioner’s use of the name, and an interim injunction pending the final determination of the case. The High Court granted the interim injunction on 30 March 2013, leading the Petitioner to appeal this decision. The Petitioner contends that the Respondent does not have exclusive rights to the name and that the use of 'Richard Pieris' in its name sufficiently distinguishes it from the Respondent. The Respondent argues that the similarity in names is likely to confuse consumers and misrepresent the source of services. Issues The key issues presented for determination include: Whether the learned High Court Judge misdirected himself in the application of Section 122 of the Intellectual Property Act No. 36 of 2003. Whether the learned High Court Judge misdirected himself in applying the principles of Intellectual Property Law related to confusion/misleading the public. Whether the learned High Court Judge failed to consider the particular customers who use the services of both parties. Whether the learned High Court Judge failed to consider the goodwill and reputation attached to the trademark 'Arpico' of the Richard Pieris Group. Whether the learned High Court Judge misdirected himself in failing to consider the irreparable loss and damage that would be caused to the Petitioner by granting the interim injunction. Applicable Law Statutes: Intellectual Property Act No. 36 of 2003 — Sections 144 and 160. Cases Referred to: Indrani v. The Municipal Board Imphal - [A.I.R 1958 Manupuri 27]; Jinadasa v. Weerasinghe - [31 N.L.R 33]; Bhauroo Singh v. Mst Dulari And Anor - [1992 1 Western Law cases 636 (Rajastan)]; Ceylon Insurance Corporation Vs. United Ceylon Insurance Company - [48 NLR page 454]; North Cheshire and Manchester Brewery Company Ltd v. Manchester Brewery Co. Ltd. - [1899 AC 83]; Parle Products Pvt Ltd v. Parle Agro Pvt Ltd - [2009 F.S.R 18]; Adrema Vs. Adrema-Werke - [1958 RPC 323]. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the learned High Court Judge misdirected himself in the application of Section 122 of the Intellectual Property Act No. 36 of 2003. Rule applied: Section 144 of the Intellectual Property Act No. 36 of 2003. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the provisions of Section 144, which protects trade names even prior to registration against unlawful acts by third parties. The Respondent argued that the Petitioner’s use of 'Arpico Finance' would mislead the public and infringe upon its established rights. The Court noted that the Respondent had been using the name since 1951 and had built significant goodwill. The Petitioner, on the other hand, contended that the name 'Richard Pieris' sufficiently distinguished its services from those of the Respondent. The Court emphasized that the similarity in names could lead to confusion among consumers, particularly given the nature of the services provided by both companies. The Court also referenced previous cases that established the importance of protecting trade names to prevent consumer deception. Finding: The Court found that the learned High Court Judge did not err in applying the law regarding trade name protection and that the Respondent had established a prima facie case for the issuance of an interim injunction. Issue 2: Whether the learned High Court Judge misdirected himself in applying the principles of Intellectual Property Law related to confusion/misleading the public. Rule applied: Principles of Unfair Competition and Passing Off. Court's reasoning: The Court analyzed the principles of unfair competition and passing off, noting that the Respondent had to demonstrate that the Petitioner’s actions were likely to cause confusion among consumers. The Court highlighted that the Respondent had a long-standing reputation and that the use of a similar name by the Petitioner could lead to a misrepresentation of the source of services. The Court also considered the sophistication of consumers in the financial services sector, concluding that even informed consumers could be misled by the similarity in names. The Court reiterated that the likelihood of confusion is a question of fact that must be established through evidence. Finding: The Court upheld the High Court's finding that the Petitioner’s use of 'Arpico Finance' was likely to mislead consumers and cause confusion. Issue 3: Whether the learned High Court Judge failed to consider the particular customers who use the services of both parties. Rule applied: Consumer Confusion Standards. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the arguments regarding consumer sophistication and the nature of the financial services market. It acknowledged that while consumers may be informed, the potential for confusion remained significant due to the similarity in names. The Court emphasized that the burden of proof lay with the Respondent to establish that consumers were likely to be misled. The Court found that the High Court had adequately considered the customer base and the potential for confusion in its decision-making process. Finding: The Court concluded that the High Court had appropriately considered the customer dynamics and the likelihood of confusion. Interaction of Issues The issues presented are interrelated, as they all revolve around the core question of whether the Petitioner’s use of a similar trade name infringes upon the Respondent’s rights and misleads consumers. The determination of whether the learned High Court Judge misdirected himself in applying the law directly impacts the findings on consumer confusion and the protection of trade names. The Court's analysis of the principles of unfair competition and passing off is crucial in understanding the broader implications of the case. Conclusion The Supreme Court upheld the decision of the High Court to grant an interim injunction against the Petitioner, affirming that the Respondent had established a prima facie case for the protection of its trade name. The Court emphasized the importance of preventing consumer confusion and protecting established goodwill in the marketplace. The Court directed the High Court to prioritize the case and expedite its resolution, reflecting the significance of the issues at stake. The Court made no order regarding costs, recognizing the complexities involved in the case. Keywords Intellectual Property Act — Unfair Competition — Passing Off — Trade Name Infringement — Interim Injunction — Goodwill — Consumer Confusion — Prima Facie Case — Balance of Convenience — Misrepresentation — Legal Standards — Judicial Discretion — Commercial Activities — Trade Name Protection
Counsel: Dr. Harsha Cabral, P.C., with Buddika Illanganthilaka and Nishan Premathirathne, instructed by M/s. Julius & Creasy for the Defendant-Petitioner. A.R. Surendran, P.C., with Shivan Kang-Iswaran, instructed by M/s. Neelakandan & Neelakandan for the Plaintiff-Respondent.

Parties: G P de Silva & Sons International (Pvt) Ltd v. Union Assurance Ltd
Bench: Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.; Sarath De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Marine insurance — Insurable interest — CIF contracts — Passing of title — Bill of lading — Loss of cargo — Contract of sale — Utmost good faith
Summary: Facts The plaintiff-appellant, G P de Silva & Sons International (Pvt) Limited, is an export company specializing in cinnamon. The defendant-respondent, Union Assurance Limited, is an insurance company. On 22 December 2003, the plaintiff-appellant obtained a marine insurance open cover policy from the defendant-respondent. The defendant-respondent confirmed that this policy included coverage for loss of cargo in containers during inland transit, as well as theft or burglary from the shipper's warehouse to the buyer's warehouse. On 10 March 2005, the plaintiff-appellant shipped a consignment of cinnamon quills to a buyer in Mexico City under CIF terms. The plaintiff-appellant subsequently alleged that the container was lost during transport by truck from the port of discharge to the buyer's warehouse. The plaintiff-appellant filed a claim for USD 68,605/63, which was later reduced to USD 16,217/63 after the consignee recovered a portion of the loss from its own insurer in Mexico. The defendant-respondent repudiated the claim, asserting that the plaintiff-appellant lacked an insurable interest because the shipment was made on CIF terms, the title had passed to the buyer, and the buyer had already taken delivery of the goods. The High Court dismissed the plaintiff-appellant's action, finding that the plaintiff-appellant lacked an insurable interest at the time of the loss. Issues Whether the plaintiff-appellant possessed an insurable interest in the consignment of cinnamon at the time of the loss under the marine insurance policy. Whether the transfer of the bill of lading and the collection of goods by the buyer extinguished the plaintiff-appellant's right to claim under the policy. Applicable Law Statutes: Not specified Cases Referred to: Clements Horst Co v. Biddel Bros - [1912/ac-18] Analysis Issue 1: Insurable interest at the time of loss Rule applied: The court applied the principle that under a CIF contract, the risk and title pass to the buyer upon shipment and the tender of shipping documents. The policy itself stipulated that the assured must have an insurable interest at the time of the loss. Court's reasoning: The court relied on the evidence of the Managing Director of the plaintiff-appellant, who admitted that the buyer had collected the goods from the port of discharge and was in possession of the original bill of lading. Citing The Sale of Goods by PS Attiyah, John N Adams, and Hector MacQueen, the court reasoned that in CIF contracts, the seller's duties are fulfilled upon delivery of goods on board and the transfer of documents; subsequent events are of no concern to the seller. Finding: The plaintiff-appellant did not have an insurable interest in the goods at the time of the loss. Issue 2: Effect of bill of lading and title transfer Rule applied: The court applied the legal principle that possession of a bill of lading is equivalent in law to possession of the goods. Court's reasoning: Referring to Payne & Ivamy's Carriage of Goods by Sea, the court noted that the holder of the bill of lading is entitled to delivery of the goods. Because the buyer held the bill of lading and had already taken delivery of the consignment, the court concluded that title had passed to the buyer. The court further noted that the plaintiff-appellant's own admission regarding the lack of title and the fact that the buyer had already recovered a portion of the loss from a separate insurer confirmed that the plaintiff-appellant had no remaining interest. Finding: The transfer of the bill of lading and the buyer's possession of the goods extinguished any insurable interest the plaintiff-appellant might have held. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the plaintiff-appellant lacked an insurable interest (Issue 1) was directly predicated on the legal conclusion that title and possession had passed to the buyer via the bill of lading (Issue 2). Together, these findings established that the plaintiff-appellant had no standing to maintain the claim under the marine insurance policy. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed with costs. The court affirmed the High Court's judgment, holding that the plaintiff-appellant lacked the necessary insurable interest to recover under the policy. Keywords Marine insurance — Insurable interest — CIF contracts — Passing of title — Bill of lading — Loss of cargo — Contract of sale — Utmost good faith

Parties: Hewagama v. Secretary of State for Higher Education and Others
Bench: Mohan Pieris, PC. C.J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC.J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — University admission criteria — Z score — District merit quota — All Island merit — Legitimate expectations — Administrative action — University Grants Commission
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Natasha Dulmi Hewagama, was a student who sat for the G.C.E. Advanced Level Examination in 2010 in the Biology stream. She obtained 2As, 1B, and a Z-score of .9375, placing her at the 99th position in the Galle District and 901st in the Island-rank. The Petitioner sought admission to the Medicine or Dental Surgery stream for the 2010/2011 academic year, contending that her district rank entitled her to a placement. The Petitioner alleged that the Respondents, specifically the University Grants Commission, failed to adhere to the admission criteria set out in the official handbook for the 2010/2011 academic year. She argued that her exclusion from these courses constituted a violation of her fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The Petitioner had previously sought relief from the Human Rights Commission, which recommended her admission to the Medicine stream. The Respondents maintained that the Petitionerâ€™s results were insufficient for selection and that the selection process was conducted in accordance with the established handbook criteria, which included provisions for all-island merit, district merit, and special allocations. Issues Whether the selection process for the Medicine and Dental Surgery streams for the 2010/2011 academic year violated the Petitioner's fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the Petitionerâ€™s interpretation of the admission criteria in the University Grants Commission handbook was legally correct. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka. Court's reasoning: The court examined the selection methodology employed by the University Grants Commission. It found that the Respondents provided a detailed, mathematically sound explanation for the student intake, which accounted for all-island merit, district merit, special grounds, and quota allocations mandated by the handbook. The court determined that the Petitionerâ€™s exclusion was a result of her rank falling outside the cutoff for the available places, rather than an arbitrary or discriminatory act by the Respondents. Finding: There was no infringement of the Petitioner's fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Issue 2: Interpretation of Admission Criteria Rule applied: Section 3.2.3.2, Notes 1 and 2, and Section 3.3 of the University Grants Commission Handbook for 2010/2011. Court's reasoning: The Petitionerâ€™s calculation relied solely on a simplified interpretation of the all-island and district merit quotas. The court held that the Petitioner failed to account for the "up to" language in the handbook, the mandatory base-year quotas (Notes 1 and 2), and the special allocations for foreign, defence, and sports quotas (Section 3.3). The court noted that the Human Rights Commission had similarly failed to consider these comprehensive factors. The court concluded that the Respondents' methodology, which integrated these variables, was correct and compliant with the handbook. Finding: The Petitionerâ€™s expectations were based on an erroneous assessment and misunderstanding of the admission criteria. Interaction of Issues The findings are intrinsically linked; the courtâ€™s determination that the Petitionerâ€™s interpretation of the handbook was legally and mathematically flawed directly negated the claim that the Respondents acted in a manner that violated Article 12(1). Because the selection process was found to be consistent with the established criteria, the allegation of a fundamental rights infringement became unsustainable. Conclusion The court held that the Petitioner failed to establish an infringement of her fundamental rights. The application is dismissed without costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — University admission criteria — Z score — District merit quota — All Island merit — Legitimate expectations — Administrative action — University Grants Commission

Parties: Sampath v. Secretary of State for Higher Education and Others
Bench: Mohan Pieris, PC. C.J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC.J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — University admission criteria — Z score — District merit quota — All Island merit — Legitimate expectations — Administrative action — University Grants Commission — Human Rights Commission
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, H.W. Rajitha Udakara Sampath, was a student who sat for the G.C.E. Advanced Level Examination in 2010 in the Biology stream. He obtained 2As and 1B with a Z-score of .9405, achieving an island-wide rank of 893 and a district rank of 98 in the Galle District. The Petitioner sought admission to the Medicine or Dental Surgery streams for the 2010/2011 academic year, contending that his district rank entitled him to a placement. He alleged that the Respondents, the Secretary of the Ministry of Higher Education and the Chairman of the University Grants Commission, violated his fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution by failing to admit him. The Petitioner had previously approached the Human Rights Commission, which recommended his admission. The Respondents maintained that the Petitionerâ€™s results were insufficient for selection under the established criteria set out in the University Grants Commission handbook. Issues Whether the selection process for the Medicine and Dental Surgery streams for the 2010/2011 academic year violated the Petitioner's fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the Petitionerâ€™s interpretation of the admission criteria in the University Grants Commission handbook was legally and mathematically correct. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution, which guarantees the right to equality and equal protection of the law. Court's reasoning: The court examined whether the Respondents acted arbitrarily or discriminatorily in the selection process. The court found that the Respondents followed the established criteria in the University Grants Commission handbook. The court noted that the Petitionerâ€™s rank of 98 in the Galle District fell outside the cutoff for the available places, as even the student ranked 97th was not admitted. The court concluded that the Respondents provided a transparent and mathematically sound justification for the selection, negating any claim of arbitrary treatment or infringement of fundamental rights. Finding: There was no infringement of the Petitionerâ€™s fundamental rights under Article 12(1). Issue 2: Interpretation of Admission Criteria Rule applied: Section 3.2.3.2, Notes 1 and 2, and Section 3.3 of the University Grants Commission handbook for 2010/2011. Court's reasoning: The Petitioner relied on a simplified calculation of 40% (all-island) and 55% (district) quotas. The court held this interpretation was erroneous because it ignored the "up to" language in the handbook, as well as specific provisions in Notes 1 and 2 and Section 3.3. These provisions account for base-year quotas (1993/94 and 2002/03), special grounds admissions (Foreign, Defence, and Sports quotas), and practical clustering issues. The court verified the Respondents' detailed calculations, which accounted for these variables, and found them to be accurate and compliant with the handbook. Finding: The Petitionerâ€™s expectations were based on a flawed understanding of the admission criteria; the Respondents' methodology was correct. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the Petitionerâ€™s interpretation of the admission criteria was mathematically and legally flawed (Issue 2) directly informs the conclusion that the Respondents did not act arbitrarily (Issue 1). Because the selection process was found to be consistent with the handbookâ€™s complex requirements, the allegation of a fundamental rights violation necessarily fails. Conclusion The application is dismissed. The court held that the Respondents acted in accordance with the established admission criteria and that no infringement of the Petitioner's fundamental rights occurred. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — University admission criteria — Z score — District merit quota — All Island merit — Legitimate expectations — Administrative action — University Grants Commission — Human Rights Commission

Parties: G P de Silva & Sons International (Pvt) Limited v. Union Assurance Limited
Bench: Priyasath Dep, P.C., J.; Sisira J. de Abrew, J; Sarath de Abrew, J.
Keywords: marine insurance — insurable interest — CIF terms — loss of cargo — shipping documents — bill of lading — good faith — insurance claim — appeal dismissal
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal by G P de Silva & Sons International (Pvt) Limited (the Plaintiff-Appellant) against the judgment of the High Court of Colombo, which dismissed their claim for damages under a marine insurance policy provided by Union Assurance Limited (the Defendant-Respondent). The Plaintiff-Appellant, an export company specializing in cinnamon, had obtained a marine insurance policy from the Defendant-Respondent on 22 December 2003. This policy was confirmed to cover losses during inland transit, including theft or burglary. On 10 March 2005, the Plaintiff-Appellant shipped a consignment of 587 bales of cinnamon worth USD 62,368.15 to a buyer in Mexico City. However, the consignment was reported lost while being transported from the port to the buyer's warehouse. The Plaintiff-Appellant subsequently filed a claim for USD 68,605.63, which was later reduced to USD 16,217.63 after the buyer's insurer in Mexico compensated the buyer for part of the loss. The Defendant-Respondent repudiated the claim, citing several reasons, including the lack of insurable interest at the time of loss, as the buyer had already collected the goods. The High Court ruled in favor of the Defendant-Respondent, leading to the appeal. Issues The primary issues for determination in this appeal are: Whether the Plaintiff-Appellant had an insurable interest in the consignment of cinnamon at the time of the loss. Whether the repudiation of the insurance claim by the Defendant-Respondent was justified based on the terms of the marine insurance policy. Applicable Law Statutes: Marine Insurance Act Cases Referred to: Clements Horst Co Vs Biddel Bros - [1912] AC 18; The Sale of Goods by PS Attiyah, John N Adams and Hector MacQueen, 11th edition.
