Analysis Issue 1: Insurable Interest Rule applied: The principle of insurable interest in marine insurance. Court's reasoning: The court meticulously examined the concept of insurable interest, emphasizing that for a claimant to recover under a marine insurance policy, they must possess an insurable interest in the subject matter at the time of the loss. The court noted that the Plaintiff-Appellant had shipped the goods under CIF (Cost, Insurance, and Freight) terms, which meant that the risk passed to the buyer upon shipment. The evidence presented indicated that the buyer had collected the goods from the port, thus transferring the title and insurable interest to the buyer. The Managing Director of the Plaintiff-Appellant admitted that the buyer had the original bill of lading, which legally equated to possession of the goods. The court referenced legal literature, including the authoritative text on the sale of goods, which supports the notion that once goods are shipped under CIF terms, the seller's obligation is fulfilled upon delivery of the shipping documents, and the buyer remains liable for payment regardless of the goods' condition post-shipment. The court also cited the case of Clements Horst Co Vs Biddel Bros, reinforcing that possession of the bill of lading is tantamount to possession of the goods. Finding: The court concluded that the Plaintiff-Appellant did not have an insurable interest in the consignment at the time of the loss, as the title had passed to the buyer. The appeal was dismissed on this basis. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as the determination of insurable interest directly impacts the validity of the insurance claim. If the Plaintiff-Appellant lacked insurable interest, the repudiation of the claim by the Defendant-Respondent was justified, leading to the dismissal of the appeal. Conclusion The Supreme Court upheld the High Court's decision, affirming that the Plaintiff-Appellant did not possess an insurable interest in the goods at the time of loss and thus was not entitled to recover under the marine insurance policy. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
Counsel: MA Sumanthiran with Eranga Tegal for the Plaintiff-Appellant. Nihal Fernando, P.C. with Rajindra Jayasinghe for the Defendant-Respondent.

Parties: Abeykoon Mayadunnage Isuru Udayantha Abeykoon v. Abeykoon Mayadunnage Somapala Abeykoon and Others
Bench: Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Sisira J. De Abrew, J; Sarath De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Partition Act — Prescriptive Title — Co-ownership — Amicable Partition — Conciliation Board — Ouster — Possession — Legal Precedents — Evidence — Land Law
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a partition action filed by the Plaintiff, Abeykoon Mayadunnage Isuru Udayantha Abeykoon, seeking to partition a land described in the schedule among himself and the Defendants, namely Abeykoon Mayadunnage Somapala Abeykoon, Abeykoon Mayadunnage Gnanalatha Abeykoon, and Hiriyadeniya Karunarathna Ananda Athapattu Mudali. The Plaintiff's claim was initially dismissed by the learned District Judge, prompting the Plaintiff and the 1st Defendant to appeal to the Provincial High Court. The High Court, in its judgment dated 16 March 2012, overturned the District Judge's decision, leading the 3rd Defendant to appeal to the Supreme Court. The Supreme Court granted leave to appeal on specific legal questions regarding the possession and partition of the land, particularly focusing on whether the 3rd Defendant had established a prescriptive title to the land and whether the parties had amicably partitioned the land in a Conciliation Board case in 1975. The crux of the matter lies in the assertion that the 3rd Defendant had possessed the relevant portion of land independently and exclusively since 1975, which was contested by the Plaintiff and the other Defendants. Issues The primary issues before the court were: 1. Whether the 3rd Defendant had established a prescriptive title to the land in question by exclusive possession since 1975. 2. Whether the parties had amicably partitioned the land in the Conciliation Board case in 1975, and if all parties had participated in that process. Applicable Law Statutes: Partition Act Cases Referred to: Wickramarathne and Others Vs Alpenis Perera [1986] 1 SLR 190; Corea Vs Appuhamy 15 NLR 65 Analysis Issue 1: Whether the 3rd Defendant had established a prescriptive title to the land Rule applied: The legal principle regarding prescriptive title and co-ownership. Court's reasoning: The court examined the nature of possession required to establish a prescriptive title, emphasizing that mere possession by one co-owner does not equate to ousting the other co-owners unless there is clear evidence of ouster. The court referenced the case of Wickramarathne and Others Vs Alpenis Perera, which established that a co-owner's possession is legally considered the possession of all co-owners. The court noted that the 3rd Defendant claimed to have possessed Lot 2 of the land exclusively since 1975, erecting fences and preventing others from entering. However, the court found that the evidence presented did not conclusively demonstrate that the other co-owners had been ousted from their rights to the land. The testimony of the 2nd Defendant indicated that neither she nor her father participated in the Conciliation Board case, which undermined the claim of an amicable partition. The court concluded that for a co-owner to acquire a prescriptive title, there must be clear evidence of ouster from common usage, which was not established in this case. Finding: The court found that the 3rd Defendant had not established a prescriptive title to the land due to the lack of evidence of ouster. Issue 2: Whether the parties had amicably partitioned the land in the Conciliation Board case in 1975. Rule applied: The requirement for all parties to participate in a Conciliation Board case for an amicable partition to be valid. Court's reasoning: The court scrutinized the circumstances surrounding the alleged amicable partition in the Conciliation Board case. It was determined that not all parties had participated in the process, particularly highlighted by the testimony of the 2nd Defendant. The court emphasized that for an amicable partition to be recognized, all co-owners must have been involved in the Conciliation Board proceedings. The court reiterated that the absence of participation by any co-owner invalidates the claim of an amicable partition. The court also referenced the legal principle that a co-owner cannot unilaterally alter the status of co-ownership without the consent of all parties involved. Finding: The court concluded that the parties had not amicably partitioned the land in the Conciliation Board case, as not all parties participated, rendering the District Judge's conclusion incorrect. Interaction of Issues The issues of prescriptive title and amicable partition are interrelated, as the establishment of a prescriptive title hinges on the notion of ouster, which in turn is contingent upon the validity of the partition process. If the land was not amicably partitioned, the prescriptive claims of the 3rd Defendant would be further weakened, as the legal framework requires that all co-owners maintain their rights unless explicitly ousted. Conclusion The Supreme Court ultimately dismissed the appeal of the 3rd Defendant, affirming the High Court's judgment that the learned District Judge had erred in his conclusions regarding both the prescriptive title and the amicable partition. The court emphasized the necessity of participation by all co-owners in any partition proceedings and the legal implications of co-ownership in relation to possession and title. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
Counsel: Sudarshani Coorey for the 3rd Defendant-Respondent-Petitioner-Appellant; Vinodh Wickramasoorioya for the Plaintiff-Appellant-Respondent-Respondent; Ms. Thanuja Hathurusinghe for the 1st Defendant-Appellant-Respondent-Respondent

Parties: Wilson and Others v. Kusumawathi
Judgment by: Sisira J De Abrew J, J.
Bench: Saleem Marsoof PC, J; Sisira J De Abrew J, J.; Sarath de Abrew J, J.
Keywords: Appeal from High Court — Civil Procedure Code Section 759 — Petition of appeal — Naming of respondents — Material prejudice — Curable defect — Notice of appeal
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent initiated a partition action in the District Court of Gampaha regarding the land called Othudena Atambagahakumbura. The District Court dismissed the action after trial. The Plaintiff-Respondent subsequently filed an appeal in the High Court of Gampaha. Prior to filing the petition of appeal, the Plaintiff-Respondent filed a notice of appeal and served notices upon all parties involved in the original District Court action. During the proceedings in the High Court, the 7th Defendant-Respondent-Petitioner-Appellant raised a preliminary objection, contending that the Plaintiff-Respondent failed to include the names of all respondents in the petition of appeal as required by Section 758 of the Civil Procedure Code. The High Court overruled this objection, determining that the defect was curable. The 7th Defendant-Respondent-Petitioner-Appellant then appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the failure to name all affected parties in a petition of appeal constitutes a curable defect under Section 759(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Whether the respondents were materially prejudiced by the Plaintiff-Respondent's failure to name all parties in the petition of appeal. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 758 Civil Procedure Code, Section 759(2) Cases Referred to: Nanayakkara v. Warnakulasuriya - [1993/v2-sri-lr-289] Jayasekara v. Lakmini - [2010/v1-sri-lr-41] Analysis Issue 1: Curability of the defect Rule applied: Section 759(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court interpreted Section 759(2) as granting the Court of Appeal wide and discretionary power to provide relief for mistakes, omissions, or defects in complying with procedural requirements, provided that the respondent has not been materially prejudiced. Relying on the precedent in Jayasekara v. Lakmini, the court affirmed that the failure to name all parties in a petition of appeal falls within the scope of a curable defect under this section. Finding: The failure to name all respondents in the petition of appeal is a curable defect under Section 759(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Issue 2: Material prejudice to respondents Rule applied: Section 759(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court applied the test of "material prejudice." It observed that the Plaintiff-Respondent had included the names of all parties in the notice of appeal and had served those notices to all parties and their registered attorneys via registered post. Consequently, the respondents were fully aware of the appeal and had ample opportunity to oppose it. The court concluded that the omission in the petition of appeal did not deprive the respondents of their right to be heard or cause them any disadvantage. Finding: The respondents were not materially prejudiced by the omission. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the determination that the defect is curable under Section 759(2) is contingent upon the finding that no material prejudice occurred. Because the court found that the respondents had been properly notified via the notice of appeal, the procedural omission in the petition became a curable defect rather than a fatal error. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The Plaintiff-Respondent is directed to file an amended caption naming all respondents, and the High Court is ordered to proceed with the appeal. No order for costs is made. Keywords Appeal from High Court — Civil Procedure Code Section 759 — Petition of appeal — Naming of respondents — Material prejudice — Curable defect — Notice of appeal

Parties: Dona Kamalawathie Diwrumpola v. Sirisena
Bench: Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC.J; Buwaneka Aluwihare, J.
Keywords: Right of way — Necessity — Prescriptive user — Adverse possession — Easement — District Court action — High Court appeal — Supreme Court appeal — Landlocked property — Grama Sevaka sketch
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Appellant-Appellant filed an action in the District Court of Embilipitiya against the Defendant-Respondent-Respondent seeking a declaration of a right to use a 10-foot wide road over the land of the Respondent to access her property. The Appellant became the owner of the land in 1983 and began constructing a house, at which time she used a footpath along the boundary of the Respondent's land with his consent. A dispute arose in 1988, leading to a Primary Court order that permitted the use of a 3-foot wide roadway. The Appellant subsequently filed the present action in 1998, claiming a 10-foot wide right of way based on necessity and prescriptive user. Evidence established that the Appellant's land is surrounded by a lake on three sides and is land-locked on the fourth side, leaving no alternative access route. While the Appellant failed to provide evidence supporting the claim for a 10-foot wide road, the evidence confirmed the continuous use of a 3-foot wide path since 1983. The District Court denied the 10-foot wide road but granted a 3-foot wide road. The High Court of Civil Appeal dismissed the Appellant's appeal, erroneously citing 1988 as the date of the District Court action to conclude that prescription had not been proven. The Appellant then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court of Civil Appeal erred in failing to consider the Appellant's claim for a right of way by necessity and the evidence regarding the lack of an alternative route. Whether the Appellant established a right of way by prescription for a 3-foot wide road through sufficient evidence of user for over ten years. Whether the High Court of Civil Appeal misdirected itself regarding the date of the District Court action and the resulting impact on the plea of prescription. Applicable Law Statutes: Not specified Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Right of way by necessity Rule applied: The common law principle of easement by necessity. Court's reasoning: The court examined the sketch produced by the Grama Sevaka (P2), which demonstrated that the Appellant's land is water-locked on three sides and land-locked on the remaining side. The court found that the Respondent did not challenge the evidence that no alternative route exists to reach the Appellant's house. Consequently, the court determined that a right of way through the Respondent's land is a matter of sheer necessity. Finding: The court found in favor of the Appellant, confirming the existence of a right of way by necessity. Issue 2: Right of way by prescription Rule applied: The law of prescription regarding the acquisition of a right of way through adverse possession for over ten years. Court's reasoning: The court noted that while the Appellant failed to prove the necessity of a 10-foot wide road, the evidence established that a 3-foot wide road had been used continuously since 1983. By the time the action was filed in 1998, the Appellant had satisfied the requirement of ten years of adverse user. Finding: The court held that the Appellant successfully proved a prescriptive right to a 3-foot wide road. Issue 3: Misdirection on procedural facts Rule applied: The court applied the principle of accurate judicial assessment of evidence and procedural history. Court's reasoning: The High Court of Civil Appeal erroneously identified 1988 as the date the District Court action was filed, which led to an incorrect conclusion regarding the duration of the Appellant's prescriptive user. The Supreme Court clarified that the action was filed in 1998, meaning the period of user from 1983 to 1998 exceeded the ten-year requirement. Finding: The High Court of Civil Appeal misdirected itself on the facts, and the Supreme Court corrected this error. Interaction of Issues The findings on necessity and prescription are mutually reinforcing. The court's determination that the land is land-locked (necessity) provides the context for the long-term usage of the path (prescription). By correcting the factual error regarding the filing date, the court established that the Appellant's long-term reliance on the path for access was both a matter of necessity and a legally acquired prescriptive right. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgments of the High Court of Civil Appeal and the District Court are set aside, and the Appellant is granted the right to use a 3-foot wide road from the Godakawela-Ratnapura main road to her premises. Keywords Right of way — Necessity — Prescriptive user — Adverse possession — Easement — District Court action — High Court appeal — Supreme Court appeal — Landlocked property — Grama Sevaka sketch

Parties: De Seram v. Dehiwela Mount Lavinia Municipal Council
Bench: Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Eva Wanasundera PC, J; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Appeal against judgment — Title to land — Vindicatory action — Prescriptive title — Adverse possession — Power of attorney — Fraudulent deed — Cemetery land — Acceptance of title
Summary: Facts Fritzroy Clarance De Seram, the Plaintiff-Respondent, initiated a vindicatory action against the Dehiwela Mount Lavinia Municipal Council, the Defendant-Appellant, seeking a declaration of title to a land identified as Lot No. 6B of Plan No. 1921 and the ejectment of the Defendant-Appellant from said land. The Plaintiff-Respondent claimed ownership through inheritance from his predecessor, Peter Thomas De Seram, and subsequent deeds. The Defendant-Appellant contested the title, alleging that the Plaintiff-Respondentâ€™s chain of title was fraudulent and asserting a claim of prescriptive title based on the land's use as a cemetery. The District Court of Mount Lavinia ruled in favor of the Plaintiff-Respondent, finding that he had established valid title and that the Defendant-Appellant had failed to prove prescriptive title. The Civil Appellate High Court of Mount Lavinia affirmed the District Court's judgment. The Defendant-Appellant subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court, which granted leave to appeal on several questions of law, including the failure to prove title, the validity of the Power of Attorney, the lack of a disclosed cause of action, and the failure to consider the Cemeteries and Burial Grounds Ordinance. Issues Whether the Plaintiff-Respondent failed to prove his title, which is an indispensable requirement in a vindicatory action. Whether the Defendant-Appellant established prescriptive title to the subject matter adverse to the rights of the Plaintiff-Respondent. Whether the courts below erred in failing to consider the validity of the Power of Attorney (P1) and the provisions of the Cemeteries and Burial Grounds Ordinance. Applicable Law Statutes: Cemeteries and Burial Grounds Ordinance Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Proof of Title Rule applied: The burden of proof in a vindicatory action requires the plaintiff to establish clear title to the property. Court's reasoning: The court examined the chain of title, noting that Lot No. 6 of Plan No. 233 was inherited by the Plaintiff-Respondent and his siblings. The court rejected the Defendant-Appellant's argument that the deed (No. 934) was fraudulent, noting that the Defendant-Appellant had previously recognized the title of a third party (Samaraweera Silva) who derived title from the same deed. The court applied the principle that a party cannot approbate and reprobate. Furthermore, the court found that the Defendant-Appellantâ€™s own correspondence (P5) contained an admission that the land belonged to the Plaintiff-Respondent. Finding: The Plaintiff-Respondent successfully established his title to Lot No. 6B of Plan No. 1921. Issue 2: Prescriptive Title Rule applied: To establish prescriptive title, a party must prove adverse possession for the statutory period. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the Defendant-Appellantâ€™s own land officer testified that the council possessed the land with the permission of the Plaintiff-Respondent and his brothers. Possession with the owner's permission negates the "adverse" requirement of prescription. Additionally, the court highlighted that the Defendant-Appellantâ€™s offer to pay compensation for the land in document P5 constituted an implied admission of the Plaintiff-Respondentâ€™s ownership, which is inconsistent with a claim of prescriptive title. Finding: The Defendant-Appellant failed to establish prescriptive title. Issue 3: Procedural and Statutory Matters Rule applied: The court evaluated the necessity of addressing the Power of Attorney (P1) and the Cemeteries and Burial Grounds Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The court observed that no evidence was led at trial to challenge the validity of the Power of Attorney (P1), and no specific issue was framed regarding it. Consequently, the court found no error in the lower courts' treatment of this document. Regarding the Cemeteries and Burial Grounds Ordinance, the court found no basis to interfere with the lower courts' findings based on the evidence presented. Finding: The arguments regarding the Power of Attorney and the Ordinance were rejected. Interaction of Issues The findings on title and prescription are mutually reinforcing. The court's determination that the Defendant-Appellant acknowledged the Plaintiff-Respondent's title through the offer of compensation (P5) simultaneously defeated the claim of adverse possession and solidified the proof of title, rendering the Defendant-Appellant's challenges to the chain of title legally inconsistent. Conclusion The Supreme Court held that the Plaintiff-Respondent established his title and that the Defendant-Appellant failed to prove prescriptive title. The appeal is dismissed with costs. Keywords Appeal against judgment — Title to land — Vindicatory action — Prescriptive title — Adverse possession — Power of attorney — Fraudulent deed — Cemetery land — Acceptance of title

Parties: Board of Investment of Sri Lanka v. Million Garments (Pvt) Ltd
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C. J; K. Sripavan, J.; E. Wanasundera, P.C. J
Keywords: Arbitration Act — Time limit for filing applications for leave to appeal — Supreme Court Rules — Reasonable time — Section 37(2) — Section 43(a) — Part 1-C of the Supreme Court Rules — Enforcement of arbitral awards
Summary: Facts The Respondent-Petitioner, Board of Investment of Sri Lanka, sought leave to appeal against a judgment of the High Court of Colombo dated 14 May 2012. The High Court had pronounced judgment and entered a decree in terms of an arbitral award pursuant to Section 31(6) of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. The Petitioner-Respondent, Million Garments (Pvt) Ltd, raised a preliminary objection regarding the maintainability of the application. The Petitioner-Respondent contended that the application for leave to appeal was filed in the Supreme Court Registry on 26 June 2012, which was the forty-third day after the High Court judgment. The Petitioner-Respondent argued that the application was time-barred as it exceeded the six-week period prescribed by law. The Respondent-Petitioner argued that the Arbitration Act did not specify a time limit for such appeals and that the application was filed within a reasonable time, further noting that the underlying arbitral award was allegedly tainted by fraud. Issues Whether the six-week time limit prescribed in Rule 7 of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990, applies to an application for leave to appeal under Section 37(2) of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. Whether the application for leave to appeal filed on the forty-third day after the High Court judgment is time-barred. Applicable Law Statutes: Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka (Article 136) Industrial Disputes Act No. 43 of 1950 High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) (Amendment) Act No. 54 of 2006 Cases Referred to: Tea Small Factories Ltd. v Weragoda - [1994/v3-sri-lr-353] Mahaweli Authority of Sri Lanka v United Agency Construction - [2002/v1-sri-lr-8] George Stuart & Co. Ltd. v Lankem Tea & Rubber Plantations Ltd. - [2004/v1-sri-lr-246] Priyanthi Chandrika Jinadasa v Pathma Hemamali - [2011/v1-sri-lr-337] Karunawathie Wickremesinghe Samaranayake v Ranjanie Warnakulasuriya - [sc-hc/ca/la-137/2010] Vithana v Weerasinghe - [1981/v1-sri-lr-52] Lanka Orix Leasing Company Limited v Pinto - [2002/v2-sri-lr-115] L.A. Sudath Rohana v Mohomed Zeena - [sc-hcca-la-111/2010] Bazalgette v. Lowe - [24-l.j.-ch.-368] Shah v Presiding Officer, Labour Court, Coimbatore - [air-1978-sc-12] Kailayar v Kandiah - [59-nlr-117] Sitamparanathan v Premaratne - [1996/v2-sri-lr-202] Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of Rule 7 Rule applied: Rule 7 and Rule 28 of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990, read with Article 136 of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Court reasoned that while the Arbitration Act is silent on a specific time limit, the application falls under Part 1-C of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990. The Court held that it is empowered by Article 136 of the Constitution to regulate appellate procedures. Relying on precedent, the Court determined that the six-week period (42 days) prescribed in Rule 7 serves as the standard for "reasonable time" for all such appeals to the Supreme Court, ensuring consistency and preventing the absurdity of indefinite appeal periods. Finding: The six-week time limit prescribed in Rule 7 of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990, is applicable to applications for leave to appeal under Section 37(2) of the Arbitration Act. Issue 2: Time-bar of the application Rule applied: Rule 7 of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990. Court's reasoning: The Court applied the principle of excluding the date of the judgment (14 May 2012) and including the date of filing. The calculation resulted in the 42nd day being 25 June 2012. The application was filed on 26 June 2012, which is the 43rd day. The Court rejected the argument that special circumstances or allegations of fraud could override the mandatory time limit. Finding: The application was filed outside the prescribed time limit and is therefore time-barred. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the determination that the six-week rule applies (Issue 1) provides the mandatory threshold against which the filing date is measured (Issue 2). The Court's reasoning emphasizes that the legislative intent of the Arbitration Act to maintain the efficacy of the arbitral process necessitates a strict, uniform time limit, thereby precluding the use of a flexible "reasonableness" test that would otherwise undermine the finality of arbitral awards. Conclusion The preliminary objection is upheld. The application for leave to appeal is dismissed as time-barred. No order for costs is made. Keywords Arbitration Act — Time limit for filing applications for leave to appeal — Supreme Court Rules — Reasonable time — Section 37(2) — Section 43(a) — Part 1-C of the Supreme Court Rules — Enforcement of arbitral awards

Parties: Don Eranga Indrajith v. George Steuart Finance Limited
Bench: Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Rohini Marasinghe, J.; Buwaneka Aluwihare, J.
Keywords: Arbitration clause — Jurisdiction of High Court — Civil Procedure Code — Section 5 of the Arbitration Act — Lease agreement — Rescheduling of loan — Preliminary objection — Dismissal of plaint
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Petitioner, Munasinghege Don Eranga Indrajith, entered into a lease agreement for a Toyota Allion motor car with Asia Commerce Ltd. The Defendant-Respondent, George Steuart Finance Limited, eventually succeeded the original lessor and rescheduled the lease agreement. Following the repossession of the vehicle by the Defendant-Respondent on 19 November 2012, the Plaintiff-Petitioner filed an action in the Commercial High Court seeking a declaration that the termination of the contract was arbitrary, a refund of payments made, and an enjoining order to prevent the sale of the vehicle. The Defendant-Respondent filed objections, asserting that the court lacked jurisdiction due to an arbitration clause in the lease agreement. The High Court upheld this preliminary objection and dismissed the plaint. The Plaintiff-Petitioner subsequently filed an application for leave to appeal to the Supreme Court. During the proceedings, the Defendant-Respondent raised preliminary objections regarding the maintainability of the leave to appeal application, arguing that the order was a final judgment requiring a direct appeal rather than a leave to appeal application. Issues Whether the order of the High Court dismissing the plaint due to an arbitration clause constitutes a final judgment or an interlocutory order for the purposes of determining the correct appellate procedure under the Civil Procedure Code. Whether the High Court was divested of jurisdiction to hear the action under Section 5 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 due to the existence of an arbitration clause in the lease agreement. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 754 Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995, Section 4, Section 5 Cases Referred to: Elgitread Lanka (Pvt) Ltd. v. Bino Tyres (Pvt) Ltd. - [2011-bar-association-law-reports] Siriwardena v. Air Ceylon Ltd. - [1984/v1-sri-lr-286] Ranjith v. Kusumawathi - [1998/v3-sri-lr-232] Salamon v. Warner - [1881-1-qb-734] S.R. Chettiar v. S.N. Chettiar - [2011-bar-association-law-journal-25] Analysis Issue 1: Classification of the High Court Order Rule applied: Section 754 of the Civil Procedure Code and the "application approach" established in Ranjith v. Kusumawathi. Court's reasoning: The court evaluated whether the dismissal of the plaint based on a preliminary objection was a final judgment or an interlocutory order. Applying the "application approach," the court reasoned that if the High Court had rejected the preliminary objection, the trial would have proceeded. Because the decision to uphold the objection did not finally dispose of the matter on its merits in a way that would preclude further proceedings had the objection been overruled, it is treated as an interlocutory order. Finding: The order is an interlocutory order; therefore, the Plaintiff-Petitioner correctly invoked the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court by filing a leave to appeal application. Issue 2: Jurisdiction under the Arbitration Act Rule applied: Section 4 and Section 5 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. Court's reasoning: The court confirmed that the lease agreement contained a mandatory arbitration clause. Under Section 5 of the Arbitration Act, if a party institutes legal proceedings in court regarding a matter agreed to be submitted to arbitration, the court is divested of jurisdiction if the other party objects to the court exercising such jurisdiction. The Defendant-Respondent explicitly objected to the High Court's jurisdiction. Finding: The High Court correctly determined that it lacked jurisdiction to hear the action, and the dismissal of the plaint was legally sound. Interaction of Issues The resolution of the first issue allowed the court to proceed to the merits of the leave to appeal application. However, the finding on the second issue—that the arbitration clause effectively ousted the jurisdiction of the High Court—rendered the Plaintiff-Petitioner's substantive challenge to the dismissal unsuccessful. Conclusion The preliminary objection regarding the maintainability of the leave to appeal application is overruled. The application for leave to appeal is dismissed as the High Court correctly held that it lacked jurisdiction due to the arbitration clause. Keywords Arbitration clause — Jurisdiction of High Court — Civil Procedure Code — Section 5 of the Arbitration Act — Lease agreement — Rescheduling of loan — Preliminary objection — Dismissal of plaint

Parties: Board of Investment of Sri Lanka v. Million Garments (Pvt) Ltd
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C. J.; K. Sripavan, J.; E. Wanasundera, P.C. J.
Keywords: Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 — Leave to Appeal — Time Limit — Supreme Court Rules — Reasonable Time — Judicial Interpretation — Appealability — Preliminary Objection — Enforcement of Arbitral Award
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for leave to appeal from a judgment delivered by the High Court of Colombo on 14 May 2012, which enforced an arbitral award under the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. The Board of Investment of Sri Lanka (the Respondent-Petitioner) sought to enforce an arbitral award against Million Garments (Pvt) Ltd (the Petitioner-Respondent). The Respondent-Petitioner filed the application for leave to appeal on 26 June 2012, which was 43 days after the judgment was pronounced. The Respondent-Petitioner raised a preliminary objection claiming that the application was time-barred as it was filed beyond the prescribed time limit. The core of the dispute centered on whether the application for leave to appeal was lodged within a reasonable time as mandated by the Arbitration Act and the Supreme Court Rules. Issues Whether the application for leave to appeal was filed within the time limit prescribed by law. Whether the absence of a specific time limit in the Arbitration Act allows for a flexible interpretation regarding the filing of the application. Applicable Law Statutes: Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 — Supreme Court Rules, 1990 Cases Referred to: Tea Small Factories Ltd. v Weragoda - [1994] 3 SLR 353; Mahaweli Authority of Sri Lanka v United Agency Construction - [2002] 1 SLR 8; George Stuart & Co. Ltd. v Lankem Tea & Rubber Plantations Ltd. - [2004] 1 SLR 246; Priyanthi Chandrika Jinadasa v Pathma Hemamali - [2011] 1 SLR 337; Karunawathie Wickremesinghe Samaranayake v Ranjanie Warnakulasuriya - SC HC/CA/LA No. 137/2010 (unreported). Analysis Issue 1: Whether the application for leave to appeal was filed within the time limit prescribed by law. Rule applied: Section 37(2) of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 and Rule 7 of the Supreme Court Rules, 1990. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the provisions of the Arbitration Act, particularly Section 37, which allows appeals from High Court orders only on questions of law with the leave of the Supreme Court. The Court noted that while the Arbitration Act does not specify a time limit for filing such applications, the Supreme Court Rules provide a framework for appeals. Rule 7 of the Supreme Court Rules mandates that applications for leave to appeal must be filed within six weeks of the judgment. The Court emphasized that the absence of a specific time limit in the Arbitration Act does not exempt parties from adhering to the procedural rules established by the Supreme Court. The Court referred to previous judgments, including Tea Small Factories Ltd. v Weragoda and Mahaweli Authority of Sri Lanka v United Agency Construction, which established that in the absence of explicit time limits, a reasonable period should be inferred, typically aligning with the six-week period outlined in Rule 7. The Court concluded that the application was indeed time-barred as it was filed on the 43rd day after the judgment, exceeding the six-week limit. Finding: The application for leave to appeal was time-barred. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as the determination of whether the application was filed within the prescribed time limit directly impacts the court's jurisdiction to hear the appeal. The absence of a specific time limit in the Arbitration Act necessitates reliance on the Supreme Court Rules, which impose a mandatory six-week period for filing applications for leave to appeal. Conclusion The Supreme Court upheld the preliminary objection raised by the Respondent-Petitioner, concluding that the application for leave to appeal was filed outside the permissible time frame. Consequently, the Court dismissed the application without making any order for costs, reinforcing the importance of adhering to procedural timelines in the appellate process. Keywords Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 — Leave to Appeal — Time Limit — Supreme Court Rules — Reasonable Time — Judicial Interpretation — Appealability — Preliminary Objection — Enforcement of Arbitral Award
Counsel: Romesh de Silva, P.C. with Hiran de Alwis for Respondent-Petitioner. Gamini Marapana, P.C. with Navin Marapana for Petitioner-Respondent.

Parties: Munasinghege Don Eranga Indrajith v. George Steuart Finance Limited
Bench: Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; R. Marasinghe, J; B.P. Aluwihare, PC, J.
Keywords: Civil Procedure Code — Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 — Lease Agreement — Jurisdiction — Enjoining Order — Preliminary Objection — Cause of Action — Final Order — Appeal — Commercial High Court — Rescheduled Lease — Repossession — Legal Interest — Restitutio in Integrum — Balance of Convenience — Prima Facie Case
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Petitioner, Munasinghege Don Eranga Indrajith, filed an application seeking leave to appeal against a judgment delivered by the High Court of Colombo on 7 May 2013. The case arose from a dispute regarding the termination of a rescheduled lease agreement for a Toyota Allion motor car valued at Rs. 6,900,000. The Plaintiff had initially obtained a leasing facility from Asia Commerce Ltd., which was succeeded by Divasa Finance Ltd., and ultimately by the Defendant, George Steuart Finance Limited. The Plaintiff alleged that the Defendant wrongfully repossessed the vehicle on 19 November 2012, despite the existence of a rescheduled lease agreement. The Plaintiff sought various reliefs, including a declaration that the termination of the lease was arbitrary, restitution of the vehicle, and an enjoining order to prevent the sale of the vehicle. The Defendant contested the action, arguing that the Plaintiff had violated the lease agreement and that the court lacked jurisdiction due to an arbitration clause in the lease. The High Court upheld the Defendant's preliminary objections, dismissing the Plaintiff's action on the grounds that the court had no jurisdiction to hear the case due to the arbitration clause. The Plaintiff subsequently sought leave to appeal this decision. Issues Whether the Plaintiff-Petitioner can maintain the application for leave to appeal given the High Court's dismissal of the action as a final order. Whether the High Court had jurisdiction to hear the case in light of the arbitration clause in the lease agreement. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 Cases Referred to: Siriwardena vs. Air Ceylon Ltd. (1984) 1SLR 286; Ranjith vs. Kusumawathi (1998) 3 Sri.LR 232; Elgitread Lanka (Pvt) Ltd. vs. Bino Tyres (Pvt) Ltd. (2011) Bar Association Law Reports 130. Analysis Issue 1: Maintainability of the Application Rule applied: Section 754 of the Civil Procedure Code Court's reasoning: The court first addressed the preliminary objection raised by the Defendant regarding the maintainability of the Plaintiff's application for leave to appeal. The Defendant contended that the order made by the High Court was a final order, and thus the proper remedy would have been to file an appeal rather than a leave to appeal application. The Supreme Court examined whether the High Court's dismissal of the Plaintiff's action constituted a judgment or an interlocutory order. The court referenced the case of Siriwardena vs. Air Ceylon Ltd., where it was established that for an order to be considered a final judgment, it must dispose of the rights of the parties. The court noted that the High Court's dismissal did not address the merits of the case, and if the preliminary objections had been overruled, the trial would have proceeded. Therefore, the court concluded that the dismissal order could be viewed as a final order, allowing the Plaintiff to invoke the Supreme Court's jurisdiction through a leave to appeal application. Finding: The court overruled the Defendant's objection regarding the maintainability of the application. Issue 2: Jurisdiction of the High Court Rule applied: Section 5 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 Court's reasoning: The court then turned to the substantive issue of whether the High Court had jurisdiction to hear the Plaintiff's action given the arbitration clause in the lease agreement. The Defendant argued that the existence of the arbitration clause precluded the court from exercising jurisdiction over the matter. The court acknowledged that the lease agreement contained an arbitration clause mandating that disputes be resolved through arbitration. The court cited Section 5 of the Arbitration Act, which states that if a party to an arbitration agreement initiates legal proceedings in court regarding a matter agreed to be submitted for arbitration, the court shall have no jurisdiction to hear the case if the other party objects. The court found that the Defendant had indeed objected to the court's jurisdiction, and since the Plaintiff did not submit the dispute to arbitration as required, the High Court correctly dismissed the action. Finding: The court upheld the High Court's decision, concluding that it lacked jurisdiction to hear the case due to the arbitration clause. Interaction of Issues The issues of maintainability and jurisdiction are interrelated, as the determination of whether the Plaintiff could maintain the leave to appeal application hinged on the nature of the High Court's order. The court's analysis of the jurisdictional issue further reinforced the conclusion that the High Court's dismissal was appropriate given the arbitration clause, thereby validating the Plaintiff's recourse to the Supreme Court. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the Plaintiff's application for leave to appeal, affirming the High Court's ruling that it lacked jurisdiction to hear the case due to the arbitration clause in the lease agreement. The court found no grounds to grant leave, and thus the application was dismissed without costs.
Counsel: Panduka Abeynayake with Yalith Wijesundera for the Plaintiff-Petitioner. Shanaka de Livera for the Defendant-Respondent Instructed by Livera Associates.

Parties: Fareeda v. Noor and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC. J; Eva Wanasundera, PC. J; Sarath De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Partition Law — Section 66 — Alienation of interest — Contingent interest — Validity of transfer — Lis pendens — Partition action
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Appellant-Appellant filed an action in the District Court of Kegalle seeking a declaration of title to a portion of land described as Lot 1C of Plan No. 2046/A, which was part of the land known as Pinneowita Watta. The Appellant claimed title through a predecessor who had purchased a portion of the property from the children of the 7th Defendant in a previous partition action, D.C. Kegalle No. 17075/P. The 1st and 2nd Defendants-Respondent-Respondents contested the claim, asserting that the 7th Defendant in the original partition action, A.W. Sithi Zubeitha, had transferred her interest in the property via Deed No. 19 dated 15.01.1966 to H.K. Piyasena and H.K. Warnelis while the partition action was still pending. These vendees subsequently transferred their interest in Lot 1C to the 14th Defendant, Brumpisingho, who then transferred the property to the 1st and 2nd Defendants. The 1st and 2nd Defendants had been in possession of the property and the house thereon for a significant period. The central dispute concerned whether the transfer by Deed No. 19, executed during the pendency of the partition action, was void under Section 66 of the Partition Law. Issues Whether the High Court of Civil Appeal erred in law by holding that Deed No. 19 dated 15.01.1966 is not obnoxious to Section 66 of the Partition Law. Applicable Law Statutes: Partition Law No. 21 of 1977 Partition Ordinance No. 10 of 1863 Registration of Documents Ordinance Cases Referred to: Babun v. Amarasekera - [1-scc-24] Louis Appuhamy v. Punchi Baba - [10-nlr-196] Subasena v. Porolis - [16-nlr-319] Khan Bhai v. Perera - [26-nlr-204] Sirisoma v. Saranelis Appuhamy - [51-nlr-337] B. Sillie Fernando v. W. Siliman Fernando - [64-nlr-404] Sirinatha v. Sirisena - [1998/v3-sri-lr-19] Analysis Issue 1: Validity of Deed No. 19 under Section 66 of the Partition Law Rule applied: Section 66 of the Partition Law No. 21 of 1977, which prohibits the voluntary alienation of undivided shares or interests in land subject to a pending partition action, and the common law principle regarding the alienation of contingent interests. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the language of Deed No. 19, which explicitly transferred the vendor's right, title, and interest to the share that would be allotted to her upon the conclusion of the partition action. The Court reasoned that Section 66 of the Partition Law (and its predecessor, Section 17 of the Partition Ordinance) is intended to prevent the delay of partition proceedings by the intervention of new parties, rather than to prohibit the alienation of a contingent interest. Relying on the precedent set in Khan Bhai v. Perera and Sirisoma v. Saranelis Appuhamy, the Court held that a party to a partition action may validly alienate the interest they expect to receive upon the final decree. The Court found that such a transfer is not an alienation of an undivided interest presently vested, but rather an alienation of a future interest that vests automatically upon the conclusion of the partition action. Finding: Deed No. 19 is not obnoxious to Section 66 of the Partition Law and constitutes a valid transfer of title. Conclusion The Supreme Court answered the question of law in the negative, holding that the deed in question was valid. The judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court was affirmed, and the appeal was dismissed. No costs were ordered. Keywords Partition Law — Section 66 — Alienation of interest — Contingent interest — Validity of transfer — Lis pendens — Partition action

Parties: Jayasinge and Thilakarathne v. Mangalasena Edirisinghe and Others
Bench: Eva Wanasundera, PC. J; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.; Sarath De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Leave to appeal — Misrepresentation of facts — Consideration of documents — Trial de novo — Partition action — Exclusion of land from corpus — Uberriesima fides — Revision application — District Court judgment
Summary: Facts The Plaintiffs initiated a partition action in 1990 regarding a land identified as Lot A2 in Plan 137/5/P. During the proceedings, the Defendants sought to include additional smaller lots into the corpus. Two survey commissions were issued, resulting in Plan X and Plan Y. Plan Y included the original land and four additional lots, with two of those lots, C and D, being further subdivided. During the trial, the 4th Defendant testified that he no longer wished to include Lots C and D in the partition as they had been alienated to third parties. The 1st Plaintiff, through counsel, consented to the partition of the remaining portions. The District Court subsequently entered judgment excluding Lots C and D from the partition. The Plaintiffs did not appeal the District Court judgment. Instead, seven months later, they filed a revision application in the Civil Appellate High Court. The 4th Defendant opposed this application, arguing that the Plaintiffs misrepresented facts and breached the duty of uberrima fides. The High Court allowed the revision application and ordered a trial de novo. The 2nd and 4th Defendants appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court erred in failing to consider whether the Plaintiffs were guilty of misrepresenting material facts in their revision application. Whether the High Court erred in failing to consider the document marked Z, which contradicted the Plaintiffs' claims regarding the necessity of a revision application. Whether the High Court erred in ordering a trial de novo when the exclusion of Lots C and D from the partition was based on uncontroverted evidence and the consent of the parties. Applicable Law Statutes: Not specified Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Misrepresentation of material facts Rule applied: The principle of uberrima fides (utmost good faith) in legal proceedings. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the Plaintiffs admitted in their written submissions to the High Court that they had failed to maintain uberrima fides. Given this admission, the court reasoned that the High Court should not have entertained or allowed the revision application, as the Plaintiffs had failed to provide valid exceptional grounds for such relief. Finding: The High Court erred by failing to address the Plaintiffs' lack of good faith and their admission of misrepresentation. Issue 2: Failure to consider evidence (Document Z) Rule applied: The requirement that judicial decisions must be based on the evidence contained in the court record. Court's reasoning: The 4th Defendant submitted document Z to demonstrate that the grounds for the revision application were fabricated. The Supreme Court found that the High Court ignored this document, which contained verified court records contradicting the Plaintiffs' assertions. The High Court failed to evaluate the evidence presented by the Appellants, relying instead on unsubstantiated allegations. Finding: The High Court erred by failing to consider the contents of document Z, which was critical to the determination of the revision application. Issue 3: Propriety of the trial de novo Rule applied: The principle that a court must base its findings on the evidence recorded in the proceedings. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the exclusion of Lots C and D was based on the 4th Defendant's testimony, which was not challenged by the Plaintiffs at the time. The Plaintiffs had received the partition they originally sought in their plaint. The Supreme Court found no record of any application for cross-examination or re-examination that was denied by the District Judge. Ordering a trial de novo twenty-one years after the action commenced, for a negligible extent of land (7.71 perches), was deemed unjustified and contrary to the evidence on record. Finding: The High Court erred in ordering a trial de novo, as the District Court judgment was consistent with the evidence and the conduct of the parties. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are interconnected; the Plaintiffs' failure to act with uberrima fides (Issue 1) and their reliance on fabricated grounds (Issue 2) directly undermined the legal basis for the High Court's decision to grant a trial de novo (Issue 3). The Supreme Court concluded that the High Court's failure to scrutinize the procedural integrity of the revision application led to an erroneous order that ignored the established record of the District Court. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, and the judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court of Gampaha dated 26.07.2011 is set aside. The judgment of the District Court dated 10.07.2009 is affirmed, and the Respondents are ordered to pay costs of Rs. 25,000 to the Appellants. Keywords Leave to appeal — Misrepresentation of facts — Consideration of documents — Trial de novo — Partition action — Exclusion of land from corpus — Uberriesima fides — Revision application — District Court judgment

Parties: Andrawas Patabendi Ganendra de Vaas Gunawardena and Others v. The Hon. Attorney-General
Bench: Mohan Pieris, PC, CJ.; S.E. Wanasundera, PC, J.; B.P. Aluwihare, PC, J.; Sisira J. De Abrew, J.; Sarath De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Code of Criminal Procedure Act — Prevention of Terrorism Act — Abuse of Process — Indictment — Fair Trial — Judicial Custody — Detention Orders — Legal Precedents — Criminal Procedure — Rights of the Accused — Trial at Bar — Attorney General — Legal Representation — Judicial Review
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal made by six accused-appellants against an order of the Trial at Bar that dismissed their objections to the maintainability of the indictment against them. The accused were charged under the Penal Code after being arrested and detained under the Prevention of Terrorism (Temporary Provisions) Act No. 48 of 1979. The appellants contended that the Attorney General acted beyond his powers by filing an indictment under the Penal Code while the investigation was conducted under the Prevention of Terrorism Act. They argued that this constituted an abuse of process and sought to quash the order of the Trial at Bar, claiming that the indictment was tainted and that their continued detention impacted their liberty. The appeal was heard on 29 October 2014, and the court was tasked with determining the legality of the indictment and the procedures followed during the investigation. Issues Whether the Attorney General had the authority to file an indictment under the Penal Code when the investigation was conducted under the Prevention of Terrorism Act. Whether the indictment was tainted due to the alleged abuse of process. Applicable Law Statutes: Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979 — Prevention of Terrorism (Temporary Provisions) Act No. 48 of 1979 Cases Referred to: R.P. Wijesiri v. Attorney General - [1980] 2 Sri.L.R 317; T.N. Fernando, Assistant Commissioner of Excise v. Nelum Gamage, Bribery Commissioner and another - [1994] 3 Sri.L.R 190; Reg. v. Derby Crown Court, Ex parte Brooks - [1984] 80 Cr.App.R. 164; Reg. v. Sang - [1980] A.C. 402. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Attorney General had the authority to file an indictment under the Penal Code when the investigation was conducted under the Prevention of Terrorism Act. Rule applied: The court examined the provisions of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979 and the Prevention of Terrorism Act No. 48 of 1979. The court noted that Section 5 of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act mandates that all offences, including those under the Penal Code, must be investigated and dealt with according to its provisions. The court emphasized that the investigators had adhered to the statutory requirements of the Code throughout the investigation. The court highlighted that the use of the Prevention of Terrorism Act was limited to the arrest and detention of the accused, and the subsequent investigation and indictment were conducted under the Code of Criminal Procedure Act. The court referenced the case of R.P. Wijesiri v. Attorney General, noting that the facts of the current case were distinguishable and did not exhibit the same procedural irregularities. The court concluded that the Attorney General acted within his authority and that the indictment was validly presented. Finding: The court found that the Attorney General had the authority to file the indictment under the Penal Code, and there was no abuse of process in this regard. Issue 2: Whether the indictment was tainted due to the alleged abuse of process. Rule applied: The court considered the arguments presented by the counsel for the accused-appellants regarding the alleged abuse of process stemming from the use of the Prevention of Terrorism Act during the investigation. The court analyzed the timeline of events, including the arrests and the subsequent legal proceedings. It noted that the investigators had consistently reported their findings in accordance with the Code of Criminal Procedure Act, and the use of the Prevention of Terrorism Act was justified based on the circumstances of the case. The court referenced the principles established in Reg. v. Derby Crown Court, Ex parte Brooks, regarding the definition of abuse of process, emphasizing that the prosecution must not manipulate the court's process to the detriment of the accused. The court concluded that the procedural steps taken by the investigators did not constitute an abuse of process, and the indictment was not tainted by any illegality. Finding: The court determined that the indictment was not tainted and that the procedures followed were lawful and appropriate. Interaction of Issues The issues of the Attorney General's authority to file the indictment and the alleged abuse of process are interrelated, as the latter hinges on the legitimacy of the former. The court's analysis demonstrated that the indictment was validly presented, which negated the claims of abuse of process. The court's findings on both issues reinforced the conclusion that the legal processes adhered to were in accordance with established law and did not infringe upon the rights of the accused. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal, affirming the order of the Trial at Bar. The court directed that the trial should proceed without delay and that the accused-appellants' application for bail was rejected. The court emphasized the importance of expeditious justice in light of the public outcry surrounding the case, ensuring that the trial would continue without adjournments due to interlocutory appeals. The court's ruling underscored the balance between the rights of the accused and the need for a fair and timely trial. Keywords Code of Criminal Procedure Act — Prevention of Terrorism Act — Abuse of Process — Indictment — Fair Trial — Judicial Custody — Detention Orders — Legal Precedents — Criminal Procedure — Rights of the Accused — Trial at Bar — Attorney General — Legal Representation — Judicial Review
Counsel: Anil Silva PC with Chandika Pieris and Sumithra Waidyasekera for the 1st -3rd Accused-Appellants. D.P. Kumarasinghe PC with Neville Abeyratne, Mahendra Kumarasinghe and Asitha Vipulanayake for the 4th & 5th Accused-Appellants. Anuja Premaratne with Chamath Wickramasinghe, Nayana Dissanayake and Iromie Jayarathne for the 6th Accused-Appellant. Ayesha Jinasena DSG with Varunika Hettige SSC and Nayomi Wickramasekera SC for the Attorney-General.

Parties: Paradeniyalage and Others v. Paradeniyalage and Others
Bench: Eva Wanasundera, PC.J; Buwaneka Aluwihare, PC.J; Priyantha Jayawardane, PC.J
Keywords: Partition action — Pedigree of ownership — Deeds as evidence — Prescriptive rights — Co-ownership — Burden of proof — District Court judgment — Court of Appeal judgment
Summary: Facts Paradeniyalage Gunapala, the substituted plaintiff-appellant, initiated a partition action regarding a land called Weliyaddehena. The plaintiff claimed a 41/120th share of the land based on a pedigree involving four original owners. The 5th defendant-respondent contested this, asserting that the land originated from only two owners and that he held full title to the entire corpus. The parties agreed to submit their documentary evidence without calling witnesses. The plaintiff relied on deeds P1 and P2, while the 5th defendant produced a series of deeds starting from 1891. The District Court judge analyzed the competing pedigrees and concluded that the plaintiff failed to prove his entitlement, while the 5th defendant demonstrated a clear chain of title and long-standing possession. The Court of Appeal affirmed this judgment, leading to the present appeal before the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Court of Appeal erred in law by failing to consider that the District Court judge did not provide reasons for rejecting the deeds representing the plaintiff's pedigree. Whether the District Court judge erred in law in her consideration of prescriptive rights that had devolved. Applicable Law Statutes: Not specified Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Rejection of Plaintiff's Deeds Rule applied: The court applied the principle that a judge is required to evaluate the evidence presented, including documentary evidence, to determine if a party has met the burden of proof regarding their claimed pedigree. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court reviewed the District Court judgment and found that the judge explicitly mentioned and analyzed the plaintiff's deeds, P1 and P2. The court noted that the plaintiff had admitted the validity of the defendant's documents (5D2 to 5D5), which predated the plaintiff's deeds and established a conflicting, well-documented chain of title starting from 1891. The District Court judgeâ€™s conclusion that the plaintiffâ€™s pedigree was not proven was based on a comparative analysis of these documents. The Supreme Court held that the mere fact that the judge did not use specific language "rejecting" the deeds does not mean they were not considered. Finding: The Court of Appeal did not err; the District Court judge sufficiently considered the plaintiff's documents before concluding they were insufficient to prove the claimed entitlement. Issue 2: Consideration of Prescriptive Rights Rule applied: The court applied the principle that while a co-owner cannot generally claim prescriptive rights against another co-owner, this rule does not apply where the claimant fails to prove their status as a co-owner. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that the plaintiff failed to establish any legal title to the land through his evidence or documents. Because the plaintiff failed to prove he was a co-owner, the prohibition against claiming prescriptive rights between co-owners was inapplicable. The District Court judgeâ€™s reference to the defendantâ€™s prescriptive rights was an additional finding, secondary to the primary finding that the plaintiff had no title to the corpus. Finding: The District Court judge did not err in her consideration of prescriptive rights, as the plaintiff's failure to prove title rendered the co-ownership rules irrelevant. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are mutually reinforcing. The resolution of the first issue—that the plaintiff failed to prove his pedigree—directly informs the second issue. By failing to establish title, the plaintiff lost the legal standing to challenge the defendant's possession or to invoke the protections afforded to co-owners, thereby rendering the defendant's prescriptive claim a valid secondary basis for the judgment. Conclusion The Supreme Court answered the questions of law in the negative and held in favor of the 5th defendant-respondent. The appeal is dismissed, and the judgments of the Court of Appeal and the District Court are affirmed. No costs are ordered. Keywords Partition action — Pedigree of ownership — Deeds as evidence — Prescriptive rights — Co-ownership — Burden of proof — District Court judgment — Court of Appeal judgment

Parties: Udawatta v. National Water Supply & Drainage Board and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Rohini Marasinghe, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Administrative action — Disciplinary action — Retirement from service — Negligence — Fraud — Superior officer's direction — Equitable jurisdiction
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, B.P. Udawatta, served as the Chief Accountant of the Finance Division of the National Water Supply and Drainage Board (NWSDB). In 1997, the Petitioner, acting under the instructions of his immediate superior, the 10th Respondent, deposited a cheque intended for the Colombo Municipal Council into a different account. This transaction was later identified as fraudulent. Following an investigation in 2009, the Petitioner was interdicted and subjected to a disciplinary inquiry. The inquiring officer found the Petitioner guilty of the charges. However, the Disciplinary Committee of the Board, after reviewing the report, recommended that the Petitioner be reinstated with back wages. Subsequently, the Board of Directors decided on 16.06.2011 to reinstate the Petitioner and simultaneously retire him from service effective that date. The Petitioner appealed this decision to the Secretary to the Ministry of Water Supply and Drainage. The Secretary, upon review, decided to retire the Petitioner effective from the date of his interdiction, 08.06.2009, thereby depriving him of back wages for the intervening period. The Petitioner filed this fundamental rights application challenging the decision to retire him and the subsequent modification of his retirement date. Issues Whether the decision to retire the Petitioner from service violated his fundamental right to equality under Article 12 of the Constitution. Whether the decision of the Secretary to the Ministry to backdate the Petitioner's retirement to the date of his interdiction was legally justifiable and equitable. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12 Industrial Disputes Act, Section 31B Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Retirement from service Rule applied: Article 12 of the Constitution (Right to Equality). Court's reasoning: The Court examined the evidence regarding the Petitioner's role in the fraudulent transaction. While the Petitioner argued he acted under the instructions of his superior, the 10th Respondent, the Court noted that as Chief Accountant, the Petitioner held a duty to verify the accuracy of financial transactions. The Court found that the Petitionerâ€™s negligence in the discharge of his duties justified the Board's decision to terminate his employment. Finding: The decision to retire the Petitioner was not arbitrary or irrational, and therefore did not violate Article 12 of the Constitution. Issue 2: Backdating of retirement Rule applied: Equitable jurisdiction of the Supreme Court. Court's reasoning: The Board of Directors had initially decided to retire the Petitioner effective 16.06.2011. The Secretary to the Ministry subsequently modified this to 08.06.2009, the date of interdiction. The Court reasoned that while the Petitioner was negligent, the Board's original decision was more equitable. Drawing an analogy to the powers of a Labour Tribunal under Section 31B of the Industrial Disputes Act, the Court determined it had the authority to make a just and equitable order to rectify the prejudice caused by the Secretary's modification. Finding: The Secretary's decision to backdate the retirement was inequitable; the Board's original decision to retire the Petitioner effective 16.06.2011 was the appropriate outcome. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected as the Court upheld the substantive decision to terminate the Petitioner's employment due to negligence (Issue 1) but exercised its equitable jurisdiction to mitigate the financial prejudice caused by the administrative modification of the retirement date (Issue 2). The resolution of the second issue effectively restored the Petitioner to the position intended by the Board of Directors, balancing the finding of guilt with the principles of fairness. Conclusion The application is dismissed, subject to the direction that the 1st Respondent must give effect to the Board of Directors' decision to retire the Petitioner effective 16.06.2011. The Petitioner is entitled to arrears of salary up to that date. No costs are ordered. Keywords Fundamental rights — Administrative action — Disciplinary action — Retirement from service — Negligence — Fraud — Superior officer's direction — Equitable jurisdiction

Parties: Yuni Motors (Pvt.) Ltd. and Yasasiri Kasturiarachchi v. Jayatillake and Others
Bench: Eva Wanasundera, PC.J; Buwaneka Aluwihare, PC.J; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Appeal from Court of Appeal — Excise (Special Provisions) Act No. 13 — HS Code classification — Motor vehicle excise duty — Locally manufactured vehicles — Harmonized Commodity Description and Coding System — Interpretation of statutes — GATT obligations
Summary: Facts The first Petitioner-Appellant is a company incorporated in Sri Lanka engaged in the business of assembling new motor cars from imported parts. The second Petitioner-Appellant is the Chairman of the company. The first Respondent, the Director General of Excise, directed the first Petitioner-Appellant to register under Section 14 of the Excise (Special Provisions) Act No. 13 of 1989 and pay excise duty on the vehicles produced. The Appellants contended that they were not liable for such registration or duty, arguing that their newly manufactured cars did not fall under the description provided in the relevant Gazette notifications issued by the Minister of Finance under Section 3 of the Act. The Appellants initially sought relief from the Court of Appeal to quash the decisions of the Respondents. Although the Court of Appeal granted interim relief in 2004, it ultimately dismissed the application in 2006. The Appellants subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court, challenging the interpretation of the HS Codes and the applicability of the excise duty to locally manufactured new vehicles. Issues Whether the Court of Appeal erred in failing to apply the principle that tax provisions must be strictly construed in favor of the person against whom the tax is directed. Whether the description under HS Code 8703.32.12, specifically the phrase "not more than three years old," excludes newly manufactured cars from the scope of excise duty. Whether the Minister of Finance is empowered under Section 3 of the Excise (Special Provisions) Act No. 13 of 1989 to distinguish between imported and locally manufactured vehicles when specifying excise duty rates. Applicable Law Statutes: Excise (Special Provisions) Act No. 13 of 1989 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Strict Construction of Tax Provisions Rule applied: The court applied the principle of statutory interpretation regarding tax legislation, noting that subordinate legislation must be interpreted in a manner consistent with the general purpose of the enabling Act to avoid anomalies. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that while tax laws are construed strictly, they must also be interpreted to give effect to the legislative intent of the parent statute. An interpretation that excludes new cars from excise duty would create an anomaly, as the Act mandates that all excisable articles, whether imported or manufactured, are subject to duty. Finding: The court found that the interpretation of the subordinate legislation was consistent with the Act and did not violate the principle of strict construction. Issue 2: Scope of HS Code 8703.32.12 Rule applied: The court interpreted the phrase "not more than three years old" within the context of the Harmonized Commodity Description and Coding System. Court's reasoning: The court determined that a newly manufactured car is, by definition, less than three years old. It rejected the Appellants' argument that "new" cars fall outside the classification, noting that the excise duty applies to all cars exceeding 2000cc regardless of whether they are imported or locally manufactured. The court observed that the Director General of Excise had consistently levied duty on such items since 2000. Finding: The court found that newly manufactured cars fall within the category of "not more than three years old" and are therefore subject to the specified excise duty. Issue 3: Minister's Power under Section 3 Rule applied: Section 3 of the Excise (Special Provisions) Act No. 13 of 1989 and Article III of the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT). Court's reasoning: The court held that the Ministerâ€™s power is limited to specifying the article and the rate of duty. The Minister cannot discriminate between imported and locally manufactured products, as this would violate Sri Lanka's obligations under GATT 1947. Consequently, the duty applies uniformly to both categories. Finding: The court found that the Minister acted within the scope of the Act and that the duty applies to locally manufactured vehicles by operation of law. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing: the courtâ€™s interpretation of the HS Code as covering new cars (Issue 2) aligns with the statutory requirement that the Minister cannot discriminate between domestic and imported goods (Issue 3). This consistency ensures that the tax regime remains non-discriminatory and avoids the legal anomalies that would arise from the Appellants' proposed interpretation (Issue 1). Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal and affirmed the judgment of the Court of Appeal. The Appellants are liable to register under the Excise (Special Provisions) Act and pay the applicable excise duty. Keywords Appeal from Court of Appeal — Excise (Special Provisions) Act No. 13 — HS Code classification — Motor vehicle excise duty — Locally manufactured vehicles — Harmonized Commodity Description and Coding System — Interpretation of statutes — GATT obligations

Parties: Dissanayake Ratnasiri v. Sri Lanka Ports Authority
Bench: Saleem Marsoof PC, J; Rohini Marasinghe, J.; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Industrial Disputes Act — Sri Lanka Ports Authority Act — Labour Tribunal jurisdiction — Notice requirement — Section 31B — Section 54 — Maintainability of application — Action in law — Civil Procedure Code — Just and equitable orders
Summary: Facts The Applicant-Appellant, Dissanayake Gamini Ratnasiri, was an employee of the Sri Lanka Ports Authority. Following the termination of his services, the Applicant-Appellant filed an application in the Labour Tribunal under Section 31B of the Industrial Disputes Act seeking reinstatement and back wages. The Respondent, Sri Lanka Ports Authority, raised a preliminary objection asserting that the application was not maintainable because the Applicant-Appellant failed to provide one month's notice as required by Section 54 of the Sri Lanka Ports Authority Act No. 51. The President of the Labour Tribunal overruled this preliminary objection. The Respondent subsequently filed a revision application in the High Court. The High Court set aside the order of the Labour Tribunal, upheld the preliminary objection, and dismissed the application. The Applicant-Appellant appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether an application filed in the Labour Tribunal under Section 31B of the Industrial Disputes Act constitutes an "action" within the meaning of Section 54 of the Sri Lanka Ports Authority Act No. 51. Whether a Labour Tribunal is precluded from entertaining an application under Section 31B of the Industrial Disputes Act if the applicant fails to provide the one month's notice required by Section 54 of the Sri Lanka Ports Authority Act. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act Sri Lanka Ports Authority Act No. 51 Civil Procedure Code Cases Referred to: Arnolda v. Gopalan - [64-nlr-153] P Welis v. Sri Lanka Ports Authority - [sc-spl-la-230/2009] RP Nandasiri v. Sri Lanka Ports Authority - [sc-spl-la-92/2012] Analysis Issue 1: Definition of "action" Rule applied: Section 54 of the Sri Lanka Ports Authority Act No. 51 and Section 6 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court examined the definition of "action" in Black's Law Dictionary, which describes it as a judicial proceeding for the enforcement or protection of a right. Furthermore, Section 6 of the Civil Procedure Code defines an action as any application to a court for relief or remedy obtainable through the exercise of its power. The court reasoned that since the Applicant-Appellant sought to enforce his right to employment through the Labour Tribunal's power, the application falls within the scope of the term "action" as used in Section 54. Finding: Yes, the application filed in the Labour Tribunal constitutes an "action" under Section 54 of the Sri Lanka Ports Authority Act. Issue 2: Requirement of notice Rule applied: Section 54 of the Sri Lanka Ports Authority Act No. 51 and Section 31B of the Industrial Disputes Act. Court's reasoning: The court held that Labour Tribunals, despite their mandate to make just and equitable orders, must operate within the prevailing laws of the country and are not immune to statutory requirements enacted by Parliament. The court noted that previous Supreme Court decisions, specifically in P Welis v. Sri Lanka Ports Authority and RP Nandasiri v. Sri Lanka Ports Authority, affirmed that the notice requirement under Section 54 is mandatory for applications filed against the Sri Lanka Ports Authority. Finding: Yes, an employee must provide one month's notice to the Sri Lanka Ports Authority before filing an application in the Labour Tribunal, and failure to do so renders the application liable to be dismissed. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the determination that the Labour Tribunal application constitutes an "action" triggers the mandatory notice requirement under Section 54. Because the court concluded that the Labour Tribunal is bound by the prevailing law, the failure to satisfy the notice requirement for an "action" necessitates the dismissal of the application. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The judgment of the High Court is affirmed, and no order for costs is made. Keywords Industrial Disputes Act — Sri Lanka Ports Authority Act — Labour Tribunal jurisdiction — Notice requirement — Section 31B — Section 54 — Maintainability of application — Action in law — Civil Procedure Code — Just and equitable orders

Parties: Cooray v. Perera and Others
Bench: Marsoof, P.C., J; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Substitution of parties — Dead party in legal proceedings — Amendment of petition — Court's inherent powers — Preliminary objections — Rectification of errors — Court of Appeal's judgment — Delay in amendment — Procedure for amendment
Summary: Facts The 1st to 5th Defendant-Respondent-Petitioners filed an application for special leave to appeal against a Court of Appeal judgment dated 3rd October 2011. During the pendency of the appeal in the Court of Appeal, the 5th Defendant, Henry Leonard Perera, passed away and was duly substituted. However, the Court of Appeal judgment inadvertently included the name of the deceased 5th Defendant in its caption. Consequently, the Petitioners filed their original petition for special leave to appeal in the Supreme Court using the same caption, thereby citing the deceased party. Upon the Plaintiff-Appellant-Respondent raising preliminary objections regarding the inclusion of a deceased party and the subsequent filing of an amended petition to substitute the 5th Defendant, the Petitioners admitted the error was a bona fide mistake. The Petitioners had filed an amended petition on 23rd January 2013 to include the substituted party without obtaining prior leave of court and after a significant delay. The Plaintiff-Appellant-Respondent moved to dismiss the application, arguing that the inclusion of a deceased party rendered the petition a nullity and that the amendment process was procedurally flawed. Issues Whether the inclusion of a deceased party in the caption of the petition for special leave to appeal renders the application a nullity. Whether the court should exercise its discretion to allow an amendment to the petition to substitute a party after a significant delay and without following proper procedural requirements. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 128 Supreme Court Rules, Rule 28 (2) Cases Referred to: [sc-appeal-39/2010] Mariam Bee Bee v. Seyed Mohamed - [68-nlr-36] Bastian v. Andiris - [14-nlr-437] Ilangakoon Mudiyanselage Gnanathileke Illangakoon v. Anula Kumarihamy - [sc-hc-la-277/11] Munasinghe v. Mohomad Jabeer Nawaz Karim - [1990/v2-sri-lr] Abeysinghe v. Abeysekera - [1995/v2-sri-lr-104] Waduganathan Chettiar v. Sena Abdul Casim - [55-nlr-184] Sivapathlingam v. Sivasubramaniam - [1990/v1-sri-lr-378] Gunasena v. Bandarathileke - [2000/v1-sri-lr-293] Analysis Issue 1: Effect of citing a deceased party Rule applied: The principle of inherent power of the court to make restitution for injuries caused by its own acts, as established in Sivapathlingam v. Sivasubramaniam.
