Court's reasoning: The court acknowledged that while citing a deceased party is generally a defect, the initial error originated from the Court of Appeal's judgment, which included the deceased 5th Defendant despite his prior substitution. The Petitioners merely replicated this error. The court held that the remaining Petitioners should not be non-suited due to a mistake caused by the court's own inadvertence. Finding: The preliminary objection regarding the nullity of the petition is overruled; the application is permitted to proceed. Issue 2: Propriety of the amended petition Rule applied: The principle that an amendment to a pleading should not be allowed if it is made belatedly, without following proper procedure, and causes prejudice, as discussed in Waduganathan Chettiar v. Sena Abdul Casim. Court's reasoning: The Petitioners failed to seek leave to amend the petition, failed to provide notice to the Respondent as directed, and filed the amendment over a year after the original petition. The court determined that the proper procedure would have been to seek leave to substitute or delete the name of the deceased party before filing the amendment. Finding: The court refused to exercise its discretion to accept the amended petition and rejected it. Interaction of Issues The court's findings on these issues are distinct but complementary. While the court refused to validate the procedurally flawed amendment (Issue 2), it utilized its inherent powers to rectify the caption by deleting the name of the deceased party (Issue 1), thereby ensuring the substantive appeal could proceed without being defeated by a technicality arising from the court's own previous error. Conclusion The preliminary objection is overruled, and the amended petition is rejected. The court, exercising its inherent powers, deletes the name of the deceased 5th Defendant from the original petition, and the application is to be fixed for support. No costs are ordered. Keywords Substitution of parties — Dead party in legal proceedings — Amendment of petition — Court's inherent powers — Preliminary objections — Rectification of errors — Court of Appeal's judgment — Delay in amendment — Procedure for amendment

Parties: Peoplesâ€™ Bank v. Ariyapala
Bench: Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Sarath De Abrew, J.; Priyantha Jayawardana, PC, J
Keywords: Industrial Disputes Act — Leave to appeal — Termination of employment — Unlawful termination — Compensation — Pension rights — Labour Tribunal jurisdiction — Evaluation of evidence — Reinstatement — Back wages
Summary: Facts The Applicant, H.L. Ariyapala, was employed as a Branch Manager at the Passara Branch of the Peoples Bank. Following an audit inspection, the Applicant was interdicted on 23 November 2000 and subsequently dismissed on 23 November 2002 after a domestic inquiry found him guilty of multiple charges related to the unauthorized granting of overdrafts and loans. The Applicant challenged his termination before the Labour Tribunal, which held that the termination was unlawful and unjustified. Although the Tribunal found the Applicant guilty of the second charge, it declined to order reinstatement as the Applicant had reached retirement age, instead awarding compensation equivalent to nine months' salary. Both the Applicant and the Peoples Bank appealed the Labour Tribunal's decision to the Provincial High Court. The High Court consolidated the appeals and held that the Applicant was not guilty of any charges, ordered the payment of back wages up to the date of retirement, and declared the Applicant entitled to pension rights. The Peoples Bank subsequently appealed this judgment to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Provincial High Court erred in law by granting relief in the form of pension rights that were not specifically prayed for in the original application to the Labour Tribunal and were not supported by sufficient evidence. Whether the Provincial High Court and the Labour Tribunal erred in law in the evaluation of evidence regarding charges 2 and 3. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act (as Amended) Cases Referred to: Caledonian (Ceylon) Tea and Rubber Estates Ltd. v. J.S. Hilman - [79-nlr-421] Jayasuriya v. Sri Lanka State Plantation Corporation - [1995/v1-sri-lr-379] Peoples Bank v. Gilbert Weerasinghe - [2008-blr-133] Associated News Papers Ceylon Ltd v. National Employees Union - [71-nlr-69] Analysis Issue 1: Granting of pension rights Rule applied: Section 33(1)(e) of the Industrial Disputes Act (as Amended). Court's reasoning: The Court noted that Labour Tribunals are not bound by the rigid pleading requirements of civil courts and possess the jurisdiction to grant relief that is just and equitable, even if not explicitly pleaded in the initial application, provided the matter falls within the scope of the dispute. However, the Court found that the Applicant failed to produce the specific rules of the Pension Scheme to demonstrate that he met the necessary criteria for entitlement. While the Bank is legally and morally obliged to pay a pension if the criteria are met, the High Court erred in making a definitive order for pension rights in the absence of evidence confirming the Applicant's eligibility. Finding: The High Court's order was varied to state that the Applicant is entitled to pension rights only if he satisfies the requirements and criteria laid down in the Bank's Pension Scheme. Issue 2: Evaluation of evidence for charges 2 and 3 Rule applied: The principle that an appellate court may only interfere with a Labour Tribunal's findings of fact if the finding is perverse or unsupported by legal evidence, as established in Caledonian (Ceylon) Tea and Rubber Estates Ltd. v. J.S. Hilman. Court's reasoning: Regarding charge 2, the Court disagreed with the High Court's finding of innocence, noting that the Applicant had granted overdrafts to accounts that were not satisfactorily maintained, which constituted a violation of bank circulars regardless of whether covering approval was obtained from the Regional Manager. Regarding charge 3, the Court upheld the finding that the Applicant was not guilty, as the loans were approved by the Branch Loan Committee and no evidence existed to prohibit such loans. Finding: The Court affirmed the Labour Tribunal's finding of guilt on charge 2 and the finding of innocence on charge 3, concluding that the termination was unlawful and unjustified despite the proven misconduct on charge 2. Interaction of Issues The findings on the charges and the relief granted are interconnected. The Court's determination that the termination was unlawful—despite the Applicant's guilt on charge 2—justified the award of back wages. However, the lack of evidence regarding the specific pension scheme criteria necessitated a modification of the High Court's order, ensuring that the Applicant's entitlement to pension benefits remains contingent upon his compliance with the Bank's internal rules, rather than an unconditional judicial mandate. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed subject to the variation of the High Court's order regarding pension rights. The Applicant is entitled to pension rights only upon satisfying the requirements and criteria of the Bank's Pension Scheme. No costs are ordered. Keywords Industrial Disputes Act — Leave to appeal — Termination of employment — Unlawful termination — Compensation — Pension rights — Labour Tribunal jurisdiction — Evaluation of evidence — Reinstatement — Back wages

Parties: Shelton Upali Paul and Others v. EG Dayananda
Bench: Saleem Marsoof PC, J.; Priyasath Dep PC, J.; Sisira J De Abrew J.
Keywords: Civil Procedure Code — Ex-parte Judgment — Due Diligence — Revisionary Jurisdiction — Inquiry — Reasonable Grounds — Default — Judicial Proceedings — Discretionary Power — Execution of Writ — High Court — District Court — Legal Representation — Appeal — Petition
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal against a judgment delivered by the Civil Appellate High Court of Mt. Lavinia concerning a dispute between the Plaintiff-Appellants, Shelton Upali Paul and Neelawathura Walawe Premawathi, and the Defendant-Respondent, EG Dayananda. The Plaintiff-Appellants initiated legal proceedings in the District Court of Mount Lavinia, seeking damages amounting to Rs. 3,000,000 for an alleged cause of action. The trial commenced on July 9, 2004, but faced multiple adjournments. On December 14, 2005, the Defendant-Respondent was absent, leading the court to fix the case for an ex-parte trial. However, the Defendant-Respondent's counsel later sought to explain the absence, claiming he was held up in another court. The District Judge allowed the case to be mentioned on January 27, 2006, but the Defendant-Respondent's application to vacate the ex-parte order was not addressed adequately. The case proceeded to an ex-parte trial, resulting in a judgment favoring the Plaintiff-Appellants on April 21, 2006. Following this, the Defendant-Respondent filed an application on March 8, 2007, under Section 86(2) of the Civil Procedure Code (CPC) to set aside the ex-parte judgment, claiming reasonable grounds for his default. However, the District Judge dismissed this application on April 27, 2007, without fixing it for inquiry. The Defendant-Respondent subsequently filed a revision application in the High Court, which set aside the District Judge's order on May 17, 2010, granting the relief sought by the Defendant-Respondent. The Plaintiff-Appellants then appealed to the Supreme Court, leading to the present judgment. Issues The Supreme Court was tasked with addressing several legal questions: 1. Whether the party making an application under Section 86(2) of the CPC must demonstrate due diligence and reasonable grounds for the default. 2. Whether the court is obliged to grant another date for supporting a petition filed under Section 86(2) of the CPC. 3. Whether the court can hold an inquiry under Section 86(2) of the CPC after the execution of a writ. 4. Whether the court can exercise discretionary power under Section 839 of the CPC when a specific section governs the question involved. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code (CPC) Cases Referred to: None explicitly cited in the judgment. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the party making an application under Section 86(2) of the CPC must demonstrate due diligence and reasonable grounds for the default. Rule applied: Section 86(2) of the CPC. Court's reasoning: The court emphasized that for a defendant to satisfy the court regarding reasonable grounds for default, they must be given an opportunity to present evidence. The court noted that the District Judge failed to fix the application for inquiry, which is a critical step in the judicial process. The court stated, "It is the Judge who has the control over judicial proceedings. Therefore when an application is made under Section 86(2) of the CPC, it becomes the duty of the District Judge to fix the matter for inquiry." The court further elaborated that the interest of justice demands that the defendant be notified of the inquiry date, as the absence of such notification undermines the defendant's ability to present their case. The court concluded that the District Judge's order rejecting the application without fixing it for inquiry was incorrect and should be set aside. Finding: The court affirmed that the Defendant-Respondent was entitled to invoke the revisionary jurisdiction of the High Court due to the erroneous order of the District Judge. Issue 2: Is the court obliged to grant another date to support a petition filed in terms of Section 86(2) of the CPC? Rule applied: Section 86(2) of the CPC. Court's reasoning: The court determined that since the District Court did not provide a date for inquiry or support for the application made under Section 86(2), this question did not arise. The court reiterated that the absence of a fixed date for inquiry is a significant procedural flaw that affects the defendant's rights. Finding: The court concluded that the question was moot due to the lack of a date provided by the District Court. Issue 3: Can the court hold an inquiry in terms of Section 86(2) of the CPC after execution of writ? Rule applied: Section 86(2) of the CPC. Court's reasoning: The court clarified that if the order rejecting the application to purge default is set aside, the District Court must hold a fresh inquiry under Section 86(2). The court stated, "If the defendant satisfies court that he had reasonable grounds for such default, the District Court will have to set aside the ex-parte judgment." The court emphasized that the execution of a writ does not bar the holding of an inquiry under Section 86(2), thus allowing for the possibility of rectifying the situation post-execution. Finding: The court affirmed that an inquiry could be held even after the execution of a writ. Issue 4: Can the court exercise discretionary power in terms of Section 839 of the CPC when there is a specific section governing the question involved? Rule applied: Section 839 of the CPC. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the revisionary jurisdiction of the Higher court is exercised at its discretion. It stated, "When the lower court makes a wrong order, the Higher court, in the exercise of its revisionary jurisdiction, can set aside such order." The court concluded that the existence of a wrong order constitutes an exceptional circumstance justifying the exercise of revisionary jurisdiction. Finding: The court held that the High Court was correct in exercising its revisionary jurisdiction. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as they all stem from the procedural handling of the Defendant-Respondent's application under Section 86(2) of the CPC. The failure of the District Judge to fix the application for inquiry directly impacted the Defendant-Respondent's ability to demonstrate reasonable grounds for default, which in turn affected the court's ability to grant relief. The court's analysis highlighted the importance of procedural fairness and the necessity for judicial oversight in ensuring that parties are afforded their rights to a fair hearing. Conclusion The Supreme Court upheld the judgment of the High Court, dismissing the appeal of the Plaintiff-Appellants. The court directed the District Judge to hold an inquiry on the Defendant-Respondent's petition dated March 8, 2007, ensuring that the Defendant-Respondent is informed of the inquiry date. The court emphasized the importance of adhering to procedural requirements to uphold the principles of justice and fairness in legal proceedings.
Counsel: Athula Bandara Herath with Mrs. Shashika de Silva for the Plaintiff-Respondent-Petitioner-Appellants; JP Gamage for the Defendant-Petitioner-Respondent.

Parties: De Silva v. Lankapura Pradeshiya Sabha and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Housing and Town Improvement Ordinance — Applicability of legislation — Pradeshiya Sabha Act — Village Council areas — Town Council areas — Interpretation of statutes — Writ of mandamus — Local authority
Summary: Facts The Appellant, Saranguhewage Garvin De Silva, acting as the honorary joint secretary of a social services organization, sought a writ of mandamus in the Court of Appeal to compel the Lankapura Pradeshiya Sabha and its Chairman to demolish a building constructed by the 3rd Respondent. The Appellant contended that the building, located at Abeypura, Pulasthigama, was constructed without the necessary approval of the Chairman of the Lankapura Pradeshiya Sabha. The Appellant argued that this construction violated the Housing and Town Improvement Ordinance No. 19 and the Pradeshiya Sabhas Act No. 15. The Court of Appeal dismissed the application, finding that the Ordinance did not apply to the area in question. The Appellant subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the provisions of the Housing and Town Improvement Ordinance (as amended) apply to the entirety of a Pradeshiya Sabha area, without exception. Whether the Appellant satisfied the burden of proof required to establish that the Housing and Town Improvement Ordinance was applicable to the specific property in question. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 128(2) Housing and Town Improvement Ordinance No. 19 Pradeshiya Sabhas Act No. 15 Evidence Ordinance, Section 101 Cases Referred to: Horsey v. Caldwell - [73-clr-304] Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of the Ordinance to Pradeshiya Sabha areas Rule applied: Section 3 of the Housing and Town Improvement Ordinance No. 19, which limits the application of the Ordinance to Municipal, Urban, and Town Council areas, or areas declared by resolution of Parliament. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that the Ordinance was enacted before the existence of Pradeshiya Sabhas. While Section 221 of the Pradeshiya Sabhas Act No. 15 provides that references to Town or Village Councils in existing law shall be deemed references to a Pradeshiya Sabha, this does not automatically extend the application of the Housing and Town Improvement Ordinance to all Pradeshiya Sabha areas. The court held that the legislature maintained a distinction between former Town Council areas and former Village Council areas. Consequently, the Ordinance only applies to a Pradeshiya Sabha area if that area was formerly a Town Council area or if Parliament has passed a specific resolution extending the Ordinance to that area. Finding: The Housing and Town Improvement Ordinance does not apply to all Pradeshiya Sabha areas without exception. Issue 2: Burden of proof regarding applicability Rule applied: Section 101 of the Evidence Ordinance, which places the burden of proof on the party asserting the existence of facts upon which a legal right or liability depends. Court's reasoning: The Appellantâ€™s claim for a writ of mandamus was contingent upon proving that the Ordinance applied to the location of the building. The court noted that the Appellant failed to provide evidence that the area was formerly a Town Council area or that Parliament had passed a resolution extending the Ordinance to the area. Conversely, the Court of Appeal had previously established that the property fell within a former Village Council area, a finding the Appellant did not successfully controvert. Finding: The Appellant failed to discharge the burden of proof to establish the applicability of the Ordinance to the subject property. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are interdependent. The resolution of the first issue established the legal framework, clarifying that the Ordinance is not universally applicable to all Pradeshiya Sabhas. This legal determination necessitated the second issue, as the Appellant was required to prove that the specific location fell within the limited category of areas where the Ordinance remains in force. The failure to meet the burden of proof on the second issue rendered the Appellant's legal argument regarding the first issue moot in the context of the specific relief sought. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The Supreme Court held that the Housing and Town Improvement Ordinance does not apply to the building in question, and the application for a writ of mandamus was correctly refused by the Court of Appeal. Keywords Housing and Town Improvement Ordinance — Applicability of legislation — Pradeshiya Sabha Act — Village Council areas — Town Council areas — Interpretation of statutes — Writ of mandamus — Local authority

Parties: Flexport (Pvt) Limited and Others v. Commercial Bank of Ceylon Limited
Bench: Marsoof, PC, J.; Dep, PC, J.; Marasinghe, J.
Keywords: Civil Procedure Code — Ex parte judgment — Section 86(2) — Interpretation Ordinance — Time limits — Public holidays — Jurisdiction — Mandatory compliance — Appeal process — Legal representation
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal filed by Flexport (Pvt) Limited and two other defendants (hereinafter referred to as the 'Appellants') against the judgment of the Provincial High Court of Civil Appeal of Mt Lavinia, which upheld the decision of the District Court of Mt Lavinia. The original action was initiated by the Commercial Bank of Ceylon Limited (hereinafter referred to as the 'Respondent') on 6th August 1996 for the recovery of money based on two causes of action. The Appellants filed an answer along with a claim in reconvention, to which the Respondent replied. The case was set for trial but faced multiple postponements. On 24th June 2003, the Appellants were absent, leading to an ex parte trial, resulting in a judgment and decree against them on 1st July 2003. The decree was served on the Appellants on 11th October 2003. Subsequently, on 27th October 2003, the Appellants filed an application under section 86(2) of the Civil Procedure Code to set aside the ex parte judgment. However, the Respondent objected, arguing that the application was filed out of time, as it was not submitted within the stipulated fourteen days. The Additional District Judge dismissed the application on 17th November 2004, leading to an appeal to the Provincial High Court, which was also dismissed on 7th June 2011. The Appellants then sought leave to appeal to the Supreme Court, which was granted on specific grounds. The Supreme Court was tasked with determining whether the dismissal of the Appellants' application to set aside the ex parte order was correct, particularly in light of the time limits imposed by section 86(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Issues The primary issues before the Supreme Court were: Whether the Appellants' application to set aside the ex parte judgment was filed within the time limit prescribed by section 86(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Whether section 8(1) of the Interpretation Ordinance applies to the time limits set forth in section 86(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code — Section 86(2) — Interpretation Ordinance — Section 8(1) Cases Referred to: Ceylon Brewery Ltd. v. Jax Fernando, Proprietor, Maradana Wine Stores - [2001] 1 SLR 270; Wickremasinghe v. De Silva - [1978-79] 2 SLR 65; Silva v. Sankaran and others - [2002] 2 SLR 65; State Trading Corporation v. Dharmadasa - [1987] 2 SLR 235; Nirmala de Mel v. Seneviratne and others - [1982] 2 SLR 569; Chandrakumar v. Kirubakaran - [1989] 2 SLR 38; Selenchina v. Mohomad Marikkar - [2000] 3 SLR 100; Mendis v. Mendis - [2004] BLR 35. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Appellants' application to set aside the ex parte judgment was filed within the time limit prescribed by section 86(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Rule applied: Section 86(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the timeline of events leading to the filing of the application to set aside the ex parte judgment. The decree was served on the Appellants on 11th October 2003, and the 14th day fell on 24th October 2003, which was a public holiday. The subsequent days, 25th October (Saturday) and 26th October (Sunday), were also non-working days. The Appellants contended that they were entitled to exclude these days under section 8(1) of the Interpretation Ordinance, which states that if the last day of a time limit falls on a day when the court is closed, the act shall be considered done in due time if performed on the next working day. The Court noted that the learned Additional District Judge and the High Court did not consider the applicability of this provision, which was crucial in determining whether the application was timely. The Respondent's counsel argued that strict compliance with the time limit was mandatory and cited several cases to support this view. However, the Supreme Court emphasized that the interpretation of the time limits must not lead to injustice or prejudice against the litigants. The Court referred to previous judgments that established the principle that if the last date for filing falls on a public holiday or a day when the court is closed, the filing can be done on the next available day. The Court concluded that the interpretation of section 86(2) must be harmonized with section 8(1) of the Interpretation Ordinance to prevent undue hardship to the Appellants. Finding: The Supreme Court found that the Appellants' application was indeed filed within the prescribed time limit when considering the exclusions mandated by the Interpretation Ordinance. Issue 2: Whether section 8(1) of the Interpretation Ordinance applies to the time limits set forth in section 86(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Rule applied: Section 8(1) of the Interpretation Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The Court analyzed the language of section 8(1) of the Interpretation Ordinance, which provides that if the last day of a time limit falls on a day when the court is closed, the act shall be considered done in due time if performed on the next working day. The Court noted that the word 'within' in section 86(2) of the Civil Procedure Code must be interpreted in light of this provision. The Respondent's counsel argued that the strict interpretation of 'within' should apply, and the time limit must be adhered to without exception. However, the Supreme Court highlighted that such a rigid interpretation could lead to unjust outcomes, particularly in cases where litigants are unable to file documents due to public holidays or court closures. The Court referenced previous judgments that supported the application of section 8(1) to various provisions of the Civil Procedure Code, reinforcing the notion that the law should facilitate access to justice rather than obstruct it. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that section 8(1) of the Interpretation Ordinance does apply to section 86(2) of the Civil Procedure Code, allowing for the exclusion of public holidays and court closures in calculating the time limits. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as the determination of whether the Appellants' application was timely hinges on the applicability of section 8(1) of the Interpretation Ordinance. If the Court found that the Ordinance applies, it would directly influence the outcome of the first issue regarding the timeliness of the application. Conclusion The Supreme Court set aside the judgments of the District Court and the Provincial High Court, allowing the appeal. The Court directed the District Judge to inquire into the Appellants' application to set aside the ex parte order and to make an appropriate order thereafter. The Court ordered that no costs be awarded in this appeal. Keywords Civil Procedure Code — Ex parte judgment — Section 86(2) — Interpretation Ordinance — Time limits — Public holidays — Jurisdiction — Mandatory compliance — Appeal process — Legal representation
Counsel: H. Withanachchi with S.N. Vijithsingh for the Defendant-Appellant-Appellants. S.A. Parathalingam, PC with V. Senadhira instructed by M/s Samararatna Associates for the Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent.

Parties: Seneviratne and Others v. State Bank of India
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, J.; K. Sripavan, J.; E. Wanasundera, P.C. J
Keywords: Appeal — Jurisdiction of Commercial High Court — Guarantee Bond — Primary debtor — Secondary liability — Demand — Cause of action — Waiver of privileges — Commercial transaction
Summary: Facts The Appellants, Nihal Seneviratne and Jeevana Priyantha Seneviratne, were directors of Nihal Brothers Marketing (Pvt.) Ltd. In their personal capacity, they executed a Guarantee Bond in favor of the Respondent, State Bank of India, to secure financial and banking facilities, including trust receipt facilities, granted to the company. The Respondent subsequently issued letters of demand to the company and the Appellants regarding outstanding sums under several trust receipts. The Respondent instituted action in the Commercial High Court against the Appellants to recover a sum of Rs. 22,448,573.62. The Appellants contested the action, arguing that the court lacked jurisdiction, that the action was premature due to the absence of a claim against the principal debtor, and that the amounts claimed were not fully due. The Commercial High Court entered judgment in favor of the Respondent for the full amount claimed. The Appellants appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Commercial High Court had subject matter jurisdiction to hear the action. Whether the plaint disclosed a cause of action against the Appellants as sureties without first proceeding against the principal debtor. Whether a cause of action existed for trust receipts where no formal demand had been made prior to the institution of the action. Whether the Respondent established the liability of the principal debtor to sustain the claim against the Appellants. Applicable Law Statutes: High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996 Companies Act No. 07 of 2007 Intellectual Property Act No. 36 of 2003 Debt Recovery (Special Provisions) Act No. 2 of 1990 Cases Referred to: Phillip v. Commissioner of Inland Revenue - [1-sri-skanthas-law-reports-133] Brunswick Exports Ltd v. Hatton National Bank - [1999/v1-sri-lr-219] Cornell and Company Limited v. Mitsui and Company Limited - [2000/v1-sri-lr-57] R.M. Investment & Trading Co. Pvt. Ltd. v. Boeing Co. and Another - [1994/v1-scr-837] Comed Chemicals Ltd. v. C.N. Ramchand - [AIR 2009 SC 494] Analysis Issue 1: Subject matter jurisdiction Rule applied: Section 2(1) of the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996. Court's reasoning: The court held that the term "commercial transaction" must be construed broadly to include activities integral to banking and commerce. Relying on Brunswick Exports Ltd v. Hatton National Bank and Cornell and Company Limited v. Mitsui and Company Limited, the court determined that the guarantee bond, being a banking document executed in the course of business, fell within the scope of the Commercial High Courtâ€™s jurisdiction. Finding: The Commercial High Court had the requisite jurisdiction to hear the matter. Issue 2: Cause of action against sureties Rule applied: Clause 22 of the Guarantee Bond. Court's reasoning: The court examined the express terms of the Guarantee Bond, which explicitly stated that the Appellants renounced the benefits of beneficium ordinis sue excussionis and beneficum divisionis. The bond permitted the Respondent to sue the Appellants without first proceeding against the principal debtor. Finding: The action was maintainable against the Appellants as the specific contractual provisions waived the right to require prior recourse against the principal debtor. Issue 3: Absence of demand Rule applied: Clause 1 of the Guarantee Bond. Court's reasoning: The court found that the obligation to pay under the bond was triggered "on demand." Since no evidence was provided that a demand was made for Trust Receipts P5 and P6, the court held that no cause of action had arisen for those specific amounts. Furthermore, the court accounted for payments already made by the principal debtor. Finding: The claim was only valid for the principal sum of Rs. 12,640,000, as no demand was established for the remaining claimed amounts. Issue 4: Liability of the principal debtor Rule applied: General principles of evidence and contract. Court's reasoning: The court reviewed the bank statements and deposit slips provided by both parties. It concluded that the Respondent successfully demonstrated that the deposits relied upon by the Appellants were related to other transactions and did not discharge the liability for the trust receipts in question. Finding: The liability of the principal debtor was established to the extent of Rs. 12,640,000. Interaction of Issues The findings on the absence of demand (Issue 3) and the establishment of liability (Issue 4) directly necessitated a reduction in the quantum of the judgment. While the court upheld the jurisdiction and the right to sue the sureties directly, the failure to prove a demand for certain trust receipts limited the total recoverable amount. Conclusion The appeal is allowed in part. The judgment of the Commercial High Court is varied, and the Appellants are ordered to pay the sum of Rs. 18,052,573.62, which reflects the principal sum of Rs. 12,640,000 plus interest as of the date of the original judgment. No costs are awarded. Keywords Appeal — Jurisdiction of Commercial High Court — Guarantee Bond — Primary debtor — Secondary liability — Demand — Cause of action — Waiver of privileges — Commercial transaction

Parties: Lake House Employees Union and Others v. Associated Newspapers of Ceylon Ltd and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J; Eva Wanasundera, P.C., J; Buwaneka Aluwihare, P.C., J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Freedom of speech and expression — Freedom of association — Time bar — Continuing violation — Prior restraint — Illegality
Summary: Facts The Petitioners, the Lake House Employees Union and its office bearers, filed an application under Article 126 of the Constitution alleging violations of their fundamental rights under Articles 12(1), 14(1)(a), and 14(1)(c). The 1st Respondent, Associated Newspapers of Ceylon Ltd (ANCL), provided notice boards for trade unions to communicate with members. The management of ANCL issued notices in 2004 and 2006 requiring prior approval from the Chairman for any material displayed on these boards. The Petitioners alleged that the management forcibly removed various documents and correspondence from their assigned notice boards, including transfer letters and communications addressed to state authorities. The Petitioners contended that these removals and the requirement for prior approval constituted an arbitrary exercise of power and a violation of their freedom of speech and association. The Respondents raised preliminary objections, arguing that the application was time-barred under Article 126(2) of the Constitution and that the Petitioners could not seek relief for actions resulting from their own non-compliance with company regulations. Issues Whether the application is time-barred under Article 126(2) of the Constitution, given that the alleged acts of removal occurred outside the one-month limitation period. Whether the alleged removal of notices constitutes a "continuing violation" that would extend the time limit for filing the application. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1), 14(1)(a), 14(1)(c), 126. Cases Referred to: Samarasinghe v The Associated Newspaper of Ceylon Ltd - [2011/v1-b-l-r-46] Vasudeva Nanayakkara v Choksy - [2008/v1-sri-lr-134] National Railroad Passenger Corp. v Morgan - [536-u-s-101] Analysis Issue 1: Time Bar Rule applied: Article 126(2) of the Constitution, which requires applications for fundamental rights violations to be filed within one month of the alleged infringement. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the specific communications identified by the Petitioners were removed on the dates they were posted, with the latest incident occurring on 16 July 2009. The application was filed on 25 August 2009. Citing Samarasinghe v The Associated Newspaper of Ceylon Ltd, the court held that the Petitioners failed to invoke the court's jurisdiction within the mandatory one-month period from the date of the alleged infringements. Finding: The application is time-barred. Issue 2: Continuing Violation Rule applied: The doctrine of "continuing violation" as discussed in National Railroad Passenger Corp. v Morgan. Court's reasoning: The court distinguished between discrete acts of discrimination and a series of acts constituting a continuing violation. Adopting the reasoning in Morgan, the court held that even if the removals were viewed as a series of acts, the cause of action accrues on the date of the last component act. Since the last act occurred on 16 July 2009, the one-month period expired before the filing on 25 August 2009. Finding: The doctrine of continuing violation does not save the application from being time-barred. Interaction of Issues The findings on the time bar and the continuing violation doctrine are dispositive. Because the court determined that the application was filed outside the statutory time limit—even under the most favorable interpretation of the "continuing violation" theory—the substantive merits of the fundamental rights claims and the second preliminary objection regarding illegality became moot and did not require adjudication. Conclusion The application is dismissed as it is time-barred under Article 126(2) of the Constitution. No costs are ordered. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Freedom of speech and expression — Freedom of association — Time bar — Continuing violation — Prior restraint — Illegality

Parties: Rajapaksha v. Temawathie and Nona
Bench: Hon. Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.; Sarath De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Leave to appeal — Succession to land — Land Development Ordinance — Rei vindicatio action — Possessory action — Prescription Ordinance — Prevention of Frauds Ordinance — State land — Inheritance — Fraudulent documents — Restoration of possession
Summary: Facts Wanniarachchi Kankanamlage Babun Appuhamy was the father of the two Respondents, Temawathie and Julie Nona. Babun Appuhamy was allotted a 40-perch land in Kataragama by the government in lieu of land acquired for the Kataragama Sacred City Project. Although no formal permit was issued under the Land Development Ordinance, he was put into occupation of the land. Babun Appuhamy died in 1977, and his wife subsequently possessed the land until her death in 1989. The Respondents continued to possess the land until they were evicted on 27 November 1996 pursuant to an order obtained by the Appellant in the Primary Court of Tissamaharama. The Respondents filed an action in the District Court of Tissamaharama seeking a declaration of title, ejectment of the Appellant, and damages. The Appellant claimed the land based on documents marked me1 and js1, which purported to transfer the land from Babun Appuhamy to the Appellant. The District Court granted relief to the Respondents, and the Civil Appellate High Court of the Southern Province affirmed this decision. The Appellant appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Respondents inherited the title of Babun Appuhamy under the Land Development Ordinance No. 19 of 1935 despite the absence of a formal permit. Whether the action should be characterized as a rei vindicatio action or a possessory action. Whether the Respondents' action was time-barred under Section 4 of the Prescription Ordinance No. 22 of 1871. Whether the lower courts erred in disregarding the evidence of the witness who prepared and signed document js1. Applicable Law Statutes: Land Acquisition Act No. 9 of 1950 Primary Courts Procedure Act No. 44 of 1979 Land Development Ordinance No. 19 of 1935 Prevention of Frauds Ordinance No. 7 of 1840 Prescription Ordinance No. 22 of 1871 Civil Procedure Code Cases Referred to: Palisena v. Perera - [56-nlr-407] Weragama v. Bandara - [77-nlr-289] Vangadasalem v. Chettiyar - [29-nlr-446] Danapala v. Babynona - [77-nlr-95] Latheef v. Mansoor - [2011/v1-sri-lr-189] Allis Appu v. Edris Hamy - [3-scr-87] Pathirana v. Jayasundara - [58-nlr-169] Mudalihamy v. Appuhamy - [clrep-67] Rawter v. Ross - [3-scc-145] Analysis Issue 1: Succession to State Land Rule applied: Section 170(1), Section 72, and the Third Schedule of the Land Development Ordinance No. 19 of 1935. Court's reasoning: The court noted that succession to State land alienated under the Ordinance is governed exclusively by the Ordinance. While the Respondents could not prove they were the sole heirs under the Third Schedule (which prioritizes sons), the court found that the Respondents' possession was founded on their status as heirs of a permit-holder. The court held that the lower courts erred in declaring the Respondents as the owners, but acknowledged the Respondents' right to seek a declaration regarding the rights of their father. Finding: The Respondents did not strictly inherit title under the Third Schedule, but the court recognized their interest as heirs of the original occupant. Issue 2: Nature of the Action Rule applied: Principles of rei vindicatio and restitutio in integrum. Court's reasoning: The court clarified that while the action was not a rei vindicatio in the strict sense, it was an analogous action for declaration of title and ejectment. The court found that the Appellant's possession was based on fraudulent documents (me1 and js1) that violated the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance. The court exercised its power of restitutio in integrum to set aside the Primary Court order obtained through fraud. Finding: The action is an analogous rei vindicatio action where a presumption of title arises in favor of the Respondents due to their prior possession and the Appellant's lack of valid title. Issue 3: Prescription Rule applied: Section 4 of the Prescription Ordinance No. 22 of 1871. Court's reasoning: The Appellant argued the action was a possessory action subject to the one-year limitation period under Section 4. The court rejected this, holding that the action was a declaration of title and not a mere possessory action. Finding: The action was not prescribed. Issue 4: Evidence of Witness Rule applied: Section 2 of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance No. 7 of 1840. Court's reasoning: The court found that document js1 was legally void for failing to comply with the attestation requirements of the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance. Consequently, the testimony of the witness who prepared it was irrelevant to establishing the Appellant's title. Finding: The lower courts did not err in disregarding the witness's evidence. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected: the determination that the action was an analogous rei vindicatio (Issue 2) rendered the prescription argument (Issue 3) inapplicable. Furthermore, the invalidity of the Appellant's documents under the Prevention of Frauds Ordinance (Issue 4) reinforced the court's decision to grant the Respondents relief, as the Appellant failed to rebut the presumption of title arising from the Respondents' prior possession. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed, and the judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court is varied to reflect that the Respondents are entitled to the relief prayed for in the plaint as heirs of the original occupant. The Respondents are awarded costs in the sum of Rs. 50,000. Keywords Leave to appeal — Succession to land — Land Development Ordinance — Rei vindicatio action — Possessory action — Prescription Ordinance — Prevention of Frauds Ordinance — State land — Inheritance — Fraudulent documents — Restoration of possession

Parties: Fernando v. de Silva and de Silva
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C., J; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.
Keywords: Rent Act — Excepted premises — Annual value assessment — Business premises — Anuradhapura Urban Council — Tenancy — Illegal tenancy — Burden of proof
Summary: Facts The original Plaintiff instituted an action in the District Court of Anuradhapura in 1982 seeking the ejectment of the Defendants from the premises in suit, which were described as a guest house and hotel. The Plaintiff alleged that the premises were excepted premises under the Rent Act and that the tenancy had been terminated by a notice to quit. The Defendants contended that they had occupied the property since 1965 and that the rent had been paid without default. During the pendency of the trial, the original Plaintiff passed away, and Kaduge Mary Margaret Fernando was substituted as the Plaintiff. The District Court concluded that the premises were excepted premises because the annual value exceeded the threshold stipulated in the Rent Act. Consequently, the District Court granted the relief sought for ejectment and damages. The Defendants appealed to the Civil Appellate High Court, which set aside the District Court judgment. The Civil Appellate High Court held that the tenancy was illegal due to non-compliance with conditions under the Anuradhapura Preservation Board Act No. 32 and that the Plaintiff failed to prove the premises were excepted. The Substituted Plaintiff then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court erred in holding that the Appellant failed to discharge the burden of proving that the premises in suit were excepted premises under the Rent Act No. 7 of 1972. Whether the Appellant sufficiently proved that the annual value of the premises exceeded the threshold for excepted premises, thereby excluding the application of the Rent Act. Applicable Law Statutes: Rent Act No. 7 of 1972 Rent (Amendment) Act No. 10 of 1977 Rent (Amendment) Act No. 55 of 1980 Anuradhapura Preservation Board Act No. 32 of 1961 Civil Procedure Code Cases Referred to: Gunapala v. Babynona - [1986/v2-sri-lr-374] Silva v. Ranaweera - [2006/v1-blr-95] Malwattage v. Dharmawardena - [1991/v2-sri-lr-141] Amarasekara v. Abeygunawardena - [56-nlr-361] Analysis Issue 1: Burden of proof regarding excepted premises Rule applied: Section 2(4)(a) and Section 2(5) of the Rent Act No. 7 of 1972, read with Regulation 3 made under Section 43. Court's reasoning: The Court examined whether the premises qualified as "excepted premises," which are excluded from the protection of the Rent Act. The Court noted that the burden of proof lies on the party asserting the status of the premises. The Court found that the Appellant provided documentary evidence (P2 and P2a) and oral testimony from a Revenue Inspector to establish the assessment history. The Court rejected the High Court's finding that the Appellant failed to discharge this burden, noting that the evidence clearly demonstrated the annual value exceeded the statutory threshold of Rs. 2,000.00. Finding: The High Court erred in its assessment of the burden of proof; the Appellant sufficiently established the premises were excepted. Issue 2: Determination of annual value Rule applied: Regulation 3 of the Rent Act Regulations. Court's reasoning: The Court analyzed the assessment records (me.2 and js10) provided by both parties. The documents showed that the premises, whether identified under assessment number 42 or 48, consistently carried an annual value exceeding the Rs. 2,000.00 threshold required for excepted status. The Court observed that even the Defendants' own evidence confirmed that the annual value was well above the limit. The Court further noted that the conduct of the parties, including payments made to the local authority, supported the conclusion that the premises were treated as excepted. Finding: The premises in suit are excepted premises within the meaning of the Rent Act. Interaction of Issues The resolution of the status of the premises as "excepted" rendered the Rent Act inapplicable to the tenancy. This finding effectively nullified the High Court's reliance on the alleged illegality of the tenancy under the Anuradhapura Preservation Board Act, as the primary dispute regarding the right to ejectment was governed by the contractual terms rather than the Rent Act's protections. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, and the judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court is set aside. The judgment of the District Court is affirmed, granting the Appellant the relief of ejectment and damages, excluding the claim for damages related to the alleged physical loss to the property. Keywords Rent Act — Excepted premises — Annual value assessment — Business premises — Anuradhapura Urban Council — Tenancy — Illegal tenancy — Burden of proof

Parties: Liyanage v. Commissioner of Elections
Bench: K. Sripavan, J.; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.; Sarath De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Recognition of political party — Parliamentary Election Act — Commissioner of Elections — Writ jurisdiction — Ouster clause — Judicial review — Reasons for decision — Natural justice — Fundamental rights
Summary: Facts The Petitioner-Appellant, Jayantha Liyanage, acting as the Secretary of the Sinhala Jathika Peramuna, submitted multiple applications to the Commissioner of Elections seeking recognition of his party as a political party under the Parliamentary Election Act No. 1. The Respondent-Respondent, the Commissioner of Elections, rejected these applications in 2000, 2006, and 2008. Following the final rejection in 2008, the Petitioner filed a writ application in the Court of Appeal seeking a writ of certiorari to quash the decision and a writ of mandamus to compel the Respondent to recognize the party and assign it a symbol. The Court of Appeal dismissed the petition, holding that the decision of the Commissioner was final and not subject to judicial review due to an ouster clause in the Act. The Petitioner subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Court of Appeal erred in holding that the finality clause in Section 9(7) of the Parliamentary Election Act precluded judicial review of the Commissioner's decision. Whether the Commissioner of Elections' decision to reject the Petitioner's application for recognition as a political party was justified based on the material available. Applicable Law Statutes: Parliamentary Election Act No. 1 Interpretation Ordinance Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka Cases Referred to: B Sirisena Coory v. Tissa Dias Bandaranayake - [1999/v1-sri-lr-1] Seimans Engineering v. Union of India - [A I R (1976) S.C. 1785] Analysis Issue 1: Finality Clause and Judicial Review Rule applied: Article 140 of the Constitution of the Republic of Sri Lanka and Section 22 of the Interpretation Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the writ jurisdiction of the Superior Courts is derived from the Constitution, which is the supreme law of the country. Consequently, ordinary legislation cannot supersede this constitutional power or oust the jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal. The Court relied on the precedent in B Sirisena Coory v. Tissa Dias Bandaranayake, which established that the writ jurisdiction of superior courts is unfettered by ouster clauses in ordinary statutes. Finding: The Court of Appeal erred in its finding; the ouster clause in the Parliamentary Election Act does not prevent the Court of Appeal from exercising its writ jurisdiction to review the Commissioner's decision. Issue 2: Justification of the Commissioner's Decision Rule applied: Section 7(5) of the Parliamentary Election Act No. 1. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the grounds provided by the Commissioner for rejection. It found the requirement that a party must be a "breakaway group" to be legally baseless and unacceptable. Furthermore, the Court noted that the Petitioner and his party members had previously participated in elections, satisfying the criteria that the party is a political entity organized to contest elections. The Court concluded that the Respondent had sufficient material to grant recognition and that the Commissioner's refusal was based on arbitrary and unreasonable grounds. Finding: The Commissioner's decision was wrong and lacked a valid legal basis; therefore, it must be quashed. Interaction of Issues The resolution of the first issue regarding the Court's jurisdiction was a necessary prerequisite to addressing the merits of the second issue. By establishing that the ouster clause did not bar judicial review, the Court was empowered to examine the substantive grounds of the Commissioner's decision, ultimately finding them to be arbitrary and unsupported by the evidence. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, the judgment of the Court of Appeal is set aside, and the decision of the Commissioner of Elections dated 21.1.2008 is quashed. A writ of mandamus is issued directing the Commissioner of Elections to recognize the Sinhala Jathika Peramuna as a political party and to assign it an appropriate symbol. Keywords Recognition of political party — Parliamentary Election Act — Commissioner of Elections — Writ jurisdiction — Ouster clause — Judicial review — Reasons for decision — Natural justice — Fundamental rights

Parties: Issadeen v. Karunaratne and Others
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC, J; Rohini Marasinghe, J.; Priyantha Jayawardena, PC, J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Political victimization — Cabinet decisions — Establishment Code — Public Service Commission — Seniority — Appointment dates — Promotions — Deputy Superintendent of Customs
Summary: Facts The Petitioner applied for the post of Assistant Superintendent of Customs Class II following a Gazette notification and successfully passed a competitive examination held on 22nd August 1992. Despite performing well at a subsequent interview, he was not selected for the appointment. The Petitioner later complained to a Political Victimization Committee, which found that he had secured the 7th position in the order of merit but was overlooked in favor of candidates with lower marks. The Committee recommended his appointment, and the Cabinet of Ministers approved it, granting him an appointment effective from 31.08.1992. The Petitioner was subsequently issued a letter of appointment dated 21.07.1999, which reflected the Cabinet-approved date of 31.08.1992. However, the Department of Customs refused to recognize this seniority for promotional purposes, citing Section 1.9 of the Establishment Code, which ties the effective date of appointment to the date of assuming duties. Because the Petitioner assumed duties on 15.06.1998, the Department treated him as junior to the 1993 batch of recruits. Consequently, the Petitioner was excluded from interviews for the post of Deputy Superintendent of Customs. Although he was later called before an interview board, the board treated his appearance as a clarification of his appeal rather than a formal assessment for promotion. The Petitioner then filed this application alleging a violation of his fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Issues Whether the failure of the Department of Customs to recognize the Petitionerâ€™s seniority based on the Cabinet-approved appointment date violated his fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the Department of Customs was entitled to rely on Section 1.9 of the Establishment Code to override a Cabinet decision intended to rectify political victimization. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1), Article 55, Article 57. Establishment Code (Volume I), Section 1.9, Section 1.10, Section 5.3.1, Section 5.3.2. Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution, which guarantees equality before the law and equal protection of the law. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that the Petitioner was denied equal protection of the law when he was overlooked for the initial appointment despite his merit-based ranking. The subsequent refusal by the Department of Customs to honor the Cabinet-approved seniority date further perpetuated this inequality. The court emphasized that administrative processes must be conducted fairly and that authorities are obligated to rectify errors that cause undue harm or prejudice to public servants. Finding: The Petitionerâ€™s fundamental rights under Article 12(1) were violated by the denial of his seniority and the resulting exclusion from promotional opportunities. Issue 2: Applicability of the Establishment Code Rule applied: Section 1.9 of the Establishment Code and the constitutional powers of the Cabinet of Ministers under Article 55. Court's reasoning: The court held that while the Establishment Code provides general rules for recruitment and promotion, the Cabinet of Ministers possesses the supreme authority to grant redress to public servants who have suffered injustice. The court determined that the specific condition in the Petitionerâ€™s letter of appointment, which incorporated the Cabinetâ€™s decision to ante-date his appointment, superseded the general provisions of Section 1.9 of the Establishment Code. The Department of Customs lacked the authority to unilaterally alter or ignore a Cabinet-mandated rectification. Finding: The Department of Customs could not rely on Section 1.9 of the Establishment Code to override the Cabinetâ€™s decision to grant the Petitioner seniority. Interaction of Issues The findings are intrinsically linked; the violation of Article 12(1) was exacerbated by the Departmentâ€™s rigid application of the Establishment Code. By prioritizing a technical provision of the Code over a Cabinet-approved remedial measure, the Department effectively denied the Petitioner the equal protection of the law, thereby confirming that the administrative action was both discriminatory and ultra vires. Conclusion The court declared that the Petitionerâ€™s fundamental rights under Article 12(1) were violated. The 1st Respondent is directed to place the Petitioner at the 7th position in the 1993 batch seniority list and to conduct a fresh interview for the post of Deputy Superintendent of Customs, recognizing his effective appointment date as 15.07.1993. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Political victimization — Cabinet decisions — Establishment Code — Public Service Commission — Seniority — Appointment dates — Promotions — Deputy Superintendent of Customs

Parties: Gamini Ranasinghe v. Commercial Bank of Ceylon Limited
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C. J.; Priyasath Dep, P.C. J.; Rohini Marasinghe, J.
Keywords: Letter of Credit — discrepancies — burden of proof — UCP 400 — set-off — wrongful payment — commercial jurisdiction — strict compliance — banking law — documentary credits — evidence — injunction — declarations — appeal
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal filed by Gamini Ranasinghe (the Appellant) against the Commercial Bank of Ceylon Limited (the Respondent) regarding the bank's actions related to a Letter of Credit (L/C) opened for the purchase of timber. The Appellant, acting on behalf of his business, Malindu Timber Stores, placed an order for timber with Zenith Corporation in Malaysia and requested the Respondent to open a Letter of Credit for the transaction. The Appellant issued a letter of set-off allowing the bank to debit his accounts for any payments made under the L/C. After the timber was shipped, the Respondent accepted the shipping documents and made payment under the L/C. The Appellant later contested the payment, claiming discrepancies in the shipping documents and a delay in their presentation. He sought a declaration that the Respondent had no right to debit his account and requested an injunction against such actions. The Commercial High Court dismissed the Appellant's claims, leading to this appeal. Issues The primary issues framed for consideration were: 1. Whether the Respondent bank wrongfully made payments on the Letter of Credit despite discrepancies in the shipping documents. 2. Whether the documents were presented for negotiation within the stipulated time frame as per the terms of the Letter of Credit. 3. Whether the burden of proof regarding the timing of document presentation lay with the Appellant or the Respondent. Applicable Law Statutes: - High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No 10 of 1996 - Evidence Ordinance No 14 of 1895 Cases Referred to: - Indica Traders (Pvt) Ltd v Seoul Lanka Construction (Pvt) Ltd et al [1994] 3 Sri LR 392 - United City Merchants v Royal Bank of Canada [1982] 2 WLR 1039 - Equitable Trust Co of New York v Dawson Partners Ltd (1927) 27 Lloyd’s Law Rep 49 - Moralice (London) Ltd v ED and F Man (1954) 2 Lloyd’s Law Rep 526 - Beyene v Irving Trust Co Ltd (1985) 762 Fed Rep 2d 4 - Seaconsar Far East Ltd v Bank Markazi Jomhouri Islami Iran (1993) 1 Lloyd’s Rep 236 Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Respondent bank wrongfully made payments on the Letter of Credit despite discrepancies in the shipping documents. Rule applied: The principle of strict compliance in documentary credits as governed by UCP 400. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the discrepancies raised by the Appellant, particularly focusing on the description of goods in the Bill of Lading versus the Letter of Credit. The Appellant argued that the absence of a multiplication mark in the description constituted a material discrepancy. The Court noted that the description in the Bill of Lading was ambiguous and that the discrepancies were substantial enough to warrant rejection of the documents by the bank. The Court emphasized that strict compliance is essential in banking transactions involving Letters of Credit, as banks are not in a position to interpret the commercial intent behind the documents. The Court also referenced the case of Indica Traders, asserting that a bank must pay according to the terms of the L/C unless there is clear fraud. Finding: The Court concluded that the Respondent should have rejected the documents due to the material discrepancies, thus the payment made was wrongful. Issue 2: Whether the documents were presented for negotiation within the stipulated time frame. Rule applied: UCP 400 Articles 46 and 48 regarding the timing of document presentation. Court's reasoning: The Court highlighted the importance of adhering to the stipulated time frame for document presentation as outlined in the Letter of Credit. The Appellant alleged that the documents were presented after the expiry date, but the Respondent failed to provide clear evidence regarding the timing of the presentation. The Court noted that the burden of proof regarding the timing of presentation lay with the Respondent, as the details were within their exclusive knowledge. The lack of evidence from the Respondent led the Court to draw a presumption against them, concluding that the documents were likely presented late. Finding: The Court determined that the Respondent had no right to set off the amount paid under the L/C from the Appellant's account due to the late presentation of documents. Interaction of Issues The issues of discrepancies in the shipping documents and the timing of their presentation are interrelated, as both pertain to the Respondent's obligations under the Letter of Credit. The failure to present compliant documents within the stipulated time frame directly impacts the validity of the payment made by the Respondent. The Court's findings on both issues ultimately led to the conclusion that the Respondent acted wrongfully in debiting the Appellant's account. Conclusion The Supreme Court set aside the judgment of the Commercial High Court, ruling in favor of the Appellant. The Court held that the Respondent had no right to set off the amount paid under the Letter of Credit from the Appellant's account and ordered the release of the funds along with interest. The Appellant was awarded costs for the appeal. The judgment underscored the importance of strict compliance in banking transactions and clarified the burden of proof in cases involving documentary credits. Keywords Letter of Credit — discrepancies — burden of proof — UCP 400 — set-off — wrongful payment — commercial jurisdiction — strict compliance — banking law — documentary credits — evidence — injunction — declarations — appeal
Counsel: G. Alagaratnam, P.C. with Suren Fernando and Mrs. M.A.S.J. Nayeem for Plaintiff-Appellant. S.A. Parathalingam, P.C. with N. Parathalingam for Defendant-Respondent.

Parties: Nihal Seneviratne and Jeevana Priyantha Seneviratne v. State Bank of India
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, P.C. J.; K. Sripavan J; E. Wanasundera P.C. J.
Keywords: Guarantee Bond — Commercial High Court — Trust Receipt Facility — Personal Guarantee — Jurisdiction — Cause of Action — Secondary Liability — Demand Letters — Banking Transactions — Interest Rate — Principal Debtor — Legal Costs — Appeal — Judgment Variation
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal filed by the Appellants, Nihal Seneviratne and Jeevana Priyantha Seneviratne, who are directors of Nihal Brothers Marketing (Pvt.) Ltd, against a judgment delivered by the Commercial High Court of the Western Province. The Respondent, State Bank of India, had granted financial facilities to the Appellants' company for importing sugar, secured by a Guarantee Bond executed by the Appellants. The company obtained advances against several Trust Receipts, totaling Rs. 17,221,000. Following a demand for repayment, the Respondent initiated legal action against the Appellants, resulting in a judgment that ordered the Appellants to pay Rs. 22,448,573.62, including interest. The Appellants contended that the Commercial High Court erred in its judgment, claiming that the court lacked jurisdiction, failed to establish a cause of action, and did not properly consider the liability of the principal debtor. Issues The primary issues raised in this appeal are: Whether the Commercial High Court had jurisdiction to entertain the action instituted by the Respondent. Whether the plaint disclosed a cause of action against the Appellants without first having recourse to the principal debtor. Whether the absence of a demand prior to the institution of action negated the cause of action against the Appellants. Whether the Respondent established the liability of the principal debtor, Nihal Brothers Marketing (Pvt.) Ltd, to sustain the claim against the Appellants. Applicable Law Statutes: High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996 — Civil Procedure Code Cases Referred to: Phillip v. Commissioner of Inland Revenue - [1 Sri Skantha’s Law Reports 133]; Brunswick Exports Ltd v. Hatton National Bank - [1999] 1 Sri LR 219; Cornell and Company Limited v. Mitsui and Company Limited - [2000] 1 Sri LR 57; R.M. Investment & Trading Co. Pvt. Ltd. v. Boeing Co. and Another - [1994] 1 SCR 837; Comed Chemicals Ltd. v. C.N Ramchand - [AIR 2009 SC 494]. Analysis Issue 1: Jurisdiction Rule applied: Section 2(1) of the High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996. Court's reasoning: The Appellants argued that the Commercial High Court lacked jurisdiction as the action was based on a personal guarantee, which they claimed was secondary to the loan transaction between the Respondent and the principal debtor. They cited the case of Phillip v. Commissioner of Inland Revenue, asserting that a director is not personally liable for the company's debts. However, the Respondent countered that the action fell within the definition of a 'commercial transaction' as per the Act, which includes banking documents. The court referenced the broad interpretation of 'commercial transaction' established in previous cases, emphasizing that the nature of the transaction was indeed commercial. The court concluded that the Commercial High Court did not err in assuming jurisdiction. Finding: The Commercial High Court had jurisdiction to hear the case. Issue 2: Cause of Action Rule applied: Legal principles regarding secondary liability and guarantees. Court's reasoning: The Appellants contended that the plaint did not disclose a cause of action as they were sued as sureties without recourse to the principal debtor. They cited legal principles stating that a guarantor cannot be held liable without first pursuing the principal debtor. The Respondent argued that the Guarantee Bond allowed them to sue the Appellants directly. The court examined the Guarantee Bond's clauses, particularly the waiver of the Appellants' rights as sureties, which indicated that they accepted liability as principal debtors. The court found that the Appellants had renounced their rights, thus establishing a cause of action against them. Finding: The plaint disclosed a valid cause of action against the Appellants. Issue 3: Absence of Demand Rule applied: Legal principles regarding the necessity of demand for establishing liability. Court's reasoning: The Appellants argued that the absence of a demand for certain Trust Receipts negated the cause of action. They highlighted that the Respondent had not established that demands were made for all Trust Receipts. The court acknowledged that while a demand is typically necessary, the Guarantee Bond's terms allowed the Respondent to proceed against the Appellants without prior demand. The court noted that the Appellants had waived their rights to demand and that the Respondent had established liability for the amounts claimed. Finding: The absence of demand did not negate the cause of action against the Appellants. Issue 4: Liability of Principal Debtor Rule applied: Evidence required to establish liability. Court's reasoning: The Appellants claimed that the Respondent failed to establish the liability of the principal debtor, Nihal Brothers Marketing (Pvt.) Ltd. They presented evidence suggesting that the company had settled its debts. The Respondent countered that the Appellants' new defense was not raised during the trial. The court found that the Appellants had not sufficiently proven their claims regarding the principal debtor's liability. The court concluded that the Respondent had established the principal amount due based on the evidence presented. Finding: The Respondent established the liability of the principal debtor to the extent of Rs. 12,640,000. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as the jurisdiction of the Commercial High Court is contingent upon the nature of the cause of action. The court's findings on the cause of action and the necessity of demand directly impact the determination of liability. The court's analysis of the Guarantee Bond's terms played a crucial role in resolving these interconnected issues. Conclusion The Supreme Court varied the judgment of the Commercial High Court, reducing the amount owed by the Appellants to Rs. 18,052,573.62, affirming the interest rate and terms of payment. The appeal was allowed in part, and no costs were awarded. The court's decision underscores the importance of understanding the implications of guarantees and the jurisdictional scope of commercial transactions in Sri Lanka. Keywords Guarantee Bond — Commercial High Court — Trust Receipt Facility — Personal Guarantee — Jurisdiction — Cause of Action — Secondary Liability — Demand Letters — Banking Transactions — Interest Rate — Principal Debtor — Legal Costs — Appeal — Judgment Variation
Counsel: S.P. Sriskantha with T. Sri Pathmanathan for the Defendants-Appellants. Avindra Rodrigo with Ananda Tikitiratne and Ms. Raneesha de Alwis for the Plaintiff-Respondent.

Parties: M. Tudor Danister Anthony Fernando v. Rankiri Hettiarachchige Fredie Perera
Bench: Saleem Marsoof, PC, J.; B. Aluwihare, PC, J.; Priyantha Jayawardene, PC, J.
Keywords: affidavit — right of way — permanent injunction — counter claim — District Court — High Court — Supreme Court Rules — Oaths and Affirmation Ordinance — validity of affidavit — legal representation — evidence — procedural law — civil procedure — judicial authority — deponent — affirmation
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a dispute concerning a right of way claimed by the Plaintiff-Respondent-Petitioner, M. Tudor Danister Anthony Fernando, against the Defendant-Appellant-Respondent, Rankiri Hettiarachchige Fredie Perera. The Plaintiff filed a plaint in the District Court of Negombo, asserting his entitlement to a right of way as detailed in the third schedule of his plaint. He sought a permanent injunction to prevent the Defendant from obstructing this right of way. In response, the Defendant filed an answer denying the Plaintiff's claims and counterclaimed for a declaration of ownership over his land, asserting that it was not subject to any right of way or servitude. The trial proceeded with both parties presenting evidence, and the District Judge initially ruled in favor of the Plaintiff. However, the Defendant appealed to the High Court of Civil Appeals in Gampaha, which overturned the District Court's decision, dismissing the Plaintiff's action. This prompted the Plaintiff to seek leave to appeal to the Supreme Court, supported by a petition and an affidavit. A preliminary objection was raised by the Defendant's counsel regarding the validity of the affidavit submitted by the Plaintiff, specifically pointing out a discrepancy between the wording of the affidavit and the jurat. Issues The primary issue presented before the Supreme Court was whether the affidavit filed by the Plaintiff was valid, given the objection raised regarding the use of the terms 'oath' and 'affirmation' within the document. Applicable Law Statutes: Supreme Court Rules of 1990; Oaths and Affirmation Ordinance No. 3 of 1842; Civil Procedure Code No. 2 of 1889. Cases Referred to: Rustomjee v. Khan - [18 NLR 120]; De Silva and Others v. L.B. Finance Ltd. - [(1993) 1 SLR 371]; Kanagasabai v. Kirupamoorthy - [62 NLR 54]; Mohamed Rauf Mohamed Facy v. Mohamed Azath Sanoon Sally - [SC minutes S.C. Appeal No. 4/2004]; Trico Freighters (Pvt) Ltd. v. Yang Civil Engineering Lanka (Pvt). Ltd - [(2000) 2 SLR 136]; Mark Rajandran v. First Capital Ltd. - [(2010) 1 SLR 60]; Clifford Ratwatte v. Thilanga Sumathipala and Others - [(2001) 2 SLR 55]; Jeganathan v. Safyath - [(2003) 2 SLR 372]. Analysis Issue 1: Validity of the Affidavit Rule applied: Supreme Court Rules of 1990; Oaths and Affirmation Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the affidavit submitted by the Plaintiff, which began with the statement that he, being a Christian, made an oath. However, the jurat of the affidavit stated that he 'affirmed.' The Court noted that the Oaths and Affirmation Ordinance allows individuals of different faiths to affirm instead of swearing an oath. The Court referenced previous cases, including Rustomjee v. Khan, which established that non-Christians could affirm rather than swear. The Court also highlighted that the jurat must be considered in its entirety and that the essential elements of an affidavit, such as the date and place of signing, were present. The Court concluded that the discrepancy between 'oath' and 'affirmation' did not invalidate the affidavit, as the intent to provide truthful testimony was clear. The Court emphasized that the fundamental purpose of an affidavit is to ensure the deponent tells the truth, and the procedural irregularity did not undermine this principle. Finding: The preliminary objection regarding the validity of the affidavit was overruled, allowing the Plaintiff's application to proceed. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in this case are interrelated, as the validity of the affidavit directly impacts the Plaintiff's ability to seek leave to appeal. The Court's analysis of the affidavit's validity also reflects broader principles regarding the administration of oaths and affirmations in a multi-religious society. Conclusion The Supreme Court ruled in favor of the Plaintiff-Respondent-Petitioner, overruling the preliminary objection regarding the affidavit's validity. The Court emphasized the importance of ensuring that procedural requirements do not obstruct the pursuit of justice, particularly in cases involving fundamental rights such as access to a right of way. The Court ordered no costs in this matter, reflecting a consideration of the circumstances surrounding the case. Keywords affidavit — right of way — permanent injunction — counter claim — District Court — High Court — Supreme Court Rules — Oaths and Affirmation Ordinance — validity of affidavit — legal representation — evidence — procedural law — civil procedure — judicial authority — deponent — affirmation
Counsel: Gamini Marapana, PC with Navin Marapona for the Plaintiff-Respondent-Petitioner; Romesh de Silva, PC with Sugath Caldera for the Defendant-Appellant-Respondent

Parties: Distilleries Company of Sri Lanka No. 110, Norris Canal Road Colombo 10 v. Randenigala Distilleries Lanka (Private) Limited No. 162, Rajagiriya Road, Rajagiriya
Bench: Mohan Pieris, J.; Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Rohini Marasinghe, J.
Keywords: Passing off — Goodwill — Misrepresentation — Confusion of the public — Unfair competition — Code of Intellectual Property Act — Section 142 — Res judicata — Trademark infringement — Bottle shape and label — Exclusion of competition
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent, Distilleries Company of Sri Lanka, initiated an action against the Defendant-Appellant, Randenigala Distilleries Lanka (Private) Limited, alleging passing off and unfair competition regarding the get-up, labels, and bottles used for their respective coconut blended arrack products. The Plaintiff-Respondent contended that the Defendant-Appellantâ€™s product packaging was deceptively similar to their own, leading to a misappropriation of goodwill. The Commercial High Court previously ruled in favor of the Plaintiff-Respondent. The Defendant-Appellant appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. The Supreme Court noted that the parties had engaged in prior litigation regarding similar subject matter, but determined that the current action was not barred by res judicata because the specific bottle and label designs in question had changed since the previous case. During the trial, the Plaintiff-Respondent attempted to establish exclusivity over the term "extra special arrack," but the Court clarified that the focus of the current proceedings was limited to the physical get-up of the products. The Plaintiff-Respondent relied heavily on the increase in the Defendant-Appellantâ€™s sales volume as evidence of damage, while the Defendant-Appellant maintained that their packaging was distinct and that no deception of the consuming public had occurred. Issues Whether the Defendant-Appellantâ€™s labels and bottles are sufficiently similar to those of the Plaintiff-Respondent to constitute the tort of passing off. Whether the Defendant-Appellantâ€™s conduct constitutes an act of unfair competition under Section 142 of the Code of Intellectual Property Act No. 52. Applicable Law Statutes: Code of Intellectual Property Act No. 52 Cases Referred to: The Leather Cloth Co v The American Cloth Co - [1865-11-h.l-cas-538] Perry v Truefit - [1843-6-beav-66] Rekitt & Colman v Borden - [1990-1-wlr-491] LOreal v Bellure - [2006/v1-ewhc-2355-ch] Hodgkinson Corby Limited and Another v Wards Mobility Services Limited - [1995-f.s-169] Limited biscuit (UK) Ltd v Asda Stores - [1997] Thomson Holidays v Norwegian Cruise Line - [2002/v1-ip&t-299] Lego System v Lego M. Lemelstrich - [1983-f.s.r-155] Sumeet Research and Holdings Ltd v Elite Radio and Engineering Co. Ltd - [1997/v2-sri-lr-393] Hexagon Pvt Ltd. v Australian Broadcasting Commission - [1975-7-alr-233] Analysis Issue 1: Passing Off Rule applied: The three-part test for passing off established in Rekitt & Colman v Borden: (1) existence of goodwill; (2) misrepresentation likely to deceive the public; and (3) resulting damage. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the Plaintiff-Respondent failed to prove the likelihood of confusion. A side-by-side comparison revealed significant physical differences, including label orientation, color schemes, and text. The Court emphasized that the law protects against deception, not mere copying. Furthermore, the Plaintiff-Respondent failed to provide evidence of actual damage beyond a simple assertion that the Defendant-Appellantâ€™s sales had increased. Finding: The claim for passing off failed as the Plaintiff-Respondent did not establish the necessary elements of misrepresentation and damage. Issue 2: Unfair Competition Rule applied: Section 142 of the Code of Intellectual Property Act No. 52, which prohibits acts of competition contrary to honest practices.
