Court's reasoning: The Court held that while Section 142 is broader than passing off, it is not intended to stifle competition or protect a market position. The Court applied the standard of "honest practices" and concluded that the Defendant-Appellantâ€™s actions did not involve underhand or sharp conduct. Since the Plaintiff-Respondent failed to prove that the Defendant-Appellantâ€™s packaging created confusion, there was no basis to find an act of unfair competition. Finding: The claim for unfair competition was dismissed. Interaction of Issues The Courtâ€™s findings on both issues were reinforced by the determination that there was no likelihood of confusion between the products. Because the Plaintiff-Respondent could not prove that the public was being deceived, both the tort of passing off and the statutory claim for unfair competition failed, as both rely on the premise of preventing deceptive market practices. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgment of the Commercial High Court dated 31.08.2010 is set aside, and the Plaintiff-Respondent's action is dismissed with costs of Rs. 50,000/-. Keywords Passing off — Goodwill — Misrepresentation — Confusion of the public — Unfair competition — Code of Intellectual Property Act — Section 142 — Res judicata — Trademark infringement — Bottle shape and label — Exclusion of competition

Parties: Distilleries Company of Sri Lanka v. Randenigala Distilleries Lanka (Private) Limited
Bench: Mohan Pieris, PC, CJ; Chandra Ekanayake, J; Rohini Marasinghe, J
Keywords: passing off — unfair competition — Intellectual Property Act — goodwill — misrepresentation — res judicata — market competition — consumer confusion — trademark infringement — commercial ethics — legal standards — evidence of damage — distinctiveness — legal principles
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal from the judgment of the High Court of the Western Province, which was delivered on 31 August 2010. The central issue at hand is whether the labels and/or bottles used by Randenigala Distilleries Lanka (the Defendant-Appellant) are visually and/or phonetically similar enough to those of Distilleries Company of Sri Lanka (the Plaintiff-Respondent) to mislead consumers, thereby constituting passing off and/or unfair competition under Section 142 of the Code of Intellectual Property Act No. 52 of 1979. The Plaintiff-Respondent claims that the Defendant-Appellant's products are likely to confuse consumers and misappropriate the goodwill associated with their brand. The appeal was argued on 22 September 2014 and decided on 19 December 2014. The Supreme Court, led by Chief Justice Mohan Pieris, examined the evidence and legal principles surrounding passing off and unfair competition. Issues The primary issues identified in this case are: Whether the labels and/or bottles used by the Defendant-Appellant are visually and/or phonetically similar enough to those of the Plaintiff-Respondent to constitute passing off. Whether the Defendant-Appellant's actions violate Section 142 of the Code of Intellectual Property Act No. 52 of 1979, which addresses unfair competition. Applicable Law Statutes: Code of Intellectual Property Act No. 52 of 1979 — Section 142 Cases Referred to: The Leather Cloth Co v The American Cloth Co - [1865] 11 H.L Cas. 538; Rekitt & Colman v Borden - [1990] 1 WLR 491; L’Oreal v Bellure - [2006] EWHC 2355 Ch; Hodgkinson Corby Limited and Another v Wards Mobility Services Limited - [1995] F.S. 169; Thomson Holidays v Norwegian Cruise Line - [2002] IP&T 299; Lego System v Lego M. Lemelstrich - [1983] F.S.R. 155; Sumeet Research and Holdings Ltd v Elite Radio and Engineering Co. Ltd - [1997] 2 SLR 393; Hexagon Pvt Ltd. v Australian Broadcasting Commission - [1975] 7 ALR 233. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the labels and/or bottles used by the Defendant-Appellant are visually and/or phonetically similar enough to those of the Plaintiff-Respondent to constitute passing off. Rule applied: The law of passing off, as articulated in the case of Rekitt & Colman v Borden, requires the Plaintiff to establish three elements: (1) the Plaintiff's mark has goodwill; (2) the Defendant has made a misrepresentation likely to deceive the public; and (3) the misrepresentation has caused damage. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court emphasized that the essence of passing off lies in the misappropriation of goodwill developed among the public due to a distinguishing mark. The Court noted that the Respondent must prove that they have developed a reputation sufficient to establish goodwill in the mark they seek to protect. The Court referenced the necessity of showing that the Appellant's actions have caused confusion among consumers regarding the source of the goods. The Court analyzed the physical characteristics of the labels and bottles of both parties, highlighting distinct differences such as the orientation of the labels, the design elements, and the wording used. The Court concluded that the differences were significant enough to prevent consumer confusion. The Court also pointed out that the Respondent's evidence did not sufficiently demonstrate that the Appellant's actions had resulted in a misrepresentation that caused damage to the Respondent's goodwill. Finding: The Court found that the Respondent failed to establish a case for passing off against the Appellant. Issue 2: Whether the Defendant-Appellant's actions violate Section 142 of the Code of Intellectual Property Act No. 52 of 1979, which addresses unfair competition. Rule applied: Section 142 prohibits acts of competition contrary to honest practices in industrial or commercial matters. Court's reasoning: The Court recognized that unfair competition encompasses a broader scope than passing off, aiming to promote fair market practices. However, the Court noted that the Respondent's claim under Section 142 was contingent upon establishing that the Appellant's actions constituted unfair competition. The Court reiterated that merely copying or imitating a product does not inherently constitute unfair competition unless it involves deceitful practices that mislead consumers. The Court found that the Respondent had not provided adequate evidence to support their claim of unfair competition, as the distinctions between the products were clear and did not create confusion among consumers. Finding: The Court concluded that the Respondent did not establish a case for unfair competition under Section 142. Interaction of Issues The issues of passing off and unfair competition are interrelated, as both hinge on the concepts of consumer confusion and the protection of goodwill. The Court's analysis of the likelihood of confusion directly impacted its findings on both issues. The failure to establish passing off due to a lack of evidence regarding consumer confusion similarly undermined the Respondent's claim of unfair competition. Conclusion The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, setting aside the judgment of the Commercial High Court dated 31 August 2010, and dismissed the Plaintiff's action with costs amounting to Rs. 50,000. The Court emphasized the importance of protecting fair competition in the marketplace and the necessity for clear evidence when alleging passing off or unfair competition. The judgment reinforces the principle that competition should not be stifled without substantial proof of consumer deception or misappropriation of goodwill. Keywords passing off — unfair competition — Intellectual Property Act — goodwill — misrepresentation — res judicata — market competition — consumer confusion — trademark infringement — commercial ethics — legal standards — evidence of damage — distinctiveness — legal principles
Counsel: D.P. Kumarasinghe PC with Mahendra Kumarasinghe and Jayerani Kumarasinghe for the Defendant-Appellant. S.A Parthalingam PC with V.K Choksy for the Plaintiff-Respondent.

Parties: Nona v. Wimaladasa
Judgment by: Sisira J De Abrew J, J.
Bench: Eva Wanasundera PC, J; Buwaneka Aluvihare PC, J; Sisira J De Abrew J, J.
Keywords: Appeal against judgment — Land Development Ordinance — State Land Ordinance — Crown Land Ordinance — Personal permit — Spouse of permit holder — State land ownership — Ejectment — Writ of execution
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent initiated an action in the District Court of Ampara seeking a declaration of title and the ejectment of the Defendant-Appellant from a portion of land described in the second schedule to the plaint. The Plaintiff-Respondent claimed that her late husband was declared the owner of the land in a previous District Court case and that she and her predecessor in title had been in possession until the Defendant-Appellant entered the land in 1994. It was common ground that the land in question was State land and that it had been allocated to the Plaintiff-Respondent's husband via a permit. The District Court and the Civil Appellate High Court both held in favor of the Plaintiff-Respondent, concluding that the permit was issued under the Land Development Ordinance and that the Plaintiff-Respondent was entitled to succeed to the land under Section 48A of that Ordinance. The Defendant-Appellant appealed this decision, asserting that the permit was issued under the State Land Ordinance, which carries different legal consequences regarding succession and the personal nature of the grant. Issues Whether the judges of the Civil Appellate High Court erred in law by applying the provisions of the Land Development Ordinance to a permit issued under the State Land Ordinance. Whether the Plaintiff-Respondent had a valid legal title to the land following the death of her husband, given the conditions of the permit and the provisions of the State Land Ordinance. Applicable Law Statutes: Land Development Ordinance State Land Ordinance State Lands (Recovery of Possession) Act Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of the Land Development Ordinance Rule applied: The court examined the specific terms of Permit No. 8440, which explicitly stated it was issued under the provisions of the Crown Land Ordinance No. 8. Court's reasoning: The court found that both the District Court and the High Court failed to examine the actual permit document. Upon review, the Supreme Court determined that the permit was issued under the State Land Ordinance, not the Land Development Ordinance. Consequently, the reliance by the lower courts on Section 48A of the Land Development Ordinance to establish the Plaintiff-Respondent's succession rights was legally erroneous. Finding: The lower courts erred in law by applying the Land Development Ordinance to a permit governed by the State Land Ordinance. Issue 2: Validity of Plaintiff-Respondent's Title Rule applied: Section 16(1) and 16(2) of the State Land Ordinance, which dictate that rights under a permit designated as "personal" to the grantee are determined upon the death of the grantee, with the land reverting to the State. Court's reasoning: Condition No. 5 of the permit expressly stated that the permit was personal to the grantee. As the Plaintiff-Respondent admitted her husband was deceased, the court reasoned that her rights under the permit terminated upon his death. Therefore, she possessed no legal title to the land to support her claim for a declaration of title or ejectment. Finding: The Plaintiff-Respondentâ€™s claim failed because her rights to the land ceased upon the death of the original permit holder. Interaction of Issues The resolution of the first issue regarding the governing statute directly necessitated the application of the State Land Ordinance in the second issue. By determining that the permit was not issued under the Land Development Ordinance, the court effectively invalidated the basis for the Plaintiff-Respondent's claim of succession, rendering her title claim unsustainable under the State Land Ordinance. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, and the judgments of the District Court and the High Court are set aside. The court refused to restore the Defendant-Appellant to possession due to his illegal occupation and directed the District Court to recall the writ of execution that had placed the Plaintiff-Respondent in possession. Keywords Appeal against judgment — Land Development Ordinance — State Land Ordinance — Crown Land Ordinance — Personal permit — Spouse of permit holder — State land ownership — Ejectment — Writ of execution

Parties: Attorney-General v. Rahim and Others
Bench: Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Buwaneka Aluwihare, P.C. J; Sarath De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Regulation 6 of Emergency Regulations — Sinhala vs. English text — Inconsistency in legislation — Minimum mandatory sentence — Constitutional validity — Article 4(c) — Article 11 — Article 12(1) — Illegal sentence — Sentencing discretion
Summary: Facts The Appellants, Ayiduroos Abdul Rahim, Shavul Hameed Nasir, Abdul Gaffoor Amanullah, and Sahibu Mohideen, were indicted before the High Court of Colombo for possessing outboard motors exceeding fifteen horsepower in violation of Regulation 2 of the Emergency (Restricted use of Outboard Motors) Regulation No. 8. Upon appearing before the High Court on 30 November 2010, the Appellants entered unqualified pleas of guilty. The High Court convicted the Appellants and sentenced each to three months of imprisonment and a fine of five hundred thousand rupees, with a default sentence of one year of imprisonment. Additionally, the court ordered the forfeiture of the seven outboard motors to the state. The Appellants subsequently sought revision in the Court of Appeal. The Court of Appeal suspended the three-month prison sentence for a period of ten years but maintained the fine of five hundred thousand rupees. The Appellants then appealed to the Supreme Court, contending that the sentencing was illegal due to an inconsistency between the Sinhala and English versions of Regulation 6 of the relevant Gazette Notification. Issues Whether the imposition of both a term of imprisonment and a fine under Regulation 6 of the Emergency (Restricted use of Outboard Motors) Regulation No. 8 constitutes an illegal sentence due to an inconsistency between the Sinhala and English texts of the regulation. Whether the Sinhala text of the regulation prevails over the English text in the event of an inconsistency. Applicable Law Statutes: Public Security Ordinance (Chapter 40) Emergency (Restricted use of Outboard Motors) Regulation No. 8 Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka Cases Referred to: [sc-reference-03/2008] Analysis Issue 1: Legality of cumulative sentencing Rule applied: Regulation 6 of the Emergency (Restricted use of Outboard Motors) Regulation No. 8. Court's reasoning: The court examined the Sinhala and English versions of the regulation. The English text provided for both imprisonment and a fine, whereas the Sinhala text utilized a disjunctive structure, implying that the court could impose either imprisonment or a fine, but not both. The court accepted the argument that the Sinhala text is the authoritative version in cases of inconsistency. Consequently, the imposition of both penalties by the High Court was deemed to be in excess of the legal authority granted by the regulation. Finding: The sentence imposed by the High Court, which included both imprisonment and a fine, was illegal. Issue 2: Primacy of the Sinhala text Rule applied: Constitutional principles regarding the interpretation of statutes in the event of linguistic inconsistency. Court's reasoning: The court acknowledged the established legal principle that in the event of an inconsistency between the Sinhala and English texts of a statute or regulation, the Sinhala text prevails. The Senior State Counsel representing the Attorney General concurred with this interpretation. Finding: The Sinhala text of the regulation is the authoritative version and must prevail. Interaction of Issues The resolution of the second issue regarding the primacy of the Sinhala text directly dictated the outcome of the first issue. Because the Sinhala text mandates a disjunctive penalty, the court's finding that the Sinhala text prevails necessitated the conclusion that the cumulative sentence imposed by the High Court was legally impermissible. Conclusion The appeal is partly allowed. The order of the Court of Appeal and the fine of five hundred thousand rupees imposed by the High Court are set aside, while the sentence of three months imprisonment is affirmed. The High Court is directed to verify that the Appellants have already served the term of imprisonment. Keywords Regulation 6 of Emergency Regulations — Sinhala vs. English text — Inconsistency in legislation — Minimum mandatory sentence — Constitutional validity — Article 4(c) — Article 11 — Article 12(1) — Illegal sentence — Sentencing discretion

Parties: Union Trust and Investments Ltd. v. Wijesena and Others
Bench: Eva Wanasundera, PC,J; Sarath De Abrew, J.; Priyantha Jayawardena, PC.J
Keywords: Leave to appeal — Provincial High Court jurisdiction — Erroneous judgment — Miscarriage of justice — Failure of justice — Execution of decree — Section 337 of the Civil Procedure Code — Fraud — Change of address — Duty to inform court — Revisionary jurisdiction — Exceptional circumstances
Summary: Facts Union Trust and Investments Ltd. entered into a hire-purchase agreement with Madagodage Thusitha Wijesena, with Swarna Wijesena and Wadisinghe Arachchige Kapilaratne acting as guarantors. Following a default in payments, the Appellant filed action in the District Court of Colombo in 1990. The Defendants appeared and filed answers but subsequently failed to attend trial. Ex-parte judgments and decrees were entered against the Defendants. The Appellant attempted to execute the decree, but service of the writ was frustrated as the Respondents could not be located at the addresses provided in the court record. In 2007, the Appellant filed a fresh application to execute the writ after identifying new addresses for the Respondents. The Respondents objected, citing the lapse of time. The Additional District Judge of Colombo held an inquiry and permitted the execution of the writ. The Respondents then invoked the revisionary jurisdiction of the Provincial High Court of the Western Province, which set aside the District Court order, ruling that the application was barred by the ten-year limitation period prescribed in Section 337 of the Civil Procedure Code. The Appellant subsequently appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Provincial High Court erred in law by holding that the application to execute the decree was barred by Section 337 of the Civil Procedure Code. Whether the Provincial High Court erred in law by exercising its revisionary jurisdiction in the absence of exceptional or extraordinary grounds. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 337 Cases Referred to: Fernando v. Latibu - [18-nlr-95] Cross World (Pvt.) Ltd. v. Union Trust and Investment - [sc-appeal-36/2010] Analysis Issue 1: Execution of Decree and Section 337 Rule applied: Section 337(2) of the Civil Procedure Code, which permits the execution of a decree after ten years if the judgment debtor has prevented execution through fraud or force. Court's reasoning: The Court reasoned that the systematic evasion of service by a judgment debtor constitutes fraud. Because the Respondents had initially appeared in court, they were under a duty to inform the court of any change of address. By moving without notification and avoiding service of the decree and subsequent writ notices, the Respondents intentionally obstructed the process. The Court held that such conduct amounts to fraud within the meaning of Section 337(2), thereby preventing the statutory time bar from operating as a barrier to the execution of the writ. Finding: The Provincial High Court erred in its interpretation; the Appellant is entitled to execute the writ despite the lapse of ten years. Issue 2: Exercise of Revisionary Jurisdiction Rule applied: The principle that revisionary jurisdiction is reserved for exceptional circumstances or extraordinary grounds that shock the conscience of the court. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that the Respondents failed to plead or disclose any extraordinary grounds in their revision application. The High Court improperly entertained the application based on minor errors, such as a diary entry mistake, which do not meet the threshold for invoking revisionary powers. Finding: The Provincial High Court erred in law by exercising its revisionary jurisdiction in this matter. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the Respondents committed fraud by evading service (Issue 1) confirms that the District Courtâ€™s original order was legally sound. Consequently, the High Courtâ€™s intervention via revisionary jurisdiction was not only procedurally improper (Issue 2) but also substantively incorrect, as there was no miscarriage of justice to rectify. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgment of the Provincial High Court dated 03.11.2011 is set aside, and the order of the District Court of Colombo dated 15.02.2010 is affirmed. Taxed costs are ordered against all Respondents. Keywords Leave to appeal — Provincial High Court jurisdiction — Erroneous judgment — Miscarriage of justice — Failure of justice — Execution of decree — Section 337 of the Civil Procedure Code — Fraud — Change of address — Duty to inform court — Revisionary jurisdiction — Exceptional circumstances

Parties: Ory Owners Association No.475 1/1 v. H.D. Hemaratna
Bench: K. Sripavan, J.; Rohini Marasinghe, J.; Sarath De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Tea Control Act — Reasonable Price Formula — Tea Commissioner's powers — Inquiry by the Tea Commissioner — Ultra vires action — Abolition of Tea Controller — Sri Lanka Tea Board Law — Interpretation of statutes — Delay in filing application — Writ of certiorari
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, the Private Tea Factory Owners Association, filed a writ application in the Court of Appeal challenging the decision of the Tea Commissioner to impose a Reasonable Price Formula (RPF) through various circulars. The Petitioner sought to quash these circulars and specific letters issued to tea factories, arguing that the Tea Commissioner lacked the statutory authority to impose such a formula and that the enforcement mechanism under the Tea Control Act was illegal. The Petitioner further contended that the imposition of the formula was arbitrary and violated the principle of audi alteram partem. The Court of Appeal dismissed the application, holding that the Tea Control Act empowered the Tea Commissioner to issue directions and that the form of inquiry was at the discretion of the Commissioner. The Petitioner subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court, which granted special leave to appeal on questions of law regarding the interpretation of the Tea Control Act, the authority of the Tea Commissioner, and the impact of the delay in filing the writ application. Issues Whether the Tea Commissioner possesses the legal authority under the Tea Control Act to impose a uniform Reasonable Price Formula for all tea factories. Whether the Office of the Tea Controller was validly succeeded by the Tea Commissioner such that the powers under the Tea Control Act remain enforceable. Whether the writ application was rendered unmaintainable due to the unreasonable delay in challenging the circulars. Applicable Law Statutes: Tea Control Act No. 51 Sri Lanka Tea Board Law No. 14 Tea Control (Amendment) Act No. 3 of 1993 Cases Referred to: Pearlberg v. Varty - [1972/v1-w-l-r-534] Saraswati Industrial Syndicate v. Union of India - [air-1975-sc-460] State of Karnataka v. Ranganatha Reddy - [air-1978-sc-215] Biso Menika v. Cyril de Alwis - [1982/v1-sri-lr-368] Analysis Issue 1: Authority to impose a uniform Reasonable Price Formula Rule applied: Section 8(2)(b) of the Tea Control Act. Court's reasoning: The court interpreted the phrase "having regard to the price fetched for made tea manufactured at that factory" to mean that the reasonable price must be factory-specific. The court found that the Tea Commissionerâ€™s attempt to apply a common formula based on elevational averages was based on extraneous considerations and ignored the legislative intent that the price should reflect the specific circumstances of each factory. Finding: The letters and circulars imposing a uniform Reasonable Price Formula were ultra vires the powers of the Tea Commissioner. Issue 2: Succession of the Tea Commissioner Rule applied: Section 9(1) and 9(2) of the Sri Lanka Tea Board Law No. 14 and Section 6 of the Tea Control (Amendment) Act No. 3 of 1993. Court's reasoning: The court held that while the office of the Tea Controller was abolished by Law No. 14, the legislature explicitly transferred the powers, functions, and duties of the Tea Controller to the Tea Commissioner. The court rejected the argument that the use of the word "Controller" in subsequent amendments rendered the powers unenforceable, noting that the legislative intent was clearly to vest these powers in the Tea Commissioner. Finding: The Tea Commissioner is legally empowered to exercise the functions previously vested in the Tea Controller. Issue 3: Impact of delay (Laches) Rule applied: Principles of laches and discretionary relief in writ jurisdiction as discussed in Biso Menika v. Cyril de Alwis. Court's reasoning: The court acknowledged that the application was filed after a significant delay (up to 26 years for the earliest circular). While the court affirmed that it has the discretion to grant relief even in cases of delay if an order is a nullity, it determined that in this specific instance, quashing the circulars would cause significant injustice and unsettle long-settled matters regarding payments already made. Finding: Although the circulars were ultra vires, the court declined to exercise its discretion to quash them due to the unreasonable delay and the resulting prejudice. Interaction of Issues The findings on the legality of the formula and the issue of delay are in tension. While the court concluded that the Tea Commissioner acted ultra vires (Issue 1), it refused to grant the remedy of certiorari (Issue 3) because the Petitioner failed to approach the court expeditiously. The courtâ€™s interpretation of the Tea Commissioner's powers (Issue 2) clarified the legal framework, but the final disposition was governed by the court's equitable discretion regarding laches. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The court held that while the Tea Commissioner acted ultra vires in imposing a uniform formula, the writ application is denied due to the unreasonable delay in challenging the circulars. Keywords Tea Control Act — Reasonable Price Formula — Tea Commissioner's powers — Inquiry by the Tea Commissioner — Ultra vires action — Abolition of Tea Controller — Sri Lanka Tea Board Law — Interpretation of statutes — Delay in filing application — Writ of certiorari

Parties: Attorney General v. Sampath
Bench: Eva Wanasundera, PC. J; Sarath De Abrew, J.; P. Jayawardena, PC. J
Keywords: Special Leave to Appeal — Question of law — Rape — Statutory rape — Minimum mandatory sentence — Judicial discretion — Sentencing — Penal Code — Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Child's best interest
Summary: Facts The Appellant, Ambagala Mudiyanselage Samantha Sampath, was indicted by the Attorney General in the High Court of Kurunegala for the rape of a girl under 16 years of age, an offence punishable under Section 364(2)(e) of the Penal Code. The Appellant, who had been living with the victim and her parents, pleaded guilty to the charge. The High Court sentenced the Appellant to a term of rigorous imprisonment suspended for a period of years, imposed a fine, and ordered the payment of compensation to the victim. The victim had given birth to a child fathered by the Appellant, and the Appellant continued to support the child financially and through active parenting. The Attorney General appealed the sentence to the Court of Appeal, contending that the High Court failed to impose the mandatory minimum sentence of 10 years rigorous imprisonment required by law. The Court of Appeal set aside the suspended sentence and substituted it with the 10-year mandatory term of rigorous imprisonment. The Appellant subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court, challenging the Court of Appeal's judgment on the basis that the mandatory minimum sentence provision improperly restricts judicial discretion. Issues Whether the mandatory minimum sentence prescribed by Section 364(2)(e) of the Penal Code is in conflict with Articles 4(c), 11, and 12(1) of the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka. Whether the High Court is permitted to exercise judicial discretion to impose a sentence below the mandatory minimum prescribed by statute when the circumstances of the case warrant such a departure. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 4(c), 11, 12(1), 80(3), 125(1), 128(2) Penal Code, Section 364(2)(e) Penal Code (Amendment) Act No. 22 of 1995 Cases Referred to: S.C. Reference 03/2008 - [sc-reference-03/2008] Dharma Sri Tissa Kumara Wijenaike v. Attorney General - [sc-appeal-179/2012] Analysis Issue 1: Constitutionality of mandatory minimum sentencing Rule applied: Articles 4(c), 11, and 12(1) of the Constitution and the precedent established in S.C. Reference 03/2008. Court's reasoning: The Court reasoned that mandatory minimum sentences result in legislative determination of punishment, which erodes essential judicial discretion. By forcing identical sentences regardless of individual circumstances, the law treats unequals as equals, violating the principle of equal treatment under Article 12(1) and potentially amounting to cruel punishment under Article 11. The Court affirmed that the judiciary is the guardian of the law and must retain the power to consider factors such as the offender's age, antecedents, and the specific circumstances of the offence. Finding: The mandatory minimum sentence prescribed in Section 364(2)(e) of the Penal Code is in conflict with the Constitution. Issue 2: Exercise of judicial discretion Rule applied: The principle that the Court acts as the "upper guardian" of a child and the requirement to consider the "best interest of the child" under the Children's Charter 1992. Court's reasoning: The Court emphasized that sentencing is a judicial function that requires a judge to "see, smell, and feel" the case. In this instance, the existence of a child born to the victim and the Appellant, who is currently being supported by the Appellant, necessitates a compassionate approach. Imposing a 10-year mandatory sentence would deprive the child of the father's support and presence, causing the child to suffer for an offence she did not commit. The Court held that the High Court correctly exercised its discretion to prioritize the welfare of the child over a rigid application of the mandatory minimum. Finding: The High Court was not inhibited by the mandatory minimum provision and acted within its judicial discretion in imposing a suspended sentence. Interaction of Issues The findings are intrinsically linked; the determination that the mandatory minimum sentence is unconstitutional (Issue 1) provides the legal foundation for the High Court to exercise its discretion (Issue 2). By resolving that the statute is in conflict with the Constitution, the Court effectively removed the legal barrier that would have otherwise prevented the High Court from considering the specific humanitarian circumstances of the Appellant's family life. Conclusion The judgment of the Court of Appeal dated 24.07.2012 is set aside, and the judgment of the High Court dated 28.10.2008 is affirmed. The Appellant's sentence of a suspended term of imprisonment is restored. Keywords Special Leave to Appeal — Question of law — Rape — Statutory rape — Minimum mandatory sentence — Judicial discretion — Sentencing — Penal Code — Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Child's best interest

Parties: Sakir v. The Principal Holy Family Convent Kurunegala and Others
Bench: Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Upaly Abeyratne, J; Anil Gooneratne, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Equality before the law — School admissions — Residency requirement — Proximity to school — Administrative action — Selection procedure — Interpretation of circular — Mala fides
Summary: Facts The Petitioner sought relief under Article 126 of the Constitution, alleging a violation of his fundamental right to equality under Article 12(1) regarding the admission of his daughter to Year 1 at Holy Family Convent, Kurunegala, for the 2013 academic year. The Petitioner contended that the selection process was arbitrary and capricious, specifically challenging the marks awarded to the 4th Respondentâ€™s child. The Petitioner argued that the 4th Respondentâ€™s child was improperly granted extra marks for a deed of residence and that the Petitionerâ€™s child, having secured 90 marks, should have been selected over the 4th Respondentâ€™s child. The official Respondents contended that the Petitionerâ€™s application was fundamentally flawed because the Petitioner did not reside at the address provided in his application, which was identified as a commercial premises used for a gas sales outlet. Furthermore, the Respondents argued that even if the 4th Respondentâ€™s marks were adjusted, the Petitionerâ€™s child was not the most proximate candidate to the school, as other applicants with equal marks resided closer to the school. The school authorities maintained that the interview board acted within its discretion under Circular No. 2012/19 in interpreting the admission criteria. Following a site inspection and review of evidence, the authorities concluded that the Petitioner failed to meet the residency requirements, leading to the rejection of his application. Issues Whether the selection of the 4th Respondentâ€™s child over the Petitionerâ€™s child constituted an arbitrary and capricious act violating Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the Petitioner established that he met the residency requirements stipulated in Circular No. 2012/19. Whether the court should exercise its fundamental rights jurisdiction in matters involving administrative errors or miscalculations in school admissions. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1), Article 126. Cases Referred to: 10 NLR 242 W.K. Nimala Wijesinghe v. A.G - [sc-application-13/1979] SCFR Application 41/2013 - [sc-fr-41/2013] Analysis Issue 1: Arbitrary selection and Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution, which guarantees equality before the law and equal protection of the law. Court's reasoning: The court noted that Article 12(1) ensures equality of opportunity for consideration but does not guarantee a specific outcome in a competitive selection process. The court found that the Petitioner failed to provide evidence of mala fides on the part of the selection board. Furthermore, the court observed that even if the 4th Respondentâ€™s marks were contested, the Petitioner was not the most proximate candidate to the school, rendering his claim to a seat unsuccessful regardless of the 4th Respondent's score. Finding: The selection process did not violate Article 12(1) as the Petitioner failed to prove arbitrary discrimination or mala fides. Issue 2: Residency requirements Rule applied: Circular No. 2012/19, which mandates specific residency requirements for school admissions. Court's reasoning: The Respondents provided evidence, including photographs and a letter from the local Jumma Mosque, indicating that the Petitionerâ€™s provided address was a commercial business premises rather than a residence. The court held that the Petitioner failed to establish his residency at the stated address, which, under the circular, necessitated the rejection of his application. Finding: The Petitioner failed to satisfy the residency requirement, justifying the rejection of his application. Issue 3: Scope of fundamental rights jurisdiction Rule applied: The principle established in W.K. Nimala Wijesinghe v. A.G, which distinguishes between ordinary administrative errors and breaches of fundamental rights. Court's reasoning: The court held that not every administrative mistake or error of judgment constitutes a violation of fundamental rights. The court emphasized that the fundamental rights jurisdiction is limited to cases where a constitutional guarantee has been infringed. Mere allegations of miscalculation or procedural lapses in school admissions do not automatically invoke the court's jurisdiction under Article 126. Finding: The court determined that the matter did not warrant the exercise of its fundamental rights jurisdiction. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected: the Petitionerâ€™s failure to meet the residency requirement (Issue 2) and his lack of proximity to the school (Issue 1) demonstrate that he lacked the standing to challenge the selection process. Because the Petitioner failed to establish a valid claim to admission, the alleged administrative errors (Issue 3) did not result in a violation of his fundamental rights. Conclusion The court concluded that the Petitioner failed to establish an infringement of his fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Consequently, the application is dismissed. No costs are awarded. Keywords Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Equality before the law — School admissions — Residency requirement — Proximity to school — Administrative action — Selection procedure — Interpretation of circular — Mala fides

Parties: Prins Gunasekera v. Associated News Papers of Ceylon Limited
Bench: Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Eva Wanasundera, P.C., J.; Sisira J De Abrew, J.
Keywords: defamation — damages — libel — publication — burden of proof — public interest — fair comment — reputation — evidence — tort — appeal — judgment — Court of Appeal — Sri Lanka
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff, Prins Gunasekera, initiated legal proceedings against the Defendant, Associated News Papers of Ceylon Limited, seeking damages amounting to Rs. 5,000,000 for a libelous article published in the Ceylon Daily News on May 17, 1990. The District Court of Colombo dismissed the Plaintiff's case, prompting an appeal to the Court of Appeal, which overturned the District Court's decision and awarded the Plaintiff the full amount sought. The Defendant, dissatisfied with the Court of Appeal's ruling, appealed to the Supreme Court, raising significant legal questions regarding the nature of the alleged defamation and the appropriateness of the damages awarded. The Supreme Court granted leave to appeal on two specific questions of law: whether the article in question contained the necessary elements to establish defamation and whether the Court of Appeal properly considered the relevant law in determining the damages awarded. The article in question accused certain individuals, including the Plaintiff, of engaging in a campaign of vilification against the Sri Lankan government while residing in the UK. The article suggested that these individuals were misappropriating funds collected for humanitarian causes, thereby damaging their reputations. Issues The primary issues before the Supreme Court were: 1. Whether the article published in the Ceylon Daily News is attributable to the Plaintiff, Prins Gunasekera. 2. Whether the article is per se defamatory of the Plaintiff. 3. Whether the damages awarded by the Court of Appeal were excessive. Applicable Law Statutes: The relevant statutes concerning defamation and tort law were cited, including principles from Roman-Dutch law. Cases Referred to: The judgment referenced various legal texts and principles regarding defamation, including the standard for determining whether statements are defamatory per se. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the article is attributable to the Plaintiff. Rule applied: The court examined the principles of attribution in defamation cases, focusing on whether the article explicitly or implicitly referred to the Plaintiff. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court analyzed the content of the article, noting that it explicitly named the Plaintiff as a leader of a group accused of misappropriating funds. The court emphasized that the article's context and the inclusion of the Plaintiff's photograph made it clear that the statements were directed at him. The court stated, "The only photograph published in the article is that of the plaintiff. When I consider the above matters, there is no difficulty in deciding that the said article has referred to the plaintiff and many groups of people." This reasoning underscored the importance of context in defamation claims, particularly when the identity of the individual is made clear through accompanying images or descriptions. Finding: The court concluded that the article was indeed attributable to the Plaintiff. Issue 2: Whether the article is per se defamatory of the Plaintiff. Rule applied: The court applied the standard for determining whether statements are defamatory per se, which involves assessing whether the words used would lower the Plaintiff in the estimation of society. Court's reasoning: The court examined the language of the article, which accused the Plaintiff and others of engaging in deceptive practices and misappropriating funds. The court cited legal texts stating that statements which tend to lower a person in the estimation of reasonable individuals are considered defamatory. The court noted, "The article describes that the small groups of people including the group led by the plaintiff have got involved in collecting funds and pocketing the same." This statement, along with others in the article, was deemed to have a clear defamatory implication. The court further referenced the testimony of a witness, Jeyaraj Fernandopulle, who attested to the Plaintiff's high reputation, reinforcing the notion that the article's claims were damaging. Finding: The court determined that the article was per se defamatory of the Plaintiff. Issue 3: Whether the damages awarded were excessive. Rule applied: The court considered the principles governing the assessment of damages in defamation cases, particularly the need for evidence demonstrating the impact of the defamatory statements on the Plaintiff's reputation. Court's reasoning: The court acknowledged that while the Plaintiff had established that the article was defamatory, he had not provided sufficient evidence to justify the full amount of damages awarded by the Court of Appeal. The court noted, "The plaintiff must, by way of evidence, say as to how the article affects his reputation both in Sri Lanka and London." The absence of evidence regarding the actual impact of the article on the Plaintiff's reputation led the court to conclude that the original award of Rs. 5,000,000 was excessive. Finding: The court reduced the damages to Rs. 2,000,000, acknowledging the Plaintiff's reputation but emphasizing the need for concrete evidence of harm. Interaction of Issues The issues of attribution and whether the article was defamatory were closely intertwined, as establishing that the article referred to the Plaintiff was essential for determining whether it was defamatory. Additionally, the assessment of damages was contingent upon the court's findings regarding the defamatory nature of the article and the evidence presented regarding its impact on the Plaintiff's reputation. Conclusion The Supreme Court affirmed the Court of Appeal's finding that the article was defamatory but modified the damages awarded, reducing them to Rs. 2,000,000. The court's decision highlighted the importance of evidence in defamation cases and clarified the standards for establishing both attribution and the defamatory nature of statements. The judgment underscored the balance between protecting reputations and ensuring that damages awarded are proportionate to the harm demonstrated. Keywords defamation — damages — libel — publication — burden of proof — public interest — fair comment — reputation — evidence — tort — appeal — judgment — Court of Appeal — Sri Lanka
Counsel: Gomin Dayasiri for the Defendant-Respondent-Appellant; Manohara de Silva, P.C. for the Plaintiff-Appellant-Respondent

Parties: Prins Gunasekera v. Associated Newspapers of Ceylon Limited
Bench: Chandra Ekanayake, J.; S. Eva Wanasundera, P.C., J.; Sisira J. de Abrew, J.
Keywords: defamation — damages — public interest — fair comment — tort of defamation — reasonable person standard — vilification — burden of proof — res judicata — reciprocal enforcement of judgments — evidence — publication — judgment — appeal — legal principles
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal concerning a defamation claim made by Prins Gunasekera against the Associated Newspapers of Ceylon Limited. The dispute originated from a newspaper article titled 'Tarbrush Campaign Against Lanka in London,' published on 17 May 1990, which Gunasekera alleged was defamatory. He sought damages amounting to five million rupees in the District Court of Colombo, claiming that the article harmed his reputation. The District Court initially dismissed his claim on 12 September 1996, stating that Gunasekera had not proven his case. Following this, Gunasekera appealed to the Court of Appeal, which ruled in his favor on 28 February 2011, awarding him the claimed damages. The Defendant-Appellant, dissatisfied with this ruling, sought special leave to appeal to the Supreme Court, raising questions regarding the elements of defamation and the assessment of damages. The Supreme Court was tasked with determining whether the article contained the necessary elements to establish defamation and whether the Court of Appeal had appropriately considered the relevant law in quantifying damages. Issues The primary issues for determination were: Whether the newspaper article contained the necessary ingredients to establish defamation as claimed by the Plaintiff-Respondent. Whether the Court of Appeal properly considered the relevant law in determining and quantifying damages awarded to the Plaintiff-Respondent. Applicable Law Statutes: Article 128(2) of the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka; Reciprocal Enforcement of Judgments Ordinance (Cap. 94). Cases Referred to: Muir Vs January 1990 BLR 388; Independent Newspaper Vs. Nissanka Wijeratne 1995 2SLR 253; Charleston & Smith Vs. News Graph Newspapers Ltd. (1995) 2 AC 65. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the newspaper article contained the necessary ingredients to establish defamation as claimed by the Plaintiff-Respondent. Rule applied: The tort of defamation requires the plaintiff to prove that the publication was false and defamatory. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the content of the article in detail, noting that the Plaintiff-Respondent had not specified which words or phrases were defamatory. The Court emphasized that in defamation cases, the actual words used must be pleaded, and it is insufficient to merely describe the substance of the publication. The judgment highlighted that the Plaintiff-Respondent's witness failed to identify any specific defamatory statements, which is crucial in establishing a defamation claim. The Court further analyzed the meaning of 'vilification' and concluded that the article did not contain per se defamatory statements. The article was deemed to be a commentary on public interest, discussing the actions of certain Sri Lankans in the UK, including Gunasekera. The Court noted that the Plaintiff-Respondent must prove that the statements were false to shift the burden of proof to the Defendant-Appellant. The Supreme Court found that the article did not lower the Plaintiff-Respondent's reputation in the eyes of a reasonable person, as it did not contain any abusive or derogatory language directed specifically at him. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the article did not contain the necessary elements to establish defamation. Issue 2: Whether the Court of Appeal properly considered the relevant law in determining and quantifying damages awarded to the Plaintiff-Respondent. Rule applied: Damages in defamation cases must be based on proven harm to reputation and must be quantifiable. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court scrutinized the Court of Appeal's judgment regarding the assessment of damages. It noted that the Court of Appeal had awarded damages without a proper basis for quantification, merely granting the amount requested by the Plaintiff-Respondent. The Supreme Court criticized this approach, stating that damages must be substantiated by evidence demonstrating the extent of harm caused by the defamatory publication. The Court emphasized that the Plaintiff-Respondent had not provided sufficient evidence to support his claim for damages, nor had he established how the alleged defamation had impacted his reputation or livelihood. The Supreme Court referenced legal principles regarding the calculation of damages in defamation cases, asserting that the Court of Appeal had misinterpreted these principles. Finding: The Supreme Court determined that the Court of Appeal's award of damages lacked a proper legal foundation and was therefore unjustified. Interaction of Issues The issues of whether the article was defamatory and whether the damages were appropriately quantified are interrelated. The determination of defamation directly impacts the assessment of damages; if the article is not deemed defamatory, there can be no basis for awarding damages. The Supreme Court's analysis of the article's content and the Plaintiff-Respondent's failure to prove defamation ultimately led to the conclusion that the damages awarded by the Court of Appeal were unwarranted. Conclusion The Supreme Court set aside the judgment of the Court of Appeal, affirming the District Court's decision to dismiss the Plaintiff-Respondent's claim for defamation. The Court ordered no costs, emphasizing the lack of merit in the Plaintiff-Respondent's case. The judgment underscored the importance of substantiating claims of defamation with clear evidence and the necessity for courts to adhere to established legal principles when assessing damages in such cases.
Counsel: Gomin Dayasiri with Palitha Gamage and Ms. Manoli Jinadasa for Defendant-Respondent-Appellant. Manohara de Silva, P.C. with R. Hathurusinghe and Hirosha Munasinghe for Plaintiff-Appellant-Respondent.

Parties: Fernando v. National Water Supply and Drainage Board and Others
Bench: Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; S.E. Wanasundera, PC. J; Buwaneka Aluwihare, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Scheme of recruitment — Qualification requirements — Master of Business Administration — Master of Public Management — Equivalent qualifications
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, a civil engineer employed by the National Water Supply and Drainage Board, applied for the post of Deputy General Manager (Commercial) following an advertisement published in January 2007. The Petitioner possessed a degree in Civil Engineering and a Master of Public Management from the Sri Lanka Institute of Development Administration. He participated in an interview on 20th April 2011 and secured the highest marks among the candidates. Despite his performance, the Board did not appoint the Petitioner to the position. The Board subsequently re-advertised the post in October 2011 for both internal and external candidates. The Petitioner contended that his Master of Public Management degree was equivalent to the Master of Business Administration qualification required by the scheme of recruitment. The Board maintained that the scheme of recruitment specifically required a Master of Business Administration and did not provide for equivalent or similar qualifications. Consequently, the Board argued that the Petitioner lacked the necessary qualifications for the post. Issues Whether the failure of the National Water Supply and Drainage Board to appoint the Petitioner to the post of Deputy General Manager (Commercial) violated his fundamental rights under Article 12(1) and Article 14(1)(g) of the Constitution. Whether the Petitioner possessed the requisite qualifications as stipulated in the scheme of recruitment for the post of Deputy General Manager (Commercial). Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 14(1)(g) Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Fundamental Rights Rule applied: Article 12(1) and Article 14(1)(g) of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The court evaluated whether the Board's decision to re-advertise the post and deny the Petitioner the appointment constituted discriminatory or arbitrary action. The court determined that the Board acted within its authority to enforce the specific requirements of the recruitment scheme. Because the Petitioner failed to meet the mandatory educational criteria, the Board's refusal to appoint him was not an infringement of his fundamental rights. Finding: The court found no violation of the Petitioner's fundamental rights. Issue 2: Requisite Qualifications Rule applied: The scheme of recruitment for the post of Deputy General Manager (Commercial). Court's reasoning: The court examined the recruitment scheme, which explicitly required a "Post Graduate Degree in Business Administration." The court noted that the scheme did not include language allowing for "equivalent" or "similar" qualifications. Although the Sri Lanka Institute of Development Administration confirmed the Petitioner's degree was equivalent to a Master of Business Administration, the court held that the Board was bound by the strict terms of the advertisement. The court reasoned that allowing an equivalent qualification might have unfairly excluded other potential candidates who did not apply because they lacked the specific degree requested. Finding: The Petitioner did not possess the specific requisite qualification mandated by the scheme of recruitment. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the determination that the Petitioner lacked the specific qualification required by the recruitment scheme directly negated the claim that the Board's refusal to appoint him was arbitrary or discriminatory. Because the Petitioner was not qualified under the strict terms of the advertisement, the Board's decision to re-advertise was a lawful administrative act, thereby precluding a finding of a fundamental rights violation. Conclusion The court held that the Board acted correctly in not appointing the Petitioner due to his lack of the specified post-graduate qualification. The application is dismissed with no costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Scheme of recruitment — Qualification requirements — Master of Business Administration — Master of Public Management — Equivalent qualifications

Parties: Banneheka and Others v. Thillakaratne and Others
Bench: Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Rohini Marasinghe, J.; Buwanaka Aluwihare, PC. J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Admission to school — Circular No.23/2013 — Proximity of schools — Misrepresentation of facts — Appeals and Objections Board — Cut off marks
Summary: Facts The 1st Petitioner submitted an application on behalf of his son, the 1A Petitioner, for admission to Grade 1 of Maliyadeva College under the category of children residing in close proximity to the school, pursuant to Ministry of Education Circular No. 23/2013. The Interview Board initially awarded the 1A Petitioner 85 marks, placing him on a temporary waiting list. Subsequently, the 1st Petitioner was summoned before the Appeals and Objections Board following objections regarding the proximity of other schools to his residence. The Appeals and Objections Board conducted a field inspection and determined that the 1st Petitioner had failed to disclose the existence of nearby schools, specifically Nissanka Vidyalaya. Consequently, the Board deducted five marks, reducing the 1A Petitioner's total to 80 marks. The 1st Petitioner signed a document acknowledging this decision. The final cut-off mark for admission under the distance category was 83.5, rendering the 1A Petitioner ineligible for admission. The Petitioners alleged that the 1A Petitioner was wrongfully denied admission and that other students were admitted unlawfully, prompting this fundamental rights application. Issues Whether the decision of the Appeals and Objections Board to deduct marks from the 1A Petitionerâ€™s application violated the Petitioners' fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the admission of the 12A-16A Respondents to Maliyadeva College was unlawful under the criteria set forth in Circular No. 23/2013. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Deduction of marks Rule applied: Ministry of Education Circular No. 23/2013. Court's reasoning: The court found that the 1st Petitioner had misled the Interview Board regarding the proximity of his residence to other schools. Upon investigation, the Appeals and Objections Board confirmed the existence of Nissanka Vidyalaya in close proximity to the 1A Petitioner's residence. The court noted that the 1st Petitioner accepted the Board's decision to reduce his marks to 80 by signing the relevant document. As the 1A Petitioner failed to meet the required cut-off mark of 83.5, the decision to deny admission was based on objective criteria rather than arbitrary action. Finding: There was no infringement of the Petitioners' fundamental rights. Issue 2: Lawfulness of other admissions Rule applied: Ministry of Education Circular No. 23/2013. Court's reasoning: The 1st Respondent provided a detailed explanation regarding the marks allotted to the 12A-16A Respondents. The court reviewed these submissions and determined that these students had secured marks above the established cut-off mark of 83.5. Finding: The 12A-16A Respondents were lawfully admitted to the school in accordance with the applicable circular. Interaction of Issues The courtâ€™s finding that the 1A Petitioner failed to meet the required cut-off mark due to his own non-disclosure of relevant facts directly informs the conclusion that the admission process was applied fairly. Because the 1A Petitioner did not meet the threshold, his claim of discrimination regarding the admission of other students (12A-16A Respondents) was rendered unsubstantiated, as those students had satisfied the objective criteria that the 1A Petitioner failed to meet. Conclusion The court held that there was no infringement of the fundamental rights of the Petitioners. The application is dismissed with no costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Admission to school — Circular No.23/2013 — Proximity of schools — Misrepresentation of facts — Appeals and Objections Board — Cut off marks

Parties: Sampath and Others v. Aviruppola and Others
Bench: Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Sarath De Abrew, J.; Anil Gooneratne, J.
Keywords: School admissions — Vishaka Vidyalaya — Fundamental rights application — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Proximity of residence — Circular P2 — Deed of gift — Deed of revocation — Electoral list — Interview marks — Administrative action — Selection procedure — Appeals Board
Summary: Facts The Petitioners, R.P.P.N. Sujeewa Sampath and his daughter, filed this application following the denial of the 2nd Petitioner's admission to Grade 1 of Vishaka Vidyalaya for the 2014 academic year. The Petitioners applied under the proximity of residence category, asserting that the 1st Petitioner had resided at the specified address for over twenty years. During the interview process, the school authorities declined to allocate marks for the title deed submitted by the 1st Petitioner because the property had been gifted to a relative in 2007. Although the 1st Petitioner subsequently executed a deed of revocation in 2013, the school authorities and the Appeals Board maintained the rejection of the application. The Petitioners challenged the school's decision, alleging that the selection process was flawed and that other successful applicants were admitted irregularly in violation of the Ministry of Education Circular P2. The Petitioners further contended that they were entitled to additional marks for electoral registration and other residency documents. The official Respondents argued that the Petitioners failed to disclose the transfer of the property in their affidavit and that the application lacked good faith. The Respondents maintained that the selection process was conducted in accordance with the established criteria and that the Petitioners failed to meet the required threshold for admission. Issues Whether the decision of the school authorities to deny the 2nd Petitioner admission to Grade 1 of Vishaka Vidyalaya constituted an infringement of Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the selection process, including the allocation of marks for residency and electoral registration, was conducted in an arbitrary or discriminatory manner in violation of Circular P2. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1), 12(2), 126. Cases Referred to: Buddhan Choudhury v. State of Bihar - [1955 AIR (SC) 191] Snowden v. Hughes - [1943 321 U.S. 1] Analysis Issue 1: Infringement of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution, which guarantees the right to equal protection of the law. Court's reasoning: The court held that the burden of proving eligibility based on the prescribed criteria rested with the applicant. The Petitioners failed to establish proximity of residence because the deed relied upon was not in the name of the 1st Petitioner's mother during the relevant period. The court found that the subsequent revocation of the deed occurred outside the scheme contemplated by Circular P2. Consequently, the authorities were justified in rejecting the application. Finding: No infringement of Article 12(1) was established, as the rejection was based on the Petitioners' failure to meet the required criteria rather than intentional or purposeful discrimination. Issue 2: Arbitrariness of the selection process Rule applied: Ministry of Education Circular P2. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the Petitioners' claims regarding electoral list marks were unsubstantiated, as the authorities correctly relied on available data from 2010 to 2012. Regarding the challenge to other admitted students, the court noted that those respondents provided lease agreements that were verified through site inspections, which the Petitioners could not trigger due to their failure to establish initial residency proof. The court emphasized that the Constitution does not guarantee immunity from merely erroneous action, and there was no evidence of hostile discrimination. Finding: The selection process was not shown to be arbitrary or discriminatory, and the court declined to interfere with the findings of the selection and appeals panels. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the Petitioners failed to meet the residency criteria (Issue 1) directly precluded them from challenging the validity of the admissions of other students (Issue 2), as the Petitioners lacked the standing and the evidentiary basis to claim unequal treatment. Conclusion The court held that the Petitioners failed to establish an infringement of their fundamental rights under Article 12 of the Constitution. The application is dismissed with no order as to costs. Keywords School admissions — Vishaka Vidyalaya — Fundamental rights application — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Proximity of residence — Circular P2 — Deed of gift — Deed of revocation — Electoral list — Interview marks — Administrative action — Selection procedure — Appeals Board

Parties: Sunil Pathirana alias Sinhala Pedige Karunaratne v. Hemalatha Edirisinghe and Others
Bench: S. Eva Wanasundera, PC. J.; B. Aluwihare, PC. J.; U. Abeyratne, J.
Keywords: Civil Procedure Code — Appeal — Notice of Appeal — Preliminary Objections — Material Prejudice — Substitution of Parties — Ejectment — Tenancy Rights — Judicial Discretion
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal concerning a tenancy dispute where the Plaintiff, Sunil Pathirana, sought to eject his tenant, Hemalatha Edirisinghe, and her sub-tenant, P.M. Premarathne, from the property due to unlawful sub-letting. The District Court of Kegalle ruled in favor of the Plaintiff, leading to an appeal by the 2nd Defendant, P.M. Premarathne, in the High Court of Civil Appeals, Kegalle. However, the appeal was dismissed on the grounds of procedural defects, primarily that the notice of appeal was improperly addressed and that a deceased person was named as a respondent. The 1st Defendant, Hemalatha Edirisinghe, had passed away during the proceedings, and her heirs were substituted as 1A and 1B Defendants. The High Court held that the appeal was invalid due to these issues, prompting the 2nd Defendant to seek relief from the Supreme Court. The Supreme Court was tasked with determining whether the High Court erred in its dismissal of the appeal based on these procedural grounds. Issues The Supreme Court identified several key legal questions: Whether the learned Judges of the High Court of Provinces Civil Appeals, Kegalle failed to consider the law relating to Section 755 of the Civil Procedure Code? Whether there was prejudice caused to any party due to the naming of the deceased 1st Defendant as a Respondent? Whether the High Court erred in law regarding the necessity of addressing the notice of appeal to the original Court? Whether the High Court failed to consider that the use of the word 'order' instead of 'judgment' did not materially affect the appeal? Whether the High Court erred in its factual and legal determinations? Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code Sections 754, 755, 759, 770. Cases Referred to: Jayasekera Vs. Lakmini - [2010] 1 SLR 41; Talayaratne Vs. Talayaratne - [1957] 61 NLR 112; Wimalasiri Vs. Premasiri - [2003] 3 SLR 330. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the learned Judges of the High Court of Provinces Civil Appeals, Kegalle failed to consider the law relating to Section 755 of the Civil Procedure Code? Rule applied: Section 755 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the procedural requirements for lodging an appeal as outlined in the Civil Procedure Code, particularly focusing on Section 755, which mandates that every notice of appeal must be distinctly written and signed, containing specific particulars. The Court noted that the High Court dismissed the appeal on the basis that the notice was not addressed to the original Court, which was the District Court of Mawanella. However, the Supreme Court opined that the essence of the notice being filed in the registry of the original Court within the stipulated time frame was paramount. The Court emphasized that the procedural lapse of addressing the notice incorrectly should not automatically invalidate the appeal, especially when the notice was filed in a timely manner. The Court further highlighted that the naming of the deceased 1st Defendant in the notice was a mere clerical error and should not bar the appellant from seeking relief. The judgment referenced the case of Jayasekera Vs. Lakmini, which established that minor procedural defects should not preclude an appeal if no material prejudice is demonstrated. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the High Court erred in dismissing the appeal based on these procedural grounds. Issue 2: Whether there was prejudice caused to any party due to the naming of the deceased 1st Defendant as a Respondent? Rule applied: The principle of material prejudice. Court's reasoning: The Court analyzed whether the procedural defects in naming the deceased as a Respondent caused any material prejudice to the parties involved. It was noted that the Plaintiff and the substituted Defendants were aware of the ongoing proceedings and had been substituted promptly in the District Court. The Supreme Court asserted that the mere lapse of not updating the caption in the appeal documents should not deprive the 2nd Defendant of his right to appeal. The Court reiterated that the focus should be on whether any party suffered material prejudice as a result of the procedural errors. Finding: The Supreme Court found that no material prejudice was caused to any party, thus allowing for the correction of the notice and petition of appeal. Interaction of Issues The issues presented were interrelated, as they all stemmed from the procedural handling of the appeal and the implications of naming parties correctly. The Supreme Court's analysis underscored the importance of judicial discretion in correcting procedural errors that do not materially affect the rights of the parties involved. Conclusion The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, set aside the High Court's order rejecting the notice and petition of appeal, and directed the High Court to hear the matter on its merits after correcting the procedural defects. The judgment emphasized the need for courts to exercise discretion in favor of justice, particularly in cases where procedural lapses do not result in material prejudice to any party. Keywords Civil Procedure Code — Appeal — Notice of Appeal — Preliminary Objections — Material Prejudice — Substitution of Parties — Ejectment — Tenancy Rights — Judicial Discretion
Counsel: Sunil Abeyratne for the 2nd Defendant-Appellant-Appellant. Dr. Sunil F.A. Cooray for Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent.

Parties: Geekiyanage Thanuja Sanjeewani Amarasinghe and Others v. Geekiyanage Nirosha Prasadini Kahandawarachchi and Chanaka Ravindra Kahandawarachchi
Bench: Priyasath Dep, P.C., J.; Sarath de Abrew, J; Priyantha Jayawardana, J.
Keywords: injunction — property rights — life interest — commission — damages — revisionary jurisdiction — High Court — District Court — Judicature Act — interim relief — legal principles — ownership — assessment of damages — status quo — funtus officio
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a dispute concerning property ownership and the rights of the parties involved. The Petitioners, consisting of Geekiyanage Thanuja Sanjeewani Amarasinghe, Geekiyanage Sardha Maheshini Amarasinghe, and Dona Kusuma Sardhalatha Amarasinghe, initiated legal proceedings in the District Court of Kuliyapitiya against the Respondents, Geekiyanage Nirosha Prasadini Kahandawarachchi and Chanaka Ravindra Kahandawarachchi. The Petitioners sought several reliefs, including declarations of ownership over specific lands, an order for the eviction of the Respondents from the property, and an interim injunction to prevent the Respondents from damaging the property, particularly by cutting down trees and obstructing the rights of the third Petitioner, who held a life interest in the land. The District Court initially refused to grant the interim injunction on December 11, 2009, and subsequently on December 16, 2010, despite the Petitioners' claims of ongoing damage to their property. The Petitioners argued that the Respondents were causing irreparable harm by cutting down trees and obstructing their rights, prompting them to seek a commission to assess the damages. The District Court allowed the commission, but the Respondents challenged this decision in the High Court of Civil Appeal, which ultimately dismissed their application. The Petitioners then sought to revise the District Court's refusal of the interim injunction based on new evidence from the commissioner's report, which indicated that the Respondents had indeed caused significant damage. Issues The primary issues in this case can be framed as follows: Whether the High Court of Civil Appeal erred in refusing to grant the interim relief sought by the Petitioners based on the commissioner's report. Whether the Petitioners were entitled to seek a revision of the District Court's order refusing the interim injunction after new evidence emerged. Whether the District Court had the jurisdiction to entertain a new application for an interim injunction after previously refusing it. Applicable Law Statutes: Judicature Act, No. 2 of 1978, Section 54; High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) (Amendment) Act, No. 54 of 2006; Article 127 of the Constitution of Sri Lanka. Cases Referred to: K. S. M. K. v. A. S. S. - [2005] 1 Sri LR 123; Fernando v. Fernando - [1990] 1 Sri LR 1. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the High Court of Civil Appeal erred in refusing to grant the interim relief sought by the Petitioners based on the commissioner's report. Rule applied: The jurisdiction to grant injunctions is provided under Section 54 of the Judicature Act, which allows courts to prevent acts that would cause injury to the plaintiff. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the circumstances under which the Petitioners sought interim relief after the District Court's refusal. The Court noted that the Petitioners had initially sought an injunction to prevent the Respondents from causing damage to their property, which was denied due to a lack of sufficient grounds. However, the emergence of new evidence from the commissioner's report, which indicated ongoing damage, raised questions about the appropriateness of the District Court's earlier refusal. The Court emphasized that the purpose of an interim injunction is to maintain the status quo and protect the subject matter of the case. The Court further elaborated that if fresh acts of damage were committed by the Respondents after the initial refusal, the Petitioners were entitled to invoke the jurisdiction of the District Court again. The Court referenced the principle of quia timet actions, which allows parties to seek relief in anticipation of future harm. Finding: The Supreme Court found that the High Court of Civil Appeal had erred in refusing the interim relief based on the commissioner's report, as the new evidence warranted a reconsideration of the Petitioners' application. Issue 2: Whether the Petitioners were entitled to seek a revision of the District Court's order refusing the interim injunction after new evidence emerged. Rule applied: The principle of revising court orders based on new evidence. Court's reasoning: The Court analyzed the procedural aspects of the Petitioners' application for revision. It noted that the Petitioners had filed their application based on the commissioner's report, which provided substantial evidence of the damage caused by the Respondents. The Court highlighted that the High Court's refusal to grant interim relief was based on the assumption that the Petitioners had not acted promptly after the District Court's refusal. However, the Court clarified that the emergence of new evidence justified the Petitioners' request for revision, as it directly related to the ongoing harm they were experiencing. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the Petitioners were justified in seeking a revision of the District Court's order, given the new evidence presented. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as they both concern the Petitioners' rights to seek relief based on new evidence that emerged after the initial refusal of the interim injunction. The Court's analysis of the first issue directly impacts the second, as the determination of whether the High Court erred in refusing the interim relief influences the validity of the Petitioners' revision application. Conclusion The Supreme Court ultimately upheld the Petitioners' right to seek interim relief based on the commissioner's report, emphasizing the importance of protecting property rights and preventing irreparable harm. The Court refused leave to appeal, affirming the High Court's decision while recognizing the need for the District Court to reconsider the Petitioners' application in light of the new evidence. The Court's ruling underscores the significance of judicial discretion in granting injunctions to safeguard the rights of parties involved in property disputes. Keywords: injunction — property rights — life interest — commission — damages — revisionary jurisdiction — High Court — District Court — Judicature Act — interim relief — legal principles — ownership — assessment of damages — status quo — funtus officio
Counsel: Rohan Sahabandu, P.C. for the Plaintiff-Petitioners; Chrishmal Warnasooriya for the Defendant-Respondents

Parties: Ranasinghe and Others v. Adhikari and Others
Bench: Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Rohini Marasinghe, J.; Anil Gooneratne, J.
Keywords: Partition decree — Prescriptive title — Adverse possession — Ouster — Inheritance — Testamentary case — Burden of proof — Rei vindication action — Evidence Ordinance — Trial De Novo
Summary: Facts The original Plaintiff, Malani Ranasinghe, filed an action in the District Court of Kuliyapitiya seeking a declaration of title to a land described as lot 2 of Meegahamulawatta alias Kongahamulawatta, along with damages and the ejectment of the original Defendant, Adhikari Appuhamilage Appuhamy. The Plaintiff asserted that the land was partitioned in 1954 and that the original Defendant occupied the land with the permission of the Plaintiff's grandfather. The original Defendant claimed title based on prescription. Following an initial judgment, the Court of Appeal set aside the decree and ordered a trial de novo, noting shortcomings in the evidence and the need for a proper commission to identify the land. During the second trial, both the original Plaintiff and the original Defendant passed away, leading to the substitution of their respective heirs. The Plaintiffs' party produced evidence including surveyor reports and depositions from the first trial under Section 33 of the Evidence Ordinance. The District Court ultimately dismissed the Defendant's claim of prescriptive title and entered judgment in favor of the Plaintiffs. The High Court (Civil Appeals) subsequently dismissed the appeal filed by the substituted Defendant. The matter reached the Supreme Court upon the granting of leave to appeal. Issues Whether the Civil Appellate High Court failed to consider if the District Court properly investigated the title of the original Plaintiff. Whether the trial de novo was conducted in compliance with the Court of Appeal's directions regarding the adoption of evidence from the previous abortive trial. Whether the Plaintiffs failed to establish title, thereby rendering the consideration of the Defendant's prescriptive claim unnecessary in a rei vindicatio action. Whether the courts below misdirected themselves by evaluating the nature of possession based on the original Defendant rather than the substituted Defendant. Applicable Law Statutes: Evidence Ordinance, Section 33 Prescription Ordinance, Section 3 Cases Referred to: Wanigaratne v. Juwanis Appuhamy - [65-nlr-167] Karunadasa v. Abdul Hameed - [60-nlr-352] S.S. Fernando v. The Queen - [55-nlr-392] King v. Sudu Banda - [47-nlr-183] Rajapakse v. Hendrick Singho - [61-nlr-32] Navaratne v. Jayatunge - [44-nlr-517] Naguda Marikar v. Mohammedu - [7-nlr-96] Sirajudeen v.
