Abbas - [1994/v2-sri-lr-365] Analysis Issue 1: Investigation of Title Rule applied: The burden of proof in a rei vindicatio action rests on the Plaintiff to prove title as against the Defendant. Court's reasoning: The court found that the District Court and the High Court adequately investigated the title of the Plaintiffs. The Plaintiffs produced documentary evidence, including deeds and partition decree documents, which were admitted without objection. The court noted that the trial judge correctly analyzed the devolution of title through inheritance and testamentary documents. Finding: The High Court did not fail to consider the investigation of title; the issue is answered in the negative. Issue 2: Compliance with Trial De Novo Rule applied: The Court of Appeal's order for a trial de novo. Court's reasoning: The Court of Appeal's previous order did not mandate the adoption of evidence from the abortive trial. It merely permitted parties to lead further oral or documentary evidence. The District Court acted within its discretion by allowing the production of depositions under Section 33 of the Evidence Ordinance. Finding: The District Court complied with the Court of Appeal's directions. Issue 3: Necessity of Proving Title Rule applied: Rei vindicatio principles requiring the Plaintiff to establish title. Court's reasoning: The court affirmed that while a Plaintiff must prove title, the Plaintiffs in this case successfully established their paper title through partition decrees and subsequent deeds. Consequently, the court was required to address the Defendant's prescriptive claim. Finding: The Plaintiffs established their title, and the court correctly proceeded to examine the prescriptive claim. Issue 4: Nature of Possession Rule applied: Section 3 of the Prescription Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The court held that the Defendant failed to prove adverse possession. Evidence showed the original Defendant entered with permission, and no evidence of "ouster" was provided to convert that permissive possession into adverse possession. The court noted that the substituted Defendant failed to move to have the original Defendant's evidence read under Section 33 of the Evidence Ordinance. Finding: The courts below correctly evaluated the nature of possession, and the prescriptive claim was rightly rejected. Interaction of Issues The findings on title and prescription are interdependent. Because the Plaintiffs successfully established paper title, the burden shifted to the Defendant to prove prescriptive title. The failure to prove ouster or adverse possession, combined with the valid establishment of the Plaintiffs' title, necessitated the dismissal of the appeal. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed, and the judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court is affirmed. There shall be no costs. Keywords Partition decree — Prescriptive title — Adverse possession — Ouster — Inheritance — Testamentary case — Burden of proof — Rei vindication action — Evidence Ordinance — Trial De Novo

Parties: Sees Lanka (Private) Limited v. Board of Investment of Sri Lanka
Bench: K. Sripavan, C.J.; C. Ekanayake, J.; P. Dep, P.C., J.
Keywords: Power of Attorney — locus standi — jurisdiction — Civil Procedure Code — Companies Act — recognized agent — arbitration clause — leave to appeal — Supreme Court jurisdiction — legal representation — preliminary objections — legal authority — corporate law — statutory interpretation — procedural law
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an application for leave to appeal to the Supreme Court concerning the jurisdiction of the District Court in a matter involving Sees Lanka (Private) Limited and the Board of Investment of Sri Lanka. The Plaintiff, Sees Lanka, represented by its Power of Attorney holder, Don Lalith Hilary Ganlath, filed a plaint in the District Court, asserting its rights under an agreement with the Board of Investment. The Defendant raised preliminary objections regarding the maintainability of the application, arguing that the Plaintiff was not properly registered under the Companies Act and lacked locus standi due to the Power of Attorney being for a company incorporated in Sri Lanka. The Plaintiff's address was outside the local limits of the District Court's jurisdiction, which further complicated the matter. The case was initially heard in 2011, with the Defendant later withdrawing one of its preliminary objections, focusing instead on the second objection regarding the Plaintiff's standing. The Court had to determine whether the Plaintiff had the right to institute the action and whether it was properly represented before the Court. Issues The primary issues framed for consideration were: 1. Whether the Plaintiff-Respondent-Petitioner had locus standi to maintain the action in the District Court given the Power of Attorney was for a company incorporated in Sri Lanka. 2. Whether the Plaintiff was properly before the Supreme Court based on the Power of Attorney. Applicable Law Statutes: - Companies Act No. 7 of 2007 - Civil Procedure Code - Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 Cases Referred to: - Paul Pereira Vs. Chelliah - Udeshi vs. Mather - Jalaldeen Vs. Rajaratnam - Science House (Ceylon) Limited Vs. IPCA Laboratories Private Ltd. - Lanka Estates Agency Ltd. Vs. Corea - Alia Markar Vs. Natchia - Segu Mohamadu vs. Govinden Kangany Analysis Issue 1: Locus Standi Rule applied: The principle of locus standi in relation to corporate entities and the validity of Power of Attorney. Court's reasoning: The Court examined the nature of the Power of Attorney and its implications for the Plaintiff's ability to maintain the action. It noted that the Plaintiff had claimed to be a body corporate duly incorporated under Sri Lankan law, yet the Defendant contested this assertion based on the Power of Attorney's validity. The Court referenced the Civil Procedure Code, which stipulates that a Power of Attorney must be executed by a recognized agent. The Court emphasized that the Defendant's initial objection to jurisdiction was based on the arbitration clause in the agreement, which did not specifically challenge the Power of Attorney at the outset. The Court cited the case of Paul Pereira Vs. Chelliah, highlighting that objections to jurisdiction must be clearly articulated and cannot be raised in a piecemeal manner. The Court concluded that the Defendant's failure to object to the Power of Attorney at the earliest opportunity precluded it from later raising this issue. Finding: The Court found that the Plaintiff had locus standi to maintain the action in the District Court. Issue 2: Proper Representation Rule applied: The requirements for legal representation under the Civil Procedure Code and the Companies Act. Court's reasoning: The Court scrutinized the Power of Attorney to determine whether it conferred sufficient authority for the Plaintiff to be represented in the Supreme Court. It noted that the Power of Attorney was a special one, allowing the holder to act on behalf of the company in specific matters, including legal proceedings. However, the Court also referenced the definition of a recognized agent under the Civil Procedure Code, which includes those holding general Powers of Attorney from parties not resident within the local limits of the jurisdiction. The Court concluded that since both the Plaintiff and the Power of Attorney holder were within the jurisdiction, the Power of Attorney could not be used to authorize the holder to act as a recognized agent. The Court cited previous cases that established the necessity for a recognized agent to be outside the jurisdiction for a Power of Attorney to be valid in this context. Finding: The Court upheld the objection regarding the Plaintiff's representation, concluding that the Plaintiff was not properly represented before the Supreme Court. Interaction of Issues The issues of locus standi and proper representation are interlinked, as the validity of the Power of Attorney directly impacts the Plaintiff's ability to maintain the action. The Court's analysis highlighted that the failure to properly establish the Power of Attorney's validity undermined the Plaintiff's standing in both the District Court and the Supreme Court. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the leave to appeal application, concluding that the Plaintiff was not properly represented and lacked the necessary locus standi to pursue the appeal. The Court emphasized the importance of adhering to procedural requirements and the implications of corporate representation under Sri Lankan law.
Counsel: Maithri Wickremasinghe, P.C. with Rakitha Jayatunge for Plaintiff-Respondent-Petitioner. Hiran De Alwis with Kalpa Virajith and A. Ranasinghe for Defendant-Petitioner-Respondent.

Parties: Chandraratne v. Balalla and Others
Bench: Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Upaly Abeyratne J, J.; Anil Gooneratne J, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Provincial public service — Promotion to Librarian Grade III — Disruption of service — Reinstatement — Qualifications for promotion — Scheme of recruitment — Acting Librarians — Discrimination
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, J. A. Lionel Chandraratne, was employed as a Library Assistant in the North Western Provincial Public Service. He alleged that he served for approximately 30 years without being promoted to the post of Librarian Grade III, despite possessing the necessary qualifications. The Petitioner contended that his career progression was hindered by political animosity and an abortive disciplinary inquiry, which led to two disruptions in his service. During these periods, he was re-appointed as a new employee to lower positions. The Petitioner argued that his fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution were violated when the 6th Respondent appointed 11 acting Librarians to the post of Librarian Grade III in 2008. He claimed these individuals were junior to him and less qualified. The Petitioner noted that these 11 appointments followed the withdrawal of a High Court writ application filed by nine of those individuals, which had challenged the eligibility criteria for the post. The Petitioner sought a direction from the court to be appointed to the post of Librarian Grade III, asserting that he was similarly circumstanced to those who received the appointments. The Respondents maintained that the Petitioner did not meet the recruitment criteria, was not similarly circumstanced to the 11 appointees, and that his claims were time-barred. Issues Whether the Petitionerâ€™s fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution were violated by the failure to promote him to the post of Librarian Grade III. Whether the Petitioner was "similarly circumstanced" to the 11 individuals who were appointed to the post of Librarian Grade III in 2008. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Cases Referred to: C.W. Mackie & Co. Ltd. v. H. Mologoda, Commissioner General of Inland Revenue - [1986/v1-sri-lr-300] Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Fundamental Rights Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution and the principle established in C.W. Mackie & Co. Ltd. v. H. Mologoda regarding the burden of proof in discrimination claims. Court's reasoning: The court held that to succeed in a claim of discrimination under Article 12(1), a petitioner must prove that they were treated differently from others who were similarly circumstanced without a reasonable basis. The court found that the Petitioner failed to establish that he met the requirements of the applicable recruitment scheme (2R1). Furthermore, the court noted that the Petitionerâ€™s allegations of political animosity were mere assertions unsupported by evidence of malice. The court emphasized that it cannot grant promotions outside of established recruitment schemes or based purely on sympathy. Finding: The Petitioner failed to establish a violation of his fundamental rights. Issue 2: Similarly Circumstanced Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The court examined the status of the 11 individuals appointed in 2008 and found they were all holding the post of "Acting Librarian Grade III" at the time of their appointment, which was a result of a settlement in a High Court case. In contrast, the Petitioner held the post of "Library Assistant." The court concluded that the Petitioner was not in the same category or position as the 11 appointees, and therefore, the principle of equal treatment did not apply to him in the same manner. Finding: The Petitioner was not similarly circumstanced to the 11 individuals who received the appointments. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are interdependent. Because the Petitioner was not "similarly circumstanced" to the 11 appointees, he could not satisfy the second limb of the test for discrimination under Article 12(1). Consequently, the failure to prove he was similarly circumstanced directly resulted in the failure to establish a violation of his fundamental rights. Conclusion The application of the Petitioner is dismissed. No costs are ordered. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Provincial public service — Promotion to Librarian Grade III — Disruption of service — Reinstatement — Qualifications for promotion — Scheme of recruitment — Acting Librarians — Discrimination

Parties: Anura Mangala v. The Inspector General of Police and Others
Bench: Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Priyantha Jayawardena P.C., J; Anil Gooneratne, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Time limit for application — Delay in filing — Criminal prosecution vs. disciplinary proceedings — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Article 13(3) of the Constitution — Article 13(5) of the Constitution — Establishment Code — Disciplinary inquiry — Termination of services
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, D.M. Anura Mangala, joined the police force in 1988 and was confirmed as a Police Constable in 2006. In 2007, the Petitioner was arrested on charges of bribery and corruption and subsequently interdicted. A disciplinary inquiry was conducted by the Police Department, resulting in the Petitioner being found guilty and his services terminated on August 21, 2008. This order was communicated to the Petitioner on September 25, 2009. The Petitioner appealed the termination to the National Police Commission, which rejected the appeal, and the decision was communicated to him on May 24, 2010. Separately, the Commission to Investigate Allegations of Bribery and Corruption instituted criminal proceedings against the Petitioner in the High Court. On July 2, 2013, the High Court acquitted the Petitioner after the prosecution indicated it did not wish to proceed with the case. Following his acquittal, the Petitioner sought reinstatement through various representations to the authorities, which were unsuccessful. The Petitioner filed the present Fundamental Rights application on September 16, 2014, seeking to set aside the disciplinary orders and requesting reinstatement. The Respondents raised a preliminary objection, contending that the application was filed out of time. Issues Whether the Fundamental Rights application was filed within the time limit prescribed by law. Whether the acquittal of the Petitioner in criminal proceedings in the High Court necessitates the setting aside of the disciplinary order and his reinstatement. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 12(1), 13(3), 13(5) Establishment Code, Sections 28.3, 28.6, 28.7 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Timeliness of the Application Rule applied: The Court applied the constitutional requirement that Fundamental Rights applications must be filed within one month of the date of the executive or administrative act complained of. Court's reasoning: The Petitioner sought to challenge disciplinary orders dated August 21, 2008, and September 25, 2009. The Court noted that the Petitioner had waited several years to file the application, far exceeding the one-month period mandated for invoking the Court's jurisdiction. The Court held that the application was filed well outside the statutory time limit. Finding: The preliminary objection was upheld, and the application was found to be time-barred. Issue 2: Impact of Criminal Acquittal on Disciplinary Proceedings Rule applied: Sections 28.6 and 28.7 of the Establishment Code. Court's reasoning: The Court reasoned that criminal prosecutions and disciplinary proceedings are independent of one another. Under the Establishment Code, an acquittal by a court of law does not constitute grounds to set aside a disciplinary order if there was sufficient material to support the disciplinary findings. The Court further clarified that Articles 13(3) and 13(5) of the Constitution regarding fair trial and the presumption of innocence do not conflict with disciplinary proceedings conducted under the Establishment Code. Finding: The High Court acquittal had no legal bearing on the validity of the prior disciplinary termination. Interaction of Issues The finding that the application was time-barred rendered the substantive merits of the Petitioner's challenge to the disciplinary proceedings moot. The Court's determination that the disciplinary process was independent of the criminal proceedings reinforced the conclusion that the Petitioner's reliance on the 2013 acquittal did not provide a valid basis to bypass the strict one-month time limit for filing the application. Conclusion The preliminary objection is upheld, and the application is dismissed. The Court held that the application was filed out of time and that the criminal acquittal did not invalidate the disciplinary termination. Keywords Fundamental rights — Time limit for application — Delay in filing — Criminal prosecution vs. disciplinary proceedings — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Article 13(3) of the Constitution — Article 13(5) of the Constitution — Establishment Code — Disciplinary inquiry — Termination of services

Parties: Lanka Banku Sevaka Sangamaya (On behalf of EAA Dayananda) v. Peoples Bank
Judgment by: Sisira J De Abrew J, J.
Bench: Eva Wanasundera PC, J; Sisira J De Abrew J, J.; Priyantha Jayawardene PC, J
Keywords: Industrial Disputes Act — Termination of services — Misconduct — Dishonesty — Compensation — Bank employees — Circulars — Evaluation of evidence
Summary: Facts The workman was employed as a clerk at the Peoples Bank for twenty-five years. The bank terminated his services following an inquiry into his conduct regarding the issuance of cheques from his personal account without sufficient funds. The workman had previously been warned and disciplined by the bank for similar conduct in 2001, which included the closure of his current account and a transfer to a remote station. Despite these measures, the workman subsequently issued cheques for large sums to third parties from a closed account. The workman challenged his termination before the Labour Tribunal. The Labour Tribunal held that the termination was justified but ordered the bank to pay the workman compensation in the amount of Rs. 584,425/25. The Peoples Bank appealed this order to the Provincial High Court, which affirmed the Labour Tribunal's decision. The Peoples Bank then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the order of the Provincial High Court and the Labour Tribunal awarding compensation to the workman, where the termination of services was held to be justified due to misconduct, is erroneous in law. Whether the Provincial High Court erred in law in the evaluation of evidence and failed to consider the totality of the evidence. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act (as amended) Cases Referred to: Saleem v. Hatton National Bank - [1994/v3-sri-lr-409] Somawathie v. Baksons - [79-nlr-204] National Savings Bank v. Ceylon Bank Employees Union - [1982/v2-sri-lr-629] Board of Governors for Zahira College v. Naina Mohamad - [1999/v2-sri-lr-309] Alexander v. Gnanam - [2002/v1-sri-lr-274] Analysis Issue 1: Entitlement to compensation for misconduct Rule applied: The court applied the principle that employees of banking institutions are held to a high standard of honesty and that compensation should not be awarded where an employeeâ€™s conduct is dishonest and undermines public confidence in the banking system. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that while the workman's actions were private transactions, his dishonest conduct in issuing cheques from a closed account and without sufficient funds directly affected the reputation of the bank. The court distinguished this case from precedents where compensation was awarded, noting that in those instances, there was an absence of dishonesty. The court emphasized that awarding compensation to an employee found guilty of such misconduct would encourage further indiscipline. Finding: The workman is not entitled to compensation as his dishonest conduct justified his dismissal and violated the bank's disciplinary circulars. Issue 2: Evaluation of evidence Rule applied: The court applied the standard of appellate review regarding the evaluation of evidence and the necessity of considering the totality of the evidence. Court's reasoning: The court found that the lower tribunals failed to properly weigh the evidence of the workman's prior disciplinary record. Contrary to the claim that the workman had an unblemished record, the evidence showed he had been previously warned and sanctioned for similar misconduct in 2001. The court concluded that the lower courts failed to adequately consider the gravity of the workmanâ€™s repeated dishonest acts. Finding: The Provincial High Court erred in its evaluation of the evidence by failing to properly account for the workman's history of misconduct. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the workmanâ€™s conduct was dishonest (Issue 1) directly informs the conclusion that the lower courts failed to properly evaluate the evidence (Issue 2). By failing to recognize the severity of the workman's repeated dishonest acts, the lower courts erroneously concluded that compensation was warranted. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgments of the Labour Tribunal and the Provincial High Court awarding compensation to the workman are set aside. Keywords Industrial Disputes Act — Termination of services — Misconduct — Dishonesty — Compensation — Bank employees — Circulars — Evaluation of evidence

Parties: Alli Company (Pvt) Ltd. v. Mohamad Noohu Abdul Salam
Bench: Chandra Ekanayake Acting C J; Priyasath Dep PCJ; Sisira J De Abrew J
Keywords: Intellectual Property Act — Trade Mark Infringement — Unfair Competition — Honest Concurrent User — Passing Off — Consumer Confusion — Registered Trade Mark — Trade Mark Registration — Commercial Law — Sri Lankan Law
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Appellant, Alli Company (Pvt) Ltd., is the registered owner of trade marks No.24094 and No.56213, which are associated with their products 'ALLI BEEDI' and 'Alli Suruttu' (Alli cigars). The Plaintiff-Appellant has been marketing these products for over 45 years and 25 years respectively. The Defendant-Respondent, Mohamad Noohu Abdul Salam, is the registered owner of trade mark No.44765, under which he sells his products 'Apli Beedi' and 'Apli Suruttu' for the last 22 years. The Plaintiff-Appellant contends that the Defendant-Respondent's products are deceptively similar to theirs, leading to potential consumer confusion. The Plaintiff-Appellant sought a permanent injunction against the Defendant-Respondent for using similar trade marks and packaging that could mislead consumers into believing they are purchasing the Plaintiff-Appellant's products. The Plaintiff-Appellant argued that the Defendant-Respondent's actions violate Section 121 of the Intellectual Property Act No.36 of 2003, which protects registered trade marks, and also constitutes unfair competition under Section 160 of the same Act. However, the Plaintiff-Appellant did not have registered trade marks for the specific packaging and labels they claimed were infringed. The learned trial judge dismissed the Plaintiff-Appellant's action, leading to this appeal. Issues Whether the Plaintiff-Appellant has established a cause of action under Section 121 of the Intellectual Property Act No.36 of 2003. Whether the Defendant-Respondent's use of the trade mark 'Apli' constitutes unfair competition under Section 160 of the Act. Whether the defence of 'honest concurrent user' is applicable in this case. Applicable Law Statutes: Intellectual Property Act No.36 of 2003 Cases Referred to: Cornish on Intellectual Property - [4th edition]; P Narayanan, Law of Trade Marks and Passing Off - [5th edition] Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Plaintiff-Appellant has established a cause of action under Section 121 of the Intellectual Property Act No.36 of 2003. Rule applied: Section 121 of the Intellectual Property Act No.36 of 2003. Court's reasoning: The court examined the Plaintiff-Appellant's claims regarding their trade marks and the alleged infringement by the Defendant-Respondent. The Plaintiff-Appellant argued that their trade marks were being infringed due to the similarity of the Defendant-Respondent's products. However, the court noted that the Plaintiff-Appellant had not registered the specific packaging and labels (P11, P12, P13, and P14) that they claimed were infringed. The court emphasized that without registration, the Plaintiff-Appellant could not claim relief under Section 121. The learned trial judge's conclusion that the Plaintiff-Appellant had not established a cause of action under this section was upheld by the Supreme Court. Finding: The Plaintiff-Appellant did not establish a cause of action under Section 121 of the Act. Issue 2: Whether the Defendant-Respondent's use of the trade mark 'Apli' constitutes unfair competition under Section 160 of the Act. Rule applied: Section 160 of the Intellectual Property Act No.36 of 2003. Court's reasoning: The court acknowledged that the learned trial judge found the Defendant-Respondent guilty of unfair competition. However, the judge also concluded that the Defendant-Respondent was entitled to the defence of 'honest concurrent user.' The Plaintiff-Appellant's counsel contended that this defence is not recognized under the Intellectual Property Act. The court analyzed the principles of 'honest concurrent user' as established in English law, which is applicable in Sri Lanka due to the silence of the local law on this matter. The court noted that both brands, 'Alli' and 'Apli,' have been in the market for over 22 years, and consumers are likely to be aware of both brands. The court concluded that the Defendant-Respondent's long-standing use of the 'Apli' mark justified the application of the 'honest concurrent user' defence, as consumers would not be misled given the established presence of both brands in the market. Finding: The Defendant-Respondent is entitled to the defence of 'honest concurrent user.' Interaction of Issues The issues of whether the Plaintiff-Appellant established a cause of action under Section 121 and whether the Defendant-Respondent's actions constituted unfair competition are interrelated. The lack of registration for the Plaintiff-Appellant's specific trade marks weakened their claim under Section 121, which in turn influenced the court's analysis of unfair competition under Section 160. The court's acceptance of the 'honest concurrent user' defence further complicated the Plaintiff-Appellant's position, as it indicated that even if there was some level of unfair competition, the Defendant-Respondent's established use of his mark mitigated the Plaintiff-Appellant's claims. Conclusion The Supreme Court upheld the judgment of the learned trial judge, dismissing the appeal with costs. The court found no merit in the appeal, affirming the trial judge's conclusions regarding both the lack of a cause of action under Section 121 and the applicability of the 'honest concurrent user' defence. The Plaintiff-Appellant's failure to register their trade marks for the specific packaging and labels claimed as infringed ultimately led to the dismissal of their claims. Keywords Intellectual Property Act — Trade Mark Infringement — Unfair Competition — Honest Concurrent User — Passing Off — Consumer Confusion — Registered Trade Mark — Trade Mark Registration — Commercial Law — Sri Lankan Law
Counsel: Basheer Ahamed with M Riyaz and MS Sonali Silva for the Plaintiff-Appellant. Murshid Maharoof with Shamir Zavahir and Muzeena Kaleel for the Defendant-Respondent

Parties: Perera and another v. Kuruppu
Bench: Chandra Ekanayake, J.; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.; Anil Gooneratne, J.
Keywords: Application for leave to appeal — Specific prayer for leave — Civil Procedure Code — Section 757(1) — Section 758 — Amendment of petition — Technicalities — Court of Justice
Summary: Facts The Defendant-Appellant filed an application for leave to appeal in the Civil Appellate High Court against an order of the District Court dated 12.5.2010. The Plaintiff-Respondent raised a preliminary objection, contending that the application should be dismissed because it lacked a specific prayer for leave to appeal. Prior to the inquiry into this objection, the Defendant-Appellant filed an amended petition containing the specific prayer without seeking the court's permission. The High Court refused to consider the amended petition and, relying on the precedent in Sirinivaso Thero v. Sudassi Thero, dismissed the original petition of appeal on the basis that it lacked a specific prayer for leave to appeal. Issues Whether a specific prayer for leave to appeal is a mandatory requirement in an application for leave to appeal under the Civil Procedure Code. Whether the High Court erred in law by dismissing the application for leave to appeal despite the petition and affidavit clearly indicating that the petitioner was seeking such leave. Applicable Law Statutes: The High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 54 of 1998 Civil Procedure Code, Section 757(1) Civil Procedure Code, Section 758 Cases Referred to: Sirinivaso Thero v. Sudassi Thero - [63-nlr-31] Vellupillai v. Chairman Urban District Council - [39-nlr-464] Analysis Issue 1: Necessity of a specific prayer Rule applied: Section 757(1) and Section 758 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: Section 757(1) requires an application for leave to appeal to be made by petition, supported by affidavit, and to contain particulars required by Section 758. Section 758 requires the petition to contain a demand in the form of relief claimed. The court reasoned that the requirement is satisfied if the petition contains a clear demand for the relief sought, rather than a rigid, specific formulaic prayer. Finding: A specific prayer is not a mandatory requirement if the petition otherwise contains a clear demand for the relief of leave to appeal. Issue 2: Dismissal based on technicality Rule applied: The principle that the Supreme Court is a Court of Justice and not an academy of law, as established in Vellupillai v. Chairman Urban District Council. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the Defendant-Appellantâ€™s original petition and affidavit explicitly moved the court to grant leave to appeal. Dismissing the petition on the ground of a missing specific prayer, when the intent was clearly expressed, was deemed a highly technical approach that contradicts the court's role as a forum for justice. The court further noted that the filing of an unauthorized amended petition did not constitute an abandonment of the original petition. Finding: The High Court erred in law by dismissing the application on technical grounds. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are interdependent; the determination that the petition complied with the substantive requirements of the Civil Procedure Code (Issue 1) directly informs the conclusion that the dismissal was an improper application of technical procedure (Issue 2). Together, they establish that the court must prioritize the substance of the relief sought over formalistic defects. Conclusion The judgment of the High Court dated 3.12.2010 is set aside. The High Court is directed to consider the original Petition of Appeal filed by the Defendant-Appellant. Keywords Application for leave to appeal — Specific prayer for leave — Civil Procedure Code — Section 757(1) — Section 758 — Amendment of petition — Technicalities — Court of Justice

Parties: Jayathilake v. Abeyweera
Bench: Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Upaly Abeyrathne, J; Anil Goonerathne, J
Keywords: Appeal from Civil Appellate High Court — District Court judgment — Transfer of land — Deeds — Loan transaction — Prescriptive rights — Evidence Ordinance — Attestation of documents — Burden of proof — Beneficial interest — Trust Ordinance — Section 83 — Attendant circumstances
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent initiated an action in the District Court of Mawanella seeking a declaration of title to a land known as Paranawatte and the ejectment of the Defendant-Appellant-Petitioner. The Plaintiff claimed title through a series of deeds, starting with the Defendant transferring the property to a third party, followed by subsequent transfers, ultimately resulting in the Plaintiff purchasing the land from one Somalatha. The Defendant-Appellant-Petitioner contended that the transactions, including the deed he executed, were not absolute transfers but were instead loan transactions where the deeds served as security. The trial court examined evidence regarding the execution of deeds P1 through P4. The Defendant admitted to signing deed P4 as a witness. Evidence was led that the Plaintiff had taken possession of the land upon purchase, although this possession was later disturbed. The Defendant argued that the transactions were shams intended to secure loans, yet he failed to produce the vendor, Somalatha, to testify regarding the nature of the transactions. The District Court found in favor of the Plaintiff, and the Civil Appellate High Court of Kegalle Province subsequently dismissed the Defendant's appeal. The Defendant then appealed to the Court of Appeal. Issues Whether the deeds P1 to P4 constituted absolute transfers of title or were merely security for loan transactions. Whether the execution of the deeds was sufficiently proved in accordance with the Evidence Ordinance. Whether the Defendant-Appellant-Petitioner established the existence of a constructive trust under Section 83 of the Trust Ordinance. Applicable Law Statutes: Evidence Ordinance Trust Ordinance Cases Referred to: [1993/v1-sri-lr-119] [20-nlr-332] [1955/v1-all-england-reports-326] [20-nlr-282] Velupillai v. Sivakamipillai - [1907/v1-a.c.r-180] Marian v. Jesuthasan - [59-nlr-348] Fernando v. Ceylon Tea Co. Ltd. - [3-scr-35] De Silva v. Isan Appu - [31-nlr-225] [55-nlr-116] [70-nlr-313] Piyadasa v. Binduva Alias Gunasekera - [1992/v1-sri-lr-108] W. Branchy Appu v. J. Poidohamy - [2-br-rep-221] Perera v. Fernando - [17-nlr-486] Adaicappa Chetty v. Caruppen Chetty - [22-nlr-417] [62-nlr-559] Penderlan v. Penderlan - [50-nlr-513] Analysis Issue 1: Nature of the transactions Rule applied: The law regarding the burden of proof in civil cases and the interpretation of notarial deeds. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the deeds were absolute on their face and contained no conditions suggesting they were loan transactions. The Defendant, who was a witness to the final deed, failed to produce the vendor, Somalatha, to support his claim that the transfers were shams. The court held that the Defendant failed to discharge the burden of proving that the transactions were anything other than what they purported to be. Finding: The transactions were absolute transfers of title. Issue 2: Proof of execution Rule applied: Sections 68 and 70 of the Evidence Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the Defendant admitted to signing the deeds as a witness and was the original transferor of the land. Under Section 70 of the Evidence Ordinance, an admission of execution by a party to an attested document dispenses with the need for further proof. The court found that the Defendant's own admissions in court satisfied the requirements for proving the execution of the documents. Finding: The deeds were duly proved. Issue 3: Constructive trust Rule applied: Section 83 of the Trust Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The court held that to establish a constructive trust, the transferor must prove that the parties did not intend to dispose of the beneficial interest. The Defendant failed to provide evidence of attendant circumstances to support this claim, and the court noted that the Defendant's mere assertion of a loan transaction was insufficient to overcome the legal effect of the registered deeds. Finding: No constructive trust was established. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing; the failure to prove the transactions were loans (Issue 1) and the failure to establish a constructive trust (Issue 3) are consistent with the valid execution of the deeds (Issue 2). The court's reliance on the Defendant's own admissions regarding the deeds effectively precluded his arguments regarding the nature of the transactions. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed without costs. The judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court is affirmed. Keywords Appeal from Civil Appellate High Court — District Court judgment — Transfer of land — Deeds — Loan transaction — Prescriptive rights — Evidence Ordinance — Attestation of documents — Burden of proof — Beneficial interest — Trust Ordinance — Section 83 — Attendant circumstances

Parties: Samarakoon Mudiyanselage Jayathilake v. Balahinna Arachchige Sarath Abeyweera
Bench: Priyasath Dep P.C., J.; Upaly Abeyrathne J.; Anil Goonerathne J.
Keywords: land title — ejectment — loan transaction — deeds — possession — evidence — burden of proof — trust — prescriptive rights — attestation — Evidence Ordinance — Civil Procedure — appeal
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a dispute concerning the title to a piece of land known as 'Paranawatte'. The Plaintiff-Respondent, Balahinna Arachchige Sarath Abeyweera, initiated legal proceedings in the District Court of Mawanella seeking a declaration of title to the land and an order to eject the Defendant-Appellant, Samarakoon Mudiyanselage Jayathilake, from the property. The Plaintiff claimed that he had purchased the land from Somalatha, who had previously leased the land to the Defendant. The Defendant contended that the transactions involving the land were not outright transfers but rather loan transactions, asserting that the deeds executed were merely security for loans. The trial proceeded with several admissions and issues, including the identification of the land and the nature of the transactions. The Plaintiff produced several deeds (P1 to P4) to substantiate his claim, while the Defendant maintained that these deeds were not indicative of ownership but rather loans. The District Court ruled in favor of the Plaintiff, leading to an appeal by the Defendant to the Civil Appellate High Court, which upheld the lower court's decision. The Defendant then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues The primary issues raised in this appeal include: Whether the transactions involving the land were outright transfers or merely loan transactions. Whether the Plaintiff had established his title to the land based on the evidence presented. Whether the Defendant's claims regarding the nature of the deeds were credible and supported by evidence. Applicable Law Statutes: Evidence Ordinance, Trust Ordinance Cases Referred to: Velupillai V. Sivakamipillai - [1907(1) A.C.R 180]; Marian Vs. Jesuthasan - [59 NLR 348]; Fernando Vs. Ceylon Tea Co. Ltd. - [1894 3 SCR 35]; Piyadasa Vs. Binduva Alias Gunasekera - [1992(1) SLR 108]; W. Branchy Appu V. J. Poidohamy - [1902 2 Br. Rep 221]; Penderlan Vs. Pendarlan - [50 NLR 513]. Analysis Issue 1: Nature of Transactions Rule applied: The distinction between outright transfers and loan transactions. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court meticulously examined the evidence presented, particularly focusing on the deeds (P1 to P4) and the testimonies of both parties. The Court noted that the Defendant had signed as a witness to the deed (P4) through which the Plaintiff claimed ownership. The Court emphasized that the deeds did not contain any conditions suggesting they were loan transactions. The Defendant's assertion that the deeds were merely security for loans was not substantiated by any credible evidence. The Court highlighted that the Defendant failed to produce Somalatha, the vendor, to corroborate his claims regarding the nature of the transactions. The Court reiterated that the burden of proof lay with the Defendant to establish that the transactions were not outright transfers. The evidence presented by the Plaintiff, including the clear identification of the land and the circumstances surrounding the transactions, was deemed sufficient to affirm the Plaintiff's title. The Court also referenced the provisions of the Evidence Ordinance, particularly Section 68, which pertains to the attestation of deeds. The Defendant's admission of signing the deeds further reinforced the validity of the transactions. Finding: The Court concluded that the transactions were indeed outright transfers and dismissed the Defendant's claims regarding the nature of the deeds. Issue 2: Establishment of Title Rule applied: The necessity of proving ownership through credible evidence. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court underscored the importance of establishing title through clear and convincing evidence. The Plaintiff's evidence, including the deeds and the survey plan, was meticulously analyzed. The Court noted that the Plaintiff had gone into possession of the land following the purchase and that the Defendant's possession was disturbed. The Court found that the Plaintiff had adequately demonstrated his title through the chain of ownership established by the deeds. The Defendant's failure to provide compelling evidence to support his claims of a loan transaction further weakened his position. The Court reiterated that the trial court had correctly assessed the evidence and arrived at a conclusion based on the balance of probabilities. Finding: The Plaintiff successfully established his title to the land based on the evidence presented. Interaction of Issues The issues of the nature of the transactions and the establishment of title are interrelated. The Defendant's claims regarding the transactions being loans directly impacted the assessment of the Plaintiff's title. The Court's analysis of the deeds and the testimonies revealed that the Defendant's assertions lacked credibility, thereby reinforcing the Plaintiff's claim to ownership. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal, affirming the judgments of the lower courts. The Court found no merit in the Defendant's claims and upheld the Plaintiff's title to the land. The appeal was dismissed without costs, solidifying the Plaintiff's right to possess the property. The judgment serves as a significant affirmation of the principles governing land transactions and the evidentiary standards required to establish ownership.
Counsel: Sunil Abeyratne with Uditha Collure for the Defendant-Appellant-Petitioner. D.M.G. Dissanayake for Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent.

Parties: ACME Lanka Distillers (Pvt.) Ltd. v. Karunanayake and Others
Bench: K. Sripavan, J.; C. Ekanayake, J; Rohini Marasinghe, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Article 126 of the Constitution — Time limit for filing application — Awareness of infringement — Budget proposals — Gazette notification — Administrative action — Excise Notification
Summary: Facts The Petitioners, including ACME Lanka Distillers (Pvt.) Ltd., Uva Glen (Pvt) Ltd., Nipponexpo (Private) Limited, and Scotland Distillers and Blenders (Pvt) Ltd., filed four separate fundamental rights applications challenging the validity of Excise Notification No. 973. This notification was published in the Gazette Extraordinary No. 1901/19 on 13th February 2015 by the Minister of Finance. The Respondents argued that the applications were time-barred under Article 126(2) of the Constitution, contending that the limitation period commenced on 29th January 2015, the date the budget proposal was presented in Parliament. The Petitioners asserted that they only became aware of the gazetted notification and were able to obtain a copy thereof after 27th February 2015. The applications were filed between 12th March 2015 and 17th March 2015. The court consolidated the matters for the purpose of determining the preliminary objection regarding the time bar. Issues Whether the fundamental rights applications were filed within the one-month time limit prescribed by Article 126(2) of the Constitution. Whether the period of limitation for challenging an executive act commences from the date of a budget speech in Parliament or from the date of the subsequent publication of a gazette notification. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 126(2) Cases Referred to: Siriwardena v. Brigadier J. Rodrigo - [1986/v1-sri-lr-384] Analysis Issue 1: Compliance with Article 126(2) Rule applied: Article 126(2) of the Constitution, which requires fundamental rights applications to be filed within one month of the alleged infringement. Court's reasoning: The court held that the limitation period does not commence upon the mere announcement of a budget proposal in Parliament, as such proposals lack legal force until authorized by subsequent executive or legislative action. The court applied the principle that the one-month period is computed from the date a petitioner becomes aware of the infringement and is in a position to take effective legal steps. Since the Petitioners could only access the impugned Gazette on or after 27th February 2015, the court determined that the applications filed in March 2015 were within the prescribed timeframe. Finding: The preliminary objection was overruled; the applications were filed within the time limit. Issue 2: Commencement of the limitation period Rule applied: Article 126(2) of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The court distinguished between policy decisions/budget speeches and executive actions. It reasoned that an infringement of fundamental rights occurs only when the Minister of Finance acts upon a budget speech by publishing a gazette notification that carries legal weight. Consequently, the date of the budget speech is irrelevant for the purpose of calculating the limitation period under Article 126(2). Finding: The limitation period commences from the date of the publication of the gazette notification, not the date of the budget speech. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are interdependent. The determination that the limitation period only begins upon the publication of the gazette (Issue 2) directly informs the conclusion that the applications were filed within the statutory timeframe (Issue 1). By clarifying the nature of the "infringement" as the executive act of gazetting rather than the parliamentary speech, the court established a clear trigger point for the limitation period. Conclusion The preliminary objection raised by the Respondents is overruled. The applications are set down for hearing on their merits. Keywords Fundamental rights — Article 126 of the Constitution — Time limit for filing application — Awareness of infringement — Budget proposals — Gazette notification — Administrative action — Excise Notification

Parties: ACME Lanka Distillers (Pvt) Ltd. v. Hon Ravi Karunanayake and Others
Bench: K. Sripavan, C.J.; C. Ekanayake, J.; R. Marasinghe, J.
Keywords: Fundamental Rights — Article 126 — Budget Proposals — Gazette Notification — Legal Impact — Rule of Law — Administrative Action — Infringement — Jurisdiction — Generous Construction
Summary: Facts The case involves four applications filed under Article 126 of the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, concerning alleged violations of fundamental rights by the Respondents, specifically the Hon. Minister of Finance and other officials related to the Excise Department. The Petitioners in these applications challenged the Excise Notification No. 973 published in the Gazette Extraordinary No. 1901/19 dated 13th February 2015, which was part of the budget proposals presented in Parliament on 29th January 2015. The Petitioners contended that they were not aware of the Gazette notification until after 27th February 2015, which is when they were able to obtain a copy. The applications were filed on 12th March 2015, 13th March 2015, and 17th March 2015, respectively. The Respondents raised a preliminary objection, arguing that the applications were filed outside the one-month period stipulated in Article 126(2) of the Constitution, asserting that the infringement occurred when the budget proposals were made public. Issues The primary issues before the court were: Whether the applications were filed within the time limit prescribed by Article 126(2) of the Constitution. Whether the infringement of fundamental rights occurred at the time of the budget speech or upon the publication of the Gazette notification. Applicable Law Statutes: Article 126 of the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka. Cases Referred to: Siriwardena and Others Vs. Brigadier J. Rodrigo and Others - [1986] 1 SLR 384. Analysis Issue 1: Timeliness of Applications Rule applied: Article 126(2) of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The court examined the timeline of events leading to the filing of the applications. The learned Deputy Solicitor General (D.S.G.) argued that the Petitioners should have filed their applications within one month of the budget speech on 29th January 2015. However, the court noted that the infringement of rights was not merely based on the budget speech but rather on the subsequent administrative action taken by the Minister of Finance when the Gazette notification was published. The court emphasized that the legal impact of budget proposals only materializes when Parliament confers legal authority upon them. The court referred to the precedent set in Siriwardena and Others Vs. Brigadier J. Rodrigo and Others, where it was established that the one-month period for filing applications should commence from the date the Petitioners became aware of the infringement. In this case, the court found that the Petitioners became aware of the Gazette notification only after 27th February 2015, thus allowing them to file their applications within the prescribed time frame. Finding: The preliminary objection raised by the D.S.G. was overruled, and the court concluded that the applications were filed within the appropriate time limit. Interaction of Issues The issues of timeliness and the actual occurrence of the infringement are interrelated, as the determination of when the infringement occurred directly affects the validity of the applications. The court's analysis clarified that the infringement was tied to the administrative action of publishing the Gazette notification rather than the budget speech itself. Conclusion The Supreme Court ruled in favor of the Petitioners, allowing the applications to proceed on their merits. The court's decision underscored the importance of the rule of law and the right of individuals to challenge administrative actions that infringe upon their fundamental rights. The court's interpretation of Article 126(2) was generous and purposive, ensuring that individuals are not unduly restricted in their ability to seek redress for violations of their rights. Keywords Fundamental Rights — Article 126 — Budget Proposals — Gazette Notification — Legal Impact — Rule of Law — Administrative Action — Infringement — Jurisdiction — Generous Construction
Counsel: R. Chula Bandara with S.L. Samarakoon and E.M.L.K. Edirisinghe, M.L.R. De Silva, Mangala Jeevendra and R. De Silva for the Petitioner. Viraj Dayaratne, DSG with Ms. Viveka Siriwardena, DSG and Dr. Avanti Perera, SSC for the Respondents.

Parties: De Silva v. De Silva and Jayawardena
Bench: Eva Wanasundera P.C., J; Sisira J. de Abrew J, J.; Anil Gooneratne J, J.
Keywords: Divorce suit — Malicious desertion — Adultery — Matrimonial property — Section 615 of the Civil Procedure Code — Transfer of property — Reasonableness — Forfeiture of benefits — Common law principles — Separate property — Permanent alimony
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Petitioner (wife) filed a divorce suit against the Defendant-Appellant-Respondent (husband) in the District Court of Colombo. The District Court granted a divorce on the grounds of malicious desertion and adultery. In addition to the divorce, the District Court ordered the husband to transfer his undivided half share of the matrimonial house to the wife, citing Section 615(1) of the Civil Procedure Code. The husband appealed this decision to the High Court. The High Court vacated the District Courtâ€™s judgment and ordered a trial de novo. The wife subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. During the proceedings, the parties agreed to accept the Decree of Divorce granted by the District Court and waived the requirement for a re-trial. The Supreme Court limited the scope of the appeal to the question of whether the order directing the transfer of the husband's share of the matrimonial property was legally correct. Issues Whether the trial judge was correct in law in directing the husband to transfer his share of the matrimonial property to the wife under Section 615(1) of the Civil Procedure Code. Whether the conduct of the parties (matrimonial fault) should influence the court's exercise of discretion in ordering the redistribution of property under Section 615(1) of the Civil Procedure Code. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 615(1) Civil Procedure Code, Section 615(1)(a) Civil Procedure Code, Section 618 Cases Referred to: Huang v. Secretary of State for the Home Department - [2007/v2-ac-167] Dondris v. Kudatchi - [7-nlr-107] De Silva v. De Silva - [27-nlr-289] Fernando v. Fernando - [63-nlr-416] P. Samarasinghe v. L. Samarasinghe - [1990/v1-sri-lr-31] Miller v. Miller - [2006/v2-ac-262] Analysis Issue 1: Transfer of matrimonial property Rule applied: Section 615(1)(a) of the Civil Procedure Code, which grants the court discretion to order a conveyance or settlement of property if it "thinks fit" and if such an order is "reasonable." Court's reasoning: The court observed that both parties contributed to the purchase of the matrimonial property. While the wife and her family provided significant financial assistance, the husband also contributed through mortgage repayments and business earnings. The court held that Section 615(1)(a) requires an assessment of reasonableness and entitlement rather than a punitive redistribution. Because both parties held title and contributed to the acquisition, transferring the husband's entire share to the wife was deemed unreasonable and unfair. Finding: The order to transfer the husband's share of the property was incorrect in law and was set aside. Issue 2: Influence of matrimonial fault Rule applied: Section 615(1) of the Civil Procedure Code and the common law principle of forfeiture of benefits. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that while common law principles of forfeiture of benefits apply to benefits derived specifically from the marriage, they do not extend to the separate property of an offending spouse. The court emphasized that the grounds for divorce (adultery and desertion) should not influence the trial judgeâ€™s discretion when exercising powers under Section 615(1)(a). The court noted that the trial judge was improperly prejudiced by the husband's conduct, which led to an inequitable property order. Finding: Matrimonial fault is not a valid basis for ordering the transfer of property that does not constitute a benefit derived from the marriage. Interaction of Issues The court's findings on the two issues are intrinsically linked. By determining that matrimonial fault should not dictate property redistribution, the court clarified that the "reasonableness" requirement in Section 615(1) must be assessed independently of the conduct that led to the divorce. This reinforced the conclusion that the trial judgeâ€™s order was legally flawed because it was motivated by the husband's misconduct rather than an equitable assessment of property contributions. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed, and the order of the High Court is affirmed only insofar as it set aside the District Court's direction to transfer the husband's property share. The matrimonial home remains co-owned by both parties. The court further ordered the husband to pay a sum of 3 million rupees as permanent alimony to the wife. Keywords Divorce suit — Malicious desertion — Adultery — Matrimonial property — Section 615 of the Civil Procedure Code — Transfer of property — Reasonableness — Forfeiture of benefits — Common law principles — Separate property — Permanent alimony

Parties: Ace Containers (Pvt) Ltd v. Commercial Bank of Ceylon PLC
Bench: Chandra Ekanayake, J.; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.; Upaly Abeyrathne, J
Keywords: Appeal from High Court order — Addition of necessary party — Lankem Development Ltd. — Advance payment guarantee — Contractual interpretation — Demand guarantee — Uniform Rules for Demand Guarantees — Autonomy principle — Bank guarantees — Commercial High Court
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff Respondent, Commercial Bank of Ceylon PLC, entered into a contract with Lankem Development Ltd. for the surfacing of a container yard. As part of this agreement, Lankem Development Ltd. provided an Advance Payment Guarantee issued by the Defendant Appellant, Ace Containers (Pvt) Ltd. The guarantee was subject to reduction based on repayment certificates issued by Lankem Development Ltd. or the Respondent. Following a claim by the Respondent for the full value of the guarantee, the Appellant issued a pay order for a reduced amount, citing payment certificates provided by Lankem Development Ltd. The Respondent challenged this reduction and filed an action in the Commercial High Court. The Appellant subsequently moved to add Lankem Development Ltd. as a necessary party to the action, arguing that the guarantee was not a standard demand guarantee and that the involvement of Lankem Development Ltd. was essential to determine the validity of the reduced payment. The High Court refused the application, leading to the present appeal. Issues Whether the Advance Payment Guarantee constitutes a demand guarantee, thereby rendering the underlying contract between the Respondent and Lankem Development Ltd. irrelevant to the Appellant's obligation to pay. Whether Lankem Development Ltd. is a necessary party to the action under Section 18(1) of the Civil Procedure Code to enable the court to effectually and completely adjudicate the matter. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 18(1) High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 10 of 1996 Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 118 Cases Referred to: Arumugam Coomaraswamy v. Andiris Appuhamy - [1985/v2-sri-lr-219] Esal (Commodities) Ltd and Reltor Ltd. v. Oriental Credit Ltd and Wells Fargo Bank NA - [1985/v2-lloyds-rep-546] Siporex Trade SA v. Banque Indosuez - [1986/v2-lloyds-rep-146] Edward Owen Engineering Ltd v. Barclays Bank International Ltd - [1978/q.b.-159] Power Curber International Ltd. v. National Bank of Kuwait SAK - [1981/v3-all-er-607] Harbottle (Mercantile) Ltd. v. National Westminster Bank Ltd. - [1977/v2-all-er-862] Boliventer Oil SA v. Chase Manhattan Bank - [1984/v1-all-er-351] Analysis Issue 1: Nature of the Guarantee Rule applied: The principle of autonomy in demand guarantees, where the guarantor's obligation is independent of the underlying contract between the beneficiary and the principal. Court's reasoning: The court examined the terms of the guarantee and determined it functioned as a demand guarantee. Citing Edward Owen Engineering Ltd v. Barclays Bank International Ltd and Power Curber International Ltd. v. National Bank of Kuwait SAK, the court held that a bank must honor a demand guarantee according to its terms without becoming involved in disputes regarding the underlying contract. The court rejected the Appellant's argument that the inclusion of a reduction clause based on payment certificates altered the fundamental nature of the demand guarantee. Finding: The guarantee is a demand guarantee, and the Appellant's obligation to pay is independent of the underlying contract between the Respondent and Lankem Development Ltd. Issue 2: Addition of Necessary Party Rule applied: Section 18(1) of the Civil Procedure Code regarding the addition of parties necessary for effectual and complete adjudication. Court's reasoning: The court distinguished the present case from Arumugam Coomaraswamy v. Andiris Appuhamy, noting that the necessity of adding a party depends on the specific facts. Because the guarantee is an autonomous contract, the dispute regarding the reduction of the guarantee amount is strictly between the Appellant and the Respondent. The court reasoned that the Appellant could prove its entitlement to reduce the liability by producing the relevant certificates as evidence without requiring Lankem Development Ltd. to be a party to the litigation. Finding: Lankem Development Ltd. is not a necessary party to the action. Interaction of Issues The finding that the guarantee is an autonomous demand guarantee directly informs the conclusion that Lankem Development Ltd. is not a necessary party. Because the Appellant's liability is independent of the underlying contract, the presence of the principal debtor is not required for the court to adjudicate the dispute between the bank and the beneficiary. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed with costs. The order of the High Court refusing to add Lankem Development Ltd. as a party is affirmed. Keywords Appeal from High Court order — Addition of necessary party — Lankem Development Ltd. — Advance payment guarantee — Contractual interpretation — Demand guarantee — Uniform Rules for Demand Guarantees — Autonomy principle — Bank guarantees — Commercial High Court

Parties: Safra Travels and Tours (Pvt) Ltd. and Others v. Zameel and Haleem
Bench: Eva Wanasundera, PC., J; P. Jayawardena, PC., J; Anil Gooneratne, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Hajj pilgrimage — Quota allocation — Interview process — Evaluation criteria — Arbitrariness — Judicial review
Summary: Facts The Petitioners, Safra Travels and Tours (Pvt) Ltd., Nawas Samsudeen Mohamed Althaf and Ainul Fouzia Mansoor, and Kara Travels and Tours (Pvt) Ltd., filed this application alleging that their fundamental rights under Articles 12 and 14 of the Constitution were infringed by the 1st to 9th Respondents. The dispute concerns the allocation of quotas for Hajj travel operators by the Department of Muslim Religious and Cultural Affairs. The government regulates Hajj pilgrimages through a Hajj Committee, which registers operators and allocates quotas based on a fixed number of pilgrims permitted by the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. Following an interim order from the Supreme Court, the 1st Respondent conducted fresh interviews for Hajj travel operators based on established guidelines. The evaluation criteria included registration, physical and financial capabilities, experience, and reliability, with a total of 100 marks available. Applicants scoring below 50 were ineligible, while those scoring 50 or above were allocated quotas from the static total of 2240. The 1st Respondent submitted mark sheets and the final quota allocation list to the Court. The Petitioners challenged the allocation method, arguing that the quota distribution table was arbitrary and that their individual marks were calculated incorrectly. The Respondents maintained that the allocation was based on a mathematical formula designed to ensure fairness among all 93 selected operators. Issues Whether the quota allocation system implemented by the Hajj Committee violated the Petitioners' fundamental rights under Articles 12(1) and 14(1)(e) of the Constitution. Whether the evaluation of marks for the Petitioners was conducted in an arbitrary or discriminatory manner. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 12, 14, 17, 126. Cases Referred to: SC. FR. 264/2013 - [sc-fr-264/2013] SC. FR. 345/06 - [sc-fr-345/06] SC. FR. 500/12 - [sc-fr-500/12] Analysis Issue 1: Quota allocation system Rule applied: The Court applied the principle that administrative actions must adhere to established guidelines and that any departure must be based on an intelligent rationale. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the total number of Hajj quotas is static and determined by the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. The Hajj Committee utilized a mathematical formula to distribute these quotas among 93 eligible operators based on their interview scores. The Court observed that the resulting table, which categorized operators into bands based on marks, was a necessary tool to manage the distribution without discrimination. The Court concluded that the process was not arbitrary but rather a complex mathematical task performed to ensure equitable distribution of a fixed resource. Finding: The quota allocation system did not infringe upon the Petitioners' fundamental rights. Issue 2: Evaluation of marks Rule applied: The Court applied the evaluation criteria set out in the Guidelines for Hajj/Umra Pilgrimages (P1A). Court's reasoning: The Petitioners argued they were entitled to higher marks in specific sub-categories. The Court noted that even if the Petitioners were granted the additional marks they claimed, it would not significantly alter their quota allocation. Furthermore, the Court emphasized that the other 90 operators who would be affected by a reallocation of quotas were not parties to the case. The Court held that the Hajj Committee members, as competent and experienced individuals, were entitled to exercise their discretion in awarding marks within the framework of the guidelines. Finding: The evaluation process was conducted in a rightful manner, and no infringement of fundamental rights occurred. Interaction of Issues The findings on the evaluation of marks and the quota allocation system are mutually reinforcing. Because the Court found the evaluation process to be fair and within the discretion of the Hajj Committee, the resulting quota distribution—which was derived mathematically from those marks—was deemed inherently non-arbitrary. The resolution of the evaluation issue rendered the challenge to the quota distribution moot, as the Petitioners failed to demonstrate that any adjustment would be legally permissible or practically feasible without affecting non-party operators. Conclusion The Court held that the fundamental rights of the Petitioners were not infringed. The application is dismissed with no order as to costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Hajj pilgrimage — Quota allocation — Interview process — Evaluation criteria — Arbitrariness — Judicial review

Parties: Kumarasinghe and Others v. Hettiarachchi and Others
Bench: Chandra Ekanayake, J.; P. B. Aluwihare P.C., J; Anil Gooneratne, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Detention orders — Illegal arrest — Personal liberty — Reasonable suspicion — Conspiracy against the Government — Army deserters — Assault — Emergency Regulations
Summary: Facts The Petitioner filed this application on behalf of thirteen detainees who were former non-ranking officers of the Sri Lanka Army. The detainees, along with seven civilians, were traveling to Anuradhapura on a pilgrimage to invoke blessings for a presidential candidate when they were intercepted and arrested by the 7th Respondent and other individuals at Gokarella. The detainees were subsequently taken to the Gokarella Police Station and later transferred to the Criminal Investigations Department. The detainees alleged that they were subjected to assault and forced to provide statements implicating the presidential candidate in a conspiracy to commit violence. The Respondents contended that the detainees were arrested due to suspicious behavior and contradictory responses during questioning near the Gokarella area during the presidential election period. The detainees were produced before the Magistrate and remanded, after which detention orders were issued by the 1st Respondent. Following investigations, no incriminating evidence was found, and the detainees were discharged by the Magistrate on 22.02.2010. Issues Whether the arrest and detention of the detainees violated their fundamental rights under Articles 12(1), 12(2), 13(1), and 13(2) of the Constitution. Whether the detention orders issued by the 1st Respondent were based on a valid exercise of discretion or were the result of a mechanical process lacking sufficient material. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 12(1), 12(2), 13(1), 13(2), 126(2) Penal Code, Sections 113(b), 140 Emergency Regulations No. 1 Cases Referred to: Pelawattage (AAL) for Piyasena v. O.I.C. Wadduwa - [sc-application-494/93] Channa Pieris v. A.G. - [1994/v1-sri-lr-2/3] Hussien v. Chong Fook Kam - [1969/v3-a-e-r-1626] McArdle v. Egan - [1933/v150-l-t-412] Jayaratne v. Chandrananda de Silva - [1998/v2-sri-lr-129/130] Weerakoon v. Weeraratne - [sc-minutes] Sasanasiritissa Thero v. De Silva - [sc-minutes] Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Fundamental Rights Rule applied: Articles 12(1), 12(2), 13(1), and 13(2) of the Constitution regarding the right to personal liberty and protection from arbitrary arrest and detention. Court's reasoning: The court found that the Respondents failed to provide cogent reasons for the arrest. The evidence presented, specifically document 4R1, did not support the claim that the detainees' behavior was suspicious or contradictory. The court emphasized that supporting a political candidate is a lawful exercise of individual choice and does not constitute a basis for arrest. Relying on Pelawattage v. O.I.C. Wadduwa, the court held that extra-judicial arrests based on mere suspicion without prima facie proof are detrimental to liberty. Finding: The arrest and detention were arbitrary and violated the detainees' fundamental rights. Issue 2: Validity of Detention Orders Rule applied: Emergency Regulations No. 1 and the requirement for the Secretary to form a personal opinion based on sufficient material. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the detention orders were issued mechanically. The material submitted to the 1st Respondent was vague, hearsay, and lacked sufficient evidence to suggest the detainees posed a threat to national security. Citing Jayaratne v. Chandrananda de Silva, the court held that the Secretary must personally form an opinion based on non-vague, admissible material. The process here was a "mechanical process" where the Secretary acted as a "rubber stamp" for the police. Finding: The detention orders were invalid as they were not based on a genuine, informed opinion. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are mutually reinforcing. The illegality of the initial arrest (Issue 1) established a pattern of arbitrary state action that continued through the mechanical issuance of detention orders (Issue 2). The lack of prima facie evidence, which rendered the arrest illegal, directly contributed to the inability of the 1st Respondent to form a valid, reasonable opinion for the detention orders, confirming that the entire process was an abuse of state power. Conclusion The court held that the fundamental rights of the detainees under Articles 12(1), 13(1), and 13(2) were infringed by the 3rd, 4th, 5th, 6th, and 8th Respondents. The State was directed to pay each detainee Rs. 20,000 as compensation and Rs. 25,000 as costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Detention orders — Illegal arrest — Personal liberty — Reasonable suspicion — Conspiracy against the Government — Army deserters — Assault — Emergency Regulations

Parties: Fernando v. Dharmabandu
Bench: Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.; Aluwihare PC, J
Keywords: Special leave to appeal — Withdrawal of application for probate — Prosecution of application by respondent — Power of District Court — Testamentary actions — Civil Procedure Code — Inherent powers of the Court — Section 516 and 517 of the Civil Procedure Code — Letters of Administration
Summary: Facts The original petitioner initiated testamentary proceedings in the District Court of Colombo to prove the Last Will and Testament of his deceased wife, T. Sushila de Silva, and to obtain probate. The respondent, N. S. Sadhana Dharmabandu, filed objections to the application, asserting that the document was not the act and deed of the deceased, and simultaneously moved for letters of administration on the basis of intestacy. Mallika Homes Limited subsequently intervened in the proceedings as a beneficiary under the alleged will. During the inquiry, after evidence had been led, the original petitioner moved to withdraw his application for probate. The District Court allowed the withdrawal but ordered the inquiry to continue regarding the claims of the respondent and the intervenient. The substituted petitioner appealed this order to the Court of Appeal, which dismissed the appeal. The matter was subsequently brought before the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the District Court has the power to permit a respondent or intervenient to prosecute an application for probate after the original petitioner has withdrawn the application. Whether the withdrawal of an application under Section 517 of the Civil Procedure Code necessitates the termination of all testamentary proceedings. Whether the District Court is required to order fresh publication when a party other than the original petitioner seeks to proceed with the proof of a will. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code (Amendment Act No. 14 of 1993) Insolvency Ordinance Cases Referred to: M.L. Marikar Abdul Aziz - [1-nlr-196] Abeyratne v. Wijemanne - [63-nlr-173] Seneviratne v. Abeykoon - [1986/v2-sri-lr-1] Analysis Issue 1: Power to continue proceedings Rule applied: Section 839 of the Civil Procedure Code regarding the inherent powers of the court. Court's reasoning: The court held that where the Civil Procedure Code lacks express provisions for a specific contingency, the court possesses inherent power under Section 839 to make orders necessary for the ends of justice or to prevent abuse of process. Since issues had already been framed by the respondent and the intervenient regarding the validity of the will, the withdrawal of the original petitioner did not render the court powerless to resolve the existing dispute. Finding: The District Court acted correctly in permitting the respondent and the intervenient to continue the inquiry. Issue 2: Termination of proceedings Rule applied: Sections 516 and 517 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court distinguished between the commencement of proceedings under Section 516 and the specific application for probate under Section 517. The withdrawal of the latter does not automatically terminate the former. The court noted that the testamentary process is designed to conclusively determine the validity of a will and the administration of an estate, and the court is obligated to try all issues framed under Sections 532(1) and 533. Finding: The proceedings do not terminate upon the withdrawal of the original petitioner's application. Issue 3: Requirement for fresh publication Rule applied: Sections 529, 532(1), 534(1), and 536 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that the Civil Procedure Code requires only one publication for an estate. Once the initial publication is made and parties respond, the court is empowered to consider all claims and objections. Section 536 specifically allows interested parties to intervene and file objections, ensuring that the court can conclusively determine the matter without redundant procedural steps. Finding: Fresh publication is not required; the existing inquiry is sufficient to address the claims of all parties. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected as they reinforce the court's role in ensuring the efficient administration of justice. The court's interpretation of its inherent powers (Issue 1) supports the conclusion that testamentary proceedings are not strictly tied to the original petitioner (Issue 2), which in turn validates the procedural efficiency of avoiding fresh publication (Issue 3). Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The order of the District Court allowing the inquiry to proceed between the respondent and the intervenient is upheld, with the clarification that the respondent is at liberty to amend or frame additional issues. Keywords Special leave to appeal — Withdrawal of application for probate — Prosecution of application by respondent — Power of District Court — Testamentary actions — Civil Procedure Code — Inherent powers of the Court — Section 516 and 517 of the Civil Procedure Code — Letters of Administration

Parties: Nihal v. Board of Directors and Others
Bench: Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Rohini Marasinghe, J.; Buwaneka Aluwihare, P.C.J
Keywords: Preliminary objections — Maintainability of action — Industrial Disputes Act — Section 49 — State or Government employer — Control test — Section 31B — Labour Tribunal jurisdiction — Section 31C — Duty to inquire — Written submissions — Sufficiency of material
Summary: Facts The Applicant-Respondent, R.L.P. Nihal, was employed as a camera technician at the Salacine Television Institute from 1st March 1988. Following the termination of his services, he filed an application before the Labour Tribunal alleging wrongful termination. The Respondent-Petitioners, who were the Chairman and Directors of the Institute, raised two preliminary objections before the Labour Tribunal. They contended that the application was not maintainable because the Institute was not a legal entity and that the Tribunal lacked jurisdiction under Section 49 of the Industrial Disputes Act because the Institute was a state-run entity. The President of the Labour Tribunal overruled these preliminary objections based on written submissions. The Appellants challenged this order in the High Court, which affirmed the Labour Tribunal's decision. The Appellants subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court, which granted leave to appeal on questions of law regarding the jurisdiction of the Labour Tribunal and the status of the Appellants as Respondents after they ceased to hold office. Issues Whether the Labour Tribunal lacked jurisdiction to hear the application under Section 49 of the Industrial Disputes Act, given the contention that the employer was the State. Whether the Appellants, having ceased to hold their positions as Directors of the Salacine Television Institute, could remain as Respondents in the Labour Tribunal proceedings.
