Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act (as amended) High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 19 of 1990 Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka Business Undertakings (Acquisition) Act No. 35 of 1971 Cases Referred to: The Superintendent, Nakiyadeniya Group, Nakiayadeniya v. Cornelishamy - [71-nlr-142] Superintendent Deeside Estate Maskeliya v. I.T Kazakam - [70-nlr-279] Coconut Research Board v. Subramaniam - [72-nlr-422] Colombo Gas and Water Company Workers Union v. Government of Sri Lanka - [1986-calr-iii-169] Dahanayake v. De Silva - [1978/v1-sri-lr-41] Dharmadasa v. P.H. Wilfred De Silva - [sc-24/69] Albert v. Gunesekara - [ca-729/83] Analysis Issue 1: Jurisdiction under Section 49 Rule applied: Section 49 of the Industrial Disputes Act, which excludes the State or Government as an employer from the Act's application, and Section 31C(1) of the Act, which mandates the Tribunal to conduct necessary inquiries. Court's reasoning: The court held that determining whether an entity is an "alter ego" of the State requires applying the "control test." This involves examining the degree of government control over appointments, administration, and funding. The court found that the Labour Tribunal failed to record evidence or conduct a proper inquiry into these factors, relying instead on insufficient written submissions. Finding: The findings of the Labour Tribunal and the High Court regarding the applicability of Section 49 were set aside, and the matter was remitted for a full inquiry. Issue 2: Status of former Directors as Respondents Rule applied: Section 31B(6) of the Industrial Disputes Act. Court's reasoning: The court noted that Section 31B(6) explicitly allows proceedings to be commenced or continued against a person who has ceased to be an employer, provided they were the employer at the time of the termination. The court affirmed the principle that former members of a management committee in office at the time of termination remain liable under the Act. Finding: The Appellants remain proper parties to the proceedings, and the inquiry may continue by adding the incumbent directors as additional respondents. Interaction of Issues The resolution of the jurisdictional issue (Issue 1) is pivotal to the maintainability of the action. While the court confirmed that the Appellants remain proper parties (Issue 2), the entire proceeding remains contingent upon the Tribunal first determining whether it has jurisdiction over the employer entity under Section 49. Conclusion The appeal is allowed in part. The orders of the Labour Tribunal and the High Court regarding the applicability of Section 49 of the Industrial Disputes Act are set aside, and the Labour Tribunal is directed to conduct a full inquiry into the jurisdictional issue as part of the main proceedings. Keywords Preliminary objections — Maintainability of action — Industrial Disputes Act — Section 49 — State or Government employer — Control test — Section 31B — Labour Tribunal jurisdiction — Section 31C — Duty to inquire — Written submissions — Sufficiency of material

Parties: Paproth and Others v. Geissler and Others
Bench: Chandra Ekanayake, J.; P. B. Aluwihare P.C., J; Anil Gooneratne, J.
Keywords: Land case — Declaration of title — Right of way — Partition action — Intervention in partition action — Prescription — Forged deeds — Laying by a case — Finality of partition decree — Civil Procedure Code — Easements
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent initiated a land case, D.C. Tangalle Case No. 2687/L, seeking a declaration of title and a right of way over lands belonging to the 1st Defendant-Respondent-Respondent and the 2nd Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant. While this suit was pending, a third party initiated a partition action regarding the land known as Indihena, which included the land over which the right of way was claimed. The Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent subsequently intervened in the partition action to protect his alleged right of way. The 2nd Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant moved to have the land case (2687/L) laid by until the final determination of the partition action. The District Court refused this application, and the High Court affirmed that decision. The 2nd Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant appealed to the Supreme Court, contending that the right of way claim would be rendered futile if the partition action proceeded to final decree without the right of way being established therein. Issues Whether the Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondentâ€™s case (2687/L) should be laid by pending the final determination of the partition action. Whether the finality and conclusive nature of a partition decree under the Partition Ordinance necessitate the suspension of a separate action involving a right of way over the same corpus. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code Partition Ordinance No. 10 Cases Referred to: Fernando v. Curera - [2-nlr-29] Samsudeen v. Eagle Star Insurance Co. Ltd. - [64-nlr-372] Selvadurai v. Raja - [41-nlr-423] Girigoris v. Mammadu Meedin - [1-bal-report-177] Chellan v. Ponnan - [56-nlr-95] Rodrigo v. Narayanasamy - [56-nlr-402] Amarasinghe v. Wanigasuriya - [1994/v2-sri-lr-207] Bernard v. Fernando - [16-nlr-438] Abdul Caffoor v. Pattumuttu - [17-nlr-173] Fernanod v. Marsal Appu - [23-nlr-370] Umma Sheefa v. Colombo Municipal Council - [36-nlr-38] Muthumenika v. Appuhamy - [50-nlr-162] Dharmadasa v. Meraya - [50-nlr-197] Hendrick v. Podinona - [57-nlr-494] Mohamedaly Adamjee v. Hadad Sadeen - [58-nlr-217] Analysis Issue 1: Laying by the case Rule applied: The court exercised its inherent power to stay proceedings where matters in dispute are identical to those in another pending action, as recognized in Selvadurai v. Raja. Court's reasoning: The court acknowledged that while laying by cases should be avoided as a general practice, this case is distinguishable because the right of way claimed in 2687/L directly affects the corpus of the partition action. Since the Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent is an added party in the partition suit, the final decree in that action will be conclusive and could extinguish the right of way if not properly established therein. Finding: The application to lay by the case is allowed. Issue 2: Finality of partition decree Rule applied: Section 9 of the Partition Ordinance and the principle that a partition decree creates a new title, conclusive against all persons, as established in Mohamedaly Adamjee v. Hadad Sadeen. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that a partition decree is a judgment in rem and is final and conclusive. Because the right of way is claimed over the land being partitioned, the partition decree takes precedence. Proceeding with the right of way case (2687/L) independently would be futile if the partition decree, which is binding, is entered without the right of way being recognized. Finding: The partition decreeâ€™s finality necessitates that the right of way claim be determined within the context of the partition action. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the conclusive nature of the partition decree (Issue 2) creates the legal necessity to stay the separate right of way action (Issue 1). Because the partition decree would extinguish any easement not provided for therein, the court concluded that the right of way case must be stayed to prevent a potential miscarriage of justice and to ensure the Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondentâ€™s claim is adjudicated within the forum that holds final authority over the land's title. Conclusion The appeal is allowed, and the orders of the District Court and the High Court are set aside. The trial judge is directed to lay by case No. 2687/L until finality is reached in the partition action. No costs are ordered. Keywords Land case — Declaration of title — Right of way — Partition action — Intervention in partition action — Prescription — Forged deeds — Laying by a case — Finality of partition decree — Civil Procedure Code — Easements

Parties: Attorney General v. Rajagopalan
Bench: Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.; Priyantha Jayawardena, PC. J; Upaly Abeyrathne, J
Keywords: Amendment of pleadings — Civil Procedure Code Section 93(2) — Grave and irremediable injustice — Laches — Delay in amendment application — Trial of action — Prejudice to plaintiff
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Appellant, Mapatunage Roland Perera, instituted action against the Defendant-Appellant-Respondent, Sunder Ayyar Rajagopalan, in the District Court of Colombo regarding a house situated on a small plot of land. The Defendant filed an answer to the amended plaint, and the Plaintiff subsequently filed a replication. On the first date fixed for trial, the Defendant moved to amend his answer. The District Court judge refused the application for amendment after considering the objections and written submissions of the parties. The Defendant appealed this decision to the Civil Appellate High Court of Colombo. The Civil Appellate High Court set aside the District Court judgment and allowed the amendment. The Plaintiff then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Civil Appellate High Court erred in its interpretation and application of Section 93(2) of the Civil Procedure Code regarding the requirements for amending pleadings on or after the first date of trial. Whether the Defendant satisfied the legal burden of proving that grave and irremediable injustice would be caused if the amendment were not permitted and that he was not guilty of laches. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code (as amended by Act No. 9 of 1991), Section 93(1) and Section 93(2). Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 127(2). High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 54 of 1996. Cases Referred to: Gunasekera v. Abdul Latiff - [1995/v1-sri-lr-225] Colombo Shipping Company Ltd. v. Chiragu Clothing (Pvt) Ltd. - [1995/v2-sri-lr-97] Nanayakkara v. Attygalla - [Bar Journal 1998 Vol.2 Part 2 Pg. 333] Ceylinco Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Nanayakkara - [1999/v3-sri-lr-50] Analysis Issue 1: Interpretation of Section 93(2) Rule applied: Section 93(2) of the Civil Procedure Code (as amended by Act No. 9 of 1991). Court's reasoning: The Court held that for applications made on or after the first date of trial, the court's power to allow amendments is severely curtailed. The applicant must satisfy the court that grave and irremediable injustice will occur if the amendment is denied and that the applicant is not guilty of laches. The Court noted that the Civil Appellate High Court incorrectly focused on the absence of prejudice to the Plaintiff rather than the mandatory statutory requirements. Finding: The Civil Appellate High Court misdirected itself in law by failing to apply the strict criteria mandated by Section 93(2). Issue 2: Satisfaction of Statutory Requirements Rule applied: Section 93(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Defendant failed to provide any explanation for the delay in seeking the amendment, which the Court characterized as laches. Furthermore, the proposed amendments were found to be unnecessary as the substance of the claims was already contained in the original answer. The Defendant failed to demonstrate how the denial of the amendment would result in grave and irremediable injustice, relying instead on the erroneous argument that the amendment would not prejudice the Plaintiff. Finding: The Defendant failed to satisfy the two-fold test of proving grave and irremediable injustice and the absence of laches. Interaction of Issues The findings are intrinsically linked; because the Defendant failed to meet the evidentiary burden required by Section 93(2) (Issue 2), the Civil Appellate High Courtâ€™s decision to allow the amendment was necessarily a misapplication of the law (Issue 1). The failure to satisfy the statutory hurdles rendered the lower appellate court's reasoning regarding "prejudice" legally irrelevant. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court of Colombo is set aside, and the order of the District Court of Colombo is affirmed. The Defendant-Appellant-Respondent is ordered to pay costs of Rs. 10,000/- to the Plaintiff-Respondent-Appellant. Keywords Amendment of pleadings — Civil Procedure Code Section 93(2) — Grave and irremediable injustice — Laches — Delay in amendment application — Trial of action — Prejudice to plaintiff

Parties: Jeyarajah and Yogarajah v. Thambirajah
Bench: Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.; Buwaneka Aluwihare, PC.J; Upaly Abeyrathne, J
Keywords: Appeal from Civil Appellate High Court — Rei vindicatio action — Action based on privity of contract — Declaration of title — Ejectment of over-holding lessee — Lease agreement — Proof of title — Statutory tenant — Contractual relationship — Overstaying
Summary: Facts The Plaintiffs-Respondents-Appellants, Dr. Rasiah Jeyarajah and Rassiah Yogarajah, filed an action in the District Court of Kandy against the Defendant-Appellant-Respondent, Yogambihai Thambirajah, seeking a declaration of title, ejectment from business premises, and damages. The Defendant's husband originally occupied the premises as a lessee, and following his death, the Defendant entered into a lease agreement with the Plaintiffs. Upon the expiration of the lease, the Defendant failed to vacate the premises and ceased paying rent. The Defendant subsequently claimed the status of a statutory tenant. The District Court granted the reliefs prayed for by the Plaintiffs. The Civil Appellate High Court reversed this decision, holding that the action was a rei vindicatio action and that the Plaintiffs had failed to prove their title to the property, as no specific issues regarding title were raised at trial. Issues Whether the Civil Appellate High Court erred in characterizing the action as a rei vindicatio action rather than an action based on privity of contract. Whether the Civil Appellate High Court erred in dismissing the entire action for failure to prove title, despite the existence of a proven lease agreement and the claim for ejectment. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 127 Cases Referred to: Pathirana v. Jayasundera - [58-nlr-169] Majubudeen v. Simon Perera - [2003/v2-sri-lr-341] Analysis Issue 1: Characterization of the action Rule applied: The principle that an action by a lessor against an over-holding tenant is an action in personam based on the contractual relationship, as established in Pathirana v. Jayasundera and Majubudeen v. Simon Perera. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that the primary objective of the Plaintiffs was the ejectment of an over-holding lessee. While the plaint included a prayer for a declaration of title, this was incidental to the contractual dispute. The Court held that when a lease agreement is admitted, the lessor is entitled to seek ejectment based on privity of contract, which precludes the tenant from denying the lessor's title. Finding: The action is one based on privity of contract, not a rei vindicatio action. Issue 2: Dismissal of the action Rule applied: The principle that a lessor is entitled to eject an over-holding lessee upon proof of the contractual relationship, regardless of whether formal title is proven, as supported by Majubudeen v. Simon Perera. Court's reasoning: The High Court erred by focusing exclusively on the lack of formal proof of title and ignoring the overwhelming evidence of the lease agreement and the Defendant's status as an over-holding lessee. The Court reasoned that the High Court should have granted the relief of ejectment based on the contractual breach, even if the declaration of title was not formally established. Finding: The High Court erred in dismissing the entire action; the Plaintiffs were entitled to the relief of ejectment. Interaction of Issues The resolution of the first issue—that the case is governed by privity of contract—rendered the second issue regarding the failure to prove title moot. Because the contractual relationship between the lessor and the lessee prevents the tenant from challenging the landlord's title, the failure to frame issues or lead evidence on title did not defeat the Plaintiffs' right to ejectment. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court is set aside, and the judgment of the District Court of Kandy is affirmed. No costs are ordered. Keywords Appeal from Civil Appellate High Court — Rei vindicatio action — Action based on privity of contract — Declaration of title — Ejectment of over-holding lessee — Lease agreement — Proof of title — Statutory tenant — Contractual relationship — Overstaying

Parties: Dharmasena and Others v. Abeywickrama and Others
Bench: Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Sarath De Abrew, J.; Priyantha Jayawardena, PC, J
Keywords: Industrial Disputes Act — Labour Tribunal jurisdiction — Preliminary objections — Evidence Ordinance — Just and equitable order — Inquiry procedure — Employer definition — Maintainability of application
Summary: Facts The Workman, W.A.A.M. Dharmasena, filed an application in the Labour Tribunal of Ratnapura against the Superintendent of Kekunagoda Estate and Lal Wasantha Abeywickrama, alleging unjustifiable termination of his services. The Workman claimed he was employed by Kekunagoda Construction (Pvt.) Ltd. and subsequently served as a field officer at Kekunagoda Estate. The Respondents filed a joint answer denying these claims, asserting that the named company did not exist and that the Workman had entered into a contract with one Ms. K.C. Abeywickrama, which had since expired. During the inquiry before the Labour Tribunal, while the Workman was testifying, the 2nd Respondent raised preliminary objections, arguing that he was not the employer and that the alleged company was not a party to the proceedings. The Respondents submitted documents in support of these objections alongside their written submissions, though these documents were not formally produced in evidence. The President of the Labour Tribunal overruled the objections, holding that the issues required a full inquiry into the evidence. The 2nd Respondent sought to revise this order in the Provincial High Court of Ratnapura. The High Court dismissed the revision application, ruling that the objections involved mixed questions of fact and law that could not be determined as preliminary issues and noting a procedural failure by the 2nd Respondent to include the evidence led at the Tribunal in his revision petition. The 2nd Respondent subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Labour Tribunal and the High Court erred in failing to dismiss the application on the basis that the alleged employer, Kekunagoda Construction and Development (Pvt.) Ltd., was not made a party to the proceedings. Whether the Labour Tribunal and the High Court erred in holding that the preliminary objections raised by the 2nd Respondent could not be determined at the outset and required a full inquiry. Whether the High Court erred in determining that the failure to annex a copy of the evidence led in the Labour Tribunal violated the Court of Appeal (Appellate Procedure) Rules 1990. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act No. 43 of 1950 (as amended) Industrial Disputes (Amendment) Act No. 62 of 1957 Industrial Disputes (Amendment) Act No. 4 of 1962 Industrial Disputes (Amendment) Act No. 32 of 1990 Industrial Disputes (Hearing and Determination of Proceeding) (Special Provisions) Act No. 13 of 2003 Judicature Act No. 2 of 1978 Cases Referred to: Meril J. Fernando & Co. v. Deiman Singho - [1988/v2-sri-lr-242] Indrajith Rodrigo v. Central Engineering Consultancy Bureau - [2009/v1-sri-lr-248] Up Country Distributors (Pvt) Ltd. v. Subasinghe - [1996/v2-sri-lr-330] Millers Ltd. v. Ceylon Mercantile Industries and General Workers Union - [1993/v1-sri-lr-179] Associated Cables Ltd. v. Kalutarage - [1999/v2-sri-lr-314] Ceylon University Clerical and Technical Association v. University of Ceylon - [72-nlr-84] Anura Bandaranaike v. Ranasinghe Premadasa - [BALR-1983-Vol-1-Part-1] Ariyadsa v. Weerasinghe and (Western) Provincial Housing Commissioner - [2005/v2-Appellate-Law-Recorder-19] Analysis Issue 1: Non-joinder of parties Rule applied: Section 31C (1) of the Industrial Disputes Act. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the identity of the employer and the existence of the company were matters dependent on evidence. Because these were mixed questions of fact and law, they could not be resolved as preliminary objections without a full inquiry. Finding: The objection regarding the failure to join the company was premature and could not be sustained at the preliminary stage. Issue 2: Determination of preliminary objections Rule applied: Section 31C (1) of the Industrial Disputes Act. Court's reasoning: The court emphasized that the Labour Tribunal is mandated to conduct inquiries and hear evidence to arrive at a "just and equitable" order. The court noted that documents submitted with written arguments, which were not formally led in evidence, could not be considered. Since the objections required factual findings, the Tribunal correctly exercised its discretion to proceed with the inquiry. Finding: The President of the Labour Tribunal did not err in overruling the preliminary objections. Issue 3: Procedural compliance Rule applied: Rule 3(1)(b) and Rule 3(1)(a) of the Court of Appeal (Appellate Procedure) Rules 1990. Court's reasoning: The court focused primarily on the substantive legality of the Tribunal's order under the Industrial Disputes Act. It found that the resolution of the main issue regarding the Tribunal's powers rendered the consideration of the procedural rule violation unnecessary for the final determination of the appeal. Finding: The court did not find it necessary to rule on this issue as the appeal was resolved on the merits of the Tribunal's jurisdiction. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected because the court's interpretation of the Labour Tribunal's inquisitorial powers under Section 31C (1) dictates that any objection involving factual disputes must be resolved through a full inquiry rather than a preliminary ruling. This procedural requirement effectively mooted the need to address the specific evidentiary documents or the technical compliance with appellate rules. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed, and the matter is remitted to the Labour Tribunal of Ratnapura to continue the inquiry. The Labour Tribunal is directed to consider the Respondents' objections after conducting a full inquiry as required by law. No costs are ordered. Keywords Industrial Disputes Act — Labour Tribunal jurisdiction — Preliminary objections — Evidence Ordinance — Just and equitable order — Inquiry procedure — Employer definition — Maintainability of application

Parties: Wanigasinghe v. Hector Kobbekaduwa Agrarian Research and Training Institute
Bench: Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.; Buwaneka Aluwihare, PC.J; Priyantha Jayawardena, PC.J
Keywords: Proportionality of punishment — Doctrine of proportionality — Termination of services — Loss of confidence — Fiduciary position — Repeated misconduct — Labour Tribunal — High Court — Employment
Summary: Facts The Appellant, Kosgolle Gedara Greeta Shirani Wanigasinghe, commenced employment with the Respondent, Hector Kobbekaduwa Agrarian Research and Training Institute, on 10.01.1990. Following a three-year probationary period and various disciplinary warnings regarding her attitude and behavior, she was confirmed as a Statistical Assistant Grade 1 on 15.02.1999. Over the subsequent years, the Appellant was involved in numerous conflicts with co-workers and superiors, resulting in multiple warnings and internal complaints. The Respondent eventually interdicted the Appellant and served a charge sheet dated 16.06.2006 alleging incompetence, persistent unauthorized absence, insubordination, and disruptive behavior. Following a domestic inquiry where she was found guilty, her services were terminated on 06.03.2008. The Appellant challenged this termination in the Labour Tribunal, which held on 03.09.2010 that the termination was just and equitable. The Appellant subsequently appealed to the Provincial High Court of Colombo, which dismissed the appeal on 10.06.2013. The Appellant then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Labour Tribunal and the High Court failed to consider the Doctrine of Proportionality in determining that the termination of the Appellantâ€™s services was justified. Whether the Appellant held a fiduciary position such that the charge of loss of confidence could validly form a basis for the termination of her services. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 128(2) Cases Referred to: State Gem Corporation v. Srima Costa - [1998/v3-sri-lr-191] The Electricity Equipment & Construction Company v. Cooray - [63-nlr-164] Reckit & Colman Ltd. v. Peris - [2-nlr-229] Colombo Apothecaries Co. Ltd. v. Ceylon Press Workers Union - [75-nlr-182] Analysis Issue 1: Doctrine of Proportionality Rule applied: The court applied the principle that in labour disputes, the reasonableness of a termination must be assessed against the cumulative background of the conduct and relationship between the parties, rather than viewing the final act of misconduct in isolation. Court's reasoning: The court reviewed 29 letters of warning and complaints spanning several years, which evidenced a pattern of consistent negligence, insubordination, and disruptive behavior. The court noted that the Labour Tribunal and the High Court had both evaluated this cumulative record. The court reasoned that an employer who has been tolerant of repeated defaults should not be penalized for that consideration, and that the termination was the final result of an incorrigible pattern of behavior that rendered the employment relationship untenable. Finding: The Labour Tribunal and the High Court correctly considered the doctrine of proportionality, and the termination was found to be just and equitable. Issue 2: Fiduciary Position and Loss of Confidence Rule applied: The court applied the principle that all employees are bound to their employer by a bond of trust, and that loss of confidence, when supported by cogent evidence, constitutes a valid ground for termination. Court's reasoning: The court rejected the Appellant's argument that she did not hold a fiduciary position. It held that every employee, regardless of rank, is entrusted with duties and must act with care and discipline. The court cited S.R. De Silvaâ€™s The Law of Dismissal, noting that while loss of confidence is often associated with specific roles, it remains a valid ground for termination when the employee's conduct destroys the necessary trust required for the employment relationship to function. Finding: The Appellant held a position of trust, and the charge of loss of confidence was sufficiently proven by the evidence. Interaction of Issues The findings on proportionality and loss of confidence are mutually reinforcing. The court determined that the Appellantâ€™s persistent misconduct (the basis for the proportionality analysis) directly resulted in the employerâ€™s loss of confidence. Because the employer had exhausted all avenues of correction, the termination was the only reasonable outcome to restore the integrity of the workplace. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The judgment of the High Court is affirmed, and the termination of the Appellantâ€™s services is held to be just and equitable. Keywords Proportionality of punishment — Doctrine of proportionality — Termination of services — Loss of confidence — Fiduciary position — Repeated misconduct — Labour Tribunal — High Court — Employment

Parties: Alles v. Illangakoon and Others
Bench: K. Sripavan, J.; E. Wanasundera, P.C., J; Rohini Marasinghe, J.
Keywords: Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Article 13(1) of the Constitution — Arbitrary arrest — Due process of law — Reasonable suspicion — Police discretion — Burden of proof — Standard of review — Fundamental rights — Mala fide
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Tiran P.C. Alles, filed an application under Article 126 of the Constitution alleging an imminent violation of his fundamental rights. The Petitioner contended that there was a real risk of his arrest without due process, asserting that such an arrest would be motivated by political considerations and would be mala fide, arbitrary, and capricious. The Respondents, including the Inspector General of Police and other police officers, sought to justify potential arrest powers under Section 32(1)(b) of the Criminal Procedure Code. The Court examined the material provided by the second Respondent to determine if there were reasonable grounds for an arrest. The evidence presented included a complaint regarding a fraud involving third parties, agreements that were not produced before the Court, and a confession made by a third party to a Magistrate. The Petitioner had consistently complied with police requests to appear for investigations, and a travel ban had already been imposed by the Magistrate. Issues Whether the proposed arrest of the Petitioner by the police would constitute an infringement of his fundamental rights under Articles 12(1) and 13(1) of the Constitution. Whether the Respondents established sufficient objective material to justify the exercise of the power of arrest under Section 32(1)(b) of the Criminal Procedure Code. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 12(1), 13(1), 13(5), 126 Criminal Procedure Code, Section 32(1)(b) Cases Referred to: Dumbell v. Roberts - [1944] 1 All E R 326 Muthusamy v. Kannangara - [52-nlr-324] Dissanayaka v. Superintendent, Mahara Prison - [1991/v2-sri-lr-247] Analysis Issue 1: Infringement of fundamental rights Rule applied: Articles 12(1) and 13(1) of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the power of arrest must be exercised based on objective standards and is subject to judicial review. The Court emphasized that it will not surrender its judgment to the executive, as doing so would defeat the constitutional protection against arbitrary arrest. The Court found that the Respondents failed to provide sufficient material to justify the necessity of an arrest, noting that the Petitioner had cooperated with investigations and was already subject to a travel ban. Finding: The Court found that there was an imminent danger of violation of the Petitioner's rights under Articles 12(1) and 13(1). Issue 2: Justification for arrest under Section 32(1)(b) Rule applied: Section 32(1)(b) of the Criminal Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Court evaluated the evidence provided by the second Respondent and found it deficient. Specifically, the Court noted discrepancies in the dates of the initial complaint, the lack of evidence linking the Petitioner to the alleged agreements, and the absence of witness statements from relevant government officials. The Court concluded that the Respondents failed to establish a "reasonable suspicion" as required by the statute, relying instead on insufficient material. Finding: The Respondents failed to place sufficient material before the Court to satisfy the objective standard required to justify an arrest. Interaction of Issues The findings on the lack of objective justification for the arrest (Issue 2) directly informed the conclusion that the Petitioner's fundamental rights were at risk (Issue 1). Because the police failed to meet the statutory threshold for a reasonable suspicion, the potential arrest was deemed arbitrary, thereby reinforcing the finding that the executive action would violate the constitutional protections afforded to the Petitioner. Conclusion The Court granted leave to proceed with the application for the alleged imminent violation of Articles 12(1) and 13(1) of the Constitution. The parties were directed to maintain the status quo until the final hearing and conclusion of the application. Keywords Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Article 13(1) of the Constitution — Arbitrary arrest — Due process of law — Reasonable suspicion — Police discretion — Burden of proof — Standard of review — Fundamental rights — Mala fide

Parties: Disanayake and Others v. Secretary of State for Public Administration and Home Affairs and Others
Bench: K. Sripavan, J.; B. Aluwihare, P.C., J; P. Jayewardene, P.C., J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Public Service Commission — Scheme of recruitment — Seniority — Administrative action — Arbitrary action — Discrimination
Summary: Facts The Petitioners are Statistical Officers Grade I employed by the Department of Census and Statistics. They were initially appointed as Statistical Investigators between 1984 and 1991, subsequently absorbed as Statistical Officers Grade II in 2000, and later as Statistical Officers Grade I in 2011. The Petitioners challenged a new recruitment scheme for the post of Statistician introduced by the 3rd Respondent in 2012, which required candidates to pass a written examination. The Petitioners contended that this new method was a significant departure from the previous recruitment scheme, which had relied on seniority, performance appraisals, and educational qualifications rather than a competitive examination. They alleged that they were not consulted regarding the change and that the new scheme prejudiced their promotional prospects. The Respondents maintained that the new scheme was formulated in accordance with Public Administration Circular No. 6 to ensure uniformity, following consultations with the National Salaries and Cadres Commission and relevant trade unions. The Respondents argued that the examination was a merit-based tool to assess necessary skills and that the Petitioners' experience provided them with an advantage. Issues Whether the introduction of a new recruitment scheme for the post of Statistician, which replaced a seniority-based system with a written examination, constitutes an arbitrary act violating the right to equality under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the court has the authority to interfere with the policy-making powers of the Public Service Commission regarding the formulation of recruitment schemes. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Cases Referred to: Union of India v. S.L. Dutta - [1991/v1-scc-505] Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 12(1) Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution, which guarantees equality before the law and equal protection of the law. Court's reasoning: The court held that equality under Article 12(1) requires that equals be treated equally. The Petitioners failed to demonstrate that the new recruitment scheme resulted in discriminatory treatment or that they were treated differently from other similarly situated employees. The court noted that the requirement of service for non-graduates to sit for the examination was set at 10 years, which was not inherently discriminatory. Furthermore, the Petitioners did not deny that the Respondents had consulted with trade unions and relevant commissions before implementing the change. Finding: The court found that the new recruitment scheme was not arbitrary and did not violate the Petitioners' fundamental rights under Article 12(1). Issue 2: Judicial Interference in Policy Rule applied: The principle that administrative authorities have the competence to adjust recruitment schemes based on changing circumstances, as supported by the reasoning in Union of India v. S.L. Dutta. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that a recruitment scheme is not immutable and that the appropriate authority has the power to adjust it to meet changing compulsions. The court emphasized that it is not the function of the judiciary to advise on promotional policies or to substitute its judgment for that of expert bodies. Judicial intervention is only warranted if a scheme is shown to be wholly unreasonable or in violation of the Constitution or a statute. Finding: The court concluded that it should not interfere with the Public Service Commission's policy decision, as the matter involves administrative expertise beyond the scope of judicial review. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are mutually reinforcing. Because the court determined that the formulation of recruitment policy is a matter for the expert authority (Issue 2), it necessarily followed that the policy could not be deemed "arbitrary" under Article 12(1) (Issue 1) unless it was shown to be wholly unreasonable. The lack of evidence of discriminatory treatment further solidified the conclusion that the administrative change was a valid exercise of authority. Conclusion The court held that the Petitioners failed to establish an infringement of their fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The application is dismissed with no order as to costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Public Service Commission — Scheme of recruitment — Seniority — Administrative action — Arbitrary action — Discrimination

Parties: Nimalsiri v. Fernando and Others
Bench: Chandra Ekanayake, J.; S.E. Wanasundera, PC, J; Priyantha Jayawardena, PC, J
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Re-enlistment to the Army — Legitimate expectation — Soldiers Service Regulations — Administrative action — Discretionary powers — Appeal to Commander of the Army
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Ginigathgala Mohandiramlage Nimalsiri, joined the Sri Lanka Army Regular Force on 21.01.1986. His service was governed by the Soldiers Service Regulations No. 01 on Enlistment and Re-engagement, which provided for an initial enlistment of five years and a subsequent seven-year period. The Petitioner completed his first term and was re-enlisted for a second term ending on 20.01.1998. During this second term, the Petitioner was arrested on 07.05.1996 on suspicion of murdering his wife and was remanded until 20.08.1996. Following his release on bail, he was interdicted from service on 15.09.1997. The Petitioner sought re-enlistment for a third term, but the Army authorities declined, citing the pending criminal proceedings and his lack of eligibility. Although the Petitioner was paid half-pay until 30.09.1998, the Army later characterized this as an administrative error. The Petitioner was eventually discharged from the criminal case on 05.12.2008 after the Attorney General decided not to proceed. Subsequently, the Petitioner requested a formal discharge from the Army based on his 12 years of service, which was granted with effect from 20.01.1998. The Petitioner then filed this application alleging that the refusal to grant him a third term of enlistment violated his fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Issues Whether the refusal to re-enlist the Petitioner for a third term was illegal, irrational, or arbitrary, thereby violating Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the Petitioner possessed a legitimate expectation of being re-enlisted upon the conclusion of the criminal proceedings against him. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Soldiers Service Regulations No. 01 on Enlistment and Re-engagement Criminal Procedure Code, Section 396 Army Act No. 17 of 1949 Cases Referred to: Dissanayake v. Kaleel - [1993/v2-sri-lr-135] Dayaratne v. Minister of Health and Indigenous Medicine - [1999/v1-sri-lr-393] Vasana v. Incorporated Council of Legal Education and Others - [2004/v1-sri-lr-154] Analysis Issue 1: Legality and Rationality of Refusal Rule applied: Soldiers Service Regulations No. 01, specifically Regulation 4, which stipulates that re-engagement is discretionary and contingent upon a soldier being efficient, well-behaved, and recommended by a Commanding Officer. Court's reasoning: The Court found that re-enlistment is not an automatic right. The Petitioner had been convicted of three charges under the Army Act, which undermined his claim to being "well-behaved." Furthermore, the criminal charges pending against him at the time his second term expired provided a reasonable basis for the Army to withhold re-enlistment. The Court determined that the decision was based on objective facts and applicable regulations rather than arbitrary or irrational considerations. Finding: The refusal to re-enlist the Petitioner was neither illegal, irrational, nor arbitrary. Issue 2: Doctrine of Legitimate Expectation Rule applied: The doctrine of legitimate expectation, which requires a promise, undertaking, or established practice by an authority to be enforceable. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the Petitioner failed to demonstrate any express representation or established past practice of re-enlisting soldiers in similar circumstances. The payment of salary beyond the second term was identified as an administrative error, which cannot form the basis of a legitimate expectation. Additionally, the Petitioner had requested to be discharged from the Army on several occasions, which contradicted his claim of expecting re-enlistment. Finally, the Petitioner failed to exhaust his administrative remedies by not appealing the Commanding Officer's decision to the Commander of the Army as permitted by Regulation 4(1). Finding: The Petitioner failed to establish a legitimate expectation of re-enlistment. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the Petitioner had no statutory right to re-enlistment (Issue 1) directly informs the conclusion that his expectation of re-enlistment was not "legitimate" (Issue 2). Because the Army acted within its discretionary powers under the relevant regulations, the Petitionerâ€™s failure to meet the criteria for re-enlistment rendered his claim of a legitimate expectation legally unsustainable. Conclusion The Petitioner failed to establish that his fundamental rights under Article 12(1) of the Constitution were violated. The application is dismissed with no order as to costs. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Re-enlistment to the Army — Legitimate expectation — Soldiers Service Regulations — Administrative action — Discretionary powers — Appeal to Commander of the Army

Parties: Horathalge Thilak Lalitha Kumara â€œDharmashriâ€ v. Hewapathirana and Others
Bench: Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Upaly Abeyrathne, J; Anil Gooneratne, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Selection criteria — Marking scheme — Public service employment — Mala fides — Bias — Qualifications for employment — Experience
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, a holder of a Bachelor of Science degree in Mechanical Engineering, served as a Training Assistant in the Department of Technical Education and Training. Following the retirement of the incumbent Maintenance Engineer, the Department called for applications for the vacant post via a Gazette notification. The Petitioner applied for the position and participated in a structured interview. The Petitioner alleged that the selection process was flawed because the marking scheme and selection criteria were not disclosed to candidates prior to the interview. Furthermore, the Petitioner alleged that the appointment of the 7th Respondent was illegal and motivated by mala fides, citing his previous objections to corrupt practices within the department. The Respondents maintained that the selection process was conducted fairly and that all candidates were treated equally under the same undisclosed marking scheme. The Respondents further contended that the Petitioner lacked the necessary professional qualifications and experience compared to other candidates. The Petitioner filed the instant application alleging a violation of his fundamental right to equality under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Issues Whether the failure to disclose the marking scheme prior to the interview constituted a violation of Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Whether the selection process was vitiated by mala fides or bias against the Petitioner. Whether the Petitioner was discriminated against in the evaluation of his qualifications and experience. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 12(1) Cases Referred to: Deradason v. Union of India - [AIR (1964) SC 179] Arkansas Gas Co. v. Railway Commissions - [261 U.S. 379] Lindslay v. Natural Carbonic Gas Co. - [2004 US 61] Analysis Issue 1: Non-disclosure of the marking scheme Rule applied: Article 12(1) of the Constitution (Equality before the law). Court's reasoning: The court acknowledged that while procedural rules generally envisage the inclusion of marking schemes in advertisements, the non-disclosure in this instance applied to all candidates equally. The court found that the Petitioner failed to demonstrate that he was specifically prejudiced by this non-disclosure or that any other candidate received an unfair advantage. Finding: The non-disclosure did not violate Article 12(1) as all candidates were treated equally and the process remained transparent in its application to all participants. Issue 2: Allegations of mala fides Rule applied: The principle that allegations of mala fides must be properly demonstrated and explained rather than based on mere suspicion. Court's reasoning: The Petitioner relied on documents regarding his refusal to approve certain payments and his involvement in workplace disputes to suggest bias. The court observed that the Respondent Department had tendered apologies and withdrawn disciplinary allegations, and that the Petitionerâ€™s evidence did not establish fraudulent or dishonest intent by the interview panel. Finding: The Petitioner failed to establish mala fides or bias; the evidence provided was insufficient to support the claim. Issue 3: Evaluation of qualifications Rule applied: The principle of reasonable classification under Article 12(1) of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The court reviewed the marking scheme (2R9) and the interview marks (2R11). It found that the panel awarded marks based on objective criteria such as educational degrees, professional qualifications, and experience. The Petitioner lacked the professional qualifications for which other candidates received marks. The court held that equality does not forbid reasonable classification based on differences in education and experience. Finding: The evaluation was reasonable and fair, and the Petitioner was not discriminated against, as other candidates were objectively more qualified. Interaction of Issues The findings on the lack of mala fides and the reasonableness of the marking scheme reinforce the conclusion that the selection process was merit-based. Because the court found the classification of candidates to be reasonable and the process to be applied equally to all, the procedural complaint regarding the non-disclosure of the marking scheme became insufficient to establish a constitutional violation. Conclusion The court held that the Petitioner failed to establish a violation of Article 12(1) of the Constitution. The application is dismissed. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Selection criteria — Marking scheme — Public service employment — Mala fides — Bias — Qualifications for employment — Experience

Parties: Perera v. Fernando and Premaratne
Bench: Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.; Anil Gooneratne, J.
Keywords: Specific performance of sales agreement — Advance payment — Tender of money — Breach of contract — Lis pendens — Registration of agreement — Trust Ordinance Section 93 — Notice to subsequent purchaser — Willful abstention from inquiry — Fraudulent sale
Summary: Facts The original Plaintiff, Anthony Kanicius Malcolm Perera, entered into a sales agreement marked P2 with the 1st Defendant, Warushahennedige Nimalasiri Fernando, on May 1, 1985, for the purchase of a land. The agreement was duly registered at the Land Registry. The Plaintiff paid an advance of Rs. 10,000 and agreed to pay the balance of Rs. 72,500 within six months. Before the expiration of this period, the Plaintiff deposited the balance with his lawyer and requested the 1st Defendant to attend the lawyer's office to receive the payment and execute the transfer deed. The 1st Defendant failed to appear. The Plaintiff subsequently filed an action for specific performance in the District Court and registered a lis pendens against the property. Despite the pending litigation and the registered lis pendens, the 1st Defendant sold the land to the Added 2nd Defendant, Wagoda Pathirage Premaratne, on December 1, 1985. The Added 2nd Defendant was subsequently joined to the action. Following the death of the original Plaintiff, his wife, D.H.K. Yasawathie, was substituted as the Plaintiff-Respondent. The District Court granted the relief of specific performance, and the Provincial High Court of Kalutara (Civil Appeals) affirmed this decision. The Added 2nd Defendant appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Provincial High Court erred in upholding the District Court's finding regarding the payment of the advance and the Plaintiff's conduct in initiating the action. Whether the deposit of the balance consideration with the lawyer constituted a proper tender of money for the performance of the agreement. Whether the Plaintiff was required to wait until the final date of the agreement before initiating an action for specific performance. Whether the Added 2nd Defendant acquired the property subject to the existing contract under Section 93 of the Trust Ordinance. Applicable Law Statutes: Trust Ordinance No. 9, Section 3 Trust Ordinance No. 9, Section 93 Cases Referred to: Silva v. Salo Nona - [32-nlr-81] Thidoris Perera v. Eliza Nona - [50-nlr-177] De Silva v. Senaratne - [50-nlr-313] Muhandiram v. Salam - [49-nlr-80] Analysis Issue 1: Payment of Advance and Plaintiff's Conduct Rule applied: The court evaluated the evidence of the agreement P2 and the conduct of the parties. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the 1st Defendant admitted his signature and handwriting on the protocol of the deed, which acknowledged receipt of the Rs. 10,000 advance. The court rejected the 1st Defendant's claim that he had not received the money, noting he was an educated individual who failed to act consistently with his claim of non-payment. Finding: The court found no error in the lower courts' assessment of the evidence regarding the advance payment. Issue 2: Proper Tender of Money Rule applied: The court applied the principle that making funds available to the vendor via a legal representative constitutes a valid tender. Court's reasoning: Relying on Muhandiram v. Salam, the court held that when a purchaser deposits the balance with a lawyer and invites the vendor to collect it and sign the deed, the purchaser has fulfilled their obligation. The 1st Defendant's failure to attend the lawyer's office constituted a breach. Finding: The deposit of Rs. 72,500 with the lawyer was a valid and proper tender of the purchase price. Issue 3: Timing of the Action Rule applied: The law of contract regarding breach of agreement. Court's reasoning: The court held that a cause of action for specific performance arises immediately upon the breach of the contract. The Plaintiff was not required to wait until the final date stipulated in the agreement if the vendor had already demonstrated an intention not to perform. Finding: The Plaintiff was entitled to file the action upon the 1st Defendant's failure to perform, regardless of the remaining time on the agreement. Issue 4: Section 93 of the Trust Ordinance Rule applied: Section 93 of the Trust Ordinance No. 9. Court's reasoning: The court held that the registration of the agreement P2 served as notice to the world. Under Silva v. Salo Nona, a purchaser who fails to search the land registry is guilty of willful abstention from inquiry. The Added 2nd Defendant, having failed to conduct a proper search, acquired the property subject to the Plaintiff's rights. Finding: The Added 2nd Defendant holds the property in trust for the benefit of the Substituted Plaintiff. Interaction of Issues The findings on the validity of the tender and the breach of contract established the Plaintiff's right to specific performance. This, combined with the finding that the agreement was duly registered, triggered the application of Section 93 of the Trust Ordinance, which rendered the subsequent sale to the Added 2nd Defendant subject to the Plaintiff's prior claim. Conclusion The appeal is dismissed. The judgment of the Provincial High Court of Kalutara is affirmed, and the Substituted Plaintiff-Respondent is entitled to the reliefs granted by the District Court. Keywords Specific performance of sales agreement — Advance payment — Tender of money — Breach of contract — Lis pendens — Registration of agreement — Trust Ordinance Section 93 — Notice to subsequent purchaser — Willful abstention from inquiry — Fraudulent sale

Parties: De Silva v. Lokugalappaththi and Others
Bench: B. P. Aluvihare, PC, J; Upaly Abeyrathne, J; Anil Goonaratne, J
Keywords: Appeal from High Court judgment — Section 86(2) of the Civil Procedure Code — Reasonable grounds for default — Medical certificate — Ayurvedic physician's evidence — Dislocation of knee joint — Constructive malicious desertion — Setting aside ex-parte judgment — Burden of proof
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent instituted an action for divorce against the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant on the ground of constructive malicious desertion. The Appellant filed an answer denying the allegations and sought a divorce in reconvention. On the date fixed for trial, the Appellant revoked the proxy of her Attorney-at-Law, and the case was re-fixed for trial. On the subsequent trial date, the Appellant failed to appear, and the District Court dismissed her claim in reconvention and entered a decree nisi in favor of the Respondent after an ex-parte trial. The Appellant subsequently filed an application under Section 86(2) of the Civil Procedure Code to set aside the ex-parte judgment and decree. At the inquiry, the Appellant testified that she was unable to attend court due to a knee joint dislocation caused by a fall. She provided medical evidence from an Ayurvedic physician, who also testified and confirmed that he had treated the Appellant and issued a medical certificate recommending leave during the relevant period. The Respondent did not lead any evidence to contradict the Appellant's testimony or the medical evidence. The District Court refused the application, and the High Court of Civil Appeal subsequently dismissed the Appellant's appeal against that order. Issues Whether the High Court of Civil Appeal misdirected itself in law by failing to accept the medical evidence and the Appellant's testimony as establishing reasonable grounds for her default under Section 86(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Whether the lower courts erred in law by failing to consider the totality of the evidence and by placing an incorrect burden of proof on the Appellant regarding her absence on the trial date. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 86(2) Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Reasonable grounds for default Rule applied: Section 86(2) of the Civil Procedure Code, which requires a defendant to satisfy the court that they had "reasonable grounds" for their default to have an ex-parte judgment set aside. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the Appellant provided consistent evidence regarding her medical condition, supported by the testimony of an Ayurvedic physician and a medical certificate. The Respondent failed to contradict this evidence during cross-examination or by leading counter-evidence. The court noted that the Appellant's physical condition was further corroborated by her need for a chair during the inquiry. The court found that the lower courts' dismissal of the medical certificate as "not genuine" was unjustified, as a physician who has not treated a patient would not issue such a certificate, and the evidence remained unchallenged. Finding: The Appellant established reasonable grounds for her default to the satisfaction of the court. Issue 2: Burden of proof and evaluation of evidence Rule applied: Section 86(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court held that the burden of proof under Section 86(2) is not equivalent to the standard of "balance of probability" but is a lower threshold requiring the applicant to provide reasonable grounds for default. The court found that the District Court erred by focusing on perceived contradictions between the Appellant's petition and her oral testimony that were never challenged by the Respondent during the inquiry. By focusing on trivial infirmities rather than the totality of the evidence, both the District Court and the High Court of Civil Appeal failed to reach a fair and just conclusion. Finding: The lower courts misdirected themselves by imposing an excessive burden of proof and failing to consider the totality of the evidence. Interaction of Issues The findings on these issues are interdependent; the court's determination that the lower courts misapplied the burden of proof (Issue 2) directly led to the incorrect rejection of the Appellant's evidence (Issue 1). By failing to recognize the lower threshold of proof required by Section 86(2), the lower courts erroneously dismissed the unchallenged evidence of the Appellant's medical condition, which, when viewed correctly, constituted sufficient grounds to set aside the ex-parte decree. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgment of the High Court of Civil Appeal and the order of the District Court are set aside, and the Appellant is permitted to proceed with her defense from the stage of default. Keywords Appeal from High Court judgment — Section 86(2) of the Civil Procedure Code — Reasonable grounds for default — Medical certificate — Ayurvedic physician's evidence — Dislocation of knee joint — Constructive malicious desertion — Setting aside ex-parte judgment — Burden of proof

Parties: Sanicoch Group of Companies v. Kala Traders (Pvt.) Limited
Bench: Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Upaly Abeyrathne, J.; Anil Gooneratne, J.
Keywords: Civil Procedure Code — Ex-parte judgment — Default — Reasonable grounds — Mismanagement — Juristic person — Board of Directors — Natural Justice — Liberal approach — Due diligence — Power of Attorney — Company law — Death threats — Inquiry — Costs
Summary: Facts The case revolves around an appeal to the Supreme Court from an order of the High Court of Western Province, which refused to vacate an ex-parte judgment entered against the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant, Kala Traders (Pvt.) Limited. The Plaintiff-Respondent, Sanicoch Group of Companies, sought to recover a sum of Rs. 147,180,000 from the Defendant. The summons was served on the Defendant on 23 September 2007, with a returnable date set for 29 October 2007. However, the Defendant was unrepresented on that date, leading to an ex-parte trial and judgment. The Defendant later sought to vacate this judgment, citing the absence of its directors due to extraordinary circumstances, including the kidnapping and suspected murder of its Managing Director, which severely impacted the company's ability to respond to legal proceedings. The inquiry into the application to vacate the judgment revealed that the only witness, an employee of the company, testified about the dire situation faced by the company, including the absence of its directors and the threats faced by the family of the Managing Director. The High Court, however, refused to vacate the judgment, emphasizing that internal mismanagement could not excuse the default. Issues Whether the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant had reasonable grounds for defaulting on the summons returnable date. Whether the circumstances surrounding the absence of the directors constituted a valid reason to set aside the ex-parte judgment. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 86(2) Cases Referred to: De Fonseka Vs. Dharmawardena (1994) 3 SLR 49; Wimalawathie Vs. Thotamuna 1998 (2) SLR Vol. 1 at pg. 1; Foss Vs. Harbottle (1843) 2 Hare 461. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant had reasonable grounds for defaulting on the summons returnable date. Rule applied: Section 86(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court analyzed the circumstances surrounding the Defendant's failure to appear on the summons returnable date. The Court noted that the Managing Director of the Defendant Company had been missing since July 2006, which created a significant disruption in the company's management. The evidence presented indicated that the only other director, the Managing Director's daughter, was residing in Australia for her studies and was unable to participate in the company's affairs. The Court emphasized that the absence of both directors resulted in a state of mismanagement that was beyond the control of the Defendant. The inquiry revealed that the employee who testified had been with the company since 1996 and confirmed that the company was in a state of collapse due to the absence of decision-makers. The Court highlighted that the High Court had failed to adequately consider the extreme circumstances faced by the Defendant, which included death threats to the family of the Managing Director. The Court pointed out that the High Court's conclusion that mismanagement could not excuse default was overly rigid and did not take into account the unique and extraordinary situation that the Defendant was in. The Court further noted that the inquiry into the application to vacate the ex-parte judgment must be conducted in accordance with the principles of natural justice and fairness, which the High Court had not fully adhered to. Finding: The Supreme Court found that the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant had reasonable grounds for default and set aside the order of the High Court. Issue 2: Whether the circumstances surrounding the absence of the directors constituted a valid reason to set aside the ex-parte judgment. Rule applied: The principle of reasonable grounds as outlined in Section 86(2) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court elaborated on the importance of interpreting Section 86(2) with a liberal approach, emphasizing that the law allows for the curing of defaults when reasonable grounds are established. The Court noted that the High Court had not adequately analyzed the evidence presented regarding the extraordinary circumstances that led to the Defendant's inability to appear in court. The Court highlighted that the absence of an effective Board of Directors due to the Managing Director's disappearance and the daughter's residence abroad created a situation where the company could not adequately defend itself. The Court criticized the High Court for not considering the implications of the death threats faced by the family, which had a profound impact on the company's operations. The Supreme Court reiterated that the inquiry into the application to set aside the ex-parte judgment should have taken into account the subjective nature of the circumstances and the reasonable grounds for default. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the circumstances surrounding the absence of the directors did constitute a valid reason to set aside the ex-parte judgment. Interaction of Issues The issues are interrelated as they both revolve around the concept of reasonable grounds for default. The Court's analysis of the Defendant's circumstances directly impacts the determination of whether the default can be excused under the provisions of the Civil Procedure Code. The Court's findings on the extraordinary situation faced by the Defendant also inform its conclusion regarding the validity of the reasons presented for the default. Conclusion The Supreme Court allowed the appeal, set aside the order of the High Court, and vacated the ex-parte judgment. The Court permitted the Defendant to file an answer and defend the action, while also directing the Defendant to pay Rs. 100,000 as costs to the Plaintiff-Respondent prior to filing the answer. The Registrar of the Supreme Court was instructed to send a copy of the judgment to the Registrar of the relevant High Court of Colombo.
Counsel: Ikram Mohamed P.C. with Padma Bandara and N. Udalagama for the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant; S.C.B. Walgampaya P.C., with Upendra Walgampaya for the Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent

Parties: Perera v. Unknown
Bench: Chandra Ekanayake, J.; Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; B. Aluwihare, P.C., J
Keywords: Special Leave to Appeal — University Services Appeals Board — Disciplinary proceedings — Sabbatical leave — Establishment Code — Animus revertendi — Court of Appeal's role — Relief sought — Proportionality
Summary: Facts The Respondent, V. I. D. J. Perera, was a Senior Lecturer Grade 1 at the University of Colombo. In 2001, he was granted sabbatical leave, which he utilized partially before returning to duty. Subsequently, disciplinary proceedings were initiated against him for misconduct, during which he was placed on compulsory leave with full pay. While these proceedings were pending, the Respondent was awarded a Japan Foundation Fellowship and sought to utilize the remainder of his previously approved sabbatical leave to undertake research. The Respondent submitted his application for leave on June 25, 2004, and sent subsequent reminders to the University administration. Receiving no response, he departed for Japan on August 10, 2004. The disciplinary inquiry concluded in June 2004, and the findings were placed before the University Council. In May 2005, the Council decided to treat the Respondent as having vacated his post effective January 5, 2005, citing his departure without the concurrence of the disciplinary authority. The Respondent appealed this decision to the University Services Appeals Board (USAB), which dismissed his appeal. The Respondent then filed a writ application in the Court of Appeal, which quashed the USAB decision and directed the Board to consider the merits of the case. The University appealed this decision to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Court of Appeal erred in law by construing the requirement in Paragraph 20:1 of Chapter XXII of the Establishment Code as a mere technicality. Whether the Court of Appeal erred in concluding that the Respondent lacked the animus revertendi to vacate his post. Whether the Respondent is entitled to seek additional relief in the Supreme Court without filing a proper petition of appeal against the Court of Appeal judgment. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Article 128 (2) Cases Referred to: Prof. J.W. Wickramasinghe v. The University of Sri Jayawardenapura - [2004/v1-sri-lr-321] Julian v. Sirisena Cooray - [1993/v1-sri-lr-238] Perera v. Fernando - [1999/v3-sri-lr-259] Analysis Issue 1: Interpretation of Paragraph 20:1 Rule applied: Paragraph 20:1 of Chapter XXII of the Establishment Code of the University Grants Commission. Court's reasoning: The court acknowledged that the Establishment Code has the force of written law. However, it held that the burden under Paragraph 20:1 rests on the administration to seek concurrence when leave is sought. The court found that the University failed to respond to the Respondent's application and reminders for months, effectively forcing the Respondent into a "Hobson's choice." The court emphasized that the Respondent's actions should not be viewed in isolation but in the context of the administration's failure to process his request. Finding: The Court of Appeal did not err; the administration's failure to act on the leave application mitigated the Respondent's non-compliance. Issue 2: Animus Revertendi Rule applied: Common law principles regarding the vacation of post and the requirement of animus revertendi. Court's reasoning: The court examined the Respondent's correspondence, specifically letters dated January 19, 2005, and April 18, 2005, where he expressed his intent to return to duty and explained the circumstances of his departure. The court concluded that this evidence clearly demonstrated that the Respondent had no intention of abandoning his post. Finding: The Court of Appeal correctly concluded that the Respondent possessed the requisite animus revertendi. Issue 3: Entitlement to Additional Relief Rule applied: Rules of the Supreme Court regarding appeals and cross-appeals. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the Respondent failed to file a cross-appeal or a separate petition of appeal against the Court of Appeal's judgment. Citing Julian v. Sirisena Cooray and Perera v. Fernando, the court held that it is not open to a party to seek additional relief that was not granted by the lower court in the absence of a proper appeal. Finding: The Respondent is not entitled in law to seek the additional relief requested. Interaction of Issues The findings on the administrative failure (Issue 1) and the Respondent's intent (Issue 2) reinforce the conclusion that the University's decision to treat the post as vacated was unreasonable and lacked merit. These findings support the Court of Appeal's order to remit the matter to the USAB for a proper determination on the merits. The procedural finding on Issue 3 ensures that the scope of the Supreme Court's review remains confined to the matters properly brought before it. Conclusion The Supreme Court affirmed the order of the Court of Appeal and dismissed the appeal. The University Services Appeals Board is directed to comply with the Court of Appeal's order within three months. Keywords Special Leave to Appeal — University Services Appeals Board — Disciplinary proceedings — Sabbatical leave — Establishment Code — Animus revertendi — Court of Appeal's role — Relief sought — Proportionality

Parties: Hatton National Bank Limited v. Chandrasiri
Bench: Buwaneka Aluwihare, P.C.J; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.; Anil Gooneratne, J.
Keywords: Arbitration Act No 11 — Enforcement of arbitral award — Setting aside of arbitral award — Section 31(6) — Section 32 — Prescription — Public policy — Penal costs
Summary: Facts Hatton National Bank Limited, the Petitioner, entered into a financial agreement with Sella Hennadige Chandrasiri, the Respondent, on 3.4.1997. Following the Respondent's default on repayment, the Petitioner terminated the agreement and initiated legal action in the District Court of Monaragala. This action was subsequently dismissed on 3.12.2008 due to the Petitioner's failure to pursue the matter. The Petitioner later issued a notice to the Respondent to refer the dispute to arbitration pursuant to the original agreement. The Respondent failed to respond to the notice or participate in the proceedings. A sole arbitrator was appointed, and after evidence was led, an arbitral award was issued on 17.12.2010 directing the Respondent to pay a specific sum with interest. The Petitioner filed an application in the High Court of Colombo under Section 31 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 to enforce this award. The High Court dismissed the application, citing grounds including the prior dismissal of the District Court action, the alleged prescription of the cause of action, and the suppression of material facts by the Petitioner. The High Court also imposed penal costs of Rs. 100,000 against the Petitioner. Issues Whether the High Court erred in law by refusing to enforce the arbitral award in the absence of an application to set aside the award under Section 32 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. Whether the High Court erred in law by considering grounds for refusal of enforcement outside the scope of Sections 31(6), 33, and 34 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. Whether the High Court erred in law by imposing penal costs of Rs. 100,000 against the Petitioner. Applicable Law Statutes: Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Enforcement of the Award Rule applied: Section 31(6) and Section 32 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that the Arbitration Act provides a specific mechanism for challenging an award under Section 32, which requires an application to be made within sixty days. Since the Respondent failed to file such an application, the High Court was restricted in its ability to refuse enforcement. Under Section 31(6), the court is mandated to enforce the award unless there is a pending application to set it aside or if grounds under Sections 33 and 34 apply. Finding: The High Court erred by failing to enforce the award in the absence of a Section 32 application. Issue 2: Scope of Judicial Review Rule applied: Section 31(6) of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. Court's reasoning: The court held that in an application for enforcement under Section 31(1), the High Court cannot look beyond the criteria set out in Section 31(6). The grounds cited by the High Court—such as the prior dismissal of the District Court case, prescription, and suppression of facts—are not valid grounds for refusing enforcement under the Act. Furthermore, the court noted that the Sri Lankan Arbitration Act does not contain provisions allowing for a plea of prescription to be raised during enforcement proceedings. Finding: The High Court acted outside its legal authority by considering extraneous grounds for refusal. Issue 3: Imposition of Penal Costs Rule applied: Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. Court's reasoning: The court examined the statutory framework and concluded that there is no provision within the Arbitration Act that authorizes the High Court to impose "penal costs" against a party seeking enforcement of an award. Finding: The order for penal costs was legally unsustainable. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected as the court's interpretation of the limited scope of Section 31(6) effectively rendered the High Court's reliance on external grounds (such as prescription and prior litigation) legally irrelevant. By establishing that the High Court was bound to enforce the award in the absence of a Section 32 application, the court simultaneously invalidated the High Court's reasoning for both the dismissal of the application and the imposition of penal costs. Conclusion The appeal is allowed and the judgment of the High Court is set aside. The High Court is directed to enforce the arbitral award and enter judgment and decree in accordance with Section 31(6) of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. Keywords Arbitration Act No 11 — Enforcement of arbitral award — Setting aside of arbitral award — Section 31(6) — Section 32 — Prescription — Public policy — Penal costs

Parties: Rajashilpa v. Ceylon Heavy Industries and Construction Co. Ltd. and Others
Bench: Buwaneka Aluwihare, J.; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.; Anil Goonerathne, J
Keywords: Industrial Disputes Act — Labour Tribunal order — Reinstatement of workman — General Manager post — Magistrate's jurisdiction — Interpretation of orders — Section 43(1) and 43(2) — Compliance with orders — Penal sanctions
Summary: Facts The Commissioner of Labour instituted action in the Magistrate's Court of Colombo against Ceylon Heavy Industries and Construction Co. Ltd. for failing to comply with a Labour Tribunal order. The Labour Tribunal had ordered the company to reinstate the workman in the same post he held at the time of termination, without a break in service, and with due seniority. During the trial, the company reinstated the workman to the post of Manager, which was the position he held in 1986. The Magistrate, however, interpreted the Labour Tribunal's order to mean that the workman should be appointed to the post of General Manager. The Magistrate subsequently ordered the company to appoint the workman to the post of General Manager. The company appealed this decision to the High Court of Colombo, which affirmed the Magistrate's order. The company then appealed to the Supreme Court, challenging the legality of the Magistrate's order and the High Court's affirmation. Issues Whether the Magistrate erred in law by ordering the appointment of the workman to the post of General Manager, which was not the order made by the Labour Tribunal. Whether the Magistrate acted beyond his jurisdiction in interpreting the Labour Tribunal's order to include a promotion to the post of General Manager. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act No. 43 of 1950 (as amended) Constitution of Sri Lanka Cases Referred to: R v. Chertsey - [1961 2 Q.B 152] Regina v. Williams - [1962 1 W.L.R. 1268] Analysis Issue 1: Legality of the appointment order Rule applied: Section 43(1) and Section 43(2) of the Industrial Disputes Act No. 43 of 1950. Court's reasoning: The court held that the Magistrate's jurisdiction in cases of non-compliance with a Labour Tribunal order is limited to imposing penal sanctions and ordering payments as stipulated in the Act. The Magistrate is not empowered to redefine the terms of the original Labour Tribunal order or to direct an employer to appoint a workman to a specific post not contemplated by the original order. By ordering the appointment to the post of General Manager, the Magistrate exceeded the scope of his authority. Finding: The Magistrate erred in law by ordering an appointment that deviated from the original Labour Tribunal order. Issue 2: Jurisdiction to interpret Labour Tribunal orders Rule applied: Section 34(1) of the Industrial Disputes Act No. 43 of 1950. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the Industrial Disputes Act provides a specific mechanism for resolving ambiguities in Labour Tribunal orders under Section 34. The Magistrate is not the forum vested with the power to interpret or modify such orders. The court emphasized that penal provisions must be construed strictly, and a Magistrate cannot expand the scope of an order under the guise of interpretation to grant additional reliefs. Finding: The Magistrate acted without jurisdiction in interpreting the order to include a promotion. Interaction of Issues The findings on both issues are mutually reinforcing. Because the Magistrate lacked the jurisdiction to interpret the Labour Tribunal order, the subsequent order to appoint the workman to the post of General Manager was inherently ultra vires. The resolution of the jurisdictional issue confirms that the Magistrate's substantive order was legally unsustainable. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The orders of the Magistrate and the High Court directing the appointment of the workman to the post of General Manager are set aside, while the remainder of the Magistrate's order is affirmed. Keywords Industrial Disputes Act — Labour Tribunal order — Reinstatement of workman — General Manager post — Magistrate's jurisdiction — Interpretation of orders — Section 43(1) and 43(2) — Compliance with orders — Penal sanctions

Parties: Batuwanage Siripala and Others v. Jayatilleke and Others
Bench: Rohini Marasinghe, J.; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.; Priyantha Jayawardene PC, J
Keywords: Prescriptive rights — Adverse possession — Burden of proof in declaration action — Partition decree — Prescription Ordinance — Uninterrupted possession — Adverse possession
