Summary: Facts Batuwanage Siripala instituted an action for declaration of title and ejectment against RA Jayatilleke regarding a land described in the schedule to the plaint. The land in dispute was part of a larger property partitioned in DC Kegalle case number 9740/P, where Lot 2 of plan 701/A was allotted to TA Liliyan Margret Dayawathi by a final decree dated 11.9.1963. The Plaintiff-Appellant derived title through a series of deeds from the original allottee. The District Court entered judgment in favor of the Plaintiff-Appellant. Upon appeal, the Civil Appellate High Court of Kegalle set aside the District Court judgment, concluding that the Defendant-Respondent had acquired prescriptive rights to the land. The Plaintiff-Appellant subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court erred in concluding that the Defendant-Respondent acquired prescriptive rights to the land in dispute. Whether the High Court erred in failing to recognize that the Plaintiff-Appellant, having established title by valid deeds, was not required to prove possession of the corpus. Whether the High Court erred in finding that the Defendant-Respondentâ€™s possession superseded the Plaintiff-Appellantâ€™s paper title. Applicable Law Statutes: Prescription Ordinance (Section 3) Cases Referred to: Wanigarathne v. Juwanis Appuhamy - [65-nlr-167] Lokumanika v. Gunasekara - [1997/v2-sri-lr-281] Peeris v. Savunhamy - [54-nlr-281] Sirajudeen v. Abbas - [1994/v2-sri-lr-365] Ranasinghe v. Somawathi - [2004/v2-sri-lr-154] Analysis Issue 1: Acquisition of prescriptive rights Rule applied: Section 3 of the Prescription Ordinance requires proof of undisturbed, uninterrupted, and adverse possession for a period of ten years. Court's reasoning: The court held that mere general statements by witnesses regarding possession are insufficient to establish prescriptive title. The court examined the journal entries of the partition case 9740/P, which demonstrated that the Defendant-Respondent was not in possession of the land and had failed to successfully exclude the lot in previous litigation. Consequently, the court rejected the testimony of the Defendant-Respondent's son as unreliable. Finding: The Defendant-Respondent failed to prove the necessary elements of adverse, uninterrupted, and undisturbed possession. Issue 2: Burden of proof regarding title Rule applied: The principle that in an action rei vindicatio, the plaintiff must establish their own title rather than relying on the weakness of the defendant's title. Court's reasoning: The court found that the Plaintiff-Appellant successfully traced his title to the final partition decree of 1963 and subsequent valid deeds (P4 and P5). Having established dominium, the Plaintiff-Appellant discharged the burden of proof required in an action for declaration of title. Finding: The Plaintiff-Appellant sufficiently proved his title to the land. Issue 3: Supersession of paper title Rule applied: The requirement that possession must be of a character incompatible with the title of the owner to supersede paper title. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the High Court erred in its assessment of the evidence. The record from the partition case indicated that the original plaintiff in that case had taken possession of the land, contradicting the Defendant-Respondent's claim of adverse possession. Finding: The Defendant-Respondent's alleged possession did not supersede the Plaintiff-Appellant's valid paper title. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected because the failure of the Defendant-Respondent to establish the factual basis for prescriptive possession (Issue 1) directly reinforces the validity of the Plaintiff-Appellant's paper title (Issue 2). Because the Defendant-Respondent could not prove adverse possession, the paper title remained superior and undisturbed (Issue 3), rendering the High Court's decision to set aside the District Court judgment legally unsustainable. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The judgment of the High Court dated 27.4.2009 is set aside, and the judgment of the District Court dated 29.7.2004 is affirmed. Keywords Prescriptive rights — Adverse possession — Burden of proof in declaration action — Partition decree — Prescription Ordinance — Uninterrupted possession — Adverse possession

Parties: Punchiappuihamy and Others v. Dingiribanda
Bench: Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Eva Wanasundera, PC, J.; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Appeal from Provincial High Court judgment — Civil action — Declaration of title — Ejectment — Co-ownership of land — Undivided share — Prescriptive title — Trespasser — Partition action — Attanayake v. Ramyawathie
Summary: Facts The Plaintiffs, a father and son, initiated an action in the District Court of Mawanella seeking a declaration of title to the entirety of a land known as Bilinchagahamula Hena and the ejectment of the Defendant, who is the brother of the 1st Plaintiff. The Plaintiffs claimed title to an 11/24th share via deeds and asserted prescriptive title to the remainder of the land. The Defendant contested the claim, arguing that he and his son were co-owners and that the Plaintiffs had failed to prove the Defendant was a trespasser. The Defendant further contended that his son had acquired a 13/24th share of the land through Deed No. 5151. The District Court ruled in favor of the Plaintiffs, granting them title to the entire land and ordering the ejectment of the Defendant. Upon appeal, the Provincial High Court of Civil Appeals of the Sabaragamuwa Province reversed the District Court's judgment and dismissed the Plaintiffs' action. The Plaintiffs subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Provincial High Court erred in finding that the principle allowing a plaintiff to receive a lesser relief than the one prayed for was inapplicable to this case. Whether the Provincial High Court erred in holding that the decision in Attanayake v. Ramyawathie was inapplicable. Whether the Plaintiffs are entitled to eject the Defendant in view of Deed No. 5151 dated 25.11.1997. Applicable Law Statutes: Not specified Cases Referred to: Attanayake v. Ramyawathie - [2003/v1-sri-lr-401] Hewavitharana v. Dungan Rubber Company Ltd. - [17-nlr-49] Harriette v. Pathmasiri - [1996/v1-sri-lr-358] Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of lesser relief principle Rule applied: The principle that a plaintiff may be granted a lesser relief than the one prayed for if entitled to it. Court's reasoning: The Court observed that while the Plaintiffs prayed for a declaration of title to the whole land, they were only able to prove title to an 11/24th share. The Court clarified that while the High Court correctly identified that the Plaintiffs were not entitled to the whole land, it erred by dismissing the action entirely instead of granting a declaration of title to the 11/24th share that the Plaintiffs had proven they owned as co-owners. Finding: The High Court erred in dismissing the action entirely; the Plaintiffs were entitled to a declaration of title to their 11/24th share. Issue 2: Application of Attanayake v. Ramyawathie Rule applied: The principle that a plaintiff failing to prove exclusive title to a larger area may still be entitled to a declaration of title to a portion as a co-owner. Court's reasoning: The Court distinguished the present case from Attanayake v. Ramyawathie, noting that in Attanayake, the plaintiff failed to prove any title. Here, the Plaintiffs proved title to an 11/24th share. The Court held that the principle regarding "lesser relief" does not equate ejectment of a trespasser with a declaration of title; these are distinct legal remedies. Finding: Attanayake v. Ramyawathie is applicable only to the extent of granting the Plaintiffs a declaration of title to their 11/24th share, but it does not support the ejectment of the Defendant. Issue 3: Ejectment and Deed No. 5151 Rule applied: The right of a co-owner to sue a trespasser for ejectment. Court's reasoning: The Court found that the Plaintiffs failed to prove the Defendant was a trespasser. Evidence indicated the parties were co-owners who had been occupying the land for a significant period. Furthermore, the Defendantâ€™s son acquired a valid title to a portion of the land via Deed No. 5151. As the Defendant and his son hold a valid interest in the land, they cannot be classified as trespassers. Finding: The Plaintiffs are not entitled to the ejectment of the Defendant. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected: the failure to prove the Defendant was a trespasser (Issue 3) precludes the remedy of ejectment, even though the Plaintiffs successfully established their status as co-owners (Issue 1). Consequently, the "lesser relief" (Issue 2) is limited to a declaration of title to the 11/24th share, as the claim for ejectment fails on the merits of the evidence. Conclusion The judgment of the Provincial High Court is varied to declare that the Plaintiffs are entitled to an 11/24th share of the land. The request for the ejectment of the Defendant is denied, and the appeal is otherwise dismissed with no order as to costs. Keywords Appeal from Provincial High Court judgment — Civil action — Declaration of title — Ejectment — Co-ownership of land — Undivided share — Prescriptive title — Trespasser — Partition action — Attanayake v. Ramyawathie

Parties: J.R.Punchiappuhamy and J.R. Ratnasiri Senarath Bandara v. J.R. Dingiribanda
Bench: Priyasath Dep, P.C., J.; S. Eva Wanasundera, P.C., J.; Sisira J. de Abrew, J.
Keywords: land ownership — ejectment — co-ownership — prescriptive title — declaration of title — trespasser — partition action — legal relief — Civil Appellate High Court — Supreme Court
Summary: Facts The case revolves around a land dispute involving two plaintiffs, J.R. Punchiappuhamy and his son J.R. Ratnasiri Senarath Bandara, who filed an action in the District Court of Mawanella on 22 October 1997 concerning a piece of land known as 'Bilinchagahamula Hena'. The plaintiffs claimed a title to 11/24th share of the land based on deeds and prescription, alleging that the defendant, J.R. Dingiribanda, who is the brother of the first plaintiff, was a trespasser who had illegally occupied part of the land since 3 October 1993. The plaintiffs sought a declaration of title to the entire land, ejectment of the defendant, and damages. The defendant countered by asserting that he had been on the land since before the plaintiffs' claim and that he had a valid title deed for a portion of the land. The District Court ruled in favor of the plaintiffs on 11 February 2005, granting them ownership of the entire land and the right to eject the defendant. However, the defendant appealed this decision to the Civil Appellate High Court, which reversed the District Court's ruling on 27 July 2009, dismissing the plaintiffs' action without costs. The Supreme Court subsequently granted special leave to appeal on specific legal questions regarding the applicability of certain legal principles and the entitlement of the parties to relief. Issues The Supreme Court identified several key issues for determination: Whether the Provincial High Court erred in concluding that the principle of law stating that a plaintiff's request for greater relief does not preclude them from receiving lesser relief was inapplicable to this case. Whether the Provincial High Court erred in holding that the principle established in the case of Attanayake v. Ramyawathie was not relevant to the current case. Whether the appellant was entitled to eject the respondent based on Deed No. 5151 dated 25 November 1997. Applicable Law Statutes: The relevant statutes include those governing land ownership and co-ownership principles. Cases Referred to: The case of Attanayake v. Ramyawathie, 2003, 1 SLR 401; Hewawitharana vs. Dungan Rubber Company Ltd., 1913, 17 NLR 49; Harriette Vs. Pathmasiri, 1996, 1 SLR 358. Analysis Issue 1: Applicability of Legal Principles Rule applied: The principle that a plaintiff's request for greater relief does not prevent them from receiving lesser relief. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the arguments presented by both parties regarding the applicability of the legal principle from Attanayake v. Ramyawathie. The Court noted that the plaintiffs had initially sought a declaration of title to the entire land but were denied this relief by the Civil Appellate High Court. The Court emphasized that the plaintiffs were entitled to a declaration of their 11/24th share as co-owners, which was not granted. The Court highlighted that the principle cited by the plaintiffs should have been considered, as it allows for the possibility of receiving lesser relief even if greater relief is sought. The Court further analyzed the facts of the case, noting that the plaintiffs had established their ownership of the 11/24th share through deeds and had claimed prescriptive rights to the rest of the land. The Court concluded that the Civil Appellate High Court's dismissal of the plaintiffs' action was erroneous, as it failed to recognize their entitlement to at least a declaration of their co-ownership. Finding: The Court found that the plaintiffs were entitled to a declaration of their 11/24th share of the land. Issue 2: Ejectment of the Defendant Rule applied: The legal principles governing ejectment actions and the definition of a trespasser. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined whether the defendant could be classified as a trespasser. The Court noted that the evidence presented indicated that both parties had been occupying the land for an extended period, and the plaintiffs had not sufficiently proven that the defendant was a trespasser. The Court referenced the deed obtained by the defendant's son, which granted him a valid title to a portion of the land. The Court concluded that the defendant could not be considered a trespasser, as he had a legitimate claim to part of the land based on the deed. The Court emphasized that the plaintiffs had failed to meet the burden of proof required to establish the defendant's status as a trespasser. Finding: The Court determined that the plaintiffs were not entitled to eject the defendant from the land. Interaction of Issues The issues presented in this case are interrelated, as the determination of the plaintiffs' entitlement to relief directly impacts the question of whether the defendant can be ejected. The Court's analysis of the legal principles regarding relief and the definition of a trespasser were crucial in reaching a conclusion on both issues. The failure to establish the defendant's status as a trespasser ultimately influenced the Court's decision regarding the plaintiffs' claims. Conclusion The Supreme Court varied the judgment of the Civil Appellate High Court, granting the plaintiffs a declaration of their 11/24th share of the land but denying their request for ejectment of the defendant. The Court emphasized the need for the parties to consider filing a partition action to resolve their co-ownership issues amicably. The appeal was dismissed, and no costs were ordered. Keywords land ownership — ejectment — co-ownership — prescriptive title — declaration of title — trespasser — partition action — legal relief — Civil Appellate High Court — Supreme Court
Counsel: Dr. S.F.A. Cooray for Plaintiff-Respondent-Appellants. S.N. Vijithsingh with Laknath Seneviratne for Defendant-Appellant-Respondent.

Parties: Merchant Bank of Sri Lanka Ltd. v. Deguruge Nihal Perera and D.C.A. Ramani Mallika
Bench: B.P. Aluwihare P.C., J.; Upaly Abeyrathne, J.; Anil Gooneratne, J.
Keywords: ex-parte judgment — default of appearance — Civil Procedure Code — affidavit validity — reasonable grounds — due diligence — natural justice — case management — appeal dismissal — legal representation
Summary: Facts The appeal in question arises from a decision made by the High Court of Western Province, which refused to purge the default of appearance by the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellants, Deguruge Nihal Perera and D.C.A. Ramani Mallika, in a civil action initiated by the Merchant Bank of Sri Lanka Ltd. The Plaintiff Bank sought a judgment against the Defendants for a sum of Rs. 3,827,668.89, which represented amounts due for facilities extended to the first Defendant. The second Defendant had guaranteed the repayment of these facilities. On the trial date, 22 March 2004, the Defendants were absent and unrepresented, leading to an ex-parte trial on 28 May 2004, where the Plaintiff presented evidence and obtained a judgment against the Defendants. The Defendants subsequently filed an application to purge the default, which was refused by the High Court on 11 January 2007, citing the inadmissibility of their counter-affidavit and lack of reasonable grounds for their absence. The Defendants appealed this decision, arguing that they had been present in court on the wrong date and that their attorneys had made an error in recording the trial date. Issues The primary issues for determination in this appeal are: Whether the High Court erred in refusing to purge the default of appearance by the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellants? Whether the affidavit submitted by the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellants was valid and admissible? Whether the Defendants demonstrated reasonable grounds for their default of appearance? Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code Cases Referred to: A.G. vs. Herath and another - [2002 (2) SLR 162]; David Appuhamy Vs. Yassassi Thero - [1987 (1) SLR 253]; Pakeer Mohideen Vs. Cassim - [4 NLR 299]; Jayathilake and Another Vs. Kaleel and Others - [1994 (1) SLR 319]; Inaya Vs. Orix Leasing Co. - [1993 (3) SLR 197]; Coomaraswamy Vs. Mariamma - [2001 (3) SLR 312]. Analysis Issue 1: Whether the High Court erred in refusing to purge the default of appearance by the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellants? Rule applied: The principle that an ex-parte judgment cannot be entered without a hearing and adjudication, as established in the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Supreme Court examined the procedural history leading to the ex-parte judgment, noting that the Defendants had failed to appear on the trial date and had not provided sufficient evidence to support their claim of having been present in court on a different date. The Court emphasized that the High Court had acted within its discretion in refusing to vacate the ex-parte judgment, as the Defendants had not demonstrated due diligence in ensuring their presence at the trial. The judgment highlighted the importance of proper case management and the responsibility of legal representatives to keep track of court dates. The Court also noted that the Defendants' claim of having been misinformed about the trial date was not substantiated by any credible evidence, and the failure to act promptly to rectify the situation further weakened their position. Finding: The Supreme Court upheld the High Court's decision, concluding that the Defendants had not provided valid grounds to purge the default. Issue 2: Whether the affidavit submitted by the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellants was valid and admissible? Rule applied: The requirement for affidavits to be sworn before an independent party, as per Section 86(3) of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The Court scrutinized the validity of the affidavit submitted by the Defendants, which had been affirmed before their own attorney. It referenced established legal precedents that disallow affidavits sworn before a party's own counsel, emphasizing the necessity for strict compliance with procedural rules. The Court reiterated that the failure to adhere to these requirements rendered the affidavit inadmissible, thereby undermining the Defendants' application to purge the default. Finding: The Supreme Court concluded that the affidavit was invalid and could not support the Defendants' case. Interaction of Issues The issues of the validity of the affidavit and the absence of reasonable grounds for default are interrelated, as the lack of a valid affidavit directly impacted the Defendants' ability to demonstrate any reasonable grounds for their absence. The Court's analysis highlighted that both issues stemmed from the Defendants' failure to act diligently and responsibly in managing their legal representation and court appearances. Conclusion The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal, affirming the High Court's refusal to purge the default of appearance by the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellants. The Court emphasized the importance of diligence in legal representation and the necessity for parties to adhere to procedural rules to ensure fair trial rights. The appeal was dismissed without costs, reflecting the Court's view that the Defendants had not acted in good faith throughout the proceedings. Keywords ex-parte judgment — default of appearance — Civil Procedure Code — affidavit validity — reasonable grounds — due diligence — natural justice — case management — appeal dismissal — legal representation
Counsel: Pubudu Alwis with Premachandra Epa for the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellants; Prasanna Jayawardena P.C. for the Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent

Parties: Wahalathanthri and Gunasekara v. Wickramaratne and Others
Bench: Wanasundara P.C., J; Jayawardena P.C., J; Gooneratne J, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Political expression — Freedom of speech — Arrest and detention — Police inaction — Provincial Council Elections — Display of banners — Criticism of government — Section 120 of the Penal Code — Section 74(1) of the Provincial Councils Elections Act
Summary: Facts The Petitioners are members of the Janatha Vimukthi Peramuna (JVP), a recognized political party. The 1st Petitioner was a candidate for the Southern Provincial Council Elections in 2009. Following an arson attack on a JVP election office and a pandol, the 2nd Petitioner displayed a banner at the site questioning the democratic nature of the government. On 24.09.2009, police officers arrested the 2nd Petitioner without informing him of the specific reasons for the arrest at the time. The police subsequently produced the 2nd Petitioner before the Magistrate, alleging violations of Section 120 of the Penal Code and Section 74(1) of the Provincial Councils Elections Act No. 2 of 1988. The 2nd Petitioner was remanded and later released on bail. The Petitioners filed this application alleging violations of their fundamental rights under Articles 12(1), 12(2), 13(1), 13(2), and 14(1)(a) of the Constitution, claiming the police acted under political dictation to suppress legitimate political criticism. Issues Whether the arrest and detention of the 2nd Petitioner violated his fundamental rights under Article 13(1) and 13(2) of the Constitution. Whether the display of the banner constituted an offence under Section 120 of the Penal Code or Section 74(1) of the Provincial Councils Elections Act No. 2 of 1988. Whether the actions of the Respondents violated the Petitioners' fundamental rights to freedom of speech and expression and equality under Articles 12(1), 12(2), and 14(1)(a) of the Constitution. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 12(1), 12(2), 13(1), 13(2), 14(1)(a) Penal Code, Section 120 Provincial Councils Elections Act No. 2 of 1988, Section 74(1) Cases Referred to: City of Chicago v. Tribune Co - [139-ne-86] Derbyshire County Council v. Times Newspapers Ltd - [1993-ac-534] Channa Peiris v. A.G - [1994/v1-sri-lr-1] Lingens v. Austria - [9815/82] Amaratunga v. Sirimal - [1993/v1-sri-lr-264] Joseph Perera v. The Attorney General - [1992/v1-sri-lr-199] Kedar Nath Singh v. The State of Bihar - [1962-air-955] Analysis Issue 1: Legality of Arrest Rule applied: Article 13(1) of the Constitution, which mandates that a person must be informed of the reason for their arrest. Court's reasoning: The court observed that the Respondents failed to specifically plead or provide evidence that the 2nd Petitioner was informed of the reasons for his arrest at the time it occurred. The court emphasized that the constitutional requirement to inform a suspect of the reasons for arrest is mandatory and cannot be bypassed by later justifications or police notes. Finding: The arrest was arbitrary and violated the 2nd Petitioner's fundamental rights under Article 13(1). Issue 2: Scope of Section 120 and Section 74(1) Rule applied: Section 120 of the Penal Code (Sedition) and Section 74(1) of the Provincial Councils Elections Act No. 2 of 1988. Court's reasoning: The court held that Section 120 requires an intent to incite violence or public disorder. Political criticism, even when harsh or using strong language, is protected under the explanation to Section 120. Regarding Section 74(1), the court found the banner was a protest against an arson attack and not election propaganda material for the Provincial Council election. Finding: The banner did not constitute an offence under either statute. Issue 3: Freedom of Expression and Equality Rule applied: Articles 12(1), 12(2), and 14(1)(a) of the Constitution. Court's reasoning: The court determined that the police were motivated by extraneous factors, specifically the 2nd Petitioner's political affiliation and his criticism of the government. The court affirmed that citizens have a right to criticize the government and that such criticism is essential to democracy. Finding: The Respondents violated the Petitioners' rights to equality and freedom of speech and expression. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected; the lack of a valid legal basis for the arrest (Issue 2) directly confirms that the arrest was arbitrary and violated the constitutional protections of liberty (Issue 1). Furthermore, because the arrest was based on protected political speech, it inherently constituted a violation of the freedom of expression and equality (Issue 3), as the police targeted the Petitioner due to his political identity. Conclusion The court held that the Respondents violated the Petitioners' fundamental rights under Articles 12(1), 12(2), 13(1), and 14(1)(a) of the Constitution. The application is allowed, and the Inspector General of Police is ordered to pay Rs. 20,000 to the 2nd Petitioner on behalf of the State. Keywords Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action — Political expression — Freedom of speech — Arrest and detention — Police inaction — Provincial Council Elections — Display of banners — Criticism of government — Section 120 of the Penal Code — Section 74(1) of the Provincial Councils Elections Act

Parties: Property Finance and Investments Kandy (Private) Limited v. Ms.KHA Meegasmulla and Others
Bench: Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Eva Wanasundera PC, J; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Arbitration Act No. 11 — Arbitral award — Enforcement of award — Setting aside of award — Section 31 of the Arbitration Act — Section 32 of the Arbitration Act — Public policy — Dispute resolution — Contractual agreement
Summary: Facts The Appellant Municipal Council entered into an agreement with the Petitioner-Respondent for the management of a car park and the collection of parking fees in Kandy. The agreement required the Petitioner-Respondent to pay monthly rentals to the Appellant Municipal Council. A dispute arose when vehicle owners refused to pay parking fees and the Appellant Municipal Council failed to initiate prosecutions against them, leading the Petitioner-Respondent to withhold rental payments. The Petitioner-Respondent referred the dispute to the Provincial Commissioner of Local Government, who acted as an arbitrator and issued an award on 3.4.2006. The Petitioner-Respondent subsequently filed an application in the High Court under Section 31 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 to enforce the award. The Appellant Municipal Council filed objections but did not file an application to set aside the award under Section 32 of the Act. The High Court enforced the award, prompting the Appellant Municipal Council to appeal to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the document dated 3.4.2006 is an arbitral award as contemplated under the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. Whether the High Court erred in law by failing to consider the nature of the document as a threshold issue. Whether the High Court was obliged under Section 31(6) of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 to consider objections to the award in the absence of an application to set it aside under Section 32. Applicable Law Statutes: Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995 Cases Referred to: Not specified Analysis Issue 1: Nature of the document Rule applied: Section 50 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995, which defines an award as a decision of the arbitral tribunal on the substance of the dispute. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the Appellant Municipal Council had explicitly admitted in its statement of objections filed in the High Court that the decision dated 3.4.2006 was an arbitral award. Furthermore, the arbitrator had adjudicated on the substance of the dispute regarding the management of the car park and the failure to prosecute non-paying vehicle owners. The court rejected the argument that a letter from the Petitioner-Respondent questioning the arbitrator's capacity negated the nature of the award, as the Petitioner-Respondent subsequently sought enforcement under the Act. Finding: The document is an arbitral award. Issue 2: Threshold issue Rule applied: The court's procedural discretion regarding the order of consideration of issues. Court's reasoning: Having determined that the document was indeed an arbitral award, the court found that the question of whether the High Court failed to treat it as a threshold issue became moot. Finding: The issue does not arise for consideration. Issue 3: Obligation to consider objections Rule applied: Sections 31(1), 31(6), and 32 of the Arbitration Act No. 11 of 1995. Court's reasoning: The court reasoned that Section 32 provides the exclusive procedure for setting aside an award within sixty days. Because the Appellant Municipal Council failed to file such an application, it could not challenge the validity of the award during enforcement proceedings under Section 31. The court held that in local arbitrations, the High Court is only required to be satisfied that no application to set aside the award exists under Section 32 and that there are no grounds to refuse enforcement under the Act. Finding: The High Court was not obliged to consider the objections, and the answer to the question is in the negative. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected; the determination that the document was a valid arbitral award (Issue 1) rendered the procedural threshold argument (Issue 2) moot. Furthermore, the failure to challenge the award under the specific statutory mechanism of Section 32 (Issue 3) precluded the Appellant Municipal Council from raising substantive objections during the enforcement stage, thereby reinforcing the finality of the arbitral award. Conclusion The Supreme Court affirmed the judgment of the High Court and dismissed the appeal with costs. The court held that the decision was a valid arbitral award and that the failure to challenge it under Section 32 of the Arbitration Act barred the Appellant from contesting it during enforcement. Keywords Arbitration Act No. 11 — Arbitral award — Enforcement of award — Setting aside of award — Section 31 of the Arbitration Act — Section 32 of the Arbitration Act — Public policy — Dispute resolution — Contractual agreement

Parties: Amarasinghe v. Wijeratne and Others
Bench: K. Sripavan, J.; Rohini Marasinghe, J.; Priyantha Jayawardena, P.C., J
Keywords: Ceiling on Housing Property Law — Declaration of excess houses — Time frame for declaration — Extension of time — Vesting of houses — Natural justice — Right to be heard — Transferees — Fraudulent transactions — Commissioner's powers — Board of Review
Summary: Facts The late Mr. R. Wijeratne was the tenant of premises bearing assessment No. 27/1 (presently 27B), Sir Ernest de Silva Mawatha, Colombo 07, since 1965. The owner of the premises was Mrs. Lalitha Rajapakse. Upon the commencement of the Ceiling on Housing Property Law No. 01 of 1973, the family of the late George Rajapakse owned houses in excess of the permitted limit. Mrs. Lalitha Rajapakse failed to file the required declaration within the stipulated twelve-week period. Subsequently, she informed the Commissioner of National Housing that she did not intend to retain the excess houses and offered them for sale to the tenants. The tenant, Mr. R. Wijeratne, declined the offer. Mrs. Lalitha Rajapakse sought and was granted extensions of time by the Commissioner to dispose of the properties under Section 11 of the Law. Within these extended periods, she sold the premises to the 1st Respondent. Following the sale, the tenant made representations to the Minister and the Commissioner, seeking to purchase the house under the Law. The Commissioner initiated an inquiry and, despite requests from the owner to notice the new purchasers, issued a vesting order on 16th September 1982 without granting a hearing to the 1st Respondent. The Ceiling on Housing Property Board of Review subsequently affirmed this order. The 1st Respondent challenged these decisions in the Court of Appeal, which quashed the orders of the Commissioner and the Board of Review. The Appellant, having been substituted for the deceased tenant, appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Commissioner of National Housing acted illegally in granting extensions of time to dispose of excess houses after the lapse of the statutory period. Whether the transferees of the property were necessary parties to the inquiry, and whether the failure to grant them a hearing constituted a breach of the principles of natural justice. Applicable Law Statutes: Ceiling on Housing Property Law No. 01 of 1973 Ceiling on Housing Property (Amendment) Law No. 18 Ceiling on Housing Property (Amendment) Law No. 34 Interpretation Ordinance No. 21 Cases Referred to: Ridge v. Baldwin - [1964] AC 40 Russell v. Duke of Norfolk - [1949] 1 All E.R. 109 O'Reilly v. Mackman - [1983] 2 AC 237 AG v. Ryan - [1980] AC 718 Desmond de Perera v. Karunaratne - [1997/v1-sri-lr-148] Analysis Issue 1: Legality of time extensions Rule applied: Section 11(1) of the Ceiling on Housing Property Law No. 01 of 1973. Court's reasoning: The Court held that the Commissioner is empowered to grant extensions of time if satisfied that failure to dispose of a house was due to circumstances beyond the owner's control. The Court found that the Commissioner had validly exercised this discretion, and the extensions granted were within the statutory framework. Furthermore, the Court noted that the tenant failed to comply with the mandatory four-month period under Section 9 to apply for the purchase of the house, thereby extinguishing his right to purchase. Finding: The Commissioner did not act illegally in granting the extensions of time. Issue 2: Breach of Natural Justice Rule applied: Principles of natural justice (audi altera partem). Court's reasoning: The Court emphasized that the Commissioner performs quasi-judicial functions and must observe the principles of natural justice when making decisions that affect individual property rights. By failing to notify or hear the 1st Respondent, who was the legal owner of the property at the time of the inquiry, the Commissioner committed a fundamental procedural error. The Court further held that a decision reached in violation of natural justice is void, and such a defect cannot be cured by a subsequent hearing before an appellate body like the Board of Review. Finding: The Commissionerâ€™s failure to grant the 1st Respondent a hearing rendered the vesting order null and void. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing. The determination that the tenant failed to comply with the mandatory requirements of Section 9 of the Law meant that the Commissioner lacked the legal basis to initiate the vesting process. This, combined with the procedural failure to observe natural justice regarding the 1st Respondent, confirms that the Commissioner's order was a nullity from its inception. Conclusion The Supreme Court affirmed the judgment of the Court of Appeal. The appeal was dismissed, and the orders of the Commissioner of National Housing and the Board of Review were held to be null and void. Keywords Ceiling on Housing Property Law — Declaration of excess houses — Time frame for declaration — Extension of time — Vesting of houses — Natural justice — Right to be heard — Transferees — Fraudulent transactions — Commissioner's powers — Board of Review

Parties: Somapala v. Udawatta
Bench: Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Rohini Marasinghe, J.; Anil Gooneratne, J.
Keywords: Lease agreements — Construction of boutique rooms — Concrete slab — Breach of contract — Evidence Ordinance — Extrinsic evidence — Stamp Duty Ordinance — Notaries Ordinance — Fundamental rights — Equality before the law
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent (Plaintiff) initiated an action in the District Court of Mawanella seeking a declaration of title to a property, the termination of two lease agreements (P2 and P3), and the eviction of the Defendant-Appellant-Petitioner (Defendant). The dispute centered on the construction of shop premises on the leased land. The Plaintiff contended that under lease P2, the Defendant was required to construct a shop with a concrete slab roof to facilitate the Plaintiff's business, while lease P3 required the construction of a second shop on that concrete slab. The Defendant argued that the lease agreements pertained only to the land itself and not to the construction of two separate shop premises. Furthermore, the Defendant claimed to have incurred additional construction expenses and sought recovery through a claim in reconvention, relying on a disputed document marked V1. The District Court answered the majority of the issues in favor of the Plaintiff, finding that the Defendant had violated the terms of the lease agreements. The High Court subsequently affirmed the judgment of the District Court. The Defendant appealed to the Supreme Court, which granted leave to appeal on several questions of law regarding the interpretation of the lease agreements, the admissibility of evidence, and the evaluation of the construction requirements. Issues Whether the lease agreements P2 and P3 required the construction of two separate boutique rooms. Whether the Defendant breached the terms of the lease agreements, specifically regarding construction costs and the requirement for lessor approval under Clause 8 of P2. Whether the document V1 was a valid and enforceable agreement regarding additional construction expenses. Applicable Law Statutes: Evidence Ordinance, Section 91 Evidence Ordinance, Section 92 Prevention of Frauds Ordinance Stamp Duty Ordinance Notaries Ordinance Cases Referred to: Mohamadu Bhai v. James - [21-nlr-234] Pathbeniya v. Kachohamy - [24-nlr-487] [22-nlr-343] [74-nlr-142] [1986/v1-sri-lr-390] [58-nlr-457] [18-nlr-264] [22-nlr-54] Analysis Issue 1: Construction requirements Rule applied: Section 92 of the Evidence Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The court examined the terms of lease agreements P2 and P3. It found that both agreements explicitly contemplated the construction of boutique rooms. The court reasoned that oral evidence regarding the necessity of two shops did not violate the parol evidence rule under Section 92, as such evidence served to explain the practical necessity of the construction within the available space as intended by the parties. Finding: The court held that the agreements required the construction of two separate boutique rooms. Issue 2: Breach of lease terms Rule applied: Clause 8 of lease agreement P2. Court's reasoning: The court noted that Clause 8 of P2 required the Defendant to obtain the Plaintiff's approval if construction costs exceeded the stipulated amount. The evidence established that the Defendant exceeded these costs without obtaining the required authorization. The court emphasized that the terms of the lease were fundamental to the contract and that the Defendantâ€™s failure to comply constituted a breach. Finding: The court found that the Defendant breached the lease agreement by failing to obtain necessary approval for excess construction costs. Issue 3: Validity of document V1 Rule applied: Prevention of Frauds Ordinance, Stamp Duty Ordinance, and Notaries Ordinance. Court's reasoning: The court found that document V1 was disputed, lacked proper proof, and was rejected by the Plaintiff, who alleged it was a forgery. The court noted that the Notary who attested P2 and P3 did not attest V1, and there was no evidence to justify its validity. Consequently, the court held that V1 could not be recognized as a valid legal instrument. Finding: The court concluded that document V1 was not a valid or enforceable agreement. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected as the breach of the construction requirements (Issue 1) and the failure to adhere to the financial approval process (Issue 2) form the basis for the termination of the lease. The invalidity of V1 (Issue 3) removed the Defendant's only defense regarding the unauthorized expenditure, thereby reinforcing the conclusion that the Defendant violated the fundamental terms of the lease agreements. Conclusion The Supreme Court affirmed the judgment of the District Court and the High Court. The appeal was dismissed, and the orders for eviction and termination of the lease agreements were upheld. Keywords Lease agreements — Construction of boutique rooms — Concrete slab — Breach of contract — Evidence Ordinance — Extrinsic evidence — Stamp Duty Ordinance — Notaries Ordinance — Fundamental rights — Equality before the law

Parties: Lanka Banku Sevaka Sangamaya v. Peopleâ€™s Bank Head Office
Bench: Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Rohini Marasinghe, J.; Buwaneka Aluwihare, P.C.J
Keywords: Industrial Disputes Act — Labour Tribunal — Provincial High Court — Termination of employment — Gross misconduct — Breach of trust — Evaluation of evidence — Reinstatement — Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action
Summary: Facts The Applicant-Respondent-Respondent, a trade union, filed an application in the Labour Tribunal on behalf of a workman, L.D. Dayananda, challenging his termination by the Peoples Bank. The bank terminated the workman following an internal investigation into fraudulent withdrawals from a dormant savings account belonging to a customer named Thamara Dayani Kannangara. The investigation revealed that four unauthorized withdrawals occurred while the account was dormant. The workman, serving as a cashier, admitted to performing three of these transactions and recording National Identity Card details on the withdrawal slips. The Labour Tribunal President held that the termination was unjust and ordered reinstatement, a decision subsequently affirmed by the Provincial High Court of Kalutara. The High Court reasoned that the bank failed to prove the workman's sole culpability and noted that other employees involved in the transactions received more lenient treatment. The bank appealed to the Supreme Court, contending that the lower courts failed to evaluate the totality of the evidence and erred in their assessment of the workman's breach of trust. Issues Whether the Labour Tribunal and the Provincial High Court erred in their evaluation of evidence by failing to consider the totality of the circumstances regarding the workman's misconduct. Whether the granting of relief to a workman based on the fact that other employees involved in the same transaction received lesser punishment is legally justified. Whether the order for reinstatement is erroneous in law given the loss of confidence in a bank employee holding a position of trust. Applicable Law Statutes: Industrial Disputes Act No. 43 of 1950 (as amended) Evidence Ordinance Cases Referred to: Associated Battery Manufacturers Ceylon Ltd v. United Engineering Workers Union - [77-nlr-541] Sithamparanathan v. Peoples Bank - [1986/v1-sri-lr-411] Premadasa Rodrigo v. Ceylon Petroleum Cooperation - [1991/v2-sri-lr-182] Bank of Ceylon v. Manivasagasivam - [1995/v2-sri-lr-79] Sithamparanathan v. Peoples Bank - [1989/v1-sri-lr-124] Bristol Myera Squibbs (Phils) Inc. v. Baban - [574-scra-198] Gunarathne v. Peoples Bank - [2001/v1-sri-lr-381] W.M. Mendis and Co. Ltd. v. Sunil Liyanage - [sc-appeal-132/2004] Democratic Workers Congress v. De Mel and Wanigasekara - [CGG 12, 432 19th May 1961] Analysis Issue 1: Evaluation of Evidence Rule applied: The standard of proof in Labour Tribunal proceedings is the balance of probabilities, not proof beyond reasonable doubt. Court's reasoning: The court found that the workman admitted to performing the transactions on a dormant account without proper authorization. The absence of the required "dormant account" endorsement on the slips, combined with the workman's admission of issuing new passbooks without verifying the cancellation of old ones, demonstrated that the transactions were not genuine. The lower courts failed to properly weigh these admissions and the expert evidence regarding bank procedures. Finding: The Labour Tribunal and High Court failed to evaluate the totality of the evidence, leading to an incorrect conclusion regarding the workman's complicity. Issue 2: Disparity in Punishment Rule applied: Employers have the discretion to impose different punishments based on the degree of culpability of individual employees. Court's reasoning: The court held that there is no legal principle requiring identical punishment for all employees involved in a common irregularity. The degree of culpability must be assessed individually. The High Court's reliance on the lenient treatment of other employees to invalidate the workman's termination was legally erroneous. Finding: The lower courts erred in holding that the termination was unjust based on the treatment of other employees. Issue 3: Reinstatement and Loss of Confidence Rule applied: The employment relationship in a bank is founded on trust and confidence; a breach of this trust justifies termination. Court's reasoning: A bank employee occupies a position of responsibility. Once the trust and confidence between the employer and employee are destroyed, it is unreasonable to compel the employer to reinstate the employee. The workman's dishonest participation in the withdrawals fundamentally collapsed this relationship. Finding: The order for reinstatement was erroneous as the bank had lost the necessary confidence in the workman. Interaction of Issues The findings are interconnected: the failure to properly evaluate the evidence (Issue 1) led the lower courts to incorrectly apply the principle of equal treatment (Issue 2), which in turn resulted in an inappropriate order for reinstatement (Issue 3). By establishing that the workman's misconduct was proven on a balance of probabilities, the court rendered the reinstatement order untenable. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The orders of the Labour Tribunal and the Provincial High Court are set aside. The workman is entitled to all statutory dues, but the termination is upheld. Keywords Industrial Disputes Act — Labour Tribunal — Provincial High Court — Termination of employment — Gross misconduct — Breach of trust — Evaluation of evidence — Reinstatement — Fundamental rights — Equality before the law — Administrative action

Parties: Wijewardena v. Minister of Lands and Others
Bench: Priyasath Dep, PC, J.; Priyantha Jayawardena P.C., J; Anil Gooneratne, J.
Keywords: Land Acquisition Act — Vesting orders — Revocation of vesting orders — Divestiture — Public purpose — Notice to landowners — Section 39A of the Land Acquisition Act — Writ of Mandamus — Discretionary remedy
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Anusha Wijewardena, sought a Writ of Mandamus to compel the divestiture of land and the revocation of vesting orders P17 and P19 issued under the Land Acquisition Act. The Petitioner's husband had acquired the land by deed in 1978 and subsequently gifted it to their daughter. The Petitioner only discovered that the land had been acquired by the State when a boundary dispute arose while attempting to develop the property. The Petitioner contended that she had received insufficient notice of the acquisition and that the land had not been utilized for a public purpose. The State maintained that the land was acquired via Gazette Notifications in 1979 and 1997, both based on preliminary plan 5415, which included the Petitioner's land as lot 1. The State argued that proper notice was provided through the publication and exhibition of Section 2 notices prior to the Section 38 vesting orders. The Court of Appeal dismissed the Petitioner's application for a Writ of Mandamus. The Petitioner appealed this dismissal to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Court of Appeal erred in failing to appreciate the significance of the alleged exclusion of the Petitioner's land from the 1997 acquisition. Whether there is a legal impediment to the revocation or divestiture of the Petitioner's land. Whether the Court of Appeal misdirected itself in its interpretation of the law regarding revocation and divestiture. Whether the Court of Appeal erred in concluding that "piecemeal" divestiture is not available in law. Whether the Petitioner is entitled to relief under Section 4(a) of the Land Acquisition Act. Applicable Law Statutes: Land Acquisition Act (Section 2, Section 7, Section 38, Section 39A, Section 40(a)) Cases Referred to: [2-clw-14] [10-times-65] [12-law-rec-176] [1-clw-306] [34-nlr-33] [1-nlr-33] Analysis Issue 1: Exclusion of land Rule applied: Land Acquisition Act (Section 38). Court's reasoning: The court examined the Gazette Notifications and the associated preliminary plan 5415. It found that the Petitioner's land was explicitly included as lot 1 in the plan upon which the 1979 acquisition was based. The court rejected the Petitioner's argument that the 1997 acquisition excluded her land, noting that both acquisitions pertained to the same land and extent. Finding: The Petitioner's land was not excluded; the court found no merit in the argument that the land was omitted from the acquisition process. Issue 2: Revocation and Divestiture Rule applied: Land Acquisition Act (Section 39A). Court's reasoning: The court noted that Section 39A grants the Minister discretionary power to divest land, provided specific conditions are met, such as no compensation being paid and the land not being used for a public purpose. The court observed that the land had been utilized for the construction of the Govijana Mandiraya building. Furthermore, the court held that a former owner cannot demand the exercise of this discretionary power as a matter of right. Finding: The conditions for divestiture under Section 39A were not satisfied, and the Petitioner failed to establish a legal right to demand such an order. Issue 3: Nature of Mandamus Rule applied: Principles governing the Writ of Mandamus. Court's reasoning: The court emphasized that Mandamus is a discretionary remedy, not a writ of right. A petitioner must demonstrate a clear legal right and a corresponding legal obligation on the part of the respondent. The court found that an order under Section 38 of the Land Acquisition Act serves as conclusive evidence of the State's title. Finding: The Petitioner failed to establish the necessary legal right or obligation to warrant the issuance of a Writ of Mandamus. Interaction of Issues The findings on the validity of the acquisition (Issue 1) and the discretionary nature of the divestiture power (Issue 2) reinforce the court's conclusion that the Petitioner lacks a statutory basis for relief. Because the acquisition was found to be valid and the land was utilized for a public purpose, the statutory preconditions for divestiture under Section 39A could not be met, rendering the Petitioner's request for Mandamus legally unsustainable. Conclusion The Supreme Court answered all five questions of law in the negative. The appeal is dismissed without costs. Keywords Land Acquisition Act — Vesting orders — Revocation of vesting orders — Divestiture — Public purpose — Notice to landowners — Section 39A of the Land Acquisition Act — Writ of Mandamus — Discretionary remedy

Parties: Karunawasa v. Sarathchandra Textiles
Bench: S.E. Wanasundera P.C., J; Buwaneka Aluwihare, J.; Anil Gooneratne, J.
Keywords: Leave to appeal — High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act — District Court order — Promissory deed — Conditional transfer — Entitlement to possession — Prescription — Evidence Ordinance — Prevention of Frauds Ordinance
Summary: Facts The Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent instituted an action in the District Court of Embilipitiya seeking a declaration of title, eviction, and damages against the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant regarding a land of approximately 30 perches. The Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant filed an answer asserting a claim in reconvention, seeking a declaration of title and the right to retain the property until improvements were compensated. The dispute centers on a document marked as X, described as a promissory deed, through which the Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent obtained a loan of Rs. 150,000 from the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant, using the land as security. The deed contained a clause stipulating that if the loan was not repaid within the prescribed period, the possession, ownership, and rights to the land would pass to the Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant. The District Court framed 21 issues, with issues 11, 12, and 13 designated as preliminary issues of law. The District Judge, relying solely on written submissions and admissions, ruled in favor of the Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent. The Defendant-Petitioner-Appellant appealed to the High Court, which affirmed the District Court's order. The Supreme Court subsequently granted leave to appeal to determine whether the courts below erred in their handling of the preliminary issues and the interpretation of the deed. Issues Whether the District Court and High Court erred in attempting to dispose of the action on preliminary issues of law without conducting a full trial. Whether the nature of the transaction, specifically the "promissory deed" (Deed X), constitutes a conditional transfer that requires the leading of evidence to determine the rights of the parties. Applicable Law Statutes: Civil Procedure Code, Section 147 Evidence Ordinance, Section 115 Prevention of Frauds Ordinance, Section 02 Prescription Ordinance, Section 10 Trusts Ordinance, Section 93 Cases Referred to: [15-nlr-389] [1994/v3-sri-lr-11] [1997/v3-sri-lr-202] Mohinudeen v. Lanka Bankuwa - [2001/v1-sri-lr-290] Thambipillai v. Muthukumaraswamy - [58-nlr-387] Setuwa v. Ukku - [Not specified] Velupillai Sanmugam v. Kathiravelu Thambiaiya - [1990/v1-sri-lr-27] Gnanasambandam v. Bin Adaham - [1998/v2-sri-lr-305] Analysis Issue 1: Disposal on preliminary issues Rule applied: Section 147 of the Civil Procedure Code. Court's reasoning: The court noted that while Section 147 allows for the disposal of a case on preliminary issues of law, this power is discretionary. The court emphasized that when questions of law are intricately tied to factual matters, such as the interpretation of a deed or a plea of prescription, it is improper to decide the case without a full trial. The trial judge had observed that the parties were at variance regarding the meaning of the conditions in the deed, yet attempted to decide the matter without evidence. Finding: The courts below erred in attempting to resolve the dispute based solely on admissions and written submissions. Issue 2: Nature of the transaction Rule applied: Principles governing conditional transfers and pactum de retrovendendo. Court's reasoning: The court identified that Deed X, despite being labeled a "promissory deed," functions as a conditional transfer. Citing Thambipillai v. Muthukumaraswamy, the court noted that in such contracts, time is of the essence, and failure to comply with conditions can be fatal to a claim. However, because the parties disagreed on the interpretation of the conditions and the factual circumstances surrounding the transaction, oral evidence is required to clarify the intent and the legal effect of the deed. Finding: A full trial de novo is necessary to ascertain the factual positions and properly apply the law regarding conditional transfers and prescription. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the court's determination that the case involves complex factual disputes (Issue 2) directly necessitates the conclusion that the preliminary issues of law could not be resolved in isolation (Issue 1). The resolution of the legal status of the deed is contingent upon the evidence to be led at trial, rendering the previous attempt to decide the case on preliminary issues premature. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. Both the judgments of the District Court and the High Court are set aside, and the case is remanded to the District Court with a direction to commence a trial de novo. Keywords Leave to appeal — High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act — District Court order — Promissory deed — Conditional transfer — Entitlement to possession — Prescription — Evidence Ordinance — Prevention of Frauds Ordinance

Parties: Coral Sands Hotel (Private) Limited and Goonewardena v. Karunanayaka and Others
Bench: K. Sripavan, J.; P. Jayawardena, P.C., J; U. Abeyrathne, J
Keywords: Excise Ordinance — Excise licence fees — Gazette Notification — Parliamentary approval — Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Ultra vires action — Public finance — Article 148 of the Constitution
Summary: Facts The Petitioners, Coral Sands Hotel (Private) Limited and its Managing Director, challenged Excise Notification No. 974, which imposed enhanced annual licence fees. The Petitioners were granted leave to proceed for alleged violations of Articles 12(1) and 13(6) of the Constitution. While the application was pending, the third Respondent issued a letter requiring the Petitioners to pay arrears of the enhanced licence fees for the year 2015 as a condition for considering their application for an excise licence for the year 2016. The Petitioners sought interim relief to restrain the Respondents from enforcing the payment of these enhanced fees. Issues Whether the third Respondent is entitled to recover enhanced licence fees under Excise Notification No. 974 in the absence of Parliamentary confirmation and gazetting as required by Section 32 of the Excise Ordinance. Whether the requirement to pay arrears of enhanced licence fees as a condition for granting a future licence constitutes an ultra-vires exercise of power. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 12(1), 13(6), 126, 148 Excise Ordinance, Sections 2, 25, 32 Cases Referred to: Rajanayagam v. Wijeratne - [1998/v3-sri-lr-129] Analysis Issue 1: Parliamentary confirmation of Excise Notification Rule applied: Section 32 of the Excise Ordinance, which requires rules made by the Minister to be confirmed by Parliament and notified in the Gazette to have the force of law. Court's reasoning: The court observed that there was no evidence that Excise Notification No. 974 had been placed before or confirmed by Parliament. Citing Rajanayagam v. Wijeratne, the court emphasized that Section 32(1) imperatively requires Parliamentary approval for rules made under the Ordinance. Without such confirmation and subsequent gazetting, the notification lacks the force of law. Finding: The third Respondent cannot recover the enhanced licence fees because the notification has not attained the status of law. Issue 2: Ultra-vires exercise of power Rule applied: Article 148 of the Constitution, which mandates that no tax or levy shall be imposed except by or under the authority of a law passed by Parliament. Court's reasoning: The court held that the authority granting a licence must act within the limits of the law. By conditioning the 2016 licence on the payment of arrears for 2015 based on an unconfirmed notification, the authority relied on irrelevant or extraneous considerations. Furthermore, Article 148 requires that any levy imposed on a citizen must have the approval of Parliament. Finding: The demand for payment of arrears based on the unconfirmed notification is ultra-vires and bad in law. Interaction of Issues The findings are mutually reinforcing; the failure to satisfy the procedural requirements of Section 32 of the Excise Ordinance renders the notification legally ineffective, which in turn means that any attempt to enforce payment under that notification violates the constitutional principle of Parliamentary control over public finance under Article 148. Conclusion The court issued an interim order restraining the Respondents from requiring the Petitioners to pay the purported arrears for the year 2015 under Excise Notification No. 974. Consequently, all actions taken pursuant to the third Respondent's letter dated 07.10.2015 are stayed until the final determination of the application. Keywords Excise Ordinance — Excise licence fees — Gazette Notification — Parliamentary approval — Fundamental rights — Article 12(1) of the Constitution — Ultra vires action — Public finance — Article 148 of the Constitution

Parties: Hangidigedara Thilakaratne v. Galkaduwegedara Jayathilake and Others
Bench: Eva Wanasundera PC, J; B. Aluwihare PC, J; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Partition action — Transfer of rights — Deed of transfer — Final decree — Allotment of land — Section 66 of the Partition Law — Section 67 of the Partition Act — Exceptio rei venditae et traditiae — Title to land — Burden of proof — Rei vindicatio
Summary: Facts The original plaintiff, Hangidigedara Thilakaratne, instituted an action in the District Court of Kandy seeking a declaration of title to a land described in schedule B of the plaint, the eviction of the defendant, and damages. Following the death of the original plaintiff, his wife, RAG Sumanawathi, was substituted as the plaintiff. The plaintiff claimed title through a chain of deeds starting with a transfer from one Sethuwa to Sirisena via deed No. 2621, and a subsequent transfer from Sirisena and Welliya to the original plaintiff via deed No. 933. During the pendency of these transactions, a partition action No. 7445/P was ongoing regarding the land. In the final decree of that partition action, Sethuwa was allotted Lot No. 4, which constitutes the corpus of the present dispute. The defendant, Galkaduwegedara Sunil Jayathilake, contested the claim, asserting that Sethuwa had transferred his rights to him via deed No. 10839 and that he had remained in uninterrupted possession of the land. The District Court dismissed the plaintiff's action, finding that the plaintiff failed to prove his title. The Civil Appellate High Court affirmed this judgment. The plaintiff subsequently appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the High Court and District Court erred in law by finding that the vendors of deed No. 933 were not allotted any share from the corpus, despite the plaintiff's claim that they were entitled via deed No. 2621. Whether the courts below erred in law by concluding that the plaintiff failed to prove title due to the absence of a reference to deed No. 2621 in deed No. 933. Whether the courts below erred in law by failing to consider evidence that the vendors in deed No. 933 transferred rights to the corpus to the plaintiff. Whether the maxim of exceptio rei venditae et traditiae is applicable in the circumstances of this case. Applicable Law Statutes: Partition Law No. 21 of 1977 (Section 66) Partition Act No. 16 of 1950 (Section 67) Cases Referred to: MWAP Jayathilake v. PG Somadasa - [70-nlr-25] B. Sillie Fernando v. W Silman Fernando - [64-nlr-404] Luwis Singho v. Ponnamperuma - [1996/v2-sri-lr-320] Loku Menika v. Gunasekare - [1997/v2-sri-lr-281] Analysis Issue 1, 2, and 3: Proof of Title and Deed Validity Rule applied: Section 66 of the Partition Law No. 21 of 1977 and Section 67 of the Partition Act No. 16 of 1950. Court's reasoning: The court examined the chain of title and found that Sirisena and Welliya were not parties to the partition action No. 7445/P. Consequently, they were never allotted any share of the corpus in the final decree. Deed No. 933, upon which the plaintiff relied, purported to transfer rights that might be allotted to the vendors in the partition case. Since the vendors had no standing in the partition action, they held no title to transfer. Furthermore, the court noted that deed No. 933 failed to reference the interest allegedly derived from Sethuwa via deed No. 2621. Finding: The plaintiff failed to establish a valid chain of title to the corpus, and the lower courts correctly identified this failure. Issue 4: Applicability of exceptio rei venditae et traditiae Rule applied: The Roman-Dutch common law doctrine of exceptio rei venditae et traditiae. Court's reasoning: The court noted that this doctrine applies where a vendor sells property without title but subsequently acquires it, causing the title to accrue to the benefit of the purchaser. However, the court found the doctrine inapplicable here because the vendors in deed No. 933 were not parties to the partition action and never acquired any title to the corpus that could have accrued to the plaintiff. Finding: The doctrine of exceptio rei venditae et traditiae does not apply to the facts of this case. Interaction of Issues The findings on the validity of the deeds and the failure to prove title are reinforced by the inapplicability of the exceptio rei venditae et traditiae. Because the vendors lacked any legal interest in the partition action, the failure to prove title is absolute, rendering the plaintiff's claim for rei vindicatio unsustainable. Conclusion The Supreme Court answered the questions of law in the negative and upheld the judgments of the District Court and the Civil Appellate High Court. The appeal is dismissed. Keywords Partition action — Transfer of rights — Deed of transfer — Final decree — Allotment of land — Section 66 of the Partition Law — Section 67 of the Partition Act — Exceptio rei venditae et traditiae — Title to land — Burden of proof — Rei vindicatio

Parties: Perera v. Ranasinghe and Others
Bench: K. Sripavan, J.; Eva Wanasundera, P.C. J; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.
Keywords: Fundamental rights — Article 11 (torture) — Article 12(1) (equality before the law) — Article 13(1) and 13(2) — Police brutality — Illegal arrest — Assault by police officer — Compensation
Summary: Facts The Petitioner, Hapugodage Jagath Perera, visited the Mirigama Police Station on 19.11.2009 to assist his employee, Asanka Sanjaya Kumara, who had been summoned for an inquiry. During the inquiry, the Petitioner requested the 1st Respondent, the Officer-in-Charge of the Minor Crimes Branch, to encourage the parties to reconcile. The 1st Respondent left the room and returned with the 2nd Respondent, the Officer-in-Charge of the Traffic Branch. The 2nd Respondent questioned the Petitioner and subsequently took him inside the police station building, where he assaulted the Petitioner, causing him to fracture his teeth. The Petitioner was then placed in a police cell and later produced before a Magistrate on a B report. The Petitioner informed the Magistrate of the assault at the time of his production. The Petitioner was released on bail on 26.11.2009 and subsequently admitted to the Ragama Hospital for treatment. The 1st, 2nd, and 3rd Respondents contended that the Petitioner had behaved in a disorderly manner, abused the 2nd Respondent, and pulled his uniform. They alleged that the Petitioner sustained injuries after falling while the 2nd Respondent was using minimum force to control him. The Petitioner filed the instant application alleging violations of his fundamental rights under Articles 11, 12(1), 13(1), and 13(2) of the Constitution. Issues Whether the 2nd Respondent violated the Petitioner's fundamental rights guaranteed by Article 11 (freedom from torture) and Article 12(1) (right to equality/equal protection of the law) of the Constitution. Whether the arrest of the Petitioner was illegal, thereby violating his fundamental rights. Whether the State is liable to pay compensation for the fundamental rights violations committed by the 2nd Respondent. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka, Articles 11, 12(1), 13(1), 13(2) Cases Referred to: Amal Sudath Silva v. Kodituwakku Inspector of Police - [1987/v2-sri-lr-119] Analysis Issue 1: Violation of Article 11 and 12(1) Rule applied: Article 11 of the Constitution, which mandates that no person shall be subjected to torture or to cruel, inhuman, or degrading treatment or punishment. Court's reasoning: The court rejected the Respondents' version of events, noting that the 2nd Respondent failed to mention the alleged fall or the Petitioner's resistance in the B report filed on the day of the incident. The court found the medical evidence, including the Prison Doctor's report and the Medico Legal Report from the Judicial Medical Officer, confirmed the fracture of the Petitioner's teeth. The court concluded that the 2nd Respondent used excessive force against an unarmed man, constituting torture and cruel, inhuman, and degrading treatment. Finding: The 2nd Respondent violated the Petitioner's fundamental rights under Article 11 and Article 12(1). Issue 2: Legality of the arrest Rule applied: Article 13(1) of the Constitution regarding the right to be free from arbitrary arrest. Court's reasoning: The court evaluated the Respondents' claim that the arrest was necessitated by the Petitioner's conduct at the police station. Having rejected the Respondents' account of the incident as untruthful and finding no evidence of a criminal record for the Petitioner, the court determined there were no reasonable grounds for the arrest. Finding: The arrest of the Petitioner was illegal. Issue 3: State liability for compensation Rule applied: The principle of State liability for acts committed by public officers in the course of their official duties. Court's reasoning: The court noted that the 2nd Respondent was acting in his capacity as a police officer during the incident. Although the 2nd Respondent is deceased, the court held that the State remains liable to compensate the Petitioner for the permanent damage sustained. Finding: The State is ordered to pay compensation to the Petitioner. Interaction of Issues The court's finding that the Respondents' version of events was fabricated (Issue 1) directly undermined the justification for the arrest (Issue 2). Because the arrest was found to be without reasonable grounds and the force used was excessive, the court established a clear basis for the violation of constitutional protections, which necessitated the award of compensation by the State (Issue 3). Conclusion The court held that the 2nd Respondent violated the Petitioner's fundamental rights under Articles 11 and 12(1) of the Constitution. The State is ordered to pay the Petitioner a sum of Rs. 500,000/- as compensation. The application against the 1st and 3rd Respondents is dismissed. Keywords Fundamental rights — Article 11 (torture) — Article 12(1) (equality before the law) — Article 13(1) and 13(2) — Police brutality — Illegal arrest — Assault by police officer — Compensation

Parties: Jathika Sevaka Sangamaya v. Sri Lanka Hadabima Authority
Bench: S. E. Wanasundera, PC. J; K.S.J. De Abrew, J.; Priyantha Jayawardena, PC. J
Keywords: Separation of powers — Judicial review — Cabinet decisions — Labour Tribunal jurisdiction — Industrial Disputes Act — State Agricultural Corporations Act — Independent legal entity — Government policy — Administrative decisions
Summary: Facts The Jathika Sevaka Sangamaya filed applications before the Labour Tribunal of Kandy on behalf of five workmen alleging that their services with the Sri Lanka Hadabima Authority were wrongfully terminated effective June 1, 2005. The Respondent Authority initially denied the termination, claiming the contracts could not be extended due to a lack of provisions. The Appellant alleged the termination was politically motivated. The Respondent Authority subsequently filed an amended answer asserting that the terminations were the result of a Cabinet of Ministers decision dated March 2, 2005, and Circular No. 27/2001, which rendered appointments made after October 1, 2001, invalid. The Labour Tribunal consolidated the cases and ruled in favor of the Respondent Authority, holding that the Tribunal lacked jurisdiction to override a Cabinet decision. The Appellant appealed this decision to the High Court of the Central Province. The High Court affirmed the Labour Tribunal's order, concluding that the services were terminated pursuant to a central government decision rather than by the Respondent Authority itself, and therefore, the Tribunal correctly determined it lacked jurisdiction. The Appellant then appealed to the Supreme Court. Issues Whether the Labour Tribunal and the High Court erred in law by holding that the Labour Tribunal lacked jurisdiction to consider a termination of services merely because it was based on a decision of the Cabinet of Ministers. Whether the High Court erred in law by affirming the Labour Tribunal's award without properly evaluating the evidence or considering the independent legal status of the Respondent Authority. Applicable Law Statutes: Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka (Articles 3, 4, 42, 55, 111C, 114, 116, 170) Industrial Disputes Act (as amended) (Section 31B, 31C, 31DD) High Court of the Provinces (Special Provisions) Act No. 19 of 1990 (Section 9) State Agricultural Corporations Act No. 11 (Section 2, 6, 8) Cases Referred to: In re the nineteenth amendment to the Constitution - [2002/v3-sri-lr-85] Attorney-General v. Dr. Upathissa Atapattu Bandaranayake Wasala Mudiyanse Ralahamige Shirani Anshumala Bandaranayake - [sc-minutes-dated-21st-february-2014] Upali Newspapers Ltd. v. Eksath Kamkaru Samithiya - [1999/v3-sri-lr-205] Walker Sons & Co. Ltd. v. F.C.W. Fry - [68-nlr-73] Insurance Corporation of Sri Lanka v. Ceylon Mercantile Union - [iii-srisk-lr-13] Karunaratne v. Uva Regional Transport Board - [1986/v3-calr-93] Analysis Issue 1: Jurisdiction regarding Cabinet decisions Rule applied: Articles 4, 111C, and 170 of the Constitution; Industrial Disputes Act. Court's reasoning: The Court held that Labour Tribunals exercise judicial power and are independent of the executive and legislature. While the Cabinet of Ministers performs executive functions, its decisions do not automatically oust the jurisdiction of a Labour Tribunal. The Tribunal has the power to examine the legality and applicability of such decisions in the context of an industrial dispute, provided it does not attempt to review the policy itself. The lower courts erred by concluding that the mere existence of a Cabinet decision deprived the Tribunal of its statutory jurisdiction to adjudicate the termination. Finding: The Labour Tribunal has jurisdiction to inquire into the termination of services even if the Respondent Authority acted pursuant to a Cabinet decision. Issue 2: Evaluation of evidence and independent status Rule applied: State Agricultural Corporations Act No. 11. Court's reasoning: The Respondent Authority is a body corporate established under the State Agricultural Corporations Act, making it a distinct legal entity from a government department. The Court noted that the lower courts failed to evaluate whether the Respondent Authority followed the proper procedures mandated by its own governing statute when implementing the Cabinet decision. By failing to conduct an inquiry under Section 31C of the Industrial Disputes Act, the Tribunal failed to determine if the termination was just and equitable. Finding: The High Court erred in affirming the award without ensuring that the statutory requirements for termination were met by the independent corporate entity. Interaction of Issues The findings are interdependent; the determination that the Tribunal possesses jurisdiction (Issue 1) necessitates a substantive inquiry into whether the Respondent Authority, as an independent corporate entity, followed the law (Issue 2). The failure to recognize the Tribunal's jurisdiction prevented the necessary factual inquiry into the legality of the termination process. Conclusion The appeal is allowed. The orders of the High Court and the Labour Tribunal are set aside, and the President of the Labour Tribunal is directed to proceed with the inquiry under Section 31C of the Industrial Disputes Act to determine if the termination was just and equitable. Keywords Separation of powers — Judicial review — Cabinet decisions — Labour Tribunal jurisdiction — Industrial Disputes Act — State Agricultural Corporations Act — Independent legal entity — Government policy — Administrative decisions
